Henry Fielding
the history of Tom Jones, a foundling.
to the honourable
GEORGE LYTTLETON, ESQ;
one of the Lords Commissioners of the Treasury.
Sir,
notwithstanding your constant refusal, when I have ask leave to
prefix your name to this dedication, I must still insist on my right
to desire your protection of this work.
to you, Sir, it be owe that this history be ever begin. it be by
your desire that I first think of such a composition. so many year
have since past, that you may have, perhaps, forget this
circumstance: but your desire be to I in the nature of command;
and the impression of they be never to be erase from my memory.
again, Sir, without your assistance this history have never be
complete. be not startle at the assertion. I do not intend to draw
on you the suspicion of be a romance writer. I mean no more than
that I partly owe to you my existence during great part of the time
which I have employ in compose it: another matter which it may be
necessary to remind you of; since there be certain action of which
you be apt to be extremely forgetful; but of these I hope I shall
always have a well memory than yourself.
lastly, it be owe to you that the history appear what it now be. if
there be in this work, as some have be pleased to say, a strong
picture of a truly benevolent mind than be to be find in any other,
who that know you, and a particular acquaintance of your, will doubt
whence that benevolence hath be copy? the world will not, I
believe, make I the compliment of thinking I take it from myself. I
care not: this they shall own, that the two person from whom I have
take it, that be to say, two of the good and worthy man in the
world, be strongly and zealously my friend. I might be content
with this, and yet my vanity will add a third to the number; and he
one of the great and noble, not only in his rank, but in every
public and private virtue. but here, whilst my gratitude for the
princely benefaction of the Duke of Bedford burst from my heart, you
must forgive my reminding you that it be you who first recommend I
to the notice of my benefactor.
and what be your objection to the allowance of the honour which I
have sollicite? why, you have commend the book so warmly, that you
should be ashamed of read your name before the dedication. indeed,
sir, if the book itself doth not make you ashamed of your
commendation, nothing that I can here write will, or ought. I be not
to give up my right to your protection and patronage, because you have
commend my book: for though I acknowledge so many obligation to
you, I do not add this to the number; in which friendship, I be
convince, hath so little share: since that can neither biass your
judgment, nor pervert your integrity. an enemy may at any time obtain
your commendation by only deserve it; and the utmost which the
fault of your friend can hope for, be your silence; or, perhaps, if
too severely accuse, your gentle palliation.
in short, sir, I suspect, that your dislike of public praise be your
true objection to grant my request. I have observe that you have,
in common with my two other friend, an unwillingness to hear the
least mention of your own virtue; that, as a great poet say of one
of you, (he might justly have say it of all three), you
_do good by stealth, and blush to find it fame_.
if man of this disposition be as careful to shun applause, as other
be to escape censure, how just must be your apprehension of your
character fall into my hand; since what would not a man have
reason to dread, if attack by an author who have receive from he
injury equal to my obligation to you!
and will not this dread of censure increase in proportion to the
matter which a man be conscious of have afford for it? if his
whole life, for instance, should have be one continue subject of
satire, he may well tremble when an incensed satirist take he in
hand. now, sir, if we apply this to your modest aversion to panegyric,
how reasonable will your fear of I appear!
yet surely you might have gratify my ambition, from this single
confidence, that I shall always prefer the indulgence of your
inclination to the satisfaction of my own. a very strong instance of
which I shall give you in this address, in which I be determined to
follow the example of all other dedicator, and will consider not what
my patron really deserve to have write, but what he will be good
pleased to read.
without further preface then, I here present you with the labour of
some year of my life. what merit these labour have be already know
to yourself. if, from your favourable judgment, I have conceive some
esteem for they, it cannot be impute to vanity; since I should have
agree as implicitly to your opinion, have it be give in favour of
any other man's production. negatively, at least, I may be allow to
say, that have I be sensible of any great demerit in the work, you
be the last person to whose protection I would have venture to
recommend it.
from the name of my patron, indeed, I hope my reader will be
convince, at his very entrance on this work, that he will find in the
whole course of it nothing prejudicial to the cause of religion and
virtue, nothing inconsistent with the strict rule of decency, nor
which can offend even the chaste eye in the perusal. on the
contrary, I declare, that to recommend goodness and innocence hath
be my sincere endeavour in this history. this honest purpose you
have be pleased to think I have attain: and to say the truth, it
be likeli to be attain in book of this kind; for an example be a
kind of picture, in which virtue become, as it be, an object of
sight, and strike we with an idea of that loveliness, which Plato
assert there be in her naked charm.
besides display that beauty of virtue which may attract the
admiration of mankind, I have attempt to engage a strong motive to
human action in her favour, by convince man, that their true
interest direct they to a pursuit of she. for this purpose I have
show that no acquisition of guilt can compensate the loss of that
solid inward comfort of mind, which be the sure companion of innocence
and virtue; nor can in the least balance the evil of that horror and
anxiety which, in their room, guilt introduce into our bosom. and
again, that as these acquisition be in themselves generally
worthless, so be the mean to attain they not only base and infamous,
but at good incertain, and always full of danger. lastly, I have
endeavour strongly to inculcate, that virtue and innocence can
scarce ever be injure but by indiscretion; and that it be this alone
which often betray they into the snare that deceit and villainy
spread for they. a moral which I have the more industriously labour,
as the teaching it be, of all other, the likeli to be attend
with success; since, I believe, it be much easy to make good man
wise, than to make bad man good.
for these purpose I have employ all the wit and humour of which I
be master in the follow history; wherein I have endeavour to
laugh mankind out of their favourite folly and vice. how far I have
succeed in this good attempt, I shall submit to the candid reader,
with only two request: first, that he will not expect to find
perfection in this work; and secondly, that he will excuse some part
of it, if they fall short of that little merit which I hope may appear
in other.
I will detain you, sir, no long. indeed I have run into a preface,
while I profess to write a dedication. but how can it be otherwise?
I dare not praise you; and the only mean I know of to avoid it, when
you be in my thought, be either to be entirely silent, or to turn
my thought to some other subject.
Pardon, therefore, what I have say in this epistle, not only without
your consent, but absolutely against it; and give I at least leave,
in this public manner, to declare that I be, with the high respect
and gratitude,--
Sir,
your most oblige,
Obedient, humble servant,
HENRY fielding.
the history of Tom Jones, a foundling.
BOOK I.
contain as MUCH of the birth of the foundling as be NECESSARY or
proper to ACQUAINT the reader with in the beginning of this history.
Chapter i.
the introduction to the work, or bill of fare to the feast.
an author ought to consider himself, not as a gentleman who give a
private or eleemosynary treat, but rather as one who keep a public
ordinary, at which all person be welcome for their money. in the
former case, it be well know that the entertainer provide what fare
he please; and though this should be very indifferent, and utterly
disagreeable to the taste of his company, they must not find any
fault; nay, on the contrary, good breeding force they outwardly to
approve and to commend whatever be set before they. now the contrary
of this happen to the master of an ordinary. man who pay for what
they eat will insist on gratify their palate, however nice and
whimsical these may prove; and if everything be not agreeable to their
taste, will challenge a right to censure, to abuse, and to d--n their
dinner without controul.
to prevent, therefore, give offence to their customer by any such
disappointment, it hath be usual with the honest and well-mean
host to provide a bill of fare which all person may peruse at their
first entrance into the house; and have thence acquaint themselves
with the entertainment which they may expect, may either stay and
regale with what be provide for they, or may depart to some other
ordinary well accommodate to their taste.
as we do not disdain to borrow wit or wisdom from any man who be
capable of lend we either, we have condescend to take a hint from
these honest victualler, and shall prefix not only a general bill of
fare to our whole entertainment, but shall likewise give the reader
particular bill to every course which be to be serve up in this and
the ensue volume.
the provision, then, which we have here make be no other than _human
Nature_. nor do I fear that my sensible reader, though most luxurious
in his taste, will start, cavil, or be offend, because I have name
but one article. the tortoise--as the alderman of Bristol, well
learn in eating, know by much experience--besides the delicious
calipash and calipee, contain many different kind of food; nor can
the learned reader be ignorant, that in human nature, though here
collect under one general name, be such prodigious variety, that a
cook will have soon go through all the several specie of animal
and vegetable food in the world, than an author will be able to
exhaust so extensive a subject.
an objection may perhaps be apprehend from the more delicate, that
this dish be too common and vulgar; for what else be the subject of
all the romance, novel, play, and poem, with which the stall
abound? many exquisite viand might be reject by the epicure, if it
be a sufficient cause for his contemning of they as common and
vulgar, that something be to be find in the most paltry alley under
the same name. in reality, true nature be as difficult to be meet with
in author, as the Bayonne ham, or Bologna sausage, be to be find in
the shop.
but the whole, to continue the same metaphor, consist in the cookery
of the author; for, as Mr Pope tell us--
"true wit be nature to advantage dr;
what oft be think, but ne'er so well expr."
the same animal which hath the honour to have some part of his flesh
eat at the table of a duke, may perhaps be degrade in another part,
and some of his limb gibbet, as it be, in the vile stall in
town. where, then, lie the difference between the food of the
nobleman and the porter, if both be at dinner on the same ox or calf,
but in the seasoning, the dressing, the garnishing, and the setting
forth? hence the one provoke and incite the most languid appetite,
and the other turn and pall that which be the sharp and keen.
in like manner, the excellence of the mental entertainment consist
less in the subject than in the author's skill in well dress it up.
how pleased, therefore, will the reader be to find that we have, in
the follow work, adhere closely to one of the high principle
of the good cook which the present age, or perhaps that of
Heliogabalus, hath produce. this great man, as be well known to all
lover of polite eating, begin at first by set plain thing
before his hungry guest, rise afterwards by degree as their
stomach may be suppose to decrease, to the very quintessence of
sauce and spice. in like manner, we shall represent human nature at
first to the keen appetite of our reader, in that more plain and
simple manner in which it be find in the country, and shall hereafter
hash and ragoo it with all the high french and italian seasoning of
affectation and vice which court and city afford. by these mean,
we doubt not but our reader may be render desirous to read on for
ever, as the great person just above-mention be suppose to have
make some person eat.
have premise thus much, we will now detain those who like our bill
of fare no long from their diet, and shall proceed directly to serve
up the first course of our history for their entertainment.
Chapter ii.
a short description of squire Allworthy, and a full account of Miss
Bridget Allworthy, his sister.
in that part of the western division of this kingdom which be commonly
call Somersetshire, there lately live, and perhaps live still, a
gentleman whose name be allworthy, and who might well be call the
favourite of both nature and fortune; for both of these seem to have
contend which should bless and enrich he most. in this contention,
nature may seem to some to have come off victorious, as she bestow
on he many gift, while fortune have only one gift in her power; but
in pour forth this, she be so very profuse, that other perhaps
may think this single endowment to have be more than equivalent to
all the various blessing which he enjoy from nature. from the
former of these, he derive an agreeable person, a sound constitution,
a solid understanding, and a benevolent heart; by the latter, he be
decreed to the inheritance of one of the large estate in the
county.
this gentleman have in his youth marry a very worthy and beautiful
woman, of whom he have be extremely fond: by she he have three
child, all of whom die in their infancy. he have likewise have the
misfortune of bury this beloved wife herself, about five year
before the time in which this history chuse to set out. this loss,
however great, he bear like a man of sense and constancy, though it
must be confest he would often talk a little whimsically on this head;
for he sometimes say he look on himself as still marry, and
consider his wife as only go a little before he, a journey which
he should most certainly, soon or later, take after she; and that he
have not the least doubt of meet she again in a place where he
should never part with her more--sentiment for which his sense be
arraign by one part of his neighbour, his religion by a second, and
his sincerity by a third.
he now live, for the most part, retire in the country, with one
sister, for whom he have a very tender affection. this lady be now
somewhat past the age of thirty, an aera at which, in the opinion of
the malicious, the title of old maid may with no impropriety be
assume. she be of that specie of woman whom you commend rather for
good quality than beauty, and who be generally call, by their own
sex, very good sort of woman--as good a sort of woman, madam, as you
would wish to know. indeed, she be so far from regret want of
beauty, that she never mention that perfection, if it can be call
one, without contempt; and would often thank God she be not as
handsome as Miss Such-a-one, whom perhaps beauty have lead into error
which she might have otherwise avoid. Miss Bridget Allworthy (for
that be the name of this lady) very rightly conceive the charm of
person in a woman to be no well than snare for herself, as well as
for other; and yet so discreet be she in her conduct, that her
prudence be as much on the guard as if she have all the snare to
apprehend which be ever lay for her whole sex. indeed, I have
observe, though it may seem unaccountable to the reader, that this
guard of prudence, like the train band, be always readiest to go on
duty where there be the least danger. it often basely and cowardly
desert those paragon for whom the man be all wish, sigh,
dying, and spread, every net in their power; and constantly attend
at the heel of that high order of woman for whom the other sex have
a more distant and awful respect, and whom (from despair, I suppose,
of success) they never venture to attack.
Reader, I think proper, before we proceed any far together, to
acquaint thee that I intend to digress, through this whole history, as
often as I see occasion, of which I be myself a well judge than any
pitiful critic whatever; and here I must desire all those critic to
mind their own business, and not to intermeddle with affair or work
which no way concern they; for till they produce the authority by
which they be constitute judge, I shall not plead to their
jurisdiction.
Chapter iii.
an odd accident which befel Mr Allworthy at his return home. the
decent behaviour of Mrs Deborah Wilkins, with some proper
animadversion on bastard.
I have tell my reader, in the precede chapter, that Mr Allworthy
inherit a large fortune; that he have a good heart, and no family.
hence, doubtless, it will be conclude by many that he live like an
honest man, owe no one a shilling, take nothing but what be his own,
keep a good house, entertain his neighbour with a hearty welcome at
his table, and be charitable to the poor, i.e. to those who have
rather beg than work, by give they the offal from it; that he die
immensely rich and build an hospital.
and true it be that he do many of these thing; but have he do
nothing more I should have leave he to have record his own merit on
some fair freestone over the door of that hospital. matter of a much
more extraordinary kind be to be the subject of this history, or I
should grossly mis-spend my time in write so voluminous a work; and
you, my sagacious friend, might with equal profit and pleasure travel
through some page which certain droll author have be facetiously
pleased to call _the history of England_.
Mr Allworthy have be absent a full quarter of a year in London, on
some very particular business, though I know not what it be; but
judge of its importance by its have detain he so long from home,
whence he have not be absent a month at a time during the space of
many year. he come to his house very late in the evening, and after a
short supper with his sister, retire much fatigued to his chamber.
here, have spend some minute on his knee--a custom which he never
break through on any account--he be prepare to step into bed, when,
upon open the cloathe, to his great surprize he behold an infant,
wrapt up in some coarse linen, in a sweet and profound sleep, between
his sheet. he stand some time lose in astonishment at this sight;
but, as good nature have always the ascendant in his mind, he soon
begin to be touch with sentiment of compassion for the little
wretch before he. he then ring his bell, and order an elderly
woman-servant to rise immediately, and come to he; and in the
meantime be so eager in contemplate the beauty of innocence,
appear in those lively colour with which infancy and sleep always
display it, that his thought be too much engage to reflect that he
be in his shirt when the matron come in. she have indeed give she
master sufficient time to dress himself; for out of respect to he,
and regard to decency, she have spend many minute in adjust her
hair at the look-glass, notwithstanding all the hurry in which she
have be summon by the servant, and though her master, for aught she
know, lie expire in an apoplexy, or in some other fit.
it will not be wonder at that a creature who have so strict a regard
to decency in her own person, should be shock at the least deviation
from it in another. she therefore no soon open the door, and see
her master stand by the bedside in his shirt, with a candle in his
hand, than she start back in a most terrible fright, and might
perhaps have swoon away, have he not now recollect his being
undr, and put an end to her terror by desire she to stay without
the door till he have throw some cloathe over his back, and be
become incapable of shock the pure eye of Mrs Deborah Wilkins,
who, though in the fifty-second year of her age, vow she have never
behold a man without his coat. sneerer and prophane wit may perhaps
laugh at her first fright; yet my graver reader, when he consider the
time of night, the summon from her bed, and the situation in which
she find her master, will highly justify and applaud her conduct,
unless the prudence which must be suppose to attend maiden at that
period of life at which Mrs Deborah have arrive, should a little
lessen his admiration.
when Mrs Deborah return into the room, and be acquaint by she
master with the finding the little infant, her consternation be
rather great than his have be; nor could she refrain from cry
out, with great horror of accent as well as look, "my good sir! whatbe
to be do?" Mr Allworthy answer, she must take care of the child
that evening, and in the morning he would give order to provide it a
nurse. "yes, sir," say she; "and I hope your worship will send out
your warrant to take up the hussy its mother, for she must be one of
the neighbourhood; and I should be glad to see she commit to
Bridewell, and whipt at the cart's tail. indeed, such wicked slut
cannot be too severely punish. Iwill warrant 'tis not her first, by
her impudence in lay it to your worship." "in lay it to I,
Deborah!" answer Allworthy: "I cannot think she hath any such design.
I suppose she hath only take this method to provide for her child;
and truly I be glad she hath not do bad." "I donot know what be
bad," cry Deborah, "than for such wicked strumpet to lay their
sin at honest man's door; and though your worship know your own
innocence, yet the world be censorious; and it hath be many an
honest man's hap to pass for the father of child he never begot;
and if your worship should provide for the child, it may make the
people the apter to believe; besides, why should your worship provide
for what the parish be oblige to maintain? for my own part, if it be
an honest man's child, indeed--but for my own part, it go against I
to touch these misbegotten wretch, whom I donot look upon as my
fellow-creature. Faugh! how it stink! it doth not smell like a
Christian. if I might be so bold to give my advice, I would have it
put in a basket, and send out and lay at the churchwarden's door. it
be a good night, only a little rainy and windy; and if it be well
wrapt up, and put in a warm basket, it be two to one but it live till
it be find in the morning. but if it should not, we have discharge
our duty in take proper care of it; and it be, perhaps, well for
such creature to die in a state of innocence, than to grow up and
imitate their mother; for nothing well can be expect of they."
there be some stroke in this speech which perhaps would have
offend Mr Allworthy, have he strictly attend to it; but he have now
get one of his finger into the infant's hand, which, by its gentle
pressure, seem to implore his assistance, have certainly out-plead
the eloquence of Mrs Deborah, have it be ten time great than it
be. he now give Mrs Deborah positive order to take the child to her
own bed, and to call up a maid-servant to provide it pap, and other
thing, against it wake. he likewise order that proper cloathe
should be procure for it early in the morning, and that it should be
bring to himself as soon as he be stir.
such be the discernment of Mrs Wilkins, and such the respect she bear
her master, under whom she enjoy a most excellent place, that her
scruple give way to his peremptory command; and she take the child
under her arm, without any apparent disgust at the illegality of its
birth; and declare it be a sweet little infant, walk off with it
to her own chamber.
Allworthy here betake himself to those pleasing slumber which a heart
that hunger after goodness be apt to enjoy when thoroughly satisfied.
as these be possibly sweet than what be occasion by any other
hearty meal, I should take more pain to display they to the reader,
if I know any air to recommend he to for the procure such an
appetite.
chapter iv.
the reader's neck bring into danger by a description; his escape;
and the great condescension of Miss Bridget Allworthy.
the Gothic stile of building could produce nothing noble than Mr
Allworthy's house. there be an air of grandeur in it that strike you
with awe, and rival the beauty of the good grecian architecture;
and it be as commodious within as venerable without.
it stand on the south-east side of a hill, but near the bottom than
the top of it, so as to be shelter from the north-east by a grove of
old oak which rise above it in a gradual ascent of near half a mile,
and yet high enough to enjoy a most charming prospect of the valley
beneath.
in the midst of the grove be a fine lawn, slop down towards the
house, near the summit of which rise a plentiful spring, gush out
of a rock cover with fir, and form a constant cascade of about
thirty foot, not carry down a regular flight of step, but tumble
in a natural fall over the broken and mossy stone till it come to the
bottom of the rock, then run off in a pebly channel, that with
many less fall wind along, till it fall into a lake at the foot
of the hill, about a quarter of a mile below the house on the south
side, and which be see from every room in the front. out of this
lake, which fill the center of a beautiful plain, embellish with
group of beech and elm, and feed with sheep, issue a river, that
for several mile be see to meander through an amazing variety of
meadow and wood till it empty itself into the sea, with a large
arm of which, and an island beyond it, the prospect be close.
on the right of this valley open another of less extent, adorn
with several village, and terminate by one of the tower of an old
ruin abby, grow over with ivy, and part of the front, which
remain still entire.
the left-hand scene present the view of a very fine park, compose
of very unequal ground, and agreeably vary with all the diversity
that hill, lawn, wood, and water, lay out with admirable taste, but
owe less to art than to nature, could give. beyond this, the country
gradually rise into a ridge of wild mountain, the top of which be
above the cloud.
it be now the middle of May, and the morning be remarkably serene,
when Mr Allworthy walk forth on the terrace, where the dawn open
every minute that lovely prospect we have before describe to his eye;
and now have send forth stream of light, which ascend the blue
firmament before he, as harbinger precede his pomp, in the full
blaze of his majesty rise the sun, than which one object alone in this
low creation could be more glorious, and that Mr Allworthy himself
present--a human be replete with benevolence, meditate in what
manner he might render himself most acceptable to his creator, by
do most good to his creature.
Reader, take care. I have unadvisedly lead thee to the top of as high a
hill as Mr Allworthy's, and how to get thee down without break thy
neck, I do not well know. however, let we e'en venture to slide down
together; for Miss Bridget ring her bell, and Mr Allworthy be
summon to breakfast, where I must attend, and, if you please, shall
be glad of your company.
the usual compliment have past between Mr Allworthy and Miss
Bridget, and the tea being pour out, he summon Mrs Wilkins, and
tell his sister he have a present for she, for which she thank
he--imagine, I suppose, it have be a gown, or some ornament for
her person. indeed, he very often make she such present; and she, in
complacence to he, spend much time in adorn herself. I say in
complacence to he, because she always expr the great contempt
for dress, and for those lady who make it their study.
but if such be her expectation, how be she disappoint when Mrs
Wilkins, accord to the order she have receive from her master,
produce the little infant? great surprize, as hath be observe,
be apt to be silent; and so be Miss Bridget, till her brother begin,
and tell she the whole story, which, as the reader know it already,
we shall not repeat.
Miss Bridget have always exprest so great a regard for what the lady
be pleased to call virtue, and have herself maintain such a severity
of character, that it be expect, especially by Wilkins, that she
would have vent much bitterness on this occasion, and would have
vote for send the child, as a kind of noxious animal, immediately
out of the house; but, on the contrary, she rather take the
good-natured side of the question, intimate some compassion for the
helpless little creature, and commend her brother's charity in what
he have do.
perhaps the reader may account for this behaviour from her
condescension to Mr Allworthy, when we have inform he that the good
man have end his narrative with own a resolution to take care of
the child, and to breed he up as his own; for, to acknowledge the
truth, she be always ready to oblige her brother, and very seldom, if
ever, contradict his sentiment. she would, indeed, sometimes make a
few observation, as that man be headstrong, and must have their own
way, and would wish she have be blest with an independent fortune;
but these be always vent in a low voice, and at the most amount
only to what be call mutter.
however, what she withhold from the infant, she bestow with the
utmost profuseness on the poor unknown mother, whom she call an
impudent slut, a wanton hussy, an audacious harlot, a wicked jade, a
vile strumpet, with every other appellation with which the tongue of
virtue never fail to lash those who bring a disgrace on the sex.
a consultation be now enter into how to proceed in order to
discover the mother. a scrutiny be first make into the character of
the female servant of the house, who be all acquit by Mrs
Wilkins, and with apparent merit; for she have collect they herself,
and perhaps it would be difficult to find such another set of
scarecrow.
the next step be to examine among the inhabitant of the parish; and
this be refer to Mrs Wilkins, who be to enquire with all
imaginable diligence, and to make her report in the afternoon.
matter be thus settle, Mr Allworthy withdraw to his study, as be
his custom, and leave the child to his sister, who, at his desire, have
undertake the care of it.
Chapter v.
contain a few common matter, with a very uncommon observation upon
they.
when her master be depart, Mrs Deborah stand silent, expect she
cue from Miss Bridget; for as to what have past before her master, the
prudent housekeeper by no mean rely upon it, as she have often know
the sentiment of the lady in her brother's absence to differ greatly
from those which she have express in his presence. Miss Bridget do
not, however, suffer she to continue long in this doubtful situation;
for have look some time earnestly at the child, as it lie asleep
in the lap of Mrs Deborah, the good lady could not forbear give it a
hearty kiss, at the same time declare herself wonderfully please
with its beauty and innocence. Mrs Deborah no soon observe this
than she fall to squeeze and kissing, with as great rapture as
sometimes inspire the sage dame of forty and five towards a youthful
and vigorous bridegroom, cry out, in a shrill voice, "o, the dear
little creature!--the dear, sweet, pretty creature! well, I vow it be
as fine a boy as ever be see!"
these exclamation continue till they be interrupt by the lady,
who now proceed to execute the commission give she by her brother,
and give order for provide all necessary for the child,
appoint a very good room in the house for his nursery. her order
be indeed so liberal, that, have it be a child of her own, she
could not have exceed they; but, lest the virtuous reader may
condemn she for show too great regard to a base-bear infant, to
which all charity be condemn by law as irreligious, we think proper
to observe that she conclude the whole with say, "since it be her
brother's whim to adopt the little brat, she suppose little master
must be treat with great tenderness. for her part, she could not
help think it be an encouragement to vice; but that she know too
much of the obstinacy of mankind to oppose any of their ridiculous
humour."
with reflection of this nature she usually, as have be hint,
accompany every act of compliance with her brother's inclination;
and surely nothing could more contribute to heighten the merit of this
compliance than a declaration that she know, at the same time, the
folly and unreasonableness of those inclination to which she
submit. tacit obedience imply no force upon the will, and
consequently may be easily, and without any pain, preserve; but when
a wife, a child, a relation, or a friend, perform what we desire,
with grumbling and reluctance, with expression of dislike and
dissatisfaction, the manifest difficulty which they undergo must
greatly enhance the obligation.
as this be one of those deep observation which very few reader can
be suppose capable of make themselves, I have think proper to
lend they my assistance; but this be a favour rarely to be expect in
the course of my work. indeed, I shall seldom or never so indulge he,
unless in such instance as this, where nothing but the inspiration
with which we writer be gift, can possibly enable any one to make
the discovery.
Chapter vi.
Mrs Deborah be introduce into the parish with a simile. a short
account of Jenny Jones, with the difficulty and discouragement
which may attend young woman in the pursuit of learn.
Mrs Deborah, have dispose of the child accord to the will of she
master, now prepare to visit those habitation which be suppose to
conceal its mother.
not otherwise than when a kite, tremendous bird, be beheld by the
feather generation soar aloft, and hover over their head, the
amorous dove, and every innocent little bird, spread wide the alarm,
and fly tremble to their hiding-place. he proudly beat the air,
conscious of his dignity, and meditate intend mischief.
so when the approach of Mrs Deborah be proclaim through the street,
all the inhabitant run tremble into their house, each matron
dread lest the visit should fall to her lot. she with stately step
proudly advance over the field: aloft she bear her tower head,
fill with conceit of her own pre-eminence, and scheme to effect her
intend discovery.
the sagacious reader will not from this simile imagine these poor
people have any apprehension of the design with which Mrs Wilkins be
now come towards they; but as the great beauty of the simile may
possibly sleep these hundred year, till some future commentator shall
take this work in hand, I think proper to lend the reader a little
assistance in this place.
it be my intention, therefore, to signify, that, as it be the nature
of a kite to devour little bird, so be it the nature of such person
as Mrs Wilkins to insult and tyrannize over little people. this be
indeed the mean which they use to recompense to themselves their
extreme servility and condescension to their superior; for nothing
can be more reasonable, than that slave and flatterer should exact
the same taxis on all below they, which they themselves pay to all
above they.
whenever Mrs Deborah have occasion to exert any extraordinary
condescension to Mrs Bridget, and by that mean have a little soured
her natural disposition, it be usual with she to walk forth among
these people, in order to refine her temper, by venting, and, as it
be, purge off all ill humour; on which account she be by no
mean a welcome visitant: to say the truth, she be universally
dread and hat by they all.
on her arrival in this place, she go immediately to the habitation
of an elderly matron; to whom, as this matron have the good fortune to
resemble herself in the comeliness of her person, as well as in her
age, she have generally be more favourable than to any of the rest.
to this woman she impart what have happen, and the design upon
which she be come thither that morning. these two begin presently to
scrutinize the character of the several young girl who live in any
of those house, and at last fix their strong suspicion on one
Jenny Jones, who, they both agree, be the likeli person to have
commit this fact.
this Jenny Jones be no very comely girl, either in her face or
person; but nature have somewhat compensate the want of beauty with
what be generally more esteemed by those lady whose judgment be
arrive at year of perfect maturity, for she have give she a very
uncommon share of understanding. this gift Jenny have a good deal
improve by erudition. she have live several year a servant with a
schoolmaster, who, discover a great quickness of part in the girl,
and an extraordinary desire of learn--for every leisure hour she
be always find reading in the book of the scholar--have the
good-nature, or folly--just as the reader please to call it--to
instruct she so far, that she obtain a competent skill in the Latin
language, and be, perhaps, as good a scholar as most of the young man
of quality of the age. this advantage, however, like most other of an
extraordinary kind, be attend with some small inconvenience: for
as it be not to be wonder at, that a young woman so well
accomplish should have little relish for the society of those whom
fortune have make she equal, but whom education have render so much
her inferior; so be it matter of no great astonishment, that this
superiority in Jenny, together with that behaviour which be its
certain consequence, should produce among the rest some little envy
and ill-will towards she; and these have, perhaps, secretly burn in
the bosom of her neighbour ever since her return from her service.
their envy do not, however, display itself openly, till poor Jenny,
to the surprize of everybody, and to the vexation of all the young
woman in these part, have publickly shine forth on a Sunday in a new
silk gown, with a laced cap, and other proper appendage to these.
the flame, which have before lain in embryo, now burst forth. Jenny
have, by her learning, increase her own pride, which none of she
neighbour be kind enough to feed with the honour she seem to
demand; and now, instead of respect and adoration, she gain nothing
but hatred and abuse by her finery. the whole parish declare she
could not come honestly by such thing; and parent, instead of
wish their daughter the same, felicitate themselves that their
child have they not.
hence, perhaps, it be, that the good woman first mention the name
of this poor girl to Mrs Wilkins; but there be another circumstance
that confirm the latter in her suspicion; for Jenny have lately be
often at Mr Allworthy's house. she have officiate as nurse to Miss
Bridget, in a violent fit of illness, and have sit up many night with
that lady; besides which, she have be see there the very day before
Mr Allworthy's return, by Mrs Wilkins herself, though that sagacious
person have not at first conceive any suspicion of she on that
account: for, as she herself say, "she have always esteem Jenny as a
very sober girl (though indeed she know very little of she), and have
rather suspect some of those wanton trollop, who give themselves
air, because, forsooth, they think themselves handsome."
Jenny be now summon to appear in person before Mrs Deborah, which
she immediately do. when Mrs Deborah, put on the gravity of a
judge, with somewhat more than his austerity, begin an oration with
the word, "you audacious strumpet!" in which she proceed rather to
pass sentence on the prisoner than to accuse she.
Though Mrs Deborah be fully satisfied of the guilt of Jenny, from the
reason above shewn, it be possible Mr Allworthy might have require
some strong evidence to have convict she; but she save she
accuser any such trouble, by freely confess the whole fact with
which she be charge.
this confession, though deliver rather in term of contrition, as it
appear, do not at all mollify Mrs Deborah, who now pronounce a
second judgment against she, in more opprobrious language than before;
nor have it any well success with the bystander, who be now grow
very numerous. many of they cry out, "they think what madam's silk
gown would end in;" other speak sarcastically of her learning. not a
single female be present but find some mean of express she
abhorrence of poor Jenny, who bear all very patiently, except the
malice of one woman, who reflect upon her person, and toss up her
nose, say, "the man must have a good stomach who would give silk
gown for such sort of trumpery!" Jenny reply to this with a
bitterness which might have surprize a judicious person, who have
observe the tranquillity with which she bear all the affront to her
chastity; but her patience be perhaps tired out, for this be a virtue
which be very apt to be fatigue by exercise.
Mrs Deborah have succeed beyond her hope in her inquiry, return
with much triumph, and, at the appoint hour, make a faithful report
to Mr Allworthy, who be much surprize at the relation; for he have
hear of the extraordinary part and improvement of this girl, whom
he intend to have give in marriage, together with a small living,
to a neighbouring curate. his concern, therefore, on this occasion,
be at least equal to the satisfaction which appear in Mrs Deborah,
and to many reader may seem much more reasonable.
Miss Bridget bless herself, and say, "for her part, she should
never hereafter entertain a good opinion of any woman." for Jenny
before this have the happiness of be much in her good grace also.
the prudent housekeeper be again dispatch to bring the unhappy
culprit before Mr Allworthy, in order, not as it be hop by some,
and expect by all, to be send to the house of correction, but to
receive wholesome admonition and reproof; which those who relish that
kind of instructive writing may peruse in the next chapter.
Chapter vii.
contain such grave matter, that the reader cannot laugh once
through the whole chapter, unless peradventure he should laugh at the
author.
when Jenny appear, Mr Allworthy take she into his study, and speak
to she as follow: "you know, child, it be in my power as a
magistrate, to punish you very rigorously for what you have do; and
you will, perhaps, be the more apt to fear I should execute that
power, because you have in a manner lay your sin at my door.
"but, perhaps, this be one reason which hath determine I to act in a
milder manner with you: for, as no private resentment should ever
influence a magistrate, I will be so far from consider your have
deposit the infant in my house as an aggravation of your offence,
that I will suppose, in your favour, this to have proceed from a
natural affection to your child, since you might have some hope to
see it thus well provide for than be in the power of yourself, or
its wicked father, to provide for it. I should indeed have be highly
offend with you have you expose the little wretch in the manner of
some inhuman mother, who seem no less to have abandon their
humanity, than to have part with their chastity. it be the other
part of your offence, therefore, upon which I intend to admonish you,
I mean the violation of your chastity;--a crime, however lightly it
may be treat by debauched person, very heinous in itself, and very
dreadful in its consequence.
"the heinous nature of this offence must be sufficiently apparent to
every Christian, inasmuch as it be commit in defiance of the law
of our religion, and of the express command of he who found that
religion.
"and here its consequence may well be argue to be dreadful; for what
can be more so, than to incur the divine displeasure, by the breach of
the divine command; and that in an instance against which the high
vengeance be specifically denounce?
"but these thing, though too little, I be afraid, regard, be so
plain, that mankind, however they may want to be remind, can never
need information on this head. a hint, therefore, to awaken your sense
of this matter, shall suffice; for I would inspire you with
repentance, and not drive you to desperation.
"there be other consequence, not indeed so dreadful or replete with
horror as this; and yet such, as, if attentively consider, must, one
would think, deter all of your sex at least from the commission of
this crime.
"for by it you be render infamous, and drive, like leper of old,
out of society; at least, from the society of all but wicked and
reprobate person; for no other will associate with you.
"if you have fortune, you be hereby render incapable of enjoy
they; if you have none, you be disabled from acquire any, nay
almost of procure your sustenance; for no person of character will
receive you into their house. thus you be often drive by necessity
itself into a state of shame and misery, which unavoidably end in the
destruction of both body and soul.
"can any pleasure compensate these evil? can any temptation have
sophistry and delusion strong enough to persuade you to so simple a
bargain? or can any carnal appetite so overpower your reason, or so
totally lie it asleep, as to prevent your fly with affright and
terror from a crime which carry such punishment always with it?
"how base and mean must that woman be, how void of that dignity of
mind, and decent pride, without which we be not worthy the name of
human creature, who can bear to level herself with the low animal,
and to sacrifice all that be great and noble in she, all her heavenly
part, to an appetite which she hath in common with the vile branch
of the creation! for no woman, sure, will plead the passion of love
for an excuse. this would be to own herself the mere tool and bubble
of the man. love, however barbarously we may corrupt and pervert its
meaning, as it be a laudable, be a rational passion, and can never be
violent but when reciprocal; for though the scripture bid we love our
enemy, it mean not with that fervent love which we naturally bear
towards our friend; much less that we should sacrifice to they our
life, and what ought to be dearer to we, our innocence. now in what
light, but that of an enemy, can a reasonable woman regard the man who
solicit she to entail on herself all the misery I have describe to
you, and who would purchase to himself a short, trivial, contemptible
pleasure, so greatly at her expense! for, by the law of custom, the
whole shame, with all its dreadful consequence, fall intirely upon
she. can love, which always seek the good of its object, attempt to
betray a woman into a bargain where she be so greatly to be the loser?
if such corrupter, therefore, should have the impudence to pretend a
real affection for she, ought not the woman to regard he not only as
an enemy, but as the bad of all enemy, a false, designing,
treacherous, pretended friend, who intend not only to debauch her
body, but her understanding at the same time?"
here Jenny express great concern, Allworthy pause a moment, and
then proceed: "I have talk thus to you, child, not to insult you
for what be past and irrevocable, but to caution and strengthen you
for the future. nor should I have take this trouble, but from some
opinion of your good sense, notwithstanding the dreadful slip you have
make; and from some hope of your hearty repentance, which be found
on the openness and sincerity of your confession. if these do not
deceive I, I will take care to convey you from this scene of your
shame, where you shall, by be unknown, avoid the punishment which,
as I have say, be allot to your crime in this world; and I hope,
by repentance, you will avoid the much heavy sentence denounce
against it in the other. be a good girl the rest of your day, and
want shall be no motive to your going astray; and, believe I, there
be more pleasure, even in this world, in an innocent and virtuous
life, than in one debauched and vicious.
"as to your child, let no thought concern it mol you; I will
provide for it in a well manner than you can ever hope. and now
nothing remain but that you inform I who be the wicked man that
seduce you; for my anger against he will be much great than you
have experience on this occasion."
Jenny now lift her eye from the ground, and with a modest look and
decent voice thus began:--
"to know you, sir, and not love your goodness, would be an argument of
total want of sense or goodness in any one. in I it would amount to
the high ingratitude, not to feel, in the most sensible manner, the
great degree of goodness you have be pleased to exert on this
occasion. as to my concern for what be past, I know you will spare my
blush the repetition. my future conduct will much well declare my
sentiment than any profession I can now make. I beg leave to assure
you, sir, that I take your advice much kind than your generous offer
with which you conclude it; for, as you be pleased to say, sir, it
be an instance of your opinion of my understanding."--Here her tear
flow apace, she stop a few moment, and then proceed
thus:--"indeed, sir, your kindness overcome I; but I will endeavour
to deserve this good opinion: for if I have the understanding you be
so kindly pleased to allow I, such advice cannot be throw away upon
I. I thank you, sir, heartily, for your intend kindness to my poor
helpless child: he be innocent, and I hope will live to be grateful
for all the favour you shall show he. but now, sir, I must on my
knee entreat you not to persist in ask I to declare the father of
my infant. I promise you faithfully you shall one day know; but I be
under the most solemn tie and engagement of honour, as well as the
most religious vow and protestation, to conceal his name at this
time. and I know you too well, to think you would desire I should
sacrifice either my honour or my religion."
Mr Allworthy, whom the least mention of those sacred word be
sufficient to stagger, hesitate a moment before he reply, and then
tell she, she have do wrong to enter into such engagement to a
villain; but since she have, he could not insist on she break they.
he say, it be not from a motive of vain curiosity he have inquire,
but in order to punish the fellow; at least, that he might not
ignorantly confer favour on the undeserving.
as to these point, Jenny satisfy he by the most solemn assurance,
that the man be entirely out of his reach; and be neither subject to
his power, nor in any probability of become an object of his
goodness.
the ingenuity of this behaviour have gain Jenny so much credit with
this worthy man, that he easily believe what she tell he; for as she
have disdain to excuse herself by a lie, and have hazard his further
displeasure in her present situation, rather than she would forfeit
her honour or integrity by betray another, he have but little
apprehension that she would be guilty of falsehood towards himself.
he therefore dismiss she with assurance that he would very soon
remove she out of the reach of that obloquy she have incur;
conclude with some additional document, in which he recommend
repentance, say, "consider, child, there be one still to reconcile
yourself to, whose favour be of much great importance to you than
mine."
Chapter viii.
a dialogue between Mesdames Bridget and Deborah; contain more
amusement, but less instruction, than the former.
when Mr Allworthy have retire to his study with Jenny Jones, as hath
be see, Mrs Bridget, with the good housekeeper, have betake
themselves to a post next adjoining to the say study; whence, through
the conveyance of a keyhole, they suck in at their ear the
instructive lecture deliver by Mr Allworthy, together with the
answer of Jenny, and indeed every other particular which pass in
the last chapter.
this hole in her brother's study-door be indeed as well know to Mrs
Bridget, and have be as frequently apply to by she, as the famous
hole in the wall be by Thisbe of old. this serve to many good
purpose. for by such mean Mrs Bridget become often acquainted with
her brother's inclination, without give he the trouble of
repeat they to she. it be true, some inconvenience attend this
intercourse, and she have sometimes reason to cry out with Thisbe, in
Shakspeare, "o, wicked, wicked wall!" for as Mr Allworthy be a
justice of peace, certain thing occur in examination concern
bastard, and such like, which be apt to give great offence to the
chaste ear of virgin, especially when they approach the age of
forty, as be the case of Mrs Bridget. however, she have, on such
occasion, the advantage of conceal her blush from the eye of
man; and _De non apparentibus, et non existentibus eadem est
ratio_--in English, "when a woman be not see to blush, she doth not
blush at all."
both the good woman keep strict silence during the whole scene between
Mr Allworthy and the girl; but as soon as it be end, and that
gentleman be out of hearing, Mrs Deborah could not help exclaim
against the clemency of her master, and especially against his
suffer she to conceal the father of the child, which she swear she
would have out of she before the sun set.
at these word Mrs Bridget discompose her feature with a smile (a
thing very unusual to she). not that I would have my reader imagine,
that this be one of those wanton smile which Homer would have you
conceive come from Venus, when he call she the laughter-love
goddess; nor be it one of those smile which Lady Seraphina shoot
from the stage-box, and which Venus would quit her immortality to be
able to equal. no, this be rather one of those smile which might be
suppose to have come from the dimpled cheek of the august tisiphone,
or from one of the miss, her sister.
with such a smile then, and with a voice sweet as the evening breeze
of Boreas in the pleasant month of November, Mrs Bridget gently
reprove the curiosity of Mrs Deborah; a vice with which it seem the
latter be too much taint, and which the former inveigh against
with great bitterness, add, "that, among all her fault, she
thank Heaven her enemy could not accuse she of pry into the
affair of other people."
she then proceed to commend the honour and spirit with which Jenny
have act. she say, she could not help agree with her brother,
that there be some merit in the sincerity of her confession, and in
her integrity to her lover: that she have always think she a very
good girl, and doubt not but she have be seduce by some rascal,
who have be infinitely more to blame than herself, and very probably
have prevail with she by a promise of marriage, or some other
treacherous proceeding.
this behaviour of Mrs Bridget greatly surprise Mrs Deborah; for this
well-breed woman seldom open her lip, either to her master or his
sister, till she have first sound their inclination, with which her
sentiment be always consonant. here, however, she think she might
have launch forth with safety; and the sagacious reader will not
perhaps accuse she of want of sufficient forecast in so do, but
will rather admire with what wonderful celerity she tack about, when
she find herself steer a wrong course.
"Nay, madam," say this able woman, and truly great politician, "I
must own I cannot help admire the girl's spirit, as well as your
ladyship. and, as your ladyship say, if she be deceive by some
wicked man, the poor wretch be to be pity. and to be sure, as your
ladyship say, the girl hath always appear like a good, honest,
plain girl, and not vain of her face, forsooth, as some wanton hussey
in the neighbourhood be."
"you say true, Deborah," say Miss Bridget. "if the girl have be one
of those vain trollop, of which we have too many in the parish, I
should have condemn my brother for his lenity towards she. I see two
farmer' daughter at church, the other day, with bare neck. I
protest they shock I. if wench will hang out lur for fellow,
it be no matter what they suffer. I detest such creature; and it
would be much well for they that their face have be seam with
the smallpox; but I must confess, I never see any of this wanton
behaviour in poor Jenny: some artful villain, I be convince, hath
betray, nay perhaps force she; and I pity the poor wretch with all
my heart."
Mrs Deborah approve all these sentiment, and the dialogue conclude
with a general and bitter invective against beauty, and with many
compassionate consideration for all honest plain girl who be
delude by the wicked art of deceitful man.
Chapter ix.
contain matter which will surprize the reader.
Jenny return home well pleased with the reception she have meet with
from Mr Allworthy, whose indulgence to she she industriously make
public; partly perhaps as a sacrifice to her own pride, and partly
from the more prudent motive of reconcile her neighbour to she, and
silence their clamour.
but though this latter view, if she indeed have it, may appear
reasonable enough, yet the event do not answer her expectation; for
when she be convene before the justice, and it be universally
apprehend that the house of correction would have be her fate,
though some of the young woman cry out "it be good enough for she,"
and divert themselves with the thought of her beat hemp in a
silk gown; yet there be many other who begin to pity her condition:
but when it be know in what manner Mr Allworthy have behave, the
tide turn against she. one say, "Iwill assure you, madam hath have
good luck." a second cryed, "see what it be to be a favourite!" a
third, "ay, this come of her learning." every person make some
malicious comment or other on the occasion, and reflect on the
partiality of the justice.
the behaviour of these people may appear impolitic and ungrateful to
the reader, who consider the power and benevolence of Mr Allworthy.
but as to his power, he never use it; and as to his benevolence, he
exert so much, that he have thereby disoblige all his neighbour;
for it be a secret well known to great man, that, by confer an
obligation, they do not always procure a friend, but be certain of
create many enemy.
Jenny be, however, by the care and goodness of Mr Allworthy, soon
remove out of the reach of reproach; when malice be no long able
to vent its rage on she, begin to seek another object of its
bitterness, and this be no less than Mr Allworthy, himself; for a
whisper soon go abroad, that he himself be the father of the
foundling child.
this supposition so well reconcile his conduct to the general
opinion, that it meet with universal assent; and the outcry against his
lenity soon begin to take another turn, and be change into an
invective against his cruelty to the poor girl. very grave and good
woman exclaim against man who begot child, and then disown
they. nor be there want some, who, after the departure of Jenny,
insinuate that she be spirit away with a design too black to be
mention, and who give frequent hint that a legal inquiry ought to
be make into the whole matter, and that some people should be force
to produce the girl.
these calumny might have probably produce ill consequence, at the
least might have occasion some trouble, to a person of a more
doubtful and suspicious character than Mr Allworthy be bless with;
but in his case they have no such effect; and, be heartily despise
by he, they serve only to afford an innocent amusement to the good
gossip of the neighbourhood.
but as we cannot possibly divine what complection our reader may be
of, and as it will be some time before he will hear any more of Jenny,
we think proper to give he a very early intimation, that Mr Allworthy
be, and will hereafter appear to be, absolutely innocent of any
criminal intention whatever. he have indeed commit no other than an
error in politic, by temper justice with mercy, and by refuse to
gratify the good-natured disposition of the mob,[*] with an object for
their compassion to work on in the person of poor Jenny, whom, in
order to pity, they desire to have see sacrifice to ruin and
infamy, by a shameful correction in Bridewell.
[*]whenever this word occur in our writing, it intend person
without virtue or sense, in all station; and many of the high
rank be often mean by it.
so far from comply with this their inclination, by which all hope
of reformation would have be abolish, and even the gate shut
against she if her own inclination should ever hereafter lead she to
chuse the road of virtue, Mr Allworthy rather choose to encourage the
girl to return thither by the only possible mean; for too true I be
afraid it be, that many woman have become abandon, and have sink to
the last degree of vice, by be unable to retrieve the first slip.
this will be, I be afraid, always the case while they remain among
their former acquaintance; it be therefore wisely do by Mr
Allworthy, to remove Jenny to a place where she might enjoy the
pleasure of reputation, after have taste the ill consequence of
lose it.
to this place therefore, wherever it be, we will wish she a good
journey, and for the present take leave of she, and of the little
foundle her child, have matter of much high importance to
communicate to the reader.
Chapter x.
the hospitality of Allworthy; with a short sketch of the character of
two brother, a doctor and a captain, who be entertain by that
gentleman.
neither Mr Allworthy's house, nor his heart, be shut against any
part of mankind, but they be both more particularly open to man of
merit. to say the truth, this be the only house in the kingdom where
you be sure to gain a dinner by deserve it.
above all other, man of genius and learning share the principal
place in his favour; and in these he have much discernment: for though
he have miss the advantage of a learn education, yet, be blest
with vast natural ability, he have so well profit by a vigorous
though late application to letter, and by much conversation with man
of eminence in this way, that he be himself a very competent judge in
most kind of literature.
it be no wonder that in an age when this kind of merit be so little in
fashion, and so slenderly provide for, person possess of it should
very eagerly flock to a place where they be sure of be receive
with great complaisance; indeed, where they might enjoy almost the
same advantage of a liberal fortune as if they be entitle to it in
their own right; for Mr Allworthy be not one of those generous
person who be ready most bountifully to bestow meat, drink, and
lodge on man of wit and learning, for which they expect no other
return but entertainment, instruction, flattery, and subserviency; in
a word, that such person should be enrol in the number of
domestic, without wear their master's cloathe, or receive
wage.
on the contrary, every person in this house be perfect master of his
own time: and as he might at his pleasure satisfy all his appetite
within the restriction only of law, virtue, and religion; so he
might, if his health require, or his inclination prompt he to
temperance, or even to abstinence, absent himself from any meal, or
retire from they, whenever he be so disposed, without even a
sollicitation to the contrary: for, indeed, such sollicitation from
superior always savour very strongly of command. but all here be
free from such impertinence, not only those whose company be in all
other place esteem a favour from their equality of fortune, but
even those whose indigent circumstance make such an eleemosynary
abode convenient to they, and who be therefore less welcome to a
great man's table because they stand in need of it.
among other of this kind be Dr Blifil, a gentleman who have the
misfortune of lose the advantage of great talent by the obstinacy
of a father, who would breed he to a profession he dislike. in
obedience to this obstinacy the doctor have in his youth be oblige
to study physic, or rather to say he study it; for in reality book
of this kind be almost the only one with which he be unacquainted;
and unfortunately for he, the doctor be master of almost every other
science but that by which he be to get his bread; the consequence of
which be, that the doctor at the age of forty have no bread to eat.
such a person as this be certain to find a welcome at Mr Allworthy's
table, to whom misfortune be ever a recommendation, when they be
derive from the folly or villany of other, and not of the
unfortunate person himself. besides this negative merit, the doctor
have one positive recommendation;--this be a great appearance of
religion. whether his religion be real, or consist only in
appearance, I shall not presume to say, as I be not possess of any
touchstone which can distinguish the true from the false.
if this part of his character please Mr Allworthy, it delight Miss
Bridget. she engage he in many religious controversy; on which
occasion she constantly express great satisfaction in the doctor's
knowledge, and not much less in the compliment which he frequently
bestow on her own. to say the truth, she have read much english
divinity, and have puzzle more than one of the neighbouring curate.
indeed, her conversation be so pure, her look so sage, and her whole
deportment so grave and solemn, that she seem to deserve the name of
saint equally with her namesake, or with any other female in the roman
kalendar.
as sympathy of all kind be apt to beget love, so experience
teach we that none have a more direct tendency this way than those
of a religious kind between person of different sex. the doctor
find himself so agreeable to Miss Bridget, that he now begin to
lament an unfortunate accident which have happen to he about ten
year before; namely, his marriage with another woman, who be not
only still alive, but, what be bad, know to be so by Mr Allworthy.
this be a fatal bar to that happiness which he otherwise see
sufficient probability of obtain with this young lady; for as to
criminal indulgence, he certainly never think of they. this be
owe either to his religion, as be most probable, or to the purity of
his passion, which be fix on those thing which matrimony only, and
not criminal correspondence, could put he in possession of, or could
give he any title to.
he have not long ruminate on these matter, before it occur to his
memory that he have a brother who be under no such unhappy incapacity.
this brother he make no doubt would succeed; for he discern, as he
think, an inclination to marriage in the lady; and the reader
perhaps, when he hear the brother's qualification, will not blame
the confidence which he entertain of his success.
this gentleman be about thirty-five year of age. he be of a middle
size, and what be call well-build. he have a scar on his forehead,
which do not so much injure his beauty as it denote his valour (for
he be a half-pay officer). he have good tooth, and something affable,
when he please, in his smile; though naturally his countenance, as
well as his air and voice, have much of roughness in it: yet he could
at any time deposit this, and appear all gentleness and good-humour.
he be not ungenteel, nor entirely devoid of wit, and in his youth have
abound in sprightliness, which, though he have lately put on a more
serious character, he could, when he please, resume.
he have, as well as the doctor, an academic education; for his father
have, with the same paternal authority we have mention before,
decreed he for holy order; but as the old gentleman die before he
be ordain, he choose the church military, and prefer the king's
commission to the bishop's.
he have purchase the post of lieutenant of dragoon, and afterwards
come to be a captain; but have quarrel with his colonel, be by
his interest oblige to sell; from which time he have entirely
rusticate himself, have betake himself to study the scripture,
and be not a little suspect of an inclination to methodism.
it seem, therefore, not unlikely that such a person should succeed
with a lady of so saint-like a disposition, and whose inclination
be no otherwise engage than to the marriage state in general; but
why the doctor, who certainly have no great friendship for his brother,
should for his sake think of make so ill a return to the hospitality
of Allworthy, be a matter not so easy to be account for.
be it that some nature delight in evil, as other be think to
delight in virtue? or be there a pleasure in be accessory to a
theft when we cannot commit it ourselves? or lastly (which experience
seem to make probable), have we a satisfaction in aggrandize our
family, even though we have not the least love or respect for they?
whether any of these motif operate on the doctor, we will not
determine; but so the fact be. he send for his brother, and easily
find mean to introduce he at Allworthy's as a person who intend
only a short visit to himself.
the captain have not be in the house a week before the doctor have
reason to felicitate himself on his discernment. the captain be
indeed as great a master of the art of love as Ovid be formerly. he
have besides receive proper hint from his brother, which he fail
not to improve to the good advantage.
Chapter xi.
contain many rule, and some example, concern fall in love:
description of beauty, and other more prudential inducement to
matrimony.
it hath be observe, by wise man or woman, I forget which, that all
person be doom to be in love once in their life. no particular
season be, as I remember, assign for this; but the age at which Miss
Bridget be arrive, seem to I as proper a period as any to be fix
on for this purpose: it often, indeed, happen much early; but when
it doth not, I have observe it seldom or never fail about this time.
moreover, we may remark that at this season love be of a more serious
and steady nature than what sometimes show itself in the young
part of life. the love of girl be uncertain, capricious, and so
foolish that we cannot always discover what the young lady would be
at; nay, it may almost be doubt whether she always know this
herself.
now we be never at a loss to discern this in woman about forty; for
as such grave, serious, and experienced lady well know their own
meaning, so it be always very easy for a man of the least sagacity to
discover it with the utmost certainty.
Miss Bridget be an example of all these observation. she have not be
many time in the captain's company before she be seize with this
passion. nor do she go pin and mop about the house, like a
puny, foolish girl, ignorant of her distemper: she feel, she know, and
she enjoy, the pleasing sensation, of which, as she be certain it
be not only innocent but laudable, she be neither afraid nor
ashamed.
and to say the truth, there be, in all point, great difference
between the reasonable passion which woman at this age conceive
towards man, and the idle and childish liking of a girl to a boy,
which be often fix on the outside only, and on thing of little
value and no duration; as on cherry-cheek, small, lily-white hand,
sloe-black eye, flow lock, downy chin, dapper shape; nay,
sometimes on charm more worthless than these, and less the party's
own; such be the outward ornament of the person, for which man be
beholden to the taylor, the laceman, the periwig-maker, the hatter,
and the milliner, and not to nature. such a passion girl may well be
ashamed, as they generally be, to own either to themselves or other.
the love of Miss Bridget be of another kind. the captain owe nothing
to any of these fop-maker in his dress, nor be his person much more
beholden to nature. both his dress and person be such as, have they
appear in an assembly or a drawing-room, would have be the
contempt and ridicule of all the fine lady there. the former of
these be indeed neat, but plain, coarse, ill-fancied, and out of
fashion. as for the latter, we have expressly describe it above. so
far be the skin on his cheek from be cherry-colour, that you
could not discern what the natural colour of his cheek be, they
be totally overgrown by a black beard, which ascend to his eye.
his shape and limb be indeed exactly proportion, but so large
that they denote the strength rather of a ploughman than any other.
his shoulder be broad beyond all size, and the calf of his leg
large than those of a common chairman. in short, his whole person
want all that elegance and beauty which be the very reverse of
clumsy strength, and which so agreeably set off most of our fine
gentleman; be partly owe to the high blood of their ancestor,
viz., blood make of rich sauce and generous wine, and partly to an
early town education.
though Miss Bridget be a woman of the great delicacy of taste, yet
such be the charm of the captain's conversation, that she totally
overlook the defect of his person. she imagine, and perhaps very
wisely, that she should enjoy more agreeable minute with the captain
than with a much pretty fellow; and forewent the consideration of
please her eye, in order to procure herself much more solid
satisfaction.
the captain no soon perceive the passion of Miss Bridget, in which
discovery he be very quick-sighted, than he faithfully return it.
the lady, no more than her lover, be remarkable for beauty. I would
attempt to draw her picture, but that be do already by a more able
master, Mr Hogarth himself, to whom she sit many year ago, and hath
be lately exhibit by that gentleman in his print of a winter's
morning, of which she be no improper emblem, and may be see walk
(for walk she doth in the print) to Covent Garden church, with a
starve foot-boy behind carry her prayer-book.
the captain likewise very wisely preferred the more solid enjoyment
he expect with this lady, to the fleeting charm of person. he be
one of those wise man who regard beauty in the other sex as a very
worthless and superficial qualification; or, to speak more truly, who
rather chuse to possess every convenience of life with an ugly woman,
than a handsome one without any of those convenience. and have a
very good appetite, and but little nicety, he fancy he should play
his part very well at the matrimonial banquet, without the sauce of
beauty.
to deal plainly with the reader, the captain, ever since his arrival,
at least from the moment his brother have propose the match to he,
long before he have discover any flattering symptom in Miss Bridget,
have be greatly enamour; that be to say, of Mr Allworthy's house
and garden, and of his land, tenement, and hereditament; of all
which the captain be so passionately fond, that he would most
probably have contract marriage with they, have he be oblige to
have take the witch of Endor into the bargain.
as Mr Allworthy, therefore, have declare to the doctor that he never
intend to take a second wife, as his sister be his near
relation, and as the doctor have fish out that his intention be to
make any child of hers his heir, which indeed the law, without his
interposition, would have do for he; the doctor and his brother
think it an act of benevolence to give be to a human creature,
who would be so plentifully provide with the most essential mean of
happiness. the whole thought, therefore, of both the brother be
how to engage the affection of this amiable lady.
but fortune, who be a tender parent, and often doth more for her
favourite offspring than either they deserve or wish, have be so
industrious for the captain, that whilst he be lay scheme to
execute his purpose, the lady conceive the same desire with himself,
and be on her side contrive how to give the captain proper
encouragement, without appear too forward; for she be a strict
observer of all rule of decorum. in this, however, she easily
succeed; for as the captain be always on the look-out, no glance,
gesture, or word escape he.
the satisfaction which the captain receive from the kind behaviour of
Miss Bridget, be not a little abate by his apprehension of Mr
Allworthy; for, notwithstanding his disinterested profession, the
captain imagine he would, when he come to act, follow the example of
the rest of the world, and refuse his consent to a match so
disadvantageous, in point of interest, to his sister. from what oracle
he receive this opinion, I shall leave the reader to determine: but
however he come by it, it strangely perplex he how to regulate his
conduct so as at once to convey his affection to the lady, and to
conceal it from her brother. he at length resolve to take all private
opportunity of make his address; but in the presence of Mr
Allworthy to be as reserve and as much upon his guard as be
possible; and this conduct be highly approve by the brother.
he soon find mean to make his address, in express term, to his
mistress, from whom he receive an answer in the proper form, viz.:
the answer which be first make some thousand of year ago, and which
hath be hand down by tradition from mother to daughter ever since.
if I be to translate this into Latin, I should render it by these two
word, _Nolo Episcopari_: a phrase likewise of immemorial use on
another occasion.
the captain, however he come by his knowledge, perfectly well
understand the lady, and very soon after repeat his application with
more warmth and earnestness than before, and be again, accord to
due form, reject; but as he have increase in the eagerness of his
desire, so the lady, with the same propriety, decrease in the
violence of her refusal.
not to tire the reader, by lead he through every scene of this
courtship (which, though in the opinion of a certain great author, it
be the pleasant scene of life to the actor, be, perhaps, as dull
and tiresome as any whatever to the audience), the captain make his
advance in form, the citadel be defend in form, and at length, in
proper form, surrender at discretion.
during this whole time, which fill the space of near a month, the
captain preserve great distance of behaviour to his lady in the
presence of the brother; and the more he succeed with she in
private, the more reserved be he in public. and as for the lady, she
have no soon secure her lover than she behave to he before company
with the high degree of indifference; so that Mr Allworthy must
have have the insight of the devil (or perhaps some of his bad
quality) to have entertain the least suspicion of what be go
forward.
Chapter xii.
contain what the reader may, perhaps, expect to find in it.
in all bargain, whether to fight or to marry, or concern any other
such business, little previous ceremony be require to bring the
matter to an issue when both party be really in earnest. this be
the case at present, and in less than a month the captain and his lady
be man and wife.
the great concern now be to break the matter to Mr Allworthy; and
this be undertake by the doctor.
one day, then, as Allworthy be walk in his garden, the doctor come
to he, and, with great gravity of aspect, and all the concern which
he could possibly affect in his countenance, say, "I be come, sir, to
impart an affair to you of the utmost consequence; but how shall I
mention to you what it almost distract I to think of!" he then
launch forth into the most bitter invective both against man and
woman; accuse the former of have no attachment but to their
interest, and the latter of be so addicted to vicious inclination
that they could never be safely trust with one of the other sex.
"could I," say he, "sir, have suspect that a lady of such prudence,
such judgment, such learning, should indulge so indiscreet a passion!
or could I have imagine that my brother--why do I call he so? he be
no long a brother of mine----"
"indeed but he be," say Allworthy, "and a brother of mine too."
"bless I, sir!" say the doctor, "do you know the shocking affair?"
"Look'ee, Mr Blifil," answer the good man, "it hath be my constant
maxim in life to make the good of all matter which happen. my sister,
though many year young than I, be at least old enough to be at the
age of discretion. have he impose on a child, I should have be more
averse to have forgive he; but a woman upwards of thirty must
certainly be suppose to know what will make she most happy. she hath
marry a gentleman, though perhaps not quite she equal in fortune;
and if he hath any perfection in her eye which can make up that
deficiency, I see no reason why I should object to her choice of her
own happiness; which I, no more than herself, imagine to consist only
in immense wealth. I might, perhaps, from the many declaration I have
