make of comply with almost any proposal, have expect to have be
consult on this occasion; but these matter be of a very delicate
nature, and the scruple of modesty, perhaps, be not to be overcome.
as to your brother, I have really no anger against he at all. he hath
no obligation to I, nor do I think he be under any necessity of
ask my consent, since the woman be, as I have say, _sui juris_,
and of a proper age to be entirely answerable only, to herself for her
conduct."
the doctor accuse Mr Allworthy of too great lenity, repeat his
accusation against his brother, and declare that he should never
more be bring either to see, or to own he for his relation. he then
launch forth into a panegyric on Allworthy's goodness; into the
high encomium on his friendship; and conclude by say, he
should never forgive his brother for have put the place which he
bear in that friendship to a hazard.
Allworthy thus answer: "have I conceive any displeasure against your
brother, I should never have carry that resentment to the innocent:
but I assure you I have no such displeasure. your brother appear to
I to be a man of sense and honour. I do not disapprove the taste of
my sister; nor will I doubt but that she be equally the object of his
inclination. I have always think love the only foundation of
happiness in a married state, as it can only produce that high and
tender friendship which should always be the cement of this union;
and, in my opinion, all those marriage which be contract from
other motif be greatly criminal; they be a profanation of a most
holy ceremony, and generally end in disquiet and misery: for surely we
may call it a profanation to convert this most sacred institution into
a wicked sacrifice to lust or avarice: and what well can be say of
those match to which man be induce merely by the consideration of
a beautiful person, or a great fortune?
"to deny that beauty be an agreeable object to the eye, and even
worthy some admiration, would be false and foolish. beautiful be an
epithet often use in Scripture, and always mention with honour. it
be my own fortune to marry a woman whom the world think handsome,
and I can truly say I like she the well on that account. but to
make this the sole consideration of marriage, to lust after it so
violently as to overlook all imperfection for its sake, or to require
it so absolutely as to reject and disdain religion, virtue, and sense,
which be quality in their nature of much high perfection, only
because an elegance of person be want: this be surely inconsistent,
either with a wise man or a good Christian. and it be, perhaps, be
too charitable to conclude that such person mean anything more by
their marriage than to please their carnal appetite; for the
satisfaction of which, we be teach, it be not ordain.
"in the next place, with respect to fortune. worldly prudence,
perhaps, exact some consideration on this head; nor will I absolutely
and altogether condemn it. as the world be constitute, the demand of
a married state, and the care of posterity, require some little regard
to what we call circumstance. yet this provision be greatly
increase, beyond what be really necessary, by folly and vanity, which
create abundantly more want than nature. equipage for the wife, and
large fortune for the child, be by custom enrol in the list of
necessary; and to procure these, everything truly solid and sweet,
and virtuous and religious, be neglect and overlook.
"and this in many degree; the last and great of which seem scarce
distinguishable from madness;--I mean where person of immense
fortune contract themselves to those who be, and must be,
disagreeable to they--to fool and knave--in order to increase an
estate already large even than the demand of their pleasure. surely
such person, if they will not be think mad, must own, either that
they be incapable of taste the sweet of the tender friendship,
or that they sacrifice the great happiness of which they be
capable to the vain, uncertain, and senseless law of vulgar opinion,
which owe as well their force as their foundation to folly."
here Allworthy conclude his sermon, to which Blifil have listen with
the profound attention, though it cost he some pain to prevent
now and then a small discomposure of his muscle. he now praise every
period of what he have hear with the warmth of a young divine, who
hath the honour to dine with a bishop the same day in which his
lordship hath mount the pulpit.
Chapter xiii.
which conclude the first book; with an instance of ingratitude,
which, we hope, will appear unnatural.
the reader, from what hath be say, may imagine that the
reconciliation (if indeed it could be so call) be only matter of
form; we shall therefore pass it over, and hasten to what must surely
be think matter of substance.
the doctor have acquaint his brother with what have past between Mr
Allworthy and he; and add with a smile, "I promise you I pay you
off; nay, I absolutely desire the good gentleman not to forgive you:
for you know after he have make a declaration in your favour, I might
with safety venture on such a request with a person of his temper; and
I be willing, as well for your sake as for my own, to prevent the
least possibility of a suspicion."
Captain Blifil take not the least notice of this, at that time; but he
afterwards make a very notable use of it.
one of the maxim which the devil, in a late visit upon earth, leave to
his disciple, be, when once you be get up, to kick the stool from
under you. in plain English, when you have make your fortune by the
good office of a friend, you be advise to discard he as soon as
you can.
whether the captain act by this maxim, I will not positively
determine: so far we may confidently say, that his action may be
fairly derive from this diabolical principle; and indeed it be
difficult to assign any other motive to they: for no soon be he
possess of Miss Bridget, and reconcile to Allworthy, than he begin
to show a coldness to his brother which increase daily; till at
length it grow into rudeness, and become very visible to every one.
the doctor remonstrate to he privately concern this behaviour,
but could obtain no other satisfaction than the follow plain
declaration: "if you dislike anything in my brother's house, sir, you
know you be at liberty to quit it." this strange, cruel, and almost
unaccountable ingratitude in the captain, absolutely break the poor
doctor's heart; for ingratitude never so thoroughly pierce the human
breast as when it proceed from those in whose behalf we have be
guilty of transgression. reflection on great and good action,
however they be receive or return by those in whose favour they
be perform, always administer some comfort to we; but what
consolation shall we receive under so bite a calamity as the
ungrateful behaviour of our friend, when our wound conscience at the
same time fly in our face, and upbraid we with have spot it in
the service of one so worthless!
Mr Allworthy himself speak to the captain in his brother's behalf, and
desire to know what offence the doctor have commit; when the
hard-hearted villain have the baseness to say that he should never
forgive he for the injury which he have endeavour to do he in his
favour; which, he say, he have pump out of he, and be such a
cruelty that it ought not to be forgive.
Allworthy speak in very high term upon this declaration, which, he
say, become not a human creature. he express, indeed, so much
resentment against an unforgive temper, that the captain at last
pretend to be convince by his argument, and outwardly profess to
be reconcile.
as for the bride, she be now in her honeymoon, and so passionately
fond of her new husband that he never appear to she to be in the
wrong; and his displeasure against any person be a sufficient reason
for her dislike to the same.
the captain, at Mr Allworthy's instance, be outwardly, as we have
say, reconcile to his brother; yet the same rancour remain in his
heart; and he find so many opportunity of give he private hint
of this, that the house at last grow insupportable to the poor doctor;
and he choose rather to submit to any inconvenience which he might
encounter in the world, than long to bear these cruel and ungrateful
insult from a brother for whom he have do so much.
he once intend to acquaint Allworthy with the whole; but he could
not bring himself to submit to the confession, by which he must take
to his share so great a portion of guilt. besides, by how much the
bad man he represent his brother to be, so much the great would
his own offence appear to Allworthy, and so much the great, he have
reason to imagine, would be his resentment.
he feign, therefore, some excuse of business for his departure, and
promise to return soon again; and take leave of his brother with so
well-dissemble content, that, as the captain play his part to the
same perfection, Allworthy remain well satisfied with the truth of
the reconciliation.
the doctor go directly to London, where he die soon after of a
broken heart; a distemper which kill many more than be generally
imagine, and would have a fair title to a place in the bill of
mortality, do it not differ in one instance from all other
disease--viz., that no physician can cure it.
now, upon the most diligent enquiry into the former life of these two
brother, I find, besides the cursed and hellish maxim of policy above
mention, another reason for the captain's conduct: the captain,
besides what we have before say of he, be a man of great pride and
fierceness, and have always treat his brother, who be of a different
complexion, and greatly deficient in both these quality, with the
utmost air of superiority. the doctor, however, have much the large
share of learning, and be by many repute to have the well
understanding. this the captain know, and could not bear; for though
envy be at good a very malignant passion, yet be its bitterness
greatly heighten by mix with contempt towards the same object;
and very much afraid I be, that whenever an obligation be join to
these two, indignation and not gratitude will be the product of all
three.
BOOK II.
contain SCENES of matrimonial felicity in different degrees of
life; and various other transaction during the first two year after
the marriage between CAPTAIN BLIFIL and MISS BRIDGET ALLWORTHY.
Chapter i.
show what kind of a history this be; what it be like, and what it
be not like.
though we have properly enough entitle this our work, a history, and
not a life; nor an apology for a life, as be more in fashion; yet we
intend in it rather to pursue the method of those writer, who profess
to disclose the revolution of country, than to imitate the painful
and voluminous historian, who, to preserve the regularity of his
series, think himself oblige to fill up as much paper with the
detail of month and year in which nothing remarkable happen, as he
employ upon those notable aera when the great scene have be
transact on the human stage.
such history as these do, in reality, very much resemble a
newspaper, which consist of just the same number of word, whether
there be any news in it or not. they may likewise be compare to a
stage coach, which perform constantly the same course, empty as well
as full. the writer, indeed, seem to think himself oblige to keep
even pace with time, whose amanuensis he be; and, like his master,
travel as slowly through century of monkish dulness, when the world
seem to have be asleep, as through that bright and busy age so
nobly distinguish by the excellent Latin poet--
_ad confligendum venientibus undique poeni,
Omnia cum belli trepido concussa tumultu
Horrida contremuere sub altis aetheris auris;
in dubioque fuit sub utrorum regna cadendum
Omnibus humani esset, terraque marique._
of which we wish we could give our reader a more adequate translation
than that by Mr Creech--
when dreadful Carthage fright Rome with arm,
and all the world be shake with fierce alarm;
Whilst undecided yet, which part should fall,
which nation rise the glorious lord of all.
now it be our purpose, in the ensue page, to pursue a contrary
method. when any extraordinary scene present itself (as we trust will
often be the case), we shall spare no pain nor paper to open it at
large to our reader; but if whole year should pass without produce
anything worthy his notice, we shall not be afraid of a chasm in our
history; but shall hasten on to matter of consequence, and leave such
period of time totally unobserved.
these be indeed to be consider as blank in the grand lottery of
time. we therefore, who be the register of that lottery, shall
imitate those sagacious person who deal in that which be draw at
Guildhall, and who never trouble the public with the many blank they
dispose of; but when a great prize happen to be draw, the newspaper
be presently fill with it, and the world be sure to be inform at
whose office it be sell: indeed, commonly two or three different
office lie claim to the honour of have dispose of it; by which, I
suppose, the adventurer be give to understand that certain broker
be in the secret of Fortune, and indeed of her cabinet council.
my reader then be not to be surprize, if, in the course of this work,
he shall find some chapter very short, and other altogether as long;
some that contain only the time of a single day, and other that
comprise year; in a word, if my history sometimes seem to stand
still, and sometimes to fly. for all which I shall not look on myself
as accountable to any court of critical jurisdiction whatever: for as
I be, in reality, the founder of a new province of writing, so I be at
liberty to make what law I please therein. and these law, my
reader, whom I consider as my subject, be bind to believe in and
to obey; with which that they may readily and cheerfully comply, I do
hereby assure they that I shall principally regard their ease and
advantage in all such institution: for I do not, like a _jure divino_
tyrant, imagine that they be my slave, or my commodity. I be,
indeed, set over they for their own good only, and be create for
their use, and not they for mine. nor do I doubt, while I make their
interest the great rule of my writing, they will unanimously concur
in support my dignity, and in render I all the honour I shall
deserve or desire.
Chapter ii.
religious caution against show too much favour to bastard; and a
great discovery make by Mrs Deborah Wilkins.
eight month after the celebration of the nuptial between Captain
Blifil and Miss Bridget Allworthy, a young lady of great beauty,
merit, and fortune, be Miss Bridget, by reason of a fright, deliver
of a fine boy. the child be indeed to all appearance perfect; but
the midwife discover it be bear a month before its full time.
though the birth of an heir by his beloved sister be a circumstance
of great joy to Mr Allworthy, yet it do not alienate his affection
from the little foundling, to whom he have be godfather, have give
his own name of Thomas, and whom he have hitherto seldom fail of
visit, at least once a day, in his nursery.
he tell his sister, if she please, the new-bear infant should be breed
up together with little Tommy; to which she consent, though with
some little reluctance: for she have truly a great complacence for her
brother; and hence she have always behave towards the foundling with
rather more kindness than lady of rigid virtue can sometimes bring
themselves to show to these child, who, however innocent, may be
truly call the live monument of incontinence.
the captain could not so easily bring himself to bear what he
condemn as a fault in Mr Allworthy. he give he frequent hint, that
to adopt the fruit of sin, be to give countenance to it. he quote
several text (for he be well read in Scripture), such as, _he visit
the sin of the father upon the child; and the father have eat
sour grape, and the child's tooth be set on edge_,&c. whence he
argue the legality of punish the crime of the parent on the
bastard. he say, "though the law do not positively allow the
destroy such base-bear child, yet it hold they to be the
child of nobody; that the Church consider they as the child of
nobody; and that at the good, they ought to be bring up to the
low and vilest office of the commonwealth."
Mr Allworthy answer to all this, and much more, which the captain
have urge on this subject, "that, however guilty the parent might be,
the child be certainly innocent: that as to the text he have
quote, the former of they be a particular denunciation against the
Jews, for the sin of idolatry, of relinquish and hat their
heavenly king; and the latter be parabolically speak, and rather
intend to denote the certain and necessary consequence of sin, than
any express judgment against it. but to represent the Almighty as
avenge the sin of the guilty on the innocent, be indecent, if not
blasphemous, as it be to represent he act against the first
principle of natural justice, and against the original notion of
right and wrong, which he himself have implant in our mind; by which
we be to judge not only in all matter which be not reveal, but
even of the truth of revelation itself. he say he know many hold the
same principle with the captain on this head; but he be himself
firmly convinced to the contrary, and would provide in the same manner
for this poor infant, as if a legitimate child have have fortune to have
be find in the same place."
while the captain be take all opportunity to press these and such
like argument, to remove the little foundling from Mr Allworthy's, of
whose fondness for he he begin to be jealous, Mrs Deborah have make a
discovery, which, in its event, threaten at least to prove more
fatal to poor Tommy than all the reasoning of the captain.
whether the insatiable curiosity of this good woman have carry she on
to that business, or whether she do it to confirm herself in the good
grace of Mrs Blifil, who, notwithstanding her outward behaviour to
the foundling, frequently abuse the infant in private, and her
brother too, for his fondness to it, I will not determine; but she have
now, as she conceive, fully detect the father of the foundling.
now, as this be a discovery of great consequence, it may be necessary
to trace it from the fountain-head. we shall therefore very minutely
lie open those previous matter by which it be produce; and for that
purpose we shall be oblige to reveal all the secret of a little
family with which my reader be at present entirely unacquainted; and
of which the oeconomy be so rare and extraordinary, that I fear it
will shock the utmost credulity of many married person.
Chapter iii.
the description of a domestic government found upon rule directly
contrary to those of Aristotle.
my reader may please to remember he hath be inform that Jenny
Jones have live some year with a certain schoolmaster, who have, at
her earnest desire, instruct she in Latin, in which, to do justice
to her genius, she have so improve herself, that she be become a
well scholar than her master.
indeed, though this poor man have undertake a profession to which
learning must be allow necessary, this be the least of his
commendation. he be one of the well-natured fellow in the world,
and be, at the same time, master of so much pleasantry and humour,
that he be repute the wit of the country; and all the neighbouring
gentleman be so desirous of his company, that as deny be not his
talent, he spend much time at their house, which he might, with more
emolument, have spend in his school.
it may be imagine that a gentleman so qualified and so disposed, be
in no danger of become formidable to the learn seminary of Eton
or Westminster. to speak plainly, his scholar be divide into two
class: in the upper of which be a young gentleman, the son of a
neighbouring squire, who, at the age of seventeen, be just enter
into his Syntaxis; and in the low be a second son of the same
gentleman, who, together with seven parish-boy, be learn to read
and write.
the stipend arise hence would hardly have indulge the schoolmaster
in the luxury of life, have he not add to this office those of
clerk and barber, and have not Mr Allworthy add to the whole an
annuity of ten pound, which the poor man receive every Christmas,
and with which he be enable to cheer his heart during that sacred
festival.
among his other treasure, the pedagogue have a wife, whom he have
marry out of Mr Allworthy's kitchen for her fortune, viz., twenty
pound, which she have there amass.
this woman be not very amiable in her person. whether she sit to my
friend Hogarth, or no, I will not determine; but she exactly resemble
the young woman who be pour out her mistress's tea in the third
picture of the Harlot's Progress. she be, besides, a prof follower
of that noble sect found by Xantippe of old; by mean of which she
become more formidable in the school than her husband; for, to confess
the truth, he be never master there, or anywhere else, in her
presence.
though her countenance do not denote much natural sweetness of
temper, yet this be, perhaps, somewhat sour by a circumstance which
generally poison matrimonial felicity; for child be rightly
call the pledge of love; and her husband, though they have be
marry nine year, have give she no such pledge; a default for which
he have no excuse, either from age or health, be not yet thirty
year old, and what they call a jolly brisk young man.
hence arise another evil, which produce no little uneasiness to the
poor pedagogue, of whom she maintain so constant a jealousy, that he
durst hardly speak to one woman in the parish; for the least degree of
civility, or even correspondence, with any female, be sure to bring
his wife upon she back, and his own.
in order to guard herself against matrimonial injury in her own
house, as she keep one maid-servant, she always take care to chuse she
out of that order of female whose face be take as a kind of
security for their virtue; of which number Jenny Jones, as the reader
hath be before inform, be one.
as the face of this young woman might be call pretty good security
of the before-mention kind, and as her behaviour have be always
extremely modest, which be the certain consequence of understanding in
woman; she have pass above four year at Mr Partridge's (for that be
the schoolmaster's name) without create the least suspicion in her
mistress. Nay, she have be treat with uncommon kindness, and her
mistress have permit Mr Partridge to give she those instruction
which have be before commemorate.
but it be with jealousy as with the gout: when such distemper be in
the blood, there be never any security against their break out; and
that often on the slight occasion, and when least suspect.
thus it happen to Mrs Partridge, who have submit four year to her
husband's teaching this young woman, and have suffer she often to
neglect her work, in order to pursue her learning. for, pass by one
day, as the girl be read, and her master lean over she, the
girl, I know not for what reason, suddenly start up from her chair:
and this be the first time that suspicion ever enter into the head
of her mistress. this do not, however, at that time discover itself,
but lie lurk in her mind, like a conceal enemy, who wait for a
reinforcement of additional strength before he openly declare himself
and proceed upon hostile operation: and such additional strength
soon arrive to corroborate her suspicion; for not long after, the
husband and wife be at dinner, the master say to his maid, _Da
mihi aliquid potum:_ upon which the poor girl smile, perhaps at the
badness of the Latin, and, when her mistress cast her eye on she,
blush, possibly with a consciousness of have laugh at her
master. Mrs Partridge, upon this, immediately fall into a fury, and
discharge the trencher on which she be eat, at the head of poor
Jenny, cry out, "you impudent whore, do you play trick with my
husband before my face?" and at the same instant rise from her chair
with a knife in her hand, with which, most probably, she would have
execute very tragical vengeance, have not the girl take the advantage
of be nearer the door than her mistress, and avoid her fury by
run away: for, as to the poor husband, whether surprize have
render he motionless, or fear (which be full as probable) have
restrain he from venture at any opposition, he sit star and
tremble in his chair; nor do he once offer to move or speak, till
his wife, return from the pursuit of Jenny, make some defensive
measure necessary for his own preservation; and he likewise be
oblige to retreat, after the example of the maid.
this good woman be, no more than Othello, of a disposition
to make a life of jealousy
and follow still the change of the moon
with fresh suspicions--
with she, as well as he,
--to be once in doubt,
be once to be resolvd--
she therefore order Jenny immediately to pack up her all and
begone, for that she be determined she should not sleep that night
within her wall.
Mr Partridge have profit too much by experience to interpose in a
matter of this nature. he therefore have recourse to his usual receipt
of patience, for, though he be not a great adept in Latin, he
remember, and well understand, the advice contain in these word
--_Leve fit quod bene fertur onus_
in English:
a burden become lightest when it be well borne--
which he have always in his mouth; and of which, to say the truth, he
have often occasion to experience the truth.
Jenny offer to make protestation of her innocence; but the temp
be too strong for she to be hear. she then betook herself to the
business of packing, for which a small quantity of brown paper
suffice, and, have receive her small pittance of wage, she
return home.
the schoolmaster and his consort pass their time unpleasantly enough
that evening, but something or other happen before the next morning,
which a little abate the fury of Mrs Partridge; and she at length
admit her husband to make his excuse: to which she give the
readier belief, as he have, instead of desire she to recall Jenny,
profess a satisfaction in she be dismiss, say, she be grow
of little use as a servant, spend all her time in reading, and be
become, moreover, very pert and obstinate; for, indeed, she and her
master have lately have frequent dispute in literature; in which, as
hath be say, she be become greatly his superior. this, however, he
would by no means allow; and as he call she persist in the right,
obstinacy, he begin to hate she with no small inveteracy.
chapter iv.
contain one of the most bloody battle, or rather duel, that be
ever record in domestic history.
for the reason mention in the precede chapter, and from some
other matrimonial concession, well know to most husband, and which,
like the secret of freemasonry, should be divulge to none who be
not member of that honourable fraternity, Mrs Partridge be pretty
well satisfied that she have condemn her husband without cause, and
endeavour by act of kindness to make he amend for her false
suspicion. her passion be indeed equally violent, whichever way
they incline; for as she could be extremely angry, so could she be
altogether as fond.
but though these passion ordinarily succeed each other, and scarce
twenty-four hour ever pass in which the pedagogue be not, in some
degree, the object of both; yet, on extraordinary occasion, when the
passion of anger have rag very high, the remission be usually
long: and so be the case at present; for she continue long in a
state of affability, after this fit of jealousy be end, than she
husband have ever know before: and, have it not be for some little
exercise, which all the follower of Xantippe be oblige to perform
daily, Mr Partridge would have enjoy a perfect serenity of several
month.
perfect calm at sea be always suspect by the experienced mariner
to be the forerunner of a storm, and I know some person, who,
without be generally the devotee of superstition, be apt to
apprehend that great and unusual peace or tranquillity will be
attend with its opposite. for which reason the antient use, on
such occasion, to sacrifice to the goddess Nemesis, a deity who be
think by they to look with an invidious eye on human felicity, and
to have a peculiar delight in overturn it.
as we be very far from believe in any such heathen goddess, or from
encourage any superstition, so we wish Mr John fr----, or some other
such philosopher, would bestir himself a little, in order to find out
the real cause of this sudden transition from good to bad fortune,
which hath be so often remark, and of which we shall proceed to
give an instance; for it be our province to relate fact, and we shall
leave cause to person of much high genius.
Mankind have always take great delight in know and descant on
the action of other. hence there have be, in all age and nation,
certain place set apart for public rendezvous, where the curious
might meet and satisfy their mutual curiosity. among these, the
barber' shop have justly bear the pre-eminence. among the Greeks,
barber' news be a proverbial expression; and Horace, in one of his
epistle, make honourable mention of the roman barber in the same
light.
those of England be know to be no wise inferior to their greek or
roman predecessor. you there see foreign affair discuss in a
manner little inferior to that with which they be handle in the
coffee-house; and domestic occurrence be much more largely and
freely treat in the former than in the latter. but this serve only
for the man. now, whereas the female of this country, especially
those of the low order, do associate themselves much more than those
of other nation, our polity would be highly deficient, if they have
not some place set apart likewise for the indulgence of their
curiosity, see they be in this no way inferior to the other half
of the specie.
in enjoy, therefore, such place of rendezvous, the british fair
ought to esteem themselves more happy than any of their foreign
sister; as I do not remember either to have read in history, or to
have see in my travel, anything of the like kind.
this place then be no other than the chandler's shop, the know seat
of all the news; or, as it be vulgarly call, gossip, in every
parish in England.
Mrs Partridge be one day at this assembly of female, be ask by
one of her neighbour, if she have hear no news lately of Jenny Jones?
to which she answer in the negative. upon this the other reply,
with a smile, that the parish be very much oblige to she for have
turn Jenny away as she do.
Mrs Partridge, whose jealousy, as the reader well know, be long
since cure, and who have no other quarrel to her maid, answer
boldly, she do not know any obligation the parish have to she on that
account; for she believe Jenny have scarce leave she equal behind she.
"no, truly," say the gossip, "I hope not, though I fancy we have
slut enow too. then you have not hear, it seem, that she hath be
bring to bed of two bastard? but as they be not bear here, my
husband and the other overseer say we shall not be oblige to keep
they."
"two bastard!" answer Mrs Partridge hastily: "you surprize I! I
donot know whether we must keep they; but I be sure they must have
be beget here, for the wench hath not be nine month go
away."
nothing can be so quick and sudden as the operation of the mind,
especially when hope, or fear, or jealousy, to which the two other
be but journeymen, set it to work. it occur instantly to she, that
Jenny have scarce ever be out of her own house while she live with
she. the lean over the chair, the sudden start up, the Latin,
the smile, and many other thing, rush upon she all at once. the
satisfaction her husband express in the departure of Jenny, appear
now to be only dissemble; again, in the same instant, to be real; but
yet to confirm her jealousy, proceed from satiety, and a hundred
other bad cause. in a word, she be convince of her husband's guilt,
and immediately leave the assembly in confusion.
as fair Grimalkin, who, though the young of the feline family,
degenerate not in ferocity from the eld branch of her house, and
though inferior in strength, be equal in fierceness to the noble tiger
himself, when a little mouse, whom it hath long torment in sport,
escape from her clutch for a while, fret, scold, growl, swear;
but if the trunk, or box, behind which the mouse lie hide be again
remove, she fly like lightning on her prey, and, with envenomed
wrath, bite, scratch, mumble, and tear the little animal.
not with less fury do Mrs Partridge fly on the poor pedagogue. her
tongue, tooth, and hand, fall all upon he at once. his wig be in an
instant tear from his head, his shirt from his back, and from his face
descend five stream of blood, denote the number of claw with
which nature have unhappily arm the enemy.
Mr Partridge act for some time on the defensive only; indeed he
attempt only to guard his face with his hand; but as he find that
his antagonist abate nothing of her rage, he think he might, at
least, endeavour to disarm she, or rather to confine her arm; in
do which her cap fall off in the struggle, and her hair be too
short to reach her shoulder, erect itself on her head; her stay
likewise, which be lace through one single hole at the bottom,
burst open; and her breast, which be much more redundant than her
hair, hang down below her middle; her face be likewise mark with
the blood of her husband: her tooth gnash with rage; and fire, such
as sparkle from a smith's forge, dart from her eye. so that,
altogether, this amazonian heroine might have be an object of terror
to a much bold man than Mr Partridge.
he have, at length, the good fortune, by get possession of her
arm, to render those weapon which she wear at the end of her
finger useless; which she no soon perceive, than the softness of
her sex prevail over her rage, and she presently dissolve in tear,
which soon after conclude in a fit.
that small share of sense which Mr Partridge have hitherto preserve
through this scene of fury, of the cause of which he be hitherto
ignorant, now utterly abandon he. he run instantly into the street,
hallow out that his wife be in the agony of death, and
beseech the neighbour to fly with the utmost haste to her
assistance. several good woman obey his summon, who enter his
house, and apply the usual remedy on such occasion, Mrs
Partridge be at length, to the great joy of her husband, bring to
herself.
as soon as she have a little recollect her spirit, and somewhat
compose herself with a cordial, she begin to inform the company of
the manifold injury she have receive from her husband; who, she
say, be not content to injure she in her bed; but, upon she
upbraid he with it, have treat she in the cruel manner
imaginable; have tear her cap and hair from her head, and her stay
from her body, give she, at the same time, several blow, the mark
of which she should carry to the grave.
the poor man, who bear on his face many more visible mark of the
indignation of his wife, stand in silent astonishment at this
accusation; which the reader will, I believe, bear witness for he,
have greatly exceed the truth; for indeed he have not strike she once;
and this silence be interpret to be a confession of the charge by
the whole court, they all begin at once, _una voce_, to rebuke and
revile he, repeat often, that none but a coward ever strike a
woman.
Mr Partridge bear all this patiently; but when his wife appeal to
the blood on her face, as an evidence of his barbarity, he could not
help lay claim to his own blood, for so it really be; as he
think it very unnatural, that this should rise up (as we be teach
that of a murder person often doth) in vengeance against he.
to this the woman make no other answer, than that it be a pity it have
not come from his heart, instead of his face; all declare, that, if
their husband should lift their hand against they, they would have
their heart' blood out of their body.
after much admonition for what be past, and much good advice to Mr
Partridge for his future behaviour, the company at length depart,
and leave the husband and wife to a personal conference together, in
which Mr Partridge soon learn the cause of all his suffering.
Chapter v.
contain much matter to exercise the judgment and reflection of the
reader.
I believe it be a true observation, that few secret be divulge to
one person only; but certainly, it would be next to a miracle that a
fact of this kind should be know to a whole parish, and not transpire
any far.
and, indeed, a very few day have past, before the country, to use a
common phrase, rung of the schoolmaster of Little Baddington; who be
say to have beat his wife in the most cruel manner. Nay, in some
place it be report he have murder she; in other, that he have
break her arm; in other, her leg: in short, there be scarce an
injury which can be do to a human creature, but what Mrs Partridge
be somewhere or other affirm to have receive from her husband.
the cause of this quarrel be likewise variously report; for as some
people say that Mrs Partridge have catch her husband in bed with his
maid, so many other reason, of a very different kind, go abroad.
Nay, some transfer the guilt to the wife, and the jealousy to the
husband.
Mrs Wilkins have long ago hear of this quarrel; but, as a different
cause from the true one have reach her ear, she think proper to
conceal it; and the rather, perhaps, as the blame be universally lay
on Mr Partridge; and his wife, when she be servant to Mr Allworthy,
have in something offend Mrs Wilkins, who be not of a very forgiving
temper.
but Mrs Wilkins, whose eye could see object at a distance, and who
could very well look forward a few year into futurity, have perceive
a strong likelihood of Captain Blifil's be hereafter her master;
and as she plainly discern that the captain bear no great goodwill
to the little foundling, she fancy it would be render he an
agreeable service, if she could make any discovery that might lessen
the affection which Mr Allworthy seem to have contract for this
child, and which give visible uneasiness to the captain, who could not
entirely conceal it even before Allworthy himself; though his wife,
who act her part much well in public, frequently recommend to
he her own example, of connive at the folly of her brother, which,
she say, she at least as well perceive, and as much resent, as any
other possibly could.
Mrs Wilkins have therefore, by accident, get a true scent of the
above story,--though long after it have happen, fail not to satisfy
herself thoroughly of all the particular; and then acquaint the
captain, that she have at last discover the true father of the little
bastard, which she be sorry, she say, to see her master lose his
reputation in the country, by take so much notice of.
the captain chide she for the conclusion of her speech, as an improper
assurance in judging of her master's action: for if his honour, or
his understanding, would have suffer the captain to make an alliance
with Mrs Wilkins, his pride would by no mean have admit it. and to
say the truth, there be no conduct less politic, than to enter into
any confederacy with your friend's servant against their master: for
by these mean you afterwards become the slave of these very servant;
by whom you be constantly liable to be betray. and this
consideration, perhaps it be, which prevent Captain Blifil from
be more explicit with Mrs Wilkins, or from encourage the abuse
which she have bestow on Allworthy.
but though he declare no satisfaction to Mrs Wilkins at this
discovery, he enjoy not a little from it in his own mind, and
resolve to make the good use of it he be able.
he keep this matter a long time conceal within his own breast, in
hope that Mr Allworthy might hear it from some other person; but Mrs
Wilkins, whether she resent the captain's behaviour, or whether his
cunning be beyond she, and she fear the discovery might displease
he, never afterwards open her lip about the matter.
I have think it somewhat strange, upon reflection, that the
housekeeper never acquaint Mrs Blifil with this news, as woman be
more inclined to communicate all piece of intelligence to their own
sex, than to ours. the only way, as it appear to I, of solve this
difficulty, be, by impute it to that distance which be now grow
between the lady and the housekeeper: whether this arise from a
jealousy in Mrs Blifil, that Wilkins show too great a respect to the
foundling; for while she be endeavour to ruin the little infant,
in order to ingratiate herself with the captain, she be every day
more and more commend it before Allworthy, as his fondness for it
every day increase. this, notwithstanding all the care she take at
other time to express the direct contrary to Mrs Blifil, perhaps
offend that delicate lady, who certainly now hat Mrs Wilkins; and
though she do not, or possibly could not, absolutely remove she from
her place, she find, however, the mean of make her life very
uneasy. this Mrs Wilkins, at length, so resent, that she very openly
show all manner of respect and fondness to little Tommy, in
opposition to Mrs Blifil.
the captain, therefore, find the story in danger of perish, at
last take an opportunity to reveal it himself.
he be one day engage with Mr Allworthy in a discourse on charity: in
which the captain, with great learning, prove to Mr Allworthy, that
the word charity in Scripture nowhere mean beneficence or generosity.
"the christian religion," he say, "be institute for much nobler
purpose, than to enforce a lesson which many heathen philosopher have
teach we long before, and which, though it might perhaps be call a
moral virtue, savour but little of that sublime, Christian-like
disposition, that vast elevation of thought, in purity approach to
angelic perfection, to be attain, express, and feel only by grace.
those," he say, "come near to the scripture meaning, who understand
by it candour, or the forming of a benevolent opinion of our brother,
and pass a favourable judgment on their action; a virtue much
high, and more extensive in its nature, than a pitiful distribution
of alm, which, though we would never so much prejudice, or even ruin
our family, could never reach many; whereas charity, in the other
and truer sense, might be extend to all mankind."
he say, "consider who the disciple be, it would be absurd to
conceive the doctrine of generosity, or give alm, to have be
preach to they. and, as we could not well imagine this doctrine
should be preach by its Divine Author to man who could not practise
it, much less should we think it understand so by those who can
practise it, and do not.
"but though," continue he, "there be, I be afraid, little merit in
these benefaction, there would, I must confess, be much pleasure in
they to a good mind, if it be not abate by one consideration. I
mean, that we be liable to be impose upon, and to confer our
choicest favour often on the undeserving, as you must own be your
case in your bounty to that worthless fellow Partridge: for two or
three such example must greatly lessen the inward satisfaction which
a good man would otherwise find in generosity; nay, may even make he
timorous in bestowing, lest he should be guilty of support vice,
and encourage the wicked; a crime of a very black dye, and for which
it will by no mean be a sufficient excuse, that we have not actually
intend such an encouragement; unless we have use the utmost caution
in chuse the object of our beneficence. a consideration which, I
make no doubt, hath greatly check the liberality of many a worthy
and pious man."
Mr Allworthy answer, "he could not dispute with the captain in the
greek language, and therefore could say nothing as to the true sense
of the word which be translate charity; but that he have always
think it be interpret to consist in action, and that give alm
constitute at least one branch of that virtue.
"as to the meritorious part," he say, "he readily agree with the
captain; for where could be the merit of barely discharge a duty?
which," he say, "let the word charity have what construction it
would, it sufficiently appear to be from the whole tenor of the New
Testament. and as he think it an indispensable duty, enjoin both
by the christian law, and by the law of nature itself; so be it
withal so pleasant, that if any duty could be say to be its own
reward, or to pay we while we be discharge it, it be this.
"to confess the truth," say he, "there be one degree of generosity
(of charity I would have call it), which seem to have some show of
merit, and that be, where, from a principle of benevolence and
christian love, we bestow on another what we really want ourselves;
where, in order to lessen the distress of another, we condescend to
share some part of they, by give what even our own necessity
cannot well spare. this be, I think, meritorious; but to relieve our
brother only with our superfluity; to be charitable (I must use the
word) rather at the expense of our coffer than ourselves; to save
several family from misery rather than hang up an extraordinary
picture in our house or gratify any other idle ridiculous
vanity--this seem to be only be human creature. Nay, I will
venture to go far, it be be in some degree epicure: for what
could the great epicure wish rather than to eat with many mouth
instead of one? which I think may be predicate of any one who know
that the bread of many be owe to his own largess.
"as to the apprehension of bestow bounty on such as may hereafter
prove unworthy object, because many have prove such; surely it can
never deter a good man from generosity. I do not think a few or many
example of ingratitude can justify a man's harden his heart
against the distress of his fellow-creature; nor do I believe it
can ever have such effect on a truly benevolent mind. nothing less
than a persuasion of universal depravity can lock up the charity of a
good man; and this persuasion must lead he, I think, either into
atheism, or enthusiasm; but surely it be unfair to argue such
universal depravity from a few vicious individual; nor be this, I
believe, ever do by a man, who, upon search his own mind, find
one certain exception to the general rule." he then conclude by
ask, "who that Partridge be, whom he have call a worthless
fellow?"
"I mean," say the captain, "partridge the barber, the schoolmaster,
what do you call he? Partridge, the father of the little child which
you find in your bed."
Mr Allworthy expr great surprize at this account, and the captain
as great at his ignorance of it; for he say he have know it above a
month: and at length recollect with much difficulty that he be tell
it by Mrs Wilkins.
upon this, Wilkins be immediately summon; who have confirm what
the captain have say, be by Mr Allworthy, by and with the captain's
advice, dispatch to Little Baddington, to inform herself of the
truth of the fact: for the captain expr great dislike at all hasty
proceeding in criminal matter, and say he would by no mean have Mr
Allworthy take any resolution either to the prejudice of the child or
its father, before he be satisfied that the latter be guilty; for
though he have privately satisfy himself of this from one of
Partridge's neighbour, yet he be too generous to give any such
evidence to Mr Allworthy.
Chapter vi.
the trial of Partridge, the schoolmaster, for incontinency; the
evidence of his wife; a short reflection on the wisdom of our law;
with other grave matter, which those will like good who understand
they most.
it may be wonder that a story so well know, and which have furnish
so much matter of conversation, should never have be mention to Mr
Allworthy himself, who be perhaps the only person in that country who
have never hear of it.
to account in some measure for this to the reader, I think proper to
inform he, that there be no one in the kingdom less interested in
oppose that doctrine concern the meaning of the word charity,
which hath be see in the precede chapter, than our good man.
indeed, he be equally intitle to this virtue in either sense; for as
no man be ever more sensible of the want, or more ready to relieve
the distress of other, so none could be more tender of their
character, or slow to believe anything to their disadvantage.
scandal, therefore, never find any access to his table; for as it
hath be long since observe that you may know a man by his
companion, so I will venture to say, that, by attend to the
conversation at a great man's table, you may satisfy yourself of his
religion, his politic, his taste, and indeed of his entire
disposition: for though a few odd fellow will utter their own
sentiment in all place, yet much the great part of mankind have
enough of the courtier to accommodate their conversation to the taste
and inclination of their superior.
but to return to Mrs Wilkins, who, have execute her commission with
great dispatch, though at fifteen mile distance, bring back such a
confirmation of the schoolmaster's guilt, that Mr Allworthy determine
to send for the criminal, and examine he _viva voce_. Mr Partridge,
therefore, be summon to attend, in order to his defence (if he
could make any) against this accusation.
at the time appoint, before Mr Allworthy himself, at Paradise-hall,
come as well the say Partridge, with Anne, his wife, as Mrs Wilkins
his accuser.
and now Mr Allworthy be seat in the chair of justice, Mr
Partridge be bring before he. have hear his accusation from the
mouth of Mrs Wilkins, he plead not guilty, make many vehement
protestation of his innocence.
Mrs Partridge be then examine, who, after a modest apology for be
oblige to speak the truth against her husband, relate all the
circumstance with which the reader hath already be acquaint; and
at last conclude with her husband's confession of his guilt.
whether she have forgive he or no, I will not venture to determine;
but it be certain she be an unwilling witness in this cause; and it
be probable from certain other reason, would never have be bring
to depose as she do, have not Mrs Wilkins, with great art, fish all
out of she at her own house, and have she not indeed make promise, in
Mr Allworthy's name, that the punishment of her husband should not be
such as might anywise affect his family.
Partridge still persist in assert his innocence, though he
admit he have make the above-mention confession; which he however
endeavour to account for, by protest that he be force into it
by the continue importunity she use: who vow, that, as she be
sure of his guilt, she would never leave torment he till he have
own it; and faithfully promise, that, in such case, she would never
mention it to he more. hence, he say, he have be induce falsely to
confess himself guilty, though he be innocent; and that he believe
he should have confest a murder from the same motive.
Mrs Partridge could not bear this imputation with patience; and have
no other remedy in the present place but tear, she call forth a
plentiful assistance from they, and then address herself to Mr
Allworthy, she say (or rather cry), "may it please your worship,
there never be any poor woman so injure as I be by that base man;
for this be not the only instance of his falsehood to I. no, may it
please your worship, he hath injure my bed manybe the good time and
often. I could have put up with his drunkenness and neglect of his
business, if he have not break one of the sacred commandment. besides,
if it have be out of door I have not matter it so much; but with my
own servant, in my own house, under my own roof, to defile my own
chaste bed, which to be sure he hath, with his beastly stink
whore. yes, you villain, you have defile my own bed, you have; and
then you have charge I with bullocke you into own the truth. it
be very likely, an't please your worship, that I should bullock he? I
have mark enow about my body to show of his cruelty to I. if you have
be a man, you villain, you would have scorn to injure a woman in
that manner. but you an't half a man, you know it. nor have you be
half a husband to I. you need run after whore, you need, when Ibe
sure--and since he provoke I, I be ready, an't please your worship,
to take my bodily oath that I find they a-bed together. what, you
have forget, I suppose, when you beat I into a fit, and make the
blood run down my forehead, because I only civilly tax you with
adultery! but I can prove it by all my neighbour. you have almost
break my heart, you have, you have."
here Mr Allworthy interrupt, and beg she to be pacify,
promise she that she should have justice; then turn to Partridge,
who stand aghast, one half of his wit be hurry away by surprize
and the other half by fear, he say he be sorry to see there be so
wicke a man in the world. he assure he that his prevaricating and
lie backward and forward be a great aggravation of his guilt; for
which the only atonement he could make be by confession and
repentance. he exhort he, therefore, to begin by immediately
confess the fact, and not to persist in deny what be so plainly
prove against he even by his own wife.
here, reader, I beg your patience a moment, while I make a just
compliment to the great wisdom and sagacity of our law, which refuse
to admit the evidence of a wife for or against her husband. this, say
a certain learn author, who, I believe, be never quote before in
any but a law-book, would be the mean of create an eternal
dissension between they. it would, indeed, be the mean of much
perjury, and of much whipping, fin, imprisoning, transporting, and
hang.
Partridge stand a while silent, till, be bid to speak, he say he
have already speak the truth, and appeal to Heaven for his
innocence, and lastly to the girl herself, whom he desire his worship
immediately to send for; for he be ignorant, or at least pretend to
be so, that she have leave that part of the country.
Mr Allworthy, whose natural love of justice, join to his coolness of
temper, make he always a most patient magistrate in hear all the
witness which an accuse person could produce in his defence, agree
to defer his final determination of this matter till the arrival of
Jenny, for whom he immediately dispatch a messenger; and then have
recommend peace between Partridge and his wife (though he address
himself chiefly to the wrong person), he appoint they to attend
again the third day; for he have send Jenny a whole day's journey from
his own house.
at the appoint time the party all assemble, when the messenger
return bring word, that Jenny be not to be find; for that she
have leave her habitation a few day before, in company with a
recruiting officer.
Mr Allworthy then declare that the evidence of such a slut as she
appear to be would have deserve no credit; but he say he could not
help think that, have she be present, and would have declare the
truth, she must have confirm what so many circumstance, together
with his own confession, and the declaration of his wife that she have
catch her husband in the fact, do sufficiently prove. he therefore
once more exhort Partridge to confess; but he still avow his
innocence, Mr Allworthy declare himself satisfied of his guilt, and
that he be too bad a man to receive any encouragement from he. he
therefore deprive he of his annuity, and recommend repentance to
he on account of another world, and industry to maintain himself and
his wife in this.
there be not, perhaps, many more unhappy person than poor
Partridge. he have lose the good part of his income by the evidence of
his wife, and yet be daily upbraided by she for have, among other
thing, be the occasion of deprive she of that benefit; but such
be his fortune, and he be oblige to submit to it.
though I call he poor Partridge in the last paragraph, I would have
the reader rather impute that epithet to the compassion in my temper
than conceive it to be any declaration of his innocence. whether he
be innocent or not will perhaps appear hereafter; but if the historic
muse hath entrust I with any secret, I will by no mean be guilty
of discover they till she shall give I leave.
here therefore the reader must suspend his curiosity. certain it be
that, whatever be the truth of the case, there be evidence more than
sufficient to convict he before Allworthy; indeed, much less would
have satisfy a bench of justice on an order of bastardy; and yet,
notwithstanding the positiveness of Mrs Partridge, who would have
take the sacrament upon the matter, there be a possibility that the
schoolmaster be entirely innocent: for though it appear clear on
compare the time when Jenny depart from Little Baddington with
that of her delivery that she have there conceive this infant, yet it
by no mean follow of necessity that Partridge must have be its
father; for, to omit other particular, there be in the same house a
lad near eighteen, between whom and Jenny there have subsist
sufficient intimacy to found a reasonable suspicion; and yet, so blind
be jealousy, this circumstance never once enter into the head of the
enraged wife.
whether Partridge repent or not, accord to Mr Allworthy's advice,
be not so apparent. certain it be that his wife repent heartily of
the evidence she have give against he: especially when she find Mrs
Deborah have deceive she, and refuse to make any application to Mr
allworthy on her behalf. she have, however, somewhat well success
with Mrs Blifil, who be, as the reader must have perceive, a much
well-temper woman, and very kindly undertake to solicit her
brother to restore the annuity; in which, though good-nature might
have some share, yet a strong and more natural motive will appear in
the next chapter.
these solicitation be nevertheless unsuccessful: for though Mr
Allworthy do not think, with some late writer, that mercy consist
only in punish offender; yet he be as far from think that it
be proper to this excellent quality to pardon great criminal
wantonly, without any reason whatever. any doubtfulness of the fact,
or any circumstance of mitigation, be never disregard: but the
petition of an offender, or the intercession of other, do not in
the least affect he. in a word, he never pardon because the
offender himself, or his friend, be unwilling that he should be
punish.
Partridge and his wife be therefore both oblige to submit to their
fate; which be indeed severe enough: for so far be he from double
his industry on the account of his lessen income, that he do in a
manner abandon himself to despair; and as he be by nature indolent,
that vice now increase upon he, by which mean he lose the little
school he have; so that neither his wife nor himself would have have any
bread to eat, have not the charity of some good Christian interpose,
and provide they with what be just sufficient for their sustenance.
as this support be convey to they by an unknown hand, they
imagine, and so, I doubt not, will the reader, that Mr Allworthy
himself be their secret benefactor; who, though he would not openly
encourage vice, could yet privately relieve the distress of the
vicious themselves, when these become too exquisite and
disproportionate to their demerit. in which light their wretchedness
appear now to Fortune herself; for she at length take pity on this
miserable couple, and considerably lessen the wretched state of
Partridge, by put a final end to that of his wife, who soon after
catch the small-pox, and die.
the justice which Mr Allworthy have execute on Partridge at first meet
with universal approbation; but no soon have he feel its
consequence, than his neighbour begin to relent, and to
compassionate his case; and presently after, to blame that as rigour
and severity which they before call justice. they now exclaim
against punish in cold blood, and sing forth the praise of mercy
and forgiveness.
these cry be considerably increase by the death of Mrs Partridge,
which, though owe to the distemper above mention, which be no
consequence of poverty or distress, many be not ashamed to impute to
Mr Allworthy's severity, or, as they now term it, cruelty.
Partridge have now lose his wife, his school, and his annuity, and
the unknown person have now discontinue the last-mention charity,
resolve to change the scene, and leave the country, where he be in
danger of starving, with the universal compassion of all his
neighbour.
Chapter vii.
a short sketch of that felicity which prudent couple may extract from
hatred: with a short apology for those people who overlook
imperfection in their friend.
though the captain have effectually demolish poor Partridge, yet have
he not reap the harvest he hop for, which be to turn the
foundling out of Mr Allworthy's house.
on the contrary, that gentleman grow every day fonder of little Tommy,
as if he intend to counterbalance his severity to the father with
extraordinary fondness and affection towards the son.
this a good deal sour the captain's temper, as do all the other
daily instance of Mr Allworthy's generosity; for he look on all
such largess to be diminution of his own wealth.
in this, we have say, he do not agree with his wife; nor, indeed, in
anything else: for though an affection place on the understanding be,
by many wise person, think more durable than that which be found
on beauty, yet it happen otherwise in the present case. Nay, the
understanding of this couple be their principal bone of contention,
and one great cause of many quarrel, which from time to time arise
between they; and which at last end, on the side of the lady, in a
sovereign contempt for her husband; and on the husband's, in an utter
abhorrence of his wife.
as these have both exercise their talent chiefly in the study of
divinity, this be, from their first acquaintance, the most common
topic of conversation between they. the captain, like a well-breed man,
have, before marriage, always give up his opinion to that of the lady;
and this, not in the clumsy awkward manner of a conceited blockhead,
who, while he civilly yield to a superior in an argument, be desirous
of be still know to think himself in the right. the captain, on
the contrary, though one of the proud fellow in the world, so
absolutely yield the victory to his antagonist, that she, who have
not the least doubt of his sincerity, retire always from the dispute
with an admiration of her own understanding and a love for his.
but though this complacence to one whom the captain thoroughly
despise, be not so uneasy to he as it would have be have any hope
of preferment make it necessary to show the same submission to a
Hoadley, or to some other of great reputation in the science, yet even
this cost he too much to be endure without some motive. Matrimony,
therefore, have remove all such motif, he grow weary of this
condescension, and begin to treat the opinion of his wife with that
haughtiness and insolence, which none but those who deserve some
contempt themselves can bestow, and those only who deserve no contempt
can bear.
when the first torrent of tenderness be over, and when, in the calm
and long interval between the fit, reason begin to open the eye of
the lady, and she see this alteration of behaviour in the captain, who
at length answer all her argument only with pish and pshaw, she be
far from endure the indignity with a tame submission. indeed, it at
first so highly provoke she, that it might have produce some
tragical event, have it not take a more harmless turn, by fill she
with the utmost contempt for her husband's understanding, which
somewhat qualify her hatred towards he; though of this likewise she
have a pretty moderate share.
the captain's hatred to she be of a purer kind: for as to any
imperfection in her knowledge or understanding, he no more despise
she for they, than for she not be six foot high. in his opinion of
the female sex, he exceed the moroseness of Aristotle himself: he
look on a woman as on an animal of domestic use, of somewhat high
consideration than a cat, since her office be of rather more
importance; but the difference between these two be, in his
estimation, so small, that, in his marriage contract with Mr
Allworthy's land and tenement, it would have be pretty equal which
of they he have take into the bargain. and yet so tender be his
pride, that it feel the contempt which his wife now begin to express
towards he; and this, add to the surfeit he have before take of her
love, create in he a degree of disgust and abhorrence, perhaps
hardly to be exceed.
one situation only of the married state be exclude from pleasure: and
that be, a state of indifference: but as many of my reader, I hope,
know what an exquisite delight there be in conveying pleasure to a
beloved object, so some few, I be afraid, may have experience the
satisfaction of torment one we hate. it be, I apprehend, to come at
this latter pleasure, that we see both sex often give up that ease
in marriage which they might otherwise possess, though their mate be
never so disagreeable to they. hence the wife often put on fit of
love and jealousy, nay, even deny herself any pleasure, to disturb
and prevent those of her husband; and he again, in return, put
frequent restraint on himself, and stay at home in company which he
dislike, in order to confine his wife to what she equally detest.
hence, too, must flow those tear which a widow sometimes so
plentifully shed over the ash of a husband with whom she lead a life
of constant disquiet and turbulency, and whom now she can never hope
to torment any more.
but if ever any couple enjoy this pleasure, it be at present
experience by the captain and his lady. it be always a sufficient
reason to either of they to be obstinate in any opinion, that the
other have previously assert the contrary. if the one propose any
amusement, the other constantly object to it: they never love or
hat, commend or abuse, the same person. and for this reason, as
the captain look with an evil eye on the little foundling, his wife
begin now to caress it almost equally with her own child.
the reader will be apt to conceive, that this behaviour between the
husband and wife do not greatly contribute to Mr Allworthy's repose,
as it tend so little to that serene happiness which he have design
for all three from this alliance; but the truth be, though he might be
a little disappointed in his sanguine expectation, yet he be far
from be acquaint with the whole matter; for, as the captain be,
from certain obvious reason, much on his guard before he, the lady
be oblige, for fear of her brother's displeasure, to pursue the same
conduct. in fact, it be possible for a third person to be very
intimate, nay even to live long in the same house, with a married
couple, who have any tolerable discretion, and not even guess at the
sour sentiment which they bear to each other: for though the whole
day may be sometimes too short for hatred, as well as for love; yet
the many hour which they naturally spend together, apart from all
observer, furnish people of tolerable moderation with such ample
opportunity for the enjoyment of either passion, that, if they love,
they can support be a few hour in company without toy, or if
they hate, without spit in each other's face.
it be possible, however, that Mr Allworthy see enough to render he a
little uneasy; for we be not always to conclude, that a wise man be
not hurt, because he doth not cry out and lament himself, like those
of a childish or effeminate temper. but indeed it be possible he might
see some fault in the captain without any uneasiness at all; for man
of true wisdom and goodness be content to take person and thing
as they be, without complain of their imperfection, or attempt
to amend they. they can see a fault in a friend, a relation, or an
acquaintance, without ever mention it to the party themselves, or
to any other; and this often without lessen their affection.
indeed, unless great discernment be temper with this overlook
disposition, we ought never to contract friendship but with a degree
of folly which we can deceive; for I hope my friend will pardon I
when I declare, I know none of they without a fault; and I should be
sorry if I could imagine I have any friend who could not see mine.
forgiveness of this kind we give and demand in turn. it be an exercise
of friendship, and perhaps none of the least pleasant. and this
forgiveness we must bestow, without desire of amendment. there be,
perhaps, no surer mark of folly, than an attempt to correct the
natural infirmity of those we love. the fine composition of human
nature, as well as the fine china, may have a flaw in it; and this,
I be afraid, in either case, be equally incurable; though,
nevertheless, the pattern may remain of the high value.
upon the whole, then, Mr Allworthy certainly see some imperfection in
the captain; but as this be a very artful man, and eternally upon his
guard before he, these appear to he no more than blemish in a
good character, which his goodness make he overlook, and his wisdom
prevent he from discover to the captain himself. very different
would have be his sentiment have he discover the whole; which
perhaps would in time have be the case, have the husband and wife
long continue this kind of behaviour to each other; but this kind
Fortune take effectual mean to prevent, by force the captain to do
that which render he again dear to his wife, and restore all her
tenderness and affection towards he.
Chapter viii.
a receipt to regain the lost affection of a wife, which hath never
be know to fail in the most desperate case.
the captain be make large amend for the unpleasant minute which he
pass in the conversation of his wife (and which be as few as he
could contrive to make they), by the pleasant meditation he enjoy
when alone.
these meditation be entirely employ on Mr Allworthy's fortune;
for, first, he exercise much thought in calculate, as well as he
could, the exact value of the whole: which calculation he often see
occasion to alter in his own favour: and, secondly and chiefly, he
please himself with intend alteration in the house and garden,
and in project many other scheme, as well for the improvement of
the estate as of the grandeur of the place: for this purpose he
apply himself to the study of architecture and gardening, and read
over many book on both these subject; for these science, indeed,
employ his whole time, and form his only amusement. he at last
complete a most excellent plan: and very sorry we be, that it be not
in our power to present it to our reader, since even the luxury of the
present age, I believe, would hardly match it. it have, indeed, in a
superlative degree, the two principal ingredient which serve to
recommend all great and noble design of this nature; for it require
an immoderate expense to execute, and a vast length of time to bring
it to any sort of perfection. the former of these, the immense wealth
of which the captain suppose Mr Allworthy possess, and which he
think himself sure of inheriting, promise very effectually to
supply; and the latter, the soundness of his own constitution, and his
time of life, which be only what be call middle-age, remove all
apprehension of his not live to accomplish.
nothing be want to enable he to enter upon the immediate
execution of this plan, but the death of Mr Allworthy; in calculate
which he have employ much of his own algebra, besides purchase
every book extant that treat of the value of life, reversion, &c.
from all which he satisfy himself, that as he have every day a chance
of this happening, so have he more than an even chance of its happening
within a few year.
but while the captain be one day busy in deep contemplation of
this kind, one of the most unlucky as well as unseasonable accident
happen to he. the utmost malice of Fortune could, indeed, have
contrive nothing so cruel, so mal-a-propos, so absolutely destructive
to all his scheme. in short, not to keep the reader in long suspense,
just at the very instant when his heart be exult in meditation on
the happiness which would accrue to he by Mr Allworthy's death, he
himself--die of an apoplexy.
this unfortunately befel the captain as he be take his evening walk
by himself, so that nobody be present to lend he any assistance, if
indeed, any assistance could have preserve he. he take, therefore,
measure of that proportion of soil which be now become adequate to
all his future purpose, and he lie dead on the ground, a great
(though not a living) example of the truth of that observation of
Horace:
_Tu secanda marmora
Locas sub ipsum funus; et sepulchri
Immemor, struis domos._
which sentiment I shall thus give to the english reader: "you provide
the noble material for building, when a pickaxe and a spade be
only necessary: and build house of five hundred by a hundred foot,
forget that of six by two."
Chapter ix.
a proof of the infallibility of the forego receipt, in the
lamentation of the widow; with other suitable decoration of death,
such as physician, &c., and an epitaph in the true stile.
Mr Allworthy, his sister, and another lady, be assemble at the
accustomed hour in the supper-room, where, have wait a
considerable time long than usual, Mr Allworthy first declare he
begin to grow uneasy at the captain's stay (for he be always most
punctual at his meal); and give order that the bell should be ring
without the door, and especially towards those walk which the
captain be willnot to use.
all these summon prove ineffectual (for the captain have, by
perverse accident, betake himself to a new walk that evening), Mrs
Blifil declare she be seriously frightened. upon which the other
lady, who be one of her most intimate acquaintance, and who well know
the true state of her affection, endeavour all she could to pacify
she, tell she--to be sure she could not help be uneasy; but that
she should hope the good. that, perhaps the sweetness of the evening
have intice the captain to go far than his usual walk: or he might
be detain at some neighbour's. Mrs Blifil answer, no; she be sure
some accident have befall he; for that he would never stay out
without send her word, as he must know how uneasy it would make
she. the other lady, have no other argument to use, betook herself
to the entreaty usual on such occasion, and beg she not to
frighten herself, for it might be of very ill consequence to her own
health; and, fill out a very large glass of wine, advise, and at
last prevail with she to drink it.
Mr Allworthy now return into the parlour; for he have be himself in
search after the captain. his countenance sufficiently show the
consternation he be under, which, indeed, have a good deal deprive
he of speech; but as grief operate variously on different mind, so
the same apprehension which depress his voice, elevate that of Mrs
Blifil. she now begin to bewail herself in very bitter term, and
flood of tear accompany her lamentation; which the lady, her
companion, declare she could not blame, but at the same time
dissuade she from indulge; attempt to moderate the grief of her
friend by philosophical observation on the many disappointment to
which human life be daily subject, which, she say, be a sufficient
consideration to fortify our mind against any accident, how sudden
or terrible soever. she say her brother's example ought to teach she
patience, who, though indeed he could not be suppose as much
concerned as herself, yet be, doubtless, very uneasy, though his
resignation to the Divine will have restrain his grief within due
bound.
"Mention not my brother," say Mrs Blifil; "I alone be the object of
your pity. what be the terror of friendship to what a wife feel on
these occasion? oh, he be lose! somebody hath murder he--I shall
never see he more!"--here a torrent of tear have the same consequence
with what the suppression have occasion to Mr Allworthy, and she
remain silent.
at this interval a servant come run in, out of breath, and cry
out, the captain be find; and, before he could proceed far, he
be follow by two more, bear the dead body between they.
here the curious reader may observe another diversity in the
operation of grief: for as Mr Allworthy have be before silent, from
the same cause which have make his sister vociferous; so do the
present sight, which draw tear from the gentleman, put an entire stop
to those of the lady; who first give a violent scream, and presently
after fall into a fit.
the room be soon full of servant, some of whom, with the lady
visitant, be employ in care of the wife; and other, with Mr
Allworthy, assist in carry off the captain to a warm bed; where
every method be try, in order to restore he to life.
and glad should we be, could we inform the reader that both these
body have be attend with equal success; for those who undertake
the care of the lady succeed so well, that, after the fit have
continue a decent time, she again revive, to their great
satisfaction: but as to the captain, all experiment of bleeding,
chafing, dropping, &c., prove ineffectual. death, that inexorable
judge, have pass sentence on he, and refuse to grant he a
reprieve, though two doctor who arrive, and be fee'd at one and
the same instant, be his counsel.
these two doctor, whom, to avoid any malicious application, we shall
distinguish by the name of Dr Y. and Dr Z., have feel his pulse; to
wit, Dr Y. his right arm, and Dr Z. his left; both agree that he be
absolutely dead; but as to the distemper, or cause of his death, they
differ; Dr Y. hold that he die of an apoplexy, and Dr Z. of an
epilepsy.
hence arise a dispute between the learn man, in which each deliver
the reason of their several opinion. these be of such equal force,
that they serve both to confirm either doctor in his own sentiment,
and make not the least impression on his adversary.
to say the truth, every physician almost hath his favourite disease,
to which he ascribe all the victory obtain over human nature. the
gout, the rheumatism, the stone, the gravel, and the consumption, have
all their several patron in the faculty; and none more than the
nervous fever, or the fever on the spirit. and here we may account
for those disagreement in opinion, concern the cause of a
patient's death, which sometimes occur, between the most learn of
the college; and which have greatly surprize that part of the world
who have be ignorant of the fact we have above assert.
the reader may perhaps be surprize, that, instead of endeavour to
revive the patient, the learn gentleman should fall immediately into
a dispute on the occasion of his death; but in reality all such
experiment have be make before their arrival: for the captain be
put into a warm bed, have his vein scarify, his forehead chafe, and
all sort of strong drop apply to his lip and nostril.
the physician, therefore, find themselves anticipate in
everything they order, be at a loss how to apply that portion of
time which it be usual and decent to remain for their fee, and be
therefore necessitate to find some subject or other for discourse;
and what could more naturally present itself than that before
mention?
our doctor be about to take their leave, when Mr Allworthy, have
give over the captain, and acquiesce in the Divine will, begin to
enquire after his sister, whom he desire they to visit before their
departure.
this lady be now recover of her fit, and, to use the common phrase,
as well as could be expect for one in her condition. the doctor,
therefore, all previous ceremony be comply with, as this be a
new patient, attend, accord to desire, and lay hold on each of
her hand, as they have before do on those of the corpse.
the case of the lady be in the other extreme from that of her
husband: for as he be past all the assistance of physic, so in
reality she require none.
there be nothing more unjust than the vulgar opinion, by which
physician be misrepresent, as friend to death. on the contrary, I
believe, if the number of those who recover by physic could be oppose
to that of the martyr to it, the former would rather exceed the
latter. Nay, some be so cautious on this head, that, to avoid a
possibility of kill the patient, they abstain from all method of
cure, and prescribe nothing but what can neither do good nor harm. I
have hear some of these, with great gravity, deliver it as a maxim,
