"that nature should be leave to do her own work, while the physician
stand by as it be to clap she on the back, and encourage she when
she doth well."
so little then do our doctor delight in death, that they discharge
the corpse after a single fee; but they be not so disgusted with
their live patient; concern whose case they immediately agree,
and fall to prescribe with great diligence.
whether, as the lady have at first persuade her physician to believe
her ill, they have now, in return, persuade she to believe herself so,
I will not determine; but she continue a whole month with all the
decoration of sickness. during this time she be visit by
physician, attend by nurse, and receive constant message from
her acquaintance to enquire after her health.
at length the decent time for sickness and immoderate grief being
expire, the doctor be discharge, and the lady begin to see
company; be alter only from what she be before, by that colour
of sadness in which she have dress her person and countenance.
the captain be now inter, and might, perhaps, have already make a
large progress towards oblivion, have not the friendship of Mr
Allworthy take care to preserve his memory, by the follow epitaph,
which be write by a man of as great genius as integrity, and one
who perfectly well know the captain.
HERE lie,
in EXPECTATION of a JOYFUL rising,
the body of
CAPTAIN JOHN BLIFIL.
LONDON
had the honour of his BIRTH,
OXFORD
of his education.
his part
be an honour to his profession
and to his country:
his life, to his religion
and HUMAN NATURE.
he be a DUTIFUL son,
a tender husband,
an affectionate FATHER,
a MOST kind BROTHER,
a SINCERE FRIEND,
a devout christian,
and a good MAN.
his inconsolable WIDOW
HATH erect this stone,
the MONUMENT of
his virtue
and of she AFFECTION.
BOOK III.
contain the MOST MEMORABLE transaction which pass in the FAMILY
of MR ALLWORTHY, from the TIME when TOMMY JONES ARRIVED at the AGE of
FOURTEEN, till he attain the AGE of nineteen. in this BOOK the
reader may PICK up some hint concern the EDUCATION of child.
Chapter i.
contain little or nothing.
the reader will be pleased to remember, that, at the beginning of the
second book of this history, we give he a hint of our intention to
pass over several large period of time, in which nothing happen
worthy of be record in a chronicle of this kind.
in so do, we do not only consult our own dignity and ease, but the
good and advantage of the reader: for besides that by these mean we
prevent he from throw away his time, in reading without either
pleasure or emolument, we give he, at all such season, an
opportunity of employ that wonderful sagacity, of which he be
master, by fill up these vacant space of time with his own
conjecture; for which purpose we have take care to qualify he in
the precede page.
for instance, what reader but know that Mr Allworthy feel, at first,
for the loss of his friend, those emotion of grief, which on such
occasion enter into all man whose heart be not compose of flint,
or their head of as solid material? again, what reader doth not know
that philosophy and religion in time moderate, and at last
extinguish, this grief? the former of these teach the folly and
vanity of it, and the latter correct it as unlawful, and at the
same time assuage it, by raise future hope and assurance, which
enable a strong and religious mind to take leave of a friend, on his
deathbe, with little less indifference than if he be prepare for a
long journey; and, indeed, with little less hope of see he again.
nor can the judicious reader be at a great loss on account of Mrs
Bridget Blifil, who, he may be assure, conduct herself through the
whole season in which grief be to make its appearance on the outside
of the body, with the strict regard to all the rule of custom and
decency, suit the alteration of her countenance to the several
alteration of her habit: for as this change from weed to black,
from black to grey, from grey to white, so do her countenance change
from dismal to sorrowful, from sorrowful to sad, and from sad to
serious, till the day come in which she be allow to return to her
former serenity.
we have mention these two, as example only of the task which may be
impose on reader of the low class. much high and hard
exercise of judgment and penetration may reasonably be expect from
the upper graduate in criticism. many notable discovery will, I
doubt not, be make by such, of the transaction which happen in the
family of our worthy man, during all the year which we have think
proper to pass over: for though nothing worthy of a place in this
history occur within that period, yet do several incident happen
of equal importance with those report by the daily and weekly
historian of the age; in read which great number of person
consume a considerable part of their time, very little, I be afraid,
to their emolument. now, in the conjecture here propose, some of the
most excellent faculty of the mind may be employ to much
advantage, since it be a more useful capacity to be able to foretel
the action of man, in any circumstance, from their character, than
to judge of their character from their action. the former, I own,
require the great penetration; but may be accomplish by true
sagacity with no less certainty than the latter.
as we be sensible that much the great part of our reader be very
eminently possess of this quality, we have leave they a space of
twelve year to exert it in; and shall now bring forth our heroe, at
about fourteen year of age, not question that many have be long
impatient to be introduce to his acquaintance.
Chapter ii.
the heroe of this great history appear with very bad omen. a little
tale of so low a kind that some may think it not worth their notice. a
word or two concern a squire, and more relate to a gamekeeper and
a schoolmaster.
as we determine, when we first sit down to write this history, to
flatter no man, but to guide our pen throughout by the direction of
truth, we be oblige to bring our heroe on the stage in a much more
disadvantageous manner than we could wish; and to declare honestly,
even at his first appearance, that it be the universal opinion of all
Mr Allworthy's family that he be certainly bear to be hang.
indeed, I be sorry to say there be too much reason for this
conjecture; the lad have from his early year discover a
propensity to many vice, and especially to one which hath as direct a
tendency as any other to that fate which we have just now observe to
have be prophetically denounce against he: he have be already
convict of three robbery, viz., of rob an orchard, of steal
a duck out of a farmer's yard, and of pick Master Blifil's pocket
of a ball.
the vice of this young man be, moreover, heighten by the
disadvantageous light in which they appear when oppose to the
virtue of Master Blifil, his companion; a youth of so different a
cast from little Jones, that not only the family but all the
neighbourhood resound his praise. he be, indeed, a lad of a
remarkable disposition; sober, discreet, and pious beyond his age;
quality which gain he the love of every one who know he: while
Tom Jones be universally dislike; and many express their wonder
that Mr Allworthy would suffer such a lad to be educate with his
nephew, lest the moral of the latter should be corrupt by his
example.
an incident which happen about this time will set the character of
these two lad more fairly before the discerning reader than be in the
power of the long dissertation.
Tom Jones, who, bad as he be, must serve for the heroe of this
history, have only one friend among all the servant of the family; for
as to Mrs Wilkins, she have long since give he up, and be perfectly
reconcile to her mistress. this friend be the gamekeeper, a fellow
of a loose kind of disposition, and who be think not to entertain
much strict notion concern the difference of _meum_ and _tuum_
than the young gentleman himself. and hence this friendship give
occasion to many sarcastical remark among the domestic, most of
which be either proverb before, or at least be become so now; and,
indeed, the wit of they all may be comprise in that short Latin
proverb, "_Noscitur a socio;_" which, I think, be thus express in
English, "you may know he by the company he keep."
to say the truth, some of that atrocious wickedness in Jones, of which
we have just mention three example, might perhaps be derive from
the encouragement he have receive from this fellow, who, in two or
three instance, have be what the law call an accessary after the
fact: for the whole duck, and great part of the apple, be convert
to the use of the gamekeeper and his family; though, as Jones alone
be discover, the poor lad bore not only the whole smart, but the
whole blame; both which fall again to his lot on the follow
occasion.
Contiguous to Mr Allworthy's estate be the manor of one of those
gentleman who be call preserver of the game. this specie of man,
from the great severity with which they revenge the death of a hare or
partridge, might be think to cultivate the same superstition with
the Bannians in India; many of whom, we be tell, dedicate their whole
life to the preservation and protection of certain animal; be it
not that our English Bannians, while they preserve they from other
enemy, will most unmercifully slaughter whole horse-load
themselves; so that they stand clearly acquit of any such
heathenish superstition.
I have, indeed, a much well opinion of this kind of man than be
entertain by some, as I take they to answer the order of Nature, and
the good purpose for which they be ordain, in a more ample manner
than many other. now, as Horace tell we that there be a set of
human being
_fruge consumere nati,_
"bear to consume the fruit of the earth;" so I make no manner of
doubt but that there be other
_Feras consumere nati,_
"bear to consume the beast of the field;" or, as it be commonly
call, the game; and none, I believe, will deny but that those
squire fulfil this end of their creation.
Little Jones go one day a shooting with the gamekeeper; when
happen to spring a covey of partridge near the border of that
manor over which Fortune, to fulfil the wise purpose of Nature, have
plant one of the game consumer, the bird fly into it, and be
mark (as it be call) by the two sportsman, in some furze bush,
about two or three hundred pace beyond Mr Allworthy's dominion.
Mr Allworthy have give the fellow strict order, on pain of forfeit
his place, never to trespass on any of his neighbour; no more on
those who be less rigid in this matter than on the lord of this
manor. with regard to other, indeed, these order have not be always
very scrupulously keep; but as the disposition of the gentleman with
whom the partridge have take sanctuary be well know, the gamekeeper
have never yet attempt to invade his territory. nor have he do it
now, have not the young sportsman, who be excessively eager to
pursue the fly game, over-persuade he; but Jones be very
importunate, the other, who be himself keen enough after the sport,
yield to his persuasion, enter the manor, and shoot one of the
partridge.
the gentleman himself be at that time on horse-back, at a little
distance from they; and hear the gun go off, he immediately make
towards the place, and discover poor Tom; for the gamekeeper have
leapt into the thick part of the furze-brake, where he have happily
conceal himself.
the gentleman have search the lad, and find the partridge upon
he, denounce great vengeance, swear he would acquaint Mr
Allworthy. he be as good as his word: for he ride immediately to his
house, and complain of the trespass on his manor in as high term
and as bitter language as if his house have be break open, and the
most valuable furniture steal out of it. he add, that some other
person be in his company, though he could not discover he; for that
two gun have be discharge almost in the same instant. and, say he,
"we have find only this partridge, but the Lord know what mischief
they have do."
at his return home, Tom be presently convene before Mr Allworthy. he
own the fact, and alledge no other excuse but what be really true,
viz., that the covey be originally spring in Mr Allworthy's own
manor.
Tom be then interrogate who be with he, which Mr Allworthy
declare he be resolve to know, acquaint the culprit with the
circumstance of the two gun, which have be depose by the squire and
both his servant; but Tom stoutly persist in assert that he be
alone; yet, to say the truth, he hesitate a little at first, which
would have confirm Mr Allworthy's belief, have what the squire and
his servant say want any further confirmation.
the gamekeeper, be a suspect person, be now send for, and the
question put to he; but he, rely on the promise which Tom have make
he, to take all upon himself, very resolutely deny be in company
with the young gentleman, or indeed have see he the whole
afternoon.
Mr Allworthy then turn towards Tom, with more than usual anger in
his countenance, and advise he to confess who be with he;
repeat, that he be resolve to know. the lad, however, still
maintain his resolution, and be dismiss with much wrath by Mr
Allworthy, who tell he he should have to the next morning to consider
of it, when he should be question by another person, and in another
manner.
Poor Jones spend a very melancholy night; and the more so, as he be
without his usual companion; for Master Blifil be go abroad on a
visit with his mother. fear of the punishment he be to suffer be on
this occasion his least evil; his chief anxiety be, lest his
constancy should fail he, and he should be bring to betray the
gamekeeper, whose ruin he know must now be the consequence.
nor do the gamekeeper pass his time much well. he have the same
apprehension with the youth; for whose honour he have likewise a much
tenderer regard than for his skin.
in the morning, when Tom attend the reverend Mr Thwackum, the person
to whom Mr Allworthy have commit the instruction of the two boy, he
have the same question put to he by that gentleman which he have be
ask the evening before, to which he return the same answer. the
consequence of this be, so severe a whipping, that it possibly fall
little short of the torture with which confession be in some
country extort from criminal.
Tom bear his punishment with great resolution; and though his master
ask he, between every stroke, whether he would not confess, he be
content to be flead rather than betray his friend, or break the
promise he have make.
the gamekeeper be now relieve from his anxiety, and Mr Allworthy
himself begin to be concern at Tom's suffering: for besides that Mr
Thwackum, be highly enraged that he be not able to make the boy
say what he himself please, have carry his severity much beyond the
good man's intention, this latter begin now to suspect that the squire
have be mistake; which his extreme eagerness and anger seem to
make probable; and as for what the servant have say in confirmation
of their master's account, he lay no great stress upon that. now, as
cruelty and injustice be two idea of which Mr Allworthy could by no
mean support the consciousness a single moment, he send for Tom, and
after many kind and friendly exhortation, say, "I be convince, my
dear child, that my suspicion have wrong you; I be sorry that you
have be so severely punish on this account." and at last give he
a little horse to make he amend; again repeat his sorrow for what
have past.
Tom's guilt now fly in his face more than any severity could make it.
he could more easily bear the lash of Thwackum, than the generosity
of Allworthy. the tear burst from his eye, and he fall upon his
knee, cry, "oh, sir, you be too good to I. indeed you be.
indeed I donot deserve it." and at that very instant, from the fulness
of his heart, have almost betray the secret; but the good genius of
the gamekeeper suggest to he what might be the consequence to the
poor fellow, and this consideration seal his lip.
Thwackum do all he could to persuade Allworthy from show any
compassion or kindness to the boy, say, "he have persist in an
untruth;" and give some hint, that a second whipping might probably
bring the matter to light.
but Mr Allworthy absolutely refuse to consent to the experiment. he
say, the boy have suffer enough already for conceal the truth,
even if he be guilty, see that he could have no motive but a
mistaken point of honour for so do.
"Honour!" cry Thwackum, with some warmth, "mere stubbornness and
obstinacy! can honour teach any one to tell a lie, or can any honour
exist independent of religion?"
this discourse happen at table when dinner be just end; and there
be present Mr Allworthy, Mr Thwackum, and a third gentleman, who now
enter into the debate, and whom, before we proceed any far, we
shall briefly introduce to our reader's acquaintance.
Chapter iii.
the character of Mr Square the philosopher, and of Mr Thwackum the
divine; with a dispute concerning----
the name of this gentleman, who have then reside some time at Mr
Allworthy's house, be Mr Square. his natural part be not of the
first rate, but he have greatly improve they by a learn education.
he be deeply read in the antient, and a prof master of all the
work of Plato and Aristotle. upon which great model he have
principally form himself; sometimes accord with the opinion of
the one, and sometimes with that of the other. in moral he be a
prof Platonist, and in religion he incline to be an Aristotelian.
but though he have, as we have say, form his moral on the platonic
model, yet he perfectly agree with the opinion of Aristotle, in
consider that great man rather in the quality of a philosopher or a
speculatist, than as a legislator. this sentiment he carry a great
way; indeed, so far, as to regard all virtue as matter of theory only.
this, it be true, he never affirm, as I have hear, to any one; and
yet upon the least attention to his conduct, I cannot help think it
be his real opinion, as it will perfectly reconcile some
contradiction which might otherwise appear in his character.
this gentleman and Mr Thwackum scarce ever meet without a disputation;
for their tenet be indeed diametrically opposite to each other.
Square hold human nature to be the perfection of all virtue, and that
vice be a deviation from our nature, in the same manner as deformity
of body be. Thwackum, on the contrary, maintain that the human mind,
since the fall, be nothing but a sink of iniquity, till purify and
redeem by grace. in one point only they agree, which be, in all
their discourse on morality never to mention the word goodness. the
favourite phrase of the former, be the natural beauty of virtue; that
of the latter, be the divine power of grace. the former measure all
action by the unalterable rule of right, and the eternal fitness of
thing; the latter decide all matter by authority; but in do
this, he always use the scripture and their commentator, as the
lawyer doth his Coke upon Lyttleton, where the comment be of equal
authority with the text.
after this short introduction, the reader will be pleased to remember,
that the parson have conclude his speech with a triumphant question,
to which he have apprehend no answer; viz., can any honour exist
independent on religion?
to this Square answer; that it be impossible to discourse
philosophically concern word, till their meaning be first
establish: that there be scarce any two word of a more vague and
uncertain signification, than the two he have mention; for that there
be almost as many different opinion concern honour, as
concern religion. "but," say he, "if by honour you mean the true
natural beauty of virtue, I will maintain it may exist independent of
any religion whatever. Nay," add he, "you yourself will allow it may
exist independent of all but one: so will a Mahometan, a Jew, and all
the maintainer of all the different sect in the world."
Thwackum reply, this be argue with the usual malice of all the
enemy to the true Church. he say, he doubt not but that all the
infidel and heretick in the world would, if they could, confine
honour to their own absurd error and damnable deception; "but
honour," say he, "be not therefore manifold, because there be many
absurd opinion about it; nor be religion manifold, because there be
various sect and heresy in the world. when I mention religion, I
mean the christian religion; and not only the christian religion, but
the protestant religion; and not only the protestant religion, but the
Church of England. and when I mention honour, I mean that mode of
divine grace which be not only consistent with, but dependent upon,
this religion; and be consistent with and dependent upon no other. now
to say that the honour I here mean, and which be, I think, all the
honour I could be suppose to mean, will uphold, much less dictate an
untruth, be to assert an absurdity too shocking to be conceive."
"I purposely avoid," say Square, "draw a conclusion which I
think evident from what I have say; but if you perceive it, I be
sure you have not attempt to answer it. however, to drop the article
of religion, I think it be plain, from what you have say, that we
have different idea of honour; or why do we not agree in the same
term of its explanation? I have assert, that true honour and true
virtue be almost synonymous term, and they be both found on the
unalterable rule of right, and the eternal fitness of thing; to which
an untruth be absolutely repugnant and contrary, it be certain that
true honour cannot support an untruth. in this, therefore, I think we
be agree; but that this honour can be say to be found on
religion, to which it be antecedent, if by religion be mean any
positive law--"
"I agree," answer Thwackum, with great warmth, "with a man who
assert honour to be antecedent to religion! Mr Allworthy, do I
agree--?"
he be proceed when Mr Allworthy interpose, tell they very
coldly, they have both mistake his meaning; for that he have say
nothing of true honour.--it be possible, however, he would not have
easily quiet the disputant, who be grow equally warm, have not
another matter now fall out, which put a final end to the
conversation at present.
chapter iv.
contain a necessary apology for the author; and a childish
incident, which perhaps require an apology likewise.
before I proceed far, I shall beg leave to obviate some
misconstruction into which the zeal of some few reader may lead
they; for I would not willingly give offence to any, especially to man
who be warm in the cause of virtue or religion.
I hope, therefore, no man will, by the gross misunderstanding or
perversion of my meaning, misrepresent I, as endeavour to cast any
ridicule on the great perfection of human nature; and which do,
indeed, alone purify and ennoble the heart of man, and raise he above
the brute creation. this, reader, I will venture to say (and by how
much the well man you be yourself, by so much the more will you be
incline to believe I), that I would rather have bury the
sentiment of these two person in eternal oblivion, than have do
any injury to either of these glorious cause.
on the contrary, it be with a view to their service, that I have take
upon I to record the life and action of two of their false and
pretend champion. a treacherous friend be the most dangerous enemy;
and I will say boldly, that both religion and virtue have receive
more real discredit from hypocrite than the witty profligate or
infidel could ever cast upon they: nay, far, as these two, in
their purity, be rightly call the band of civil society, and be
indeed the great of blessing; so when poison and corrupt with
fraud, pretence, and affectation, they have become the bad of civil
curse, and have enable man to perpetrate the most cruel mischief to
their own specie.
indeed, I doubt not but this ridicule will in general be allow: my
chief apprehension be, as many true and just sentiment often come
from the mouth of these person, lest the whole should be take
together, and I should be conceive to ridicule all alike. now the
reader will be pleased to consider, that, as neither of these man be
fool, they could not be suppose to have holden none but wrong
principle, and to have utter nothing but absurdity; what
injustice, therefore, must I have do to their character, have I
select only what be bad! and how horribly wretched and maim must
their argument have appear!
upon the whole, it be not religion or virtue, but the want of they,
which be here expose. have not Thwackum too much neglect virtue, and
Square, religion, in the composition of their several system, and have
not both utterly discard all natural goodness of heart, they have
never be represent as the object of derision in this history; in
which we will now proceed.
this matter then, which put an end to the debate mention in the last
chapter, be no other than a quarrel between Master Blifil and Tom
Jones, the consequence of which have be a bloody nose to the former;
for though Master Blifil, notwithstanding he be the young, be in
size above the other's match, yet Tom be much his superior at the
noble art of boxing.
Tom, however, cautiously avoid all engagement with that youth; for
besides that Tommy Jones be an inoffensive lad amidst all his
roguery, and really love Blifil, Mr Thwackum be always the second
of the latter, would have be sufficient to deter he.
but well say a certain author, no man be wise at all hour; it be
therefore no wonder that a boy be not so. a difference arise at play
between the two lad, Master Blifil call Tom a beggarly bastard.
upon which the latter, who be somewhat passionate in his disposition,
immediately cause that phenomenon in the face of the former, which we
have above remember.
Master Blifil now, with his blood run from his nose, and the tear
gallop after from his eye, appear before his uncle and the
tremendous Thwackum. in which court an indictment of assault, battery,
and wounding, be instantly prefer against Tom; who in his excuse
only plead the provocation, which be indeed all the matter that
Master Blifil have omit.
it be indeed possible that this circumstance might have escape his
memory; for, in his reply, he positively insist, that he have make
use of no such appellation; add, "Heaven forbid such naughty word
should ever come out of his mouth!"
Tom, though against all form of law, rejoin in affirmance of the
word. upon which Master Blifil say, "it be no wonder. those who will
tell one fib, will hardly stick at another. if I have tell my master
such a wicked fib as you have do, I should be ashamed to show my
face."
"what fib, child?" cry Thwackum pretty eagerly.
"why, he tell you that nobody be with he a shooting when he kill
the partridge; but he know" (here he burst into a flood of tear),
"yes, he know, for he confess it to I, that Black George the
gamekeeper be there. Nay, he say--yes you do--deny it if you can,
that you would not have conf the truth, though master have cut you
to piece."
at this the fire flash from Thwackum's eye, and he cry out in
triumph--"Oh! ho! this be your mistaken notion of honour! this be the
boy who be not to be whip again!" but Mr Allworthy, with a more
gentle aspect, turn towards the lad, and say, "be this true, child?
how come you to persist so obstinately in a falsehood?"
Tom say, "he scorn a lie as much as any one: but he think his
honour engage he to act as he do; for he have promise the poor
fellow to conceal he: which," he say, "he think himself far
oblige to, as the gamekeeper have beg he not to go into the
gentleman's manor, and have at last go himself, in compliance with
his persuasion." he say, "this be the whole truth of the matter,
and he would take his oath of it;" and conclude with very
passionately beg Mr Allworthy "to have compassion on the poor
fellow's family, especially as he himself only have be guilty, and
the other have be very difficultly prevail on to do what he do.
indeed, sir," say he, "it could hardly be call a lie that I tell;
for the poor fellow be entirely innocent of the whole matter. I
should have go alone after the bird; nay, I do go at first, and he
only follow I to prevent more mischief. do, pray, sir, let I be
punish; take my little horse away again; but pray, sir, forgive poor
George."
Mr Allworthy hesitate a few moment, and then dismiss the boy,
advise they to live more friendly and peaceably together.
Chapter v.
the opinion of the divine and the philosopher concern the two
boy; with some reason for their opinion, and other matter.
it be probable, that by disclose this secret, which have be
communicate in the utmost confidence to he, young Blifil preserve
his companion from a good lashing; for the offence of the bloody nose
would have be of itself sufficient cause for Thwackum to have
proceed to correction; but now this be totally absorb in the
consideration of the other matter; and with regard to this, Mr
Allworthy declare privately, he think the boy deserve reward
rather than punishment, so that Thwackum's hand be withhold by a
general pardon.
Thwackum, whose meditation be full of birch, exclaim against this
weak, and, as he say he would venture to call it, wicked lenity. to
remit the punishment of such crime be, he say, to encourage they.
he enlarge much on the correction of child, and quote many text
from Solomon, and other; which be to be find in so many other
book, shall not be find here. he then apply himself to the vice of
lying, on which head he be altogether as learn as he have be on
the other.
Square say, he have be endeavour to reconcile the behaviour of
Tom with his idea of perfect virtue, but could not. he own there be
something which at first sight appear like fortitude in the action;
but as fortitude be a virtue, and falsehood a vice, they could by no
mean agree or unite together. he add, that as this be in some
measure to confound virtue and vice, it might be worth Mr Thwackum's
consideration, whether a large castigation might not be lay on upon
the account.
as both these learn man concur in censuring Jones, so be they
no less unanimous in applaud Master Blifil. to bring truth to
light, be by the parson assert to be the duty of every religious
man; and by the philosopher this be declare to be highly conformable
with the rule of right, and the eternal and unalterable fitness of
thing.
all this, however, weigh very little with Mr Allworthy. he could not
be prevail on to sign the warrant for the execution of Jones. there
be something within his own breast with which the invincible fidelity
which that youth have preserve, correspond much well than it have
do with the religion of Thwackum, or with the virtue of Square. he
therefore strictly order the former of these gentleman to abstain
from lay violent hand on Tom for what have past. the pedagogue be
oblige to obey those order; but not without great reluctance, and
frequent muttering that the boy would be certainly spoil.
towards the gamekeeper the good man behave with more severity. he
presently summon that poor fellow before he, and after many bitter
remonstrance, pay he his wage, and dismist he from his service;
for Mr Allworthy rightly observe, that there be a great difference
between be guilty of a falsehood to excuse yourself, and to excuse
another. he likewise urge, as the principal motive to his inflexible
severity against this man, that he have basely suffer Tom Jones to
undergo so heavy a punishment for his sake, whereas he ought to have
prevent it by make the discovery himself.
when this story become public, many people differ from Square and
Thwackum, in judge the conduct of the two lad on the occasion.
Master Blifil be generally call a sneaking rascal, a poor-spirit
wretch, with other epithet of the like kind; whilst Tom be honour
with the appellation of a brave lad, a jolly dog, and an honest
fellow. indeed, his behaviour to Black George much ingratiate he
with all the servant; for though that fellow be before universally
dislike, yet he be no soon turn away than he be as universally
pitied; and the friendship and gallantry of Tom Jones be celebrate
by they all with the high applause; and they condemn Master
Blifil as openly as they durst, without incur the danger of
offend his mother. for all this, however, poor Tom smart in the
flesh; for though Thwackum have be inhibit to exercise his arm on
the forego account, yet, as the proverb say, it be easy to find a
stick, &c. So be it easy to find a rod; and, indeed, the not be
able to find one be the only thing which could have keep Thwackum any
long time from chastise poor Jones.
have the bare delight in the sport be the only inducement to the
pedagogue, it be probable Master Blifil would likewise have have his
share; but though Mr Allworthy have give he frequent order to make
no difference between the lad, yet be Thwackum altogether as kind
and gentle to this youth, as he be harsh, nay even barbarous, to the
other. to say the truth, Blifil have greatly gain his master's
affection; partly by the profound respect he always show his
person, but much more by the decent reverence with which he receive
his doctrine; for he have get by heart, and frequently repeat, his
phrase, and maintain all his master's religious principle with a
zeal which be surprize in one so young, and which greatly endear
he to the worthy preceptor.
Tom Jones, on the other hand, be not only deficient in outward token
of respect, often forget to pull off his hat, or to bow at his
master's approach; but be altogether as unmindful both of his
master's precept and example. he be indeed a thoughtless, giddy
youth, with little sobriety in his manner, and less in his
countenance; and would often very impudently and indecently laugh at
his companion for his serious behaviour.
Mr Square have the same reason for his preference of the former lad;
for Tom Jones show no more regard to the learn discourse which
this gentleman would sometimes throw away upon he, than to those of
Thwackum. he once venture to make a jest of the rule of right; and at
another time say, he believe there be no rule in the world capable
of make such a man as his father (for so Mr Allworthy suffer
himself to be call).
Master Blifil, on the contrary, have address enough at sixteen to
recommend himself at one and the same time to both these opposite.
with one he be all religion, with the other he be all virtue. and
when both be present, he be profoundly silent, which both
interpret in his favour and in their own.
nor be Blifil content with flatter both these gentleman to their
face; he take frequent occasion of praise they behind their back
to Allworthy; before whom, when they two be alone, and his uncle
commend any religious or virtuous sentiment (for many such come
constantly from he) he seldom fail to ascribe it to the good
instruction he have receive from either Thwackum or Square; for he
know his uncle repeat all such compliment to the person for whose
use they be mean; and he find by experience the great impression
which they make on the philosopher, as well as on the divine: for, to
say the truth, there be no kind of flattery so irresistible as this,
at second hand.
the young gentleman, moreover, soon perceive how extremely grateful
all those panegyric on his instructor be to Mr Allworthy himself,
as they so loudly resound the praise of that singular plan of
education which he have lay down; for this worthy man have observe
the imperfect institution of our public school, and the many vice
which boy be there liable to learn, have resolve to educate his
nephew, as well as the other lad, whom he have in a manner adopt, in
his own house; where he think their moral would escape all that
danger of be corrupt to which they would be unavoidably expose
in any public school or university.
have, therefore, determine to commit these boy to the tuition of a
private tutor, Mr Thwackum be recommend to he for that office, by
a very particular friend, of whose understanding Mr Allworthy have a
great opinion, and in whose integrity he place much confidence. this
Thwackum be fellow of a college, where he almost entirely reside;
and have a great reputation for learning, religion, and sobriety of
manner. and these be doubtless the qualification by which Mr
Allworthy's friend have be induce to recommend he; though indeed
this friend have some obligation to Thwackum's family, who be the
most considerable person in a borough which that gentleman
represent in parliament.
Thwackum, at his first arrival, be extremely agreeable to Allworthy;
and indeed he perfectly answer the character which have be give of
he. upon long acquaintance, however, and more intimate
conversation, this worthy man see infirmity in the tutor, which he
could have wish he to have be without; though as those seem
greatly overbalance by his good quality, they do not incline Mr
Allworthy to part with he: nor would they indeed have justify such
a proceeding; for the reader be greatly mistaken, if he conceive that
Thwackum appear to Mr Allworthy in the same light as he doth to he
in this history; and he be as much deceive, if he imagine that the
most intimate acquaintance which he himself could have have with that
divine, would have inform he of those thing which we, from our
inspiration, be enable to open and discover. of reader who, from
such conceit as these, condemn the wisdom or penetration of Mr
Allworthy, I shall not scruple to say, that they make a very bad and
ungrateful use of that knowledge which we have communicate to they.
these apparent error in the doctrine of Thwackum serve greatly to
palliate the contrary error in that of Square, which our good man no
less saw and condemn. he think, indeed, that the different
exuberancie of these gentleman would correct their different
imperfection; and that from both, especially with his assistance, the
two lad would derive sufficient precept of true religion and virtue.
if the event happen contrary to his expectation, this possibly
proceed from some fault in the plan itself; which the reader hath my
leave to discover, if he can: for we do not pretend to introduce any
infallible character into this history; where we hope nothing will be
find which hath never yet be see in human nature.
to return therefore: the reader will not, I think, wonder that the
different behaviour of the two lad above commemorate, produce the
different effect of which he hath already see some instance; and
besides this, there be another reason for the conduct of the
philosopher and the pedagogue; but this be matter of great
importance, we shall reveal it in the next chapter.
Chapter vi.
contain a well reason still for the before-mention opinion.
it be to be know then, that those two learn personage, who have
lately make a considerable figure on the theatre of this history, have,
from their first arrival at Mr Allworthy's house, take so great an
affection, the one to his virtue, the other to his religion, that they
have meditate the close alliance with he.
for this purpose they have cast their eye on that fair widow, whom,
though we have not for some time make any mention of she, the reader,
we trust, hath not forget. Mrs Blifil be indeed the object to which
they both aspire.
it may seem remarkable, that, of four person whom we have
commemorate at Mr Allworthy's house, three of they should fix their
inclination on a lady who be never greatly celebrate for her
beauty, and who be, moreover, now a little descend into the vale of
year; but in reality bosom friend, and intimate acquaintance, have a
kind of natural propensity to particular female at the house of a
friend--viz., to his grandmother, mother, sister, daughter, aunt,
niece, or cousin, when they be rich; and to his wife, sister,
daughter, niece, cousin, mistress, or servant-maid, if they should be
handsome.
we would not, however, have our reader imagine, that person of such
character as be support by Thwackum and Square, would undertake a
matter of this kind, which hath be a little censure by some rigid
moralist, before they have thoroughly examine it, and consider
whether it be (as Shakespear phrase it) "stuff o' th' conscience,"
or no. Thwackum be encourage to the undertaking by reflect that
to covet your neighbour's sister be nowhere forbidden: and he know it
be a rule in the construction of all law, that "_Expressum facit
cessare tacitum._" the sense of which be, "when a lawgiver set down
plainly his whole meaning, we be prevent from make he mean what
we please ourselves." as some instance of woman, therefore, be
mention in the divine law, which forbid we to covet our neighbour's
good, and that of a sister omit, he conclude it to be lawful. and
as to Square, who be in his person what be call a jolly fellow, or
a widow's man, he easily reconcile his choice to the eternal fitness
of thing.
now, as both of these gentleman be industrious in take every
opportunity of recommend themselves to the widow, they apprehend
one certain method be, by give her son the constant preference to
the other lad; and as they conceive the kindness and affection which
Mr Allworthy show the latter, must be highly disagreeable to she,
they doubt not but the lay hold on all occasion to degrade and
vilify he, would be highly pleasing to she; who, as she hat the
boy, must love all those who do he any hurt. in this Thwackum have
the advantage; for while Square could only scarify the poor lad's
reputation, he could flea his skin; and, indeed, he consider every
lash he give he as a compliment pay to his mistress; so that he
could, with the utmost propriety, repeat this old flog line,
_"Castigo te non quod odio habeam, se quod_ AMEM. I chastise thee not
out of hatred, but out of love." and this, indeed, he often have in his
mouth, or rather, accord to the old phrase, never more properly
apply, at his finger' end.
for this reason, principally, the two gentleman concur, as we have
see above, in their opinion concern the two lad; this being,
indeed, almost the only instance of their concur on any point;
for, beside the difference of their principle, they have both long ago
strongly suspect each other's design, and hat one another with no
little degree of inveteracy.
this mutual animosity be a good deal increase by their alternate
success; for Mrs Blifil know what they would be at long before they
imagine it; or, indeed, intend she should: for they proceed with
great caution, lest she should be offend, and acquaint Mr Allworthy.
but they have no reason for any such fear; she be well enough please
with a passion, of which she intend none should have any fruit but
herself. and the only fruit she design for herself be, flattery
and courtship; for which purpose she soothe they by turn, and a long
time equally. she be, indeed, rather inclined to favour the parson's
principle; but Square's person be more agreeable to her eye, for he
be a comely man; whereas the pedagogue do in countenance very nearly
resemble that gentleman, who, in the Harlot's progress, be see
correct the lady in Bridewell.
whether Mrs Blifil have be surfeit with the sweet of marriage, or
disgust by its bitter, or from what other cause it proceed, I
will not determine; but she could never be bring to listen to any
second proposal. however, she at last converse with Square with such
a degree of intimacy that malicious tongue begin to whisper thing of
she, to which, as well for the sake of the lady, as that they be
highly disagreeable to the rule of right and the fitness of thing, we
will give no credit, and therefore shall not blot our paper with they.
the pedagogue, 'tis certain, whip on, without get a step nearer
to his journey's end.
indeed he have commit a great error, and that Square discover much
soon than himself. Mrs Blifil (as, perhaps, the reader may have
formerly guess) be not over and above pleased with the behaviour of
her husband; nay, to be honest, she absolutely hat he, till his
death at last a little reconcile he to her affection. it will not
be therefore greatly wonder at, if she have not the most violent
regard to the offspring she have by he. and, in fact, she have so
little of this regard, that in his infancy she seldom see her son, or
take any notice of he; and hence she acquiesce, after a little
reluctance, in all the favour which Mr Allworthy shower on the
foundling; whom the good man call his own boy, and in all thing put
on an entire equality with Master Blifil. this acquiescence in Mrs
Blifil be consider by the neighbour, and by the family, as a mark
of her condescension to her brother's humour, and she be imagine by
all other, as well as Thwackum and Square, to hate the foundling in
her heart; nay, the more civility she show he, the more they
conceive she detest he, and the surer scheme she be lay for
his ruin: for as they think it her interest to hate he, it be very
difficult for she to persuade they she do not.
Thwackum be the more confirmed in his opinion, as she have more than
once slily cause he to whip Tom Jones, when Mr Allworthy, who be an
enemy to this exercise, be abroad; whereas she have never give any
such order concern young Blifil. and this have likewise impose
upon Square. in reality, though she certainly hat her own son--of
which, however monstrous it appear, I be assure she be not a
singular instance--she appear, notwithstanding all her outward
compliance, to be in her heart sufficiently displease with all the
favour show by Mr Allworthy to the foundling. she frequently
complain of this behind her brother's back, and very sharply
censure he for it, both to Thwackum and Square; nay, she would throw
it in the tooth of Allworthy himself, when a little quarrel, or miff,
as it be vulgarly call, arise between they.
however, when Tom grow up, and give token of that gallantry of temper
which greatly recommend man to woman, this disinclination which she
have discover to he when a child, by degree abate, and at last she
so evidently demonstrate her affection to he to be much strong
than what she bear her own son, that it be impossible to mistake she
any long. she be so desirous of often see he, and discover
such satisfaction and delight in his company, that before he be
eighteen year old he be become a rival to both Square and Thwackum;
and what be bad, the whole country begin to talk as loudly of her
inclination to Tom, as they have before do of that which she have
show to Square: on which account the philosopher conceive the most
implacable hatred for our poor heroe.
Chapter vii.
in which the author himself make his appearance on the stage.
though Mr Allworthy be not of himself hasty to see thing in a
disadvantageous light, and be a stranger to the public voice, which
seldom reach to a brother or a husband, though it ring in the ear
of all the neighbourhood; yet be this affection of Mrs Blifil to Tom,
and the preference which she too visibly give he to her own son, of
the utmost disadvantage to that youth.
for such be the compassion which inhabit Mr Allworthy's mind, that
nothing but the steel of justice could ever subdue it. to be
unfortunate in any respect be sufficient, if there be no demerit to
counterpoise it, to turn the scale of that good man's pity, and to
engage his friendship and his benefaction.
when therefore he plainly see Master Blifil be absolutely detest
(for that he be) by his own mother, he begin, on that account only,
to look with an eye of compassion upon he; and what the effect of
compassion be, in good and benevolent mind, I need not here explain
to most of my reader.
Henceforward he see every appearance of virtue in the youth through
the magnifying end, and view all his fault with the glass invert,
so that they become scarce perceptible. and this perhaps the amiable
temper of pity may make commendable; but the next step the weakness of
human nature alone must excuse; for he no soon perceive that
preference which Mrs Blifil give to Tom, than that poor youth (however
innocent) begin to sink in his affection as he rise in her. this, it
be true, would of itself alone never have be able to eradicate Jones
from his bosom; but it be greatly injurious to he, and prepare Mr
Allworthy's mind for those impression which afterwards produce the
mighty event that will be contain hereafter in this history; and to
which, it must be confest, the unfortunate lad, by his own wantonness,
wildness, and want of caution, too much contribute.
in record some instance of these, we shall, if rightly understand,
afford a very useful lesson to those well-dispose youth who shall
hereafter be our reader; for they may here find, that goodness of
heart, and openness of temper, though these may give they great
comfort within, and administer to an honest pride in their own mind,
will by no mean, alas! do their business in the world. Prudence and
circumspection be necessary even to the good of man. they be indeed,
as it be, a guard to Virtue, without which she can never be safe. it
be not enough that your design, nay, that your action, be
intrinsically good; you must take care they shall appear so. if your
inside be never so beautiful, you must preserve a fair outside also.
this must be constantly look to, or malice and envy will take care
to blacken it so, that the sagacity and goodness of an Allworthy will
not be able to see through it, and to discern the beauty within. let
this, my young reader, be your constant maxim, that no man can be
good enough to enable he to neglect the rule of prudence; nor will
Virtue herself look beautiful, unless she be bedeck with the outward
ornament of decency and decorum. and this precept, my worthy
disciple, if you read with due attention, you will, I hope, find
sufficiently enforce by example in the follow page.
I ask pardon for this short appearance, by way of chorus, on the
stage. it be in reality for my own sake, that, while I be discover
the rock on which innocence and goodness often split, I may not be
misunderstood to recommend the very mean to my worthy reader, by
which I intend to show they they will be undo. and this, as I could
not prevail on any of my actor to speak, I myself be oblige to
declare.
Chapter viii.
a childish incident, in which, however, be see a good-natured
disposition in Tom Jones.
the reader may remember that Mr Allworthy give Tom Jones a little
horse, as a kind of smart-money for the punishment which he imagine
he have suffer innocently.
this horse Tom keep above half a year, and then ride he to a
neighbouring fair, and sell he.
at his return, be question by Thwackum what he have do with the
money for which the horse be sell, he frankly declare he would not
tell he.
"Oho!" say Thwackum, "you will not! then I will have it out of your
br--h;" that be the place to which he always apply for
information on every doubtful occasion.
Tom be now mount on the back of a footman, and everything prepare
for execution, when Mr Allworthy, enter the room, give the criminal
a reprieve, and take he with he into another apartment; where, be
alone with Tom, he put the same question to he which Thwackum have
before ask he.
Tom answer, he could in duty refuse he nothing; but as for that
tyrannical rascal, he would never make he any other answer than with
a cudgel, with which he hop soon to be able to pay he for all his
barbarity.
Mr Allworthy very severely reprimand the lad for his indecent and
disrespectful expression concern his master; but much more for his
avow an intention of revenge. he threaten he with the entire
loss of his favour, if he ever hear such another word from his mouth;
for, he say, he would never support or befriend a reprobate. by these
and the like declaration, he extort some compunction from Tom, in
which that youth be not over-sincere; for he really meditate some
return for all the smarting favour he have receive at the hand of
the pedagogue. he be, however, bring by Mr Allworthy to express a
concern for his resentment against Thwackum; and then the good man,
after some wholesome admonition, permit he to proceed, which he
do as follows:--
"indeed, my dear sir, I love and honour you more than all the world: I
know the great obligation I have to you, and should detest myself if
I think my heart be capable of ingratitude. could the little horse
you give I speak, I be sure he could tell you how fond I be of your
present; for I have more pleasure in feed he than in rid he.
indeed, sir, it go to my heart to part with he; nor would I have
sell he upon any other account in the world than what I do. you
yourself, sir, I be convince, in my case, would have do the same:
for none ever so sensibly feel the misfortune of other. what would
you feel, dear sir, if you think yourself the occasion of they?
indeed, sir, there never be any misery like theirs."
"like whose, child?" say Allworthy: "what do you mean?"
"oh, sir!" answer Tom, "your poor gamekeeper, with all his large
family, ever since your discard he, have be perish with all
the misery of cold and hunger: I could not bear to see these poor
wretch naked and starve, and at the same time know myself to have
be the occasion of all their suffering. I could not bear it, sir;
upon my soul, I could not." [here the tear run down his cheek, and
he thus proceed.] "it be to save they from absolute destruction I
part with your dear present, notwithstanding all the value I have for
it: I sell the horse for they, and they have every farthing of the
money."
Mr Allworthy now stand silent for some moment, and before he speak
the tear start from his eye. he at length dismiss Tom with a
gentle rebuke, advise he for the future to apply to he in case of
distress, rather than to use extraordinary mean of relieve they
himself.
this affair be afterwards the subject of much debate between Thwackum
and Square. Thwackum hold, that this be fly in Mr Allworthy's
face, who have intend to punish the fellow for his disobedience. he
say, in some instance, what the world call charity appear to he
to be oppose the will of the Almighty, which have mark some
particular person for destruction; and that this be in like manner
act in opposition to Mr Allworthy; concluding, as usual, with a
hearty recommendation of birch.
Square argue strongly on the other side, in opposition perhaps to
Thwackum, or in compliance with Mr Allworthy, who seem very much to
approve what Jones have do. as to what he urge on this occasion, as
I be convince most of my reader will be much abler advocate for
poor Jones, it would be impertinent to relate it. indeed it be not
difficult to reconcile to the rule of right an action which it would
have be impossible to deduce from the rule of wrong.
chapter ix.
contain an incident of a more heinous kind, with the comment of
Thwackum and Square.
it hath be observe by some man of much great reputation for
wisdom than myself, that misfortune seldom come single. an instance
of this may, I believe, be see in those gentleman who have the
misfortune to have any of their roguery detect; for here discovery
seldom stop till the whole be come out. thus it happen to poor Tom;
who be no soon pardon for sell the horse, than he be
discover to have some time before sell a fine Bible which Mr
Allworthy give he, the money arise from which sale he have dispose
of in the same manner. this Bible Master Blifil have purchase, though
he have already such another of his own, partly out of respect for the
book, and partly out of friendship to Tom, be unwilling that the
Bible should be sell out of the family at half-price. he therefore
deposit the say half-price himself; for he be a very prudent lad,
and so careful of his money, that he have lay up almost every penny
which he have receive from Mr Allworthy.
some people have be note to be able to read in no book but their
own. on the contrary, from the time when Master Blifil be first
possess of this Bible, he never use any other. Nay, he be see
read in it much oftener than he have before be in his own. now, as
he frequently ask Thwackum to explain difficult passage to he,
that gentleman unfortunately take notice of Tom's name, which be
write in many part of the book. this bring on an inquiry, which
oblige Master Blifil to discover the whole matter.
Thwackum be resolve a crime of this kind, which he call sacrilege,
should not go unpunished. he therefore proceed immediately to
castigation: and not content with that he acquaint Mr Allworthy,
at their next meeting, with this monstrous crime, as it appear to
he: inveigh against Tom in the most bitter term, and liken he
to the buyer and seller who be drive out of the temple.
Square see this matter in a very different light. he say, he could
not perceive any high crime in sell one book than in sell
another. that to sell Bibles be strictly lawful by all law both
Divine and human, and consequently there be no unfitness in it. he
tell Thwackum, that his great concern on this occasion bring to his
mind the story of a very devout woman, who, out of pure regard to
religion, steal Tillotson's sermon from a lady of her acquaintance.
this story cause a vast quantity of blood to rush into the parson's
face, which of itself be none of the pale; and he be go to
reply with great warmth and anger, have not Mrs Blifil, who be present
at this debate, interpose. that lady declare herself absolutely of
Mr Square's side. she argue, indeed, very learnedly in support of his
opinion; and conclude with say, if Tom have be guilty of any
fault, she must confess her own son appear to be equally culpable;
for that she could see no difference between the buyer and the seller;
both of whom be alike to be drive out of the temple.
Mrs Blifil have declare her opinion, put an end to the debate.
Square's triumph would almost have stopt his word, have he need
they; and Thwackum, who, for reason before-mention, durst not
venture at disoblige the lady, be almost choake with indignation.
as to Mr Allworthy, he say, since the boy have be already punish
he would not deliver his sentiment on the occasion; and whether he
be or be not angry with the lad, I must leave to the reader's own
conjecture.
soon after this, an action be bring against the gamekeeper by
Squire Western (the gentleman in whose manor the partridge be
kill), for depredation of the like kind. this be a most
unfortunate circumstance for the fellow, as it not only of itself
threaten his ruin, but actually prevent Mr Allworthy from
restore he to his favour: for as that gentleman be walk out one
evening with Master Blifil and young Jones, the latter slily draw he
to the habitation of Black George; where the family of that poor
wretch, namely, his wife and child, be find in all the misery
with which cold, hunger, and nakedness, can affect human creature:
for as to the money they have receive from Jones, former debt have
consume almost the whole.
such a scene as this could not fail of affect the heart of Mr
Allworthy. he immediately give the mother a couple of guineas, with
which he bid her cloath her child. the poor woman burst into tear
at this goodness, and while she be thank he, could not refrain
from express her gratitude to Tom; who have, she say, long
preserve both she and her from starve. "we have not," say she,
"have a morsel to eat, nor have these poor child have a rag to put
on, but what his goodness hath bestow on we." for, indeed, besides
the horse and the Bible, Tom have sacrifice a night-gown, and other
thing, to the use of this distressed family.
on their return home, Tom make use of all his eloquence to display the
wretchedness of these people, and the penitence of Black George
himself; and in this he succeed so well, that Mr Allworthy say, he
think the man have suffer enough for what be past; that he would
forgive he, and think of some mean of provide for he and his
family.
Jones be so delighted with this news, that, though it be dark when
they return home, he could not help go back a mile, in a shower
of rain, to acquaint the poor woman with the glad tiding; but, like
other hasty divulger of news, he only bring on himself the trouble
of contradict it: for the ill fortune of Black George make use of
the very opportunity of his friend's absence to overturn all again.
Chapter x.
in which Master Blifil and Jones appear in different light.
Master Blifil fall very short of his companion in the amiable quality
of mercy; but he as greatly exceed he in one of a much high kind,
namely, in justice: in which he follow both the precept and example
of Thwackum and Square; for though they would both make frequent use
of the word mercy, yet it be plain that in reality Square hold it to
be inconsistent with the rule of right; and Thwackum be for do
justice, and leave mercy to heaven. the two gentleman do indeed
somewhat differ in opinion concern the object of this sublime
virtue; by which Thwackum would probably have destroy one half of
mankind, and Square the other half.
Master Blifil then, though he have keep silence in the presence of
Jones, yet, when he have well consider the matter, could by no
mean endure the thought of suffer his uncle to confer favour on
the undeserving. he therefore resolve immediately to acquaint he
with the fact which we have above slightly hint to the reader. the
truth of which be as follow:
the gamekeeper, about a year after he be dismiss from Mr
Allworthy's service, and before Tom's sell the horse, be in want
of bread, either to fill his own mouth or those of his family, as he
pass through a field belong to Mr Western espy a hare sit
in her form. this hare he have basely and barbarously knock on the
head, against the law of the land, and no less against the law of
sportsman.
the higgler to whom the hare be sell, be unfortunately take many
month after with a quantity of game upon he, be oblige to make his
peace with the squire, by become evidence against some poacher. and
now Black George be pitch upon by he, as be a person already
obnoxious to Mr Western, and one of no good fame in the country. he
be, besides, the good sacrifice the higgler could make, as he have
supply he with no game since; and by this mean the witness have an
opportunity of screen his well customer: for the squire, be
charm with the power of punish Black George, whom a single
transgression be sufficient to ruin, make no further enquiry.
have this fact be truly lay before Mr Allworthy, it might probably
have do the gamekeeper very little mischief. but there be no zeal
blinder than that which be inspire with the love of justice against
offender. Master Blifil have forget the distance of the time. he
vary likewise in the manner of the fact: and by the hasty addition
of the single letter s he considerably alter the story; for he say
that George have wire hare. these alteration might probably have
be set right, have not Master Blifil unluckily insist on a promise
of secrecy from Mr Allworthy before he reveal the matter to he; but
by that mean the poor gamekeeper be condemn without have an
opportunity to defend himself: for as the fact of kill the hare,
and of the action bring, be certainly true, Mr Allworthy have no
doubt concern the rest.
Short-live then be the joy of these poor people; for Mr Allworthy
the next morning declare he have fresh reason, without assign it,
for his anger, and strictly forbad Tom to mention George any more:
though as for his family, he say he would endeavour to keep they from
starve; but as to the fellow himself, he would leave he to the
law, which nothing could keep he from break.
Tom could by no mean divine what have incense Mr Allworthy, for of
Master Blifil he have not the least suspicion. however, as his
friendship be to be tired out by no disappointment, he now
determine to try another method of preserve the poor gamekeeper
from ruin.
Jones be lately grow very intimate with Mr Western. he have so
greatly recommend himself to that gentleman, by leap over
five-bar gate, and by other act of sportsmanship, that the squire
have declare Tom would certainly make a great man if he have but
sufficient encouragement. he often wish he have himself a son with
such part; and one day very solemnly assert at a drinking bout,
that Tom should hunt a pack of hound for a thousand pound of his
money, with any huntsman in the whole country.
by such kind of talent he have so ingratiate himself with the squire,
that he be a most welcome guest at his table, and a favourite
companion in his sport: everything which the squire hold most dear, to
wit, his gun, dog, and horse, be now as much at the command of
Jones, as if they have be his own. he resolve therefore to make use
of this favour on behalf of his friend Black George, whom he hop to
introduce into Mr Western's family, in the same capacity in which he
have before serve Mr Allworthy.
the reader, if he consider that this fellow be already obnoxious to
Mr Western, and if he consider far the weighty business by which
that gentleman's displeasure have be incur, will perhaps condemn
this as a foolish and desperate undertaking; but if he should totally
condemn young Jones on that account, he will greatly applaud he for
strengthen himself with all imaginable interest on so arduous an
occasion.
for this purpose, then, Tom apply to Mr Western's daughter, a young
lady of about seventeen year of age, whom her father, next after
those necessary implement of sport just before mention, love and
esteem above all the world. now, as she have some influence on the
squire, so Tom have some little influence on she. but this be the
intend heroine of this work, a lady with whom we ourselves be
greatly in love, and with whom many of our reader will probably be in
love too, before we part, it be by no mean proper she should make she
appearance at the end of a book.
BOOK IV.
contain the TIME of a year.
Chapter i.
contain five page of paper.
as truth distinguish our writing from those idle romance which be
fill with monster, the production, not of nature, but of
distemper brain; and which have be therefore recommend by an
eminent critic to the sole use of the pastry-cook; so, on the other
hand, we would avoid any resemblance to that kind of history which a
celebrate poet seem to think be no less calculated for the emolument
of the brewer, as the reading it should be always attend with a
tankard of good ale--
While--history with her comrade ale,
soothe the sad series of her serious tale
for as this be the liquor of modern historian, nay, perhaps their
muse, if we may believe the opinion of Butler, who attribute
inspiration to ale, it ought likewise to be the potation of their
reader, since every book ought to be read with the same spirit and in
the same manner as it be writ. thus the famous author of Hurlothrumbo
tell a learn bishop, that the reason his lordship could not taste
the excellence of his piece be, that he do not read it with a fiddle
in his hand; which instrument he himself have always have in his own,
when he compose it.
that our work, therefore, might be in no danger of be liken to
the labour of these historian, we have take every occasion of
intersperse through the whole sundry simile, description, and
other kind of poetical embellishment. these be, indeed, design to
supply the place of the said ale, and to refresh the mind, whenever
those slumber, which in a long work be apt to invade the reader as
well as the writer, shall begin to creep upon he. without
interruption of this kind, the good narrative of plain matter of fact
must overpower every reader; for nothing but the ever last
watchfulness, which Homer have ascribe only to Jove himself, can be
proof against a newspaper of many volume.
we shall leave to the reader to determine with what judgment we have
choose the several occasion for insert those ornamental part of
our work. surely it will be allow that none could be more proper
than the present, where we be about to introduce a considerable
character on the scene; no less, indeed, than the heroine of this
heroic, historical, prosaic poem. here, therefore, we have think
proper to prepare the mind of the reader for her reception, by fill
it with every pleasing image which we can draw from the face of
nature. and for this method we plead many precedent. first, this be
an art well know to, and much practise by, our tragick poet, who
seldom fail to prepare their audience for the reception of their
principal character.
thus the heroe be always introduce with a flourish of drum and
trumpet, in order to rouse a martial spirit in the audience, and to
accommodate their ear to bombast and fustian, which Mr Locke's blind
man would not have grossly err in liken to the sound of a
trumpet. again, when lover be come forth, soft music often
conduct they on the stage, either to soothe the audience with the
softness of the tender passion, or to lull and prepare they for that
gentle slumber in which they will most probably be compose by the
ensue scene.
and not only the poet, but the master of these poet, the manager
of playhouse, seem to be in this secret; for, besides the aforesaid
kettle-drum, &c., which denote the heroe's approach, he be generally
usher on the stage by a large troop of half a dozen scene-shifter;
and how necessary these be imagine to his appearance, may be
conclude from the follow theatrical story:--
King Pyrrhus be at dinner at an ale-house border on the theatre,
when he be summon to go on the stage. the heroe, be unwilling to
quit his shoulder of mutton, and as unwilling to draw on himself the
indignation of Mr Wilks (his brother-manager) for make the audience
wait, have bribe these his harbinger to be out of the way. while Mr
Wilks, therefore, be thunder out, "where be the carpenter to
walk on before King Pyrrhus?" that monarch very quietly eat his
mutton, and the audience, however impatient, be oblige to entertain
themselves with music in his absence.
to be plain, I much question whether the politician, who hath
generally a good nose, hath not scent out somewhat of the utility of
this practice. I be convinced that awful magistrate my lord-mayor
contract a good deal of that reverence which attend he through the
year, by the several pageant which precede his pomp. Nay, I must
confess, that even I myself, who be not remarkably liable to be
captivate with show, have yield not a little to the impression of
much precede state. when I have see a man strut in a
procession, after other whose business be only to walk before he, I
have conceive a high notion of his dignity than I have feel on
see he in a common situation. but there be one instance, which
come exactly up to my purpose. this be the custom of send on a
basket-woman, who be to precede the pomp at a coronation, and to strew
the stage with flower, before the great personage begin their
procession. the antient would certainly have invoke the goddess
Flora for this purpose, and it would have be no difficulty for their
priest, or politician to have persuade the people of the real
presence of the deity, though a plain mortal have personate she and
perform her office. but we have no such design of impose on our
reader; and therefore those who object to the heathen theology, may,
if they please, change our goddess into the above-mention
basket-woman. our intention, in short, be to introduce our heroine
with the utmost solemnity in our power, with an elevation of stile,
and all other circumstance proper to raise the veneration of our
reader.--indeed we would, for certain cause, advise those of our male
reader who have any heart, to read no far, be we not well
assure, that how amiable soever the picture of our heroine will
appear, as it be really a copy from nature, many of our fair
countrywoman will be find worthy to satisfy any passion, and to
answer any idea of female perfection which our pencil will be able to
raise.
and now, without any further preface, we proceed to our next chapter.
Chapter ii.
a short hint of what we can do in the sublime, and a description of
Miss Sophia Western.
hush be every ruder breath. may the heathen ruler of the wind
confine in iron chain the boisterous limb of noisy Boreas, and the
sharp-pointed nose of bitter-biting Eurus. do thou, sweet Zephyrus,
rise from thy fragrant bed, mount the western sky, and lead on those
delicious gale, the charm of which call forth the lovely Flora from
her chamber, perfume with pearly dew, when on the 1st of June, her
birth-day, the bloom maid, in loose attire, gently trip it over
the verdant mead, where every flower rise to do her homage, till the
whole field become enamel, and colour contend with sweet which
shall ravish she most.
so charming may she now appear! and you the feather chorister of
nature, whose sweetest note not even Handel can excell, tune your
melodious throat to celebrate her appearance. from love proceed your
music, and to love it return. Awaken therefore that gentle passion in
every swain: for lo! adorn with all the charm in which nature can
array she; bedeck with beauty, youth, sprightliness, innocence,
modesty, and tenderness, breathe sweetness from her rosy lip, and
dart brightness from her sparkling eye, the lovely Sophia come!
Reader, perhaps thou hast see the statue of the _Venus de Medicis_.
perhaps, too, thou hast see the gallery of beauty at Hampton Court.
Thou may'st remember each bright Churchill of the galaxy, and all the
toast of the Kit-cat. or, if their reign be before thy time, at
least thou hast see their daughter, the no less dazzling beauty of
the present age; whose name, should we here insert, we apprehend they
would fill the whole volume.
now if thou hast see all these, be not afraid of the rude answer
which Lord Rochester once give to a man who have see many thing. no.
if thou hast see all these without know what beauty be, thou hast
no eye; if without feel its power, thou hast no heart.
yet be it possible, my friend, that thou mayest have see all these
without be able to form an exact idea of Sophia; for she do not
exactly resemble any of they. she be most like the picture of Lady
Ranelagh: and, I have hear, more still to the famous dutchess of
Mazarine; but most of all she resemble one whose image never can
depart from my breast, and whom, if thou dost remember, thou hast
then, my friend, an adequate idea of Sophia.
but lest this should not have be thy fortune, we will endeavour with
our utmost skill to describe this paragon, though we be sensible that
our high ability be very inadequate to the task.
Sophia, then, the only daughter of Mr Western, be a middle-sized
woman; but rather incline to tall. her shape be not only exact, but
extremely delicate: and the nice proportion of her arm promise the
true symmetry in her limb. her hair, which be black, be so
luxuriant, that it reach she middle, before she cut it to comply
with the modern fashion; and it be now curl so gracefully in her
neck, that few could believe it to be her own. if envy could find any
part of the face which demand less commendation than the rest, it
might possibly think her forehead might have be high without
prejudice to she. her eyebrow be full, even, and arch beyond the
power of art to imitate. her black eye have a lustre in they, which
all her softness could not extinguish. her nose be exactly regular,
and her mouth, in which be two row of ivory, exactly answer Sir
John Suckling's description in those lines:--
her lip be red, and one be thin,
Compar'd to that be next her chin.
some bee have sting it newly.
her cheek be of the oval kind; and in her right she have a dimple,
which the least smile discover. her chin have certainly its share in
form the beauty of her face; but it be difficult to say it be
either large or small, though perhaps it be rather of the former
kind. her complexion have rather more of the lily than of the rose; but
when exercise or modesty increase her natural colour, no vermilion
could equal it. then one might indeed cry out with the celebrated Dr
Donne:
--her pure and eloquent blood
Spoke in her cheek, and so distinctly work
that one might almost say her body think.
her neck be long and finely turn: and here, if I be not afraid of
offend her delicacy, I might justly say, the high beauty of
the famous _Venus de Medicis_ be outdo. here be whiteness which
no lily, ivory, nor alabaster could match. the fine cambric might
indeed be suppose from envy to cover that bosom which be much whiter
than itself.--It be indeed,
_nitor splenden Pario marmore purius_.
a gloss shin beyond the pure brightness of parian marble.
such be the outside of Sophia; nor be this beautiful frame disgrace
by an inhabitant unworthy of it. her mind be every way equal to her
person; nay, the latter borrow some charm from the former; for when
she smile, the sweetness of her temper diffuse that glory over her
countenance which no regularity of feature can give. but as there be
no perfection of the mind which do not discover themselves in that
perfect intimacy to which we intend to introduce our reader with this
charming young creature, so it be needless to mention they here: nay,
it be a kind of tacit affront to our reader's understanding, and may
also rob he of that pleasure which he will receive in form his own
judgment of her character.
it may, however, be proper to say, that whatever mental
accomplishment she have derive from nature, they be somewhat
improve and cultivate by art: for she have be educate under the
care of an aunt, who be a lady of great discretion, and be
thoroughly acquaint with the world, have live in her youth about
the court, whence she have retire some year since into the country.
by her conversation and instruction, Sophia be perfectly well breed,
though perhaps she want a little of that ease in her behaviour which
be to be acquire only by habit, and live within what be call the
polite circle. but this, to say the truth, be often too dearly
purchase; and though it hath charm so inexpressible, that the
French, perhaps, among other quality, mean to express this, when
they declare they know not what it be; yet its absence be well
compensate by innocence; nor can good sense and a natural gentility
ever stand in need of it.
Chapter iii.
wherein the history go back to commemorate a trifling incident that
happen some year since; but which, trifle as it be, have some
future consequence.
the amiable Sophia be now in her eighteenth year, when she be
introduce into this history. her father, as hath be say, be
fonder of she than of any other human creature. to she, therefore, Tom
Jones apply, in order to engage her interest on the behalf of his
friend the gamekeeper.
but before we proceed to this business, a short recapitulation of some
previous matter may be necessary.
though the different temper of Mr Allworthy and of Mr Western do not
admit of a very intimate correspondence, yet they live upon what be
call a decent footing together; by which mean the young people of
both family have be acquaint from their infancy; and as they be
all near of the same age, have be frequent playmate together.
the gaiety of Tom's temper suit well with Sophia, than the grave
and sober disposition of Master Blifil. and the preference which she
give the former of these, would often appear so plainly, that a lad of
a more passionate turn than Master Blifil be, might have show some
displeasure at it.
as he do not, however, outwardly express any such disgust, it would
be an ill office in we to pay a visit to the inmost recess of his
mind, as some scandalous people search into the most secret affair of
their friend, and often pry into their closet and cupboard, only to
discover their poverty and meanness to the world.
however, as person who suspect they have give other cause of
offence, be apt to conclude they be offend; so Sophia impute an
action of Master Blifil to his anger, which the superior sagacity of
Thwackum and Square discern to have arise from a much well
principle.
Tom Jones, when very young, have present Sophia with a little bird,
which he have take from the nest, have nurse up, and teach to sing.
of this bird, Sophia, then about thirteen year old, be so extremely
fond, that her chief business be to feed and tend it, and her chief
pleasure to play with it. by these mean little Tommy, for so the bird
