be call, be become so tame, that it would feed out of the hand of
its mistress, would perch upon the finger, and lie content in her
bosom, where it seem almost sensible of its own happiness; though
she always keep a small string about its leg, nor would ever trust it
with the liberty of fly away.
one day, when Mr Allworthy and his whole family din at Mr Western's,
Master Blifil, be in the garden with little Sophia, and observe
the extreme fondness that she show for her little bird, desire she
to trust it for a moment in his hand. Sophia presently comply with
the young gentleman's request, and after some previous caution,
deliver he her bird; of which he be no soon in possession, than
he slipt the string from its leg and toss it into the air.
the foolish animal no soon perceive itself at liberty, than
forget all the favour it have receive from Sophia, it fly
directly from she, and perch on a bough at some distance.
Sophia, see her bird go, scream out so loud, that Tom Jones,
who be at a little distance, immediately run to her assistance.
he be no soon informed of what have happen, than he curse Blifil
for a pitiful malicious rascal; and then immediately strip off his
coat he apply himself to climb the tree to which the bird
escape.
Tom have almost recover his little namesake, when the branch on which
it be perch, and that hang over a canal, break, and the poor lad
plump over head and ear into the water.
Sophia's concern now change its object. and as she apprehend the
boy's life be in danger, she scream ten time loud than before;
and indeed Master Blifil himself now second she with all the
vociferation in his power.
the company, who be sit in a room next the garden, be
instantly alarm, and come all forth; but just as they reach the
canal, Tom (for the water be luckily pretty shallow in that part)
arrive safely on shore.
Thwackum fall violently on poor Tom, who stand drop and shiver
before he, when Mr Allworthy desire he to have patience; and
turn to Master Blifil, say, "pray, child, what be the reason of
all this disturbance?"
Master Blifil answer, "indeed, uncle, I be very sorry for what I
have do; I have be unhappily the occasion of it all. I have Miss
Sophia's bird in my hand, and think the poor creature languish
for liberty, I own I could not forbear give it what it desire; for
I always think there be something very cruel in confine anything.
it seem to be against the law of nature, by which everything hath a
right to liberty; nay, it be even unchristian, for it be not do
what we would be do by; but if I have imagine Miss Sophia would have
be so much concerned at it, I be sure I never would have do it;
nay, if I have know what would have happen to the bird itself: for
when Master Jones, who climb up that tree after it, fall into the
water, the bird take a second flight, and presently a nasty hawk
carry it away."
Poor Sophia, who now first hear of her little Tommy's fate (for her
concern for Jones have prevent her perceive it when it happen),
shed a shower of tear. these Mr Allworthy endeavour to assuage,
promise she a much fine bird: but she declare she would never have
another. her father chide she for cry so for a foolish bird; but
could not help tell young Blifil, if he be a son of his, his
backside should be well flead.
Sophia now return to her chamber, the two young gentleman be send
home, and the rest of the company return to their bottle; where a
conversation ensue on the subject of the bird, so curious, that we
think it deserve a chapter by itself.
chapter iv.
contain such very deep and grave matter, that some reader,
perhaps, may not relish it.
Square have no soon light his pipe, than, address himself to
allworthy, he thus begin: "Sir, I cannot help congratulate you on
your nephew; who, at an age when few lad have any idea but of
sensible object, be arrive at a capacity of distinguish right
from wrong. to confine anything, seem to I against the law of
nature, by which everything hath a right to liberty. these be his
word; and the impression they have make on I be never to be
eradicate. can any man have a high notion of the rule of right, and
the eternal fitness of thing? I cannot help promise myself, from
such a dawn, that the meridian of this youth will be equal to that of
either the elder or the young Brutus."
here Thwackum hastily interrupt, and spill some of his wine, and
swallow the rest with great eagerness, answer, "from another
expression he make use of, I hope he will resemble much well man.
the law of nature be a jargon of word, which mean nothing. I know
not of any such law, nor of any right which can be derive from it. to
do as we would be do by, be indeed a christian motive, as the boy
well express himself; and I be glad to find my instruction have
bear such good fruit."
"if vanity be a thing fit," say Square, "I might indulge some on the
same occasion; for whence only he can have learn his notion of right
or wrong, I think be pretty apparent. if there be no law of nature,
there be no right nor wrong."
"how!" say the parson, "do you then banish revelation? be I talk
with a deist or an atheist?"
"drink about," say western. "pox of your law of nature! I donot know
what you mean, either of you, by right and wrong. to take away my
girl's bird be wrong, in my opinion; and my neighbour Allworthy may
do as he please; but to encourage boy in such practice, be to breed
they up to the gallow."
Allworthy answer, "that he be sorry for what his nephew have do,
but could not consent to punish he, as he act rather from a
generous than unworthy motive." he say, "if the boy have steal the
bird, none would have be more ready to vote for a severe
chastisement than himself; but it be plain that be not his design:"
and, indeed, it be as apparent to he, that he could have no other
view but what he have himself avow. (for as to that malicious purpose
which Sophia suspect, it never once enter into the head of Mr
Allworthy.) he at length conclude with again blame the action as
inconsiderate, and which, he say, be pardonable only in a child.
Square have deliver his opinion so openly, that if he be now silent,
he must submit to have his judgment censure. he say, therefore, with
some warmth, "that Mr Allworthy have too much respect to the dirty
consideration of property. that in pass our judgment on great and
mighty action, all private regard should be lay aside; for by
adhere to those narrow rule, the young Brutus have be condemn
of ingratitude, and the elder of parricide."
"and if they have be hang too for those crime," cry Thwackum,
"they would have have no more than their desert. a couple of
heathenish villain! Heaven be praise we have no brutus now-a-day!
I wish, Mr Square, you would desist from fill the mind of my
pupil with such antichristian stuff; for the consequence must be,
while they be under my care, its being well scourge out of they
again. there be your disciple Tom almost spoil already. I overhear
he the other day dispute with Master Blifil that there be no merit
in faith without work. I know that be one of your tenet, and I
suppose he have it from you."
"donot accuse I of spoil he," say Square. "who teach he to
laugh at whatever be virtuous and decent, and fit and right in the
nature of thing? he be your own scholar, and I disclaim he. no, no,
Master Blifil be my boy. young as he be, that lad's notion of moral
rectitude I defy you ever to eradicate."
Thwackum put on a contemptuous sneer at this, and reply, "ay, ay, I
will venture he with you. he be too well ground for all your
philosophical cannot to hurt. no, no, I have take care to instil such
principle into him--"
"and I have instill principle into he too," cry Square. "what
but the sublime idea of virtue could inspire a human mind with the
generous thought of give liberty? and I repeat to you again, if it
be a fit thing to be proud, I might claim the honour of have
infuse that idea."--
"and if pride be not forbid," say Thwackum, "I might boast of
have teach he that duty which he himself assign as his motive."
"so between you both," say the squire, "the young gentleman hath be
teach to rob my daughter of her bird. I find I must take care of my
partridge-mew. I shall have some virtuous religious man or other set
all my partridge at liberty." then slap a gentleman of the law,
who be present, on the back, he cry out, "what say you to this, Mr
Counsellor? be not this against law?"
the lawyer with great gravity deliver himself as follows:--
"if the case be put of a partridge, there can be no doubt but an
action would lie; for though this be _ferae naturae_, yet be
reclaim, property vest: but be the case of a singing bird,
though reclaim, as it be a thing of base nature, it must be
consider as _nullius in bonis_. in this case, therefore, I conceive
the plaintiff must be non-suited; and I should disadvise the bringing
any such action."
"well," say the squire, "if it be _nullus bonus_, let we drink about,
and talk a little of the state of the nation, or some such discourse
that we all understand; for I be sure I donot understand a word of
this. it may be learn and sense for aught I know: but you shall
never persuade I into it. pox! you have neither of you mention a
word of that poor lad who deserve to be commend: to venture
break his neck to oblige my girl be a generous-spirited action: I
have learn enough to see that. D--n I, herebe Tom's health! I
shall love the boy for it the long day I have to live."
thus be the debate interrupt; but it would probably have be soon
resume, have not Mr Allworthy presently call for his coach, and
carry off the two combatant.
such be the conclusion of this adventure of the bird, and of the
dialogue occasion by it; which we could not help recount to our
reader, though it happen some year before that stage or period of
time at which our history be now arrive.
Chapter v.
contain matter accommodate to every taste.
"Parva leve capiunt animos--small thing affect light mind," be the
sentiment of a great master of the passion of love. and certain it be,
that from this day Sophia begin to have some little kindness for Tom
Jones, and no little aversion for his companion.
many accident from time to time improve both these passion in her
breast; which, without our recounting, the reader may well conclude,
from what we have before hint of the different temper of these
lad, and how much the one suit with her own inclination more than
the other. to say the truth, Sophia, when very young, discern that
Tom, though an idle, thoughtless, rattle rascal, be nobody's enemy
but his own; and that Master Blifil, though a prudent, discreet, sober
young gentleman, be at the same time strongly attach to the
interest only of one single person; and who that single person be the
reader will be able to divine without any assistance of our.
these two character be not always receive in the world with the
different regard which seem severally due to either; and which one
would imagine mankind, from self-interest, should show towards they.
but perhaps there may be a political reason for it: in find one of
a truly benevolent disposition, man may very reasonably suppose they
have find a treasure, and be desirous of keep it, like all other
good thing, to themselves. hence they may imagine, that to trumpet
forth the praise of such a person, would, in the vulgar phrase, be
cry roast-meat, and call in partaker of what they intend to
apply solely to their own use. if this reason do not satisfy the
reader, I know no other mean of account for the little respect
which I have commonly see pay to a character which really do great
honour to human nature, and be productive of the high good to
society. but it be otherwise with Sophia. she honour Tom Jones, and
scorn Master Blifil, almost as soon as she know the meaning of those
two word.
Sophia have be absent upwards of three year with her aunt; during
all which time she have seldom see either of these young gentleman.
she din, however, once, together with her aunt, at Mr Allworthy's.
this be a few day after the adventure of the partridge, before
commemorate. Sophia hear the whole story at table, where she say
nothing: nor indeed could her aunt get many word from she as she
return home; but her maid, when undress she, happen to say,
"well, miss, I suppose you have see young Master Blifil to-day?" she
answer with much passion, "I hate the name of Master Blifil, as I do
whatever be base and treacherous: and I wonder Mr Allworthy would
suffer that old barbarous schoolmaster to punish a poor boy so cruelly
for what be only the effect of his good-nature." she then recount
the story to she maid, and conclude with say, "donot you think he
be a boy of noble spirit?"
this young lady be now return to her father; who give she the
command of his house, and place she at the upper end of his table,
where Tom (who for his great love of hunting be become a great
favourite of the squire) often din. young man of open, generous
disposition be naturally inclined to gallantry, which, if they have
good understanding, as be in reality Tom's case, exert itself in an
oblige complacent behaviour to all woman in general. this greatly
distinguish Tom from the boisterous brutality of mere country
squire on the one hand, and from the solemn and somewhat sullen
deportment of Master Blifil on the other; and he begin now, at twenty,
to have the name of a pretty fellow among all the woman in the
neighbourhood.
Tom behave to Sophia with no particularity, unless perhaps by show
she a high respect than he pay to any other. this distinction her
beauty, fortune, sense, and amiable carriage, seem to demand; but as
to design upon her person he have none; for which we shall at present
suffer the reader to condemn he of stupidity; but perhaps we shall be
able indifferently well to account for it hereafter.
Sophia, with the high degree of innocence and modesty, have a
remarkable sprightliness in her temper. this be so greatly increase
whenever she be in company with Tom, that have he not be very young
and thoughtless, he must have observe it: or have not Mr Western's
thought be generally either in the field, the stable, or the
dog-kennel, it might have perhaps create some jealousy in he: but so
far be the good gentleman from entertain any such suspicion, that
he give Tom every opportunity with his daughter which any lover could
have wish; and this Tom innocently improve to well advantage, by
follow only the dictate of his natural gallantry and good-nature,
than he might perhaps have do have he have the deep design on the
young lady.
but indeed it can occasion little wonder that this matter escape the
observation of other, since poor Sophia herself never remark it;
and her heart be irretrievably lose before she suspect it be in
danger.
matter be in this situation, when Tom, one afternoon, find
Sophia alone, begin, after a short apology, with a very serious face,
to acquaint she that he have a favour to ask of she which he hop she
goodness would comply with.
though neither the young man's behaviour, nor indeed his manner of
open this business, be such as could give she any just cause of
suspecting he intend to make love to she; yet whether Nature
whisper something into her ear, or from what cause it arise I will
not determine; certain it be, some idea of that kind must have
intrude itself; for her colour forsook her cheek, her limb
tremble, and her tongue would have falter, have Tom stop for an
answer; but he soon relieve she from her perplexity, by proceed to
inform she of his request; which be to solicit her interest on behalf
of the gamekeeper, whose own ruin, and that of a large family, must
be, he say, the consequence of Mr Western's pursue his action
against he.
Sophia presently recover her confusion, and, with a smile full of
sweetness, say, "be this the mighty favour you ask with so much
gravity? I will do it with all my heart. I really pity the poor
fellow, and no long ago than yesterday send a small matter to his
wife." this small matter be one of her gown, some linen, and ten
shilling in money, of which Tom have hear, and it have, in reality,
put this solicitation into his head.
our youth, now, embolden with his success, resolve to push the
matter far, and venture even to beg her recommendation of he to
her father's service; protest that he think he one of the
honest fellow in the country, and extremely well qualified for the
place of a gamekeeper, which luckily then happen to be vacant.
Sophia answer, "well, I will undertake this too; but I cannot
promise you as much success as in the former part, which I assure you
I will not quit my father without obtain. however, I will do what I
can for the poor fellow; for I sincerely look upon he and his family
as object of great compassion. and now, Mr Jones, I must ask you a
favour."
"a favour, madam!" cry Tom: "if you know the pleasure you have give
I in the hope of receive a command from you, you would think by
mention it you do confer the great favour on I; for by this
dear hand I would sacrifice my life to oblige you."
he then snatch her hand, and eagerly kiss it, which be the first
time his lip have ever touch she. the blood, which before have
forsake her cheek, now make her sufficient amend, by rush all
over her face and neck with such violence, that they become all of a
scarlet colour. she now first feel a sensation to which she have be
before a stranger, and which, when she have leisure to reflect on it,
begin to acquaint she with some secret, which the reader, if he doth
not already guess they, will know in due time.
Sophia, as soon as she could speak (which be not instantly), inform
he that the favour she have to desire of he be, not to lead she
father through so many danger in hunting; for that, from what she have
hear, she be terribly frighten every time they go out together,
and expect some day or other to see her father bring home with
break limb. she therefore beg he, for her sake, to be more
cautious; and as he well know Mr Western would follow he, not to ride
so madly, nor to take those dangerous leap for the future.
Tom promise faithfully to obey her command; and after thank she
for her kind compliance with his request, take his leave, and depart
highly charm with his success.
Poor Sophia be charm too, but in a very different way. her
sensation, however, the reader's heart (if he or she have any) will
well represent than I can, if I have as many mouth as ever poet
wish for, to eat, I suppose, those many dainty with which he be
so plentifully provide.
it be Mr Western's custom every afternoon, as soon as he be drunk,
to hear his daughter play on the harpsichord; for he be a great lover
of music, and perhaps, have he live in town, might have pass for a
connoisseur; for he always except against the fine composition of
Mr Handel. he never relish any music but what be light and airy;
and indeed his most favourite tune be Old Sir Simon the King, St
George he be for England, Bobbing Joan, and some other.
his daughter, though she be a perfect mistress of music, and would
never willingly have play any but Handel's, be so devoted to her
father's pleasure, that she learn all those tune to oblige he.
however, she would now and then endeavour to lead he into her own
taste; and when he require the repetition of his ballad, would
answer with a "Nay, dear sir;" and would often beg he to suffer she
to play something else.
this evening, however, when the gentleman be retire from his bottle,
she play all his favourite three time over without any
solicitation. this so please the good squire, that he start from
his couch, give his daughter a kiss, and swear her hand be greatly
improve. she take this opportunity to execute her promise to Tom; in
which she succeed so well, that the squire declare, if she would
give he t'other bout of Old Sir Simon, he would give the gamekeeper
his deputation the next morning. Sir Simon be play again and again,
till the charm of the music soothe Mr Western to sleep. in the
morning Sophia do not fail to remind he of his engagement; and his
attorney be immediately send for, order to stop any further
proceeding in the action, and to make out the deputation.
Tom's success in this affair soon begin to ring over the country, and
various be the censure pass upon it; some greatly applaud it
as an act of good nature; other sneer, and say, "no wonder that
one idle fellow should love another." Young Blifil be greatly enrage
at it. he have long hat Black George in the same proportion as Jones
delight in he; not from any offence which he have ever receive, but
from his great love to religion and virtue;--for Black George have the
reputation of a loose kind of a fellow. Blifil therefore represent
this as fly in Mr Allworthy's face; and declare, with great
concern, that it be impossible to find any other motive for do
good to such a wretch.
Thwackum and Square likewise sing to the same tune. they be now
(especially the latter) become greatly jealous of young Jones with the
widow; for he now approach the age of twenty, be really a fine
young fellow, and that lady, by her encouragement to he, seem
daily more and more to think he so.
Allworthy be not, however, move with their malice. he declare
himself very well satisfied with what Jones have do. he say the
perseverance and integrity of his friendship be highly commendable,
and he wish he could see more frequent instance of that virtue.
but Fortune, who seldom greatly relish such spark as my friend Tom,
perhaps because they do not pay more ardent address to she, give now
a very different turn to all his action, and show they to Mr
Allworthy in a light far less agreeable than that gentleman's goodness
have hitherto see they in.
Chapter vi.
an apology for the insensibility of Mr Jones to all the charm of the
lovely Sophia; in which possibly we may, in a considerable degree,
lower his character in the estimation of those man of wit and
gallantry who approve the hero in most of our modern comedy.
there be two sort of people, who, I be afraid, have already
conceive some contempt for my heroe, on account of his behaviour to
Sophia. the former of these will blame his prudence in neglect an
opportunity to possess himself of Mr Western's fortune; and the latter
will no less despise he for his backwardness to so fine a girl, who
seem ready to fly into his arm, if he would open they to receive
she.
now, though I shall not perhaps be able absolutely to acquit he of
either of these charge (for want of prudence admit of no excuse; and
what I shall produce against the latter charge will, I apprehend, be
scarce satisfactory); yet, as evidence may sometimes be offer in
mitigation, I shall set forth the plain matter of fact, and leave the
whole to the reader's determination.
Mr Jones have somewhat about he, which, though I think writer be not
thoroughly agree in its name, doth certainly inhabit some human
breast; whose use be not so properly to distinguish right from wrong,
as to prompt and incite they to the former, and to restrain and
withhold they from the latter.
this somewhat may be indeed resemble to the famous trunk-maker in the
playhouse; for, whenever the person who be possess of it doth what
be right, no ravished or friendly spectator be so eager or so loud in
his applause: on the contrary, when he doth wrong, no critic be so apt
to hiss and explode he.
to give a high idea of the principle I mean, as well as one more
familiar to the present age; it may be consider as sit on its
throne in the mind, like the Lord High Chancellor of this kingdom in
his court; where it preside, govern, direct, judge, acquit, and
condemn accord to merit and justice, with a knowledge which
nothing escape, a penetration which nothing can deceive, and an
integrity which nothing can corrupt.
this active principle may perhaps be say to constitute the most
essential barrier between we and our neighbour the brute; for if
there be some in the human shape who be not under any such dominion,
I choose rather to consider they as deserter from we to our
neighbour; among whom they will have the fate of deserter, and not
be place in the first rank.
our heroe, whether he derive it from Thwackum or Square I will not
determine, be very strongly under the guidance of this principle; for
though he do not always act rightly, yet he never do otherwise
without feel and suffer for it. it be this which teach he,
that to repay the civility and little friendship of hospitality by
rob the house where you have receive they, be to be the basest
and mean of thief. he do not think the baseness of this offence
lessen by the height of the injury commit; on the contrary, if to
steal another's plate deserve death and infamy, it seem to he
difficult to assign a punishment adequate to the rob a man of his
whole fortune, and of his child into the bargain.
this principle, therefore, prevent he from any thought of make
his fortune by such mean (for this, as I have say, be an active
principle, and doth not content itself with knowledge or belief only).
have he be greatly enamoured of Sophia, he possibly might have
think otherwise; but give I leave to say, there be great difference
between run away with a man's daughter from the motive of love,
and do the same thing from the motive of theft.
now, though this young gentleman be not insensible of the charm of
Sophia; though he greatly like her beauty, and esteem all her other
qualification, she have make, however, no deep impression on his
heart; for which, as it render he liable to the charge of stupidity,
or at least of want of taste, we shall now proceed to account.
the truth then be, his heart be in the possession of another woman.
here I question not but the reader will be surprize at our long
taciturnity as to this matter; and quite at a loss to divine who this
woman be, since we have hitherto not dropt a hint of any one likely
to be a rival to Sophia; for as to Mrs Blifil, though we have be
oblige to mention some suspicion of her affection for Tom, we have
not hitherto give the least latitude for imagine that he have any
for she; and, indeed, I be sorry to say it, but the youth of both
sex be too apt to be deficient in their gratitude for that regard
with which person more advanced in year be sometimes so kind to
honour they.
that the reader may be no long in suspense, he will be pleased to
remember, that we have often mention the family of George Seagrim
(commonly call Black George, the gamekeeper), which consist at
present of a wife and five child.
the second of these child be a daughter, whose name be Molly, and
who be esteem one of the handsome girl in the whole country.
Congreve well say there be in true beauty something which vulgar
soul cannot admire; so can no dirt or rag hide this something from
those soul which be not of the vulgar stamp.
the beauty of this girl make, however, no impression on Tom, till she
grow towards the age of sixteen, when Tom, who be near three year
old, begin first to cast the eye of affection upon she. and this
affection he have fix on the girl long before he could bring himself
to attempt the possession of her person: for though his constitution
urge he greatly to this, his principle no less forcibly restrain
he. to debauch a young woman, however low her condition be, appear
to he a very heinous crime; and the good-will he bore the father,
with the compassion he have for his family, very strongly corroborate
all such sober reflection; so that he once resolve to get the well
of his inclination, and he actually abstain three whole month
without ever go to Seagrim's house, or see his daughter.
now, though molly be, as we have say, generally think a very fine
girl, and in reality she be so, yet her beauty be not of the most
amiable kind. it have, indeed, very little of feminine in it, and would
have become a man at least as well as a woman; for, to say the truth,
youth and florid health have a very considerable share in the
composition.
nor be her mind more effeminate than her person. as this be tall and
robust, so be that bold and forward. so little have she of modesty,
that Jones have more regard for her virtue than she herself. and as
most probably she like Tom as well as he like she, so when she
perceive his backwardness she herself grow proportionably forward;
and when she see he have entirely desert the house, she find mean
of throw herself in his way, and behave in such a manner that the
youth must have have very much or very little of the heroe if her
endeavour have prove unsuccessful. in a word, she soon triumph over
all the virtuous resolution of Jones; for though she behave at last
with all decent reluctance, yet I rather chuse to attribute the
triumph to she, since, in fact, it be her design which succeed.
in the conduct of this matter, I say, molly so well play her part,
that Jones attribute the conquest entirely to himself, and consider
the young woman as one who have yield to the violent attack of his
passion. he likewise impute she yield to the ungovernable force of
her love towards he; and this the reader will allow to have be a
very natural and probable supposition, as we have more than once
mention the uncommon comeliness of his person: and, indeed, he be
one of the handsome young fellow in the world.
as there be some mind whose affection, like Master Blifil's, be
solely place on one single person, whose interest and indulgence
alone they consider on every occasion; regard the good and ill of
all other as merely indifferent, any far than as they contribute
to the pleasure or advantage of that person: so there be a different
temper of mind which borrow a degree of virtue even from self-love.
such can never receive any kind of satisfaction from another, without
love the creature to whom that satisfaction be owing, and without
make its well-being in some sort necessary to their own ease.
of this latter specie be our heroe. he consider this poor girl as
one whose happiness or misery he have cause to be dependent on
himself. her beauty be still the object of desire, though great
beauty, or a fresher object, might have be more so; but the little
abatement which fruition have occasion to this be highly
overbalance by the consideration of the affection which she visibly
bear he, and of the situation into which he have bring she. the
former of these create gratitude, the latter compassion; and both,
together with his desire for her person, raise in he a passion which
might, without any great violence to the word, be call love; though,
perhaps, it be at first not very judiciously place.
this, then, be the true reason of that insensibility which he have
show to the charm of Sophia, and that behaviour in she which might
have be reasonably enough interpret as an encouragement to his
address; for as he could not think of abandon his Molly, poor and
destitute as she be, so no more could he entertain a notion of
betray such a creature as Sophia. and surely, have he give the
least encouragement to any passion for that young lady, he must have
be absolutely guilty of one or other of those crime; either of
which would, in my opinion, have very justly subject he to that
fate, which, at his first introduction into this history, I mention
to have be generally predict as his certain destiny.
Chapter vii.
be the short chapter in this book.
her mother first perceive the alteration in the shape of Molly; and
in order to hide it from her neighbour, she foolishly clothe she in
that sack which Sophia have send she; though, indeed, that young lady
have little apprehension that the poor woman would have be weak
enough to let any of her daughter wear it in that form.
Molly be charm with the first opportunity she ever have of show
her beauty to advantage; for though she could very well bear to
contemplate herself in the glass, even when dress in rag; and
though she have in that dress conquer the heart of Jones, and perhaps
of some other; yet she think the addition of finery would much
improve her charm, and extend her conquest.
molly, therefore, have dress herself out in this sack, with a new
laced cap, and some other ornament which Tom have give she, repair
to church with her fan in her hand the very next Sunday. the great be
deceive if they imagine they have appropriate ambition and vanity to
themselves. these noble quality flourish as notably in a country
church and churchyard as in the drawing-room, or in the closet.
scheme have indeed be lay in the vestry which would hardly
disgrace the conclave. here be a ministry, and here be an opposition.
here be plot and circumvention, party and faction, equal to
those which be to be find in court.
nor be the woman here less practise in the high feminine art
than their fair superior in quality and fortune. here be prude and
coquette. here be dress and ogle, falsehood, envy, malice,
scandal; in short, everything which be common to the most splendid
assembly, or politest circle. let those of high life, therefore, no
long despise the ignorance of their inferior; nor the vulgar any
long rail at the vice of their better.
Molly have seat herself some time before she be know by her
neighbour. and then a whisper run through the whole congregation,
"who be she?" but when she be discover, such sneer, gigling,
tittering, and laugh ensue among the woman, that Mr Allworthy be
oblige to exert his authority to preserve any decency among they.
Chapter viii.
a battle sing by the muse in the homerican style, and which none but
the classical reader can taste.
Mr Western have an estate in this parish; and as his house stand at
little great distance from this church than from his own, he very
often come to Divine Service here; and both he and the charming Sophia
happen to be present at this time.
Sophia be much pleased with the beauty of the girl, whom she pity
for her simplicity in have dress herself in that manner, as she
see the envy which it have occasion among her equal. she no soon
come home than she send for the gamekeeper, and order he to bring
his daughter to she; say she would provide for she in the family,
and might possibly place the girl about her own person, when her own
maid, who be now go away, have leave she.
Poor Seagrim be thunderstruck at this; for he be no stranger to the
fault in the shape of his daughter. he answer, in a stammering
voice, "that he be afraid Molly would be too awkward to wait on her
ladyship, as she have never be at service." "no matter for that,"
say Sophia; "she will soon improve. I be pleased with the girl, and
be resolve to try she."
Black George now repair to his wife, on whose prudent counsel he
depend to extricate he out of this dilemma; but when he come
thither he find his house in some confusion. so great envy have this
sack occasion, that when Mr Allworthy and the other gentry be go
from church, the rage, which have hitherto be confine, burst into an
uproar; and, have vent itself at first in opprobrious word,
laugh, hiss, and gesture, betook itself at last to certain missile
weapon; which, though from their plastic nature they threaten
neither the loss of life or of limb, be however sufficiently
dreadful to a well-dress lady. molly have too much spirit to bear
this treatment tamely. have therefore--but hold, as we be diffident
of our own ability, let we here invite a superior power to our
assistance.
Ye Muses, then, whoever ye be, who love to sing battle, and
principally thou who whilom didst recount the slaughter in those
field where Hudibras and Trulla fight, if thou wert not starve with
thy friend Butler, assist I on this great occasion. all thing be
not in the power of all.
as a vast herd of cow in a rich farmer's yard, if, while they be
milk, they hear their calf at a distance, lament the robbery
which be then commit, roar and bellow; so roar forth the
Somersetshire mob an hallaloo, make up of almost as many squall,
scream, and other different sound as there be person, or indeed
passion among they: some be inspire by rage, other alarm by
fear, and other have nothing in their head but the love of fun; but
chiefly Envy, the sister of Satan, and his constant companion, rush
among the crowd, and blow up the fury of the woman; who no soon come
up to Molly than they pelt she with dirt and rubbish.
molly, have endeavour in vain to make a handsome retreat, face
about; and lay hold of ragged Bess, who advance in the front of
the enemy, she at one blow fell she to the ground. the whole army of
the enemy (though near a hundred in number), see the fate of their
general, give back many pace, and retire behind a new-dug grave; for
the churchyard be the field of battle, where there be to be a
funeral that very evening. molly pursue her victory, and catch up
a skull which lie on the side of the grave, discharge it with such
fury, that have hit a taylor on the head, the two skull send
equally forth a hollow sound at their meeting, and the taylor take
presently measure of his length on the ground, where the skull lay
side by side, and it be doubtful which be the more valuable of the
two. molly then take a thigh-bone in her hand, fall in among the
flying rank, and deal her blow with great liberality on either
side, overthrow the carcass of many a mighty heroe and heroine.
recount, o Muse, the name of those who fall on this fatal day. first,
Jemmy Tweedle feel on his hinder head the direful bone. he the
pleasant bank of sweetly-wind Stour have nourish, where he first
learn the vocal art, with which, wander up and down at wakes and
fair, he cheer the rural nymph and swain, when upon the green
they interweave the sprightly dance; while he himself stand fiddle
and jump to his own music. how little now avail his fiddle! he
thump the verdant floor with his carcass. next, old Echepole, the
sowgelder, receive a blow in his forehead from our amazonian heroine,
and immediately fall to the ground. he be a swing fat fellow, and
fall with almost as much noise as a house. his tobacco-box drop at
the same time from his pocket, which molly take up as lawful spoil.
then Kate of the Mill tumble unfortunately over a tombstone, which
catch hold of her ungartered stocking invert the order of nature,
and give her heel the superiority to her head. Betty Pippin, with
young Roger her lover, fall both to the ground; where, oh perverse
fate! she salute the earth, and he the sky. Tom Freckle, the smith's
son, be the next victim to her rage. he be an ingenious workman, and
make excellent patten; nay, the very patten with which he be knock
down be his own workmanship. have he be at that time sing psalm
in the church, he would have avoid a broken head. Miss Crow, the
daughter of a farmer; John Giddish, himself a farmer; Nan Slouch,
Esther Codling, Will Spray, Tom Bennet; the three Misses Potter, whose
father keep the sign of the Red Lion; Betty Chambermaid, Jack Ostler,
and many other of inferior note, lie roll among the graf.
not that the strenuous arm of Molly reach all these; for many of
they in their flight overthrew each other.
but now Fortune, fear she have act out of character, and have
incline too long to the same side, especially as it be the right
side, hastily turn about: for now Goody Brown--whom Zekiel Brown
caress in his arm; nor he alone, but half the parish besides; so
famous be she in the field of Venus, nor indeed less in those of
Mars. the trophy of both these her husband always bear about on his
head and face; for if ever human head do by its horn display the
amorous glory of a wife, Zekiel's do; nor do his well-scratch
face less denote her talent (or rather talon) of a different kind.
no long bear this Amazon the shameful flight of her party. she stopt
short, and, call aloud to all who flee, speak as follow: "Ye
Somersetshire man, or rather ye Somersetshire woman, be ye not
ashamed thus to fly from a single woman? but if no other will oppose
she, I myself and Joan Top here will have the honour of the victory."
have thus say, she fly at Molly Seagrim, and easily wrench the
thigh-bone from her hand, at the same time claw off her cap from
her head. then lay hold of the hair of Molly with her left hand,
she attack she so furiously in the face with the right, that the
blood soon begin to trickle from her nose. molly be not idle this
while. she soon remove the clout from the head of Goody Brown, and
then fasten on her hair with one hand, with the other she cause
another bloody stream to issue forth from the nostril of the enemy.
when each of the combatant have bear off sufficient spoil of hair
from the head of her antagonist, the next rage be against the
garment. in this attack they exert so much violence, that in a very
few minute they be both naked to the middle.
it be lucky for the woman that the seat of fistycuff war be not the
same with they as among man; but though they may seem a little to
deviate from their sex, when they go forth to battle, yet I have
observe, they never so far forget, as to assail the bosom of each
other; where a few blow would be fatal to most of they. this, I know,
some derive from their being of a more bloody inclination than the
male. on which account they apply to the nose, as to the part whence
blood may most easily be draw; but this seem a far-fetched as well
as ill-natured supposition.
Goody Brown have great advantage of Molly in this particular; for the
former have indeed no breast, her bosom (if it may be so call), as
well in colour as in many other property, exactly resemble an
antient piece of parchment, upon which any one might have drum a
considerable while without do she any great damage.
molly, beside her present unhappy condition, be differently form in
those part, and might, perhaps, have tempt the envy of Brown to
give she a fatal blow, have not the lucky arrival of Tom Jones at this
instant put an immediate end to the bloody scene.
this accident be luckily owe to Mr Square; for he, Master Blifil,
and Jones, have mount their horse, after church, to take the air,
and have ride about a quarter of a mile, when Square, change his
mind (not idly, but for a reason which we shall unfold as soon as we
have leisure), desire the young gentleman to ride with he another
way than they have at first purpose. this motion be comply with,
bring they of necessity back again to the churchyard.
Master Blifil, who ride first, see such a mob assemble, and two
woman in the posture in which we leave the combatant, stopt his horse
to enquire what be the matter. a country fellow, scratch his head,
answer he: "I donot know, measter, un't I; an't please your honour,
here hath be a vight, I think, between Goody Brown and Moll
Seagrim."
"who, who?" cry Tom; but without wait for an answer, have
discover the feature of his Molly through all the discomposure in
which they now be, he hastily alight, turn his horse loose, and,
leap over the wall, run to she. she now first burst into tear,
tell he how barbarously she have be treat. upon which, forget
the sex of Goody Brown, or perhaps not know it in his rage--for, in
reality, she have no feminine appearance but a petticoat, which he
might not observe--he give she a lash or two with his horsewhip; and
then fly at the mob, who be all accuse by Moll, he deal his
blow so profusely on all side, that unless I would again invoke the
muse (which the good-natured reader may think a little too hard upon
she, as she hath so lately be violently sweat), it would be
impossible for I to recount the horse-whipping of that day.
have scour the whole coast of the enemy, as well as any of Homer's
hero ever do, or as Don Quixote or any knight-errant in the world
could have do, he return to Molly, whom he find in a condition
which must give both I and my reader pain, be it to be describe
here. Tom rave like a madman, beat his breast, tear his hair, stamp
on the ground, and vow the utmost vengeance on all who have be
concern. he then pull off his coat, and button it round she, put
his hat upon her head, wipe the blood from her face as well as he
could with his handkerchief, and call out to the servant to ride as
fast as possible for a side-saddle, or a pillion, that he might carry
her safe home.
Master Blifil object to the send away the servant, as they have
only one with they; but as Square second the order of Jones, he be
oblige to comply.
the servant return in a very short time with the pillion, and Molly,
have collect her rag as well as she could, be place behind he.
in which manner she be carry home, Square, Blifil, and Jones
attend.
here Jones have receive his coat, give she a sly kiss, and
whisper she, that he would return in the evening, quit his Molly,
and ride on after his companion.
Chapter ix.
contain matter of no very peaceable colour.
Molly have no soon apparelle herself in her accustomed rag, than
her sister begin to fall violently upon she, particularly her eld
sister, who tell she she be well enough serve. "how have she the
assurance to wear a gown which young Madam Western have give to
mother! if one of we be to wear it, I think," say she, "I myself
have the good right; but I warrant you think it belong to your
beauty. I suppose you think yourself more handsomer than any of
we."--"hand she down the bit of glass from over the cupboard," cry
another; "Iwould wash the blood from my face before I talk of my
beauty."--"You'd well have mind what the parson say," cry the
eld, "and not a harken after man voke."--"Indeed, child, and so
she have," say the mother, sob: "she hath bring a disgrace upon
we all. shebe the vurst of the vamily that ever be a whore."
"you need not upbraid I with that, mother," cry molly; "you
yourself be bring-to-bed of sister there, within a week after you
be marry."
"yes, hussy," answer the enraged mother, "so I be, and what be the
mighty matter of that? I be make an honest woman then; and if you be
to be make an honest woman, I should not be angry; but you must have
to do with a gentleman, you nasty slut; you will have a bastard,
hussy, you will; and that I defy any one to say of I."
in this situation Black George find his family, when he come home for
the purpose before mention. as his wife and three daughter be all
of they talk together, and most of they cry, it be some time
before he could get an opportunity of be hear; but as soon as such
an interval occur, he acquaint the company with what Sophia have
say to he.
Goody Seagrim then begin to revile her daughter afresh. "here," say
she, "you have bring we into a fine quandary indeed. what will madam
say to that big belly? oh that ever I should live to see this day!"
molly answer with great spirit, "and what be this mighty place which
you have get for I, father?" (for he have not well understand the
phrase use by Sophia of be about her person). "I suppose it be to
be under the cook; but I shallnot wash dish for anybody. my gentleman
will provide well for I. see what he hath give I this afternoon.
he hath promise I shall never want money; and you shallnot want money
neither, mother, if you will hold your tongue, and know when you be
well." and so say, she pull out several guineas, and give she
mother one of they.
the good woman no soon feel the gold within her palm, than her
temper begin (such be the efficacy of that panacea) to be mollify.
"why, husband," say she, "would any but such a blockhead as you not
have enquire what place this be before he have accept it? perhaps,
as Molly say, it may be in the kitchen; and truly I donot care my
daughter should be a scullion wench; for, poor as I be, I be a
gentlewoman. and thof I be oblige, as my father, who be a
clergyman, die bad than nothing, and so could not give I a
shilling of _potion_, to undervalue myself by marry a poor man; yet
I would have you to know, I have a spirit above all they thing. Marry
come up! it would well become Madam Western to look at home, and
remember who her own grandfather be. some of my family, for aught I
know, might ride in their coach, when the grandfather of some voke
walk a-voot. I warrant she fancy she do a mighty matter, when she
send we that old gownd; some of my family would not have pick up
such rag in the street; but poor people be always trample
upon.--The parish need not have be in such a fluster with Molly. you
might have tell they, child, your grandmother wear well thing new
out of the shop."
"well, but consider," cry George, "what answer shall I make to
madam?"
"I donot know what answer," say she; "you be always bring your
family into one quandary or other. do you remember when you shoot the
partridge, the occasion of all our misfortune? do not I advise you
never to go into Squire Western's manor? do not I tell you many a
good year ago what would come of it? but you would have your own
headstrong way; yes, you would, you villain."
Black George be, in the main, a peaceable kind of fellow, and nothing
choleric nor rash; yet do he bear about he something of what the
antient call the irascible, and which his wife, if she have be
endow with much wisdom, would have fear. he have long experience,
that when the storm grow very high, argument be but wind, which
serve rather to increase, than to abate it. he be therefore seldom
unprovide with a small switch, a remedy of wonderful force, as he have
often essay, and which the word villain serve as a hint for his
applying.
no soon, therefore, have this symptom appear, than he have immediate
recourse to the say remedy, which though, as it be usual in all very
efficacious medicine, it at first seem to heighten and inflame the
disease, soon produce a total calm, and restore the patient to
perfect ease and tranquillity.
this be, however, a kind of horse-medicine, which require a very
robust constitution to digest, and be therefore proper only for the
vulgar, unless in one single instance, viz., where superiority of
birth break out; in which case, we should not think it very
improperly apply by any husband whatever, if the application be not
in itself so base, that, like certain application of the physical
kind which need not be mention, it so much degrade and contaminate
the hand employ in it, that no gentleman should endure the thought
of anything so low and detestable.
the whole family be soon reduce to a state of perfect quiet; for
the virtue of this medicine, like that of electricity, be often
communicate through one person to many other, who be not touch by
the instrument. to say the truth, as they both operate by friction, it
may be doubt whether there be not something analogous between they,
of which Mr Freke would do well to enquire, before he publish the
next edition of his book.
a council be now call, in which, after many debate, molly still
persist that she would not go to service, it be at length
resolve, that Goody Seagrim herself should wait on Miss Western, and
endeavour to procure the place for her eld daughter, who declare
great readiness to accept it: but Fortune, who seem to have be an
enemy of this little family, afterwards put a stop to her promotion.
Chapter x.
a story tell by Mr Supple, the curate. the penetration of squire
Western. his great love for his daughter, and the return to it make by
she.
the next morning Tom Jones hunt with Mr Western, and be at his
return invite by that gentleman to dinner.
the lovely Sophia shine forth that day with more gaiety and
sprightliness than usual. her battery be certainly level at our
heroe; though, I believe, she herself scarce yet know her own
intention; but if she have any design of charm he, she now
succeed.
Mr Supple, the curate of Mr Allworthy's parish, make one of the
company. he be a good-natured worthy man; but chiefly remarkable for
his great taciturnity at table, though his mouth be never shut at it.
in short, he have one of the good appetite in the world. however, the
cloth be no soon take away, than he always make sufficient amend
for his silence: for he be a very hearty fellow; and his conversation
be often entertaining, never offensive.
at his first arrival, which be immediately before the entrance of the
roast-beef, he have give an intimation that he have bring some news
with he, and be begin to tell, that he come that moment from Mr
Allworthy's, when the sight of the roast-beef strike he dumb,
permit he only to say grace, and to declare he must pay his
respect to the baronet, for so he call the sirloin.
when dinner be over, be remind by Sophia of his news, he begin
as follow: "I believe, lady, your ladyship observe a young woman at
church yesterday at even-song, who be drest in one of your outlandish
garment; I think I have see your ladyship in such a one. however, in
the country, such dress be
_rara avis in terris, nigroque simillima cygno._
that be, madam, as much as to say, `a rare bird upon the earth, and
very like a black swan.' the verse be in Juvenal. but to return to
what I be relate. I be say such garment be rare sight in the
country; and perchance, too, it be think the more rare, respect
be have to the person who wear it, who, they tell I, be the
daughter of Black George, your worship's gamekeeper, whose suffering,
I should have opine, might have teach he more wit, than to dress
forth his wench in such gaudy apparel. she create so much confusion
in the congregation, that if Squire Allworthy have not silence it, it
would have interrupt the service: for I be once about to stop in
the middle of the first lesson. Howbeit, nevertheless, after prayer
be over, and I be depart home, this occasion a battle in the
churchyard, where, amongst other mischief, the head of a travel
fidler be very much break. this morning the fidler come to Squire
Allworthy for a warrant, and the wench be bring before he. the
squire be inclined to have compound matter; when, lo! on a sudden
the wench appear (I ask your ladyship's pardon) to be, as it be,
at the eve of bring forth a bastard. the squire demand of she who
be the father? but she pertinaciously refuse to make any response.
so that he be about to make her mittimus to Bridewell when I
depart."
"and be a wench have a bastard all your news, doctor?" cry
western; "I think it might have be some public matter, something
about the nation."
"I be afraid it be too common, indeed," answer the parson; "but I
think the whole story altogether deserve commemorate. as to
national matter, your worship know they well. my concern extend no
far than my own parish."
"why, ay," say the squire, "I believe I do know a little of that
matter, as you say. but, come, Tommy, drink about; the bottle stand
with you."
Tom beg to be excuse, for that he have particular business; and
get up from table, escape the clutch of the squire, who be
rise to stop he, and go off with very little ceremony.
the squire give he a good curse at his departure; and then turn to
the parson, he cry out, "I smoke it: I smoke it. Tom be certainly
the father of this bastard. Zooks, parson, you remember how he
recommend the veather o' she to I. D--n un, what a sly b--ch 'tis.
ay, ay, as sure as two-pence, Tom be the veather of the bastard."
"I should be very sorry for that," say the parson.
"why sorry," cry the squire: "where be the mighty matter o't? what,
I suppose dost pretend that thee hast never get a bastard? Pox! more
good luck's thine? for I warrant hast a do a _therefore_ manybe the
good time and often."
"your worship be pleased to be jocular," answer the parson; "but I
do not only animadvert on the sinfulness of the action--though that
surely be to be greatly deprecate--but I fear his unrighteousness may
injure he with Mr Allworthy. and truly I must say, though he hath the
character of be a little wild, I never see any harm in the young
man; nor can I say I have hear any, save what your worship now
mention. I wish, indeed, he be a little more regular in his
response at church; but altogether he seem
_ingenui vultus puer ingenuique pudoris._
that be a classical line, young lady; and, be render into
English, be, `a lad of an ingenuous countenance, and of an ingenuous
modesty;' for this be a virtue in great repute both among the Latins
and Greeks. I must say, the young gentleman (for so I think I may call
he, notwithstanding his birth) appear to I a very modest, civil
lad, and I should be sorry that he should do himself any injury in
Squire Allworthy's opinion."
"Poogh!" say the squire: "Injury, with Allworthy! why, Allworthy
love a wench himself. Doth not all the country know whose son Tom be?
you must talk to another person in that manner. I remember Allworthy
at college."
"I think," say the parson, "he have never be at the university."
"yes, yes, he be," say the squire: "and many a wench have we two have
together. as arrant a whore-master as any within five mile o'un. no,
no. it will do'n no harm with he, assure yourself; nor with anybody
else. ask Sophy there--you have not the bad opinion of a young
fellow for get a bastard, have you, girl? no, no, the woman will
like un the well for't."
this be a cruel question to poor Sophia. she have observe Tom's
colour change at the parson's story; and that, with his hasty and
abrupt departure, give her sufficient reason to think her father's
suspicion not groundless. her heart now at once discover the great
secret to she which it have be so long disclosing by little and
little; and she find herself highly interested in this matter. in
such a situation, her father's malapert question rush suddenly upon
she, produce some symptom which might have alarm a suspicious
heart; but, to do the squire justice, that be not his fault. when she
rise therefore from her chair, and tell he a hint from he be always
sufficient to make she withdraw, he suffer she to leave the room,
and then with great gravity of countenance remark, "that it be
well to see a daughter over-modest than over-forward;"--a sentiment
which be highly applaud by the parson.
there now ensue between the squire and the parson a most excellent
political discourse, frame out of newspaper and political pamphlet;
in which they make a libation of four bottle of wine to the good of
their country: and then, the squire be fast asleep, the parson
light his pipe, mount his horse, and ride home.
when the squire have finish his half-hour's nap, he summon his
daughter to her harpsichord; but she beg to be excuse that
evening, on account of a violent head-ache. this remission be
presently grant; for indeed she seldom have occasion to ask he
twice, as he love she with such ardent affection, that, by gratify
she, he commonly convey the high gratification to himself. she
be really, what he frequently call she, his little darling, and she
well deserve to be so; for she return all his affection in the most
ample manner. she have preserve the most inviolable duty to he in all
thing; and this her love make not only easy, but so delightful, that
when one of her companion laugh at she for place so much merit in
such scrupulous obedience, as that young lady call it, Sophia
answer, "you mistake I, madam, if you think I value myself upon
this account; for besides that I be barely discharge my duty, I be
likewise please myself. I can truly say I have no delight equal to
that of contribute to my father's happiness; and if I value myself,
my dear, it be on have this power, and not on execute it."
this be a satisfaction, however, which poor Sophia be incapable of
taste this evening. she therefore not only desire to be excuse
from her attendance at the harpsichord, but likewise beg that he
would suffer she to absent herself from supper. to this request
likewise the squire agree, though not without some reluctance; for he
scarce ever permit she to be out of his sight, unless when he be
engage with his horse, dog, or bottle. nevertheless he yield to
the desire of his daughter, though the poor man be at the same time
oblige to avoid his own company (if I may so express myself), by
send for a neighbouring farmer to sit with he.
Chapter xi.
the narrow escape of Molly Seagrim, with some observation for which
we have be force to dive pretty deep into nature.
Tom Jones have ride one of Mr Western's horse that morning in the
chase; so that have no horse of his own in the squire's stable, he
be oblige to go home on foot: this he do so expeditiously that he
run upwards of three mile within the half-hour.
just as he arrive at Mr Allworthy's outward gate, he meet the
constable and company with Molly in their possession, whom they be
conduct to that house where the inferior sort of people may learn
one good lesson, viz., respect and deference to their superior; since
it must show they the wide distinction Fortune intend between those
person who be to be correct for their fault, and those who be
not; which lesson if they do not learn, I be afraid they very rarely
learn any other good lesson, or improve their moral, at the house of
correction.
a lawyer may perhaps think Mr Allworthy exceed his authority a
little in this instance. and, to say the truth, I question, as here
be no regular information before he, whether his conduct be
strictly regular. however, as his intention be truly upright, he
ought to be excuse in _foro conscientiae_; since so many arbitrary
act be daily commit by magistrate who have not this excuse to
plead for themselves.
Tom be no soon inform by the constable whither they be
proceed (indeed he pretty well guess it of himself), than he
catch molly in his arm, and embrace she tenderly before they all,
swear he would murder the first man who offer to lay hold of she. he
bid she dry her eye and be comfort; for, wherever she go, he
would accompany she. then turn to the constable, who stand
tremble with his hat off, he desire he, in a very mild voice, to
return with he for a moment only to his father (for so he now call
Allworthy); for he durst, he say, be assure, that, when he have
alledge what he have to say in her favour, the girl would be
discharge.
the constable, who, I make no doubt, would have surrender his
prisoner have Tom demand she, very readily consent to this request.
so back they all go into Mr Allworthy's hall; where Tom desire they
to stay till his return, and then go himself in pursuit of the good
man. as soon as he be find, Tom throw himself at his foot, and
have beg a patient hearing, confess himself to be the father of
the child of which Molly be then big. he entreat he to have
compassion on the poor girl, and to consider, if there be any guilt
in the case, it lie principally at his door.
"if there be any guilt in the case!" answer Allworthy warmly: "be
you then so profligate and abandon a libertine to doubt whether the
break the law of God and man, the corrupting and ruin a poor
girl be guilt? I own, indeed, it doth lie principally upon you; and so
heavy it be, that you ought to expect it should crush you."
"whatever may be my fate," say Tom, "let I succeed in my
intercession for the poor girl. I confess I have corrupt she! but
whether she shall be ruin, depend on you. for Heaven's sake, sir,
revoke your warrant, and do not send she to a place which must
unavoidably prove her destruction."
Allworthy bid he immediately call a servant. Tom answer there be
no occasion; for he have luckily meet they at the gate, and rely upon
his goodness, have bring they all back into his hall, where they now
wait his final resolution, which upon his knee he besought he
might be in favour of the girl; that she might be permit to go home
to her parent, and not be expose to a great degree of shame and
scorn than must necessarily fall upon she. "I know," say he, "that be
too much. I know I be the wicked occasion of it. I will endeavour to
make amend, if possible; and if you shall have hereaft the goodness
to forgive I, I hope I shall deserve it."
Allworthy hesitate some time, and at last say, "well, I will
discharge my mittimus.--You may send the constable to I." he be
instantly call, discharge, and so be the girl.
it will be believe that Mr Allworthy fail not to read Tom a very
severe lecture on this occasion; but it be unnecessary to insert it
here, as we have faithfully transcribe what he say to Jenny Jones in
the first book, most of which may be apply to the man, equally with
the woman. so sensible an effect have these reproof on the young man,
who be no hardened sinner, that he retire to his own room, where he
pass the evening alone, in much melancholy contemplation.
Allworthy be sufficiently offend by this transgression of Jones;
for notwithstanding the assertion of Mr Western, it be certain this
worthy man have never indulge himself in any loose pleasure with
woman, and greatly condemn the vice of incontinence in other.
indeed, there be much reason to imagine that there be not the least
truth in what Mr Western affirm, especially as he lay the scene of
those impurity at the university, where Mr Allworthy have never be.
in fact, the good squire be a little too apt to indulge that kind of
pleasantry which be generally call rhodomontade: but which may, with
as much propriety, be express by a much short word; and perhaps we
too often supply the use of this little monosyllable by other; since
very much of what frequently pass in the world for wit and humour,
should, in the strict purity of language, receive that short
appellation, which, in conformity to the well-breed law of custom, I
here suppress.
but whatever detestation Mr Allworthy have to this or to any other
vice, he be not so blinded by it but that he could discern any virtue
in the guilty person, as clearly indeed as if there have be no
mixture of vice in the same character. while he be angry therefore
with the incontinence of Jones, he be no less pleased with the honour
and honesty of his self-accusation. he begin now to form in his mind
the same opinion of this young fellow, which, we hope, our reader may
have conceive. and in balance his fault with his perfection, the
latter seem rather to preponderate.
it be to no purpose, therefore, that Thwackum, who be immediately
charge by Mr Blifil with the story, unbend all his rancour against
poor Tom. Allworthy give a patient hearing to their invective, and
then answer coldly: "that young man of Tom's complexion be too
generally addicted to this vice; but he believe that youth be
sincerely affect with what he have say to he on the occasion, and
he hop he would not transgress again." so that, as the day of
whipping be at an end, the tutor have no other vent but his own mouth
for his gall, the usual poor resource of impotent revenge.
but Square, who be a less violent, be a much more artful man; and as
he hat Jones more perhaps than Thwackum himself do, so he contrive
to do he more mischief in the mind of Mr Allworthy.
the reader must remember the several little incident of the
partridge, the horse, and the Bible, which be recount in the
second book. by all which Jones have rather improve than injure the
affection which Mr Allworthy be incline to entertain for he. the
same, I believe, must have happen to he with every other person who
hath any idea of friendship, generosity, and greatness of spirit, that
be to say, who hath any trace of goodness in his mind.
Square himself be not unacquainted with the true impression which
those several instance of goodness have make on the excellent heart of
Allworthy; for the philosopher very well know what virtue be, though
he be not always perhaps steady in its pursuit; but as for Thwackum,
from what reason I will not determine, no such thought ever enter
into his head: he see Jones in a bad light, and he imagine Allworthy
see he in the same, but that he be resolve, from pride and
stubbornness of spirit, not to give up the boy whom he have once
cherish; since by so do, he must tacitly acknowledge that his
former opinion of he have be wrong.
Square therefore embrace this opportunity of injure Jones
in the tender part, by give a very bad turn to all these
before-mention occurrence. "I be sorry, sir," say he, "to own I
have be deceive as well as yourself. I could not, I confess, help
be pleased with what I ascribe to the motive of friendship, though
it be carry to an excess, and all excess be faulty and vicious: but
in this I make allowance for youth. little do I suspect that the
sacrifice of truth, which we both imagine to have be make to
friendship, be in reality a prostitution of it to a depraved and
debauch appetite. you now plainly see whence all the seem
generosity of this young man to the family of the gamekeeper
proceed. he support the father in order to corrupt the daughter,
and preserve the family from starve, to bring one of they to shame
and ruin. this be friendship! this be generosity! as Sir Richard
Steele say, `glutton who give high price for delicacy, be very
worthy to be call generous.' in short I be resolve, from this
instance, never to give way to the weakness of human nature more, nor
to think anything virtue which doth not exactly quadrate with the
unerring rule of right."
the goodness of Allworthy have prevent those consideration from
occur to himself; yet be they too plausible to be absolutely and
hastily reject, when lay before his eye by another. indeed what
Square have say sink very deeply into his mind, and the uneasiness
which it there create be very visible to the other; though the good
man would not acknowledge this, but make a very slight answer, and
forcibly drive off the discourse to some other subject. it be well
perhaps for poor Tom, that no such suggestion have be make before he
be pardon; for they certainly stamp in the mind of Allworthy the
first bad impression concern Jones.
Chapter xii.
contain much clear matter; but which flow from the same
fountain with those in the precede chapter.
the reader will be please, I believe, to return with I to Sophia.
she pass the night, after we see she last, in no very agreeable
manner. sleep befriend she but little, and dream less. in the
morning, when Mrs Honour, her maid, attend she at the usual hour,
she be find already up and drest.
person who live two or three mile' distance in the country be
consider as next-door neighbour, and transaction at the one house
fly with incredible celerity to the other. Mrs Honour, therefore, have
hear the whole story of Molly's shame; which she, be of a very
communicative temper, have no soon enter the apartment of her
mistress, than she begin to relate in the follow manner:--
"La, ma'am, what doth your la'ship think? the girl that your la'ship
see at church on Sunday, whom you think so handsome; though you
would not have think she so handsome neither, if you have see she
nearer, but to be sure she hath be carry before the justice for
be big with child. she seem to I to look like a confident slut:
and to be sure she hath lay the child to young Mr Jones. and all the
parish say Mr Allworthy be so angry with young Mr Jones, that he
willnot see he. to be sure, one cannot help pity the poor young man,
and yet he doth not deserve much pity neither, for demean himself
with such kind of trumpery. yet he be so pretty a gentleman, I should
be sorry to have he turn out of door. I dare to swear the wench
be as willing as he; for she be always a forward kind of body. and
when wench be so come, young man be not so much to be blame
neither; for to be sure they do no more than what be natural. indeed
it be beneath they to meddle with such dirty draggle-tail; and
whatever happen to they, it be good enough for they. and yet, to be
sure, the vile baggage be most in fault. I wish, with all my
heart, they be well to be whip at the cart's tail; for it be pity
they should be the ruin of a pretty young gentleman; and nobody can
deny but that Mr Jones be one of the most handsome young man that
ever----"
she be run on thus, when Sophia, with a more peevish voice than
she have ever speak to she in before, cry, "Prithee, why dost thou
trouble I with all this stuff? what concern have I in what Mr Jones
doth? I suppose you be all alike. and you seem to I to be angry it
be not your own case."
"I, ma'am!" answer Mrs Honour, "I be sorry your ladyship should have
such an opinion of I. I be sure nobody can say any such thing of I.
all the young fellow in the world may go to the divil for I. because
I say he be a handsome man? everybody say it as well as i. to be
sure, I never think as it be any harm to say a young man be
handsome; but to be sure I shall never think he so any more now; for
handsome be that handsome doe. a beggar wench!--"
"stop thy torrent of impertinence," cry Sophia, "and see whether my
father want I at breakfast."
Mrs Honour then fling out of the room, mutter much to herself, of
which "Marry come up, I assure you," be all that could be plainly
distinguish.
whether Mrs Honour really deserve that suspicion, of which her
mistress give she a hint, be a matter which we cannot indulge our
reader's curiosity by resolve. we will, however, make he amend in
disclose what pass in the mind of Sophia.
the reader will be pleased to recollect, that a secret affection for
Mr Jones have insensibly steal into the bosom of this young lady. that
it have there grow to a pretty great height before she herself have
discover it. when she first begin to perceive its symptom, the
sensation be so sweet and pleasing, that she have not resolution
sufficient to check or repel they; and thus she go on cherish a
passion of which she never once consider the consequence.
this incident relate to Molly first open her eye. she now first
perceive the weakness of which she have be guilty; and though it
cause the utmost perturbation in her mind, yet it have the effect of
other nauseous physic, and for the time expel her distemper. its
operation indeed be most wonderfully quick; and in the short
interval, while her maid be absent, so entirely remove all symptom,
that when Mrs Honour return with a summon from her father, she be
become perfectly easy, and have bring herself to a thorough
indifference for Mr Jones.
the disease of the mind do in almost every particular imitate those
of the body. for which reason, we hope, that learn faculty, for whom
we have so profound a respect, will pardon we the violent hand we
have be necessitate to lay on several word and phrase, which of
right belong to they, and without which our description must have
be often unintelligible.
now there be no one circumstance in which the distemper of the mind
bear a more exact analogy to those which be call bodily, than that
aptness which both have to a relapse. this be plain in the violent
disease of ambition and avarice. I have know ambition, when cure at
court by frequent disappointment (which be the only physic for it),
to break out again in a contest for foreman of the grand jury at an
assize; and have hear of a man who have so far conquer avarice, as
to give away many a sixpence, that comfort himself, at last, on his
deathbe, by make a crafty and advantageous bargain concern his
ensue funeral, with an undertaker who have marry his only child.
in the affair of love, which, out of strict conformity with the stoic
philosophy, we shall here treat as a disease, this proneness to
relapse be no less conspicuous. thus it happen to poor Sophia; upon
whom, the very next time she see young Jones, all the former symptom
return, and from that time cold and hot fit alternately seize her
heart.
the situation of this young lady be now very different from what it
have ever be before. that passion which have formerly be so
exquisitely delicious, become now a scorpion in her bosom. she
resist it therefore with her utmost force, and summon every
argument her reason (which be surprisingly strong for her age) could
suggest, to subdue and expel it. in this she so far succeed, that
she begin to hope from time and absence a perfect cure. she resolve
therefore to avoid Tom Jones as much as possible; for which purpose
she begin to conceive a design of visit her aunt, to which she make
no doubt of obtain her father's consent.
but Fortune, who have other design in her head, put an immediate stop
to any such proceeding, by introduce an accident, which will be
relate in the next chapter.
Chapter xiii.
a dreadful accident which befel Sophia. the gallant behaviour of
Jones, and the more dreadful consequence of that behaviour to the
young lady; with a short digression in favour of the female sex.
Mr Western grow every day fonder and fonder of Sophia, insomuch that
his beloved dog themselves almost give place to she in his
affection; but as he could not prevail on himself to abandon these,
he contrive very cunningly to enjoy their company, together with that
of his daughter, by insist on she rid a hunting with he.
Sophia, to whom her father's word be a law, readily comply with his
desire, though she have not the least delight in a sport, which be of
too rough and masculine a nature to suit with her disposition. she have
however another motive, beside her obedience, to accompany the old
gentleman in the chase; for by her presence she hop in some measure
to restrain his impetuosity, and to prevent he from so frequently
expose his neck to the utmost hazard.
the strong objection be that which would have formerly be an
inducement to she, namely, the frequent meeting with young Jones, whom
she have determine to avoid; but as the end of the hunting season now
approach, she hop, by a short absence with her aunt, to reason
herself entirely out of her unfortunate passion; and have not any doubt
of be able to meet he in the field the subsequent season without
the least danger.
on the second day of her hunting, as she be return from the chase,
and be arrive within a little distance from Mr Western's house, her
horse, whose mettlesome spirit require a well rider, fall suddenly
to prance and caper in such a manner that she be in the most
imminent peril of fall. Tom Jones, who be at a little distance
behind, see this, and immediately gallop up to her assistance. as
soon as he come up, he leap from his own horse, and catch hold of
her by the bridle. the unruly beast presently rear himself an end
on his hind leg, and throw his lovely burthen from his back, and
Jones catch she in his arm.
she be so affected with the fright, that she be not immediately able
to satisfy Jones, who be very sollicitous to know whether she have
receive any hurt. she soon after, however, recover her spirit,
assure he she be safe, and thank he for the care he have take of
she. Jones answer, "if I have preserve you, madam, I be
sufficiently repay; for I promise you, I would have secure you from
the least harm at the expense of a much great misfortune to myself
than I have suffer on this occasion."
"what misfortune?" reply Sophia eagerly; "I hope you have come to no
mischief?"
"be not concern, madam," answer Jones. "Heaven be praise you have
escape so well, consider the danger you be in. if I have break my
arm, I consider it as a trifle, in comparison of what I fear upon
your account."
Sophia then scream out, "break your arm! Heaven forbid."
"I be afraid I have, madam," say Jones: "but I beg you will suffer I
first to take care of you. I have a right hand yet at your service, to
help you into the next field, whence we have but a very little walk to
your father's house."
Sophia see his left arm dangle by his side, while he be use
the other to lead she, no long doubt of the truth. she now grow
much pale than her fear for herself have make she before. all her
limb be seize with a trembling, insomuch that Jones could scarce
support she; and as her thought be in no less agitation, she could
not refrain from give Jones a look so full of tenderness, that it
almost argue a strong sensation in her mind, than even gratitude
and pity united can raise in the gentle female bosom, without the
assistance of a third more powerful passion.
Mr Western, who be advance at some distance when this accident
happen, be now return, as be the rest of the horseman. Sophia
immediately acquaint they with what have befall Jones, and beg
they to take care of he. upon which Western, who have be much
alarm by meet his daughter's horse without its rider, and be now
overjoyed to find her unhurt, cry out, "I be glad it be no bad. if
Tom hath break his arm, we will get a joiner to mend un again."
