the squire alight from his horse, and proceed to his house on
foot, with his daughter and Jones. an impartial spectator, who have meet
they on the way, would, on view their several countenance, have
conclude Sophia alone to have be the object of compassion: for as
to Jones, he exult in have probably save the life of the young
lady, at the price only of a broken bone; and Mr Western, though he
be not unconcerned at the accident which have befall Jones, be,
however, delight in a much high degree with the fortunate escape
of his daughter.
the generosity of Sophia's temper construe this behaviour of Jones
into great bravery; and it make a deep impression on her heart: for
certain it be, that there be no one quality which so generally
recommend man to woman as this; proceed, if we believe the common
opinion, from that natural timidity of the sex, which be, say Mr
Osborne, "so great, that a woman be the most cowardly of all the
creature God ever made;"--a sentiment more remarkable for its
bluntness than for its truth. Aristotle, in his politic, doth they, I
believe, more justice, when he say, "the modesty and fortitude of man
differ from those virtue in woman; for the fortitude which become a
woman, would be cowardice in a man; and the modesty which become a
man, would be pertness in a woman." nor be there, perhaps, more of
truth in the opinion of those who derive the partiality which woman
be incline to show to the brave, from this excess of their fear. Mr
Bayle (I think, in his article of Helen) impute this, and with
great probability, to their violent love of glory; for the truth of
which, we have the authority of he who of all other see farth
into human nature, and who introduce the heroine of his odyssey, the
great pattern of matrimonial love and constancy, assign the glory
of her husband as the only source of her affection towards he.[*]
[*] the english reader will not find this in the poem; for the
sentiment be entirely leave out in the translation.
however this be, certain it be that the accident operate very
strongly on Sophia; and, indeed, after much enquiry into the matter, I
be inclined to believe, that, at this very time, the charming Sophia
make no less impression on the heart of Jones; to say truth, he have
for some time become sensible of the irresistible power of her charm.
Chapter xiv.
the arrival of a surgeon.--His operation, and a long dialogue between
Sophia and her maid.
when they arrive at Mr Western's hall, Sophia, who have totter along
with much difficulty, sink down in her chair; but by the assistance of
hartshorn and water, she be prevent from faint away, and have
pretty well recover her spirit, when the surgeon who be send for
to Jones appear. Mr Western, who impute these symptom in his
daughter to her fall, advise she to be presently blood by way of
prevention. in this opinion he be second by the surgeon, who give
so many reason for bleeding, and quote so many case where person
have miscarry for want of it, that the squire become very
importunate, and indeed insist peremptorily that his daughter should
be blood.
Sophia soon yield to the command of her father, though entirely
contrary to her own inclination, for she suspect, I believe, less
danger from the fright, than either the squire or the surgeon. she
then stretch out her beautiful arm, and the operator begin to
prepare for his work.
while the servant be busy in provide material, the surgeon,
who impute the backwardness which have appear in Sophia to her
fear, begin to comfort she with assurance that there be not the
least danger; for no accident, he say, could ever happen in bleeding,
but from the monstrous ignorance of pretender to surgery, which he
pretty plainly insinuated be not at present to be apprehend. Sophia
declare she be not under the least apprehension; add, "if you
open an artery, I promise you Iwill forgive you." "will you?" cry
western: "D--n I, if I will. if he do thee the least mischief, d--n
I if I donot ha' the heart's blood o'un out." the surgeon assent to
bleed she upon these condition, and then proceed to his operation,
which he perform with as much dexterity as he have promise; and with
as much quickness: for he take but little blood from she, say, it
be much safe to bleed again and again, than to take away too much at
once.
Sophia, when her arm be bind up, retire: for she be not willing
(nor be it, perhaps, strictly decent) to be present at the operation
on Jones. indeed, one objection which she have to bleed (though she
do not make it) be the delay which it would occasion to set the
break bone. for Western, when Sophia be concern, have no
consideration but for she; and as for Jones himself, he "sit like
patience on a monument smile at grief." to say the truth, when he
see the blood spring from the lovely arm of Sophia, he scarce
think of what have happen to himself.
the surgeon now order his patient to be stript to his shirt, and
then entirely bar the arm, he begin to stretch and examine it, in
such a manner that the torture he put he to cause Jones to make
several wry face; which the surgeon observe, greatly wonder at,
cry, "what be the matter, sir? I be sure it be impossible I should
hurt you." and then hold forth the broken arm, he begin a long and
very learn lecture of anatomy, in which simple and double fracture
be most accurately consider; and the several way in which Jones
might have break his arm be discuss, with proper annotation
show how many of these would have be well, and how many bad
than the present case.
have at length finish his laboured harangue, with which the
audience, though it have greatly raise their attention and admiration,
be not much edify, as they really understand not a single syllable
of all he have say, he proceed to business, which he be more
expeditious in finishing, than he have be in begin.
Jones be then order into a bed, which Mr Western compel he to
accept at his own house, and sentence of water-gruel be pass upon
he.
among the good company which have attend in the hall during the
bone-setting, Mrs Honour be one; who be summon to her mistress
as soon as it be over, and ask by she how the young gentleman do,
presently launch into extravagant praise on the magnanimity, as she
call it, of his behaviour, which, she say, "be so charming in so
pretty a creature." she then burst forth into much warm encomium on
the beauty of his person; enumerate many particular, and end
with the whiteness of his skin.
this discourse have an effect on Sophia's countenance, which would not
perhaps have escape the observance of the sagacious waiting-woman,
have she once look her mistress in the face, all the time she be
speak: but as a look-glass, which be most commodiously place
opposite to she, give she an opportunity of survey those feature,
in which, of all other, she take most delight; so she have not once
remove her eye from that amiable object during her whole speech.
Mrs Honour be so intirely wrap up in the subject on which she
exercise her tongue, and the object before her eye, that she give
her mistress time to conquer her confusion; which have do, she
smile on her maid, and tell she, "she be certainly in love with this
young fellow."--"I in love, madam!" answer she: "upon my word, ma'am,
I assure you, ma'am, upon my soul, ma'am, I be not."--"why, if you
be," cry her mistress, "I see no reason that you should be ashamed
of it; for he be certainly a pretty fellow."--"Yes, ma'am," answer
the other, "that he be, the most handsome man I ever see in my life.
yes, to be sure, that he be, and, as your ladyship say, I donot know
why I should be ashamed of love he, though he be my better. to be
sure, gentlefolk be but flesh and blood no more than us servant.
besides, as for Mr Jones, thof squire Allworthy hath make a gentleman
of he, he be not so good as myself by birth: for thof I be a poor
body, I be an honest person's child, and my father and mother be
marry, which be more than some people can say, as high as they hold
their head. Marry, come up! I assure you, my dirty cousin! thof his
skin be so white, and to be sure it be the most whitest that ever be
see, I be a Christian as well as he, and nobody can say that I be
base bear: my grandfather be a clergyman,[*] and would have be very
angry, I believe, to have think any of his family should have take
up with Molly Seagrim's dirty leaving."
[*] this be the second person of low condition whom we have record
in this history to have spring from the clergy. it be to be hop
such instance will, in future age, when some provision be make for
the family of the inferior clergy, appear strange than they can
be think at present.
perhaps Sophia might have suffer she maid to run on in this manner,
from want sufficient spirit to stop her tongue, which the reader
may probably conjecture be no very easy task; for certainly there
be some passage in her speech which be far from be agreeable
to the lady. however, she now check the torrent, as there seem no
end of its flowing. "I wonder," say she, "at your assurance in daring
to talk thus of one of my father's friend. as to the wench, I order
you never to mention her name to I. and with regard to the young
gentleman's birth, those who can say nothing more to his disadvantage,
may as well be silent on that head, as I desire you will be for the
future."
"I be sorry I have offend your ladyship," answer Mrs Honour. "I be
sure I hate Molly Seagrim as much as your ladyship can; and as for
abuse Squire Jones, I can call all the servant in the house to
witness, that whenever any talk hath be about bastard, I have
always take his part; for which of you, say I to the footman, would
not be a bastard, if he could, to be make a gentleman of? and, say I,
I be sure he be a very fine gentleman; and he hath one of the white
hand in the world; for to be sure so he hath: and, say I, one of the
sweetest temperedest, well natured man in the world he be; and,
say I, all the servant and neighbour all round the country love
he. and, to be sure, I could tell your ladyship something, but that I
be afraid it would offend you."--"what could you tell I, Honour?"
say Sophia. "Nay, ma'am, to be sure he mean nothing by it, therefore
I would not have your ladyship be offend."--"Prithee tell I," say
Sophia; "I will know it this instant."--"why, ma'am," answer Mrs
Honour, "he come into the room one day last week when I be at work,
and there lie your ladyship's muff on a chair, and to be sure he put
his hand into it; that very muff your ladyship give I but yesterday.
La! say I, Mr Jones, you will stretch my lady's muff, and spoil it:
but he still keep his hand in it: and then he kiss it--to be sure I
hardly ever see such a kiss in my life as he give it."--"i suppose he
do not know it be mine," reply Sophia. "your ladyship shall hear,
ma'am. he kiss it again and again, and say it be the prettiest
muff in the world. La! sir, say I, you have see it a hundred time.
yes, Mrs Honour, cry he; but who can see anything beautiful in the
presence of your lady but herself?--Nay, thatbe not all neither; but I
hope your ladyship willnot be offend, for to be sure he mean nothing.
one day, as your ladyship be play on the harpsichord to my master,
Mr Jones be sit in the next room, and methought he look
melancholy. La! say I, Mr Jones, whatbe the matter? a penny for your
thought, say I. Why, hussy, say he, start up from a dream, what
can I be think of, when that angel your mistress be play? and
then squeeze I by the hand, oh! Mrs Honour, say he, how happy will
that man be!--and then he sigh. upon my troth, his breath be as
sweet as a nosegay.--but to be sure he mean no harm by it. so I hope
your ladyship will not mention a word; for he give I a crown never to
mention it, and make I swear upon a book, but I believe, indeed, it
be not the Bible."
till something of a more beautiful red than vermilion be find out, I
shall say nothing of Sophia's colour on this occasion. "Ho--nour,"
say she, "I--if you will not mention this any more to I--nor to
anybody else, I will not betray you--I mean, I will not be angry; but
I be afraid of your tongue. why, my girl, will you give it such
liberties?"--"Nay, ma'am," answer she, "to be sure, I would soon
cut out my tongue than offend your ladyship. to be sure I shall never
mention a word that your ladyship would not have I."--"Why, I would
not have you mention this any more," say Sophia, "for it may come to
my father's ear, and he would be angry with Mr Jones; though I really
believe, as you say, he mean nothing. I should be very angry myself,
if I imagined--"--"Nay, ma'am," say Honour, "I protest I believe he
mean nothing. I think he talk as if he be out of his sens;
nay, he say he believe he be beside himself when he have speak the
word. ay, sir, say I, I believe so too. yes, say he, Honour.--But I
ask your ladyship's pardon; I could tear my tongue out for offend
you." "go on," say Sophia; "you may mention anything you have not
tell I before."--"Yes, Honour, say he (this be some time
afterwards, when he give I the crown), I be neither such a coxcomb,
or such a villain, as to think of she in any other delight but as my
goddess; as such I will always worship and adore she while I have
breath.--this be all, ma'am, I will be swear, to the good of my
remembrance. I be in a passion with he myself, till I find he mean
no harm."--"Indeed, Honour," say Sophia, "I believe you have a real
affection for I. I be provoke the other day when I give you
warning; but if you have a desire to stay with I, you shall."--"To be
sure, ma'am," answer Mrs Honour, "I shall never desire to part with
your ladyship. to be sure, I almost cry my eye out when you give I
warning. it would be very ungrateful in I to desire to leave your
ladyship; because as why, I should never get so good a place again. I
be sure I would live and die with your ladyship; for, as poor Mr Jones
say, happy be the man----"
here the dinner bell interrupt a conversation which have work such
an effect on Sophia, that she be, perhaps, more obliged to she
bleed in the morning, than she, at the time, have apprehend she
should be. as to the present situation of her mind, I shall adhere to
a rule of Horace, by not attempt to describe it, from despair of
success. Most of my reader will suggest it easily to themselves; and
the few who cannot, would not understand the picture, or at least
would deny it to be natural, if ever so well draw.
BOOK V.
contain a portion of time somewhat long than half a year.
Chapter i.
of the SERIOUS in writing, and for what purpose it be introduce.
Peradventure there may be no part in this prodigious work which will
give the reader less pleasure in the perusing, than those which have
give the author the great pain in composing. among these probably
may be reckon those initial essay which we have prefix to the
historical matter contain in every book; and which we have
determine to be essentially necessary to this kind of writing, of
which we have set ourselves at the head.
for this our determination we do not hold ourselves strictly bind to
assign any reason; it be abundantly sufficient that we have lay it
down as a rule necessary to be observe in all prosai-comi-epic
writing. who ever demand the reason of that nice unity of time or
place which be now establish to be so essential to dramatic poetry?
what critic hath be ever ask, why a play may not contain two day
as well as one? or why the audience (provide they travel, like
elector, without any expense) may not be waft fifty mile as well
as five? hath any commentator well account for the limitation which
an antient critic hath set to the drama, which he will have contain
neither more nor less than five act? or hath any one live attempt
to explain what the modern judge of our theatre mean by that word
_low_; by which they have happily succeed in banish all humour
from the stage, and have make the theatre as dull as a drawing-room!
upon all these occasion the world seem to have embrace a maxim of
our law, viz., _cuicunque in arte sua perito credendum est:_ for it
seem perhaps difficult to conceive that any one should have have
enough of impudence to lay down dogmatical rule in any art or science
without the least foundation. in such case, therefore, we be apt to
conclude there be sound and good reason at the bottom, though we be
unfortunately not able to see so far.
now, in reality, the world have pay too great a compliment to
critic, and have imagine they man of much great profundity than
they really be. from this complacence, the critic have be
embolden to assume a dictatorial power, and have so far succeed,
that they be now become the master, and have the assurance to give
law to those author from whose predecessor they originally receive
they.
the critic, rightly consider, be no more than the clerk, whose
office it be to transcribe the rule and law lay down by those great
judge whose vast strength of genius hath place they in the light of
legislator, in the several science over which they preside. this
office be all which the critic of old aspire to; nor do they ever
dare to advance a sentence, without support it by the authority of
the judge from whence it be borrow.
but in process of time, and in age of ignorance, the clerk begin to
invade the power and assume the dignity of his master. the law of
writing be no long found on the practice of the author, but on
the dictate of the critic. the clerk become the legislator, and those
very peremptorily give law whose business it be, at first, only to
transcribe they.
hence arise an obvious, and perhaps an unavoidable error; for these
critic be man of shallow capacity, very easily mistook mere form
for substance. they act as a judge would, who should adhere to the
lifeless letter of law, and reject the spirit. little circumstance,
which be perhaps accidental in a great author, be by these critic
consider to constitute his chief merit, and transmit as
essential to be observe by all his successor. to these
encroachment, time and ignorance, the two great supporter of
imposture, give authority; and thus many rule for good writing have
be establish, which have not the least foundation in truth or
nature; and which commonly serve for no other purpose than to curb and
restrain genius, in the same manner as it would have restrain the
dancing-master, have the many excellent treatise on that art lay it
down as an essential rule that every man must dance in chain.
to avoid, therefore, all imputation of lay down a rule for
posterity, found only on the authority of _ipse dixit_--for which,
to say the truth, we have not the profound veneration--we shall
here waive the privilege above contend for, and proceed to lay
before the reader the reason which have induce we to intersperse
these several digressive essay in the course of this work.
and here we shall of necessity be lead to open a new vein of knowledge,
which if it hath be discover, hath not, to our remembrance, be
work on by any antient or modern writer. this vein be no other than
that of contrast, which run through all the work of the creation,
and may probably have a large share in constitute in we the idea of
all beauty, as well natural as artificial: for what demonstrate the
beauty and excellence of anything but its reverse? thus the beauty of
day, and that of summer, be set off by the horror of night and
winter. and, I believe, if it be possible for a man to have see only
the two former, he would have a very imperfect idea of their beauty.
but to avoid too serious an air; can it be doubt, but that the
fine woman in the world would lose all benefit of her charm in the
eye of a man who have never see one of another cast? the lady
themselves seem so sensible of this, that they be all industrious to
procure foil: nay, they will become foil to themselves; for I have
observe (at Bath particularly) that they endeavour to appear as ugly
as possible in the morning, in order to set off that beauty which they
intend to show you in the evening.
Most artist have this secret in practice, though some, perhaps, have
not much study the theory. the jeweller know that the fine
brilliant require a foil; and the painter, by the contrast of his
figure, often acquire great applause.
a great genius among we will illustrate this matter fully. I cannot,
indeed, range he under any general head of common artist, as he hath
a title to be place among those
_inventa qui vitam excoluere per arte._
who by invent art have life improve.
I mean here the inventor of that most exquisite entertainment, call
the English Pantomime.
this entertainment consist of two part, which the inventor
distinguish by the name of the serious and the comic. the serious
exhibit a certain number of heathen god and hero, who be
certainly the bad and dull company into which an audience be
ever introduce; and (which be a secret know to few) be actually
intend so to be, in order to contrast the comic part of the
entertainment, and to display the trick of harlequin to the well
advantage.
this be, perhaps, no very civil use of such personage: but the
contrivance be, nevertheless, ingenious enough, and have its effect.
and this will now plainly appear, if, instead of serious and comic, we
supply the word duller and dull; for the comic be certainly
dull than anything before show on the stage, and could be set off
only by that superlative degree of dulness which compose the serious.
so intolerably serious, indeed, be these god and hero, that
harlequin (though the english gentleman of that name be not at all
relate to the french family, for he be of a much more serious
disposition) be always welcome on the stage, as he relieve the
audience from bad company.
judicious writer have always practise this art of contrast with
great success. I have be surprize that Horace should cavil at this
art in Homer; but indeed he contradict himself in the very next line:
_Indignor quandoque bonus dormitat Homerus;
Verum opere in longo fas est obrepere somnum._
I grieve if e'er great Homer chance to sleep,
yet slumber on long work have right to creep.
for we be not here to understand, as perhaps some have, that an
author actually fall asleep while he be write. it be true, that
reader be too apt to be so overtaken; but if the work be as long as
any of Oldmixon, the author himself be too well entertain to be
subject to the least drowsiness. he be, as Mr Pope observe,
sleepless himself to give his reader sleep.
to say the truth, these soporific part be so many scene of serious
artfully interweave, in order to contrast and set off the rest; and
this be the true meaning of a late facetious writer, who tell the
public that whenever he be dull they might be assure there be a
design in it.
in this light, then, or rather in this darkness, I would have the
reader to consider these initial essay. and after this warning, if he
shall be of opinion that he can find enough of serious in other part
of this history, he may pass over these, in which we profess to be
laboriously dull, and begin the follow book at the second chapter.
Chapter ii.
in which Mr Jones receive many friendly visit during his
confinement; with some fine touch of the passion of love, scarce
visible to the naked eye.
Tom Jones have many visitor during his confinement, though some,
perhaps, be not very agreeable to he. Mr Allworthy see he almost
every day; but though he pity Tom's suffering, and greatly approve
the gallant behaviour which have occasion they; yet he think this
be a favourable opportunity to bring he to a sober sense of his
indiscreet conduct; and that wholesome advice for that purpose could
never be apply at a more proper season than at the present, when the
mind be soften by pain and sickness, and alarm by danger; and
when its attention be unembarrasse with those turbulent passion
which engage we in the pursuit of pleasure.
at all season, therefore, when the good man be alone with the youth,
especially when the latter be totally at ease, he take occasion to
remind he of his former miscarriage, but in the mild and
tender manner, and only in order to introduce the caution which he
prescribe for his future behaviour; "on which alone," he assure he,
"would depend his own felicity, and the kindness which he might yet
promise himself to receive at the hand of his father by adoption,
unless he should hereafter forfeit his good opinion: for as to what
have past," he say, "it should be all forgive and forget. he
therefore advise he to make a good use of this accident, that so in
the end it might prove a visitation for his own good."
Thwackum be likewise pretty assiduous in his visit; and he too
consider a sick-bed to be a convenient scene for lecture. his
stile, however, be more severe than Mr Allworthy's: he tell his
pupil, "that he ought to look on his broken limb as a judgment from
heaven on his sin. that it would become he to be daily on his knee,
pour forth thanksgiving that he have break his arm only, and not
his neck; which latter," he say, "be very probably reserve for some
future occasion, and that, perhaps, not very remote. for his part," he
say, "he have often wonder some judgment have not overtake he
before; but it might be perceive by this, that Divine punishment,
though slow, be always sure." hence likewise he advise he, "to
foresee, with equal certainty, the great evil which be yet
behind, and which be as sure as this of overtake he in his state
of reprobacy. these be," say he, "to be avert only by such a
thorough and sincere repentance as be not to be expect or hop for
from one so abandon in his youth, and whose mind, I be afraid, be
totally corrupted. it be my duty, however, to exhort you to this
repentance, though I too well know all exhortation will be vain and
fruitless. but _liberavi animam meam._ I can accuse my own conscience
of no neglect; though it be at the same time with the utmost concern I
see you travel on to certain misery in this world, and to as
certain damnation in the next."
Square talk in a very different strain; he say, "such accident as
a broken bone be below the consideration of a wise man. that it be
abundantly sufficient to reconcile the mind to any of these
mischance, to reflect that they be liable to befal the wise of
mankind, and be undoubtedly for the good of the whole." he say, "it
be a mere abuse of word to call those thing evil, in which there
be no moral unfitness: that pain, which be the bad consequence of
such accident, be the most contemptible thing in the world;" with
more of the like sentence, extract out of the second book of
Tully's Tusculan question, and from the great Lord Shaftesbury. in
pronounce these he be one day so eager, that he unfortunately bite
his tongue; and in such a manner, that it not only put an end to his
discourse, but create much emotion in he, and cause he to mutter
an oath or two: but what be bad of all, this accident give
Thwackum, who be present, and who hold all such doctrine to be
heathenish and atheistical, an opportunity to clap a judgment on his
back. now this be do with so malicious a sneer, that it totally
unhinge (if I may so say) the temper of the philosopher, which the
bite of his tongue have somewhat ruffle; and as he be disabled from
vent his wrath at his lip, he have possibly find a more violent
method of revenge himself, have not the surgeon, who be then luckily
in the room, contrary to his own interest, interpose and preserve
the peace.
Mr Blifil visit his friend Jones but seldom, and never alone. this
worthy young man, however, profess much regard for he, and as great
concern at his misfortune; but cautiously avoid any intimacy, lest,
as he frequently hint, it might contaminate the sobriety of his own
character: for which purpose he have constantly in his mouth that
proverb in which Solomon speak against evil communication. not that
he be so bitter as Thwackum; for he always express some hope of
Tom's reformation; "which," he say, "the unparalleled goodness show
by his uncle on this occasion, must certainly effect in one not
absolutely abandon:" but conclude, "if Mr Jones ever offend
hereafter, I shall not be able to say a syllable in his favour."
as to squire Western, he be seldom out of the sick-room, unless when
he be engage either in the field or over his bottle. Nay, he would
sometimes retire hither to take his beer, and it be not without
difficulty that he be prevent from force Jones to take his beer
too: for no quack ever hold his nostrum to be a more general panacea
than he do this; which, he say, have more virtue in it than be in
all the physic in an apothecary's shop. he be, however, by much
entreaty, prevail on to forbear the application of this medicine;
but from serenade his patient every hunting morning with the horn
under his window, it be impossible to withhold he; nor do he ever
lie aside that hallow, with which he enter into all company, when
he visit Jones, without any regard to the sick person's be at
that time either awake or asleep.
this boisterous behaviour, as it mean no harm, so happily it effect
none, and be abundantly compensate to Jones, as soon as he be able
to sit up, by the company of Sophia, whom the squire then bring to
visit he; nor be it, indeed, long before Jones be able to attend
her to the harpsichord, where she would kindly condescend, for hour
together, to charm he with the most delicious music, unless when the
squire think proper to interrupt she, by insist on Old Sir Simon,
or some other of his favourite piece.
notwithstanding the nice guard which Sophia endeavour to set on
her behaviour, she could not avoid let some appearance now and
then slip forth: for love may again be liken to a disease in this,
that when it be deny a vent in one part, it will certainly break out
in another. what her lip, therefore, conceal, her eye, her
blush, and many little involuntary action, betray.
one day, when Sophia be play on the harpsichord, and Jones be
attend, the squire come into the room, cry, "there, Tom, I have
have a battle for thee below-stair with thick parson Thwackum. he hath
be a tell Allworthy, before my face, that the broken bone be a
judgment upon thee. D--n it, say I, how can that be? do he not come
by it in defence of a young woman? a judgment indeed! pox, if he never
doth anything bad, he will go to heaven soon than all the parson
in the country. he hath more reason to glory in it than to be ashamed
of it."--"Indeed, sir," say Jones, "I have no reason for either; but
if it preserve Miss Western, I shall always think it the happy
accident of my life."--"and to gu," say the squire, "to zet Allworthy
against thee vor it! D--n un, if the parson have unt his petticuoat
on, I should have lend un o flick; for I love thee dearly, my boy, and
d--n I if there be anything in my power which I willnot do for thee.
sha't take thy choice of all the horse in my stable to-morrow
morning, except only the Chevalier and Miss Slouch." Jones thank
he, but decline accept the offer. "Nay," add the squire, "sha't
ha the sorrel mare that Sophy ride. she cost I fifty guineas, and
come six year old this grass." "if she have cost I a thousand,"
cry Jones passionately, "I would have give she to the dog." "Pooh!
pooh!" answer Western; "what! because she break thy arm? shouldst
forget and forgive. I think hadst be more a man than to bear
malice against a dumb creature."--Here Sophia interpose, and put an
end to the conversation, by desire her father's leave to play to
he; a request which he never refuse.
the countenance of Sophia have undergo more than one change during
the foregoing speech; and probably she impute the passionate
resentment which Jones have express against the mare, to a different
motive from that from which her father have derive it. her spirit
be at this time in a visible flutter; and she play so intolerably
ill, that have not western soon fall asleep, he must have remark
it. Jones, however, who be sufficiently awake, and be not without an
ear any more than without eye, make some observation; which be
join to all which the reader may remember to have pass formerly,
give he pretty strong assurance, when he come to reflect on the
whole, that all be not well in the tender bosom of Sophia; an opinion
which many young gentleman will, I doubt not, extremely wonder at his
not have be well confirm in long ago. to confess the truth, he
have rather too much diffidence in himself, and be not forward enough
in see the advance of a young lady; a misfortune which can be
cure only by that early town education, which be at present so
generally in fashion.
when these thought have fully take possession of Jones, they
occasion a perturbation in his mind, which, in a constitution less
pure and firm than his, might have be, at such a season, attend
with very dangerous consequence. he be truly sensible of the great
worth of Sophia. he extremely like her person, no less admire her
accomplishment, and tenderly love her goodness. in reality, as he
have never once entertain any thought of possess she, nor have ever
give the least voluntary indulgence to his inclination, he have a
much strong passion for she than he himself be acquaint with. his
heart now bring forth the full secret, at the same time that it
assure he the adorable object return his affection.
Chapter iii.
which all who have no heart will think to contain much ado about
nothing.
the reader will perhaps imagine the sensation which now arise in
Jones to have be so sweet and delicious, that they would rather tend
to produce a chearful serenity in the mind, than any of those
dangerous effect which we have mention; but in fact, sensation of
this kind, however delicious, be, at their first recognition, of a
very tumultuous nature, and have very little of the opiate in they.
they be, moreover, in the present case, embitter with certain
circumstance, which be mix with sweet ingredient, tend
altogether to compose a draught that might be term bitter-sweet;
than which, as nothing can be more disagreeable to the palate, so
nothing, in the metaphorical sense, can be so injurious to the mind.
for first, though he have sufficient foundation to flatter himself in
what he have observe in Sophia, he be not yet free from doubt of
misconstruing compassion, or at good, esteem, into a warm regard. he
be far from a sanguine assurance that Sophia have any such affection
towards he, as might promise his inclination that harvest, which, if
they be encourage and nurse, they would finally grow up to
require. besides, if he could hope to find no bar to his happiness
from the daughter, he think himself certain of meet an effectual
bar in the father; who, though he be a country squire in his
diversion, be perfectly a man of the world in whatever regard his
fortune; have the most violent affection for his only daughter, and have
often signify, in his cup, the pleasure he propose in see she
married to one of the rich man in the county. Jones be not so vain
and senseles a coxcomb as to expect, from any regard which Western
have profess for he, that he would ever be induce to lay aside
these view of advance his daughter. he well know that fortune be
generally the principal, if not the sole, consideration, which
operate on the good of parent in these matter: for friendship make
we warmly espouse the interest of other; but it be very cold to the
gratification of their passion. indeed, to feel the happiness which
may result from this, it be necessary we should possess the passion
ourselves. as he have therefore no hope of obtain her father's
consent; so he think to endeavour to succeed without it, and by such
mean to frustrate the great point of Mr Western's life, be to make a
very ill use of his hospitality, and a very ungrateful return to the
many little favour receive (however roughly) at his hand. if he see
such a consequence with horror and disdain, how much more be he
shock with what regard Mr Allworthy; to whom, as he have more than
filial obligation, so have he for he more than filial piety! he know
the nature of that good man to be so averse to any baseness or
treachery, that the least attempt of such a kind would make the sight
of the guilty person for ever odious to his eye, and his name a
detestable sound in his ear. the appearance of such unsurmountable
difficulty be sufficient to have inspire he with despair, however
ardent his wish have be; but even these be contruole by
compassion for another woman. the idea of lovely Molly now intrude
itself before he. he have swear eternal constancy in her arm, and she
have as often vow never to out-live his desert she. he now see she
in all the most shocking posture of death; nay, he consider all the
misery of prostitution to which she would be liable, and of which he
would be doubly the occasion; first by seducing, and then by desert
she; for he well know the hatred which all her neighbour, and even
her own sister, bear she, and how ready they would all be to tear she
to piece. indeed, he have expose she to more envy than shame, or
rather to the latter by mean of the former: for many woman abuse she
for be a whore, while they envy she her lover, and her finery,
and would have be themselves glad to have purchase these at the
same rate. the ruin, therefore, of the poor girl must, he foresaw,
unavoidably attend his desert she; and this think sting he to
the soul. poverty and distress seem to he to give none a right of
aggravate those misfortune. the meanness of her condition do not
represent her misery as of little consequence in his eye, nor do it
appear to justify, or even to palliate, his guilt, in bring that
misery upon she. but why do I mention justification? his own heart
would not suffer he to destroy a human creature who, he think,
love he, and have to that love sacrifice her innocence. his own good
heart plead her cause; not as a cold venal advocate, but as one
interested in the event, and which must itself deeply share in all the
agony its owner bring on another.
when this powerful advocate have sufficiently raise the pity of Jones,
by paint poor molly in all the circumstance of wretchedness; it
artfully call in the assistance of another passion, and represent
the girl in all the amiable colour of youth, health, and beauty; as
one greatly the object of desire, and much more so, at least to a good
mind, from be, at the same time, the object of compassion.
amidst these thought, poor Jones pass a long sleepless night, and
in the morning the result of the whole be to abide by Molly, and to
think no more of Sophia.
in this virtuous resolution he continue all the next day till the
evening, cherish the idea of Molly, and drive Sophia from his
thought; but in the fatal evening, a very trifling accident set all
his passion again on float, and work so total a change in his mind,
that we think it decent to communicate it in a fresh chapter.
chapter iv.
a little chapter, in which be contain a little incident.
among other visitant, who pay their compliment to the young
gentleman in his confinement, Mrs Honour be one. the reader, perhaps,
when he reflect on some expression which have formerly dropt from
she, may conceive that she herself have a very particular affection for
Mr Jones; but, in reality, it be no such thing. Tom be a handsome
young fellow; and for that specie of man Mrs Honour have some regard;
but this be perfectly indiscriminate; for have be cross in the
love which she bear a certain nobleman's footman, who have basely
desert she after a promise of marriage, she have so securely keep
together the broken remain of her heart, that no man have ever since
be able to possess himself of any single fragment. she view all
handsome man with that equal regard and benevolence which a sober and
virtuous mind bear to all the good. she might indeed be call a
lover of man, as Socrates be a lover of mankind, prefer one to
another for corporeal, as he for mental qualification; but never
carry this preference so far as to cause any perturbation in the
philosophical serenity of her temper.
the day after Mr Jones have that conflict with himself which we have
see in the precede chapter, Mrs Honour come into his room, and
find he alone, begin in the following manner:--"La, sir, where do
you think I have be? I warrant you, you would not guess in fifty
year; but if you do guess, to be sure I must not tell you
neither."--"Nay, if it be something which you must not tell I," say
Jones, "I shall have the curiosity to enquire, and I know you will not
be so barbarous to refuse I."--"i donot know," cry she, "why I
should refuse you neither, for that matter; for to be sure you willnot
mention it any more. and for that matter, if you know where I have
be, unless you know what I have be about, it would not signify
much. Nay, I donot see why it should be keep a secret for my part; for
to be sure she be the good lady in the world." upon this, Jones begin
to beg earnestly to be let into this secret, and faithfully promise
not to divulge it. she then proceed thus:--"why, you must know, sir,
my young lady send I to enquire after Molly Seagrim, and to see
whether the wench want anything; to be sure, I do not care to go,
methink; but servant must do what they be ordered.--how could you
undervalue yourself so, Mr Jones?--So my lady bid I go and carry she
some linen, and other thing. she be too good. if such forward slut
be send to Bridewell, it would be well for they. I tell my lady,
say I, madam, your la'ship be encourage idleness."--"And be my
Sophia so good?" say Jones. "my Sophia! I assure you, marry come up,"
answer Honour. "and yet if you know all--indeed, if I be as Mr
Jones, I should look a little high than such trumpery as Molly
Seagrim." "what do you mean by these word," reply Jones, "if I know
all?" "I mean what I mean," say Honour. "donot you remember put
your hand in my lady's muff once? I vow I could almost find in my
heart to tell, if I be certain my lady would never come to the
hearing on't." Jones then make several solemn protestation. and
Honour proceeded--"Then to be sure, my lady give I that muff; and
afterwards, upon hear what you have done"--"then you tell she what I
have do?" interrupt Jones. "if I do, sir," answer she, "you need
not be angry with I. Manybe the man would have give his head to have
have my lady tell, if they have known,--for, to be sure, the big
lord in the land might be proud--but, I protest, I have a great mind
not to tell you." Jones fall to entreaty, and soon prevail on she
to go on thus. "you must know then, sir, that my lady have give this
muff to I; but about a day or two after I have tell she the story, she
quarrel with her new muff, and to be sure it be the prettiest that
ever be see. Honour, say she, this be an odious muff; it be too big
for I, I cannot wear it: till I can get another, you must let I have
my old one again, and you may have this in the room on't--for shebe a
good lady, and scorn to give a thing and take a thing, I promise you
that. so to be sure I fetch it she back again, and, I believe, she
hath wear it upon her arm almost ever since, and I warrant hath give
it many a kiss when nobody hath see she."
here the conversation be interrupt by Mr Western himself, who come
to summon Jones to the harpsichord; whither the poor young fellow go
all pale and trembling. this Western observe, but, on see Mrs
Honour, impute it to a wrong cause; and have give Jones a hearty
curse between jest and earnest, he bid he beat abroad, and not poach
up the game in his warren.
Sophia look this evening with more than usual beauty, and we may
believe it be no small addition to her charm, in the eye of Mr
Jones, that she now happen to have on her right arm this very muff.
she be play one of her father's favourite tune, and he be
lean on her chair, when the muff fall over her finger, and put she
out. this so disconcert the squire, that he snatch the muff from
she, and with a hearty curse throw it into the fire. Sophia instantly
start up, and with the utmost eagerness recover it from the
flame.
though this incident will probably appear of little consequence to
many of our reader; yet, trifle as it be, it have so violent an
effect on poor Jones, that we think it our duty to relate it. in
reality, there be many little circumstance too often omit by
injudicious historian, from which event of the utmost importance
arise. the world may indeed be consider as a vast machine, in which
the great wheel be originally set in motion by those which be very
minute, and almost imperceptible to any but the strong eye.
thus, not all the charm of the incomparable Sophia; not all the
dazzle brightness, and languish softness of her eye; the harmony
of her voice, and of her person; not all her wit, good-humour,
greatness of mind, or sweetness of disposition, have be able so
absolutely to conquer and enslave the heart of poor Jones, as this
little incident of the muff. thus the poet sweetly sing of Troy--
_--Captique dolis lachrymisque coacti
Quos neque Tydides, nec Larissaeus Achilles,
Non anni domuere decem, non mille Carinae._
what Diomede or Thetis' great son,
a thousand ship, nor ten year' siege have do
false tear and fawn word the city win.
the citadel of Jones be now take by surprize. all those
consideration of honour and prudence which our heroe have lately with
so much military wisdom place as guard over the avenue of his
heart, run away from their post, and the god of love march in, in
triumph.
Chapter v.
a very long chapter, contain a very great incident.
but though this victorious deity easily expel his avowed enemy
from the heart of Jones, he find it more difficult to supplant the
garrison which he himself have place there. to lay aside all allegory,
the concern for what must become of poor Molly greatly disturb and
perplex the mind of the worthy youth. the superior merit of Sophia
totally eclipse, or rather extinguish, all the beauty of the poor
girl; but compassion instead of contempt succeed to love. he be
convince the girl have place all her affection, and all her prospect
of future happiness, in he only. for this he have, he know, give
sufficient occasion, by the utmost profusion of tenderness towards
she: a tenderness which he have take every mean to persuade she he
would always maintain. she, on her side, have assure he of her firm
belief in his promise, and have with the most solemn vow declare,
that on his fulfilling or break these promise, it depend,
whether she should be the happy or most miserable of womankind. and
to be the author of this high degree of misery to a human being,
be a thought on which he could not bear to ruminate a single moment.
he consider this poor girl as have sacrifice to he everything in
her little power; as have be at her own expense the object of his
pleasure; as sigh and languish for he even at that very
instant. shall then, say he, my recovery, for which she hath so
ardently wish; shall my presence, which she hath so eagerly
expect, instead of give she that joy with which she hath flatter
herself, cast she at once down into misery and despair? can I be such
a villain? here, when the genius of poor Molly seem triumphant, the
love of Sophia towards he, which now appear no long dubious,
rush upon his mind, and bear away every obstacle before it.
at length it occur to he, that he might possibly be able to make
molly amend another way; namely, by give she a sum of money. this,
nevertheless, he almost despair of she accept, when he
recollect the frequent and vehement assurance he have receive from
she, that the world put in balance with he would make she no amend
for his loss. however, her extreme poverty, and chiefly her egregious
vanity (somewhat of which hath be already hint to the reader),
give he some little hope, that, notwithstanding all her avow
tenderness, she might in time be bring to content herself with a
fortune superior to her expectation, and which might indulge her
vanity, by set she above all her equal. he resolve therefore to
take the first opportunity of make a proposal of this kind.
one day, accordingly, when his arm be so well recover that he could
walk easily with it sling in a sash, he steal forth, at a season when
the squire be engage in his field exercise, and visit his fair
one. her mother and sister, whom he find take their tea, inform
he first that molly be not at home; but afterwards the eld sister
acquaint he, with a malicious smile, that she be above stair
a-bed. Tom have no objection to this situation of his mistress, and
immediately ascend the ladder which lead towards her bed-chamber; but
when he come to the top, he, to his great surprize, find the door
fast; nor could he for some time obtain any answer from within; for
Molly, as she herself afterwards inform he, be fast asleep.
the extreme of grief and joy have be remark to produce very
similar effect; and when either of these rush on we by surprize, it
be apt to create such a total perturbation and confusion, that we be
often thereby deprive of the use of all our faculty. it cannot
therefore be wonder at, that the unexpected sight of Mr Jones should
so strongly operate on the mind of Molly, and should overwhelm she
with such confusion, that for some minute she be unable to express
the great rapture, with which the reader will suppose she be
affect on this occasion. as for Jones, he be so entirely possess,
and as it be enchant, by the presence of his beloved object, that
he for a while forget Sophia, and consequently the principal purpose
of his visit.
this, however, soon recur to his memory; and after the first
transport of their meeting be over, he find mean by degree to
introduce a discourse on the fatal consequence which must attend
their amour, if Mr Allworthy, who have strictly forbid he ever
see she more, should discover that he still carry on this
commerce. such a discovery, which his enemy give he reason to think
would be unavoidable, must, he say, end in his ruin, and consequently
in her. since therefore their hard fate have determine that they
must separate, he advise she to bear it with resolution, and swear he
would never omit any opportunity, through the course of his life, of
show she the sincerity of his affection, by provide for she in a
manner beyond her utmost expectation, or even beyond her wish, if
ever that should be in his power; conclude at last, that she might
soon find some man who would marry she, and who would make her much
happy than she could be by lead a disreputable life with he.
molly remain a few moment in silence, and then burst into a
flood of tear, she begin to upbraid he in the following word: "and
this be your love for I, to forsake I in this manner, now you have
ruin I! how often, when I have tell you that all man be false and
perjury alike, and grow tired of we as soon as ever they have have
their wicked will of we, how often have you swear you would never
forsake I! and can you be such a perjury man after all? what
signify all the rich in the world to I without you, now you have
gain my heart, so you have--you have--? why do you mention another
man to I? I can never love any other man as long as I live. all other
man be nothing to I. if the great squire in all the country would
come a suiting to I to-morrow, I would not give my company to he.
no, I shall always hate and despise the whole sex for your sake."--
she be proceed thus, when an accident put a stop to her tongue,
before it have run out half its career. the room, or rather garret, in
which molly lay, be up one pair of stair, that be to say, at the
top of the house, be of a sloping figure, resemble the great Delta
of the Greeks. the english reader may perhaps form a well idea of
it, by be tell that it be impossible to stand upright anywhere but
in the middle. now, as this room want the conveniency of a closet,
molly have, to supply that defect, nail up an old rug against the
rafter of the house, which enclose a little hole where her good
apparel, such as the remain of that sack which we have formerly
mention, some cap, and other thing with which she have lately
provide herself, be hang up and secure from the dust.
this enclose place exactly front the foot of the bed, to which,
indeed, the rug hang so near, that it serve in a manner to supply the
want of curtain. now, whether molly, in the agony of her rage,
push this rug with her foot; or Jones might touch it; or whether the
pin or nail give way of its own accord, I be not certain; but as Molly
pronounce those last word, which be record above, the wicked rug
get loose from its fastening, and discover everything hide behind it;
where among other female utensil appeared--(with shame I write it,
and with sorrow will it be read)--the philosopher Square, in a posture
(for the place would not near admit his stand upright) as
ridiculous as can possibly be conceive.
the posture, indeed, in which he stand, be not greatly unlike that of
a soldier who be tie neck and heel; or rather resemble the
attitude in which we often see fellow in the public street of
London, who be not suffer but deserve punishment by so stand.
he have a nightcap belong to Molly on his head, and his two large
eye, the moment the rug fall, star directly at Jones; so that when
the idea of philosophy be add to the figure now discover, it
would have be very difficult for any spectator to have refrain
from immoderate laughter.
I question not but the surprize of the reader will be here equal to
that of Jones; as the suspicion which must arise from the appearance
of this wise and grave man in such a place, may seem so inconsistent
with that character which he hath, doubtless, maintain hitherto, in
the opinion of every one.
but to confess the truth, this inconsistency be rather imaginary than
real. philosopher be compose of flesh and blood as well as other
human creature; and however sublimate and refine the theory of
these may be, a little practical frailty be as incident to they as to
other mortal. it be, indeed, in theory only, and not in practice, as
we have before hint, that consist the difference: for though such
great being think much well and more wisely, they always act
exactly like other man. they know very well how to subdue all
appetite and passion, and to despise both pain and pleasure; and
this knowledge afford much delightful contemplation, and be easily
acquire; but the practice would be vexatious and troublesome; and,
therefore, the same wisdom which teach they to know this, teach
they to avoid carry it into execution.
Mr Square happen to be at church on that Sunday, when, as the reader
may be pleased to remember, the appearance of Molly in her sack have
cause all that disturbance. here he first observe she, and be so
pleased with her beauty, that he prevail with the young gentleman to
change their intended ride that evening, that he might pass by the
habitation of Molly, and by that mean might obtain a second chance of
see she. this reason, however, as he do not at that time mention
to any, so neither do we think proper to communicate it then to the
reader.
among other particular which constitute the unfitness of thing in
Mr Square's opinion, danger and difficulty be two. the difficulty
therefore which he apprehend there might be in corrupt this young
wench, and the danger which would accrue to his character on the
discovery, be such strong dissuasive, that it be probable he at
first intend to have content himself with the pleasing idea which
the sight of beauty furnish we with. these the grave man, after a
full meal of serious meditation, often allow themselves by way of
dessert: for which purpose, certain book and picture find their way
into the most private recess of their study, and a certain liquorish
part of natural philosophy be often the principal subject of their
conversation.
but when the philosopher hear, a day or two afterwards, that the
fortress of virtue have already be subdue, he begin to give a large
scope to his desire. his appetite be not of that squeamish kind
which cannot feed on a dainty because another hath taste it. in
short, he like the girl the well for the want of that chastity,
which, if she have possess it, must have be a bar to his pleasure;
he pursue and obtain she.
the reader will be mistake, if he think Molly give Square the
preference to her young lover: on the contrary, have she be
confine to the choice of one only, Tom Jones would undoubtedly have
be, of the two, the victorious person. nor be it solely the
consideration that two be well than one (though this have its proper
weight) to which Mr Square owe his success: the absence of Jones
during his confinement be an unlucky circumstance; and in that
interval some well-choose present from the philosopher so soften
and unguarde the girl's heart, that a favourable opportunity become
irresistible, and Square triumph over the poor remain of virtue
which subsist in the bosom of Molly.
it be now about a fortnight since this conquest, when Jones pay the
above-mention visit to his mistress, at a time when she and Square
be in bed together. this be the true reason why the mother deny
she as we have see; for as the old woman share in the profit
arise from the iniquity of her daughter, she encourage and
protect she in it to the utmost of her power; but such be the envy
and hatred which the eld sister bear towards Molly, that,
notwithstanding she have some part of the booty, she would willingly
have part with this to ruin her sister and spoil her trade. hence
she have acquaint Jones with she be above-stair in bed, in hope
that he might have catch she in Square's arm. this, however, molly
find mean to prevent, as the door be fasten; which give she an
opportunity of convey her lover behind that rug or blanket where he
now be unhappily discover.
Square no soon make his appearance than molly fling herself back in
her bed, cry out she be undo, and abandon herself to despair.
this poor girl, who be yet but a novice in her business, have not
arrive to that perfection of assurance which help off a town lady in
any extremity; and either prompt she with an excuse, or else inspire
she to brazen out the matter with her husband, who, from love of
quiet, or out of fear of his reputation--and sometimes, perhaps, from
fear of the gallant, who, like Mr Constant in the play, wear a
sword--be glad to shut his eye, and content to put his horn in his
pocket. molly, on the contrary, be silence by this evidence, and
very fairly give up a cause which she have hitherto maintain with so
many tear, and with such solemn and vehement protestation of the
pure love and constancy.
as to the gentleman behind the arra, he be not in much less
consternation. he stand for a while motionless, and seem equally at
a loss what to say, or whither to direct his eye. Jones, though
perhaps the most astonished of the three, first find his tongue; and
be immediately recover from those uneasy sensation which Molly
by her upbraiding have occasion, he burst into a loud laughter, and
then salute Mr Square, advance to take he by the hand, and to
relieve he from his place of confinement.
Square be now arrive in the middle of the room, in which part only
he could stand upright, look at Jones with a very grave countenance,
and say to he, "well, sir, I see you enjoy this mighty discovery,
and, I dare swear, take great delight in the thought of expose I;
but if you will consider the matter fairly, you will find you be
yourself only to blame. I be not guilty of corrupt innocence. I
have do nothing for which that part of the world which judge of
matter by the rule of right, will condemn I. Fitness be govern by
the nature of thing, and not by custom, form, or municipal law.
nothing be indeed unfit which be not unnatural."--"Well reason, old
boy," answer Jones; "but why dost thou think that I should desire to
expose thee? I promise thee, I be never well pleased with thee in
my life; and unless thou hast a mind to discover it thyself, this
affair may remain a profound secret for I."--"Nay, Mr Jones," reply
Square, "I would not be think to undervalue reputation. good fame be
a species of the Kalon, and it be by no mean fitting to neglect it.
besides, to murder one's own reputation be a kind of suicide, a
detestable and odious vice. if you think proper, therefore, to conceal
any infirmity of mine (for such I may have, since no man be perfectly
perfect), I promise you I will not betray myself. thing may be
fitting to be do, which be not fitting to be boast of; for
by the perverse judgment of the world, that often become the
subject of censure, which be, in truth, not only innocent but
laudable."--"right!" cry Jones: "what can be more innocent than the
indulgence of a natural appetite? or what more laudable than the
propagation of our species?"--"to be serious with you," answer
Square, "I profess they always appear so to I."--"And yet," say
Jones, "you be of a different opinion when my affair with this girl
be first discover."--"why, I must confess," say Square, "as the
matter be misrepresent to I, by that parson Thwackum, I might
condemn the corruption of innocence: it be that, sir, it be
that--and that--: for you must know, Mr Jones, in the consideration of
fitness, very minute circumstance, sir, very minute circumstance
cause great alteration."--"Well," cry Jones, "be that as it will, it
shall be your own fault, as I have promise you, if you ever hear any
more of this adventure. behave kindly to the girl, and I will never
open my lip concern the matter to any one. and, Molly, do you be
faithful to your friend, and I will not only forgive your infidelity
to I, but will do you all the service I can." so say, he take a
hasty leave, and, slip down the ladder, retire with much
expedition.
Square be rejoice to find this adventure be likely to have no bad
conclusion; and as for Molly, be recover from her confusion, she
begin at first to upbraid Square with have be the occasion of her
loss of Jones; but that gentleman soon find the mean of mitigate
her anger, partly by caress, and partly by a small nostrum from his
purse, of wonderful and approve efficacy in purge off the ill
humour of the mind, and in restore it to a good temper.
she then pour forth a vast profusion of tenderness towards her new
lover; turn all she have say to Jones, and Jones himself, into
ridicule; and vow, though he once have the possession of her person,
that none but Square have ever be master of her heart.
Chapter vi.
by compare which with the former, the reader may possibly correct
some abuse which he hath formerly be guilty of in the application of
the word love.
the infidelity of Molly, which Jones have now discover, would,
perhaps, have vindicate a much great degree of resentment than he
express on the occasion; and if he have abandon she directly from
that moment, very few, I believe, would have blame he.
certain, however, it be, that he see she in the light of compassion;
and though his love to she be not of that kind which could give he
any great uneasiness at her inconstancy, yet be he not a little
shocked on reflect that he have himself originally corrupt her
innocence; for to this corruption he impute all the vice into which
she appear now so likely to plunge herself.
this consideration give he no little uneasiness, till Betty, the
eld sister, be so kind, some time afterwards, entirely to cure he
by a hint, that one will Barnes, and not himself, have be the first
seducer of Molly; and that the little child, which he have hitherto so
certainly conclude to be his own, might very probably have an equal
title, at least, to claim Barnes for its father.
Jones eagerly pursue this scent when he have first receive it; and in
a very short time be sufficiently assure that the girl have tell he
truth, not only by the confession of the fellow, but at last by that
of Molly herself.
this will Barnes be a country gallant, and have acquire as many
trophy of this kind as any ensign or attorney's clerk in the
kingdom. he have, indeed, reduce several woman to a state of utter
profligacy, have break the heart of some, and have the honour of
occasion the violent death of one poor girl, who have either drown
herself, or, what be rather more probable, have be drown by he.
among other of his conquest, this fellow have triumph over the heart
of Betty Seagrim. he have make love to she long before molly be grow
to be a fit object of that pastime; but have afterwards desert she,
and apply to her sister, with whom he have almost immediate success.
now will have, in reality, the sole possession of Molly's affection,
while Jones and Square be almost equally sacrifice to her interest
and to her pride.
hence have grow that implacable hatred which we have before see
rag in the mind of Betty; though we do not think it necessary to
assign this cause soon, as envy itself alone be adequate to all the
effect we have mention.
Jones be become perfectly easy by possession of this secret with
regard to Molly; but as to Sophia, he be far from be in a state of
tranquillity; nay, indeed, he be under the most violent perturbation;
his heart be now, if I may use the metaphor, entirely evacuate, and
Sophia take absolute possession of it. he love she with an unbounded
passion, and plainly see the tender sentiment she have for he; yet
could not this assurance lessen his despair of obtain the consent
of her father, nor the horror which attend his pursuit of she by
any base or treacherous method.
the injury which he must thus do to Mr Western, and the concern which
would accrue to Mr Allworthy, be circumstance that torment he
all day, and haunt he on his pillow at night. his life be a
constant struggle between honour and inclination, which alternately
triumph over each other in his mind. he often resolve, in the
absence of Sophia, to leave her father's house, and to see her no
more; and as often, in her presence, forget all those resolution, and
determine to pursue she at the hazard of his life, and at the
forfeiture of what be much dearer to he.
this conflict begin soon to produce very strong and visible effect:
for he lose all his usual sprightliness and gaiety of temper, and
become not only melancholy when alone, but dejected and absent in
company; nay, if ever he put on a forced mirth, to comply with Mr
Western's humour, the constraint appear so plain, that he seem to
have be give the strong evidence of what he endeavour to
conceal by such ostentation.
it may, perhaps, be a question, whether the art which he use to
conceal his passion, or the mean which honest nature employ to
reveal it, betray he most: for while art make he more than ever
reserve to Sophia, and forbad he to address any of his discourse to
she, nay, to avoid meet her eye, with the utmost caution; nature
be no less busy in counterplot he. hence, at the approach of the
young lady, he grow pale; and if this be sudden, start. if his eye
accidentally meet her, the blood rush into his cheek, and his
countenance become all over scarlet. if common civility ever oblige
he to speak to she, as to drink her health at table, his tongue be
sure to falter. if he touch she, his hand, nay his whole frame,
tremble. and if any discourse tend, however remotely, to raise the
idea of love, an involuntary sigh seldom fail to steal from his
bosom. Most of which accident nature be wonderfully industrious to
throw daily in his way.
all these symptom escape the notice of the squire: but not so of
Sophia. she soon perceive these agitation of mind in Jones, and be
at no loss to discover the cause; for indeed she recognize it in her
own breast. and this recognition be, I suppose, that sympathy which
hath be so often note in lover, and which will sufficiently
account for she be so much quick-sighted than her father.
but, to say the truth, there be a more simple and plain method of
accounting for that prodigious superiority of penetration which we
must observe in some man over the rest of the human specie, and one
which will serve not only in the case of lover, but of all other.
from whence be it that the knave be generally so quick-sighted to
those symptom and operation of knavery, which often dupe an honest
man of a much well understanding? there surely be no general
sympathy among knave; nor have they, like freemason, any common sign
of communication. in reality, it be only because they have the same
thing in their head, and their thought be turn the same way.
thus, that Sophia see, and that Western do not see, the plain
symptom of love in Jones can be no wonder, when we consider that the
idea of love never enter into the head of the father, whereas the
daughter, at present, think of nothing else.
when Sophia be well satisfied of the violent passion which torment
poor Jones, and no less certain that she herself be its object, she
have not the least difficulty in discover the true cause of his
present behaviour. this highly endear he to she, and raise in her
mind two of the good affection which any lover can wish to raise in a
mistress--these be, esteem and pity--for sure the most outrageously
rigid among her sex will excuse she pity a man whom she see
miserable on her own account; nor can they blame she for esteem one
who visibly, from the most honourable motif, endeavour to smother
a flame in his own bosom, which, like the famous Spartan theft, be
prey upon and consume his very vital. thus his backwardness, his
shun she, his coldness, and his silence, be the forward, the
most diligent, the warm, and most eloquent advocate; and wrought
so violently on her sensible and tender heart, that she soon feel for
he all those gentle sensation which be consistent with a virtuous
and elevated female mind. in short, all which esteem, gratitude, and
pity, can inspire in such towards an agreeable man--indeed, all which
the nice delicacy can allow. in a word, she be in love with he to
distraction.
one day this young couple accidentally meet in the garden, at the end
of the two walk which be both bound by that canal in which Jones
have formerly risque drown to retrieve the little bird that Sophia
have there lose.
this place have be of late much frequent by Sophia. here she use
to ruminate, with a mixture of pain and pleasure, on an incident
which, however trifle in itself, have possibly sow the first seed
of that affection which be now arrive to such maturity in her heart.
here then this young couple meet. they be almost close together
before either of they know anything of the other's approach. a
bystander would have discover sufficient mark of confusion in the
countenance of each; but they feel too much themselves to make any
observation. as soon as Jones have a little recover his first
surprize, he accost the young lady with some of the ordinary form
of salutation, which she in the same manner return; and their
conversation begin, as usual, on the delicious beauty of the morning.
hence they past to the beauty of the place, on which Jones launch
forth very high encomium. when they come to the tree whence he have
formerly tumble into the canal, Sophia could not help remind he
of that accident, and say, "I fancy, Mr Jones, you have some little
shuddering when you see that water."--"I assure you, madam," answer
Jones, "the concern you feel at the loss of your little bird will
always appear to I the high circumstance in that adventure. poor
little Tommy! there be the branch he stand upon. how could the little
wretch have the folly to fly away from that state of happiness in
which I have the honour to place he? his fate be a just punishment
for his ingratitude."--"upon my word, Mr Jones," say she, "your
gallantry very narrowly escape as severe a fate. sure the remembrance
must affect you."--"Indeed, madam," answer he, "if I have any reason
to reflect with sorrow on it, it be, perhaps, that the water have not
be a little deep, by which I might have escape many bitter
heart-ache that Fortune seem to have in store for I."--"Fie, Mr
Jones!" reply Sophia; "I be sure you cannot be in earnest now. this
affected contempt of life be only an excess of your complacence to I.
you would endeavour to lessen the obligation of have twice venture
it for my sake. beware the third time." she speak these last word
with a smile, and a softness inexpressible. Jones answer with a
sigh, "he fear it be already too late for caution:" and then
look tenderly and stedfastly on she, he cry, "oh, Miss Western!
can you desire I to live? can you wish I so ill?" Sophia, look
down on the ground, answer with some hesitation, "indeed, Mr Jones,
I do not wish you ill."--"Oh, I know too well that heavenly temper,"
cry Jones, "that divine goodness, which be beyond every other
charm."--"Nay, now," answer she, "I understand you not. I can stay
no long."--"I--I would not be understand!" cry he; "nay, I cannot
be understand. I know not what I say. meet you here so
unexpectedly, I have be unguarde: for Heaven's sake pardon I, if I
have say anything to offend you. I do not mean it. indeed, I would
rather have die--nay, the very thought would kill I."--"you surprize
I," answer she. "how can you possibly think you have offend
me?"--"fear, madam," say he, "easily run into madness; and there be
no degree of fear like that which I feel of offend you. how can I
speak then? Nay, donot look angrily at I: one frown will destroy I.
I mean nothing. blame my eye, or blame those beauty. what be I
say? pardon I if I have say too much. my heart overflow. I have
struggle with my love to the utmost, and have endeavour to conceal
a fever which prey on my vital, and will, I hope, soon make it
impossible for I ever to offend you more."
Mr Jones now fall a trembling as if he have be shake with the fit of
an ague. Sophia, who be in a situation not very different from his,
answer in these word: "Mr Jones, I will not affect to misunderstand
you; indeed, I understand you too well; but, for Heaven's sake, if you
have any affection for I, let I make the good of my way into the
house. I wish I may be able to support myself thither."
Jones, who be hardly able to support himself, offer she his arm,
which she condescend to accept, but beg he would not mention a
word more to she of this nature at present. he promise he would not;
insist only on her forgiveness of what love, without the leave of
his will, have force from he: this, she tell he, he know how to
obtain by his future behaviour; and thus this young pair totter and
tremble along, the lover not once dare to squeeze the hand of his
mistress, though it be lock in his.
Sophia immediately retire to her chamber, where Mrs Honour and the
hartshorn be summon to her assistance. as to poor Jones, the only
relief to his distempered mind be an unwelcome piece of news, which,
as it open a scene of different nature from those in which the reader
hath lately be conversant, will be communicate to he in the next

Chapter vii.
in which Mr Allworthy appear on a sick-bed.
Mr Western be become so fond of Jones that he be unwilling to part
with he, though his arm have be long since cure; and Jones, either
from the love of sport, or from some other reason, be easily
persuade to continue at his house, which he do sometimes for a
fortnight together without pay a single visit at Mr Allworthy's;
nay, without ever hear from thence.
Mr Allworthy have be for some day indispose with a cold, which have
be attend with a little fever. this he have, however, neglect; as
it be usual with he to do all manner of disorder which do not
confine he to his bed, or prevent his several faculty from
perform their ordinary functions;--a conduct which we would by no
mean be think to approve or recommend to imitation; for surely the
gentleman of the aesculapian art be in the right in advise, that
the moment the disease have enter at one door, the physician should
be introduce at the other: what else be mean by that old adage,
_Venienti occurrite morbo?_ "oppose a distemper at its first
approach." thus the doctor and the disease meet in fair and equal
conflict; whereas, by give time to the latter, we often suffer he
to fortify and entrench himself, like a french army; so that the
learn gentleman find it very difficult, and sometimes impossible,
to come at the enemy. Nay, sometimes by gain time the disease
apply to the french military politic, and corrupt nature over to
his side, and then all the power of physic must arrive too late.
agreeable to these observation be, I remember, the complaint of the
great Doctor Misaubin, who use very pathetically to lament the late
application which be make to his skill, say, "Bygar, I believe
my pation take I for de undertaker, for dey never send for I till de
physicion have kill dem."
Mr Allworthy's distemper, by mean of this neglect, gain such
ground, that, when the increase of his fever oblige he to send for
assistance, the doctor at his first arrival shake his head, wish he
have be send for soon, and intimate that he think he in very
imminent danger. Mr Allworthy, who have settle all his affair in this
world, and be as well prepared as it be possible for human nature to
be for the other, receive this information with the utmost calmness
and unconcern. he could, indeed, whenever he lay himself down to
rest, say with Cato in the tragical poem--
let guilt or fear
Disturb man's rest: Cato know neither of they;
Indifferent in his choice to sleep or die.
in reality, he could say this with ten time more reason and
confidence than Cato, or any other proud fellow among the antient or
modern hero; for he be not only devoid of fear, but might be
consider as a faithful labourer, when at the end of harvest he be
summon to receive his reward at the hand of a bountiful master.
the good man give immediate order for all his family to be summon
round he. none of these be then abroad, but Mrs Blifil, who have
be some time in London, and Mr Jones, whom the reader hath just
part from at Mr Western's, and who receive this summon just as
Sophia have leave he.
the news of Mr Allworthy's danger (for the servant tell he he be
die) drive all thought of love out of his head. he hurry
instantly into the chariot which be send for he, and order the
coachman to drive with all imaginable haste; nor do the idea of
Sophia, I believe, once occur to he on the way.
and now the whole family, namely, Mr Blifil, Mr Jones, Mr Thwackum, Mr
Square, and some of the servant (for such be Mr Allworthy's order)
be all assemble round his bed, the good man sit up in it, and be
begin to speak, when Blifil fall to blubber, and begin to
express very loud and bitter lamentation. upon this Mr Allworthy
shake he by the hand, and say, "do not sorrow thus, my dear nephew,
at the most ordinary of all human occurrence. when misfortune befal
our friend we be justly grieve; for those be accident which might
often have be avoid, and which may seem to render the lot of one
man more peculiarly unhappy than that of other; but death be
certainly unavoidable, and be that common lot in which alone the
fortune of all man agree: nor be the time when this happen to we
very material. if the wise of man hath compare life to a span,
surely we may be allow to consider it as a day. it be my fate to
leave it in the evening; but those who be take away early have
only lose a few hour, at the good little worth lamenting, and much
oftener hour of labour and fatigue, of pain and sorrow. one of the
roman poet, I remember, liken our leave life to our departure from
a feast;--a thought which hath often occur to I when I have see
man struggle to protract an entertainment, and to enjoy the company
of their friend a few moment long. alas! how short be the most
protract of such enjoyment! how immaterial the difference between
he who retire the soon, and he who stay the late! this be
see life in the good view, and this unwillingness to quit our
friend be the most amiable motive from which we can derive the fear
of death; and yet the long enjoyment which we can hope for of this
kind be of so trivial a duration, that it be to a wise man truly
contemptible. few man, I own, think in this manner; for, indeed, few
