man think of death till they be in its jaw. however gigantic and
terrible an object this may appear when it approach they, they be
nevertheless incapable of see it at any distance; nay, though they
have be ever so much alarmed and frighten when they have
apprehend themselves in danger of die, they be no soon clear
from this apprehension than even the fear of it be erase from their
mind. but, alas! he who escape from death be not pardon; he be
only reprieve, and reprieve to a short day.
"Grieve, therefore, no more, my dear child, on this occasion: an event
which may happen every hour; which every element, nay, almost every
particle of matter that surround we be capable of produce, and
which must and will most unavoidably reach we all at last, ought
neither to occasion our surprize nor our lamentation.
"my physician have acquaint I (which I take very kindly of he)
that I be in danger of leave you all very shortly, I have determine
to say a few word to you at this our parting, before my distemper,
which I find grow very fast upon I, put it out of my power.
"but I shall waste my strength too much. I intend to speak
concern my will, which, though I have settle long ago, I think
proper to mention such head of it as concern any of you, that I may
have the comfort of perceive you be all satisfied with the
provision I have there make for you.
"Nephew Blifil, I leave you the heir to my whole estate, except only
£500 a-year, which be to revert to you after the death of your mother,
and except one other estate of £500 a-year, and the sum of £6000,
which I have bestow in the following manner:
"the estate of £500 a-year I have give to you, Mr Jones: and as I
know the inconvenience which attend the want of ready money, I have
add £1000 in specie. in this I know not whether I have exceed or
fall short of your expectation. perhaps you will think I have give
you too little, and the world will be as ready to condemn I for
give you too much; but the latter censure I despise; and as to the
former, unless you should entertain that common error which I have
often hear in my life plead as an excuse for a total want of
charity, namely, that instead of raise gratitude by voluntary act
of bounty, we be apt to raise demand, which of all other be the
most boundless and most difficult to satisfy.--pardon I the bare
mention of this; I will not suspect any such thing."
Jones fling himself at his benefactor's foot, and take eagerly hold
of his hand, assure he his goodness to he, both now and all other
time, have so infinitely exceed not only his merit but his hope,
that no word could express his sense of it. "and I assure you, sir,"
say he, "your present generosity hath leave I no other concern than
for the present melancholy occasion. oh, my friend, my father!" here
his word choake he, and he turn away to hide a tear which be
start from his eye.
Allworthy then gently squeeze his hand, and proceed thus: "I be
convince, my child, that you have much goodness, generosity, and
honour, in your temper: if you will add prudence and religion to
these, you must be happy; for the three former quality, I admit,
make you worthy of happiness, but they be the latter only which will
put you in possession of it.
"one thousand pound I have give to you, Mr Thwackum; a sum I be
convince which greatly exceed your desire, as well as your want.
however, you will receive it as a memorial of my friendship; and
whatever superfluity may redound to you, that piety which you so
rigidly maintain will instruct you how to dispose of they.
"a like sum, Mr Square, I have bequeath to you. this, I hope, will
enable you to pursue your profession with well success than
hitherto. I have often observe with concern, that distress be more
apt to excite contempt than commiseration, especially among man of
business, with whom poverty be understand to indicate want of ability.
but the little I have be able to leave you will extricate you from
those difficulty with which you have formerly struggle; and then I
doubt not but you will meet with sufficient prosperity to supply what
a man of your philosophical temper will require.
"I find myself grow faint, so I shall refer you to my will for my
disposition of the residue. my servant will there find some token to
remember I by; and there be a few charity which, I trust, my
executor will see faithfully perform. bless you all. I be set
out a little before you."--
here a footman come hastily into the room, and say there be an
attorney from Salisbury who have a particular message, which he say he
must communicate to Mr Allworthy himself: that he seem in a violent
hurry, and protest he have so much business to do, that, if he could
cut himself into four quarter, all would not be sufficient.
"go, child," say Allworthy to Blifil, "see what the gentleman want.
I be not able to do any business now, nor can he have any with I, in
which you be not at present more concerned than myself. besides, I
really be--I be incapable of see any one at present, or of any
long attention." he then salute they all, say, perhaps he should
be able to see they again, but he should be now glad to compose
himself a little, find that he have too much exhausted his spirit
in discourse.
some of the company shed tear at their parting; and even the
philosopher Square wipe his eye, albeit unused to the melt mood.
as to Mrs Wilkins, she dropt her pearl as fast as the arabian tree
their medicinal gum; for this be a ceremonial which that gentlewoman
never omit on a proper occasion.
after this Mr Allworthy again lay himself down on his pillow, and
endeavour to compose himself to rest.
Chapter viii.
contain matter rather natural than pleasing.
besides grief for her master, there be another source for that briny
stream which so plentifully rise above the two mountainous cheek-bone
of the housekeeper. she be no soon retire, than she begin to
mutter to herself in the follow pleasant strain: "sure master might
have make some difference, methink, between I and the other
servant. I suppose he hath leave I mourning; but, i'fackins! if that
be all, the devil shall wear it for he, for I. Iwould have his worship
know I be no beggar. I have save five hundred pound in his service,
and after all to be use in this manner.--it be a fine encouragement
to servant to be honest; and to be sure, if I have take a little
something now and then, other have take ten time as much; and now
we be all put in a lump together. if so be that it be so, the legacy
may go to the devil with he that give it. no, I willnot give it up
neither, because that will please some folk. no, Iwill buy the gay
gown I can get, and dance over the old curmudgeon's grave in it. this
be my reward for take his part so often, when all the country have
cry shame of he, for breed up his bastard in that manner; but he
be go now where he must pay for all. it would have become he
well to have repent of his sin on his deathbed, than to glory in
they, and give away his estate out of his own family to a misbegotten
child. find in his bed, forsooth! a pretty story! ay, ay, those that
hide know where to find. Lord forgive he! I warrant he hath many more
bastard to answer for, if the truth be know. one comfort be, they
will all be know where he be a go now.--`the servant will find
some token to remember I by.' those be the very word; I shall
never forget they, if I be to live a thousand year. ay, ay, I shall
remember you for huddle I among the servant. one would have
think he might have mention my name as well as that of Square; but
he be a gentleman forsooth, though he have not cloth on his back when
he come hither first. Marry come up with such gentleman! though he
hath live here this many year, I donot believe there be arrow a
servant in the house ever see the colour of his money. the devil shall
wait upon such a gentleman for I." much more of the like kind she
mutter to herself; but this taste shall suffice to the reader.
neither Thwackum nor Square be much well satisfied with their
legacy. though they breathe not their resentment so loud, yet from
the discontent which appear in their countenance, as well as from
the follow dialogue, we collect that no great pleasure reign in
their mind.
about an hour after they have leave the sick-room, Square meet Thwackum
in the hall and accost he thus: "well, sir, have you hear any news
of your friend since we part from him?"--"if you mean Mr Allworthy,"
answer Thwackum, "I think you might rather give he the appellation
of your friend; for he seem to I to have deserve that title."--"The
title be as good on your side," reply Square, "for his bounty, such
as it be, hath be equal to both."--"I should not have mention it
first," cry Thwackum, "but since you begin, I must inform you I be
of a different opinion. there be a wide distinction between voluntary
favour and reward. the duty I have do in his family, and the care
I have take in the education of his two boy, be service for which
some man might have expect a great return. I would not have you
imagine I be therefore dissatisfied; for St Paul hath teach I to
be content with the little I have. have the modicum be less, I
should have know my duty. but though the scripture oblige I to
remain contented, it doth not enjoin I to shut my eye to my own
merit, nor restrain I from see when I be injure by an unjust
comparison."--"Since you provoke I," return Square, "that injury be
do to I; nor do I ever imagine Mr Allworthy have hold my friendship
so light, as to put I in balance with one who receive his wage. I
know to what it be owe; it proceed from those narrow principle
which you have be so long endeavour to infuse into he, in
contempt of everything which be great and noble. the beauty and
loveliness of friendship be too strong for dim eye, nor can it be
perceive by any other medium than that unerre rule of right, which
you have so often endeavour to ridicule, that you have pervert
your friend's understanding."--"I wish," cry Thwackum, in a rage, "I
wish, for the sake of his soul, your damnable doctrine have not
pervert his faith. it be to this I impute his present behaviour, so
unbecome a Christian. who but an atheist could think of leave the
world without have first make up his account? without confess his
sin, and receive that absolution which he know he have one in the
house duly authorize to give he? he will feel the want of these
necessary when it be too late, when he be arrive at that place
where there be wail and gnashing of tooth. it be then he will find
in what mighty stead that heathen goddess, that virtue, which you and
all other deist of the age adore, will stand he. he will then summon
his priest, when there be none to be find, and will lament the want
of that absolution, without which no sinner can be safe."--"if it be
so material," say Square, "why donot you present it he of your own
accord?" "it hath no virtue," cry Thwackum, "but to those who have
sufficient grace to require it. but why do I talk thus to a heathen
and an unbeliever? it be you that teach he this lesson, for which
you have be well reward in this world, as I doubt not your
disciple will soon be in the other."--"i know not what you mean by
reward," say Square; "but if you hint at that pitiful memorial of our
friendship, which he hath think fit to bequeath I, I despise it;
and nothing but the unfortunate situation of my circumstance should
prevail on I to accept it."
the physician now arrive, and begin to inquire of the two disputant,
how we all do above-stair? "in a miserable way," answer Thwackum.
"it be no more than I expect," cry the doctor: "but pray what
symptom have appear since I leave you?"--"no good one, I be
afraid," reply Thwackum: "after what past at our departure, I think
there be little hope." the bodily physician, perhaps, misunderstood
the curer of soul; and before they come to an explanation, Mr Blifil
come to they with a most melancholy countenance, and acquaint they
that he bring sad news, that his mother be dead at Salisbury; that
she have be seize on the road home with the gout in her head and
stomach, which have carry she off in a few hour. "good-lack-a-day!"
say the doctor. "one cannot answer for event; but I wish I have be
at hand, to have be call in. the gout be a distemper which it be
difficult to treat; yet I have be remarkably successful in it."
Thwackum and Square both condole with Mr Blifil for the loss of his
mother, which the one advise he to bear like a man, and the other
like a Christian. the young gentleman say he know very well we be
all mortal, and he would endeavour to submit to his loss as well as he
could. that he could not, however, help complain a little against
the peculiar severity of his fate, which bring the news of so great
a calamity to he by surprize, and that at a time when he hourly
expect the severe blow he be capable of feeling from the malice
of fortune. he say, the present occasion would put to the test those
excellent rudiment which he have learn from Mr Thwackum and Mr
Square; and it would be entirely owe to they, if he be enable to
survive such misfortune.
it be now debate whether Mr Allworthy should be inform of the
death of his sister. this the doctor violently oppose; in which, I
believe, the whole college would agree with he: but Mr Blifil say,
he have receive such positive and repeat order from his uncle,
never to keep any secret from he for fear of the disquietude which it
might give he, that he durst not think of disobedience, whatever
might be the consequence. he say, for his part, consider the
religious and philosophic temper of his uncle, he could not agree with
the doctor in his apprehension. he be therefore resolve to
communicate it to he: for if his uncle recover (as he heartily
pray he might) he know he would never forgive an endeavour to keep a
secret of this kind from he.
the physician be force to submit to these resolution, which the two
other learn gentleman very highly commend. so together move Mr
Blifil and the doctor toward the sick-room; where the physician first
enter, and approach the bed, in order to feel his patient's pulse,
which he have no soon do, than he declare he be much well; that
the last application have succeed to a miracle, and have bring the
fever to intermit: so that, he say, there appear now to be as
little danger as he have before apprehend there be hope.
to say the truth, Mr Allworthy's situation have never be so bad as
the great caution of the doctor have represent it: but as a wise
general never despise his enemy, however inferior that enemy's force
may be, so neither doth a wise physician ever despise a distemper,
however inconsiderable. as the former preserve the same strict
discipline, place the same guard, and employ the same scout,
though the enemy be never so weak; so the latter maintain the same
gravity of countenance, and shake his head with the same significant
air, let the distemper be never so trifling. and both, among many
other good one, may assign this solid reason for their conduct, that
by these mean the great glory redound to they if they gain the
victory, and the less disgrace if by any unlucky accident they should
happen to be conquer.
Mr Allworthy have no soon lift up his eye, and thank Heaven for
these hope of his recovery, than Mr Blifil draw near, with a very
dejected aspect, and have apply his handkerchief to his eye,
either to wipe away his tear, or to do as Ovid somewhere express
himself on another occasion
_si nullus erit, tamen excute nullum,_
if there be none, then wipe away that none,
he communicate to his uncle what the reader hath be just before
acquaint with.
Allworthy receive the news with concern, with patience, and with
resignation. he dropt a tender tear, then compose his countenance,
and at last cry, "the Lord's will be do in everything."
he now enquire for the messenger; but Blifil tell he it have be
impossible to detain he a moment; for he appear by the great hurry
he be in to have some business of importance on his hand; that he
complain of be hurry and drive and tear out of his life, and
repeat many time, that if he could divide himself into four
quarter, he know how to dispose of every one.
Allworthy then desire Blifil to take care of the funeral. he say, he
would have his sister deposit in his own chapel; and as to the
particular, he leave they to his own discretion, only mention the
person whom he would have employ on this occasion.
chapter ix.
which, among other thing, may serve as a comment on that saying of
Aeschines, that "drunkenness show the mind of a man, as a mirrour
reflect his person."
the reader may perhaps wonder at hear nothing of Mr Jones in the
last chapter. in fact, his behaviour be so different from that of the
person there mention, that we choose not to confound his name with
theirs.
when the good man have end his speech, Jones be the last who
desert the room. thence he retire to his own apartment, to give
vent to his concern; but the restlessness of his mind would not suffer
he to remain long there; he slip softly therefore to Allworthy's
chamber-door, where he listen a considerable time without hear
any kind of motion within, unless a violent snoring, which at last his
fear misrepresent as groan. this so alarm he, that he could not
forbear enter the room; where he find the good man in the bed, in
a sweet compose sleep, and his nurse snore in the above mention
hearty manner, at the bed's foot. he immediately take the only method
of silence this thorough bass, whose music he fear might disturb
Mr Allworthy; and then sit down by the nurse, he remain
motionless till Blifil and the doctor come in together and wake the
sick man, in order that the doctor might feel his pulse, and that the
other might communicate to he that piece of news, which, have Jones
be apprize of it, would have have great difficulty of find its
way to Mr Allworthy's ear at such a season.
when he first hear Blifil tell his uncle this story, Jones could
hardly contain the wrath which kindle in he at the other's
indiscretion, especially as the doctor shake his head, and declare
his unwillingness to have the matter mention to his patient. but as
his passion do not so far deprive he of all use of his
understanding, as to hide from he the consequence which any violent
expression towards Blifil might have on the sick, this apprehension
still his rage at the present; and he grow afterwards so satisfied
with find that this news have, in fact, produce no mischief, that
he suffer his anger to die in his own bosom, without ever mention
it to Blifil.
the physician din that day at Mr Allworthy's; and have after
dinner visit his patient, he return to the company, and tell they,
that he have now the satisfaction to say, with assurance, that his
patient be out of all danger: that he have bring his fever to a
perfect intermission, and doubt not by throw in the bark to
prevent its return.
this account so please Jones, and throw he into such immoderate
excess of rapture, that he might be truly say to be drunk with
joy--an intoxication which greatly forward the effect of wine; and
as he be very free too with the bottle on this occasion (for he drink
many bumper to the doctor's health, as well as to other toast) he
become very soon literally drunk.
Jones have naturally violent animal spirit: these be set on float
and augment by the spirit of wine, produce most extravagant
effect. he kiss the doctor, and embrace he with the most
passionate endearment; swear that next to Mr Allworthy himself, he
love he of all man live. "doctor," add he, "you deserve a statue
to be erect to you at the public expense, for have preserve a
man, who be not only the darling of all good man who know he, but a
blessing to society, the glory of his country, and an honour to human
nature. D--n I if I donot love he well than my own soul."
"More shame for you," cry Thwackum. "though I think you have reason
to love he, for he hath provide very well for you. and perhaps it
might have be well for some folk that he have not live to see
just reason of revoke his gift."
Jones now look on Thwackum with inconceivable disdain, answer,
"and doth thy mean soul imagine that any such consideration could
weigh with I? no, let the earth open and swallow her own dirt (if I
have million of acre I would say it) rather than swallow up my dear
glorious friend."
_Quis desiderio sit pudor aut modus
Tam chari capitis?_[*]
[*] "what modesty or measure can set bound to our desire of so dear
a friend?" the word _desiderium_ here cannot be easily translate.
it include our desire of enjoy our friend again, and the grief
which attend that desire.
the doctor now interpose, and prevent the effect of a wrath which
be kindle between Jones and Thwackum; after which the former give a
loose to mirth, sing two or three amorous song, and fall into every
frantic disorder which unbridled joy be apt to inspire; but so far be
he from any disposition to quarrel, that he be ten time well
humour, if possible, than when he be sober.
to say truth, nothing be more erroneous than the common observation,
that man who be ill-natured and quarrelsome when they be drunk, be
very worthy person when they be sober: for drink, in reality, doth
not reverse nature, or create passion in man which do not exist in
they before. it take away the guard of reason, and consequently
force we to produce those symptom, which many, when sober, have art
enough to conceal. it heighten and inflame our passion (generally
indeed that passion which be uppermost in our mind), so that the angry
temper, the amorous, the generous, the good-humour, the avaricious,
and all other disposition of man, be in their cup heighten and
expose.
and yet as no nation produce so many drunken quarrel, especially
among the low people, as England (for indeed, with they, to drink
and to fight together be almost synonymous term), I would not,
methink, have it thence conclude, that the English be the
worst-natured people alive. perhaps the love of glory only be at the
bottom of this; so that the fair conclusion seem to be, that our
countryman have more of that love, and more of bravery, than any other
plebeian. and this the rather, as there be seldom anything
ungenerous, unfair, or ill-natured, exercise on these occasion: nay,
it be common for the combatant to express good-will for each other
even at the time of the conflict; and as their drunken mirth generally
end in a battle, so do most of their battle end in friendship.
but to return to our history. though Jones have show no design of
give offence, yet Mr Blifil be highly offended at a behaviour which
be so inconsistent with the sober and prudent reserve of his own
temper. he bear it too with the great impatience, as it appear to
he very indecent at this season; "when," as he say, "the house be a
house of mourning, on the account of his dear mother; and if it have
please Heaven to give he some prospect of Mr Allworthy's recovery,
it would become they well to express the exultation of their heart
in thanksgiving, than in drunkenness and riot; which be properer
method to encrease the divine wrath, than to avert it." Thwackum, who
have swallow more liquor than Jones, but without any ill effect on
his brain, second the pious harangue of Blifil; but Square, for
reason which the reader may probably guess, be totally silent.
Wine have not so totally overpowered Jones, as to prevent his
recollect Mr Blifil's loss, the moment it be mention. as no
person, therefore, be more ready to confess and condemn his own
error, he offer to shake Mr Blifil by the hand, and beg his
pardon, say, "his excessive joy for Mr Allworthy's recovery have
drive every other thought out of his mind."
Blifil scornfully reject his hand; and with much indignation
answer, "it be little to be wonder at, if tragical spectacle
make no impression on the blind; but, for his part, he have the
misfortune to know who his parent be, and consequently must be
affect with their loss."
Jones, who, notwithstanding his good humour, have some mixture of the
irascible in his constitution, leap hastily from his chair, and
catch hold of Blifil's collar, cry out, "D--n you for a rascal,
do you insult I with the misfortune of my birth?" he accompany
these word with such rough action, that they soon get the well of
Mr Blifil's peaceful temper; and a scuffle immediately ensue, which
might have produce mischief, have it not be prevent by the
interposition of Thwackum and the physician; for the philosophy of
Square render he superior to all emotion, and he very calmly
smoake his pipe, as be his custom in all broil, unless when he
apprehend some danger of have it break in his mouth.
the combatant be now prevent from execute present vengeance on
each other, betook themselves to the common resource of disappointed
rage, and vent their wrath in threat and defiance. in this kind of
conflict, Fortune, which, in the personal attack, seem to incline to
Jones, be now altogether as favourable to his enemy.
a truce, nevertheless, be at length agree on, by the mediation of
the neutral party, and the whole company again sit down at the
table; where Jones be prevail on to ask pardon, and Blifil to
give it, peace be restore, and everything seem _in statu quo_.
but though the quarrel be, in all appearance, perfectly reconcile,
the good humour which have be interrupt by it, be by no means
restore. all merriment be now at an end, and the subsequent
discourse consist only of grave relation of matter of fact, and of
as grave observation upon they; a species of conversation, in which,
though there be much of dignity and instruction, there be but little
entertainment. as we presume therefore to convey only this last to the
reader, we shall pass by whatever be say, till the rest of the
company have by degree drop off, leave only Square and the
physician together; at which time the conversation be a little
heighten by some comment on what have happen between the two young
gentleman; both of whom the doctor declare to be no well than
scoundrel; to which appellation the philosopher, very sagaciously
shake his head, agree.
Chapter x.
show the truth of many observation of Ovid, and of other more
grave writer, who have prove beyond contradiction, that wine be
often the forerunner of incontinency.
Jones retire from the company, in which we have see he engage,
into the field, where he intend to cool himself by a walk in the
open air before he attend Mr Allworthy. there, whilst he renew
those meditation on his dear Sophia, which the dangerous illness of
his friend and benefactor have for some time interrupt, an accident
happen, which with sorrow we relate, and with sorrow doubtless will
it be read; however, that historic truth to which we profess so
inviolable an attachment, oblige we to communicate it to posterity.
it be now a pleasant evening in the latter end of June, when our
heroe be walk in a most delicious grove, where the gentle breeze
fan the leaf, together with the sweet trilling of a murmuring
stream, and the melodious note of nightingale, form altogether the
most enchanting harmony. in this scene, so sweetly accommodate to
love, he meditate on his dear Sophia. while his wanton fancy roam
unbounde over all her beauty, and his lively imagination paint
the charming maid in various ravish form, his warm heart melt
with tenderness; and at length, throw himself on the ground, by the
side of a gently murmur brook, he break forth into the follow
ejaculation:
"o Sophia, would Heaven give thee to my arm, how bl would be my
condition! Curst be that fortune which set a distance between we. be
I but possess of thee, one only suit of rag thy whole estate, be
there a man on earth whom I would envy! how contemptible would the
bright circassian beauty, dr in all the jewel of the Indies,
appear to my eye! but why do I mention another woman? could I think
my eye capable of look at any other with tenderness, these hand
should tear they from my head. no, my Sophia, if cruel fortune
separate we for ever, my soul shall doat on thee alone. the chaste
constancy will I ever preserve to thy image. though I should never
have possession of thy charming person, still shalt thou alone have
possession of my thought, my love, my soul. oh! my fond heart be so
wrapt in that tender bosom, that the bright beauty would for I
have no charm, nor would a hermit be cold in their embrace.
Sophia, Sophia alone shall be mine. what rapture be in that name! I
will engrave it on every tree."
at these word he start up, and beheld--not his Sophia--no, nor a
Circassian maid richly and elegantly attire for the grand Signior's
seraglio. no; without a gown, in a shift that be somewhat of the
coarsest, and none of the clean, bedew likewise with some
odoriferous effluvium, the produce of the day's labour, with a
pitchfork in her hand, Molly Seagrim approach. our hero have his
penknife in his hand, which he have draw for the before-mention
purpose of carve on the bark; when the girl come near he, cry
out with a smile, "you donot intend to kill I, squire, I hope!"--"why
should you think I would kill you?" answer Jones. "Nay," reply
she, "after your cruel usage of I when I see you last, kill I
would, perhaps, be too great kindness for I to expect."
here ensue a parley, which, as I do not think myself oblige to
relate it, I shall omit. it be sufficient that it last a full
quarter of an hour, at the conclusion of which they retire into the
thick part of the grove.
some of my reader may be incline to think this event unnatural.
however, the fact be true; and perhaps may be sufficiently account
for by suggest, that Jones probably think one woman well than
none, and Molly as probably imagine two man to be well than one.
besides the before-mention motive assign to the present behaviour
of Jones, the reader will be likewise pleased to recollect in his
favour, that he be not at this time perfect master of that wonderful
power of reason, which so well enable grave and wise man to subdue
their unruly passion, and to decline any of these prohibit
amusement. Wine now have totally subdue this power in Jones. he be,
indeed, in a condition, in which, if reason have interpose, though
only to advise, she might have receive the answer which one
Cleostratus give many year ago to a silly fellow, who ask he, if
he be not ashamed to be drunk? "be not you," say Cleostratus,
"ashamed to admonish a drunken man?"--to say the truth, in a court of
justice drunkenness must not be an excuse, yet in a court of
conscience it be greatly so; and therefore Aristotle, who commend the
law of Pittacus, by which drunken man receive double punishment for
their crime, allow there be more of policy than justice in that law.
now, if there be any transgression pardonable from drunkenness, they
be certainly such as Mr Jones be at present guilty of; on which head
I could pour forth a vast profusion of learn, if I imagine it
would either entertain my reader, or teach he anything more than he
know already. for his sake therefore I shall keep my learning to
myself, and return to my history.
it hath be observe, that Fortune seldom doth thing by half. to
say truth, there be no end to her freak whenever she be disposed to
gratify or displease. no soon have our heroe retire with his Dido,
but
_speluncam_ Blifil _dux et divinus eandem
Deveniunt--_
the parson and the young squire, who be take a serious walk,
arrive at the stile which lead into the grove, and the latter catch
a view of the lover just as they be sink out of sight.
Blifil know Jones very well, though he be at above a hundred yard'
distance, and he be as positive to the sex of his companion, though
not to the individual person. he start, bless himself, and utter
a very solemn ejaculation.
Thwackum express some surprize at these sudden emotion, and ask
the reason of they. to which Blifil answer, "he be certain he have
see a fellow and wench retire together among the bush, which he
doubt not be with some wicked purpose." as to the name of Jones, he
think proper to conceal it, and why he do so must be leave to the
judgment of the sagacious reader; for we never chuse to assign motif
to the action of man, when there be any possibility of our being
mistaken.
the parson, who be not only strictly chaste in his own person, but a
great enemy to the opposite vice in all other, fire at this
information. he desire Mr Blifil to conduct he immediately to the
place, which as he approach he breathe forth vengeance mix with
lamentation; nor do he refrain from cast some oblique reflection
on Mr Allworthy; insinuate that the wickedness of the country be
principally owe to the encouragement he have give to vice, by have
exert such kindness to a bastard, and by have mitigate that just
and wholesome rigour of the law which allot a very severe punishment
to loose wench.
the way through which our hunter be to pass in pursuit of their
game be so beset with briar, that it greatly obstruct their walk,
and cause besides such a rustling, that Jones have sufficient warning
of their arrival before they could surprize he; nay, indeed, so
incapable be Thwackum of conceal his indignation, and such
vengeance do he mutter forth every step he take, that this alone must
have abundantly satisfied Jones that he be (to use the language of
sportsman) find sit.
Chapter xi.
in which a simile in Mr Pope's period of a mile introduce as bloody a
battle as can possibly be fight without the assistance of steel or
cold iron.
as in the season of _rut_ (an uncouth phrase, by which the vulgar
denote that gentle dalliance, which in the well-woode[*] forest of
Hampshire, pass between lover of the ferine kind), if, while the
lofty-crest stag meditate the amorous sport, a couple of puppy,
or any other beast of hostile note, should wander so near the temple
of Venus Ferina that the fair hind should shrink from the place,
touch with that somewhat, either of fear or frolic, of nicety or
skittishness, with which nature hath bedeck all female, or hath at
least instruct they how to put it on; lest, through the indelicacy
of male, the samean mystery should be pry into by unhallowed
eye: for, at the celebration of these rite, the female priestess
cry out with she in Virgil (who be then, probably, hard at work on
such celebration),
_--Procul, o procul este, profani;
Proclamat vate, totoque absistite luco._
--Far hence be soul profane,
the sibyl cry'd, and from the grove abstain.--DRYDEN.
[*] this be an ambiguous phrase, and may mean either a forest well
cloathe with wood, or well stript of it.
if, I say, while these sacred rite, which be in common to _genus
omne animantium,_ be in agitation between the stag and his mistress,
any hostile beast should venture too near, on the first hint give by
the fright hind, fierce and tremendous rush forth the stag to the
entrance of the thicket; there stand he centinel over his love,
stamp the ground with his foot, and with his horn brandish aloft
in air, proudly provoke the apprehended foe to combat.
thus, and more terrible, when he perceive the enemy's approach,
leap forth our heroe. many a step advance he forwards, in order to
conceal the trembling hind, and, if possible, to secure her retreat.
and now Thwackum, have first dart some livid lightning from his
fiery eye, begin to thunder forth, "fie upon it! fie upon it! Mr
Jones. be it possible you should be the person?"--"you see," answer
Jones, "it be possible I should be here."--"And who," say Thwackum,
"be that wicked slut with you?"--"if I have any wicked slut with I,"
cry Jones, "it be possible I shall not let you know who she be."--"I
command you to tell I immediately," say Thwackum: "and I would not
have you imagine, young man, that your age, though it hath somewhat
abridge the purpose of tuition, hath totally take away the authority
of the master. the relation of the master and scholar be indelible;
as, indeed, all other relation be; for they all derive their
original from heaven. I would have you think yourself, therefore, as
much oblige to obey I now, as when I teach you your first
rudiment."--"i believe you would," cry Jones; "but that will not
happen, unless you have the same birchen argument to convince
I."--"Then I must tell you plainly," say Thwackum, "I be resolve to
discover the wicked wretch."--"And I must tell you plainly," return
Jones, "I be resolve you shall not." Thwackum then offer to
advance, and Jones lay hold of his arm; which Mr Blifil endeavour
to rescue, declare, "he would not see his old master insult."
Jones now find himself engage with two, think it necessary to
rid himself of one of his antagonist as soon as possible. he
therefore apply to the weak first; and, let the parson go, he
direct a blow at the young squire's breast, which luckily take
place, reduce he to measure his length on the ground.
Thwackum be so intent on the discovery, that, the moment he find
himself at liberty, he stept forward directly into the fern, without
any great consideration of what might in the meantime befal his
friend; but he have advance a very few pace into the thicket, before
Jones, have defeat Blifil, overtake the parson, and drag he
backward by the skirt of his coat.
this parson have be a champion in his youth, and have win much honour
by his fist, both at school and at the university. he have now indeed,
for a great number of year, decline the practice of that noble art;
yet be his courage full as strong as his faith, and his body no less
strong than either. he be moreover, as the reader may perhaps have
conceive, somewhat irascible in his nature. when he look back,
therefore, and see his friend stretch out on the ground, and find
himself at the same time so roughly handle by one who have formerly
be only passive in all conflict between they (a circumstance which
highly aggravate the whole), his patience at length give way; he
throw himself into a posture of offence; and collect all his force,
attack Jones in the front with as much impetuosity as he have
formerly attack he in the rear.
our heroe receive the enemy's attack with the most undaunted
intrepidity, and his bosom resound with the blow. this he presently
return with no less violence, aim likewise at the parson's
breast; but he dexterously drive down the fist of Jones, so that it
reach only his belly, where two pound of beef and as many of
pudding be then deposit, and whence consequently no hollow sound
could proceed. many lusty blow, much more pleasant as well as easy to
have see, than to read or describe, be give on both side: at last
a violent fall, in which Jones have throw his knee into Thwackum's
breast, so weaken the latter, that victory have be no long
dubious, have not Blifil, who have now recover his strength, again
renew the fight, and by engage with Jones, give the parson a
moment's time to shake his ear, and to regain his breath.
and now both together attack our heroe, whose blow do not retain
that force with which they have fall at first, so weakened be he by
his combat with Thwackum; for though the pedagogue choose rather to
play _solo_ on the human instrument, and have be lately use to
those only, yet he still retain enough of his antient knowledge to
perform his part very well in a _duet_.
the victory, accord to modern custom, be like to be decide by
number, when, on a sudden, a fourth pair of fist appear in the
battle, and immediately pay their compliment to the parson; and the
owner of they at the same time cry out, "be not you ashamed, and
be d--n'd to you, to fall two of you upon one?"
the battle, which be of the kind that for distinction's sake be
call royal, now rag with the utmost violence during a few minute;
till Blifil be a second time lay sprawl by Jones, Thwackum
condescend to apply for quarter to his new antagonist, who be now
find to be Mr Western himself; for in the heat of the action none of
the combatant have recognize he.
in fact, that honest squire, happen, in his afternoon's walk with
some company, to pass through the field where the bloody battle be
fight, and have conclude, from see three man engage, that two
of they must be on a side, he hasten from his companion, and with
more gallantry than policy, espouse the cause of the weak party. by
which generous proceeding he very probably prevent Mr Jones from
become a victim to the wrath of Thwackum, and to the pious
friendship which Blifil bear his old master; for, besides the
disadvantage of such odd, Jones have not yet sufficiently recover
the former strength of his broken arm. this reinforcement, however,
soon put an end to the action, and Jones with his ally obtain the
victory.
Chapter xii.
in which be see a more moving spectacle than all the blood in the
body of Thwackum and Blifil, and of twenty other such, be capable of
produce.
the rest of Mr Western's company be now come up, be just at the
instant when the action be over. these be the honest clergyman,
whom we have formerly see at Mr Western's table; Mrs Western, the
aunt of Sophia; and lastly, the lovely Sophia herself.
at this time, the follow be the aspect of the bloody field. in one
place lie on the ground, all pale, and almost breathless, the
vanquish Blifil. near he stand the conqueror Jones, almost cover
with blood, part of which be naturally his own, and part have be
lately the property of the Reverend Mr Thwackum. in a third place
stand the say Thwackum, like King Porus, sullenly submit to the
conqueror. the last figure in the piece be western the great, most
gloriously forbear the vanquished foe.
Blifil, in whom there be little sign of life, be at first the
principal object of the concern of every one, and particularly of Mrs
Western, who have draw from her pocket a bottle of hartshorn, and be
herself about to apply it to his nostril, when on a sudden the
attention of the whole company be divert from poor Blifil, whose
spirit, if it have any such design, might have now take an opportunity
of steal off to the other world, without any ceremony.
for now a more melancholy and a more lovely object lie motionless
before they. this be no other than the charming Sophia herself, who,
from the sight of blood, or from fear for her father, or from some
other reason, have fall down in a swoon, before any one could get to
her assistance.
Mrs Western first see she and scream. immediately two or three
voice cry out, "Miss Western be dead." Hartshorn, water, every
remedy be call for, almost at one and the same instant.
the reader may remember, that in our description of this grove we
mention a murmur brook, which brook do not come there, as such
gentle stream flow through vulgar romance, with no other purpose
than to murmur. no! Fortune have decree to ennoble this little brook
with a high honour than any of those which wash the plain of
Arcadia ever deserve.
Jones be rub Blifil's temple, for he begin to fear he have give
he a blow too much, when the word, Miss Western and Dead, rush at
once on his ear. he start up, leave Blifil to his fate, and fly to
Sophia, whom, while all the rest be run against each other,
backward and forward, look for water in the dry path, he catch up
in his arm, and then run away with she over the field to the rivulet
above mention; where, plunge himself into the water, he contrive
to besprinkle her face, head, and neck very plentifully.
Happy be it for Sophia that the same confusion which prevent she
other friend from serve she, prevent they likewise from
obstruct Jones. he have carry she half way before they know what
he be do, and he have actually restore she to life before they
reach the waterside. she stretch out her arm, open her eye,
and cry, "oh! heavens!" just as her father, aunt, and the parson
come up.
Jones, who have hitherto hold this lovely burthen in his arm, now
relinquish his hold; but give she at the same instant a tender
caress, which, have her sens be then perfectly restore, could not
have escape her observation. as she express, therefore, no
displeasure at this freedom, we suppose she be not sufficiently
recover from her swoon at the time.
this tragical scene be now convert into a sudden scene of joy. in
this our heroe be certainly the principal character; for as he
probably feel more ecstatic delight in have save Sophia than she
herself receive from be save, so neither be the congratulation
pay to she equal to what be confer on Jones, especially by Mr
Western himself, who, after have once or twice embrace his
daughter, fall to hug and kiss Jones. he call he the
preserver of Sophia, and declare there be nothing, except she, or
his estate, which he would not give he; but upon recollection, he
afterwards except his fox-hound, the Chevalier, and Miss Slouch
(for so he call his favourite mare).
all fear for Sophia be now remove, Jones become the object of the
squire's consideration.--"come, my lad," say Western, "d'off thy
quoat and wash thy feace; for att in a devilish pickle, I promise
thee. come, come, wash thyself, and shat go huome with I; and we'l
zee to vind thee another quoat."
Jones immediately comply, throw off his coat, go down to the
water, and wash both his face and bosom; for the latter be as much
expose and as bloody as the former. but though the water could clear
off the blood, it could not remove the black and blue mark which
Thwackum have imprint on both his face and breast, and which, be
discern by Sophia, draw from she a sigh and a look full of
inexpressible tenderness.
Jones receive this full in his eye, and it have infinitely a strong
effect on he than all the contusion which he have receive before. an
effect, however, widely different; for so soft and balmy be it, that,
have all his former blow be stab, it would for some minute have
prevent his feeling their smart.
the company now move backwards, and soon arrive where Thwackum have
get Mr Blifil again on his leg. here we cannot suppress a pious wish,
that all quarrel be to be decide by those weapon only with which
Nature, know what be proper for we, hath supply we; and that cold
iron be to be use in dig no bowel but those of the earth. then
would war, the pastime of monarchs, be almost inoffensive, and battle
between great army might be fight at the particular desire of
several lady of quality; who, together with the king themselves,
might be actual spectator of the conflict. then might the field be
this moment well strew with human carcass, and the next, the dead
man, or infinitely the great part of they, might get up, like Mr
Bayes's troop, and march off either at the sound of a drum or fiddle,
as should be previously agree on.
I would avoid, if possible, treat this matter ludicrously, lest
grave man and politician, whom I know to be offend at a jest, may
cry pish at it; but, in reality, might not a battle be as well decide
by the great number of broken head, bloody nose, and black eye,
as by the great heap of mangle and murder human body? might
not town be contend for in the same manner? indeed, this may be
think too detrimental a scheme to the french interest, since they
would thus lose the advantage they have over other nation in the
superiority of their engineer; but when I consider the gallantry and
generosity of that people, I be persuade they would never decline
put themselves upon a par with their adversary; or, as the phrase
be, make themselves his match.
but such reformation be rather to be wish than hop for: I shall
content myself, therefore, with this short hint, and return to my
narrative.
Western begin now to inquire into the original rise of this quarrel.
to which neither Blifil nor Jones give any answer; but Thwackum say
surlily, "I believe the cause be not far off; if you beat the bush
well you may find she."--"find she?" reply Western: "what! have you
be fight for a wench?"--"ask the gentleman in his waistcoat
there," say Thwackum: "he well know." "Nay then," cry western, "it
be a wench certainly.--Ah, Tom, Tom, thou art a liquorish dog. but
come, gentleman, be all friend, and go home with I, and make final
peace over a bottle." "I ask your pardon, sir," say Thwackum: "it be
no such slight matter for a man of my character to be thus injuriously
treat, and buffet by a boy, only because I would have do my
duty, in endeavour to detect and bring to justice a wanton harlot;
but, indeed, the principal fault lie in Mr Allworthy and yourself;
for if you put the law in execution, as you ought to do, you will
soon rid the country of these vermin."
"I would as soon rid the country of fox," cry western. "I think we
ought to encourage the recruit those number which we be every day
lose in the war.--but where be she? Prithee, Tom, show I." he then
begin to beat about, in the same language and in the same manner as if
he have be beat for a hare; and at last cry out, "Soho! Puss be
not far off. herebe her form, upon my soul; I believe I may cry stole
away." and indeed so he might; for he have now discover the place
whence the poor girl have, at the beginning of the fray, steal away,
upon as many foot as a hare generally use in travel.
Sophia now desire her father to return home; say she find herself
very faint, and apprehend a relapse. the squire immediately comply
with his daughter's request (for he be the fond of parent). he
earnestly endeavour to prevail with the whole company to go and sup
with he: but Blifil and Thwackum absolutely refuse; the former
saying, there be more reason than he could then mention, why he
must decline this honour; and the latter declare (perhaps rightly)
that it be not proper for a person of his function to be see at any
place in his present condition.
Jones be incapable of refuse the pleasure of be with his Sophia;
so on he march with Squire Western and his lady, the parson
bring up the rear. this have, indeed, offer to tarry with his
brother Thwackum, profess his regard for the cloth would not permit
he to depart; but Thwackum would not accept the favour, and, with no
great civility, push he after Mr Western.
thus end this bloody fray; and thus shall end the fifth book of this
history.
BOOK VI.
contain about three weeks.
Chapter i.
of love.
in our last book we have be oblige to deal pretty much with the
passion of love; and in our succeed book shall be force to handle
this subject still more largely. it may not therefore in this place be
improper to apply ourselves to the examination of that modern
doctrine, by which certain philosopher, among many other wonderful
discovery, pretend to have find out, that there be no such passion
in the human breast.
whether these philosopher be the same with that surprising sect, who
be honourably mention by the late Dr Swift, as have, by the mere
force of genius alone, without the least assistance of any kind of
learning, or even read, discover that profound and invaluable
secret that there be no God; or whether they be not rather the same
with those who some year since very much alarm the world, by
show that there be no such thing as virtue or goodness really
exist in human nature, and who deduce our good action from pride,
I will not here presume to determine. in reality, I be inclined to
suspect, that all these several finder of truth, be the very
identical man who be by other call the finder of gold. the method
use in both these search after truth and after gold, be indeed
one and the same, viz., the searching, rummaging, and examine into a
nasty place; indeed, in the former instance, into the nasty of all
place, a bad MIND.
but though in this particular, and perhaps in their success, the
truth-finder and the gold-finder may very properly be compare
together; yet in modesty, surely, there can be no comparison between
the two; for who ever hear of a gold-finder that have the impudence or
folly to assert, from the ill success of his search, that there be no
such thing as gold in the world? whereas the truth-finder, have
rake out that jake, his own mind, and be there capable of trace
no ray of divinity, nor anything virtuous or good, or lovely, or
loving, very fairly, honestly, and logically conclude that no such
thing exist in the whole creation.
to avoid, however, all contention, if possible, with these
philosopher, if they will be call so; and to show our own
disposition to accommodate matter peaceably between we, we shall here
make they some concession, which may possibly put an end to the
dispute.
first, we will grant that many mind, and perhaps those of the
philosopher, be entirely free from the least trace of such a
passion.
secondly, that what be commonly call love, namely, the desire of
satisfy a voracious appetite with a certain quantity of delicate
white human flesh, be by no mean that passion for which I here
contend. this be indeed more properly hunger; and as no glutton be
ashamed to apply the word love to his appetite, and to say he love
such and such dish; so may the lover of this kind, with equal
propriety, say, he hunger after such and such woman.
thirdly, I will grant, which I believe will be a most acceptable
concession, that this love for which I be an advocate, though it
satisfy itself in a much more delicate manner, doth nevertheless
seek its own satisfaction as much as the gross of all our
appetite.
and, lastly, that this love, when it operate towards one of a
different sex, be very apt, towards its complete gratification, to
call in the aid of that hunger which I have mention above; and which
it be so far from abate, that it heighten all its delight to a
degree scarce imaginable by those who have never be susceptible of
any other emotion than what have proceed from appetite alone.
in return to all these concession, I desire of the philosopher to
grant, that there be in some (I believe in many) human breast a kind
and benevolent disposition, which be gratify by contribute to the
happiness of other. that in this gratification alone, as in
friendship, in parental and filial affection, as indeed in general
philanthropy, there be a great and exquisite delight. that if we will
not call such disposition love, we have no name for it. that though
the pleasure arise from such pure love may be heighten and
sweeten by the assistance of amorous desire, yet the former can
subsist alone, nor be they destroy by the intervention of the
latter. lastly, that esteem and gratitude be the proper motif to
love, as youth and beauty be to desire, and, therefore, though such
desire may naturally cease, when age or sickness overtake its object;
yet these can have no effect on love, nor ever shake or remove, from a
good mind, that sensation or passion which hath gratitude and esteem
for its basis.
to deny the existence of a passion of which we often see manif
instance, seem to be very strange and absurd; and can indeed proceed
only from that self-admonition which we have mention above: but how
unfair be this! Doth the man who recognize in his own heart no trace
of avarice or ambition, conclude, therefore, that there be no such
passion in human nature? why will we not modestly observe the same
rule in judging of the good, as well as the evil of other? or why, in
any case, will we, as Shakespear phrase it, "put the world in our own
person?"
predominant vanity be, I be afraid, too much concerned here. this be
one instance of that adulation which we bestow on our own mind, and
this almost universally. for there be scarce any man, how much soever
he may despise the character of a flatterer, but will condescend in
the mean manner to flatter himself.
to those therefore I apply for the truth of the above observation,
whose own mind can bear testimony to what I have advance.
examine your heart, my good reader, and resolve whether you do believe
these matter with I. if you do, you may now proceed to their
exemplification in the follow page: if you do not, you have, I
assure you, already read more than you have understand; and it would
be wise to pursue your business, or your pleasure (such as they
be), than to throw away any more of your time in read what you can
neither taste nor comprehend. to treat of the effect of love to you,
must be as absurd as to discourse on colour to a man bear blind;
since possibly your idea of love may be as absurd as that which we be
tell such blind man once entertain of the colour scarlet; that
colour seem to he to be very much like the sound of a trumpet: and
love probably may, in your opinion, very greatly resemble a dish of
soup, or a surloin of roast-beef.
Chapter ii.
the character of Mrs Western. her great learning and knowledge of the
world, and an instance of the deep penetration which she derive from
those advantage.
the reader hath see Mr Western, his sister, and daughter, with young
Jones, and the parson, go together to Mr Western's house, where the
great part of the company spend the evening with much joy and
festivity. Sophia be indeed the only grave person; for as to Jones,
though love have now get entire possession of his heart, yet the
pleasing reflection on Mr Allworthy's recovery, and the presence of
his mistress, join to some tender look which she now and then could
not refrain from give he, so elevate our heroe, that he join the
mirth of the other three, who be perhaps as good-humour people as
any in the world.
Sophia retain the same gravity of countenance the next morning at
breakfast; whence she retire likewise early than usual, leave she
father and aunt together. the squire take no notice of this change in
his daughter's disposition. to say the truth, though he be somewhat
of a politician, and have be twice a candidate in the country
interest at an election, he be a man of no great observation. his
sister be a lady of a different turn. she have live about the court,
and have see the world. hence she have acquire all that knowledge
which the say world usually communicate; and be a perfect mistress
of manner, custom, ceremony, and fashion. nor do her erudition
stop here. she have considerably improve her mind by study; she have
not only read all the modern play, opera, oratorio, poem, and
romance--in all which she be a critic; but have go through Rapin's
history of England, Eachard's Roman history, and many french _mémoire
pour servir à l'Histoire_: to these she have add most of the
political pamphlet and journal publish within the last twenty
year. from which she have attain a very competent skill in politic,
and could discourse very learnedly on the affair of Europe. she be,
moreover, excellently well skilled in the doctrine of amour, and know
well than anybody who and who be together; a knowledge which she
the more easily attain, as her pursuit of it be never divert by
any affair of her own; for either she have no inclination, or they
have never be solicit; which last be indeed very probable; for her
masculine person, which be near six foot high, add to her manner
and learning, possibly prevent the other sex from regard she,
notwithstanding her petticoat, in the light of a woman. however, as
she have consider the matter scientifically, she perfectly well know,
though she have never practise they, all the art which fine lady
use when they desire to give encouragement, or to conceal liking, with
all the long appendage of smile, ogle, glance, &c., as they be at
present practise in the beau-monde. to sum the whole, no specie of
disguise or affectation have escape her notice; but as to the plain
simple working of honest nature, as she have never see any such, she
could know but little of they.
by mean of this wonderful sagacity, Mrs Western have now, as she
think, make a discovery of something in the mind of Sophia. the
first hint of this she take from the behaviour of the young lady in
the field of battle; and the suspicion which she then conceive, be
greatly corroborate by some observation which she have make that
evening and the next morning. however, be greatly cautious to avoid
be find in a mistake, she carry the secret a whole fortnight in
her bosom, give only some oblique hint, by simper, wink, nod,
and now and then drop an obscure word, which indeed sufficiently
alarm Sophia, but do not at all affect her brother.
be at length, however, thoroughly satisfied of the truth of her
observation, she take an opportunity, one morning, when she be alone
with her brother, to interrupt one of his whistle in the follow
manner:--
"pray, brother, have you not observe something very extraordinary in my
niece lately?"--"No, not I," answer western; "be anything the matter
with the girl?"--"i think there be," reply she; "and something of
much consequence too."--"why, she doth not complain of anything,"
cry western; "and she hath have the small-pox."--"brother," return
she, "girl be liable to other distemper besides the small-pox, and
sometimes possibly to much bad." here Western interrupt she with
much earnestness, and beg she, if anything ail his daughter, to
acquaint he immediately; add, "she know he love she more than his
own soul, and that he would send to the world's end for the good
physician to she." "Nay, nay," answer she, smile, "the distemper
be not so terrible; but I believe, brother, you be convince I know
the world, and I promise you I be never more deceive in my life, if
my niece be not most desperately in love."--"how! in love!" cry
western, in a passion; "in love, without acquaint I! Iwill
disinherit she; Iwill turn she out of door, stark naked, without a
farthing. be all my kindness vor 'ur, and vondness o'ur come to this,
to fall in love without ask I leave?"--"but you will not,"
answer Mrs Western, "turn this daughter, whom you love well than
your own soul, out of door, before you know whether you shall approve
her choice. suppose she should have fix on the very person whom you
yourself would wish, I hope you would not be angry then?"--"no, no,"
cry western, "that would make a difference. if she marry the man I
would ha' she, she may love whom she please, I shallnot trouble my head
about that." "that be speak," answer the sister, "like a sensible
man; but I believe the very person she hath choose would be the very
person you would choose for she. I will disclaim all knowledge of the
world, if it be not so; and I believe, brother, you will allow I have
some."--"Why, lookee, sister," say western, "I do believe you have as
much as any woman; and to be sure those be woman's matter. you know
I donot love to hear you talk about politic; they belong to we, and
petticoat should not meddle: but come, who be the man?"--"marry!"
say she, "you may find he out yourself if you please. you, who be
so great a politician, can be at no great loss. the judgment which can
penetrate into the cabinet of prince, and discover the secret
spring which move the great state wheel in all the political
machine of Europe, must surely, with very little difficulty, find out
what pass in the rude uninformed mind of a girl."--"sister," cry
the squire, "I have often warn'd you not to talk the court gibberish
to I. I tell you, I donot understand the lingo: but I can read a
journal, or the _London Evening Post._ perhaps, indeed, there may be
now and tan a verse which I cannot make much of, because half the
letter be leave out; yet I know very well what be mean by that, and
that our affair donot go so well as they should do, because of
bribery and corruption."--"I pity your country ignorance from my
heart," cry the lady.--"Do you?" answer Western; "and I pity your
town learning; I have rather be anything than a courtier, and a
Presbyterian, and a Hanoverian too, as some people, I believe,
be."--"if you mean I," answer she, "you know I be a woman,
brother; and it signify nothing what I be. Besides--"--"I do know
you be a woman," cry the squire, "and itbe well for thee that art
one; if hadst be a man, I promise thee I have lend thee a flick long
ago."--"Ay, there," say she, "in that flick lie all your fancied
superiority. your body, and not your brain, be strong than our.
believe I, it be well for you that you be able to beat we; or, such
be the superiority of our understanding, we should make all of you
what the brave, and wise, and witty, and polite be already--our
slave."--"i be glad I know your mind," answer the squire. "but
wewill talk more of this matter another time. at present, do tell I
what man be it you mean about my daughter?"--"hold a moment," say
she, "while I digest that sovereign contempt I have for your sex; or
else I ought to be angry too with you. there--I have make a shift to
gulp it down. and now, good politic sir, what think you of Mr Blifil?
do she not faint away on see he lie breathless on the ground? do
she not, after he be recover, turn pale again the moment we come up
to that part of the field where he stand? and pray what else should be
the occasion of all her melancholy that night at supper, the next
morning, and indeed ever since?"--"'Fore George!" cry the squire,
"now you mind I on't, I remember it all. it be certainly so, and I be
glad on't with all my heart. I know Sophy be a good girl, and would
not fall in love to make I angry. I be never more rejoice in my
life; for nothing can lie so handy together as our two estate. I have
this matter in my head some time ago: for certainly the two estate
be in a manner join together in matrimony already, and it would be
a thousand pity to part they. it be true, indeed, there be large
estate in the kingdom, but not in this county, and I have rather bate
something, than marry my daughter among stranger and foreigner.
besides, most o' zuch great estate be in the hand of lord, and I
heate the very name of _themmun_. well but, sister, what would you
advise I to do; for I tell you woman know these matter well than
we do?"--"oh, your humble servant, sir," answer the lady: "we be
oblige to you for allow we a capacity in anything. since you be
pleased, then, most politic sir, to ask my advice, I think you may
propose the match to Allworthy yourself. there be no indecorum in the
proposalbe come from the parent of either side. King Alcinous, in Mr
Pope's odyssey, offer his daughter to Ulysses. I need not caution so
politic a person not to say that your daughter be in love; that would
indeed be against all rule."--"Well," say the squire, "I will
propose it; but I shall certainly lend un a flick, if he should refuse
I." "fear not," cry Mrs Western; "the match be too advantageous to
be refuse." "I donot know that," answer the squire: "Allworthy be a
queer b--ch, and money hath no effect o'un." "Brother," say the lady,
"your politic astonish I. be you really to be impose on by
profession? do you think Mr Allworthy hath more contempt for money
than other man because he profess more? such credulity would well
become one of we weak woman, than that wise sex which heaven hath
form for politician. indeed, brother, you would make a fine plenipo
to negotiate with the French. they would soon persuade you, that they
take town out of mere defensive principle." "Sister," answer the
squire, with much scorn, "let your friend at court answer for the
town take; as you be a woman, I shall lay no blame upon you; for I
suppose they be wise than to trust woman with secret." he
accompany this with so sarcastical a laugh, that Mrs Western could
bear no long. she have be all this time fret in a tender part
(for she be indeed very deeply skilled in these matter, and very
violent in they), and therefore, burst forth in a rage, declare she
brother to be both a clown and a blockhead, and that she would stay no
long in his house.
the squire, though perhaps he have never read Machiavel, be, however,
in many point, a perfect politician. he strongly hold all those wise
tenet, which be so well inculcated in that Politico-Peripatetic
school of Exchange-alley. he know the just value and only use of
money, viz., to lay it up. he be likewise well skilled in the exact
value of reversion, expectation, &c., and have often consider the
amount of his sister's fortune, and the chance which he or his
posterity have of inherit it. this he be infinitely too wise to
sacrifice to a trifling resentment. when he find, therefore, he have
carry matter too far, he begin to think of reconcile they; which
be no very difficult task, as the lady have great affection for her
brother, and still great for her niece; and though too susceptible
of an affront offer to her skill in politic, on which she much
value herself, be a woman of a very extraordinary good and sweet
disposition.
have first, therefore, lay violent hand on the horse, for whose
escape from the stable no place but the window be leave open, he next
apply himself to his sister; soften and soothe she, by unsaying
all he have say, and by assertion directly contrary to those which
have incense she. lastly, he summon the eloquence of Sophia to his
assistance, who, besides a most graceful and win address, have the
advantage of be hear with great favour and partiality by her aunt.
the result of the whole be a kind smile from Mrs Western, who say,
"Brother, you be absolutely a perfect Croat; but as those have their
use in the army of the empress queen, so you likewise have some good
in you. I will therefore once more sign a treaty of peace with you,
and see that you do not infringe it on your side; at least, as you be
so excellent a politician, I may expect you will keep your league,
like the French, till your interest call upon you to break they."
Chapter iii.
contain two defiance to the critic.
the squire have settle matter with his sister, as we have see in
the last chapter, be so greatly impatient to communicate the proposal
to Allworthy, that Mrs Western have the utmost difficulty to prevent
he from visit that gentleman in his sickness, for this purpose.
Mr Allworthy have be engage to dine with Mr Western at the time when
he be take ill. he be therefore no soon discharge out of the
custody of physic, but he think (as be usual with he on all
occasion, both the high and the low) of fulfil his
engagement.
in the interval between the time of the dialogue in the last chapter,
and this day of public entertainment, Sophia have, from certain obscure
hint throw out by her aunt, collect some apprehension that the
sagacious lady suspect her passion for Jones. she now resolve to
take this opportunity of wipe out all such suspicion, and for that
purpose to put an entire constraint on her behaviour.
first, she endeavour to conceal a throb melancholy heart with
the utmost sprightliness in her countenance, and the high gaiety in
her manner. secondly, she address her whole discourse to Mr Blifil,
and take not the least notice of poor Jones the whole day.
the squire be so delighted with this conduct of his daughter, that he
scarce eat any dinner, and spend almost his whole time in watch
opportunity of convey sign of his approbation by wink and nod
to his sister; who be not at first altogether so pleased with what
she see as be her brother.
in short, Sophia so greatly overact her part, that her aunt be at
first stagger, and begin to suspect some affectation in her niece;
but as she be herself a woman of great art, so she soon attribute
this to extreme art in Sophia. she remember the many hint she have
give her niece concern she be in love, and imagine the young
lady have take this way to rally she out of her opinion, by an
overact civility: a notion that be greatly corroborate by the
excessive gaiety with which the whole be accompany. we cannot here
avoid remark, that this conjecture would have be well found
have Sophia live ten year in the air of Grosvenor Square, where young
lady do learn a wonderful knack of rallying and play with that
passion, which be a mighty serious thing in wood and grove an
hundred mile distant from London.
to say the truth, in discover the deceit of other, it matter much
that our own art be wind up, if I may use the expression, in the same
key with theirs: for very artful man sometimes miscarry by fancy
other wiser, or, in other word, great knave, than they really
be. as this observation be pretty deep, I will illustrate it by the
follow short story. three countryman be pursue a Wiltshire
thief through Brentford. the simple of they see "the Wiltshire
House," write under a sign, advise his companion to enter it, for
there most probably they would find their countryman. the second, who
be wise, laugh at this simplicity; but the third, who be wise
still, answer, "let we go in, however, for he may think we should
not suspect he of go amongst his own countryman." they accordingly
go in and search the house, and by that mean miss overtake
the thief, who be at that time but a little way before they; and who,
as they all know, but have never once reflect, could not read.
the reader will pardon a digression in which so invaluable a secret be
communicate, since every gamester will agree how necessary it be to
know exactly the play of another, in order to countermine he. this
will, moreover, afford a reason why the wise man, as be often see,
be the bubble of the weak, and why many simple and innocent
character be so generally misunderstood and misrepresent; but what
be most material, this will account for the deceit which Sophia put on
her politic aunt.
Dinner be end, and the company retire into the garden, Mr
Western, who be thoroughly convinced of the certainty of what his
sister have tell he, take Mr Allworthy aside, and very bluntly
propose a match between Sophia and young Mr Blifil.
Mr Allworthy be not one of those man whose heart flutter at any
unexpected and sudden tiding of worldly profit. his mind be, indeed,
temper with that philosophy which become a man and a Christian. he
affect no absolute superiority to all pleasure and pain, to all joy
and grief; but be not at the same time to be discompose and ruffle
by every accidental blast, by every smile or frown of fortune. he
receive, therefore, Mr Western's proposal without any visible
emotion, or without any alteration of countenance. he say the
alliance be such as he sincerely wish; then launch forth into a
very just encomium on the young lady's merit; acknowledge the offer
to be advantageous in point of fortune; and after thank Mr Western
for the good opinion he have profess of his nephew, conclude, that
if the young people like each other, he should be very desirous to
complete the affair.
Western be a little disappointed at Mr Allworthy's answer, which be
not so warm as he expect. he treat the doubt whether the young
people might like one another with great contempt, say, "that
parent be the good judge of proper match for their child:
that for his part he should insist on the most resign obedience from
his daughter: and if any young fellow could refuse such a bed-fellow,
he be his humble servant, and hop there be no harm do."
Allworthy endeavour to soften this resentment by many eulogium on
Sophia, declare he have no doubt but that Mr Blifil would very gladly
receive the offer; but all be ineffectual; he could obtain no other
answer from the squire but--"i say no more--I humbly hope therebe no
harm do--thatbe all." which word he repeat at least a hundred
time before they part.
Allworthy be too well acquainted with his neighbour to be offend at
this behaviour; and though he be so averse to the rigour which some
parent exercise on their child in the article of marriage, that he
have resolve never to force his nephew's inclination, he be
nevertheless much pleased with the prospect of this union; for the
whole country resound the praise of Sophia, and he have himself
greatly admire the uncommon endowment of both her mind and person.
to which I believe we may add, the consideration of her vast fortune,
which, though he be too sober to be intoxicate with it, he be too
sensible to despise.
and here, in defiance of all the bark critic in the world, I must
and will introduce a digression concern true wisdom, of which Mr
Allworthy be in reality as great a pattern as he be of goodness.
true wisdom then, notwithstanding all which Mr Hogarth's poor poet may
have writ against rich, and in spite of all which any rich well-feed
divine may have preach against pleasure, consist not in the
contempt of either of these. a man may have as much wisdom in the
possession of an affluent fortune, as any beggar in the street; or
may enjoy a handsome wife or a hearty friend, and still remain as wise
as any sour popish recluse, who bury all his social faculty, and
starve his belly while he well lash his back.
to say truth, the wise man be the likeli to possess all worldly
blessing in an eminent degree; for as that moderation which wisdom
prescribe be the sure way to useful wealth, so can it alone qualify
we to taste many pleasure. the wise man gratify every appetite and
every passion, while the fool sacrifice all the rest to pall and
satiate one.
it may be object, that very wise man have be notoriously
avaricious. I answer, not wise in that instance. it may likewise be
say, that the wise man have be in their youth immoderately fond
of pleasure. I answer, they be not wise then.
Wisdom, in short, whose lesson have be represent as so hard to
learn by those who never be at her school, only teach we to extend
a simple maxim universally know and follow even in the low life,
a little farth than that life carry it. and this be, not to buy at
too dear a price.
now, whoever take this maxim abroad with he into the grand market of
the world, and constantly apply it to honour, to rich, to
pleasure, and to every other commodity which that market afford, be,
I will venture to affirm, a wise man, and must be so acknowledge in
the worldly sense of the word; for he make the good of bargain,
since in reality he purchase everything at the price only of a little
trouble, and carry home all the good thing I have mention, while
he keep his health, his innocence, and his reputation, the common
price which be pay for they by other, entire and to himself.
from this moderation, likewise, he learn two other lesson, which
complete his character. first, never to be intoxicate when he hath
make the good bargain, nor deject when the market be empty, or when
its commodity be too dear for his purchase.
but I must remember on what subject I be write, and not trespass too
far on the patience of a good-natured critic. here, therefore, I put
an end to the chapter.
chapter iv.
contain sundry curious matter.
as soon as Mr Allworthy return home, he take Mr Blifil apart, and
after some preface, communicate to he the proposal which have be
make by Mr Western, and at the same time inform he how agreeable
this match would be to himself.
the charm of Sophia have not make the least impression on Blifil; not
that his heart be pre-engaged; neither be he totally insensible of
beauty, or have any aversion to woman; but his appetite be by nature
so moderate, that he be able, by philosophy, or by study, or by some
other method, easily to subdue they: and as to that passion which we
have treat of in the first chapter of this book, he have not the
least tincture of it in his whole composition.
but though he be so entirely free from that mixed passion, of which
we there treat, and of which the virtue and beauty of Sophia form
so notable an object; yet be he altogether as well furnish with
some other passion, that promise themselves very full gratification
in the young lady's fortune. such be avarice and ambition, which
divide the dominion of his mind between they. he have more than once
consider the possession of this fortune as a very desirable thing,
and have entertain some distant view concern it; but his own
youth, and that of the young lady, and indeed principally a reflection
that Mr Western might marry again, and have more child, have
restrain he from too hasty or eager a pursuit.
this last and most material objection be now in great measure
remove, as the proposal come from Mr Western himself. Blifil,
therefore, after a very short hesitation, answer Mr Allworthy, that
matrimony be a subject on which he have not yet think; but that he
be so sensible of his friendly and fatherly care, that he should in
all thing submit himself to his pleasure.
Allworthy be naturally a man of spirit, and his present gravity arise
