from true wisdom and philosophy, not from any original phlegm in his
disposition; for he have possess much fire in his youth, and have
marry a beautiful woman for love. he be not therefore greatly
pleased with this cold answer of his nephew; nor could he help
launch forth into the praise of Sophia, and express some wonder
that the heart of a young man could be impregnable to the force of
such charm, unless it be guard by some prior affection.
Blifil assure he he have no such guard; and then proceed to
discourse so wisely and religiously on love and marriage, that he
would have stopt the mouth of a parent much less devoutly inclined
than be his uncle. in the end, the good man be satisfied that his
nephew, far from have any objection to Sophia, have that esteem for
she, which in sober and virtuous mind be the sure foundation of
friendship and love. and as he doubt not but the lover would, in a
little time, become altogether as agreeable to his mistress, he
foresaw great happiness arise to all party by so proper and
desirable an union. with Mr Blifil's consent therefore he write the
next morning to Mr Western, acquaint he that his nephew have very
thankfully and gladly receive the proposal, and would be ready to
wait on the young lady, whenever she should be pleased to accept his
visit.
Western be much pleased with this letter, and immediately return an
answer; in which, without have mention a word to his daughter, he
appoint that very afternoon for open the scene of courtship.
as soon as he have dispatch this messenger, he go in quest of his
sister, whom he find read and expound the _Gazette_ to parson
Supple. to this exposition he be oblige to attend near a quarter of
an hour, though with great violence to his natural impetuosity, before
he be suffer to speak. at length, however, he find an opportunity
of acquaint the lady, that he have business of great consequence to
impart to she; to which she answer, "Brother, I be entirely at your
service. thing look so well in the north, that I be never in a
well humour."
the parson then withdraw, Western acquaint she with all which have
pass, and desire she to communicate the affair to Sophia, which she
readily and chearfully undertake; though perhaps her brother be a
little oblige to that agreeable northern aspect which have so
delight she, that he hear no comment on his proceeding; for they
be certainly somewhat too hasty and violent.
Chapter v.
in which be relate what pass between Sophia and her aunt.
Sophia be in her chamber, reading, when her aunt come in. the moment
she see Mrs Western, she shut the book with so much eagerness, that
the good lady could not forbear ask she, what book that be which
she seem so much afraid of show? "upon my word, madam," answer
Sophia, "it be a book which I be neither ashamed nor afraid to own I
have read. it be the production of a young lady of fashion, whose good
understanding, I think, doth honour to her sex, and whose good heart
be an honour to human nature." Mrs Western then take up the book, and
immediately after throw it down, saying--"Yes, the author be of a very
good family; but she be not much among people one know. I have never
read it; for the good judge say, there be not much in it."--"I dare
not, madam, set up my own opinion," say Sophia, "against the good
judge, but there appear to I a great deal of human nature in it;
and in many part so much true tenderness and delicacy, that it hath
cost I many a tear."--"Ay, and do you love to cry then?" say the
aunt. "I love a tender sensation," answer the niece, "and would pay
the price of a tear for it at any time."--"Well, but show I," say
the aunt, "what be you read when I come in; there be something
very tender in that, I believe, and very love too. you blush, my
dear Sophia. ah! child, you should read book which would teach you a
little hypocrisy, which would instruct you how to hide your thought a
little well."--"I hope, madam," answer Sophia, "I have no thought
which I ought to be ashamed of discover."--"ashame! no," cry the
aunt, "I donot think you have any thought which you ought to be
ashamed of; and yet, child, you blush just now when I mention the
word love. Dear Sophy, be assure you have not one thought which I
be not well acquainted with; as well, child, as the French be with
our motion, long before we put they in execution. do you think,
child, because you have be able to impose upon your father, that you
could impose upon I? do you imagine I do not know the reason of your
overact all that friendship for Mr Blifil yesterday? I have see a
little too much of the world, to be so deceived. Nay, nay, do not
blush again. I tell you it be a passion you need not be ashamed of. it
be a passion I myself approve, and have already bring your father
into the approbation of it. indeed, I solely consider your
inclination; for I would always have that gratify, if possible,
though one may sacrifice high prospect. come, I have news which
will delight your very soul. make I your confident, and I will
undertake you shall be happy to the very extent of your wish." "La,
madam," say Sophia, look more foolishly than ever she do in her
life, "I know not what to say--why, madam, should you suspect?"--"nay,
no dishonesty," return Mrs Western. "consider, you be speak to
one of your own sex, to an aunt, and I hope you be convince you
speak to a friend. consider, you be only reveal to I what I know
already, and what I plainly see yesterday, through that most artful of
all disguise, which you have put on, and which must have deceive any
one who have not perfectly know the world. lastly, consider it be a
passion which I highly approve." "La, madam," say Sophia, "you come
upon one so unawares, and on a sudden. to be sure, madam, I be not
blind--and certainly, if it be a fault to see all human perfection
assemble together--but be it possible my father and you, madam, can
see with my eye?" "I tell you," answer the aunt, "we do entirely
approve; and this very afternoon your father hath appoint for you to
receive your lover." "my father, this afternoon!" cry Sophia, with
the blood start from her face.--"Yes, child," say the aunt, "this
afternoon. you know the impetuosity of my brother's temper. I
acquaint he with the passion which I first discover in you that
evening when you faint away in the field. I see it in your fainting.
I see it immediately upon your recovery. I see it that evening at
supper, and the next morning at breakfast (you know, child, I have
see the world). well, I no soon acquaint my brother, but he
immediately want to propose it to Allworthy. he propose it
yesterday, Allworthy consent (as to be sure he must with joy), and
this afternoon, I tell you, you be to put on all your good air."
"this afternoon!" cry Sophia. "dear aunt, you frighten I out of my
sens." "o, my dear," say the aunt, "you will soon come to yourself
again; for he be a charming young fellow, thatbe the truth on't."
"Nay, I will own," say Sophia, "I know none with such perfection. so
brave, and yet so gentle; so witty, yet so inoffensive; so humane, so
civil, so genteel, so handsome! what signify his being base bear,
when compare with such qualification as these?" "Base bear? what do
you mean?" say the aunt, "Mr Blifil base bear!" Sophia turn
instantly pale at this name, and faintly repeat it. upon which the
aunt cry, "Mr Blifil--ay, Mr Blifil, of whom else have we be
talk?" "good heaven," answer Sophia, ready to sink, "of Mr
Jones, I think; I be sure I know no other who deserves--" "I
protest," cry the aunt, "you frighten I in your turn. be it Mr
Jones, and not Mr Blifil, who be the object of your affection?" "Mr
Blifil!" repeat Sophia. "sure it be impossible you can be in
earnest; if you be, I be the most miserable woman alive." Mrs Western
now stand a few moment silent, while spark of fiery rage flash
from her eye. at length, collect all her force of voice, she
thunder forth in the follow articulate sound:
"and be it possible you can think of disgrace your family by ally
yourself to a bastard? can the blood of the Westerns submit to such
contamination? if you have not sense sufficient to restrain such
monstrous inclination, I think the pride of our family would have
prevent you from give the least encouragement to so base an
affection; much less do I imagine you would ever have have the
assurance to own it to my face."
"Madam," answer Sophia, trembling, "what I have say you have
extort from I. I do not remember to have ever mention the name of
Mr Jones with approbation to any one before; nor should I now have I
not conceive he have your approbation. whatever be my thought of
that poor, unhappy young man, I intend to have carry they with I
to my grave--to that grave where only now, I find, I be to seek
repose." here she sink down in her chair, drown in her tear, and,
in all the move silence of unutterable grief, present a spectacle
which must have affect almost the hard heart.
all this tender sorrow, however, raise no compassion in her aunt. on
the contrary, she now fall into the most violent rage.--"and I would
rather," she cry, in a most vehement voice, "follow you to your
grave, than I would see you disgrace yourself and your family by such
a match. o Heavens! could I have ever suspect that I should live to
hear a niece of mine declare a passion for such a fellow? you be the
first--yes, Miss Western, you be the first of your name who ever
entertain so grovel a thought. a family so note for the
prudence of its women"--here she run on a full quarter of an hour,
till, have exhaust her breath rather than her rage, she conclude
with threaten to go immediately and acquaint her brother.
Sophia then throw herself at her foot, and lay hold of her hand,
beg she with tear to conceal what she have draw from she; urge
the violence of her father's temper, and protest that no
inclination of hers should ever prevail with she to do anything which
might offend he.
Mrs Western stand a moment look at she, and then, have
recollect herself, say, "that on one consideration only she would
keep the secret from her brother; and this be, that Sophia should
promise to entertain Mr Blifil that very afternoon as her lover, and
to regard he as the person who be to be her husband."
Poor Sophia be too much in her aunt's power to deny she anything
positively; she be oblige to promise that she would see Mr Blifil,
and be as civil to he as possible; but beg her aunt that the match
might not be hurry on. she say, "Mr Blifil be by no mean
agreeable to she, and she hop her father would be prevail on not
to make she the most wretched of woman."
Mrs Western assure she, "that the match be entirely agree upon, and
that nothing could or should prevent it. I must own," say she, "I
look on it as on a matter of indifference; nay, perhaps, have some
scruple about it before, which be actually get over by my thinking
it highly agreeable to your own inclination; but now I regard it as
the most eligible thing in the world: nor shall there be, if I can
prevent it, a moment of time lose on the occasion."
Sophia reply, "Delay at least, madam, I may expect from both your
goodness and my father's. surely you will give I time to endeavour to
get the well of so strong a disinclination as I have at present to
this person."
the aunt answer, "she know too much of the world to be so deceived;
that as she be sensible another man have her affection, she should
persuade Mr Western to hasten the match as much as possible. it would
be bad politic, indeed," add she, "to protract a siege when the
enemy's army be at hand, and in danger of relieve it. no, no,
Sophy," say she, "as I be convinced you have a violent passion which
you can never satisfy with honour, I will do all I can to put your
honour out of the care of your family: for when you be marry those
matter will belong only to the consideration of your husband. I hope,
child, you will always have prudence enough to act as become you; but
if you should not, marriage hath save many a woman from ruin."
Sophia well understand what her aunt mean; but do not think proper
to make she an answer. however, she take a resolution to see Mr
Blifil, and to behave to he as civilly as she could, for on that
condition only she obtain a promise from her aunt to keep secret the
liking which her ill fortune, rather than any scheme of Mrs Western,
have unhappily draw from she.
Chapter vi.
contain a dialogue between Sophia and Mrs Honour, which may a
little relieve those tender affection which the foregoing scene may
have raise in the mind of a good-natured reader.
Mrs Western have obtain that promise from her niece which we have
see in the last chapter, withdraw; and presently after arrive Mrs
Honour. she be at work in a neighbouring apartment, and have be
summon to the keyhole by some vociferation in the precede
dialogue, where she have continue during the remain part of it. at
her entry into the room, she find Sophia stand motionless, with
the tear trickle from her eye. upon which she immediately order
a proper quantity of tear into her own eye, and then begin, "O
Gemini, my dear lady, what be the matter?"--"nothing," cry Sophia.
"nothing! o dear Madam!" answer Honour, "you must not tell I that,
when your ladyship be in this taking, and when there hath be such a
preamble between your ladyship and Madam Western."--"Don't teaze I,"
cry Sophia; "I tell you nothing be the matter. good heaven! why be
I born?"--"Nay, madam," say Mrs Honour, "you shall never persuade I
that your la'ship can lament yourself so for nothing. to be sure I be
but a servant; but to be sure I have be always faithful to your
la'ship, and to be sure I would serve your la'ship with my life."--"my
dear Honour," say Sophia, "'tis not in thy power to be of any service
to I. I be irretrievably undo."--"heaven forbid!" answer the
waiting-woman; "but if I cannot be of any service to you, pray tell I,
madam--it will be some comfort to I to know--pray, dear ma'am, tell
I whatbe the matter."--"My father," cry Sophia, "be go to marry
I to a man I both despise and hate."--"O dear, ma'am," answer the
other, "who be this wicked man? for to be sure he be very bad, or your
la'ship would not despise he."--"his name be poison to my tongue,"
reply Sophia: "thou wilt know it too soon." indeed, to confess the
truth, she know it already, and therefore be not very inquisitive as
to that point. she then proceed thus: "I donot pretend to give your
la'ship advice, whereof your la'ship know much well than I can
pretend to, be but a servant; but, i-fackin! no father in England
should marry I against my consent. and, to be sure, the 'squire be so
good, that if he do but know your la'ship despise and hat the
young man, to be sure he would not desire you to marry he. and if
your la'ship would but give I leave to tell my master so. to be sure,
it would be more properer to come from your own mouth; but as your
la'ship doth not care to foul your tongue with his nasty name--"--"you
be mistaken, Honour," say Sophia; "my father be determine before
he ever think fit to mention it to I."--"more shame for he," cry
Honour: "you be to go to bed to he, and not master: and thof a man
may be a very proper man, yet every woman maynot think he handsome
alike. I be sure my master would never act in this manner of his own
head. I wish some people would trouble themselves only with what
belong to they; they would not, I believe, like to be serve so, if
it be their own case; for though I be a maid, I can easily believe as
how all man be not equally agreeable. and what signify your la'ship
have so great a fortune, if you cannot please yourself with the man
you think most handsome? well, I say nothing; but to be sure it be a
pity some folk have not be well bear; nay, as for that matter, I
should not mind it myself; but then there be not so much money; and
what of that? your la'ship hath money enough for both; and where can
your la'ship bestow your fortune well? for to be sure every one must
allow that he be the most handsome, charmingest, fine, tall,
properest man in the world."--"what do you mean by run on in this
manner to I?" cry Sophia, with a very grave countenance. "have I
ever give any encouragement for these liberties?"--"nay, ma'am, I ask
pardon; I mean no harm," answer she; "but to be sure the poor
gentleman hath run in my head ever since I see he this morning. to be
sure, if your la'ship have but see he just now, you must have pity
he. poor gentleman! I wish some misfortune hath not happen to
he; for he hath be walk about with his arm across, and look
so melancholy, all this morning: I vow and protest it make I almost
cry to see he."--"To see whom?" say Sophia. "Poor Mr Jones,"
answer Honour. "see he! why, where do you see he?" cry Sophia.
"by the canal, ma'am," say Honour. "there he hath be walk all
this morning, and at last there he lay himself down: I believe he
lie there still. to be sure, if it have not be for my modesty, be
a maid, as I be, I should have go and speak to he. do, ma'am, let
I go and see, only for a fancy, whether he be there still."--"Pugh!"
say Sophia. "there! no, no: what should he do there? he be go
before this time, to be sure. besides, why--what--why should you go to
see? besides, I want you for something else. go, fetch I my hat and
glove. I shall walk with my aunt in the grove before dinner." Honour
do immediately as she be bid, and Sophia put her hat on; when,
look in the glass, she fancy the ribbon with which her hat be
tie do not become she, and so send she maid back again for a ribbon
of a different colour; and then give Mrs Honour repeat charge not
to leave her work on any account, as she say it be in violent haste,
and must be finish that very day, she mutter something more about
go to the grove, and then sally out the contrary way, and walk,
as fast as her tender trembling limb could carry she, directly
towards the canal.
Jones have be there as Mrs Honour have tell she; he have indeed spend
two hour there that morning in melancholy contemplation on his
Sophia, and have go out from the garden at one door the moment she
enter it at another. so that those unlucky minute which have be
spend in change the ribbon, have prevent the lover from meeting
at this time;--a most unfortunate accident, from which my fair reader
will not fail to draw a very wholesome lesson. and here I strictly
forbid all male critic to intermeddle with a circumstance which I
have recount only for the sake of the lady, and upon which they
only be at liberty to comment.
Chapter vii.
a picture of formal courtship in miniature, as it always ought to be
draw, and a scene of a tenderer kind paint at full length.
it be well remark by one (and perhaps by more), that misfortune do
not come single. this wise maxim be now verify by Sophia, who be
not only disappointed of see the man she love, but have the
vexation of be oblige to dress herself out, in order to receive a
visit from the man she hat.
that afternoon Mr Western, for the first time, acquaint his daughter
with his intention; tell she, he know very well that she have hear
it before from her aunt. Sophia look very grave upon this, nor could
she prevent a few pearl from steal into her eye. "come, come,"
say western, "none of your maidenish air; I know all; I assure you
sister hath tell I all."
"be it possible," say Sophia, "that my aunt can have betray I
already?"--"Ay, ay," say western; "betray you! ay. why, you
betray yourself yesterday at dinner. you show your fancy very
plainly, I think. but you young girl never know what you would be at.
so you cry because I be go to marry you to the man you be in love
with! your mother, I remember, whimper and whine just in the same
manner; but it be all over within twenty-four hour after we be
marry: Mr Blifil be a brisk young man, and will soon put an end to
your squeamishness. come, chear up, chear up; I expect un every
minute."
Sophia be now convinced that her aunt have behave honourably to she:
and she determine to go through that disagreeable afternoon with as
much resolution as possible, and without give the least suspicion in
the world to her father.
Mr Blifil soon arrive; and Mr Western soon after withdraw, leave
the young couple together.
here a long silence of near a quarter of an hour ensue; for the
gentleman who be to begin the conversation have all the unbecoming
modesty which consist in bashfulness. he often attempt to speak,
and as often suppress his word just at the very point of utterance.
at last out they break in a torrent of far-fetched and high-strained
compliment, which be answer on her side by downcast look, half
bow, and civil monosyllable. Blifil, from his inexperience in the
way of woman, and from his conceit of himself, take this behaviour
for a modest assent to his courtship; and when, to shorten a scene
which she could no long support, Sophia rise up and leave the room,
he impute that, too, merely to bashfulness, and comfort himself
that he should soon have enough of her company.
he be indeed perfectly well satisfied with his prospect of success;
for as to that entire and absolute possession of the heart of his
mistress which romantic lover require, the very idea of it never
enter his head. her fortune and her person be the sole object of
his wish, of which he make no doubt soon to obtain the absolute
property; as Mr Western's mind be so earnestly bent on the match; and
as he well know the strict obedience which Sophia be always ready to
pay to her father's will, and the great still which her father would
exact, if there be occasion. this authority, therefore, together with
the charm which he fancy in his own person and conversation, could
not fail, he think, of succeed with a young lady, whose
inclination be, he doubt not, entirely disengaged.
of Jones he certainly have not even the least jealousy; and I have
often think it wonderful that he have not. perhaps he imagine the
character which Jones bear all over the country (how justly, let the
reader determine), of be one of the wild fellow in England,
might render he odious to a lady of the most exemplary modesty.
perhaps his suspicion might be lay asleep by the behaviour of
Sophia, and of Jones himself, when they be all in company together.
lastly, and indeed principally, he be well assured there be not
another self in the case. he fancy that he know Jones to the bottom,
and have in reality a great contempt for his understanding, for not
be more attached to his own interest. he have no apprehension that
Jones be in love with Sophia; and as for any lucrative motif, he
imagine they would sway very little with so silly a fellow. Blifil,
moreover, think the affair of Molly Seagrim still go on, and
indeed believe it would end in marriage; for Jones really love he
from his childhood, and have keep no secret from he, till his
behaviour on the sickness of Mr Allworthy have entirely alienate his
heart; and it be by mean of the quarrel which have ensue on this
occasion, and which be not yet reconcile, that Mr Blifil know
nothing of the alteration which have happen in the affection which
Jones have formerly bear towards Molly.
from these reason, therefore, Mr Blifil see no bar to his success
with Sophia. he conclude her behaviour be like that of all other
young lady on a first visit from a lover, and it have indeed entirely
answer his expectation.
Mr Western take care to way-lie the lover at his exit from his
mistress. he find he so elevated with his success, so enamour with
his daughter, and so satisfied with her reception of he, that the old
gentleman begin to caper and dance about his hall, and by many other
antic action to express the extravagance of his joy; for he have not
the least command over any of his passion; and that which have at any
time the ascendant in his mind hurry he to the wild excess.
as soon as Blifil be depart, which be not till after many hearty
kiss and embrace bestow on he by Western, the good squire go
instantly in quest of his daughter, whom he no soon find than he
pour forth the most extravagant rapture, bid her chuse what
clothe and jewel she please; and declare that he have no other use
for fortune but to make she happy. he then caress she again and
again with the utmost profusion of fondness, call she by the most
endear name, and protest she be his only joy on earth.
Sophia perceive her father in this fit of affection, which she do
not absolutely know the reason of (for fit of fondness be not
unusual to he, though this be rather more violent than ordinary),
think she should never have a well opportunity of disclose
herself than at present, as far at least as regard Mr Blifil; and
she too well foresaw the necessity which she should soon be under of
come to a full explanation. after have thank the squire,
therefore, for all his profession of kindness, she add, with a look
full of inexpressible softness, "and be it possible my papa can be so
good to place all his joy in his Sophy's happiness?" which western
have confirm by a great oath, and a kiss; she then lay hold of
his hand, and, fall on her knee, after many warm and passionate
declaration of affection and duty, she beg he "not to make she
the most miserable creature on earth by force she to marry a man
whom she detest. this I entreat of you, dear sir," say she, "for
your sake, as well as my own, since you be so very kind to tell I
your happiness depend on mine."--"how! what!" say western, star
wildly. "oh! sir," continue she, "not only your poor Sophy's
happiness; her very life, her being, depend upon your grant she
request. I cannot live with Mr Blifil. to force I into this marriage
would be kill I."--"you cannot live with Mr Blifil?" say Western.
"no, upon my soul I cannot," answer Sophia. "then die and be d--d,"
cry he, spurn she from he. "oh! sir," cry Sophia, catch
hold of the skirt of his coat, "take pity on I, I beseech you. donot
look and say such cruel--can you be unmove while you see your Sophy
in this dreadful condition? can the good of father break my heart?
will he kill I by the most painful, cruel, linger death?"--"pooh!
pooh!" cry the squire; "all stuff and nonsense; all maidenish
trick. kill you, indeed! will marriage kill you?"--"oh! sir,"
answer Sophia, "such a marriage be bad than death. he be not even
indifferent; I hate and det he."--"if you det un never so
much," cry western, "you shall ha'un." this he bind by an oath too
shocking to repeat; and after many violent asseveration, conclude in
these word: "I be resolve upon the match, and unless you consent to
it I will not give you a groat, not a single farthing; no, though I
see you expire with famine in the street, I would not relieve you
with a morsel of bread. this be my fix resolution, and so I leave
you to consider on it." he then break from she with such violence,
that her face dash against the floor; and he burst directly out of
the room, leave poor Sophia prostrate on the ground.
when Western come into the hall, he there find Jones; who see his
friend look wild, pale, and almost breathless, could not forbear
enquire the reason of all these melancholy appearance. upon which
the squire immediately acquaint he with the whole matter,
conclude with bitter denunciation against Sophia, and very pathetic
lamentation of the misery of all father who be so unfortunate to
have daughter.
Jones, to whom all the resolution which have be take in favour of
Blifil be yet a secret, be at first almost strike dead with this
relation; but recover his spirit a little, mere despair, as he
afterwards say, inspire he to mention a matter to Mr Western, which
seem to require more impudence than a human forehead be ever gift
with. he desire leave to go to Sophia, that he might endeavour to
obtain her concurrence with her father's inclination.
if the squire have be as quicksighte as he be remarkable for the
contrary, passion might at present very well have blind he. he
thank Jones for offer to undertake the office, and say, "go, go,
prithee, try what canst do;" and then swear many execrable oath that
he would turn she out of door unless she consent to the match.
Chapter viii.
the meeting between Jones and Sophia.
Jones depart instantly in quest of Sophia, whom he find just rise
from the ground, where her father have leave she, with the tear
trickle from her eye, and the blood run from her lip. he
presently run to she, and with a voice full at once of tenderness and
terrour, cry, "o my Sophia, what mean this dreadful sight?" she
look softly at he for a moment before she speak, and then say, "Mr
Jones, for Heaven's sake how come you here?--leave I, I beseech you,
this moment."--"Do not," say he, "impose so harsh a command upon
I--my heart bleed fast than those lip. o Sophia, how easily could
I drain my vein to preserve one drop of that dear blood."--"I have
too many obligation to you already," answer she, "for sure you
mean they such." here she look at he tenderly almost a minute, and
then burst into an agony, cry, "oh, Mr Jones, why do you save my
life? my death would have be happy for we both."--"happi for we
both!" cry he. "could rack or wheel kill I so painfully as
Sophia's--I cannot bear the dreadful sound. do I live but for she?"
both his voice and look be full of inexpressible tenderness when he
speak these word; and at the same time he lay gently hold on her
hand, which she do not withdraw from he; to say the truth, she
hardly know what she do or suffer. a few moment now pass in
silence between these lover, while his eye be eagerly fix on
Sophia, and her decline towards the ground: at last she recover
strength enough to desire he again to leave she, for that her certain
ruin would be the consequence of their be find together; add,
"oh, Mr Jones, you know not, you know not what hath pass this cruel
afternoon."--"I know all, my Sophia," answer he; "your cruel father
hath tell I all, and he himself hath send I hither to you."--"My
father send you to I!" reply she: "sure you dream."--"would to
Heaven," cry he, "it be but a dream! oh, Sophia, your father hath
send I to you, to be an advocate for my odious rival, to solicit you
in his favour. I take any mean to get access to you. o speak to I,
Sophia! comfort my bleed heart. sure no one ever love, ever doate
like I. do not unkindly withhold this dear, this soft, this gentle
hand--one moment, perhaps, tear you for ever from I--nothing less
than this cruel occasion could, I believe, have ever conquer the
respect and awe with which you have inspire I." she stand a moment
silent, and cover with confusion; then lift up her eye gently
towards he, she cry, "what would Mr Jones have I say?"--"O do but
promise," cry he, "that you never will give yourself to
Blifil."--"Name not," answer she, "the detest sound. be assure I
never will give he what be in my power to withhold from he."--"now
then," cry he, "while you be so perfectly kind, go a little
far, and add that I may hope."--"Alas!" say she, "Mr Jones,
whither will you drive I? what hope have I to bestow? you know my
father's intention."--"but I know," answer he, "your compliance
with they cannot be compel."--"what," say she, "must be the
dreadful consequence of my disobedience? my own ruin be my least
concern. I cannot bear the thought of be the cause of my father's
misery."--"He be himself the cause," cry Jones, "by exact a power
over you which nature hath not give he. think on the misery which I
be to suffer if I be to lose you, and see on which side pity will turn
the balance."--"think of it!" reply she: "can you imagine I do not
feel the ruin which I must bring on you, should I comply with your
desire? it be that thought which give I resolution to bid you fly
from I for ever, and avoid your own destruction."--"I fear no
destruction," cry he, "but the loss of Sophia. if you would save I
from the most bitter agony, recall that cruel sentence. indeed, I
can never part with you, indeed I cannot."
the lover now stand both silent and trembling, Sophia be unable to
withdraw her hand from Jones, and he almost as unable to hold it; when
the scene, which I believe some of my reader will think have last
long enough, be interrupt by one of so different a nature, that we
shall reserve the relation of it for a different chapter.
chapter ix.
be of a much more tempestuous kind than the former.
before we proceed with what now happen to our lover, it may be
proper to recount what have past in the hall during their tender
interview.
soon after Jones have leave Mr Western in the manner above mention,
his sister come to he, and be presently inform of all that have
pass between her brother and Sophia relate to Blifil.
this behaviour in her niece the good lady construe to be an absolute
breach of the condition on which she have engage to keep her love for
Mr Jones a secret. she consider herself, therefore, at full liberty
to reveal all she know to the squire, which she immediately do in the
most explicit term, and without any ceremony or preface.
the idea of a marriage between Jones and his daughter, have never once
enter into the squire's head, either in the warm minute of his
affection towards that young man, or from suspicion, or on any other
occasion. he do indeed consider a parity of fortune and circumstance
to be physically as necessary an ingredient in marriage, as difference
of sex, or any other essential; and have no more apprehension of his
daughter's fall in love with a poor man, than with any animal of a
different specie.
he become, therefore, like one thunderstruck at his sister's relation.
he be, at first, incapable of make any answer, have be almost
deprive of his breath by the violence of the surprize. this, however,
soon return, and, as be usual in other case after an intermission,
with redouble force and fury.
the first use he make of the power of speech, after his recovery from
the sudden effect of his astonishment, be to discharge a round
volley of oath and imprecation. after which he proceed hastily to
the apartment where he expect to find the lover, and murmur, or
rather indeed roar forth, intention of revenge every step he go.
as when two dove, or two wood-pigeon, or as when Strephon and
Phyllis (for that come near to the mark) be retire into some
pleasant solitary grove, to enjoy the delightful conversation of Love,
that bashful boy, who cannot speak in public, and be never a good
companion to more than two at a time; here, while every object be
serene, should hoarse thunder burst suddenly through the shatter
cloud, and rumble roll along the sky, the frightened maid start
from the mossy bank or verdant turf, the pale livery of death succeed
the red regimental in which Love have before dr her cheek, fear
shake her whole frame, and her lover scarce support her trembling
tottering limb.
or as when two gentleman, stranger to the wondrous wit of the place,
be crack a bottle together at some inn or tavern at Salisbury, if
the great Dowdy, who act the part of a madman as well as some of his
setter-on do that of a fool, should rattle his chain, and dreadfully
hum forth the grumble catch along the gallery; the fright
stranger stand aghast; scar at the horrid sound, they seek some
place of shelter from the approach danger; and if the well-bar
window do admit their exit, would venture their neck to escape the
threaten fury now come upon they.
so tremble poor Sophia, so turn she pale at the noise of her
father, who, in a voice most dreadful to hear, come on swearing,
cursing, and vow the destruction of Jones. to say the truth, I
believe the youth himself would, from some prudent consideration,
have prefer another place of abode at this time, have his terror on
Sophia's account give he liberty to reflect a moment on what any
otherway concern himself, than as his love make he partake
whatever affect she.
and now the squire, have burst open the door, behold an object which
instantly suspend all his fury against Jones; this be the ghastly
appearance of Sophia, who have faint away in her lover's arm. this
tragical sight Mr Western no soon beheld, than all his rage forsook
he; he roar for help with his utmost violence; run first to his
daughter, then back to the door call for water, and then back again
to Sophia, never consider in whose arm she then be, nor perhaps
once recollect that there be such a person in the world as Jones;
for indeed I believe the present circumstance of his daughter be
now the sole consideration which employ his thought.
Mrs Western and a great number of servant soon come to the assistance
of Sophia with water, cordial, and everything necessary on those
occasion. these be apply with such success, that Sophia in a very
few minute begin to recover, and all the symptom of life to return.
upon which she be presently lead off by her own maid and Mrs Western:
nor do that good lady depart without leave some wholesome
admonition with her brother, on the dreadful effect of his passion,
or, as she please to call it, madness.
the squire, perhaps, do not understand this good advice, as it be
deliver in obscure hint, shrug, and note of admiration: at least,
if he do understand it, he profit very little by it; for no soon
be he cure of his immediate fear for his daughter, than he relapse
into his former frenzy, which must have produce an immediate battle
with Jones, have not parson Supple, who be a very strong man, be
present, and by mere force restrain the squire from act of
hostility.
the moment Sophia be depart, Jones advance in a very suppliant
manner to Mr Western, whom the parson hold in his arm, and beg he
to be pacify; for that, while he continue in such a passion, it
would be impossible to give he any satisfaction.
"I wull have satisfaction o' thee," answer the squire; "so doff thy
clothe. _at unt_ half a man, and Iwill lick thee as well as wast ever
lick in thy life." he then bespatter the youth with abundance of
that language which pass between country gentleman who embrace
opposite side of the question; with frequent application to he to
salute that part which be generally introduce into all controversy
that arise among the low order of the english gentry at
horse-race, cock-match, and other public place. allusion to this
part be likewise often make for the sake of the jest. and here, I
believe, the wit be generally misunderstood. in reality, it lie in
desire another to kiss your a-- for have just before threaten to
kick his; for I have observe very accurately, that no one ever
desire you to kick that which belong to himself, nor offer to kiss
this part in another.
it may likewise seem surprize that in the many thousand kind
invitation of this sort, which every one who hath converse with
country gentleman must have hear, no one, I believe, hath ever see a
single instance where the desire hath be comply with;--a great
instance of their want of politeness; for in town nothing can be more
common than for the fine gentleman to perform this ceremony every
day to their superior, without have that favour once request of
they.
to all such wit, Jones very calmly answer, "Sir, this usage may
perhaps cancel every other obligation you have confer on I; but
there be one you can never cancel; nor will I be provoke by your
abuse to lift my hand against the father of Sophia."
at these word the squire grow still more outrageous than before; so
that the parson beg Jones to retire; say, "you behold, sir, how
he waxeth wrath at your abode here; therefore let I pray you not to
tarry any long. his anger be too much kindle for you to commune
with he at present. you have well, therefore, conclude your visit,
and refer what matter you have to urge in your behalf to some other
opportunity."
Jones accept this advice with thank, and immediately depart. the
squire now regain the liberty of his hand, and so much temper as to
express some satisfaction in the restraint which have be lay upon
he; declare that he should certainly have beat his brain out; and
add, "it would have vex one confoundedly to have be hang for
such a rascal."
the parson now begin to triumph in the success of his peace-make
endeavour, and proceed to read a lecture against anger, which might
perhaps rather have tend to raise than to quiet that passion in some
hasty mind. this lecture he enrich with many valuable quotation
from the antient, particularly from Seneca; who hath indeed so well
handle this passion, that none but a very angry man can read he
without great pleasure and profit. the doctor conclude this harangue
with the famous story of Alexander and Clitus; but as I find that
enter in my common-place under title Drunkenness, I shall not insert
it here.
the squire take no notice of this story, nor perhaps of anything he
say; for he interrupt he before he have finish, by call for a
tankard of beer; observe (which be perhaps as true as any
observation on this fever of the mind) that anger make a man dry.
no soon have the squire swallow a large draught than he renew the
discourse on Jones, and declare a resolution of go the next
morning early to acquaint Mr Allworthy. his friend would have
dissuade he from this, from the mere motive of good-nature; but his
dissuasion have no other effect than to produce a large volley of oath
and curse, which greatly shock the pious ear of Supple; but he do
not dare to remonstrate against a privilege which the squire claim
as a freeborn Englishman. to say truth, the parson submit to please
his palate at the squire's table, at the expense of suffer now and
then this violence to his ear. he content himself with think he
do not promote this evil practice, and that the squire would not
swear an oath the less, if he never enter within his gate. however,
though he be not guilty of ill manner by rebuke a gentleman in his
own house, he pay he off obliquely in the pulpit: which have not,
indeed, the good effect of work a reformation in the squire
himself; yet it so far operate on his conscience, that he put the
law very severely in execution against other, and the magistrate be
the only person in the parish who could swear with impunity.
Chapter x.
in which Mr Western visit Mr Allworthy.
Mr Allworthy be now retire from breakfast with his nephew, well
satisfied with the report of the young gentleman's successful visit to
Sophia (for he greatly desire the match, more on account of the young
lady's character than of her riches), when Mr Western break abruptly
in upon they, and without any ceremony begin as follows:--
"there, you have do a fine piece of work truly! you have bring up
your bastard to a fine purpose; not that I believe you have have any
hand in it neither, that is, as a man may say, designedly: but there
be a fine kettle-of-fish make on't up at our house." "what can be the
matter, Mr Western?" say Allworthy. "o, matter enow of all
conscience: my daughter hath fall in love with your bastard, thatbe
all; but I willnot ge she a hapeny, not the twentieth part of a brass
varden. I always think what would come o' breed up a bastard like
a gentleman, and let un come about to vok's house. itbe well vor
un I could not get at un: Ihave a lick'd un; Ihave a spoil'd his
caterwauling; Ihave a teach the son of a whore to meddle with meat for
his master. he shallnot ever have a morsel of meat of mine, or a varden
to buy it: if she will ha un, one smock shall be her portion. Iwould
soon ge my esteate to the zinking fund, that it may be send to
Hanover to corrupt our nation with." "I be heartily sorry," cry
allworthy. "pox o' your sorrow," say western; "it will do I
abundance of good when I have lose my only child, my poor Sophy, that
be the joy of my heart, and all the hope and comfort of my age; but I
be resolve I will turn she out o' door; she shall beg, and starve,
and rot in the street. not one hapeny, not a hapeny shall she ever
hae o' mine. the son of a bitch be always good at find a hare
sitting, an be rot to'n: I little think what puss he be look
after; but it shall be the bad he ever vound in his life. she shall
be no well than carrion: the skin o'er be all he shall ha, and zu
you may tell un." "I be in amazement," cry allworthy, "at what you
tell I, after what pass between my nephew and the young lady no
long ago than yesterday." "yes, sir," answer Western, "it be
after what pass between your nephew and she that the whole matter
come out. Mr Blifil there be no soon go than the son of a whore
come lurch about the house. little do I think when I use to love
he for a sportsman that he be all the while a poaching after my
daughter." "why truly," say Allworthy, "I could wish you have not
give he so many opportunity with she; and you will do I the
justice to acknowledge that I have always be averse to his staying
so much at your house, though I own I have no suspicion of this kind."
"why, zound," cry western, "who could have think it? what the
devil have she to do wi'n? he do not come there a courting to she; he
come there a hunting with I." "but be it possible," say Allworthy,
"that you should never discern any symptom of love between they, when
you have see they so often together?" "never in my life, as I hope to
be save," cry western: "I never so much as zeed he kiss she in all
my life; and so far from court she, he use rather to be more
silent when she be in company than at any other time; and as for the
girl, she be always less civil to'n than to any young man that come
to the house. as to that matter, I be not more easy to be deceive
than another; I would not have you think I be, neighbour." Allworthy
could scarce refrain laughter at this; but he resolve to do a
violence to himself; for he perfectly well know mankind, and have too
much good-breeding and good-nature to offend the squire in his present
circumstance. he then ask Western what he would have he do upon
this occasion. to which the other answer, "that he would have he
keep the rascal away from his house, and that he would go and lock up
the wench; for he be resolve to make she marry Mr Blifil in spite of
her tooth." he then shake Blifil by the hand, and swear he would have
no other son-in-law. presently after which he take his leave; say
his house be in such disorder that it be necessary for he to make
haste home, to take care his daughter do not give he the slip; and
as for Jones, he swear if he catch he at his house, he would qualify
he to run for the gelding' plate.
when Allworthy and Blifil be again leave together, a long silence
ensue between they; all which interval the young gentleman fill up
with sigh, which proceed partly from disappointment, but more from
hatred; for the success of Jones be much more grievous to he than
the loss of Sophia.
at length his uncle ask he what he be determined to do, and he
answer in the follow words:--"Alas! sir, can it be a question
what step a lover will take, when reason and passion point different
way? I be afraid it be too certain he will, in that dilemma, always
follow the latter. reason dictate to I, to quit all thought of a
woman who place her affection on another; my passion bid I hope
she may in time change her inclination in my favour. here, however, I
conceive an objection may be raise, which, if it could not fully be
answer, would totally deter I from any further pursuit. I mean the
injustice of endeavour to supplant another in a heart of which he
seem already in possession; but the determined resolution of Mr
Western show that, in this case, I shall, by so do, promote the
happiness of every party; not only that of the parent, who will thus
be preserve from the high degree of misery, but of both the
other, who must be undo by this match. the lady, I be sure, will be
undo in every sense; for, besides the loss of most part of her own
fortune, she will be not only married to a beggar, but the little
fortune which her father cannot withhold from she will be squander
on that wench with whom I know he yet converse. Nay, that be a
trifle; for I know he to be one of the bad man in the world; for
have my dear uncle know what I have hitherto endeavour to conceal,
he must have long since abandon so profligate a wretch." "how!" say
Allworthy; "hath he do anything bad than I already know? tell I,
I beseech you?" "no," reply Blifil; "it be now past, and perhaps he
may have repent of it." "I command you, on your duty," say
Allworthy, "to tell I what you mean." "you know, sir," say Blifil,
"I never disobey you; but I be sorry I mention it, since it may
now look like revenge, whereas, I thank Heaven, no such motive ever
enter my heart; and if you oblige I to discover it, I must be his
petitioner to you for your forgiveness." "I will have no condition,"
answer Allworthy; "I think I have show tenderness enough towards
he, and more perhaps than you ought to thank I for." "More, indeed,
I fear, than he deserve," cry Blifil; "for in the very day of your
utmost danger, when myself and all the family be in tear, he fill
the house with riot and debauchery. he drank, and sung, and roar;
and when I give he a gentle hint of the indecency of his action, he
fall into a violent passion, swear many oath, call I rascal, and
strike I." "how!" cry allworthy; "do he dare to strike you?" "I be
sure," cry Blifil, "I have forgive he that long ago. I wish I
could so easily forget his ingratitude to the good of benefactor; and
yet even that I hope you will forgive he, since he must have
certainly be possess with the devil: for that very evening, as Mr
Thwackum and myself be take the air in the field, and exult in
the good symptom which then first begin to discover themselves, we
unluckily see he engage with a wench in a manner not fit to be
mention. Mr Thwackum, with more boldness than prudence, advance to
rebuke he, when (I be sorry to say it) he fall upon the worthy man,
and beat he so outrageously that I wish he may have yet recover the
bruise. nor be I without my share of the effect of his malice,
while I endeavour to protect my tutor; but that I have long
forgive; nay, I prevail with Mr Thwackum to forgive he too, and
not to inform you of a secret which I fear might be fatal to he.
and now, sir, since I have unadvisedly drop a hint of this matter,
and your command have oblige I to discover the whole, let I
intercede with you for he." "o child!" say Allworthy, "I know not
whether I should blame or applaud your goodness, in conceal such
villany a moment: but where be Mr Thwackum? not that I want any
confirmation of what you say; but I will examine all the evidence of
this matter, to justify to the world the example I be resolve to make
of such a monster."
Thwackum be now send for, and presently appear. he corroborate
every circumstance which the other have depose; nay, he produce the
record upon his breast, where the handwriting of Mr Jones remain
very legible in black and blue. he conclude with declare to Mr
Allworthy, that he should have long since inform he of this matter,
have not Mr Blifil, by the most earnest interposition, prevent he.
"he be," say he, "an excellent youth: though such forgiveness of
enemy be carry the matter too far."
in reality, Blifil have take some pain to prevail with the parson,
and to prevent the discovery at that time; for which he have many
reason. he know that the mind of man be apt to be soften and
relax from their usual severity by sickness. besides, he imagine
that if the story be tell when the fact be so recent, and the
physician about the house, who might have unravel the real truth,
he should never be able to give it the malicious turn which he
intend. again, he resolve to hoard up this business, till the
indiscretion of Jones should afford some additional complaint; for he
think the joint weight of many fact fall upon he together,
would be the most likely to crush he; and he watch, therefore, some
such opportunity as that with which fortune have now kindly present
he. lastly, by prevail with Thwackum to conceal the matter for a
time, he know he should confirm an opinion of his friendship to Jones,
which he have greatly labour to establish in Mr Allworthy.
Chapter xi.
a short chapter; but which contain sufficient matter to affect the
good-natured reader.
it be Mr Allworthy's custom never to punish any one, not even to turn
away a servant, in a passion. he resolve therefore to delay pass
sentence on Jones till the afternoon.
the poor young man attend at dinner, as usual; but his heart be too
much load to suffer he to eat. his grief too be a good deal
aggravate by the unkind look of Mr Allworthy; whence he conclude
that Western have discover the whole affair between he and Sophia;
but as to Mr Blifil's story, he have not the least apprehension; for of
much the great part he be entirely innocent; and for the residue,
as he have forgive and forget it himself, so he suspect no
remembrance on the other side. when dinner be over, and the servant
depart, Mr Allworthy begin to harangue. he set forth, in a long
speech, the many iniquity of which Jones have be guilty,
particularly those which this day have bring to light; and conclude
by tell he, "that unless he could clear himself of the charge, he
be resolve to banish he his sight for ever."
many disadvantage attend poor Jones in make his defence; nay,
indeed, he hardly know his accusation; for as Mr Allworthy, in
recount the drunkenness, &c., while he lie ill, out of modesty sunk
everything that relate particularly to himself, which indeed
principally constitute the crime; Jones could not deny the charge.
his heart be, besides, almost break already; and his spirit be so
sunk, that he could say nothing for himself; but acknowledge the
whole, and, like a criminal in despair, throw himself upon mercy;
conclude, "that though he must own himself guilty of many folly
and inadvertency, he hop he have do nothing to deserve what would
be to he the great punishment in the world."
Allworthy answer, "that he have forgive he too often already, in
compassion to his youth, and in hope of his amendment: that he now
find he be an abandon reprobate, and such as it would be criminal
in any one to support and encourage. Nay," say Mr Allworthy to he,
"your audacious attempt to steal away the young lady, call upon I to
justify my own character in punish you. the world who have already
censure the regard I have show for you may think, with some colour
at least of justice, that I connive at so base and barbarous an
action--an action of which you must have know my abhorrence: and
which, have you have any concern for my ease and honour, as well as for
my friendship, you would never have think of undertake. fie upon
it, young man! indeed there be scarce any punishment equal to your
crime, and I can scarce think myself justifiable in what I be now
go to bestow on you. however, as I have educate you like a child
of my own, I will not turn you naked into the world. when you open
this paper, therefore, you will find something which may enable you,
with industry, to get an honest livelihood; but if you employ it to
bad purpose, I shall not think myself oblige to supply you
far, be resolve, from this day forward, to converse no more
with you on any account. I cannot avoid say, there be no part of
your conduct which I resent more than your ill-treatment of that good
young man (mean Blifil) who hath behave with so much tenderness
and honour towards you."
these last word be a dose almost too bitter to be swallow. a
flood of tear now gush from the eye of Jones, and every faculty of
speech and motion seem to have desert he. it be some time before
he be able to obey Allworthy's peremptory command of depart;
which he at length do, have first kiss his hand with a passion
difficult to be affect, and as difficult to be describe.
the reader must be very weak, if, when he consider the light in which
Jones then appear to Mr Allworthy, he should blame the rigour of his
sentence. and yet all the neighbourhood, either from this weakness, or
from some bad motive, condemn this justice and severity as the
high cruelty. Nay, the very person who have before censure the
good man for the kindness and tenderness show to a bastard (his own,
accord to the general opinion), now cry out as loudly against
turn his own child out of door. the woman especially be
unanimous in take the part of Jones, and raise more story on the
occasion than I have room, in this chapter, to set down.
one thing must not be omit, that, in their censure on this
occasion, none ever mention the sum contain in the paper which
Allworthy give Jones, which be no less than five hundred pound; but
all agree that he be send away penniless, and some say naked, from
the house of his inhuman father.
Chapter xii.
contain love-letter, &c.
Jones be command to leave the house immediately, and tell, that his
clothe and everything else should be send to he whithersoever he
should order they.
he accordingly set out, and walk above a mile, not regard, and
indeed scarce knowing, whither he go. at length a little brook
obstruct his passage, he throw himself down by the side of it; nor
could he help mutter with some little indignation, "sure my father
will not deny I this place to rest in!"
here he presently fall into the most violent agony, tear his hair
from his head, and use most other action which generally accompany
fit of madness, rage, and despair.
when he have in this manner vent the first emotion of passion, he
begin to come a little to himself. his grief now take another turn,
and discharge itself in a gentle way, till he become at last cool
enough to reason with his passion, and to consider what step be
proper to be take in his deplorable condition.
and now the great doubt be, how to act with regard to Sophia. the
thought of leave she almost rent his heart asunder; but the
consideration of reduce she to ruin and beggary still rack he, if
possible, more; and if the violent desire of possess her person
could have induce he to listen one moment to this alternative, still
he be by no means certain of her resolution to indulge his wish at
so high an expense. the resentment of Mr Allworthy, and the injury he
must do to his quiet, argue strongly against this latter; and lastly,
the apparent impossibility of his success, even if he would sacrifice
all these consideration to it, come to his assistance; and thus
honour at last back with despair, with gratitude to his benefactor,
and with real love to his mistress, get the well of burn desire,
and he resolve rather to quit Sophia, than pursue she to her ruin.
it be difficult for any who have not feel it, to conceive the glowing
warmth which fill his breast on the first contemplation of this
victory over his passion. pride flatter he so agreeably, that his
mind perhaps enjoy perfect happiness; but this be only momentary:
Sophia soon return to his imagination, and allay the joy of his
triumph with no less bitter pang than a good-natured general must
feel, when he survey the bleed heap, at the price of whose blood
he hath purchase his laurel; for thousand of tender idea lie
murder before our conqueror.
be resolve, however, to pursue the path of this giant honour, as
the gigantic poet Lee call it, he determine to write a farewel
letter to Sophia; and accordingly proceed to a house not far off,
where, be furnish with proper material, he write as follows:--
"MADAM,
"when you reflect on the situation in which I write, I be sure your
good-nature will pardon any inconsistency or absurdity which my
letter contain; for everything here flow from a heart so full,
that no language can express its dictate.
"I have resolve, madam, to obey your command, in fly for ever
from your dear, your lovely sight. Cruel indeed those command be;
but it be a cruelty which proceed from fortune, not from my Sophia.
Fortune hath make it necessary, necessary to your preservation, to
forget there ever be such a wretch as I be.
"believe I, I would not hint all my suffering to you, if I
imagine they could possibly escape your ear. I know the goodness
and tenderness of your heart, and would avoid give you any of
those pain which you always feel for the miserable. o let nothing,
which you shall hear of my hard fortune, cause a moment's concern;
for, after the loss of you, everything be to I a trifle.
"o Sophia! it be hard to leave you; it be hard still to desire you
to forget I; yet the sincere love oblige I to both. Pardon my
conceiving that any remembrance of I can give you disquiet; but if
I be so gloriously wretched, sacrifice I every way to your relief.
think I never love you; or think truly how little I deserve you;
and learn to scorn I for a presumption which can never be too
severely punished.--i be unable to say more.--May guardian angel
protect you for ever!"
he be now search his pocket for his wax, but find none, nor
indeed anything else, therein; for in truth he have, in his frantic
disposition, toss everything from he, and amongst the rest, his
pocket-book, which he have receive from Mr Allworthy, which he have
never open, and which now first occur to his memory.
the house supply he with a wafer for his present purpose, with
which, have seal his letter, he return hastily towards the brook
side, in order to search for the thing which he have there lose. in
his way he meet his old friend Black George, who heartily condole with
he on his misfortune; for this have already reach his ear, and
indeed those of all the neighbourhood.
Jones acquaint the gamekeeper with his loss, and he as readily go
back with he to the brook, where they search every tuft of grass in
the meadow, as well where Jones have not be as where he have be; but
all to no purpose, for they find nothing; for, indeed, though the
thing be then in the meadow, they omit to search the only place
where they be deposit; to wit, in the pocket of the say George;
for he have just before find they, and be luckily apprize of their
value, have very carefully put they up for his own use.
the gamekeeper have exert as much diligence in quest of the lost
good, as if he have hop to find they, desire Mr Jones to recollect
if he have be in no other place: "for sure," say he, "if you have
lose they here so lately, the thing must have be here still; for
this be a very unlikely place for any one to pass by." and indeed it
be by great accident that he himself have pass through that field,
in order to lay wire for hare, with which he be to supply a
poulterer at Bath the next morning.
Jones now give over all hope of recover his loss, and almost all
thought concern it, and turn to Black George, ask he
earnestly if he would do he the great favour in the world?
George answer with some hesitation, "Sir, you know you may command
I whatever be in my power, and I heartily wish it be in my power to
do you any service." in fact, the question stagger he; for he have,
by sell game, amass a pretty good sum of money in Mr Western's
service, and be afraid that Jones want to borrow some small matter
of he; but he be presently relieve from his anxiety, by be
desire to convey a letter to Sophia, which with great pleasure he
promise to do. and indeed I believe there be few favour which he
would not have gladly confer on Mr Jones; for he bear as much
gratitude towards he as he could, and be as honest as man who love
money well than any other thing in the universe, generally be.
Mrs Honour be agree by both to be the proper mean by which this
letter should pass to Sophia. they then separate; the gamekeeper
return home to Mr Western's, and Jones walk to an alehouse at half
a mile's distance, to wait for his messenger's return.
George no soon come home to his master's house than he meet with Mrs
Honour; to whom, have first sound she with a few previous
question, he deliver the letter for her mistress, and receive at
the same time another from she, for Mr Jones; which Honour tell he
she have carry all that day in her bosom, and begin to despair of
find any mean of deliver it.
the gamekeeper return hastily and joyfully to Jones, who, have
receive Sophia's letter from he, instantly withdraw, and eagerly
break it open, read as follows:--
"SIR,
"it be impossible to express what I have feel since I see you. your
submitting, on my account, to such cruel insult from my father,
lay I under an obligation I shall ever own. as you know his
temper, I beg you will, for my sake, avoid he. I wish I have any
comfort to send you; but believe this, that nothing but the last
violence shall ever give my hand or heart where you would be sorry
to see they bestow."
Jones read this letter a hundred time over, and kiss it a hundred
time as often. his passion now bring all tender desire back into
his mind. he repent that he have writ to Sophia in the manner we have
see above; but he repent more that he have make use of the interval
of his messenger's absence to write and dispatch a letter to Mr
Allworthy, in which he have faithfully promise and bind himself to
quit all thought of his love. however, when his cool reflection
return, he plainly perceive that his case be neither mend nor
alter by Sophia's billet, unless to give he some little glimpse of
hope, from her constancy, of some favourable accident hereafter. he
therefore resume his resolution, and take leave of Black George,
set forward to a town about five mile distant, whither he have desire
Mr Allworthy, unless he please to revoke his sentence, to send his
thing after he.
Chapter xiii.
the behaviour of Sophia on the present occasion; which none of her sex
will blame, who be capable of behave in the same manner. and the
discussion of a knotty point in the court of conscience.
Sophia have pass the last twenty-four hour in no very desirable
manner. during a large part of they she have be entertain by her
aunt with lecture of prudence, recommend to she the example of the
polite world, where love (so the good lady say) be at present
entirely laugh at, and where woman consider matrimony, as man do
office of public trust, only as the mean of make their fortune,
and of advance themselves in the world. in comment on which text
Mrs Western have display her eloquence during several hour.
these sagacious lecture, though little suit either to the taste or
inclination of Sophia, be, however, less irksome to she than her own
thought, that form the entertainment of the night, during which she
never once close her eye.
but though she could neither sleep nor rest in her bed, yet, have no
avocation from it, she be find there by her father at his return
from Allworthy's, which be not till past ten o'clock in the morning.
he go directly up to her apartment, open the door, and see she
be not up, cry, "oh! you be safe then, and I be resolve to keep
you so." he then lock the door, and deliver the key to Honour,
have first give she the strict charge, with great promise of
reward for her fidelity, and most dreadful menace of punishment in
case she should betray her trust.
Honour's order be, not to suffer her mistress to come out of her
room without the authority of the squire himself, and to admit none to
she but he and her aunt; but she be herself to attend she with
whatever Sophia please, except only pen, ink, and paper, of which she
be forbid the use.
the squire order his daughter to dress herself and attend he at
dinner; which she obey; and have sit the usual time, be again
conduct to her prison.
in the evening the gaoler Honour bring she the letter which she
receive from the gamekeeper. Sophia read it very attentively twice or
thrice over, and then throw herself upon the bed, and burst into a
flood of tear. Mrs Honour express great astonishment at this
behaviour in her mistress; nor could she forbear very eagerly beg
to know the cause of this passion. Sophia make she no answer for some
time, and then, start suddenly up, catch she maid by the hand, and
cry, "o honour! I be undo." "Marry forbid," cry Honour: "I wish
the letter have be burn before I have bring it to your la'ship. Ibe
sure I think it would have comfort your la'ship, or I would have
see it at the devil before I would have touch it." "Honour," say
Sophia, "you be a good girl, and it be vain to attempt conceal
long my weakness from you; I have throw away my heart on a man who
hath forsake I." "and be Mr Jones," answer the maid, "such a
perfidy man?" "he hath take his leave of I," say Sophia, "for ever
in that letter. Nay, he hath desire I to forget he. could he have
desire that if he have love I? could he have bear such a thought?
could he have write such a word?" "no, certainly, ma'am," cry
Honour; "and to be sure, if the good man in England be to desire I
to forget he, Iwould take he at his word. Marry, come up! I be sure
your la'ship hath do he too much honour ever to think on him;--a
young lady who may take her choice of all the young man in the
country. and to be sure, if I may be so presumptuous as to offer my
poor opinion, there be young Mr Blifil, who, besides that he be come
of honest parent, and will be one of the great squire all
hereabouts, he be to be sure, in my poor opinion, a more handsomer and
a more politer man by half; and besides, he be a young gentleman of a
sober character, and who may defy any of the neighbour to say black
be his eye; he follow no dirty trollop, nor can any bastard be lay
at his door. forget he, indeed! I thank Heaven I myself be not so
much at my last prayer as to suffer any man to bid I forget he
twice. if the good he that wear a head be for to go for to offer to
say such an affront word to I, I would never give he my company
afterwards, if there be another young man in the kingdom. and as I
be a saying, to be sure, there be young Mr Blifil." "name not his
detest name," cry Sophia. "Nay, ma'am," say Honour, "if your
la'ship doth not like he, there be more jolly handsome young man that
would court your la'ship, if they have but the least encouragement. I
donot believe there be arrow young gentleman in this county, or in the
next to it, that if your la'ship be but to look as if you have a mind
to he, would not come about to make his offer directly." "what a
wretch dost thou imagine I," cry Sophia, "by affront my ear
with such stuff! I detest all mankind." "Nay, to be sure, ma'am,"
answer Honour, "your la'ship hath have enough to give you a surfeit
of they. to be use ill by such a poor, beggarly, bastardly
fellow."--"hold your blasphemous tongue," cry Sophia: "how dare you
mention his name with disrespect before I? he use I ill? no, his
poor bleeding heart suffer more when he writ the cruel word than
mine from read they. o! he be all heroic virtue and angelic
goodness. I be ashamed of the weakness of my own passion, for blame
what I ought to admire. o, Honour! it be my good only which he
consult. to my interest he sacrifice both himself and I. the
apprehension of ruin I hath drive he to despair." "I be very
glad," say Honour, "to hear your la'ship take that into your
consideration; for to be sure, it must be nothing less than ruin to
give your mind to one that be turn out of door, and be not worth a
farthing in the world." "turn out of door!" cry Sophia hastily:
"how! what dost thou mean?" "why, to be sure, ma'am, my master no
soon tell Squire Allworthy about Mr Jones have offer to make
love to your la'ship than the squire strip he stark naked, and
turn he out of door!" "ha!" say Sophia, "I have be the cursed,
wretched cause of his destruction! turn naked out of door! here,
Honour, take all the money I have; take the ring from my finger.
here, my watch: carry he all. go find he immediately." "for Heaven's
sake, ma'am," answer Mrs Honour, "do but consider, if my master
should miss any of these thing, I should be make to answer for they.
therefore let I beg your la'ship not to part with your watch and
jewel. besides, the money, I think, be enough of all conscience; and
as for that, my master can never know anything of the matter." "here,
then," cry Sophia, "take every farthing I be worth, find he out
immediately, and give it he. go, go, lose not a moment."
Mrs Honour depart accord to order, and find Black George
below-stair, deliver he the purse, which contain sixteen
guineas, being, indeed, the whole stock of Sophia; for though her
father be very liberal to she, she be much too generous to be rich.
Black George have receive the purse, set forward towards the
alehouse; but in the way a thought occur to he, whether he should
not detain this money likewise. his conscience, however, immediately
start at this suggestion, and begin to upbraid he with ingratitude
to his benefactor. to this his avarice answer, that his conscience
should have consider the matter before, when he deprive poor Jones
of his £500. that have quietly acquiesce in what be of so much
great importance, it be absurd, if not downright hypocrisy, to
affect any qualm at this trifle. in return to which, Conscience, like
a good lawyer, attempt to distinguish between an absolute breach of
trust, as here, where the good be deliver, and a bare concealment
of what be find, as in the former case. Avarice presently treat
this with ridicule, call it a distinction without a difference, and
absolutely insist that when once all pretension of honour and
virtue be give up in any one instance, that there be no precedent
for resort to they upon a second occasion. in short, poor
Conscience have certainly be defeat in the argument, have not fear
stept in to her assistance, and very strenuously urge that the real
distinction between the two action, do not lie in the different
degree of honour but of safety: for that the secrete the £500 be a
matter of very little hazard; whereas the detaining the sixteen
guineas be liable to the utmost danger of discovery.
by this friendly aid of fear, Conscience obtain a compleat victory
in the mind of Black George, and, after make he a few compliment
on his honesty, force he to deliver the money to Jones.
Chapter xiv.
a short chapter, contain a short dialogue between Squire Western
and his sister.
Mrs Western have be engage abroad all that day. the squire meet she
at her return home; and when she enquire after Sophia, he acquaint
she that he have secure she safe enough. "she be lock up in
chamber," cry he, "and Honour keep the key." as his look be full
of prodigious wisdom and sagacity when he give his sister this
information, it be probable he expect much applause from she for
what he have do; but how be he disappoint when, with a most
disdainful aspect, she cry, "sure, brother, you be the weak of
all man. why will you not confide in I for the management of my
niece? why will you interpose? you have now undo all that I have
be spend my breath in order to bring about. while I have be
endeavour to fill her mind with maxim of prudence, you have be
provoke she to reject they. english woman, brother, I thank heaven,
be no slave. we be not to be lock up like the spanish and italian
wife. we have as good a right to liberty as yourself. we be to be
convince by reason and persuasion only, and not govern by force. I
have see the world, brother, and know what argument to make use of;
and if your folly have not prevent I, should have prevail with she
to form her conduct by those rule of prudence and discretion which I
formerly teach she." "to be sure," say the squire, "I be always in
the wrong." "Brother," answer the lady, "you be not in the wrong,
unless when you meddle with matter beyond your knowledge. you must
agree that I have see most of the world; and happy have it be for my
niece if she have not be take from under my care. it be by live at
home with you that she hath learn romantic notion of love and
nonsense." "you donot imagine, I hope," cry the squire, "that I have
teach she any such thing." "your ignorance, brother," return she,
"as the great Milton say, almost subdue my patience."[*] "D--n
Milton!" answer the squire: "if he have the impudence to say so to my
face, Iwould lend he a douse, thof he be never so great a man.
Patience! an you come to that, sister, I have more occasion of
patience, to be use like an overgrown schoolboy, as I be by you. do
you think no one hath any understanding, unless he hath be about at
court. pox! the world be come to a fine pass indeed, if we be all
fool, except a parcel of round-head and Hanover rat. pox! I hope
the time be a coming when we shall make fool of they, and every man
shall enjoy his own. thatbe all, sister; and every man shall enjoy his
own. I hope to zee it, sister, before the Hanover rat have eat up all
our corn, and leave we nothing but turnep to feed upon."--"I protest,
brother," cry she, "you be now get beyond my understanding. your
jargon of turnep and Hanover rat be to I perfectly
unintelligible."--"I believe," cry he, "you donot care to hear o'em;
but the country interest may succeed one day or other for all
that."--"I wish," answer the lady, "you would think a little of your
daughter's interest; for, believe I, she be in great danger than
the nation."--"Just now," say he, "you chid I for think on she,
and would ha' she leave to you."--"and if you will promise to interpose
no more," answer she, "I will, out of my regard to my niece,
undertake the charge."--"Well, do then," say the squire, "for you
know I always agree, that woman be the proper to manage woman."
[*] the reader may, perhaps, subdue his own patience, if he search
for this in Milton.] 
Mrs Western then depart, mutter something with an air of disdain,
concern woman and management of the nation. she immediately
repair to Sophia's apartment, who be now, after a day's
confinement, release again from her captivity.
BOOK VII.
contain three day.
Chapter i.
a comparison between the world and the stage.
the world hath be often compare to the theatre; and many grave
writer, as well as the poet, have consider human life as a great
drama, resemble, in almost every particular, those scenical
representation which Thespis be first report to have invent, and
which have be since receive with so much approbation and delight in
all polite country.
this thought hath be carry so far, and be become so general, that
some word proper to the theatre, and which be at first
metaphorically apply to the world, be now indiscriminately and
literally speak of both; thus stage and scene be by common use grow
as familiar to we, when we speak of life in general, as when we
confine ourselves to dramatic performance: and when transaction
behind the curtain be mention, St James's be more likely to occur
to our thought than Drury-lane.
it may seem easy enough to account for all this, by reflect that
the theatrical stage be nothing more than a representation, or, as
Aristotle call it, an imitation of what really exist; and hence,
perhaps, we might fairly pay a very high compliment to those who by
their writing or action have be so capable of imitate life, as
to have their picture in a manner confound with, or mistaken for,
the original.
but, in reality, we be not so fond of pay compliment to these
people, whom we use as child frequently do the instrument of their
amusement; and have much more pleasure in hiss and buffet they,
than in admire their excellence. there be many other reason which
have induce we to see this analogy between the world and the stage.
some have consider the large part of mankind in the light of
actor, as personate character no more their own, and to which in
fact they have no well title, than the player hath to be in earnest
think the king or emperor whom he represent. thus the hypocrite may
be say to be a player; and indeed the Greeks call they both by one
and the same name.
the brevity of life hath likewise give occasion to this comparison.
so the immortal shakespear--
--Lifebe a poor player,
that strut and fret his hour upon the stage,
and then be hear no more.
for which hackneyed quotation I will make the reader amend by a very
noble one, which few, I believe, have read. it be take from a poem
call the deity, publish about nine year ago, and long since
bury in oblivion; a proof that good book, no more than good man, do
always survive the bad.
from Thee[*] all human action take their spring,
the rise of empire and the fall of king!
see the vast Theatre of Time display'd,
while o'er the scene succeed hero tread!
