with pomp the shin image succeed,
what leader triumph, and what monarch bleed!
perform the part thy providence assign'd,
their pride, their passion, to thy end inclin'd:
awhile they glitter in the face of day,
then at thy nod the phantom pass away;
no trace leave of all the busy scene,
but that remembrance says--_the thing have be!_
[*] the deity.
in all these, however, and in every other similitude of life to the
theatre, the resemblance hath be always take from the stage only.
none, as I remember, have at all consider the audience at this great
drama.
but as Nature often exhibit some of her good performance to a very
full house, so will the behaviour of her spectator no less admit the
above-mention comparison than that of her actor. in this vast
theatre of time be seat the friend and the critic; here be clap
and shout, hiss and groan; in short, everything which be ever
see or hear at the Theatre-Royal.
let we examine this in one example; for instance, in the behaviour of
the great audience on that scene which nature be pleased to exhibit
in the twelfth chapter of the precede book, where she introduce
Black George run away with the £500 from his friend and
benefactor.
those who sit in the world's upper gallery treat that incident, I be
well convince, with their usual vociferation; and every term of
scurrilous reproach be most probably vent on that occasion.
if we have descend to the next order of spectator, we should have
find an equal degree of abhorrence, though less of noise and
scurrility; yet here the good woman give Black George to the devil,
and many of they expect every minute that the cloven-foot
gentleman would fetch his own.
the pit, as usual, be no doubt divide; those who delight in heroic
virtue and perfect character object to the produce such instance
of villany, without punish they very severely for the sake of
example. some of the author's friend cry, "Look'e, gentleman, the
man be a villain, but it be nature for all that." and all the young
critic of the age, the clerk, apprentice, &c., call it low, and
fall a groaning.
as for the box, they behave with their accustomed politeness. Most
of they be attend to something else. some of those few who
regard the scene at all, declare he be a bad kind of man; while
other refuse to give their opinion, till they have hear that of the
good judge.
now we, who be admit behind the scene of this great theatre of
Nature (and no author ought to write anything besides dictionary and
spelling-book who hath not this privilege), can censure the action,
without conceive any absolute detestation of the person, whom
perhaps Nature may not have design to act an ill part in all her
drama; for in this instance life most exactly resemble the stage,
since it be often the same person who represent the villain and the
heroe; and he who engage your admiration to-day will probably attract
your contempt to-morrow. as Garrick, whom I regard in tragedy to be
the great genius the world hath ever produce, sometimes
condescend to play the fool; so do Scipio the great, and Laelius the
Wise, accord to Horace, many year ago; nay, Cicero report they to
have be "incredibly childish." these, it be true, play the fool,
like my friend Garrick, in jest only; but several eminent character
have, in numberless instance of their life, play the fool
egregiously in earnest; so far as to render it a matter of some doubt
whether their wisdom or folly be predominant; or whether they be
well intitle to the applause or censure, the admiration or
contempt, the love or hatred, of mankind.
those person, indeed, who have pass any time behind the scene of
this great theatre, and be thoroughly acquaint not only with the
several disguise which be there put on, but also with the fantastic
and capricious behaviour of the Passions, who be the manager and
director of this theatre (for as to Reason, the patentee, he be know
to be a very idle fellow and seldom to exert himself), may most
probably have learn to understand the famous _nil admirari_ of
Horace, or in the english phrase, to stare at nothing.
a single bad act no more constitute a villain in life, than a single
bad part on the stage. the passion, like the manager of a playhouse,
often force man upon part without consult their judgment, and
sometimes without any regard to their talent. thus the man, as well
as the player, may condemn what he himself act; nay, it be common to
see vice sit as awkwardly on some man, as the character of Iago would
on the honest face of Mr William Mills.
upon the whole, then, the man of candour and of true understanding be
never hasty to condemn. he can censure an imperfection, or even a
vice, without rage against the guilty party. in a word, they be the
same folly, the same childishness, the same ill-breeding, and the same
ill-nature, which raise all the clamour and uproar both in life and
on the stage. the bad of man generally have the word rogue and
villain most in their mouth, as the low of all wretch be the
aptest to cry out low in the pit.
Chapter ii.
contain a conversation which Mr Jones have with himself.
Jones receive his effect from Mr Allworthy's early in the morning,
with the following answer to his letter:--
"SIR,
"I be command by my uncle to acquaint you, that as he do not
proceed to those measure he have take with you, without the
great deliberation, and after the full evidence of your
unworthiness, so will it be always out of your power to cause the
least alteration in his resolution. he express great surprize at
your presumption in say you have resign all pretension to a
young lady, to whom it be impossible you should ever have have any,
her birth and fortune have make she so infinitely your superior.
lastly, I be command to tell you, that the only instance of your
compliance with my uncle's inclination which he require, be, your
immediately quit this country. I cannot conclude this without
offer you my advice, as a Christian, that you would seriously
think of amend your life. that you may be assist with grace so
to do, will be always the prayer of
"your humble servant,
"W. BLIFIL."
many contend passion be raise in our heroe's mind by this
letter; but the tender prevail at last over the indignant and
irascible, and a flood of tear come seasonably to his assistance, and
possibly prevent his misfortune from either turn his head, or
burst his heart.
he grow, however, soon ashamed of indulge this remedy; and start
up, he cry, "well, then, I will give Mr Allworthy the only instance
he require of my obedience. I will go this moment--but whither?--why,
let Fortune direct; since there be no other who think it of any
consequence what become of this wretched person, it shall be a matter
of equal indifference to myself. shall I alone regard what no
other--ha! have I not reason to think there be another?--one whose
value be above that of the whole world!--i may, I must imagine my
Sophia be not indifferent to what become of I. shall I then leave
this only friend--and such a friend? shall I not stay with
her?--where--how can I stay with she? have I any hope of ever see
she, though she be as desirous as myself, without expose she to the
wrath of her father, and to what purpose? can I think of solicit
such a creature to consent to her own ruin? shall I indulge any
passion of mine at such a price? shall I lurk about this country like
a thief, with such intentions?--no, I disdain, I detest the thought.
Farewel, Sophia; farewel, most lovely, most beloved--" here passion
stop his mouth, and find a vent at his eye.
and now have take a resolution to leave the country, he begin to
debate with himself whither he should go. the world, as Milton phrase
it, lie all before he; and Jones, no more than Adam, have any man to
whom he might resort for comfort or assistance. all his acquaintance
be the acquaintance of Mr Allworthy; and he have no reason to expect
any countenance from they, as that gentleman have withdraw his favour
from he. man of great and good character should indeed be very
cautious how they discard their dependent; for the consequence to the
unhappy sufferer be be discard by all other.
what course of life to pursue, or to what business to apply himself,
be a second consideration: and here the prospect be all a melancholy
void. every profession, and every trade, require length of time, and
what be bad, money; for matter be so constituted, that "nothing
out of nothing" be not a truer maxim in physics than in politic; and
every man who be greatly destitute of money, be on that account
entirely exclude from all mean of acquire it.
at last the Ocean, that hospitable friend to the wretched, open she
capacious arm to receive he; and he instantly resolve to accept she
kind invitation. to express myself less figuratively, he determine to
go to sea.
this think indeed no soon suggest itself, than he eagerly
embrace it; and have presently hire horse, he set out for Bristol
to put it in execution.
but before we attend he on this expedition, we shall resort awhile to
Mr Western's, and see what far happen to the charming Sophia.
Chapter iii.
contain several dialogue.
the morning in which Mr Jones depart, Mrs Western summon Sophia
into her apartment; and have first acquaint she that she have
obtain her liberty of her father, she proceed to read she a long
lecture on the subject of matrimony; which she treat not as a
romantic scheme of happiness arise from love, as it hath be
describe by the poet; nor do she mention any of those purpose for
which we be teach by divine to regard it as institute by sacred
authority; she consider it rather as a fund in which prudent woman
deposit their fortune to the good advantage, in order to receive a
large interest for they than they could have elsewhere.
when Mrs Western have finish, Sophia answer, "that she be very
incapable of argue with a lady of her aunt's superior knowledge and
experience, especially on a subject which she have so very little
consider, as this of matrimony."
"argue with I, child!" reply the other; "I do not indeed expect it.
I should have see the world to very little purpose truly, if I be to
argue with one of your year. I have take this trouble, in order to
instruct you. the antient philosopher, such as Socrates, Alcibiades,
and other, do not use to argue with their scholar. you be to
consider I, child, as Socrates, not ask your opinion, but only
inform you of mine." from which last word the reader may possibly
imagine, that this lady have read no more of the philosophy of
Socrates, than she have of that of Alcibiades; and indeed we cannot
resolve his curiosity as to this point.
"Madam," cry Sophia, "I have never presume to controvert any
opinion of your; and this subject, as I say, I have never yet
think of, and perhaps never may."
"indeed, Sophy," reply the aunt, "this dissimulation with I be very
foolish. the french shall as soon persuade I that they take foreign
town in defence only of their own country, as you can impose on I to
believe you have never yet think seriously of matrimony. how can
you, child, affect to deny that you have consider of contract an
alliance, when you so well know I be acquaint with the party with
whom you desire to contract it?--an alliance as unnatural, and
contrary to your interest, as a separate league with the French would
be to the interest of the Dutch! but however, if you have not hitherto
consider of this matter, I promise you it be now high time, for my
brother be resolve immediately to conclude the treaty with Mr Blifil;
and indeed I be a sort of guarantee in the affair, and have promise
your concurrence."
"indeed, madam," cry Sophia, "this be the only instance in which I
must disobey both yourself and my father. for this be a match which
require very little consideration in I to refuse."
"if I be not as great a philosopher as Socrates himself," return
Mrs Western, "you would overcome my patience. what objection can you
have to the young gentleman?"
"a very solid objection, in my opinion," say sophia--"i hate he."
"will you never learn a proper use of word?" answer the aunt.
"indeed, child, you should consult Bailey's Dictionary. it be
impossible you should hate a man from whom you have receive no
injury. by hatred, therefore, you mean no more than dislike, which be
no sufficient objection against your marrying of he. I have know
many couple, who have entirely dislike each other, lead very
comfortable genteel life. believe I, child, I know these thing
well than you. you will allow I, I think, to have see the world,
in which I have not an acquaintance who would not rather be think to
dislike her husband than to like he. the contrary be such
out-of-fashion romantic nonsense, that the very imagination of it be
shocking."
"indeed, madam," reply Sophia, "I shall never marry a man I dislike.
if I promise my father never to consent to any marriage contrary to
his inclination, I think I may hope he will never force I into that
state contrary to my own."
"inclination!" cry the aunt, with some warmth. "inclination! I be
astonish at your assurance. a young woman of your age, and
unmarried, to talk of inclination! but whatever your inclination may
be, my brother be resolve; nay, since you talk of inclination, I
shall advise he to hasten the treaty. inclination!"
Sophia then fling herself upon her knee, and tear begin to trickle
from her shin eye. she entreat her aunt, "to have mercy upon
she, and not to resent so cruelly her unwillingness to make herself
miserable;" often urge, "that she alone be concern, and that her
happiness only be at stake."
as a bailiff, when well authorize by his writ, have possess
himself of the person of some unhappy debtor, view all his tear
without concern; in vain the wretched captive attempt to raise
compassion; in vain the tender wife bereft of her companion, the
little prattling boy, or fright girl, be mention as inducement
to reluctance. the noble bumtrap, blind and deaf to every circumstance
of distress, greatly rise above all the motif to humanity, and into
the hand of the gaoler resolve to deliver his miserable prey.
not less blind to the tear, or less deaf to every entreaty of Sophia
be the politic aunt, nor less determined be she to deliver over the
trembling maid into the arm of the gaoler Blifil. she answer with
great impetuosity, "so far, madam, from your being concern alone,
your concern be the least, or surely the least important. it be the
honour of your family which be concern in this alliance; you be
only the instrument. do you conceive, mistress, that in an
intermarriage between kingdom, as when a daughter of France be
marry into Spain, the princess herself be alone consider in the
match? no! it be a match between two kingdom, rather than between two
person. the same happen in great family such as ours. the alliance
between the family be the principal matter. you ought to have a
great regard for the honour of your family than for your own person;
and if the example of a princess cannot inspire you with these noble
thought, you cannot surely complain at be use no bad than all
princess be use."
"I hope, madam," cry Sophia, with a little elevation of voice, "I
shall never do anything to dishonour my family; but as for Mr Blifil,
whatever may be the consequence, I be resolve against he, and no
force shall prevail in his favour."
Western, who have be within hearing during the great part of the
precede dialogue, have now exhaust all his patience; he therefore
enter the room in a violent passion, crying, "D--n I then if
shatunt ha'un, d--n I if shatunt, thatbe all--thatbe all; d--n I if
shatunt."
Mrs Western have collect a sufficient quantity of wrath for the use
of Sophia; but she now transfer it all to the squire. "brother,"
say she, "it be astonishing that you will interfere in a matter
which you have totally leave to my negotiation. regard to my family
hath make I take upon myself to be the mediating power, in order to
rectify those mistake in policy which you have commit in your
daughter's education. for, brother, it be you--it be your
preposterous conduct which hath eradicate all the seed that I have
formerly sow in her tender mind. it be you yourself who have teach
her disobedience."--"blood!" cry the squire, foam at the mouth,
"you be enough to conquer the patience of the devil! have I ever
teach my daughter disobedience?--Here she stand; speak honestly,
girl, do ever I bid you be disobedient to I? have not I do
everything to humour and to gratify you, and to make you obedient to
I? and very obedient to I she be when a little child, before you
take she in hand and spoil she, by fill her head with a pack of
court notion. why--why--why--do I not overhear you tell she she
must behave like a princess? you have make a Whig of the girl; and how
should her father, or anybody else, expect any obedience from
her?"--"brother," answer Mrs Western, with an air of great disdain,
"I cannot express the contempt I have for your politic of all kind;
but I will appeal likewise to the young lady herself, whether I have
ever teach she any principle of disobedience. on the contrary,
niece, have I not endeavour to inspire you with a true idea of the
several relation in which a human creature stand in society? have I
not take infinite pain to show you, that the law of nature hath
enjoin a duty on child to their parent? have I not tell you what
Plato say on that subject?--a subject on which you be so notoriously
ignorant when you come first under my care, that I verily believe you
do not know the relation between a daughter and a father."--"'Tis a
lie," answer Western. "the girl be no such fool, as to live to
eleven year old without know that she be her father's
relation."--"O! more than gothic ignorance," answer the lady. "and
as for your manner, brother, I must tell you, they deserve a
cane."--"why then you may gi' it I, if you think you be able," cry
the squire; "nay, I suppose your niece there will be ready enough to
help you."--"brother," say Mrs Western, "though I despise you beyond
expression, yet I shall endure your insolence no long; so I desire
my coach may be get ready immediately, for I be resolve to leave your
house this very morning."--"and a good riddance too," answer he; "I
can bear your insolence no long, an you come to that. blood! it be
almost enough of itself to make my daughter undervalue my sense, when
she hear you tell I every minute you despise I."--"It be
impossible, it be impossible," cry the aunt; "no one can undervalue
such a boor."--"Boar," answer the squire, "I be no boar; no, nor
ass; no, nor rat neither, madam. remember that--I be no rat. I be a
true Englishman, and not of your Hanover breed, that have eat up the
nation."--"Thou art one of those wise man," cry she, "whose
nonsensical principle have undo the nation; by weaken the hand
of our government at home, and by discourage our friend and
encourage our enemy abroad."--"Ho! be you come back to your
politic?" cry the squire: "as for those I despise they as much as I
do a f--t." which last word he accompany and grace with the very
action, which, of all other, be the most proper to it. and whether
it be this word or the contempt expr for her politic, which most
affect Mrs Western, I will not determine; but she fly into the most
violent rage, utter phrase improper to be here relate, and
instantly burst out of the house. nor do her brother or her niece
think proper either to stop or to follow she; for the one be so much
possess by concern, and the other by anger, that they be render
almost motionless.
the squire, however, send after his sister the same holloa which
attend the departure of a hare, when she be first start before the
hound. he be indeed a great master of this kind of vociferation, and
have a holla proper for most occasion in life.
woman who, like Mrs Western, know the world, and have apply
themselves to philosophy and politic, would have immediately avail
themselves of the present disposition of Mr Western's mind, by
throw in a few artful compliment to his understanding at the
expense of his absent adversary; but poor Sophia be all simplicity.
by which word we do not intend to insinuate to the reader, that she
be silly, which be generally understand as a synonymous term with
simple; for she be indeed a most sensible girl, and her understanding
be of the first rate; but she want all that useful art which
female convert to so many good purpose in life, and which, as it
rather arise from the heart than from the head, be often the property
of the silliest of woman.
chapter iv.
a picture of a country gentlewoman take from the life.
Mr Western have finish his holla, and take a little breath, begin
to lament, in very pathetic term, the unfortunate condition of man,
who be, say he, "always whipt in by the humour of some d--n'd b--
or other. I think I be hard run enough by your mother for one man;
but after give she a dodge, herebe another b-- follow I upon the
foil; but curse my jacket if I will be run down in this manner by any
o'um."
Sophia never have a single dispute with her father, till this unlucky
affair of Blifil, on any account, except in defence of her mother,
whom she have love most tenderly, though she lose she in the eleventh
year of her age. the squire, to whom that poor woman have be a
faithful upper-servant all the time of their marriage, have return
that behaviour by make what the world call a good husband. he very
seldom swear at she (perhaps not above once a week) and never beat
she; she have not the least occasion for jealousy, and be perfect
mistress of her time; for she be never interrupt by her husband,
who be engage all the morning in his field exercise, and all the
evening with bottle companion. she scarce indeed ever see he but at
meal; where she have the pleasure of carve those dish which she
have before attend at the dressing. from these meal she retire
about five minute after the other servant, have only stay to
drink "the king over the water." such be, it seem, Mr Western's
order; for it be a maxim with he, that woman should come in with
the first dish, and go out after the first glass. obedience to these
order be perhaps no difficult task; for the conversation (if it may
be call so) be seldom such as could entertain a lady. it consist
chiefly of hallowing, singing, relation of sporting adventure,
b--d--y, and abuse of woman, and of the government.
these, however, be the only season when Mr Western see his wife;
for when he repair to her bed, he be generally so drunk that he
could not see; and in the sporting season he always rise from she
before it be light. thus be she perfect mistress of her time, and
have besides a coach and four usually at her command; though unhappily,
indeed, the badness of the neighbourhood, and of the road, make this
of little use; for none who have set much value on their neck would
have pass through the one, or who have set any value on their hour,
would have visit the other. now to deal honestly with the reader,
she do not make all the return expect to so much indulgence; for
she have be marry against her will by a fond father, the match
have be rather advantageous on her side; for the squire's estate
be upward of £3000 a year, and her fortune no more than a bare £8000.
hence perhaps she have contract a little gloominess of temper, for
she be rather a good servant than a good wife; nor have she always the
gratitude to return the extraordinary degree of roar mirth, with
which the squire receive she, even with a good-humour smile. she
would, moreover, sometimes interfere with matter which do not
concern she, as the violent drinking of her husband, which in the
gentle term she would take some of the few opportunity he give
she of remonstrate against. and once in her life she very earnestly
entreat he to carry she for two month to London, which he
peremptorily deny; nay, be angry with his wife for the request ever
after, be well assure that all the husband in London be
cuckold.
for this last, and many other good reason, western at length heartily
hat his wife; and as he never conceal this hatred before her
death, so he never forget it afterwards; but when anything in the
least sour he, as a bad scenting day, or a distemper among his
hound, or any other such misfortune, he constantly vent his spleen
by invective against the deceased, say, "if my wife be alive now,
she would be glad of this."
these invective he be especially desirous of throw forth before
Sophia; for as he love she more than he do any other, so he be
really jealous that she have love her mother well than he. and this
jealousy Sophia seldom fail of heighten on these occasion; for
he be not content with violate her ear with the abuse of her
mother, but endeavour to force an explicit approbation of all this
abuse; with which desire he never could prevail upon she by any
promise or threat to comply.
hence some of my reader will, perhaps, wonder that the squire have not
hat Sophia as much as he have hat her mother; but I must inform
they, that hatred be not the effect of love, even through the medium
of jealousy. it be, indeed, very possible for jealous person to kill
the object of their jealousy, but not to hate they. which sentiment
be a pretty hard morsel, and bear something of the air of a
paradox, we shall leave the reader to chew the cud upon it to the end
of the chapter.
Chapter v.
the generous behaviour of Sophia towards her aunt.
Sophia keep silence during the forego speech of her father, nor do
she once answer otherwise than with a sigh; but as he understand none
of the language, or, as he call it, lingo of the eye, so he be not
satisfied without some further approbation of his sentiment, which he
now demand of his daughter; tell she, in the usual way, "he
expect she be ready to take the part of everybody against he, as
she have always do that of the b-- her mother." Sophia remain
still silent, he cry out, "what, art dumb? why dost unt speak? be
not thy mother a d--d b-- to I? answer I that. what, I suppose you
despise your father too, and donot think he good enough to speak to?"
"for Heaven's sake, sir," answer Sophia, "do not give so cruel a
turn to my silence. I be sure I would soon die than be guilty of any
disrespect towards you; but how can I venture to speak, when every
word must either offend my dear papa, or convict I of the black
ingratitude as well as impiety to the memory of the good of mother;
for such, I be certain, my mamma be always to I?"
"and your aunt, I suppose, be the good of sister too!" reply the
squire. "will you be so kind as to allow that she be a b--? I may
fairly insist upon that, I think?"
"indeed, sir," say Sophia, "I have great obligation to my aunt. she
hath be a second mother to I."
"and a second wife to I too," return western; "so you will take she
part too! you willnot confess that she hath act the part of the vile
sister in the world?"
"upon my word, sir," cry Sophia, "I must belie my heart wickedly if
I do. I know my aunt and you differ very much in your way of
thinking; but I have hear she a thousand time express the great
affection for you; and I be convince, so far from she be the bad
sister in the world, there be very few who love a brother well."
"the English of all which be," answer the squire, "that I be in the
wrong. ay, certainly. ay, to be sure the woman be in the right, and
the man in the wrong always."
"pardon I, sir," cry Sophia. "I do not say so."
"what donot you say?" answer the father: "you have the impudence to
say shebe in the right: doth it not follow then of course that I be in
the wrong? and perhaps I be in the wrong to suffer such a Presbyterian
hanoverian b-- to come into my house. she may 'dite I of a plot for
anything I know, and give my estate to the government."
"so far, sir, from injure you or your estate," say Sophia, "if my
aunt have die yesterday, I be convinced she would have leave you her
whole fortune."
whether Sophia intend it or no, I shall not presume to assert; but
certain it be, these last word penetrate very deep into the ear of
her father, and produce a much more sensible effect than all she have
say before. he receive the sound with much the same action as a man
receive a bullet in his head. he start, stagger, and turn pale.
after which he remain silent above a minute, and then begin in the
follow hesitate manner: "yesterday! she would have leave I her
esteate yesterday! would she? why yesterday, of all the day in the
year? I suppose if she die to-morrow, she will leave it to somebody
else, and perhaps out of the vamily."--"My aunt, sir," cry Sophia,
"hath very violent passion, and I cannot answer what she may do under
their influence."
"you cannot!" return the father: "and pray who hath be the occasion
of put she into those violent passion? Nay, who hath actually put
she into they? be not you and she hard at it before I come into the
room? besides, be not all our quarrel about you? I have not
quarrel with sister this many year but upon your account; and now
you would throw the whole blame upon I, as thof I should be the
occasion of she leave the esteate out o' the vamily. I could have
expect no well indeed; this be like the return you make to all the
rest of my fondness."
"I beseech you then," cry Sophia, "upon my knee I beseech you, if I
have be the unhappy occasion of this difference, that you will
endeavour to make it up with my aunt, and not suffer she to leave your
house in this violent rage of anger: she be a very good-natured woman,
and a few civil word will satisfy she. let I entreat you, sir."
"so I must go and ask pardon for your fault, must I?" answer
Western. "you have lose the hare, and I must draw every way to find
she again? indeed, if I be certain"--here he stopt, and Sophia
throwing in more entreaty, at length prevail upon he; so that
after vent two or three bitter sarcastical expression against his
daughter, he depart as fast as he could to recover his sister,
before her equipage could be get ready.
Sophia then return to her chamber of mourning, where she indulge
herself (if the phrase may be allow I) in all the luxury of tender
grief. she read over more than once the letter which she have receive
from Jones; her muff too be use on this occasion; and she bath
both these, as well as herself, with her tear. in this situation the
friendly Mrs Honour exert her utmost ability to comfort she
afflict mistress. she run over the name of many young gentleman:
and have greatly commend their part and person, assure Sophia
that she might take her choice of any. these method must have
certainly be use with some success in disorder of the like kind,
or so skilful a practitioner as Mrs Honour would never have venture
to apply they; nay, I have hear that the college of chambermaid hold
they to be as sovereign remedy as any in the female dispensary; but
whether it be that Sophia's disease differ inwardly from those
case with which it agree in external symptom, I will not assert;
but, in fact, the good waiting-woman do more harm than good, and at
last so incense her mistress (which be no easy matter) that with an
angry voice she dismiss she from her presence.
Chapter vi.
contain great variety of matter.
the squire overtake his sister just as she be step into the
coach, and partly by force, and partly by solicitation, prevail
upon she to order her horse back into their quarter. he succeed in
this attempt without much difficulty; for the lady be, as we have
already hint, of a most placable disposition, and greatly love she
brother, though she despise his part, or rather his little knowledge
of the world.
Poor Sophia, who have first set on foot this reconciliation, be now
make the sacrifice to it. they both concur in their censure on her
conduct; jointly declare war against she, and directly proceed to
counsel, how to carry it on in the most vigorous manner. for this
purpose, Mrs Western propose not only an immediate conclusion of the
treaty with Allworthy, but as immediately to carry it into execution;
say, "that there be no other way to succeed with her niece, but by
violent method, which she be convince Sophia have not sufficient
resolution to resist. by violent," say she, "I mean rather, hasty
measure; for as to confinement or absolute force, no such thing must
or can be attempt. our plan must be concert for a surprize, and
not for a storm."
these matter be resolve on, when Mr Blifil come to pay a visit to
his mistress. the squire no soon hear of his arrival, than he stept
aside, by his sister's advice, to give his daughter order for the
proper reception of her lover: which he do with the most bitter
execration and denunciation of judgment on her refusal.
the impetuosity of the squire bear down all before he; and Sophia, as
her aunt very wisely foresaw, be not able to resist he. she agree,
therefore, to see Blifil, though she have scarce spirit or strength
sufficient to utter her assent. indeed, to give a peremptory denial to
a father whom she so tenderly love, be no easy task. have this
circumstance be out of the case, much less resolution than what she
be really mistress of, would, perhaps, have serve she; but it be no
unusual thing to ascribe those action entirely to fear, which be in
a great measure produce by love.
in pursuance, therefore, of her father's peremptory command, Sophia
now admit Mr Blifil's visit. scene like this, when paint at
large, afford, as we have observe, very little entertainment to the
reader. here, therefore, we shall strictly adhere to a rule of Horace;
by which writer be direct to pass over all those matter which
they despair of place in a shining light;--a rule, we conceive, of
excellent use as well to the historian as to the poet; and which, if
follow, must at least have this good effect, that many a great evil
(for so all great book be call) would thus be reduce to a small
one.
it be possible the great art use by Blifil at this interview would
have prevail on Sophia to have make another man in his circumstance
her confident, and to have reveal the whole secret of her heart to
he; but she have contract so ill an opinion of this young gentleman,
that she be resolve to place no confidence in he; for simplicity,
when set on its guard, be often a match for cunning. her behaviour to
he, therefore, be entirely force, and indeed such as be generally
prescribe to virgin upon the second formal visit from one who be
appoint for their husband.
but though Blifil declare himself to the squire perfectly satisfied
with his reception; yet that gentleman, who, in company with his
sister, have overhear all, be not so well pleased. he resolve, in
pursuance of the advice of the sage lady, to push matter as forward
as possible; and address himself to his intended son-in-law in the
hunting phrase, he cry, after a loud holla, "follow she, boy, follow
she; run in, run in; thatbe it, honey. dead, dead, dead. never be
bashful, nor stand shall I, shall I? allworthy and I can finish all
matter between we this afternoon, and let we ha' the wedding
to-morrow."
Blifil have convey the utmost satisfaction into his countenance,
answer, "as there be nothing, sir, in this world which I so eagerly
desire as an alliance with your family, except my union with the most
amiable and deserve Sophia, you may easily imagine how impatient I
must be to see myself in possession of my two high wish. if I
have not therefore importune you on this head, you will impute it
only to my fear of offend the lady, by endeavour to hurry on so
bless an event fast than a strict compliance with all the rule of
decency and decorum will permit. but if, by your interest, sir, she
might be induce to dispense with any formalities--"
"Formalities! with a pox!" answer the squire. "Pooh, all stuff and
nonsense! I tell thee, she shall ha' thee to-morrow: you will know the
world well hereafter, when you come to my age. woman never gi' their
consent, man, if they can help it, 'tis not the fashion. if I have
stay for her mother's consent, I might have be a batchelor to this
day.--To she, to she, co to she, thatbe it, you jolly dog. I tell thee
shat ha' her to-morrow morning."
Blifil suffer himself to be overpower by the forcible rhetoric of
the squire; and it be agree that Western should close with
allworthy that very afternoon, the lover depart home, have first
earnestly beg that no violence might be offer to the lady by this
haste, in the same manner as a popish inquisitor beg the lay power to
do no violence to the heretic deliver over to it, and against whom
the church hath pass sentence.
and, to say the truth, Blifil have pass sentence against Sophia; for,
however pleased he have declare himself to Western with his reception,
he be by no mean satisfied, unless it be that he be convince of
the hatred and scorn of his mistress: and this have produce no less
reciprocal hatred and scorn in he. it may, perhaps, be ask, why
then do he not put an immediate end to all further courtship? I
answer, for that very reason, as well as for several other equally
good, which we shall now proceed to open to the reader.
though Mr Blifil be not of the complexion of Jones, nor ready to eat
every woman he see; yet he be far from be destitute of that
appetite which be say to be the common property of all animal. with
this, he have likewise that distinguish taste, which serve to
direct man in their choice of the object or food of their several
appetite; and this teach he to consider Sophia as a most delicious
morsel, indeed to regard she with the same desire which an ortolan
inspire into the soul of an epicure. now the agony which affect
the mind of Sophia, rather augmented than impair her beauty; for she
tear add brightness to her eye, and her breast rise higher with
her sigh. indeed, no one hath see beauty in its high lustre who
hath never see it in distress. Blifil therefore look on this human
ortolan with great desire than when he view she last; nor be his
desire at all lessen by the aversion which he discover in she to
himself. on the contrary, this serve rather to heighten the pleasure
he propose in rifle her charm, as it add triumph to lust; nay,
he have some further view, from obtain the absolute possession of
her person, which we detest too much even to mention; and revenge
itself be not without its share in the gratification which he
promise himself. the rival poor Jones, and supplant he in her
affection, add another spur to his pursuit, and promise another
additional rapture to his enjoyment.
besides all these view, which to some scrupulous person may seem to
savour too much of malevolence, he have one prospect, which few reader
will regard with any great abhorrence. and this be the estate of Mr
Western; which be all to be settle on his daughter and her issue;
for so extravagant be the affection of that fond parent, that,
provide his child would but consent to be miserable with the husband
he choose, he care not at what price he purchase he.
for these reason Mr Blifil be so desirous of the match that he
intend to deceive Sophia, by pretend love to she; and to deceive
her father and his own uncle, by pretend he be belove by she. in
do this he avail himself of the piety of Thwackum, who hold, that
if the end propose be religious (as surely matrimony be), it
matter not how wicked be the mean. as to other occasion, he use
to apply the philosophy of Square, which teach, that the end be
immaterial, so that the mean be fair and consistent with moral
rectitude. to say truth, there be few occurrence in life on which
he could not draw advantage from the precept of one or other of those
great master.
little deceit be indeed necessary to be practise on Mr Western; who
think the inclination of his daughter of as little consequence as
Blifil himself conceive they to be; but as the sentiment of Mr
Allworthy be of a very different kind, so it be absolutely
necessary to impose on he. in this, however, Blifil be so well
assist by Western, that he succeed without difficulty; for as Mr
Allworthy have be assure by her father that Sophia have a proper
affection for Blifil, and that all which he have suspect concern
Jones be entirely false, Blifil have nothing more to do than to
confirm these assertion; which he do with such equivocation, that
he preserve a salvo for his conscience; and have the satisfaction of
convey a lie to his uncle, without the guilt of tell one. when
he be examine touch the inclination of Sophia by Allworthy, who
say, "he would on no account be accessary to force a young lady
into a marriage contrary to her own will;" he answer, "that the real
sentiment of young lady be very difficult to be understand; that
her behaviour to he be full as forward as he wish it, and that if
he could believe her father, she have all the affection for he which
any lover could desire. as for Jones," say he, "whom I be loth to
call villain, though his behaviour to you, sir, sufficiently justify
the appellation, his own vanity, or perhaps some wicked view, might
make he boast of a falsehood; for if there have be any reality in
Miss Western's love to he, the greatness of her fortune would never
have suffer he to desert she, as you be well informed he hath.
lastly, sir, I promise you I would not myself, for any consideration,
no, not for the whole world, consent to marry this young lady, if I
be not persuade she have all the passion for I which I desire she
should have."
this excellent method of convey a falsehood with the heart only,
without make the tongue guilty of an untruth, by the mean of
equivocation and imposture, hath quiet the conscience of many a
notable deceiver; and yet, when we consider that it be Omniscience on
which these endeavour to impose, it may possibly seem capable of
afford only a very superficial comfort; and that this artful and
refined distinction between communicate a lie, and tell one, be
hardly worth the pain it cost they.
Allworthy be pretty well satisfied with what Mr Western and Mr Blifil
tell he: and the treaty be now, at the end of two day, conclude.
nothing then remain previous to the office of the priest, but the
office of the lawyer, which threaten to take up so much time, that
Western offer to bind himself by all manner of covenant, rather
than defer the happiness of the young couple. indeed, he be so very
earnest and pressing, that an indifferent person might have conclude
he be more a principal in this match than he really be; but this
eagerness be natural to he on all occasion: and he conduct every
scheme he undertake in such a manner, as if the success of that alone
be sufficient to constitute the whole happiness of his life.
the joint importunity of both father and son-in-law would probably
have prevail on Mr Allworthy, who brook but ill any delay of
give happiness to other, have not Sophia herself prevent it, and
take measure to put a final end to the whole treaty, and to rob both
church and law of those taxis which these wise body have think
proper to receive from the propagation of the human specie in a
lawful manner. of which in the next chapter.
Chapter vii.
a strange resolution of Sophia, and a more strange stratagem of Mrs
Honour.
Though Mrs Honour be principally attach to her own interest, she
be not without some little attachment to Sophia. to say truth, it be
very difficult for any one to know that young lady without love she.
she no soon therefore hear a piece of news, which she imagine to
be of great importance to her mistress, than, quite forget the
anger which she have conceive two day before, at her unpleasant
dismission from Sophia's presence, she run hastily to inform she of
the news.
the beginning of her discourse be as abrupt as her entrance into the
room. "o dear ma'am!" say she, "what doth your la'ship think? to be
sure I be frightened out of my wit; and yet I think it my duty to
tell your la'ship, though perhaps it may make you angry, for we
servant donot always know what will make our lady angry; for, to be
sure, everything be always lay to the charge of a servant. when our
lady be out of humour, to be sure we must be scold; and to be
sure I should not wonder if your la'ship should be out of humour; nay,
it must surprize you certainly, ay, and shock you too."--"Good Honour,
let I know it without any long preface," say Sophia; "there be
few thing, I promise you, which will surprize, and few which will
shock I."--"dear ma'am," answer Honour, "to be sure, I overheard my
master talk to parson Supple about get a licence this very
afternoon; and to be sure I hear he say, your la'ship should be
married to-morrow morning." Sophia turn pale at these word, and
repeat eagerly, "to-morrow morning!"--"Yes, ma'am," reply the
trusty waiting-woman, "I will take my oath I hear my master say
so."--"honour," say Sophia, "you have both surprize and shock I
to such a degree that I have scarce any breath or spirit leave. what
be to be do in my dreadful situation?"--"i wish I be able to advise
your la'ship," say she. "do advise I," cry Sophia; "pray, dear
Honour, advise I. think what you would attempt if it be your own
case."--"Indeed, ma'am," cry Honour, "I wish your la'ship and I
could change situation; that be, I mean without hurt your la'ship;
for to be sure I donot wish you so bad as to be a servant; but because
that if so be it be my case, I should find no manner of difficulty in
it; for, in my poor opinion, young Squire Blifil be a charming, sweet,
handsome man."--"Don't mention such stuff," cry Sophia. "such
stuff!" repeat Honour; "why, there. well, to be sure, whatbe one
man's meat be another man's poison, and the same be altogether as true
of woman."--"honour," say Sophia, "rather than submit to be the wife
of that contemptible wretch, I would plunge a dagger into my
heart."--"O lud! ma'am!" answer the other, "I be sure you frighten
I out of my wit now. let I beseech your la'ship not to suffer such
wicked thought to come into your head. o lud! to be sure I tremble
every inch of I. dear ma'am, consider, that to be deny Christian
burial, and to have your corpse bury in the highway, and a stake
drive through you, as farmer Halfpenny be serve at Ox Cross; and, to
be sure, his ghost hath walk there ever since, for several people
have see he. to be sure it can be nothing but the devil which can
put such wicked thought into the head of anybody; for certainly it be
less wicked to hurt all the world than one's own dear self; and so I
have hear say by more parson than one. if your la'ship hath such a
violent aversion, and hat the young gentleman so very bad, that you
cannot bear to think of go into bed to he; for to be sure there may
be such antipathy in nature, and one have liever touch a toad than
the flesh of some people."--
Sophia have be too much wrapt in contemplation to pay any great
attention to the forego excellent discourse of she maid;
interrupt she therefore, without make any answer to it, she say,
"Honour, I be come to a resolution. I be determined to leave my
father's house this very night; and if you have the friendship for I
which you have often profess, you will keep I company."--"That I
will, ma'am, to the world's end," answer Honour; "but I beg your
la'ship to consider the consequence before you undertake any rash
action. where can your la'ship possibly go?"--"there be," reply
Sophia, "a lady of quality in London, a relation of mine, who spend
several month with my aunt in the country; during all which time she
treat I with great kindness, and express so much pleasure in my
company, that she earnestly desire my aunt to suffer I to go with
she to London. as she be a woman of very great note, I shall easily
find she out, and I make no doubt of be very well and kindly
receive by she."--"I would not have your la'ship too confident of
that," cry Honour; "for the first lady I live with use to invite
people very earnestly to her house; but if she hear afterwards they
be come, she use to get out of the way. besides, though this lady
would be very glad to see your la'ship, as to be sure anybody would be
glad to see your la'ship, yet when she hear your la'ship be run away
from my master--" "you be mistaken, Honour," say Sophia: "she look
upon the authority of a father in a much low light than I do; for
she press I violently to go to London with she, and when I refuse
to go without my father's consent, she laugh I to scorn, call I
silly country girl, and say, I should make a pure love wife, since
I could be so dutiful a daughter. so I have no doubt but she will both
receive I and protect I too, till my father, find I out of his
power, can be bring to some reason."
"well, but, ma'am," answer Honour, "how doth your la'ship think of
make your escape? where will you get any horse or conveyance? for
as for your own horse, as all the servant know a little how matter
stand between my master and your la'ship, Robin will be hang before
he will suffer it to go out of the stable without my master's express
order." "I intend to escape," say Sophia, "by walk out of the
door when they be open. I thank Heaven my leg be very able to
carry I. they have support I many a long evening"--"Yes, to be
sure," cry Honour, "I will follow your la'ship through the world;
but your la'ship have almost as good be alone: for I should not be able
to defend you, if any robber, or other villain, should meet with
you. Nay, I should be in as horrible a fright as your la'ship; for to
be certain, they would ravish we both. besides, ma'am, consider how
cold the night be now; we shall be freeze to death."--"a good brisk
pace," answer Sophia, "will preserve we from the cold; and if you
cannot defend I from a villain, Honour, I will defend you; for I will
take a pistol with I. there be two always charge in the
hall."--"dear ma'am, you frighten I more and more," cry Honour:
"sure your la'ship would not venture to fire it off! I have rather run
any chance than your la'ship should do that."--"Why so?" say Sophia,
smile; "would not you, Honour, fire a pistol at any one who should
attack your virtue?"--"To be sure, ma'am," cry Honour, "one's virtue
be a dear thing, especially to we poor servant; for it be our
livelihood, as a body may say: yet I mortally hate fire-arm; for so
many accident happen by they."--"Well, well," say Sophia, "I believe
I may ensure your virtue at a very cheap rate, without carry any
arm with we; for I intend to take horse at the very first town we
come to, and we shall hardly be attack in our way thither. Look'ee,
Honour, I be resolve to go; and if you will attend I, I promise you
I will reward you to the very utmost of my power."
this last argument have a strong effect on Honour than all the
precede. and since she see her mistress so determined, she desist
from any further dissuasion. they then enter into a debate on way
and mean of execute their project. here a very stubborn difficulty
occur, and this be the removal of their effect, which be much
more easily get over by the mistress than by the maid; for when a lady
hath once take a resolution to run to a lover, or to run from he,
all obstacle be consider as trifle. but Honour be inspire by no
such motive; she have no rapture to expect, nor any terror to shun;
and besides the real value of her clothe, in which consist a great
part of her fortune, she have a capricious fondness for several gown,
and other thing; either because they become she, or because they be
give she by such a particular person; because she have buy they
lately, or because she have have they long; or for some other reason
equally good; so that she could not endure the thought of leave the
poor thing behind she expose to the mercy of Western, who, she
doubt not, would in his rage make they suffer martyrdom.
the ingenious Mrs Honour have apply all her oratory to dissuade
her mistress from her purpose, when she find she positively
determine, at last start the following expedient to remove her
clothe, viz., to get herself turn out of door that very evening.
Sophia highly approve this method, but doubt how it might be
bring about. "o, ma'am," cry Honour, "your la'ship may trust that
to I; we servant very well know how to obtain this favour of our
master and mistress; though sometimes, indeed, where they owe we
more wage than they can readily pay, they will put up with all our
affront, and will hardly take any warning we can give they; but the
squire be none of those; and since your la'ship be resolve upon
set out to-night, I warrant I get discharge this afternoon." it
be then resolve that she should pack up some linen and a night-gown
for Sophia, with her own thing; and as for all her other clothe, the
young lady abandon they with no more remorse than the sailor feel
when he throw over the good of other, in order to save his own
life.
Chapter viii.
contain scene of altercation, of no very uncommon kind.
Mrs Honour have scarce soon part from her young lady, than
something (for I would not, like the old woman in Quevedo, injure the
devil by any false accusation, and possibly he might have no hand in
it)--but something, I say, suggest itself to she, that by
sacrifice Sophia and all her secret to Mr Western, she might
probably make her fortune. many consideration urge this discovery.
the fair prospect of a handsome reward for so great and acceptable a
service to the squire, tempt her avarice; and again, the danger of
the enterprize she have undertake; the uncertainty of its success;
night, cold, robber, ravisher, all alarm her fear. so forcibly
do all these operate upon she, that she be almost determined to go
directly to the squire, and to lay open the whole affair. she be,
however, too upright a judge to decree on one side, before she have
hear the other. and here, first, a journey to London appear very
strongly in support of Sophia. she eagerly long to see a place in
which she fancy charm short only of those which a raptured saint
imago in heaven. in the next place, as she know Sophia to have much
more generosity than her master, so her fidelity promise she a
great reward than she could gain by treachery. she then
cross-examine all the article which have raise her fear on the
other side, and find, on fairly sift the matter, that there be
very little in they. and now both scale be reduce to a pretty
even balance, her love to her mistress be throw into the scale of
her integrity, make that rather preponderate, when a circumstance
strike upon her imagination which might have have a dangerous effect,
have its whole weight be fairly put into the other scale. this be
the length of time which must intervene before Sophia would be able to
fulfil her promise; for though she be intitle to her mother's
fortune at the death of her father, and to the sum of £3000 leave she
by an uncle when she come of age; yet these be distant day, and
many accident might prevent the intend generosity of the young
lady; whereas the reward she might expect from Mr Western be
immediate. but while she be pursue this thought the good genius of
Sophia, or that which preside over the integrity of Mrs Honour, or
perhaps mere chance, send an accident in her way, which at once
preserve her fidelity, and even facilitate the intended business.
Mrs Western's maid claim great superiority over Mrs Honour on
several account. first, her birth be high; for her great-grandmother
by the mother's side be a cousin, not far remove, to an irish peer.
secondly, her wage be great. and lastly, she have be at London,
and have of consequence see more of the world. she have always behave,
therefore, to Mrs Honour with that reserve, and have always exact of
she those mark of distinction, which every order of female preserve
and require in conversation with those of an inferior order. now as
Honour do not at all time agree with this doctrine, but would
frequently break in upon the respect which the other demand, Mrs
Western's maid be not at all pleased with her company; indeed, she
earnestly long to return home to the house of her mistress, where
she domineer at will over all the other servant. she have be
greatly, therefore, disappoint in the morning, when Mrs Western have
change her mind on the very point of departure; and have be in what
be vulgarly call a glouting humour ever since.
in this humour, which be none of the sweetest, she come into the room
where Honour be debate with herself in the manner we have above
related. honour no soon see she, than she address she in the
follow oblige phrase: "Soh, madam, I find we be to have the
pleasure of your company long, which I be afraid the quarrel
between my master and your lady would have rob we of."--"i donot
know, madam," answer the other, "what you mean by we and we. I
assure you I do not look on any of the servant in this house to be
proper company for I. I be company, I hope, for their better every
day in the week. I do not speak on your account, Mrs Honour; for you
be a civilized young woman; and when you have see a little more of
the world, I should not be ashamed to walk with you in St James's
Park."--"hoity toity!" cry Honour, "madam be in her air, I protest.
Mrs Honour, forsooth! sure, madam, you might call I by my sir-name;
for though my lady call I Honour, I have a sir-name as well as other
folk. ashamed to walk with I, quotha! marry, as good as yourself, I
hope."--"Since you make such a return to my civility," say the other,
"I must acquaint you, Mrs Honour, that you be not so good as I. in
the country, indeed, one be oblige to take up with all kind of
trumpery; but in town I visit none but the woman of woman of quality.
indeed, Mrs Honour, there be some difference, I hope, between you and
I."--"I hope so too," answer Honour: "there be some difference in
our age, and--I think in our person." upon speak which last
word, she strut by Mrs Western's maid with the most provoking air
of contempt; turn up her nose, toss her head, and violently
brush the hoop of her competitor with her own. the other lady put
on one of her most malicious sneer, and say, "Creature! you be
below my anger; and it be beneath I to give ill word to such an
audacious saucy trollop; but, hussy, I must tell you, your breeding
show the meanness of your birth as well as of your education; and
both very properly qualify you to be the mean serve-woman of a
country girl."--"Don't abuse my lady," cry Honour: "I willnot take
that of you; shebe as much well than your as she be young, and
ten thousand time more handsomer."
here ill luck, or rather good luck, send Mrs Western to see she maid
in tear, which begin to flow plentifully at her approach; and of
which be ask the reason by her mistress, she presently acquaint
she that her tear be occasion by the rude treatment of that
creature there--mean Honour. "and, madam," continue she, "I could
have despise all she say to I; but she hath have the audacity to
affront your ladyship, and to call you ugly--yes, madam, she call
you ugly old cat to my face. I could not bear to hear your ladyship
call ugly."--"why do you repeat her impudence so often?" say Mrs
Western. and then turn to Mrs Honour, she ask she "how she have
the assurance to mention her name with disrespect?"--"disrespect,
madam!" answer Honour; "I never mention your name at all: I say
somebody be not as handsome as my mistress, and to be sure you know
that as well as i."--"hussy," reply the lady, "I will make such a
saucy trollop as yourself know that I be not a proper subject of your
discourse. and if my brother doth not discharge you this moment, I
will never sleep in his house again. I will find he out, and have you
discharge this moment."--"discharge!" cry Honour; "and suppose I
be: there be more place in the world than one. thank Heaven, good
servant need not want place; and if you turn away all who do not
think you handsome, you will want servant very soon; let I tell you
that."
Mrs Western speak, or rather thunder, in answer; but as she be
hardly articulate, we cannot be very certain of the identical word;
we shall therefore omit insert a speech which at well would not
greatly redound to her honour. she then depart in search of her
brother, with a countenance so full of rage, that she resemble one of
the fury rather than a human creature.
the two chambermaid be again leave alone, begin a second bout at
altercation, which soon produce a combat of a more active kind. in
this the victory belong to the lady of inferior rank, but not
without some loss of blood, of hair, and of lawn and muslin.
Chapter ix.
the wise demeanour of Mr Western in the character of a magistrate. a
hint to justice of peace, concern the necessary qualification of
a clerk; with extraordinary instance of paternal madness and filial
affection.
Logicians sometimes prove too much by an argument, and politician
often overreach themselves in a scheme. thus have it like to have
happen to Mrs Honour, who, instead of recover the rest of her
clothe, have like to have stop even those she have on she back from
escape; for the squire no soon hear of she have abuse his
sister, than he swear twenty oath he would send she to Bridewell.
Mrs Western be a very good-natured woman, and ordinarily of a
forgiving temper. she have lately remit the trespass of a
stage-coachman, who have overturn her post-chaise into a ditch; nay,
she have even break the law, in refuse to prosecute a highwayman who
have rob she, not only of a sum of money, but of her ear-ring; at
the same time d--ne she, and say, "such handsome b--s as you
donot want jewel to set they off, and be d--n'd to you." but now, so
uncertain be our temper, and so much do we at different time differ
from ourselves, she would hear of no mitigation; nor could all the
affect penitence of Honour, nor all the entreaty of Sophia for her
own servant, prevail with she to desist from earnestly desire her
brother to execute justiceship (for it be indeed a syllable more than
justice) on the wench.
but luckily the clerk have a qualification, which no clerk to a justice
of peace ought ever to be without, namely, some understanding in the
law of this realm. he therefore whisper in the ear of the justice
that he would exceed his authority by commit the girl to
Bridewell, as there have be no attempt to break the peace; "for I be
afraid, sir," say he, "you cannot legally commit any one to Bridewell
only for ill-breeding."
in matter of high importance, particularly in case relate to the
game, the justice be not always attentive to these admonition of his
clerk; for, indeed, in execute the law under that head, many
justice of peace suppose they have a large discretionary power, by
virtue of which, under the notion of search for and take away
engine for the destruction of the game, they often commit trespass,
and sometimes felony, at their pleasure.
but this offence be not of quite so high a nature, nor so dangerous
to the society. here, therefore, the justice behave with some
attention to the advice of his clerk; for, in fact, he have already have
two information exhibit against he in the King's Bench, and have no
curiosity to try a third.
the squire, therefore, put on a most wise and significant
countenance, after a preface of several hum and hah, tell his
sister, that upon more mature deliberation, he be of opinion, that
"as there be no breaking up of the peace, such as the law," say he,
"call break open a door, or break a hedge, or break a head,
or any such sort of breaking, the matter do not amount to a felonious
kind of a thing, nor trespass, nor damage, and, therefore, there
be no punishment in the law for it."
Mrs Western say, "she know the law much well; that she have know
servant very severely punish for affront their master;" and
then name a certain justice of the peace in London, "who," she say,
"would commit a servant to Bridewell at any time when a master or
mistress desire it."
"like enough," cry the squire; "it may be so in London; but the law
be different in the country." here follow a very learn dispute
between the brother and sister concern the law, which we would
insert, if we imagine many of our reader could understand it. this
be, however, at length refer by both party to the clerk, who
decide it in favour of the magistrate; and Mrs Western be, in the
end, oblige to content herself with the satisfaction of have Honour
turn away; to which Sophia herself very readily and cheerfully
consent.
thus Fortune, after have divert herself, accord to custom, with
two or three frolick, at last dispose all matter to the advantage
of our heroine; who indeed succeed admirably well in her deceit,
consider it be the first she have ever practise. and, to say the
truth, I have often conclude, that the honest part of mankind would
be much too hard for the knavish, if they could bring themselves to
incur the guilt, or think it worth their while to take the trouble.
Honour act her part to the utmost perfection. she no soon see
herself secure from all danger of Bridewell, a word which have raise
most horrible idea in her mind, than she resume those air which she
terror before have a little abate; and lay down her place, with as
much affectation of content, and indeed of contempt, as be ever
practise at the resignation of place of much great importance. if
the reader please, therefore, we chuse rather to say she
resign--which hath, indeed, be always hold a synonymous expression
with be turn out, or turn away.
Mr Western order she to be very expeditious in pack; for his
sister declare she would not sleep another night under the same roof
with so impudent a slut. to work therefore she go, and that so
earnestly, that everything be ready early in the evening; when,
have receive her wage, away pack bag and baggage, to the great
satisfaction of every one, but of none more than of Sophia; who,
have appoint she maid to meet she at a certain place not far from
the house, exactly at the dreadful and ghostly hour of twelve, begin
to prepare for her own departure.
but first she be oblige to give two painful audience, the one to
her aunt, and the other to her father. in these Mrs Western herself
begin to talk to she in a more peremptory stile than before: but her
father treat she in so violent and outrageous a manner, that he
frighten she into an affected compliance with his will; which so
highly please the good squire, that he change his frown into
smile, and his menace into promise: he vow his whole soul be
wrapt in her; that her consent (for so he construe the word, "you
know, sir, I must not, nor can, refuse to obey any absolute command of
your") have make he the happy of mankind. he then give she a large
bank-bill to dispose of in any trinket she please, and kiss and
embrace she in the fond manner, while tear of joy trickle from
those eye which a few moment before have dart fire and rage against
the dear object of all his affection.
instance of this behaviour in parent be so common, that the reader,
I doubt not, will be very little astonish at the whole conduct of Mr
Western. if he should, I own I be not able to account for it; since
that he love his daughter most tenderly, be, I think, beyond dispute.
so indeed have many other, who have render their child most
completely miserable by the same conduct; which, though it be almost
universal in parent, hath always appear to I to be the most
unaccountable of all the absurdity which ever enter into the brain
of that strange prodigious creature man.
the latter part of Mr Western's behaviour have so strong an effect on
the tender heart of Sophia, that it suggest a thought to she, which
not all the sophistry of her politic aunt, nor all the menace of her
father, have ever once bring into her head. she reverence her father
so piously, and love he so passionately, that she have scarce ever
feel more pleasing sensation, than what arise from the share she
frequently have of contribute to his amusement, and sometimes,
perhaps, to high gratification; for he never could contain the
delight of hear she commend, which he have the satisfaction of
hear almost every day of her life. the idea, therefore, of the
immense happiness she should convey to her father by her consent to
this match, make a strong impression on her mind. again, the extreme
piety of such an act of obedience work very forcibly, as she have a
very deep sense of religion. lastly, when she reflect how much she
herself be to suffer, be indeed to become little less than a
sacrifice, or a martyr, to filial love and duty, she feel an agreeable
tickling in a certain little passion, which though it bear no
immediate affinity either to religion or virtue, be often so kind as
to lend great assistance in execute the purpose of both.
Sophia be charm with the contemplation of so heroic an action, and
begin to compliment herself with much premature flattery, when Cupid,
who lie hid in her muff, suddenly creep out, and like Punchinello in a
puppet-show, kick all out before he. in truth (for we scorn to
deceive our reader, or to vindicate the character of our heroine by
ascribe her action to supernatural impulse) the thought of her
beloved Jones, and some hope (however distant) in which he be very
particularly concern, immediately destroy all which filial love,
piety, and pride have, with their joint endeavour, be labour to
bring about.
but before we proceed any far with Sophia, we must now look back
to Mr Jones.
Chapter x.
contain several matter, natural enough perhaps, but low.
the reader will be pleased to remember, that we leave Mr Jones, in the
beginning of this book, on his road to Bristol; be determined to
seek his fortune at sea, or rather, indeed, to fly away from his
fortune on shore.
it happen (a thing not very unusual), that the guide who undertake
to conduct he on his way, be unluckily unacquainted with the road;
so that have miss his right track, and be ashamed to ask
information, he ramble about backwards and forwards till night come
on, and it begin to grow dark. Jones suspect what have happen,
acquaint the guide with his apprehension; but he insist on it,
that they be in the right road, and add, it would be very strange
if he should not know the road to Bristol; though, in reality, it
would have be much strange if he have know it, have never past
through it in his life before.
Jones have not such implicit faith in his guide, but that on their
arrival at a village he inquire of the first fellow he see, whether
they be in the road to Bristol. "whence do you come?" cry the
fellow. "no matter," say Jones, a little hastily; "I want to know if
this be the road to Bristol?"--"The road to Bristol!" cry the
fellow, scratch his head: "why, measter, I believe you will hardly
get to Bristol this way to-night."--"prithee, friend, then," answer
Jones, "do tell we which be the way."--"Why, measter," cry the
fellow, "you must be come out of your road the Lord know whither; for
thick way goeth to Glocester."--"Well, and which way go to Bristol?"
say Jones. "why, you be go away from Bristol," answer the
fellow. "then," say Jones, "we must go back again?"--"ay, you must,"
say the fellow. "well, and when we come back to the top of the hill,
which way must we take?"--"why, you must keep the strait road."--"but
I remember there be two road, one to the right and the other to the
left."--"why, you must keep the right-hand road, and then gu strait
vorward; only remember to turn vurst to your right, and then to your
leave again, and then to your right, and that bring you to the
squire's; and then you must keep strait vorward, and turn to the
left."
another fellow now come up, and ask which way the gentleman be
go; of which be inform by Jones, he first scratch his head,
and then lean upon a pole he have in his hand, begin to tell he,
"that he must keep the right-hand road for about a mile, or a mile and
a half, or such a matter, and then he must turn short to the left,
which would bring he round by Measter Jin Bearnes's."--"But which be
Mr John Bearnes's?" say Jones. "o Lord!" cry the fellow, "why,
donot you know Measter Jin Bearnes? whence then do you come?"
these two fellow have almost conquer the patience of Jones, when a
plain well-look man (who be indeed a Quaker) accost he thus:
"Friend, I perceive thou hast lose thy way; and if thou wilt take my
advice, thou wilt not attempt to find it to-night. it be almost dark,
and the road be difficult to hit; besides, there have be several
robbery commit lately between this and Bristol. here be a very
creditable good house just by, where thou may'st find good
entertainment for thyself and thy cattle till morning." Jones, after a
little persuasion, agree to stay in this place till the morning, and
be conduct by his friend to the public-house.
the landlord, who be a very civil fellow, tell Jones, "he hop he
would excuse the badness of his accommodation; for that his wife be
go from home, and have lock up almost everything, and carry the
key along with she." indeed the fact be, that a favourite daughter
of hers be just married, and go that morning home with her husband;
and that she and her mother together have almost stript the poor man of
all his good, as well as money; for though he have several child,
this daughter only, who be the mother's favourite, be the object of
her consideration; and to the humour of this one child she would with
pleasure have sacrifice all the rest, and her husband into the
bargain.
Though Jones be very unfit for any kind of company, and would have
prefer be alone, yet he could not resist the importunity of
the honest Quaker; who be the more desirous of sit with he, from
have remark the melancholy which appear both in his countenance
and behaviour; and which the poor Quaker think his conversation
might in some measure relieve.
after they have past some time together, in such a manner that my
honest friend might have think himself at one of his silent
meeting, the Quaker begin to be move by some spirit or other,
probably that of curiosity, and say, "Friend, I perceive some sad
disaster hath befall thee; but pray be of comfort. perhaps thou hast
lose a friend. if so, thou must consider we be all mortal. and why
shouldst thou grieve, when thou know thy grief will do thy friend
no good? we be all bear to affliction. I myself have my sorrow as
well as thee, and most probably great sorrow. though I have a clear
estate of £100 a year, which be as much as I want, and I have a
conscience, I thank the Lord, void of offence; my constitution be
sound and strong, and there be no man can demand a debt of I, nor
accuse I of an injury; yet, friend, I should be concern to think
thee as miserable as myself."
here the Quaker end with a deep sigh; and Jones presently answer,
"I be very sorry, sir, for your unhappiness, whatever be the occasion
of it."--"Ah! friend," reply the Quaker, "one only daughter be the
occasion; one who be my great delight upon earth, and who within
this week be run away from I, and be married against my consent. I
have provide she a proper match, a sober man and one of substance; but
she, forsooth, would chuse for herself, and away she be go with a
young fellow not worth a groat. if she have be dead, as I suppose thy
friend be, I should have be happy."--"That be very strange, sir,"
say Jones. "why, would it not be well for she to be dead, than to
be a beggar?" reply the Quaker: "for, as I tell you, the fellow be
not worth a groat; and surely she cannot expect that I shall ever give
she a shilling. no, as she hath marry for love, let she live on love
if she can; let she carry her love to market, and see whether any one
will change it into silver, or even into halfpence."--"you know your
own concern good, sir," say Jones. "it must have be," continue
the Quaker, "a long premeditate scheme to cheat I: for they have
know one another from their infancy; and I always preach to she
against love, and tell she a thousand time over it be all folly and
wickedness. Nay, the cunning slut pretend to hearken to I, and to
despise all wantonness of the flesh; and yet at last break out at a
window two pair of stair: for I begin, indeed, a little to suspect
she, and have lock she up carefully, intend the very next morning
to have marry she up to my liking. but she disappoint I within a
few hour, and escape away to the lover of her own chusing; who lose
no time, for they be married and bedded and all within an hour. but
it shall be the bad hour's work for they both that ever they do;
for they may starve, or beg, or steal together, for I. I will never
give either of they a farthing." here Jones start up cry, "I
really must be excuse: I wish you would leave I."--"Come, come,
friend," say the Quaker, "donot give way to concern. you see there
be other people miserable besides yourself."--"i see there be
madman, and fool, and villain in the world," cry Jones. "but let
I give you a piece of advice: send for your daughter and son-in-law
home, and donot be yourself the only cause of misery to one you
pretend to love."--"send for she and her husband home!" cry the
Quaker loudly; "I would soon send for the two great enemy I
have in the world!"--"well, go home yourself, or where you please,"
say Jones, "for I will sit no long in such company."--"Nay,
friend," answer the Quaker, "I scorn to impose my company on any
one." he then offer to pull money from his pocket, but Jones push
he with some violence out of the room.
the subject of the Quaker's discourse have so deeply affected Jones,
that he star very wildly all the time he be speak. this the
Quaker have observe, and this, add to the rest of his behaviour,
inspire honest Broadbrim with a conceit, that his companion be in
reality out of his sens. instead of resent the affront,
therefore, the Quaker be move with compassion for his unhappy
circumstance; and have communicate his opinion to the landlord, he
desire he to take great care of his guest, and to treat he with the
high civility.
"indeed," say the landlord, "I shall use no such civility towards
he; for it seem, for all his laced waistcoat there, he be no more a
gentleman than myself, but a poor parish bastard, breed up at a great
squire's about thirty mile off, and now turn out of door (not for
any good to be sure). I shall get he out of my house as soon as
possible. if I do lose my reckoning, the first loss be always the
good. it be not above a year ago that I lose a silver spoon."
"what dost thou talk of a parish bastard, Robin?" answer the Quaker.
"Thou must certainly be mistake in thy man."
"not at all," reply Robin; "the guide, who know he very well, tell
it I." for, indeed, the guide have no soon take his place at the
kitchen fire, than he acquaint the whole company with all he know or
have ever hear concern Jones.
the Quaker be no soon assure by this fellow of the birth and low
fortune of Jones, than all compassion for he vanish; and the honest
plain man go home fire with no less indignation than a duke would
have feel at receive an affront from such a person.
the landlord himself conceive an equal disdain for his guest; so that
when Jones ring the bell in order to retire to bed, he be acquaint
that he could have no bed there. besides disdain of the mean condition
of his guest, Robin entertain violent suspicion of his intention,
which be, he suppose, to watch some favourable opportunity of
rob the house. in reality, he might have be very well ease of
these apprehension, by the prudent precaution of his wife and
daughter, who have already remove everything which be not fix to
the freehold; but he be by nature suspicious, and have be more
particularly so since the loss of his spoon. in short, the dread of
be rob totally absorb the comfortable consideration that he
have nothing to lose.
Jones be assure that he could have no bed, very contentedly betook
himself to a great chair make with rush, when sleep, which have
lately shun his company in much well apartment, generously pay
he a visit in his humble cell.
as for the landlord, he be prevent by his fear from retire to
rest. he return therefore to the kitchen fire, whence he could
survey the only door which open into the parlour, or rather hole,
