where Jones be seat; and as for the window to that room, it be
impossible for any creature large than a cat to have make his escape
through it.
Chapter xi.
the adventure of a company of soldier.
the landlord have take his seat directly opposite to the door of
the parlour, determined to keep guard there the whole night. the guide
and another fellow remain long on duty with he, though they neither
know his suspicion, nor have any of their own. the true cause of their
watching do, indeed, at length, put an end to it; for this be no
other than the strength and goodness of the beer, of which have
tipple a very large quantity, they grow at first very noisy and
vociferous, and afterwards fall both asleep.
but it be not in the power of liquor to compose the fear of Robin.
he continue still wake in his chair, with his eye fix stedfastly
on the door which lead into the apartment of Mr Jones, till a violent
thundering at his outward gate call he from his seat, and oblige
he to open it; which he have no soon do, than his kitchen be
immediately full of gentleman in red coat, who all rush upon he in
as tumultuous a manner as if they intend to take his little castle
by storm.
the landlord be now force from his post to furnish his numerous
guest with beer, which they call for with great eagerness; and upon
his second or third return from the cellar, he see Mr Jones stand
before the fire in the midst of the soldier; for it may easily be
believe, that the arrival of so much good company should put an end
to any sleep, unless that from which we be to be awaken only by the
last trumpet.
the company have now pretty well satisfied their thirst, nothing
remain but to pay the reckoning, a circumstance often productive of
much mischief and discontent among the inferior rank of gentry, who
be apt to find great difficulty in assess the sum, with exact
regard to distributive justice, which direct that every man shall pay
accord to the quantity which he drink. this difficulty occur
upon the present occasion; and it be the great, as some gentleman
have, in their extreme hurry, march off, after their first draught,
and have entirely forget to contribute anything towards the said
reckoning.
a violent dispute now arise, in which every word may be say to have
be depose upon oath; for the oath be at least equal to all the
other word speak. in this controversy the whole company speak
together, and every man seem wholly bent to extenuate the sum which
fall to his share; so that the most probable conclusion which could be
foresee be, that a large portion of the reckoning would fall to the
landlord's share to pay, or (what be much the same thing) would remain
unpaid.
all this while Mr Jones be engage in conversation with the serjeant;
for that officer be entirely unconcerned in the present dispute,
be privilege by immemorial custom from all contribution.
the dispute now grow so very warm that it seem to draw towards a
military decision, when Jones, step forward, silence all their
clamour at once, by declare that he would pay the whole reckoning,
which indeed amount to no more than three shilling and fourpence.
this declaration procure Jones the thank and applause of the whole
company. the term honourable, noble, and worthy gentleman, resound
through the room; nay, my landlord himself begin to have a well
opinion of he, and almost to disbelieve the account which the guide
have give.
the serjeant have inform Mr Jones that they be march against the
rebel, and expect to be command by the glorious Duke of
Cumberland. by which the reader may perceive (a circumstance which we
have not think necessary to communicate before) that this be the
very time when the late rebellion be at the high; and indeed the
bandit be now march into England, intend, as it be think,
to fight the king's force, and to attempt push forward to the
metropolis.
Jones have some heroic ingredient in his composition, and be a hearty
well-wisher to the glorious cause of liberty, and of the protestant
religion. it be no wonder, therefore, that in circumstance which
would have warrant a much more romantic and wild undertaking, it
should occur to he to serve as a volunteer in this expedition.
our command officer have say all in his power to encourage and
promote this good disposition, from the first moment he have be
acquaint with it. he now proclaim the noble resolution aloud,
which be receive with great pleasure by the whole company, who all
cry out, "God bless King George and your honour;" and then add,
with many oath, "we will stand by you both to the last drop of our
blood."
the gentleman who have be all night tipple at the alehouse, be
prevail on by some argument which a corporal have put into his
hand, to undertake the same expedition. and now the portmanteau
belong to Mr Jones be put up in the baggage-cart, the force
be about to move forwards; when the guide, step up to Jones,
say, "Sir, I hope you will consider that the horse have be keep
out all night, and we have travel a great way out of our way."
Jones be surprize at the impudence of this demand, and acquaint
the soldier with the merit of his cause, who be all unanimous in
condemn the guide for his endeavour to put upon a gentleman. some
say, he ought to be tie neck and heel; other that he deserve to
run the gantlope; and the serjeant shake his cane at he, and wish
he have he under his command, swear heartily he would make an
example of he.
Jones content himself however with a negative punishment, and walk
off with his new comrade, leave the guide to the poor revenge of
cursing and revile he; in which latter the landlord join, say,
"ay, ay, he be a pure one, I warrant you. a pretty gentleman, indeed,
to go for a soldier! he shall wear a laced wastecoat truly. it be an
old proverb and a true one, all be not gold that glister. I be glad
my house be well rid of he."
all that day the serjeant and the young soldier march together; and
the former, who be an arch fellow, tell the latter many entertaining
story of his campaign, though in reality he have never make any; for
he be but lately come into the service, and have, by his own
dexterity, so well ingratiate himself with his officer, that he have
promote himself to a halberd; chiefly indeed by his merit in
recruiting, in which he be most excellently well skilled.
much mirth and festivity pass among the soldier during their march.
in which the many occurrence that have pass at their last quarter
be remember, and every one, with great freedom, make what joke he
please on his officer, some of which be of the coarser kind, and
very near border on scandal. this bring to our heroe's mind the
custom which he have read of among the Greeks and Romans, of indulge,
on certain festival and solemn occasion, the liberty to slave, of
use an uncontrouled freedom of speech towards their master.
our little army, which consist of two company of foot, be now
arrive at the place where they be to halt that evening. the
serjeant then acquaint his lieutenant, who be the commanding
officer, that they have pick up two fellow in that day's march, one
of which, he say, be as fine a man as ever he see (mean the
tippler), for that he be near six foot, well proportion, and
strongly limbe; and the other (mean Jones) would do well enough
for the rear rank.
the new soldier be now produce before the officer, who have
examine the six-foot man, he be first produce, come next to
survey Jones: at the first sight of whom, the lieutenant could not
help show some surprize; for besides that he be very well dress,
and be naturally genteel, he have a remarkable air of dignity in his
look, which be rarely see among the vulgar, and be indeed not
inseparably annex to the feature of their superior.
"Sir," say the lieutenant, "my serjeant inform I that you be
desirous of enlist in the company I have at present under my
command; if so, sir, we shall very gladly receive a gentleman who
promise to do much honour to the company by bear arm in it."
Jones answer: "that he have not mention anything of enlist
himself; that he be most zealously attach to the glorious cause for
which they be go to fight, and be very desirous of serve as a
volunteer;" conclude with some compliment to the lieutenant, and
express the great satisfaction he should have in be under his
command.
the lieutenant return his civility, commend his resolution, shake
he by the hand, and invite he to dine with himself and the rest of
the officer.
Chapter xii.
the adventure of a company of officer.
the lieutenant, whom we mention in the precede chapter, and who
command this party, be now near sixty year of age. he have enter
very young into the army, and have serve in the capacity of an ensign
at the battle of tanniere; here he have receive two wound, and have
so well distinguish himself, that he be by the Duke of Marlborough
advance to be a lieutenant, immediately after that battle.
in this commission he have continue ever since, viz., near forty
year; during which time he have see vast number prefer over his
head, and have now the mortification to be command by boy, whose
father be at nurse when he first enter into the service.
nor be this ill success in his profession solely owe to his have
no friend among the man in power. he have the misfortune to incur the
displeasure of his colonel, who for many year continue in the
command of this regiment. nor do he owe the implacable ill-will which
this man bear he to any neglect or deficiency as an officer, nor
indeed to any fault in himself; but solely to the indiscretion of his
wife, who be a very beautiful woman, and who, though she be
remarkably fond of her husband, would not purchase his preferment at
the expense of certain favour which the colonel require of she.
the poor lieutenant be more peculiarly unhappy in this, that while he
feel the effect of the enmity of his colonel, he neither know, nor
suspect, that he really bear he any; for he could not suspect an
ill-will for which he be not conscious of give any cause; and his
wife, fear what her husband's nice regard to his honour might have
occasion, content herself with preserve her virtue without
enjoy the triumph of her conquest.
this unfortunate officer (for so I think he may be call) have many
good quality besides his merit in his profession; for he be a
religious, honest, good-natured man; and have behave so well in his
command, that he be highly esteemed and belove not only by the
soldier of his own company, but by the whole regiment.
the other officer who march with he be a french lieutenant, who
have be long enough out of France to forget his own language, but not
long enough in England to learn ours, so that he really speak no
language at all, and could barely make himself understand on the most
ordinary occasion. there be likewise two ensign, both very young
fellow; one of whom have be breed under an attorney, and the other
be son to the wife of a nobleman's butler.
as soon as dinner be end, Jones inform the company of the
merriment which have pass among the soldier upon their march; "and
yet," say he, "notwithstanding all their vociferation, I dare swear
they will behave more like Grecians than Trojans when they come to the
enemy."--"Grecians and Trojans!" say one of the ensign, "who the
devil be they? I have hear of all the troop in Europe, but never of
any such as these."
"donot pretend to more ignorance than you have, Mr Northerton," say
the worthy lieutenant. "I suppose you have hear of the Greeks and
Trojans, though perhaps you never read Pope's Homer; who, I remember,
now the gentleman mention it, compare the march of the Trojans to
the cackling of geese, and greatly commend the silence of the
Grecians. and upon my honour there be great justice in the cadet's
observation."
"Begar, I remember dem ver well," say the french lieutenant: "I ave
read they at school in dans Madam Daciere, des Greek, des Trojan, dey
fight for von woman--ouy, ouy, I ave read all dat."
"D--n Homo with all my heart," say Northerton; "I have the mark of
he on my a-- yet. there's Thomas, of our regiment, always carry a
Homo in his pocket; d--n I, if ever I come at it, if I donot burn it.
and therebe Corderius, another d--n'd son of a whore, that hath get I
many a flogging."
"then you have be at school, Mr Northerton?" say the lieutenant.
"ay, d--n I, have I," answer he; "the devil take my father for
send I thither! the old put want to make a parson of I, but
d--n I, think I to myself, Iwill nick you there, old cull; the devil
a smack of your nonsense shall you ever get into I. therebe Jemmy
Oliver, of our regiment, he narrowly escape be a pimp too, and
that would have be a thousand pity; for d--n I if he be not one
of the prettiest fellow in the whole world; but he go far than
I with the old cull, for Jimmey can neither write nor read."
"you give your friend a very good character," say the lieutenant,
"and a very deserved one, I dare say. but prithee, Northerton, leave
off that foolish as well as wicked custom of swearing; for you be
deceive, I promise you, if you think there be wit or politeness in
it. I wish, too, you would take my advice, and desist from abuse the
clergy. scandalous name, and reflection cast on any body of man,
must be always unjustifiable; but especially so, when throw on so
sacred a function; for to abuse the body be to abuse the function
itself; and I leave to you to judge how inconsistent such behaviour be
in man who be go to fight in defence of the protestant religion."
Mr Adderly, which be the name of the other ensign, have sit hitherto
kick his heel and hum a tune, without seem to listen to the
discourse; he now answer, "_O, Monsieur, on ne parle pas de la
religion dan la guerre_."--"Well say, Jack," cry Northerton: "if
_la religion_ be the only matter, the parson should fight their own
battle for I."
"I donot know, gentleman," say Jones, "what may be your opinion; but
I think no man can engage in a nobler cause than that of his religion;
and I have observe, in the little I have read of history, that no
soldier have fight so bravely as those who have be inspire with a
religious zeal: for my own part, though I love my king and country, I
hope, as well as any man in it, yet the protestant interest be no
small motive to my become a volunteer in the cause."
Northerton now wink on adderly, and whisper to he slily, "smoke
the prig, adderly, smoke he." then turn to Jones, say to he, "I
be very glad, sir, you have choose our regiment to be a volunteer in;
for if our parson should at any time take a cup too much, I find you
can supply his place. I presume, sir, you have be at the university;
may I crave the favour to know what college?"
"Sir," answer Jones, "so far from have be at the university, I
have even have the advantage of yourself, for I be never at school."
"I presume," cry the ensign, "only upon the information of your
great learning."--"Oh! sir," answer Jones, "it be as possible for a
man to know something without have be at school, as it be to have
be at school and to know nothing."
"well say, young volunteer," cry the lieutenant. "upon my word,
Northerton, you have well let he alone; for he will be too hard for
you."
Northerton do not very well relish the sarcasm of Jones; but he
think the provocation be scarce sufficient to justify a blow, or a
rascal, or scoundrel, which be the only repartee that suggest
themselves. he be, therefore, silent at present; but resolve to take
the first opportunity of return the jest by abuse.
it now come to the turn of Mr Jones to give a toast, as it be call;
who could not refrain from mention his dear Sophia. this he do the
more readily, as he imagine it utterly impossible that any one
present should guess the person he mean.
but the lieutenant, who be the toast-master, be not content with
Sophia only. he say, he must have her sir-name; upon which Jones
hesitate a little, and presently after name Miss Sophia Western.
Ensign Northerton declare he would not drink her health in the same
round with his own toast, unless somebody would vouch for she. "I know
one Sophy Western," say he, "that be lie with by half the young
fellow at Bath; and perhaps this be the same woman." Jones very
solemnly assure he of the contrary; assert that the young lady he
name be one of great fashion and fortune. "ay, ay," say the ensign,
"and so she be: d--n I, it be the same woman; and Iwill hold half a
dozen of Burgundy, Tom French of our regiment bring she into company
with we at any tavern in Bridges-street." he then proceed to
describe her person exactly (for he have see she with her aunt), and
conclude with say, "that her father have a great estate in
Somersetshire."
the tenderness of lover can ill brook the least jest with the
name of their mistress. however, Jones, though he have enough of the
lover and of the heroe too in his disposition, do not resent these
slander as hastily as, perhaps, he ought to have do. to say the
truth, have see but little of this kind of wit, he do not readily
understand it, and for a long time imagine Mr Northerton have really
mistaken his charmer for some other. but now, turn to the ensign
with a stern aspect, he say, "pray, sir, chuse some other subject for
your wit; for I promise you I will bear no jesting with this lady's
character." "jesting!" cry the other, "d--n I if ever I be more in
earnest in my life. Tom French of our regiment have both she and her
aunt at Bath." "then I must tell you in earnest," cry Jones, "that
you be one of the most impudent rascal upon earth."
he have no soon speak these word, than the ensign, together with a
volley of curse, discharge a bottle full at the head of Jones, which
hit he a little above the right temple, bring he instantly to
the ground.
the conqueror perceive the enemy to lie motionless before he, and
blood begin to flow pretty plentifully from his wound, begin now
to think of quit the field of battle, where no more honour be to
be get; but the lieutenant interpose, by step before the door,
and thus cut off his retreat.
Northerton be very importunate with the lieutenant for his liberty;
urge the ill consequence of his stay, ask he, what he could
have do less? "Zounds!" say he, "I be but in jest with the fellow.
I never hear any harm of Miss Western in my life." "have not you?"
say the lieutenant; "then you richly deserve to be hang, as well
for make such jest, as for use such a weapon: you be my
prisoner, sir; nor shall you stir from hence till a proper guard come
to secure you."
such an ascendant have our lieutenant over this ensign, that all that
fervency of courage which have level our poor heroe with the floor,
would scarce have animate the say ensign to have draw his sword
against the lieutenant, have he then have one dangle at his side: but
all the sword be hang up in the room, be, at the very beginning
of the fray, secure by the french officer. so that Mr Northerton be
oblige to attend the final issue of this affair.
the french gentleman and Mr Adderly, at the desire of their commanding
officer, have raise up the body of Jones, but as they could perceive
but little (if any) sign of life in he, they again let he fall,
adderly damn he for have blood his wastecoat; and the
Frenchman declare, "Begar, I no tush the Engliseman de mort: I
have hear de Englise ley, law, what you call, hang up de man dat tush
he last."
when the good lieutenant apply himself to the door, he apply
himself likewise to the bell; and the drawer immediately attend, he
dispatch he for a file of musqueteer and a surgeon. these
command, together with the drawer's report of what he have himself
see, not only produce the soldier, but presently draw up the
landlord of the house, his wife, and servant, and, indeed, every one
else who happen at that time to be in the inn.
to describe every particular, and to relate the whole conversation of
the ensue scene, be not within my power, unless I have forty pen,
and could, at once, write with they all together, as the company now
speak. the reader must, therefore, content himself with the most
remarkable incident, and perhaps he may very well excuse the rest.
the first thing do be secure the body of Northerton, who be
deliver into the custody of six man with a corporal at their head,
be by they conduct from a place which he be very willing to leave,
but it be unluckily to a place whither he be very unwilling to go.
to say the truth, so whimsical be the desire of ambition, the very
moment this youth have attain the above-mention honour, he would
have be well content to have retire to some corner of the world,
where the fame of it should never have reach his ear.
it surprize we, and so perhaps, it may the reader, that the
lieutenant, a worthy and good man, should have apply his chief care,
rather to secure the offender, than to preserve the life of the
wound person. we mention this observation, not with any view of
pretend to account for so odd a behaviour, but lest some critic
should hereafter plume himself on discover it. we would have these
gentleman know we can see what be odd in character as well as
themselves, but it be our business to relate fact as they be; which,
when we have do, it be the part of the learn and sagacious reader
to consult that original book of nature, whence every passage in our
work be transcribe, though we quote not always the particular page
for its authority.
the company which now arrive be of a different disposition. they
suspend their curiosity concern the person of the ensign, till
they should see he hereafter in a more engaging attitude. at present,
their whole concern and attention be employ about the bloody
object on the floor; which be place upright in a chair, soon begin
to discover some symptom of life and motion. these be no soon
perceive by the company (for Jones be at first generally conclude
to be dead) than they all fall at once to prescribe for he (for as
none of the physical order be present, every one there take that
office upon he).
Bleeding be the unanimous voice of the whole room; but unluckily
there be no operator at hand; every one then cry, "call the
barber;" but none stir a step. several cordial be likewise
prescribe in the same ineffective manner; till the landlord order
up a tankard of strong beer, with a toast, which he say be the good
cordial in England.
the person principally assistant on this occasion, indeed the only one
who do any service, or seem likely to do any, be the landlady: she
cut off some of her hair, and apply it to the wound to stop the
blood; she fall to chafe the youth's temple with her hand; and
have expr great contempt for her husband's prescription of beer,
she despatch one of her maid to her own closet for a bottle of
brandy, of which, as soon as it be bring, she prevail on Jones,
who be just return to his sens, to drink a very large and
plentiful draught.
soon afterwards arrive the surgeon, who have view the wound,
have shake his head, and blame everything which be do, order
his patient instantly to bed; in which place we think proper to leave
he some time to his repose, and shall here, therefore, put an end to
this chapter.
Chapter xiii.
contain the great address of the landlady, the great learning of a
surgeon, and the solid skill in casuistry of the worthy lieutenant.
when the wounded man be carry to his bed, and the house begin again
to clear up from the hurry which this accident have occasion, the
landlady thus address the command officer: "I be afraid, sir,"
say she, "this young man do not behave himself as well as he should
do to your honour; and if he have be kill, I suppose he have but
his desart: to be sure, when gentleman admit inferior parson into
their company, they oft to keep their distance; but, as my first
husband use to say, few of them know how to do it. for my own part, I
be sure I should not have suffer any fellow to _include_ themselves
into gentleman's company; but I thoft he have be an officer himself,
till the serjeant tell I he be but a recruit."
"Landlady," answer the lieutenant, "you mistake the whole matter.
the young man behave himself extremely well, and be, I believe, a
much well gentleman than the ensign who abuse he. if the young
fellow dy, the man who strike he will have most reason to be sorry
for it: for the regiment will get rid of a very troublesome fellow,
who be a scandal to the army; and if he escape from the hand of
justice, blame I, madam, thatbe all."
"ay! ay! good lack-a-day!" say the landlady; "who could have thoft
it? ay, ay, ay, I be satisfied your honour will see justice do; and
to be sure it oft to be to every one. gentleman oft not to kill poor
folk without answer for it. a poor man hath a soul to be save, as
well as his better."
"indeed, madam," say the lieutenant, "you do the volunteer wrong: I
dare swear he be more of a gentleman than the officer."
"ay!" cry the landlady; "why, look you there, now: well, my first
husband be a wise man; he use to say, you cannot always know the
inside by the outside. Nay, that might have be well enough too; for
I never _saw'd_ he till he be all over blood. who would have thoft
it? mayhap, some young gentleman cross in love. good lack-a-day, if
he should die, what a concern it will be to his parent! why, sure the
devil must possess the wicked wretch to do such an act. to be sure, he
be a scandal to the army, as your honour say; for most of the
gentleman of the army that ever I see, be quite different sort of
people, and look as if they would scorn to spill any christian blood
as much as any man: I mean, that is, in a civil way, as my first
husband use to say. to be sure, when they come into the war, there
must be bloodshe: but that they be not to be blame for. the more of
our enemy they kill there, the well: and I wish, with all my
heart, they could kill every mother's son of they."
"o fie, madam!" say the lieutenant, smile; "_all_ be rather too
bloody-minded a wish."
"not at all, sir," answer she; "I be not at all bloody-minded, only
to our enemy; and there be no harm in that. to be sure it be natural
for we to wish our enemy dead, that the war may be at an end, and
our taxis be lower; for it be a dreadful thing to pay as we do. why
now, there be above forty shilling for window-light, and yet we have
stopt up all we could; we have almost blind the house, I be sure.
say I to the exciseman, say I, I think you oft to favour we; I be
sure we be very good friend to the government: and so we be for
sartain, for we pay a mint of money to 'um. and yet I often think to
myself the government doth not imagine itself more obliged to we, than
to those that donot pay 'um a farthing. ay, ay, it be the way of the
world."
she be proceed in this manner when the surgeon enter the room.
the lieutenant immediately ask how his patient do. but he resolve
he only by say, "well, I believe, than he would have be by
this time, if I have not be call; and even as it be, perhaps it
would have be lucky if I could have be call soon."--"I hope,
sir," say the lieutenant, "the skull be not fractured."--"Hum," cry
the surgeon: "fracture be not always the most dangerous symptom.
contusion and laceration be often attend with bad phaenomena,
and with more fatal consequence, than fracture. People who know
nothing of the matter conclude, if the skull be not fractured, all be
well; whereas, I have rather see a man's skull break all to piece,
than some contusion I have meet with."--"I hope," say the lieutenant,
"there be no such symptom here."--"symptom," answer the surgeon,
"be not always regular nor constant. I have know very unfavourable
symptom in the morning change to favourable one at noon, and return
to unfavourable again at night. of wound, indeed, it be rightly and
truly say, _Nemo repente fuit turpissimus_. I be once, I remember,
call to a patient who have receive a violent contusion in his tibia,
by which the exterior cutis be lacerate, so that there be a profuse
sanguinary discharge; and the interior membrane be so divellicated,
that the os or bone very plainly appear through the aperture of the
vulnus or wound. some febrile symptom intervene at the same time
(for the pulse be exuberant and indicate much phlebotomy), I
apprehend an immediate mortification. to prevent which, I presently
make a large orifice in the vein of the left arm, whence I draw twenty
ounce of blood; which I expect to have find extremely sizy and
glutinous, or indeed coagulate, as it be in pleuretic complaint;
but, to my surprize, it appear rosy and florid, and its consistency
differ little from the blood of those in perfect health. I then
apply a fomentation to the part, which highly answer the
intention; and after three or four time dress, the wound begin to
discharge a thick pus or matter, by which mean the cohesion--but
perhaps I do not make myself perfectly well understood?"--"No,
really," answer the lieutenant, "I cannot say I understand a
syllable."--"Well, sir," say the surgeon, "then I shall not tire your
patience; in short, within six week my patient be able to walk upon
his leg as perfectly as he could have do before he receive the
contusion."--"I wish, sir," say the lieutenant, "you would be so kind
only to inform I, whether the wound this young gentleman hath have the
misfortune to receive, be likely to prove mortal."--"sir," answer
the surgeon, "to say whether a wound will prove mortal or not at first
dressing, would be very weak and foolish presumption: we be all
mortal, and symptom often occur in a cure which the great of our
profession could never foresee."--"But do you think he in danger?"
say the other.--"in danger! ay, surely," cry the doctor: "who be
there among we, who, in the most perfect health, can be say not to be
in danger? can a man, therefore, with so bad a wound as this be say
to be out of danger? all I can say at present be, that it be well I
be call as I be, and perhaps it would have be well if I have
be call soon. I will see he again early in the morning; and in
the meantime let he be keep extremely quiet, and drink liberally of
water-gruel."--"Won't you allow he sack-whey?" say the
landlady.--"Ay, ay, sack-whey," cry the doctor, "if you will,
provide it be very small."--"and a little chicken broth too?" add
she.--"Yes, yes, chicken broth," say the doctor, "be very
good."--"Mayn't I make he some jelly too?" say the landlady.--"Ay,
ay," answer the doctor, "jelly be very good for wound, for they
promote cohesion." and indeed it be lucky she have not name soup or
high sauce, for the doctor would have comply, rather than have lose
the custom of the house.
the doctor be no soon go, than the landlady begin to trumpet
forth his fame to the lieutenant, who have not, from their short
acquaintance, conceive quite so favourable an opinion of his physical
ability as the good woman, and all the neighbourhood, entertain
(and perhaps very rightly); for though I be afraid the doctor be a
little of a coxcomb, he might be nevertheless very much of a surgeon.
the lieutenant have collect from the learn discourse of the
surgeon that Mr Jones be in great danger, give order for keep Mr
Northerton under a very strict guard, design in the morning to
attend he to a justice of peace, and to commit the conducting the
troop to Gloucester to the french lieutenant, who, though he could
neither read, write, nor speak any language, be, however, a good
officer.
in the evening, our commander send a message to Mr Jones, that if a
visit would not be troublesome, he would wait on he. this civility
be very kindly and thankfully receive by Jones, and the lieutenant
accordingly go up to his room, where he find the wounded man much
well than he expect; nay, Jones assure his friend, that if he have
not receive express order to the contrary from the surgeon, he
should have get up long ago; for he appear to himself to be as well
as ever, and feel no other inconvenience from his wound but an extreme
soreness on that side of his head.
"I should be very glad," quoth the lieutenant, "if you be as well as
you fancy yourself, for then you could be able to do yourself justice
immediately; for when a matter cannot be make up, as in case of a blow,
the soon you take he out the well; but I be afraid you think
yourself well than you be, and he would have too much advantage
over you."
"Iwill try, however," answer Jones, "if you please, and will be so
kind to lend I a sword, for I have none here of my own."
"my sword be heartily at your service, my dear boy," cry the
lieutenant, kiss he; "you be a brave lad, and I love your spirit;
but I fear your strength; for such a blow, and so much loss of blood,
must have very much weaken you; and though you feel no want of
strength in your bed, yet you most probably would after a thrust or
two. I cannot consent to your take he out tonight; but I hope you
will be able to come up with we before we get many day' march
advance; and I give you my honour you shall have satisfaction, or the
man who hath injure you shallnot stay in our regiment."
"I wish," say Jones, "it be possible to decide this matter to-night:
now you have mention it to I, I shall not be able to rest."
"oh, never think of it," return the other: "a few day will make no
difference. the wound of honour be not like those in your body: they
suffer nothing by the delay of cure. it will be altogether as well for
you to receive satisfaction a week hence as now."
"but suppose," say Jones, "I should grow bad, and die of the
consequence of my present wound?"
"then your honour," answer the lieutenant, "will require no
reparation at all. I myself will do justice to your character, and
testify to the world your intention to have act properly, if you have
recover."
"still," reply Jones, "I be concern at the delay. I be almost
afraid to mention it to you who be a soldier; but though I have be
a very wild young fellow, still in my most serious moment, and at the
bottom, I be really a Christian."
"so be I too, I assure you," say the officer; "and so zealous a one,
that I be pleased with you at dinner for take up the cause of your
religion; and I be a little offended with you now, young gentleman,
that you should express a fear of declare your faith before any
one."
"but how terrible must it be," cry Jones, "to any one who be really
a Christian, to cherish malice in his breast, in opposition to the
command of he who hath expressly forbid it? how can I bear to do this
on a sick-bed? or how shall I make up my account, with such an article
as this in my bosom against I?"
"why, I believe there be such a command," cry the lieutenant; "but a
man of honour cannot keep it. and you must be a man of honour, if you
will be in the army. I remember I once put the case to our chaplain
over a bowl of punch, and he confess there be much difficulty in
it; but he say, he hop there might be a latitude grant to
soldier in this one instance; and to be sure it be our duty to hope
so; for who would bear to live without his honour? no, no, my dear
boy, be a good Christian as long as you live; but be a man of honour
too, and never put up an affront; not all the book, nor all the
parson in the world, shall ever persuade I to that. I love my
religion very well, but I love my honour more. there must be some
mistake in the wording the text, or in the translation, or in the
understanding it, or somewhere or other. but however that be, a man
must run the risque, for he must preserve his honour. so compose
yourself to-night, and I promise you you shall have an opportunity of
do yourself justice." here he give Jones a hearty buss, shake he
by the hand, and take his leave.
but though the lieutenant's reasoning be very satisfactory to
himself, it be not entirely so to his friend. Jones therefore, have
revolve this matter much in his thought, at last come to a
resolution, which the reader will find in the next chapter.
Chapter xiv.
a most dreadful chapter indeed; and which few reader ought to venture
upon in an evening, especially when alone.
Jones swallow a large mess of chicken, or rather cock, broth, with a
very good appetite, as indeed he would have do the cock it be make
of, with a pound of bacon into the bargain; and now, find in
himself no deficiency of either health or spirit, he resolve to get
up and seek his enemy.
but first he send for the serjeant, who be his first acquaintance
among these military gentleman. unluckily that worthy officer have,
in a literal sense, take his fill of liquor, have be some time
retire to his bolster, where he be snore so loud that it be not
easy to convey a noise in at his ear capable of drown that which
issue from his nostril.
however, as Jones persist in his desire of see he, a vociferous
drawer at length find mean to disturb his slumber, and to acquaint
he with the message. of which the serjeant be no soon make
sensible, than he arise from his bed, and have his clothe already
on, immediately attend. Jones do not think fit to acquaint the
serjeant with his design; though he might have do it with great
safety, for the halberdier be himself a man of honour, and have kill
his man. he would therefore have faithfully keep this secret, or
indeed any other which no reward be publish for discover. but as
Jones know not those virtue in so short an acquaintance, his caution
be perhaps prudent and commendable enough.
he begin therefore by acquaint the serjeant, that as he be now
enter into the army, he be ashamed of be without what be
perhaps the most necessary implement of a soldier; namely, a sword;
add, that he should be infinitely oblige to he, if he could
procure one. "for which," say he, "I will give you any reasonable
price; nor do I insist upon its be silver-hilte; only a good
blade, and such as may become a soldier's thigh."
the serjeant, who well know what have happen, and have hear that
Jones be in a very dangerous condition, immediately conclude, from
such a message, at such a time of night, and from a man in such a
situation, that he be light-headed. now as he have his wit (to use
that word in its common signification) always ready, he bethink
himself of make his advantage of this humour in the sick man. "Sir,"
say he, "I believe I can fit you. I have a most excellent piece of
stuff by I. it be not indeed silver-hilte, which, as you say, doth
not become a soldier; but the handle be decent enough, and the blade
one of the good in Europe. it be a blade that--a blade that--in short,
I will fetch it you this instant, and you shall see it and handle it.
I be glad to see your honour so well with all my heart."
be instantly return with the sword, he deliver it to Jones, who
take it and draw it; and then tell the serjeant it would do very well,
and bid he name his price.
the serjeant now begin to harangue in praise of his good. he say
(nay he swear very heartily), "that the blade be take from a french
officer, of very high rank, at the battle of Dettingen. I take it
myself," say he, "from his side, after I have knock he o' the head.
the hilt be a golden one. that I sell to one of our fine gentleman;
for there be some of they, an't please your honour, who value the
hilt of a sword more than the blade."
here the other stop he, and beg he to name a price. the
serjeant, who think Jones absolutely out of his sens, and very
near his end, be afraid lest he should injure his family by ask
too little. however, after a moment's hesitation, he content himself
with name twenty guineas, and swear he would not sell it for less to
his own brother.
"twenty guineas!" say Jones, in the utmost surprize: "sure you think
I be mad, or that I never see a sword in my life. twenty guineas,
indeed! I do not imagine you would endeavour to impose upon I. here,
take the sword--no, now I think on't, I will keep it myself, and show
it your officer in the morning, acquaint he, at the same time,
what a price you ask I for it."
the serjeant, as we have say, have always his wit (_in sensu
praedicto_) about he, and now plainly see that Jones be not in the
condition he have apprehend he to be; he now, therefore,
counterfeit as great surprize as the other have show, and say, "I
be certain, sir, I have not ask you so much out of the way. besides,
you be to consider, it be the only sword I have, and I must run the
risque of my officer's displeasure, by go without one myself. and
truly, put all this together, I donot think twenty shilling be
so much out of the way."
"twenty shilling!" cry Jones; "why, you just now ask I twenty
guineas."--"How!" cry the serjeant, "sure your honour must have
mistake I: or else I mistake myself--and indeed I be but half awake.
twenty guineas, indeed! no wonder your honour fly into such a
passion. I say twenty guineas too. no, no, I mean twenty shilling, I
assure you. and when your honour come to consider everything, I hope
you will not think that so extravagant a price. it be indeed true, you
may buy a weapon which look as well for less money. but----"
here Jones interrupt he, say, "I will be so far from make any
word with you, that I will give you a shilling more than your
demand." he then give he a guinea, bid he return to his bed, and
wish he a good march; add, he hop to overtake they before the
division reach Worcester.
the serjeant very civilly take his leave, fully satisfied with his
merchandize, and not a little pleased with his dexterous recovery from
that false step into which his opinion of the sick man's
light-headedness have betray he.
as soon as the serjeant be depart, Jones rise from his bed, and
dress himself entirely, put on even his coat, which, as its
colour be white, show very visibly the stream of blood which have
flow down it; and now, have grasp his new-purchase sword in his
hand, he be go to issue forth, when the thought of what he be
about to undertake lay suddenly hold of he, and he begin to reflect
that in a few minute he might possibly deprive a human being of life,
or might lose his own. "very well," say he, "and in what cause do I
venture my life? why, in that of my honour. and who be this human
be? a rascal who hath injure and insult I without provocation.
but be not revenge forbid by Heaven? yes, but it be enjoin by the
world. well, but shall I obey the world in opposition to the express
command of Heaven? shall I incur the divine displeasure rather than
be call--ha--coward--scoundrel?--i'll think no more; I be resolve,
and must fight he."
the clock have now strike twelve, and every one in the house be in
their bed, except the centinel who stand to guard Northerton, when
Jones softly open his door, issue forth in pursuit of his enemy,
of whose place of confinement he have receive a perfect description
from the drawer. it be not easy to conceive a much more tremendous
figure than he now exhibit. he have on, as we have say, a
light-colour coat, cover with stream of blood. his face, which
miss that very blood, as well as twenty ounce more draw from he
by the surgeon, be pallid. round his head be a quantity of bandage,
not unlike a turban. in the right hand he carry a sword, and in the
leave a candle. so that the bloody Banquo be not worthy to be compare
to he. in fact, I believe a more dreadful apparition be never raise
in a church-yard, nor in the imagination of any good people meet in a
winter evening over a Christmas fire in Somersetshire.
when the centinel first see our heroe approach, his hair begin gently
to lift up his grenadi cap; and in the same instant his knee fall
to blow with each other. presently his whole body be seize with
bad than an ague fit. he then fire his piece, and fall flat on his
face.
whether fear or courage be the occasion of his firing, or whether he
take aim at the object of his terror, I cannot say. if he do,
however, he have the good fortune to miss his man.
Jones see the fellow fall, guess the cause of his fright, at
which he could not forbear smile, not in the least reflect on the
danger from which he have just escape. he then pass by the fellow,
who still continue in the posture in which he fall, and enter the
room where Northerton, as he have hear, be confine. here, in a
solitary situation, he find--an empty quart pot stand on the
table, on which some beer be spill, it look as if the room have
lately be inhabit; but at present it be entirely vacant.
Jones then apprehend it might lead to some other apartment; but upon
search all round it, he could perceive no other door than that at
which he enter, and where the centinel have be post. he then
proceed to call Northerton several time by his name; but no one
answer; nor do this serve to any other purpose than to confirm the
centinel in his terror, who be now convince that the volunteer be
dead of his wound, and that his ghost be come in search of the
murderer: he now lie in all the agony of horror; and I wish, with
all my heart, some of those actor who be hereafter to represent a
man fright out of his wit have see he, that they might be teach
to copy nature, instead of perform several antic trick and
gesture, for the entertainment and applause of the gallery.
perceive the bird be fly, at least despair to find he, and
rightly apprehend that the report of the firelock would alarm the
whole house, our heroe now blow out his candle, and gently steal back
again to his chamber, and to his bed; whither he would not have be
able to have get undiscovered, have any other person be on the
same staircase, save only one gentleman who be confine to his bed by
the gout; for before he could reach the door to his chamber, the hall
where the centinel have be post be half full of people, some in
their shirt, and other not half drest, all very earnestly enquiring
of each other what be the matter.
the soldier be now find lie in the same place and posture in which
we just now leave he. several immediately apply themselves to raise
he, and some conclude he dead; but they presently see their
mistake, for he not only struggle with those who lay their hand on
he, but fall a roaring like a bull. in reality, he imagine so many
spirit or devil be handle he; for his imagination be
possess with the horror of an apparition, convert every object he
see or feel into nothing but ghost and spectre.
at length he be overpower by number, and get upon his leg; when
candle be bring, and see two or three of his comrade
present, he come a little to himself; but when they ask he what be
the matter? he answer, "I be a dead man, thatbe all, I be a dead
man, I cannot recover it, I have see he." "what hast thou see,
Jack?" say one of the soldier. "why, I have see the young volunteer
that be kill yesterday." he then imprecate the most heavy curse
on himself, if he have not see the volunteer, all over blood, vomit
fire out of his mouth and nostril, pass by he into the chamber where
Ensign Northerton be, and then seize the ensign by the throat, fly
away with he in a clap of thunder.
this relation meet with a gracious reception from the audience. all the
woman present believe it firmly, and pray Heaven to defend they
from murder. amongst the man too, many have faith in the story; but
other turn it into derision and ridicule; and a serjeant who be
present answer very coolly, "young man, you will hear more of this,
for go to sleep and dream on your post."
the soldier reply, "you may punish I if you please; but I be as
broad awake as I be now; and the devil carry I away, as he hath the
ensign, if I do not see the dead man, as I tell you, with eye as big
and as fiery as two large flambeaux."
the commander of the force, and the commander of the house, be now
both arrive; for the former be awake at the time, and hear the
centinel fire his piece, think it his duty to rise immediately,
though he have no great apprehension of any mischief; whereas the
apprehension of the latter be much great, lest her spoon and
tankard should be upon the march, without have receive any such
order from she.
our poor centinel, to whom the sight of this officer be not much more
welcome than the apparition, as he think it, which he have see
before, again relate the dreadful story, and with many addition of
blood and fire; but he have the misfortune to gain no credit with
either of the last-mention person: for the officer, though a very
religious man, be free from all terror of this kind; besides, have
so lately leave Jones in the condition we have see, he have no
suspicion of his be dead. as for the landlady, though not over
religious, she have no kind of aversion to the doctrine of spirit; but
there be a circumstance in the tale which she well know to be false,
as we shall inform the reader presently.
but whether Northerton be carry away in thunder or fire, or in
whatever other manner he be go, it be now certain that his body
be no long in custody. upon this occasion the lieutenant form a
conclusion not very different from what the serjeant be just mention
to have make before, and immediately order the centinel to be take
prisoner. so that, by a strange reverse of fortune (though not very
uncommon in a military life), the guard become the guard.
Chapter xv.
the conclusion of the foregoing adventure.
besides the suspicion of sleep, the lieutenant harbour another and
bad doubt against the poor centinel, and this be, that of
treachery; for as he believe not one syllable of the apparition, so
he imagine the whole to be an invention form only to impose upon
he, and that the fellow have in reality be bribe by Northerton to
let he escape. and this he imagine the rather, as the fright
appear to he the more unnatural in one who have the character of as
brave and bold a man as any in the regiment, have be in several
action, have receive several wound, and, in a word, have
behave himself always like a good and valiant soldier.
that the reader, therefore, may not conceive the least ill opinion of
such a person, we shall not delay a moment in rescue his character
from the imputation of this guilt.
Mr Northerton then, as we have before observe, be fully satisfied
with the glory which he have obtain from this action. he have perhaps
see, or hear, or guess, that envy be apt to attend fame. not that
I would here insinuate that he be heathenishly inclined to believe in
or to worship the goddess Nemesis; for, in fact, I be convinced he
never hear of her name. he be, besides, of an active disposition,
and have a great antipathy to those close quarter in the castle of
Gloucester, for which a justice of peace might possibly give he a
billet. nor be he moreover free from some uneasy meditation on a
certain wooden edifice, which I forbear to name, in conformity to the
opinion of mankind, who, I think, rather ought to honour than to be
ashamed of this building, as it be, or at least might be make, of more
benefit to society than almost any other public erection. in a word,
to hint at no more reason for his conduct, Mr Northerton be desirous
of depart that evening, and nothing remain for he but to
contrive the quomodo, which appear to be a matter of some
difficulty.
now this young gentleman, though somewhat crooked in his moral, be
perfectly straight in his person, which be extremely strong and well
make. his face too be account handsome by the generality of woman,
for it be broad and ruddy, with tolerably good tooth. such charm do
not fail make an impression on my landlady, who have no little relish
for this kind of beauty. she have, indeed, a real compassion for the
young man; and hear from the surgeon that affair be like to go
ill with the volunteer, she suspect they might hereafter wear no
benign aspect with the ensign. have obtain, therefore, leave to
make he a visit, and find he in a very melancholy mood, which she
considerably heighten by tell he there be scarce any hope of
the volunteer's life, she proceed to throw forth some hint, which
the other readily and eagerly take up, they soon come to a right
understanding; and it be at length agree that the ensign should, at
a certain signal, ascend the chimney, which communicate very soon
with that of the kitchen, he might there again let himself down; for
which she would give he an opportunity by keep the coast clear.
but lest our reader, of a different complexion, should take this
occasion of too hastily condemn all compassion as a folly, and
pernicious to society, we think proper to mention another particular
which might possibly have some little share in this action. the ensign
happen to be at this time possess of the sum of fifty pound,
which do indeed belong to the whole company; for the captain have
quarrel with his lieutenant, have entrust the payment of his
company to the ensign. this money, however, he think proper to
deposit in my landlady's hand, possibly by way of bail or security
that he would hereafter appear and answer to the charge against he;
but whatever be the condition, certain it be, that she have the
money and the ensign his liberty.
the reader may perhaps expect, from the compassionate temper of this
good woman, that when she see the poor centinel take prisoner for a
fact of which she know he innocent, she should immediately have
interpose in his behalf; but whether it be that she have already
exhaust all her compassion in the above-mention instance, or that
the feature of this fellow, though not very different from those of
the ensign, could not raise it, I will not determine; but, far from
be an advocate for the present prisoner, she urge his guilt to his
officer, declare, with uplifted eye and hand, that she would not
have have any concern in the escape of a murderer for all the world.
everything be now once more quiet, and most of the company return
again to their bed; but the landlady, either from the natural
activity of her disposition, or from her fear for her plate, have no
propensity to sleep, prevail with the officer, as they be to
march within little more than an hour, to spend that time with she
over a bowl of punch.
Jones have lie awake all this while, and have hear great part of the
hurry and bustle that have pass, of which he have now some curiosity
to know the particular. he therefore apply to his bell, which he
ring at least twenty time without any effect: for my landlady be in
such high mirth with her company, that no clapper could be hear there
but her own; and the drawer and chambermaid, who be sit together
in the kitchen (for neither durst he sit up nor she lie in bed alone),
the more they hear the bell ring the more they be frightened, and
as it be nail down in their place.
at last, at a lucky interval of chat, the sound reach the ear of
our good landlady, who presently send forth her summon, which both
her servant instantly obey. "Joe," say the mistress, "donot you
hear the gentleman's bell ring? why donot you go up?"--"It be not my
business," answer the drawer, "to wait upon the chamber--it be
Betty Chambermaid's."--"If you come to that," answer the maid, "it
be not my business to wait upon gentleman. I have do it indeed
sometimes; but the devil fetch I if ever I do again, since you make
your preamble about it." the bell still ring violently, their
mistress fall into a passion, and swore, if the drawer do not go up
immediately, she would turn he away that very morning. "if you do,
madam," say he, "I cannot help it. I willnot do another servant's
business." she then apply herself to the maid, and endeavour to
prevail by gentle mean; but all in vain: Betty be as inflexible as
Joe. both insist it be not their business, and they would not do
it.
the lieutenant then fall a laughing, and say, "come, I will put an
end to this contention;" and then turn to the servant, commend
they for their resolution in not give up the point; but add, he
be sure, if one would consent to go the other would. to which
proposal they both agree in an instant, and accordingly go up very
lovingly and close together. when they be go, the lieutenant
appease the wrath of the landlady, by satisfy she why they be
both so unwilling to go alone.
they return soon after, and acquaint their mistress, that the sick
gentleman be so far from be dead, that he speak as heartily as if
he be well; and that he give his service to the captain, and should
be very glad of the favour of see he before he march.
the good lieutenant immediately comply with his desire, and sit
down by his bed-side, acquaint he with the scene which have happen
below, conclude with his intention to make an example of the
centinel.
upon this Jones relate to he the whole truth, and earnestly beg
he not to punish the poor soldier, "who, I be confident," say he,
"be as innocent of the ensign's escape, as he be of forge any lie,
or of endeavour to impose on you."
the lieutenant hesitate a few moment, and then answer: "why, as
you have clear the fellow of one part of the charge, so it will be
impossible to prove the other, because he be not the only centinel.
but I have a good mind to punish the rascal for be a coward. yet
who know what effect the terror of such an apprehension may have?
and, to say the truth, he hath always behave well against an enemy.
come, it be a good thing to see any sign of religion in these fellow;
so I promise you he shall be set at liberty when we march. but hark,
the general beat. my dear boy, give I another buss. donot discompose
nor hurry yourself; but remember the christian doctrine of patience,
and I warrant you will soon be able to do yourself justice, and to
take an honourable revenge on the fellow who hath injure you." the
lieutenant then depart, and Jones endeavour to compose himself to
rest.
BOOK VIII.
contain about two day.
Chapter i.
a wonderful long chapter concern the marvellous; be much the
long of all our introductory chapter.
as we be now enter upon a book in which the course of our history
will oblige we to relate some matter of a more strange and surprize
kind than any which have hitherto occur, it may not be amiss, in
the prolegomenous or introductory chapter, to say something of that
specie of writing which be call the marvellous. to this we shall,
as well for the sake of ourselves as of other, endeavour to set some
certain bound, and indeed nothing can be more necessary, as
critic[*] of different complexion be here apt to run into very
different extreme; for while some be, with M. Dacier, ready to
allow, that the same thing which be impossible may be yet
probable,[**] other have so little historic or poetic faith, that they
believe nothing to be either possible or probable, the like to which
hath not occur to their own observation.
[*] by this word here, and in most other part of our work, we mean
every reader in the world.
[**] it be happy for M. Dacier that he be not an Irishman.
first, then, I think it may very reasonably be require of every
writer, that he keep within the bound of possibility; and still
remember that what it be not possible for man to perform, it be
scarce possible for man to believe he do perform. this conviction
perhaps give birth to many story of the antient heathen deity (for
most of they be of poetical original). the poet, be desirous to
indulge a wanton and extravagant imagination, take refuge in that
power, of the extent of which his reader be no judge, or rather
which they imagine to be infinite, and consequently they could not be
shock at any prodigy relate of it. this hath be strongly urge
in defence of Homer's miracle; and it be perhaps a defence; not, as
Mr Pope would have it, because Ulysses tell a set of foolish lie to
the Phaeacians, who be a very dull nation; but because the poet
himself write to heathens, to whom poetical fable be article of
faith. for my own part, I must confess, so compassionate be my temper,
I wish Polypheme have confine himself to his milk diet, and preserve
his eye; nor could Ulysses be much more concerned than myself, when
his companion be turn into swine by Circe, who show, I think,
afterwards, too much regard for man's flesh to be suppose capable of
convert it into bacon. I wish, likewise, with all my heart, that
Homer could have know the rule prescribe by Horace, to introduce
supernatural agent as seldom as possible. we should not then have
see his god come on trivial errand, and often behave themselves
so as not only to forfeit all title to respect, but to become the
object of scorn and derision. a conduct which must have shock the
credulity of a pious and sagacious heathen; and which could never have
be defend, unless by agree with a supposition to which I have
be sometimes almost inclined, that this most glorious poet, as he
certainly be, have an intent to burlesque the superstitious faith of
his own age and country.
but I have rest too long on a doctrine which can be of no use to a
christian writer; for as he cannot introduce into his work any of
that heavenly host which make a part of his creed, so it be horrid
puerility to search the heathen theology for any of those deity who
have be long since dethrone from their immortality. Lord
Shaftesbury observe, that nothing be more cold than the invocation of
a muse by a modern; he might have add, that nothing can be more
absurd. a modern may with much more elegance invoke a ballad, as some
have think Homer do, or a mug of ale, with the author of Hudibras;
which latter may perhaps have inspire much more poetry, as well as
prose, than all the liquor of Hippocrene or Helicon.
the only supernatural agent which can in any manner be allow to we
modern, be ghost; but of these I would advise an author to be
extremely sparing. these be indeed, like arsenic, and other dangerous
drug in physic, to be use with the utmost caution; nor would I
advise the introduction of they at all in those work, or by those
author, to which, or to whom, a horse-laugh in the reader would be
any great prejudice or mortification.
as for elf and fairy, and other such mummery, I purposely omit the
mention of they, as I should be very unwilling to confine within any
bound those surprizing imagination, for whose vast capacity the
limit of human nature be too narrow; whose work be to be
consider as a new creation; and who have consequently just right to
do what they will with their own.
Man therefore be the high subject (unless on very extraordinary
occasion indeed) which present itself to the pen of our historian,
or of our poet; and, in relate his action, great care be to be
take that we do not exceed the capacity of the agent we describe.
nor be possibility alone sufficient to justify we; we must keep
likewise within the rule of probability. it be, I think, the opinion
of Aristotle; or if not, it be the opinion of some wise man, whose
authority will be as weighty when it be as old, "that it be no excuse
for a poet who relate what be incredible, that the thing relate be
really matter of fact." this may perhaps be allow true with regard
to poetry, but it may be think impracticable to extend it to the
historian; for he be oblige to record matter as he find they,
though they may be of so extraordinary a nature as will require no
small degree of historical faith to swallow they. such be the
successless armament of Xerxes describe by Herodotus, or the
successful expedition of Alexander relate by Arrian. such of later
year be the victory of Agincourt obtain by Harry the Fifth, or
that of Narva win by Charles the Twelfth of Sweden. all which
instance, the more we reflect on they, appear still the more
astonishing.
such fact, however, as they occur in the thread of the story, nay,
indeed, as they constitute the essential part of it, the historian be
not only justifiable in recording as they really happen, but indeed
would be unpardonable should he omit or alter they. but there be
other fact not of such consequence nor so necessary, which, though
ever so well attest, may nevertheless be sacrifice to oblivion in
complacence to the scepticism of a reader. such be that memorable
story of the ghost of George Villiers, which might with more propriety
have be make a present of to Dr Drelincourt, to have keep the ghost
of Mrs Veale company, at the head of his Discourse upon death, than
have be introduce into so solemn a work as the history of the
Rebellion.
to say the truth, if the historian will confine himself to what really
happen, and utterly reject any circumstance, which, though never so
well attest, he must be well assured be false, he will sometimes
fall into the marvellous, but never into the incredible. he will often
raise the wonder and surprize of his reader, but never that
incredulous hatred mention by Horace. it be by fall into fiction,
therefore, that we generally offend against this rule, of deserting
probability, which the historian seldom, if ever, quit, till he
forsake his character and commence a writer of romance. in this,
however, those historian who relate public transaction, have the
advantage of we who confine ourselves to scene of private life. the
credit of the former be by common notoriety support for a long time;
and public record, with the concurrent testimony of many author,
bear evidence to their truth in future age. thus a Trajan and an
Antoninus, a Nero and a Caligula, have all meet with the belief of
posterity; and no one doubt but that man so very good, and so very
bad, be once the master of mankind.
but we who deal in private character, who search into the most retire
recess, and draw forth example of virtue and vice from hole and
corner of the world, be in a more dangerous situation. as we have no
public notoriety, no concurrent testimony, no record to support and
corroborate what we deliver, it become we to keep within the limit
not only of possibility, but of probability too; and this more
especially in paint what be greatly good and amiable. knavery and
folly, though never so exorbitant, will more easily meet with assent;
for ill-nature add great support and strength to faith.
thus we may, perhaps, with little danger, relate the history of
Fisher; who have long owe his bread to the generosity of Mr Derby,
and have one morning receive a considerable bounty from his hand,
yet, in order to possess himself of what remain in his friend's
scrutore, conceal himself in a public office of the Temple, through
which there be a passage into Mr Derby's chamber. here he overhear
Mr Derby for many hour solace himself at an entertainment which he
that evening give his friend, and to which Fisher have be invite.
during all this time, no tender, no grateful reflection arise to
restrain his purpose; but when the poor gentleman have let his company
out through the office, Fisher come suddenly from his lurking-place,
and walk softly behind his friend into his chamber, discharge a
pistol-ball into his head. this may be believe when the bone of
Fisher be as rotten as his heart. Nay, perhaps, it will be credit,
that the villain go two day afterwards with some young lady to
the play of Hamlet; and with an unaltered countenance hear one of the
lady, who little suspect how near she be to the person, cry out,
"Good God! if the man that murder Mr Derby be now present!"
manifest in this a more seared and callous conscience than even
Nero himself; of whom we be tell by Suetonius, "that the
consciousness of his guilt, after the death of his mother, become
immediately intolerable, and so continue; nor could all the
congratulation of the soldier, of the senate, and the people, allay
the horror of his conscience."
but now, on the other hand, should I tell my reader, that I have know
a man whose penetrate genius have enable he to raise a large
fortune in a way where no beginning be chaulke out to he; that he
have do this with the most perfect preservation of his integrity, and
not only without the least injustice or injury to any one individual
person, but with the high advantage to trade, and a vast increase
of the public revenue; that he have expend one part of the income of
this fortune in discover a taste superior to most, by work where
the high dignity be unite with the pure simplicity, and another
part in display a degree of goodness superior to all man, by act
of charity to object whose only recommendation be their merit, or
their want; that he be most industrious in search after merit in
distress, most eager to relieve it, and then as careful (perhaps too
careful) to conceal what he have do; that his house, his furniture,
his garden, his table, his private hospitality, and his public
beneficence, all denote the mind from which they flow, and be all
intrinsically rich and noble, without tinsel, or external ostentation;
that he fill every relation in life with the most adequate virtue;
that he be most piously religious to his creator, most zealously
loyal to his sovereign; a most tender husband to his wife, a kind
relation, a munificent patron, a warm and firm friend, a knowing and a
chearful companion, indulgent to his servant, hospitable to his
neighbour, charitable to the poor, and benevolent to all mankind.
should I add to these the epithet of wise, brave, elegant, and indeed
every other amiable epithet in our language, I might surely say,
_--Quis credet? nemo Hercule! nemo;
Vel duo, vel nemo;_
and yet I know a man who be all I have here describe. but a single
instance (and I really know not such another) be not sufficient to
justify we, while we be write to thousand who never hear of the
person, nor of anything like he. such _rarae ave_ should be remit
to the epitaph writer, or to some poet who may condescend to hitch he
in a distich, or to slide he into a rhime with an air of carelessness
and neglect, without give any offence to the reader.
in the last place, the action should be such as may not only be
within the compass of human agency, and which human agent may
probably be suppose to do; but they should be likely for the very
actor and character themselves to have perform; for what may be
only wonderful and surprize in one man, may become improbable, or
indeed impossible, when relate of another.
this last requisite be what the dramatic critic call conversation of
character; and it require a very extraordinary degree of judgment,
and a most exact knowledge of human nature.
it be admirably remark by a most excellent writer, that zeal can no
more hurry a man to act in direct opposition to itself, than a rapid
stream can carry a boat against its own current. I will venture to
say, that for a man to act in direct contradiction to the dictate of
his nature, be, if not impossible, as improbable and as miraculous as
anything which can well be conceive. should the good part of the
story of M. Antoninus be ascribe to Nero, or should the bad
incident of Nero's life be impute to Antoninus, what would be more
shocking to belief than either instance? whereas both these be
relate of their proper agent, constitute the truly marvellous.
our modern author of comedy have fall almost universally into the
error here hint at; their hero generally be notorious rogue, and
their heroine abandon jade, during the first four act; but in the
fifth, the former become very worthy gentleman, and the latter woman
of virtue and discretion: nor be the writer often so kind as to give
himself the least trouble to reconcile or account for this monstrous
change and incongruity. there be, indeed, no other reason to be
assign for it, than because the play be draw to a conclusion; as
if it be no less natural in a rogue to repent in the last act of a
play, than in the last of his life; which we perceive to be generally
the case at Tyburn, a place which might indeed close the scene of some
comedy with much propriety, as the hero in these be most commonly
eminent for those very talent which not only bring man to the
gallow, but enable they to make an heroic figure when they be there.
within these few restriction, I think, every writer may be permit
to deal as much in the wonderful as he please; nay, if he thus keep
within the rule of credibility, the more he can surprize the reader
the more he will engage his attention, and the more he will charm he.
as a genius of the high rank observe in his fifth chapter of the
Bathos, "the great art of all poetry be to mix truth with fiction, in
order to join the credible with the surprizing."
for though every good author will confine himself within the bound of
probability, it be by no mean necessary that his character, or his
incident, should be trite, common, or vulgar; such as happen in every
street, or in every house, or which may be meet with in the home
article of a newspaper. nor must he be inhibit from show many
person and thing, which may possibly have never fall within the
knowledge of great part of his reader. if the writer strictly
observe the rule above-mention, he hath discharge his part; and
be then intitle to some faith from his reader, who be indeed guilty
of critical infidelity if he disbelieve he.
for want of a portion of such faith, I remember the character of a
young lady of quality, which be condemn on the stage for be
unnatural, by the unanimous voice of a very large assembly of clerk
and apprentice; though it have the previous suffrage of many lady
of the first rank; one of whom, very eminent for her understanding,
declare it be the picture of half the young people of her
acquaintance.
Chapter ii.
in which the landlady pay a visit to Mr Jones.
when Jones have take leave of his friend the lieutenant, he
endeavour to close his eye, but all in vain; his spirit be too
lively and wakeful to be lull to sleep. so have amuse, or rather
torment, himself with the thought of his Sophia till it be open
daylight, he call for some tea; upon which occasion my landlady
herself vouchsafe to pay he a visit.
this be indeed the first time she have see he, or at least have take
any notice of he; but as the lieutenant have assure she that he be
certainly some young gentleman of fashion, she now determine to show
he all the respect in her power; for, to speak truly, this be one of
those house where gentleman, to use the language of advertisement,
meet with civil treatment for their money.
she have no soon begin to make his tea, than she likewise begin to
discourse:--"La! sir," say she, "I think it be great pity that such a
pretty young gentleman should under-value himself so, as to go about
with these soldier fellow. they call themselves gentleman, I warrant
you; but, as my first husband use to say, they should remember it be
we that pay they. and to be sure it be very hard upon we to be oblige
to pay they, and to keep 'um too, as we publican be. I have twenty of
'um last night, besides officer: nay, for matter o' that, I have
rather have the soldier than officer: for nothing be ever good
enough for those spark; and I be sure, if you be to see the bill;
la! sir, it be nothing. I have have less trouble, I warrant you, with a
good squire's family, where we take forty or fifty shilling of a
night, besides horse. and yet I warrant I, there be narrow a one of
those officer fellow but look upon himself to be as good as arrow a
squire of £500 a year. to be sure it doth I good to hear their man
run about after 'um, cry your honour, and your honour. Marry come
up with such honour, and an ordinary at a shill a head. then
therebe such swearing among 'um, to be sure it frighten I out o' my
wit: I think nothing can ever prosper with such wicked people. and
here one of 'um have use you in so barbarous a manner. I think
indeed how well the rest would secure he; they all hang together; for
if you have be in danger of death, which I be glad to see you be
not, it would have be all as one to such wicked people. they would
have let the murderer go. laud have mercy upon 'um; I would not have
such a sin to answer for, for the whole world. but though you be
likely, with the blessing, to recover, there be laa for he yet; and
if you will employ lawyer Small, I darest be swear hewill make the
fellow fly the country for he; though perhaps hewill have flee the
country before; for it be here to-day and go to-morrow with such
chap. I hope, however, you will learn more wit for the future, and
return back to your friend; I warrant they be all miserable for your
loss; and if they be but to know what have happen--La, my seeming! I
would not for the world they should. come, come, we know very well
what all the matter be; but if one willnot, another will; so pretty a
gentleman need never want a lady. I be sure, if I be you, I would see
the fine she that ever wear a head hang, before I would go for a
soldier for her.--Nay, donot blush so" (for indeed he do to a violent
degree). "why, you think, sir, I know nothing of the matter, I
warrant you, about Madam Sophia."--"how," say Jones, start up, "do
you know my Sophia?"--"Do I! ay marry," cry the landlady; "manybe
the time hath she lain in this house."--"With her aunt, I suppose,"
say Jones. "why, there it be now," cry the landlady. "ay, ay, ay, I
know the old lady very well. and a sweet young creature be Madam
Sophia, thatbe the truth on't."--"a sweet creature," cry Jones; "O
heaven!"
Angels be paint fair to look like she.
therebe in she all that we believe of heav'n,
amazing brightness, purity, and truth,
eternal joy and everlaste love.
"and could I ever have imagine that you have know my sophia!"--"i
wish," say the landlady, "you know half so much of she. what would
you have give to have sit by her bed-side? what a delicious neck she
hath! her lovely limb have stretch themselves in that very bed you
now lie in."--"here!" cry Jones: "hath Sophia ever lay here?"--"Ay,
ay, here; there, in that very bed," say the landlady; "where I wish
you have she this moment; and she may wish so too for anything I know
to the contrary, for she hath mention your name to I."--"ha!" cry
he; "do she ever mention her poor Jones? you flatter I now: I can
never believe so much."--"why, then," answer she, "as I hope to be
save, and may the devil fetch I if I speak a syllable more than the
truth, I have hear she mention Mr Jones; but in a civil and modest
way, I confess; yet I could perceive she think a great deal more
than she say."--"O my dear woman!" cry Jones, "her thought of I I
shall never be worthy of. oh, she be all gentleness, kindness,
goodness! why be such a rascal as I bear, ever to give her soft bosom
a moment's uneasiness? why be I curse? I, who would undergo all the
plague and misery which any daemon ever invent for mankind, to
procure she any good; nay, torture itself could not be misery to I,
do I but know that she be happy."--"why, look you there now," say
the landlady; "I tell she you be a constant lovier."--"But pray,
madam, tell I when or where you know anything of I; for I never be
here before, nor do I remember ever to have see you."--"Nor be it
possible you should," answer she; "for you be a little thing when I
have you in my lap at the squire's."--"How, the squire's?" say Jones:
"what, do you know that great and good Mr Allworthy then?"--"Yes,
marry, do I," say she: "who in the country doth not?"--"the fame of
his goodness indeed," answer Jones, "must have extend far than
this; but heaven only can know he--can know that benevolence which it
copy from itself, and send upon earth as its own pattern. mankind
be as ignorant of such divine goodness, as they be unworthy of it;
but none so unworthy of it as myself. I, who be raise by he to such
a height; take in, as you must well know, a poor base-bear child,
adopt by he, and treat as his own son, to dare by my folly to
disoblige he, to draw his vengeance upon I. yes, I deserve it all;
for I will never be so ungrateful as ever to think he hath do an act
of injustice by I. no, I deserve to be turn out of door, as I be.
and now, madam," say he, "I believe you will not blame I for turn
soldier, especially with such a fortune as this in my pocket." at
which word he shake a purse, which have but very little in it, and
which still appear to the landlady to have less.
my good landlady be (accord to vulgar phrase) strike all of a heap
by this relation. she answer coldly, "that to be sure people be
the good judge what be most proper for their circumstance. but
hark," say she, "I think I hear somebody call. come! come! the
devil's in all our volk; nobody hath any ear. I must go down-stair;
