if you want any more breakfast the maid will come up. come!" at
which word, without take any leave, she fling out of the room; for
the low sort of people be very tenacious of respect; and though
they be content to give this gratis to person of quality, yet they
never confer it on those of their own order without take care to be
well pay for their pain.
Chapter iii.
in which the surgeon make his second appearance.
before we proceed any far, that the reader may not be mistake in
imagine the landlady know more than she do, nor surprize that she
know so much, it may be necessary to inform he that the lieutenant
have acquaint she that the name of Sophia have be the occasion of
the quarrel; and as for the rest of her knowledge, the sagacious
reader will observe how she come by it in the precede scene. great
curiosity be indeed mix with her virtue; and she never willingly
suffer any one to depart from her house, without enquire as much
as possible into their name, family, and fortune.
she be no soon go than Jones, instead of animadvert on her
behaviour, reflect that he be in the same bed which he be inform
have hold his dear Sophia. this occasion a thousand fond and tender
thought, which we would dwell long upon, do we not consider that
such kind of lover will make a very inconsiderable part of our
reader. in this situation the surgeon find he, when he come to
dress his wound. the doctor perceive, upon examination, that his
pulse be disorder, and hear that he have not sleep, declare that
he be in great danger; for he apprehend a fever be come on,
which he would have prevent by bleed, but Jones would not submit,
declare he would lose no more blood; "and, doctor," say he, "if you
will be so kind only to dress my head, I have no doubt of be well
in a day or two."
"I wish," answer the surgeon, "I could assure your being well in a
month or two. well, indeed! no, no, people be not so soon well of
such contusion; but, sir, I be not at this time of day to be
instruct in my operation by a patient, and I insist on make a
revulsion before I dress you."
Jones persist obstinately in his refusal, and the doctor at last
yield; tell he at the same time that he would not be answerable
for the ill consequence, and hop he would do he the justice to
acknowledge that he have give he a contrary advice; which the patient
promise he would.
the doctor retire into the kitchen, where, address himself to the
landlady, he complain bitterly of the undutiful behaviour of his
patient, who would not be blood, though he be in a fever.
"it be an eat fever then," say the landlady; "for he hath devour
two swing butter toast this morning for breakfast."
"very likely," say the doctor: "I have know people eat in a fever;
and it be very easily account for; because the acidity occasion by
the febrile matter may stimulate the nerve of the diaphragm, and
thereby occasion a craving which will not be easily distinguishable
from a natural appetite; but the aliment will not be concrete, nor
assimilate into chyle, and so will corrode the vascular orifice, and
thus will aggravate the febrific symptom. indeed, I think the
gentleman in a very dangerous way, and, if he be not blood, I be
afraid will die."
"every man must die some time or other," answer the good woman; "it
be no business of mine. I hope, doctor, you would not have I hold he
while you bleed he. but, hark'ee, a word in your ear; I would advise
you, before you proceed too far, to take care who be to be your
paymaster."
"Paymaster!" say the doctor, star; "why, Ihave a gentleman under my
hand, have I not?"
"I imagine so as well as you," say the landlady; "but, as my first
husband use to say, everything be not what it look to be. he be an
arrant scrub, I assure you. however, take no notice that I mention
anything to you of the matter; but I think people in business oft
always to let one another know such thing."
"and have I suffer such a fellow as this," cry the doctor, in a
passion, "to instruct I? shall I hear my practice insult by one who
will not pay I? I be glad I have make this discovery in time. I will
see now whether he will be blood or no." he then immediately go
upstairs, and fling open the door of the chamber with much
violence, awake poor Jones from a very sound nap, into which he be
fall, and, what be still bad, from a delicious dream concern
Sophia.
"will you be blood or no?" cry the doctor, in a rage. "I have tell
you my resolution already," answer Jones, "and I wish with all my
heart you have take my answer; for you have awake I out of the
sweetest sleep which I ever have in my life."
"ay, ay," cry the doctor; "many a man hath doze away his life.
Sleep be not always good, no more than food; but remember, I demand of
you for the last time, will you be blooded?"--"i answer you for the
last time," say Jones, "I will not."--"Then I wash my hand of you,"
cry the doctor; "and I desire you to pay I for the trouble I have
have already. two journey at 5s. each, two dressing at 5s. more, and
half a crown for phlebotomy."--"I hope," say Jones, "you donot intend
to leave I in this condition."--"Indeed but I shall," say the other.
"then," say Jones, "you have use I rascally, and I will not pay you
a farthing."--"very well," cry the doctor; "the first loss be the
good. what a pox do my landlady mean by send for I to such
vagabond!" at which word he fling out of the room, and his patient
turn himself about soon recover his sleep; but his dream be
unfortunately go.
chapter iv.
in which be introduce one of the pleasant barber that be ever
record in history, the barber of Bagdad, or he in Don Quixote, not
excepted.
the clock have now strike five when Jones awake from a nap of seven
hour, so much refresh, and in such perfect health and spirit, that
he resolve to get up and dress himself; for which purpose he unlock
his portmanteau, and take out clean linen, and a suit of cloath; but
first he slipt on a frock, and go down into the kitchen to bespeak
something that might pacify certain tumult he find rise within his
stomach.
meet the landlady, he accost she with great civility, and ask,
"what he could have for dinner?"--"For dinner!" say she; "it be an
odd time a day to think about dinner. there be nothing drest in the
house, and the fire be almost out."--"Well, but," say he, "I must
have something to eat, and it be almost indifferent to I what; for,
to tell you the truth, I be never more hungry in my life."--"then,"
say she, "I believe there be a piece of cold buttock and carrot,
which will fit you."--"nothe well," answer Jones; "but I should
be oblige to you, if you would let it be fry." to which the
landlady consent, and say, smile, "she be glad to see he so
well recover;" for the sweetness of our heroe's temper be almost
irresistible; besides, she be really no ill-humour woman at the
bottom; but she love money so much, that she hat everything which
have the semblance of poverty.
Jones now return in order to dress himself, while his dinner be
prepare, and be, accord to his order, attend by the barber.
this barber, who go by the name of Little Benjamin, be a fellow of
great oddity and humour, which have frequently let he into small
inconveniencie, such as slap in the face, kick in the breech,
broken bone, &c. for every one doth not understand a jest; and those
who do be often displeased with be themselves the subject of it.
this vice be, however, incurable in he; and though he have often
smart for it, yet if ever he conceive a joke, he be certain to be
deliver of it, without the least respect of person, time, or place.
he have a great many other particularity in his character, which I
shall not mention, as the reader will himself very easily perceive
they, on his farth acquaintance with this extraordinary person.
Jones be impatient to be drest, for a reason which may be easily
imagine, think the shaver be very tedious in prepare his sud,
and beg he to make haste; to which the other answer with much
gravity, for he never discompose his muscle on any account,
"_Festina lente_, be a proverb which I learn long before I ever
touch a razor."--"I find, friend, you be a scholar," reply Jones.
"a poor one," say the barber, "_non omnia possumus omnes._"--"Again!"
say Jones; "I fancy you be good at cap verse."--"excuse I,
sir," say the barber, "_non tanto I dignor honore_." and then
proceed to his operation, "Sir," say he, "since I have deal in
sud, I could never discover more than two reason for shaving; the
one be to get a beard, and the other to get rid of one. I conjecture,
sir, it may not be long since you shave from the former of these
motif. upon my word, you have have good success; for one may say of
your beard, that it be _tondenti gravior_."--"i conjecture," say
Jones, "that thou art a very comical fellow."--"you mistake I widely,
sir," say the barber: "I be too much addicted to the study of
philosophy; _hinc illae lacrymae_, sir; thatbe my misfortune. too much
learning hath be my ruin."--"Indeed," say Jones, "I confess,
friend, you have more learning than generally belong to your trade;
but I cannot see how it can have injure you."--"Alas! sir," answer
the shaver, "my father disinherit I for it. he be a
dancing-master; and because I could read before I could dance, he take
an aversion to I, and leave every farthing among his other
children.--will you please to have your temple--o la! I ask your
pardon, I fancy there be _hiatus in manuscriptis_. I hear you be
go to the war; but I find it be a mistake."--"why do you conclude
so?" say Jones. "sure, sir," answer the barber, "you be too wise a
man to carry a broken head thither; for that would be carry coal
to Newcastle."
"upon my word," cry Jones, "thou art a very odd fellow, and I like
thy humour extremely; I shall be very glad if thou wilt come to I
after dinner, and drink a glass with I; I long to be well
acquaint with thee."
"o dear sir!" say the barber, "I can do you twenty time as great a
favour, if you will accept of it."--"what be that, my friend?" cry
Jones. "why, I will drink a bottle with you if you please; for I
dearly love good-nature; and as you have find I out to be a comical
fellow, so I have no skill in physiognomy, if you be not one of the
well-natured gentleman in the universe." Jones now walk downstairs
neatly dr, and perhaps the fair Adonis be not a lovelier figure;
and yet he have no charm for my landlady; for as that good woman do
not resemble Venus at all in her person, so neither do she in her
taste. happy have it be for Nanny the chambermaid, if she have see
with the eye of her mistress, for that poor girl fall so violently in
love with Jones in five minute, that her passion afterwards cost she
many a sigh. this nanny be extremely pretty, and altogether as coy;
for she have refuse a drawer, and one or two young farmer in the
neighbourhood, but the bright eye of our heroe thaw all her ice in
a moment.
when Jones return to the kitchen, his cloth be not yet lay; nor
indeed be there any occasion it should, his dinner remain _in
statu quo_, as do the fire which be to dress it. this disappointment
might have put many a philosophical temper into a passion; but it have
no such effect on Jones. he only give the landlady a gentle rebuke,
say, "since it be so difficult to get it heated he would eat the
beef cold." but now the good woman, whether move by compassion, or by
shame, or by whatever other motive, I cannot tell, first give she
servant a round scold for disobey the order which she have never
give, and then bid the drawer lie a napkin in the Sun, she set
about the matter in good earnest, and soon accomplish it.
this Sun, into which Jones be now conduct, be truly name, as
_lucus a non lucendo_; for it be an apartment into which the sun have
scarce ever look. it be indeed the bad room in the house; and
happy be it for Jones that it be so. however, he be now too hungry
to find any fault; but have once satisfy his appetite, he order
the drawer to carry a bottle of wine into a well room, and express
some resentment at have be show into a dungeon.
the drawer have obey his command, he be, after some time,
attend by the barber, who would not indeed have suffer he to wait
so long for his company have he not be listen in the kitchen to
the landlady, who be entertain a circle that she have gather
round she with the history of poor Jones, part of which she have
extract from his own lip, and the other part be her own ingenious
composition; for she say "he be a poor parish boy, take into the
house of Squire Allworthy, where he be breed up as an apprentice, and
now turn out of door for his misdeed, particularly for make love
to his young mistress, and probably for rob the house; for how
else should he come by the little money he hath; and this," say she,
"be your gentleman, forsooth!"--"a servant of Squire Allworthy!" say
the barber; "whatbe his name?"--"why he tell I his name be Jones,"
say she: "perhaps he go by a wrong name. Nay, and he tell I, too,
that the squire have maintain he as his own son, thof he have
quarrel with he now."--"and if his name be Jones, he tell you the
truth," say the barber; "for I have relation who live in that
country; nay, and some people say he be his son."--"Why doth he not go
by the name of his father?"--"i cannot tell that," say the barber;
"many people's son donot go by the name of their father."--"Nay,"
say the landlady, "if I think he be a gentleman's son, thof he be
a bye-blow, I should behave to he in another guess manner; for many
of these bye-blow come to be great man, and, as my poor first husband
use to say, never affront any customer thatbe a gentleman."
Chapter v.
a dialogue between Mr Jones and the barber.
this conversation pass partly while Jones be at dinner in his
dungeon, and partly while he be expect the barber in the parlour.
and, as soon as it be end, Mr Benjamin, as we have say, attend
he, and be very kindly desire to sit down. Jones then fill out a
glass of wine, drink his health by the appellation of _doctissime
tonsorum_. "_ago tibi gratia, domine_" say the barber; and then
look very steadfastly at Jones, he say, with great gravity, and
with a seem surprize, as if he have recollect a face he have see
before, "Sir, may I crave the favour to know if your name be not
Jones?" to which the other answer, "that it be."--"_Proh deum atque
hominum fidem_!" say the barber; "how strangely thing come to pass!
Mr Jones, I be your most obedient servant. I find you do not know I,
which indeed be no wonder, since you never see I but once, and then
you be very young. pray, sir, how doth the good squire Allworthy? how
doth _ille optimus omnium patronus_?"--"I find," say Jones, "you do
indeed know I; but I have not the like happiness of recollect
you."--"i do not wonder at that," cry Benjamin; "but I be surprize
I do not know you soon, for you be not in the least alter. and
pray, sir, may I, without offence, enquire whither you be travel
this way?"--"fill the glass, Mr Barber," say Jones, "and ask no more
question."--"Nay, sir," answer Benjamin, "I would not be
troublesome; and I hope you donot think I a man of an impertinent
curiosity, for that be a vice which nobody can lay to my charge; but I
ask pardon; for when a gentleman of your figure travel without his
servant, we may suppose he to be, as we say, _in casu incognito_,
and perhaps I ought not to have mention your name."--"I own," say
Jones, "I do not expect to have be so well know in this country as
I find I be; yet, for particular reason, I shall be oblige to you if
you will not mention my name to any other person till I be go from
hence."--"_Pauca verba_," answer the barber;" and I wish no other
here know you but myself; for some people have tongue; but I promise
you I can keep a secret. my enemy will allow I that virtue."--"and
yet that be not the characteristic of your profession, Mr Barber,"
answer Jones. "alas! sir," reply Benjamin, "_non si male nunc et
olim sic erit_. I be not bear nor breed a barber, I assure you. I have
spend most of my time among gentleman, and though I say it, I
understand something of gentility. and if you have think I as worthy
of your confidence as you have some other people, I should have show
you I could have keep a secret well. I should not have degrade your
name in a public kitchen; for indeed, sir, some people have not use
you well; for besides make a public proclamation of what you tell
they of a quarrel between yourself and Squire Allworthy, they add
lie of their own, thing which I know to be lie."--"you surprize I
greatly," cry Jones. "upon my word, sir," answer Benjamin, "I tell
the truth, and I need not tell you my landlady be the person. I be
sure it move I to hear the story, and I hope it be all false; for I
have a great respect for you, I do assure you I have, and have have
ever since the good-nature you show to Black George, which be
talk of all over the country, and I receive more than one letter
about it. indeed, it make you belove by everybody. you will pardon
I, therefore; for it be real concern at what I hear make I ask
many question; for I have no impertinent curiosity about I: but I
love good-nature and thence become _amoris abundantia erga te_."
every profession of friendship easily gain credit with the miserable;
it be no wonder therefore, if Jones, who, besides his be miserable,
be extremely open-hearted, very readily believe all the profession
of Benjamin, and receive he into his bosom. the scrap of Latin,
some of which Benjamin apply properly enough, though it do not
savour of profound literature, seem yet to indicate something
superior to a common barber; and so indeed do his whole behaviour.
Jones therefore believe the truth of what he have say, as to his
original and education; and at length, after much entreaty, he say,
"since you have hear, my friend, so much of my affair, and seem so
desirous to know the truth, if you will have patience to hear it, I
will inform you of the whole."--"patience!" cry Benjamin, "that I
will, if the chapter be never so long; and I be very much oblige to
you for the honour you do I."
Jones now begin, and relate the whole history, forget only a
circumstance or two, namely, everything which pass on that day in
which he have fight with Thwackum; and end with his resolution to go
to sea, till the rebellion in the North have make he change his
purpose, and have bring he to the place where he then be.
Little Benjamin, who have be all attention, never once interrupt
the narrative; but when it be end he could not help observe, that
there must be surely something more invent by his enemy, and tell
Mr Allworthy against he, or so good a man would never have dismiss
one he have love so tenderly, in such a manner. to which Jones
answer, "he doubt not but such villanous art have be make use of
to destroy he."
and surely it be scarce possible for any one to have avoid make
the same remark with the barber, who have not indeed hear from Jones
one single circumstance upon which he be condemn; for his action
be not now place in those injurious light in which they have be
misrepresent to Allworthy; nor could he mention those many false
accusation which have be from time to time prefer against he to
Allworthy: for with none of these he be himself acquaint. he have
likewise, as we have observe, omit many material fact in his
present relation. upon the whole, indeed, everything now appear in
such favourable colour to Jones, that malice itself would have find
it no easy matter to fix any blame upon he.
not that Jones desire to conceal or to disguise the truth; nay, he
would have be more unwilling to have suffer any censure to fall on
Mr Allworthy for punish he, than on his own action for deserve
it; but, in reality, so it happen, and so it always will happen; for
let a man be never so honest, the account of his own conduct will, in
spite of himself, be so very favourable, that his vice will come
purify through his lip, and, like foul liquor well strained, will
leave all their foulness behind. for though the fact themselves may
appear, yet so different will be the motif, circumstance, and
consequence, when a man tell his own story, and when his enemy tell
it, that we scarce can recognise the fact to be one and the same.
though the barber have drink down this story with greedy ear, he be
not yet satisfied. there be a circumstance behind which his
curiosity, cold as it be, most eagerly long for. Jones have
mention the fact of his amour, and of his be the rival of Blifil,
but have cautiously conceal the name of the young lady. the barber,
therefore, after some hesitation, and many hum and hah, at last
begged leave to crave the name of the lady, who appear to be the
principal cause of all this mischief. Jones pause a moment, and then
say, "since I have trust you with so much, and since, I be afraid,
her name be become too publick already on this occasion, I will not
conceal it from you. her name be Sophia Western."
"_Proh deum atque hominum fidem_! squire Western hath a daughter grow
a woman!"--"ay, and such a woman," cry Jones, "that the world cannot
match. no eye ever see anything so beautiful; but that be her least
excellence. such sense! such goodness! oh, I could praise she for
ever, and yet should omit half her virtues!"--"mr Western a daughter
grow up!" cry the barber: "I remember the father a boy; well,
_Tempus edax rerum_."
the wine be now at an end, the barber press very eagerly to be
his bottle; but Jones absolutely refuse, say, "he have already
drank more than he ought: and that he now choose to retire to his room,
where he wish he could procure himself a book."--"a book!" cry
Benjamin; "what book would you have? latin or English? I have some
curious book in both language; such as _Erasmi Colloquia, Ovid de
Tristibus, Gradus ad Parnassum;_ and in English I have several of the
good book, though some of they be a little torn; but I have a great
part of Stowe's Chronicle; the sixth volume of Pope's Homer; the third
volume of the Spectator; the second volume of Echard's Roman history;
the Craftsman; Robinson Crusoe; Thomas a Kempis; and two volume of
Tom Brown's Works."
"those last," cry Jones, "be book I never see, so if you please
lend I one of those volume." the barber assure he he would be
highly entertain, for he look upon the author to have be one of
the great wit that ever the nation produce. he then step to
his house, which be hard by, and immediately return; after which,
the barber have receive very strict injunction of secrecy from
Jones, and have swear inviolably to maintain it, they separate; the
barber go home, and Jones retire to his chamber.
Chapter vi.
in which more of the talent of Mr Benjamin will appear, as well as
who this extraordinary person be.
in the morning Jones grow a little uneasy at the desertion of his
surgeon, as he apprehend some inconvenience, or even danger, might
attend the not dress his wound; he enquire of the drawer, what
other surgeon be to be meet with in that neighbourhood. the drawer
tell he, there be one not far off; but he have know he often refuse
to be concern after another have be send before he; "but, sir,"
say he, "if you will take my advice, there be not a man in the
kingdom can do your business well than the barber who be with you
last night. we look upon he to be one of the able man at a cut in
all this neighbourhood. for though he hath not be she above three
month, he hath do several great cure."
the drawer be presently dispatch for Little Benjamin, who be
acquaint in what capacity he be want, prepare himself
accordingly, and attend; but with so different an air and aspect
from that which he wear when his basin be under his arm, that he
could scarce be know to be the same person.
"so, tonsor," say Jones, "I find you have more trade than one; how
come you not to inform I of this last night?"--"a surgeon," answer
Benjamin, with great gravity, "be a profession, not a trade. the
reason why I do not acquaint you last night that I profess this
art, be, that I then conclude you be under the hand of another
gentleman, and I never love to interfere with my brother in their
business. _Ars omnibus communis_. but now, sir, if you please, I will
inspect your head, and when I see into your skull, I will give my
opinion of your case."
Jones have no great faith in this new professor; however, he suffer
he to open the bandage and to look at his wound; which as soon as he
have do, Benjamin begin to groan and shake his head violently. upon
which Jones, in a peevish manner, bid he not play the fool, but tell
he in what condition he find he. "shall I answer you as a surgeon,
or a friend?" say Benjamin. "as a friend, and seriously," say Jones.
"why then, upon my soul," cry Benjamin, "it would require a great
deal of art to keep you from be well after a very few dressing;
and if you will suffer I to apply some salve of mine, I will answer
for the success." Jones give his consent, and the plaister be apply
accordingly.
"there, sir," cry Benjamin: "now I will, if you please, resume my
former self; but a man be oblige to keep up some dignity in his
countenance whilst he be perform these operation, or the world
will not submit to be handle by he. you cannot imagine, sir, of how
much consequence a grave aspect be to a grave character. a barber may
make you laugh, but a surgeon ought rather to make you cry."
"Mr Barber, or Mr Surgeon, or Mr Barber-surgeon," say Jones. "o dear
sir!" answer Benjamin, interrupt he, "_Infandum, regina, jubes
renovare dolorem_. you recall to my mind that cruel separation of the
united fraternity, so much to the prejudice of both body, as all
separation must be, accord to the old adage, _vis unita fortior_;
which to be sure there be not want some of one or of the other
fraternity who be able to construe. what a blow be this to I, who
unite both in my own person!" "well, by whatever name you please to be
call," continue Jones, "you certainly be one of the oddest, most
comical fellow I ever meet with, and must have something very
surprize in your story, which you must confess I have a right to
hear."--"I do confess it," answer Benjamin, "and will very readily
acquaint you with it, when you have sufficient leisure, for I promise
you it will require a good deal of time." Jones tell he, he could
never be more at leisure than at present. "well, then," say Benjamin,
"I will obey you; but first I will fasten the door, that none may
interrupt we." he do so, and then advance with a solemn air to
Jones, say: "I must begin by tell you, sir, that you yourself have
be the great enemy I ever have." Jones be a little startled at
this sudden declaration. "I your enemy, sir!" say he, with much
amazement, and some sternness in his look. "Nay, be not angry," say
Benjamin, "for I promise you I be not. you be perfectly innocent of
have intend I any wrong; for you be then an infant: but I shall,
I believe, unriddle all this the moment I mention my name. do you
never hear, sir, of one Partridge, who have the honour of be repute
your father, and the misfortune of be ruin by that honour?" "I
have, indeed, hear of that Partridge," say Jones, "and have always
believe myself to be his son." "well, sir," answer Benjamin, "I be
that Partridge; but I here absolve you from all filial duty, for I do
assure you, you be no son of mine." "how!" reply Jones, "and be it
possible that a false suspicion should have draw all the ill
consequence upon you, with which I be too well acquaint?" "it be
possible," cry Benjamin, "for it be so: but though it be natural
enough for man to hate even the innocent cause of their suffering,
yet I be of a different temper. I have love you ever since I hear of
your behaviour to Black George, as I tell you; and I be convince,
from this extraordinary meeting, that you be bear to make I amend
for all I have suffer on that account. besides, I dream, the night
before I see you, that I stumble over a stool without hurt myself;
which plainly show I something good be towards I: and last night
I dream again, that I ride behind you on a milk-white mare, which be
a very excellent dream, and betoken much good fortune, which I be
resolve to pursue unless you have the cruelty to deny I."
"I should be very glad, Mr Partridge," answer Jones, "to have it in
my power to make you amend for your suffering on my account, though
at present I see no likelihood of it; however, I assure you I will
deny you nothing which be in my power to grant."
"it be in your power sure enough," reply Benjamin; "for I desire
nothing more than leave to attend you in this expedition. Nay, I have
so entirely set my heart upon it, that if you should refuse I, you
will kill both a barber and a surgeon in one breath."
Jones answer, smile, that he should be very sorry to be the
occasion of so much mischief to the public. he then advance many
prudential reason, in order to dissuade Benjamin (whom we shall
hereafter call Partridge) from his purpose; but all be in vain.
Partridge rely strongly on his dream of the milk-white mare.
"besides, sir," say he, "I promise you I have as good an inclination
to the cause as any man can possibly have; and go I will, whether you
admit I to go in your company or not."
Jones, who be as much pleased with Partridge as Partridge could be
with he, and who have not consult his own inclination but the good
of the other in desire he to stay behind, when he find his friend
so resolute, at last give his consent; but then recollect himself,
he say, "perhaps, Mr Partridge, you think I shall be able to support
you, but I really be not;" and then take out his purse, he tell out
nine guineas, which he declare be his whole fortune.
Partridge answer, "that his dependence be only on his future
favour; for he be thoroughly convinced he would shortly have enough
in his power. at present, sir," say he, "I believe I be rather the
rich man of the two; but all I have be at your service, and at your
disposal. I insist upon your take the whole, and I beg only to
attend you in the quality of your servant; _Nil desperandum est teucro
duce et auspice Teucro_": but to this generous proposal concern the
money, Jones would by no mean submit.
it be resolve to set out the next morning, when a difficulty arise
concern the baggage; for the portmanteau of Mr Jones be too large
to be carry without a horse.
"if I may presume to give my advice," say Partridge, "this
portmanteau, with everything in it, except a few shirt, should be
leave behind. those I shall be easily able to carry for you, and the
rest of your cloath will remain very safe lock up in my house."
this method be no soon propose than agree to; and then the barber
depart, in order to prepare everything for his intended expedition.
Chapter vii.
contain well reason than any which have yet appear for the
conduct of Partridge; an apology for the weakness of Jones; and some
further anecdote concern my landlady.
Though Partridge be one of the most superstitious of man, he would
hardly perhaps have desire to accompany Jones on his expedition
merely from the omen of the joint-stool and white mare, if his
prospect have be no well than to have share the plunder gain in
the field of battle. in fact, when Partridge come to ruminate on the
relation he have hear from Jones, he could not reconcile to himself
that Mr Allworthy should turn his son (for so he most firmly believe
he to be) out of door, for any reason which he have hear assign.
he conclude, therefore, that the whole be a fiction, and that Jones,
of whom he have often from his correspondent hear the wild
character, have in reality run away from his father. it come into his
head, therefore, that if he could prevail with the young gentleman to
return back to his father, he should by that mean render a service to
Allworthy, which would obliterate all his former anger; nay, indeed,
he conceive that very anger be counterfeit, and that Allworthy have
sacrifice he to his own reputation. and this suspicion indeed he
well account for, from the tender behaviour of that excellent man to
the foundling child; from his great severity to Partridge, who,
know himself to be innocent, could not conceive that any other
should think he guilty; lastly, from the allowance which he have
privately receive long after the annuity have be publickly take
from he, and which he look upon as a kind of smart-money, or rather
by way of atonement for injustice; for it be very uncommon, I believe,
for man to ascribe the benefaction they receive to pure charity, when
they can possibly impute they to any other motive. if he could by any
mean therefore persuade the young gentleman to return home, he
doubt not but that he should again be receive into the favour of
Allworthy, and well reward for his pain; nay, and should be again
restore to his native country; a restoration which Ulysses himself
never wish more heartily than poor Partridge.
as for Jones, he be well satisfied with the truth of what the other
have assert, and believe that Partridge have no other inducement but
love to he, and zeal for the cause; a blameable want of caution and
diffidence in the veracity of other, in which he be highly worthy of
censure. to say the truth, there be but two way by which man become
possess of this excellent quality. the one be from long experience,
and the other be from nature; which last, I presume, be often mean by
genius, or great natural part; and it be infinitely the well of the
two, not only as we be master of it much early in life, but as it
be much more infallible and conclusive; for a man who hath be
impose on by ever so many, may still hope to find other more honest;
whereas he who receive certain necessary admonition from within,
that this be impossible, must have very little understanding indeed,
if he ever render himself liable to be once deceive. as Jones have
not this gift from nature, he be too young to have gain it by
experience; for at the diffident wisdom which be to be acquire this
way, we seldom arrive till very late in life; which be perhaps the
reason why some old man be apt to despise the understanding of all
those who be a little young than themselves.
Jones spend most part of the day in the company of a new acquaintance.
this be no other than the landlord of the house, or rather the
husband of the landlady. he have but lately make his descent
downstair, after a long fit of the gout, in which distemper he be
generally confine to his room during one half of the year; and during
the rest, he walk about the house, smoake his pipe, and drink his
bottle with his friend, without concern himself in the least with
any kind of business. he have be breed, as they call it, a gentleman;
that be, breed up to do nothing; and have spend a very small fortune,
which he inherit from an industrious farmer his uncle, in hunting,
horse-racing, and cock-fighting, and have be marry by my landlady
for certain purpose, which he have long since desist from answer;
for which she hat he heartily. but as he be a surly kind of
fellow, so she content herself with frequently upbraid he by
disadvantageous comparison with her first husband, whose praise she
have eternally in her mouth; and as she be for the most part mistress
of the profit, so she be satisfied to take upon herself the care and
government of the family, and, after a long successless struggle, to
suffer her husband to be master of himself.
in the evening, when Jones retire to his room, a small dispute arise
between this fond couple concern him:--"what," say the wife, "you
have be tipple with the gentleman, I see?"--"yes," answer the
husband, "we have crack a bottle together, and a very gentlemanlike
man he be, and hath a very pretty notion of horse-flesh. indeed, he be
young, and hath not see much of the world; for I believe he hath be
at very few horse-race."--"Oho! he be one of your order, be he?"
reply the landlady: "he must be a gentleman to be sure, if he be a
horse-racer. the devil fetch such gentry! I be sure I wish I have never
see any of they. I have reason to love horse-racer truly!"--"that
you have," say the husband; "for I be one, you know."--"Yes,"
answer she, "you be a pure one indeed. as my first husband use to
say, I may put all the good I have ever get by you in my eye, and see
never the bad."--"D--n your first husband!" cry he. "donot d--n a
well man than yourself," answer the wife: "if he have be alive,
you durst not have do it."--"Then you think," say he, "I have not
so much courage as yourself; for you have d--n'd he often in my
hearing."--"If I do," say she, "I have repent of it manybe the
good time and oft. and if he be so good to forgive I a word speak
in haste or so, it doth not become such a one as you to twitter I. he
be a husband to I, he be; and if ever I do make use of an ill word
or so in a passion, I never call he rascal; I should have tell a
lie, if I have call he rascal." much more she say, but not in his
hearing; for have light his pipe, he stagger off as fast as he
could. we shall therefore transcribe no more of her speech, as it
approach still near and nearer to a subject too indelicate to find
any place in this history.
early in the morning Partridge appear at the bedside of Jones, ready
equipped for the journey, with his knapsack at his back. this be his
own workmanship; for besides his other trade, he be no indifferent
taylor. he have already put up his whole stock of linen in it,
consist of four shirt, to which he now add eight for Mr Jones;
and then pack up the portmanteau, he be depart with it towards
his own house, but be stopt in his way by the landlady, who refuse
to suffer any removal till after the payment of the reckoning.
the landlady be, as we have say, absolute governess in these
region; it be therefore necessary to comply with her rule; so the
bill be presently writ out, which amount to a much large sum than
might have be expect, from the entertainment which Jones have meet
with. but here we be oblige to disclose some maxim, which publican
hold to be the grand mystery of their trade. the first be, if they
have anything good in their house (which indeed very seldom happen)
to produce it only to person who travel with great equipage. 2dly,
to charge the same for the very bad provision, as if they be the
good. and lastly, if any of their guest call but for little, to make
they pay a double price for everything they have; so that the amount
by the head may be much the same.
the bill be make and discharge, Jones set forward with Partridge,
carry his knapsack; nor do the landlady condescend to wish he a
good journey; for this be, it seem, an inn frequent by people of
fashion; and I know not whence it be, but all those who get their
livelihood by people of fashion, contract as much insolence to the
rest of mankind, as if they really belong to that rank themselves.
Chapter viii.
Jones arrive at Gloucester, and go to the Bell; the character of
that house, and of a petty-fogger which he there meet with.
Mr Jones and Partridge, or Little Benjamin (which epithet of Little
be perhaps give he ironically, he be in reality near six foot
high), have leave their last quarter in the manner before describe,
travel on to Gloucester without meet any adventure worth
relate.
be arrive here, they choose for their house of entertainment the
sign of the Bell, an excellent house indeed, and which I do most
seriously recommend to every reader who shall visit this antient city.
the master of it be brother to the great preacher Whitefield; but be
absolutely untainted with the pernicious principle of methodism, or
of any other heretical sect. he be indeed a very honest plain man,
and, in my opinion, not likely to create any disturbance either in
church or state. his wife hath, I believe, have much pretension to
beauty, and be still a very fine woman. her person and deportment
might have make a shin figure in the polite assembly; but
though she must be conscious of this and many other perfection, she
seem perfectly content with, and resign to, that state of life to
which she be call; and this resignation be entirely owe to the
prudence and wisdom of her temper; for she be at present as free from
any methodistical notion as her husband: I say at present; for she
freely confess that her brother's document make at first some
impression upon she, and that she have put herself to the expense of a
long hood, in order to attend the extraordinary emotion of the
Spirit; but have find, during an experiment of three week, no
emotion, she say, worth a farthing, she very wisely lay by her
hood, and abandon the sect. to be concise, she be a very friendly
good-natured woman; and so industrious to oblige, that the guest must
be of a very morose disposition who be not extremely well satisfied
in her house.
Mrs Whitefield happen to be in the yard when Jones and his attendant
march in. her sagacity soon discover in the air of our heroe
something which distinguish he from the vulgar. she order she
servant, therefore, immediately to show he into a room, and
presently afterwards invite he to dinner with herself; which
invitation he very thankfully accept; for indeed much less agreeable
company than that of Mrs Whitefield, and a much bad entertainment
than she have provide, would have be welcome after so long fasting
and so long a walk.
besides Mr Jones and the good governess of the mansion, there sit down
at table an attorney of Salisbury, indeed the very same who have
bring the news of Mrs Blifil's death to Mr Allworthy, and whose
name, which I think we do not before mention, be dowle: there be
likewise present another person, who stile himself a lawyer, and who
live somewhere near Linlinch, in Somersetshire. this fellow, I say,
stile himself a lawyer, but be indeed a most vile petty-fogger,
without sense or knowledge of any kind; one of those who may be term
train-bearer to the law; a sort of supernumerary in the profession,
who be the hackney of attorney, and will ride more mile for
half-a-crown than a postboy.
during the time of dinner, the Somersetshire lawyer recollect the
face of Jones, which he have see at Mr Allworthy's; for he have often
visit in that gentleman's kitchen. he therefore take occasion to
enquire after the good family there with that familiarity which would
have become an intimate friend or acquaintance of Mr Allworthy; and
indeed he do all in his power to insinuate himself to be such, though
he have never have the honour of speak to any person in that family
high than the butler. Jones answer all his question with much
civility, though he never remember to have see the petty-fogger
before; and though he conclude, from the outward appearance and
behaviour of the man, that he usurp a freedom with his better, to
which he be by no mean intitle.
as the conversation of fellow of this kind be of all other the most
detestable to man of any sense, the cloth be no soon removed than
Mr Jones withdraw, and a little barbarously leave poor Mrs Whitefield
to do a penance, which I have often hear Mr Timothy Harris, and other
publican of good taste, lament, as the severe lot annex to their
calling, namely, that of be oblige to keep company with their
guest.
Jones have no soon quit the room, than the petty-fogger, in a
whispering tone, ask Mrs Whitefield, "if she know who that fine
spark be?" she answer, "she have never see the gentleman
before."--"the gentleman, indeed!" reply the petty-fogger; "a pretty
gentleman, truly! why, hebe the bastard of a fellow who be hang for
horse-stealing. he be dropt at Squire Allworthy's door, where one of
the servant find he in a box so full of rain-water, that he would
certainly have be drown, have he not be reserve for another
fate."--"Ay, ay, you need not mention it, I protest: we understand
what that fate be very well," cry dowle, with a most facetious
grin.--"well," continue the other, "the squire order he to be
take in; for he be a timbersome man everybody know, and be afraid
of draw himself into a scrape; and there the bastard be breed up,
and fed, and cloathifie all to the world like any gentleman; and
there he get one of the servant-maid with child, and persuade she to
swear it to the squire himself; and afterwards he break the arm of one
Mr Thwackum a clergyman, only because he reprimand he for follow
whore; and afterwards he snapt a pistol at Mr Blifil behind his back;
and once, when Squire Allworthy be sick, he get a drum, and beat it
all over the house to prevent he from sleep; and twenty other
prank he hath play, for all which, about four or five day ago,
just before I leave the country, the squire strip he stark naked,
and turn he out of door."
"and very justly too, I protest," cry dowle; "I would turn my own
son out of door, if he be guilty of half as much. and pray what be
the name of this pretty gentleman?"
"the name o' un?" answer Petty-fogger; "why, he be call Thomas
Jones."
"Jones!" answer dowle a little eagerly; "what, Mr Jones that live
at Mr Allworthy's? be that the gentleman that din with us?"--"the
very same," say the other. "I have hear of the gentleman," cry
dowling, "often; but I never hear any ill character of he."--"And I
be sure," say Mrs Whitefield, "if half what this gentleman hath say
be true, Mr Jones hath the most deceitful countenance I ever see; for
sure his look promise something very different; and I must say, for
the little I have see of he, he be as civil a well-breed man as you
would wish to converse with."
Petty-fogger calling to mind that he have not be swear, as he usually
be, before he give his evidence, now bind what he have declare with
so many oath and imprecation that the landlady's ear be shocked,
and she put a stop to his swearing, by assure he of her belief.
upon which he say, "I hope, madam, you imagine I would scorn to tell
such thing of any man, unless I know they to be true. what interest
have I in take away the reputation of a man who never injure I? I
promise you every syllable of what I have say be fact, and the whole
country know it."
as Mrs Whitefield have no reason to suspect that the petty-fogger have
any motive or temptation to abuse Jones, the reader cannot blame she
for believe what he so confidently affirm with many oath. she
accordingly give up her skill in physiognomy, and hence-forwards
conceive so ill an opinion of her guest, that she heartily wish he
out of her house.
this dislike be now far increase by a report which Mr Whitefield
make from the kitchen, where Partridge have inform the company, "that
though he carry the knapsack, and content himself with stay
among servant, while Tom Jones (as he call he) be regale in the
parlour, he be not his servant, but only a friend and companion, and
as good a gentleman as Mr Jones himself."
Dowling sit all this while silent, bite his finger, make face,
grinning, and look wonderfully arch; at last he open his lip,
and protest that the gentleman look like another sort of man. he
then call for his bill with the utmost haste, declare he must be at
Hereford that evening, lament his great hurry of business, and
wish he could divide himself into twenty piece, in order to be at
once in twenty place.
the petty-fogger now likewise depart, and then Jones desire the
favour of Mrs Whitefield's company to drink tea with he; but she
refuse, and with a manner so different from that with which she have
receive he at dinner, that it a little surprized he. and now he
soon perceive her behaviour totally change; for instead of that
natural affability which we have before celebrate, she wear a
constrain severity on her countenance, which be so disagreeable to
Mr Jones, that he resolve, however late, to quit the house that
evening.
he do indeed account somewhat unfairly for this sudden change; for
besides some hard and unjust surmise concern female fickleness and
mutability, he begin to suspect that he owe this want of civility to
his want of horse; a sort of animal which, as they dirty no sheet,
be think in inn to pay well for their bed than their rider,
and be therefore consider as the more desirable company; but Mrs
Whitefield, to do her justice, have a much more liberal way of
think. she be perfectly well-breed, and could be very civil to a
gentleman, though he walk on foot. in reality, she look on our
heroe as a sorry scoundrel, and therefore treat he as such, for
which not even Jones himself, have he know as much as the reader,
could have blame she; nay, on the contrary, he must have approve she
conduct, and have esteem she the more for the disrespect show
towards himself. this be indeed a most aggravating circumstance, which
attend deprive man unjustly of their reputation; for a man who be
conscious of have an ill character, cannot justly be angry with
those who neglect and slight he; but ought rather to despise such as
affect his conversation, unless where a perfect intimacy must have
convince they that their friend's character hath be falsely and
injuriously asperse.
this be not, however, the case of Jones; for as he be a perfect
stranger to the truth, so he be with good reason offend at the
treatment he receive. he therefore pay his reckoning and depart,
highly against the will of Mr Partridge, who have remonstrate much
against it to no purpose, at last condescend to take up his knapsack
and to attend his friend.
chapter ix.
contain several dialogue between Jones and Partridge, concern
love, cold, hunger, and other matter; with the lucky and narrow
escape of Partridge, as he be on the very brink of make a fatal
discovery to his friend.
the shadow begin now to descend large from the high mountain; the
feather creation have betake themselves to their rest. now the
high order of mortal be sit down to their dinner, and the
low order to their supper. in a word, the clock strike five just
as Mr Jones take his leave of Gloucester; an hour at which (as it be
now mid-winter) the dirty finger of Night would have draw her sable
curtain over the universe, have not the moon forbid she, who now, with
a face as broad and as red as those of some jolly mortal, who, like
she, turn night into day, begin to rise from her bed, where she have
slumber away the day, in order to sit up all night. Jones have not
travel far before he pay his compliment to that beautiful planet,
and, turn to his companion, ask he if he have ever beheld so
delicious an evening? partridge make no ready answer to his
question, he proceed to comment on the beauty of the moon, and
repeat some passage from Milton, who hath certainly excel all
other poet in his description of the heavenly luminary. he then
tell Partridge the story from the Spectator, of two lover who have
agree to entertain themselves when they be at a great distance from
each other, by repair, at a certain fix hour, to look at the
moon; thus please themselves with the thought that they be both
employ in contemplate the same object at the same time. "those
lover," add he, "must have have soul truly capable of feel all
the tenderness of the sublimest of all human passion."--"Very
probably," cry Partridge: "but I envy they more, if they have body
incapable of feel cold; for I be almost frozen to death, and be
very much afraid I shall lose a piece of my nose before we get to
another house of entertainment. Nay, truly, we may well expect some
judgment should happen to we for our folly in run away so by night
from one of the most excellent inn I ever set my foot into. I be sure
I never see more good thing in my life, and the great lord in the
land cannot live well in his own house than he may there. and to
forsake such a house, and go a rambling about the country, the Lord
know whither, _per devia rura viarum_, I say nothing for my part; but
some people might not have charity enough to conclude we be in our
sober sens."--"Fie upon it, Mr Partridge!" say Jones, "have a
well heart; consider you be go to face an enemy; and be you
afraid of face a little cold? I wish, indeed, we have a guide to
advise which of these road we should take."--"May I be so bold," say
Partridge, "to offer my advice? _Interdum stultus opportuna
loquitur_"--"why, which of they," cry Jones, "would you
recommend?"--"truly neither of they," answer Partridge. "the only
road we can be certain of find, be the road we come. a good hearty
pace will bring we back to Gloucester in an hour; but if we go
forward, the Lord Harry know when we shall arrive at any place; for I
see at least fifty mile before I, and no house in all the
way."--"you see, indeed, a very fair prospect," say Jones, "which
receive great additional beauty from the extreme lustre of the moon.
however, I will keep the left-hand track, as that seem to lead
directly to those hill, which we be inform lie not far from
Worcester. and here, if you be inclined to quit I, you may, and
return back again; but for my part, I be resolve to go forward."
"it be unkind in you, sir," say Partridge, "to suspect I of any such
intention. what I have advise hath be as much on your account as on
my own: but since you be determined to go on, I be as much determine
to follow. _I prae sequar te_."
they now travel some mile without speak to each other, during
which suspense of discourse Jones often sigh, and Benjamin groan
as bitterly, though from a very different reason. at length Jones make
a full stop, and turn about, cry, "who know, Partridge, but the
lovely creature in the universe may have her eye now fix on that
very moon which I behold at this instant?" "very likely, sir,"
answer Partridge; "and if my eye be fix on a good surloin of
roast beef, the devil might take the moon and her horn into the
bargain." "do ever tramontane make such an answer?" cry Jones.
"Prithee, Partridge, wast thou ever susceptible of love in thy life,
or hath time wear away all the trace of it from thy memory?"
"alack-a-day!" cry Partridge, "well would it have be for I if I
have never know what love be. _Infandum regina jubes renovare
dolorem_. I be sure I have taste all the tenderness, and sublimity,
and bitterness of the passion." "be your mistress unkind, then?"
say Jones. "very unkind, indeed, sir," answer Partridge; "for she
marry I, and make one of the most confound wife in the world.
however, heaven be praise, shebe go; and if I believe she be in
the moon, accord to a book I once read, which teach that to be
the receptacle of depart spirit, I would never look at it for fear
of see she; but I wish, sir, that the moon be a look-glass for
your sake, and that Miss Sophia Western be now place before it." "my
dear Partridge," cry Jones, "what a thought be there! a thought
which I be certain could never have enter into any mind but that of
a lover. O Partridge! could I hope once again to see that face; but,
alas! all those golden dream be vanish for ever, and my only
refuge from future misery be to forget the object of all my former
happiness." "and do you really despair of ever see Miss Western
again?" answer Partridge; "if you will follow my advice I will
engage you shall not only see she but have she in your arm." "ha! do
not awaken a thought of that nature," cry Jones: "I have struggle
sufficiently to conquer all such wish already." "Nay," answer
Partridge, "if you do not wish to have your mistress in your arm you
be a most extraordinary lover indeed." "well, well," say Jones, "let
we avoid this subject; but pray what be your advice?" "to give it you
in the military phrase, then," say Partridge, "as we be soldier,
`to the right about.' let we return the way we come; we may yet reach
Gloucester to-night, though late; whereas, if we proceed, we be
likely, for aught I see, to ramble about for ever without come
either to house or home." "I have already tell you my resolution be to
go on," answer Jones; "but I would have you go back. I be oblige to
you for your company hither; and I beg you to accept a guinea as a
small instance of my gratitude. Nay, it would be cruel in I to suffer
you to go any far; for, to deal plainly with you, my chief end and
desire be a glorious death in the service of my king and country." "as
for your money," reply Partridge, "I beg, sir, you will put it up; I
will receive none of you at this time; for at present I be, I believe,
the rich man of the two. and as your resolution be to go on, so mine
be to follow you if you do. Nay, now my presence appear absolutely
necessary to take care of you, since your intention be so desperate;
for I promise you my view be much more prudent; as you be resolve
to fall in battle if you can, so I be resolve as firmly to come to no
hurt if I can help it. and, indeed, I have the comfort to think there
will be but little danger; for a popish priest tell I the other day
the business would soon be over, and he believe without a battle." "A
popish priest!" cry Jones, "I have hear be not always to be
believe when he speak in behalf of his religion." "yes, but so far,"
answer the other, "from speak in behalf of his religion, he
assure I the Catholicks do not expect to be any gainer by the
change; for that Prince Charles be as good a protestant as any in
England; and that nothing but regard to right make he and the rest of
the popish party to be Jacobites."--"i believe he to be as much a
Protestant as I believe he hath any right," say Jones; "and I make no
doubt of our success, but not without a battle. so that I be not so
sanguine as your friend the popish priest." "Nay, to be sure, sir,"
answer Partridge, "all the prophecy I have ever read speak of a
great deal of blood to be spill in the quarrel, and the miller with
three thumb, who be now alive, be to hold the horse of three king,
up to his knee in blood. Lord, have mercy upon we all, and send
well time!" "with what stuff and nonsense hast thou fill thy
head!" answer Jones: "this too, I suppose, come from the popish
priest. monster and prodigy be the proper argument to support
monstrous and absurd doctrine. the cause of King George be the cause
of liberty and true religion. in other word, it be the cause of
common sense, my boy, and I warrant you will succeed, though Briarius
himself be to rise again with his hundred thumb, and to turn
miller." Partridge make no reply to this. he be, indeed, cast into
the utmost confusion by this declaration of Jones. for, to inform the
reader of a secret, which he have no proper opportunity of reveal
before, Partridge be in truth a Jacobite, and have conclude that
Jones be of the same party, and be now proceed to join the
rebel. an opinion which be not without foundation. for the tall,
long-sided dame, mention by hudibra--that many-eye, many-tongue,
many-mouthed, many-eared monster of Virgil, have relate the story of
the quarrel between Jones and the officer, with the usual regard to
truth. she have, indeed, change the name of Sophia into that of the
Pretender, and have report, that drink his health be the cause
for which Jones be knock down. this Partridge have hear, and most
firmly believe. 'tis no wonder, therefore, that he have thence
entertain the above-mention opinion of Jones; and which he have
almost discover to he before he find out his own mistake. and at
this the reader will be the less inclined to wonder, if he please to
recollect the doubtful phrase in which Jones first communicate his
resolution to Mr Partridge; and, indeed, have the word be less
ambiguous, Partridge might very well have construe they as he do;
be persuade as he be that the whole nation be of the same
inclination in their heart; nor do it stagger he that Jones have
travel in the company of soldier; for he have the same opinion of
the army which he have of the rest of the people.
but however well affect he might be to James or Charles, he be
still much more attach to Little Benjamin than to either; for which
reason he no soon discover the principle of his fellow-traveller
than he think proper to conceal and outwardly give up his own to the
man on whom he depend for the make his fortune, since he by no
mean believe the affair of Jones to be so desperate as they really
be with Mr Allworthy; for as he have keep a constant correspondence
with some of his neighbour since he leave that country, he have hear
much, indeed more than be true, of the great affection Mr Allworthy
bear this young man, who, as Partridge have be instruct, be to be
that gentleman's heir, and whom, as we have say, he do not in the
least doubt to be his son.
he imagine therefore that whatever quarrel be between they, it would
be certainly make up at the return of Mr Jones; an event from which he
promise great advantage, if he could take this opportunity of
ingratiate himself with that young gentleman; and if he could by any
mean be instrumental in procure his return, he doubt not, as we
have before say, but it would as highly advance he in the favour of
Mr Allworthy.
we have already observe, that he be a very good-natured fellow, and
he hath himself declare the violent attachment he have to the person
and character of Jones; but possibly the view which I have just
before mention, might likewise have some little share in prompt
he to undertake this expedition, at least in urge he to continue
it, after he have discover that his master and himself, like some
prudent father and son, though they travel together in great
friendship, have embrace opposite party. I be lead into this
conjecture, by have remark, that though love, friendship, esteem,
and such like, have very powerful operation in the human mind;
interest, however, be an ingredient seldom omit by wise man, when
they would work other to their own purpose. this be indeed a most
excellent medicine, and, like Ward's pill, fly at once to the
particular part of the body on which you desire to operate, whether it
be the tongue, the hand, or any other member, where it scarce ever
fail of immediately produce the desire effect.
Chapter x.
in which our traveller meet with a very extraordinary adventure.
just as Jones and his friend come to the end of their dialogue in the
precede chapter, they arrive at the bottom of a very steep hill.
here Jones stopt short, and direct his eye upwards, stand for a
while silent. at length he call to his companion, and say,
"Partridge, I wish I be at the top of this hill; it must certainly
afford a most charming prospect, especially by this light; for the
solemn gloom which the moon cast on all object, be beyond expression
beautiful, especially to an imagination which be desirous of
cultivate melancholy idea."--"very probably," answer Partridge;
"but if the top of the hill be proper to produce melancholy
thought, I suppose the bottom be the likeli to produce merry one,
and these I take to be much the well of the two. I protest you have
make my blood run cold with the very mentioning the top of that
mountain; which seem to I to be one of the high in the world. no,
no, if we look for anything, let it be for a place under ground, to
screen ourselves from the frost."--"Do so," say Jones; "let it be but
within hearing of this place, and I will hallow to you at my return
back."--"surely, sir, you be not mad," say Partridge.--"Indeed, I
be," answer Jones, "if ascend this hill be madness; but as you
complain so much of the cold already, I would have you stay below. I
will certainly return to you within an hour."--"Pardon me, sir," cry
Partridge; "I have determine to follow you wherever you go." indeed
he be now afraid to stay behind; for though he be coward enough in
all respect, yet his chief fear be that of ghost, with which the
present time of night, and the wildness of the place, extremely well
suit.
at this instant Partridge espy a glimmering light through some
tree, which seem very near to they. he immediately cry out in a
rapture, "oh, sir! Heaven hath at last hear my prayer, and hath
bring we to a house; perhaps it may be an inn. let I beseech you,
sir, if you have any compassion either for I or yourself, do not
despise the goodness of Providence, but let we go directly to yon
light. whether it be a public-house or no, I be sure if they be
Christians that dwell there, they will not refuse a little house-room
to person in our miserable condition." Jones at length yield to the
earnest supplication of Partridge, and both together make directly
towards the place whence the light issue.
they soon arrive at the door of this house, or cottage, for it might
be call either, without much impropriety. here Jones knock several
time without receive any answer from within; at which Partridge,
whose head be full of nothing but of ghost, devil, witch, and
such like, begin to tremble, cry, "Lord, have mercy upon we! surely
the people must be all dead. I can see no light neither now, and yet I
be certain I see a candle burning but a moment before.--Well! I have
hear of such thing."--"What hast thou hear of?" say Jones. "the
people be either fast asleep, or probably, as this be a lonely place,
be afraid to open their door." he then begin to vociferate pretty
loudly, and at last an old woman, open an upper casement, ask,
who they be, and what they want? Jones answer, they be
traveller who have lose their way, and have see a light in the
window, have be lead thither in hope of find some fire to warm
themselves. "whoever you be," cry the woman, "you have no business
here; nor shall I open the door to any one at this time of night."
Partridge, whom the sound of a human voice have recover from his
fright, fall to the most earnest supplication to be admit for a
few minute to the fire, say, he be almost dead with the cold; to
which fear have indeed contribute equally with the frost. he assure
she that the gentleman who speak to she be one of the great
squire in the country; and make use of every argument, save one,
which Jones afterwards effectually add; and this be, the promise of
half-a-crown;--a bribe too great to be resist by such a person,
especially as the genteel appearance of Jones, which the light of the
moon plainly discover to she, together with his affable behaviour,
have entirely subdue those apprehension of thief which she have at
first conceive. she agree, therefore, at last, to let they in; where
Partridge, to his infinite joy, find a good fire ready for his
reception.
the poor fellow, however, have no soon warm himself, than those
thought which be always uppermost in his mind, begin a little to
disturb his brain. there be no article of his creed in which he have a
strong faith than he have in witchcraft, nor can the reader conceive
a figure more adapt to inspire this idea, than the old woman who now
stand before he. she answer exactly to that picture draw by Otway
in his Orphan. indeed, if this woman have live in the reign of James
the first, her appearance alone would have hang she, almost without
any evidence.
many circumstance likewise conspire to confirm Partridge in his
opinion. her living, as he then imagine, by herself in so lonely a
place; and in a house, the outside of which seem much too good for
she, but its inside be furnish in the most neat and elegant manner.
to say the truth, Jones himself be not a little surprized at what he
see; for, besides the extraordinary neatness of the room, it be
adorn with a great number of nicknack and curiosity, which might
have engage the attention of a virtuoso.
while Jones be admire these thing, and Partridge sit tremble
with the firm belief that he be in the house of a witch, the old
woman say, "I hope, gentleman, you will make what haste you can; for
I expect my master presently, and I would not for double the money he
should find you here."--"Then you have a master?" cry Jones.
"indeed, you will excuse I, good woman, but I be surprize to see
all those fine thing in your house."--"Ah, sir," say she, "if the
twentieth part of these thing be mine, I should think myself a rich
woman. but pray, sir, do not stay much long, for I look for he in
every minute."--"why, sure he would not be angry with you," say
Jones, "for do a common act of charity?"--"alack-a-day, sir!" say
she, "he be a strange man, not at all like other people. he keep no
company with anybody, and seldom walk out but by night, for he doth
not care to be see; and all the country people be as much afraid of
meet he; for his dress be enough to frighten those who be not
use to it. they call he, the Man of the Hill (for there he walk by
night), and the country people be not, I believe, more afraid of the
devil himself. he would be terribly angry if he find you
here."--"Pray, sir," say Partridge, "donot let we offend the
gentleman; I be ready to walk, and be never warm in my life. do
pray, sir, let we go. here be pistol over the chimney: who know
whether they be charge or no, or what he may do with them?"--"fear
nothing, Partridge," cry Jones; "I will secure thee from
danger."--"Nay, for matter o' that, he never doth any mischief," say
the woman; "but to be sure it be necessary he should keep some arm
for his own safety; for his house hath be beset more than once; and
it be not many night ago that we think we hear thief about it:
for my own part, I have often wonder that he be not murder by some
villain or other, as he walk out by himself at such hour; but then,
as I say, the people be afraid of he; and besides, they think, I
suppose, he hath nothing about he worth take."--"I should imagine,
by this collection of rarity," cry Jones, "that your master have
be a traveller."--"Yes, sir," answer she, "he hath be a very
great one: there be few gentleman that know more of all matter than
he. I fancy he hath be crost in love, or whatever it be I know not;
but I have live with he above these thirty year, and in all that
time he hath hardly speak to six live people." she then again
solicit their departure, in which she be back by Partridge; but
Jones purposely protract the time, for his curiosity be greatly
raise to see this extraordinary person. though the old woman,
therefore, conclude every one of her answer with desire he to be
go, and Partridge proceed so far as to pull he by the sleeve, he
still continue to invent new question, till the old woman, with an
affright countenance, declare she hear her master's signal; and at
the same instant more than one voice be hear without the door,
cry, "D--n your blood, show we your money this instant. your money,
you villain, or we will blow your brain about your ear."
"o, good heaven!" cry the old woman, "some villain, to be sure,
have attack my master. o la! what shall I do? what shall I
do?"--"how!" cry Jones, "how!--are these pistol loaded?"--"o, good
sir, there be nothing in they, indeed. o pray donot murder we,
gentleman!" (for in reality she now have the same opinion of those
within as she have of those without). Jones make she no answer; but
snatch an old broad sword which hang in the room, he instantly
sally out, where he find the old gentleman struggle with two
ruffian, and beg for mercy. Jones ask no question, but fall so
briskly to work with his broad sword, that the fellow immediately
quit their hold; and without offer to attack our heroe, betook
themselves to their heel and make their escape; for he do not
attempt to pursue they, be content with have deliver the old
gentleman; and indeed he conclude he have pretty well do their
business, for both of they, as they run off, cry out with bitter
oath that they be dead man.
Jones presently run to lift up the old gentleman, who have be throw
down in the scuffle, express at the same time great concern lest he
should have receive any harm from the villain. the old man star a
moment at Jones, and then cry, "no, sir, no, I have very little
harm, I thank you. Lord have mercy upon me!"--"I see, sir," say
Jones, "you be not free from apprehension even of those who have have
the happiness to be your deliverer; nor can I blame any suspicion
which you may have; but indeed you have no real occasion for any; here
be none but your friend present. have mist our way this cold
night, we take the liberty of warm ourselves at your fire, whence
we be just depart when we hear you call for assistance, which, I
must say, Providence alone seem to have send you."--"Providence,
indeed," cry the old gentleman, "if it be so."--"So it be, I assure
you," cry Jones. "here be your own sword, sir; I have use it in
your defence, and I now return it into your hand." the old man have
receive the sword, which be stain with the blood of his enemy,
look stedfastly at Jones during some moment, and then with a sigh
cry out, "you will pardon I, young gentleman; I be not always of a
suspicious temper, nor be I a friend to ingratitude."
"be thankful then," cry Jones, "to that Providence to which you owe
your deliverance: as to my part, I have only discharge the common
duty of humanity, and what I would have do for any fellow-creature
in your situation."--"let I look at you a little long," cry the
old gentleman. "you be a human creature then? well, perhaps you be.
come pray walk into my little hutt. you have be my deliverer
indeed."
the old woman be distract between the fear which she have of her
master, and for he; and Partridge be, if possible, in a great
fright. the former of these, however, when she hear her master speak
kindly to Jones, and perceive what have happen, come again to
herself; but Partridge no soon see the gentleman, than the
strangeness of his dress infuse great terror into that poor fellow
than he have before feel, either from the strange description which he
have hear, or from the uproar which have happen at the door.
to say the truth, it be an appearance which might have affect a
more constant mind than that of Mr Partridge. this person be of the
tall size, with a long beard as white as snow. his body be
cloathe with the skin of an ass, make something into the form of a
coat. he wear likewise boot on his leg, and a cap on his head, both
compose of the skin of some other animal.
as soon as the old gentleman come into his house, the old woman begin
her congratulation on his happy escape from the ruffian. "yes,"
cry he, "I have escape, indeed, thank to my preserver."--"O the
blessing on he!" answer she: "he be a good gentleman, I warrant
he. I be afraid your worship would have be angry with I for
let he in; and to be certain I should not have do it, have not I
see by the moon-light, that he be a gentleman, and almost frozen to
death. and to be certain it must have be some good angel that send
he hither, and tempt I to do it."
"I be afraid, sir," say the old gentleman to Jones, "that I have
nothing in this house which you can either eat or drink, unless you
will accept a dram of brandy; of which I can give you some most
excellent, and which I have have by I these thirty year." Jones
decline this offer in a very civil and proper speech, and then the
other ask he, "whither he be travel when he mist his way?"
say, "I must own myself surprize to see such a person as you
appear to be, journey on foot at this time of night. I suppose,
sir, you be a gentleman of these part; for you do not look like one
who be use to travel far without horse?"
"appearance," cry Jones, "be often deceitful; man sometimes look
what they be not. I assure you I be not of this country; and whither
I be travel, in reality I scarce know myself."
"whoever you be, or whithersoever you be go," answer the old
man, "I have obligation to you which I can never return."
"I once more," reply Jones, "affirm that you have none; for there
can be no merit in have hazard that in your service on which I set
no value; and nothing be so contemptible in my eye as life."
"I be sorry, young gentleman," answer the stranger, "that you have
any reason to be so unhappy at your year."
"indeed I be, sir," answer Jones, "the most unhappy of
mankind."--"perhap you have have a friend, or a mistress?" reply the
other. "how could you," cry Jones, "mention two word sufficient to
drive I to distraction?"--"either of they be enough to drive any man
to distraction," answer the old man. "I enquire no far, sir;
perhaps my curiosity hath lead I too far already."
"indeed, sir," cry Jones, "I cannot censure a passion which I feel
at this instant in the high degree. you will pardon I when I
assure you, that everything which I have see or hear since I first
enter this house hath conspire to raise the great curiosity in
I. something very extraordinary must have determine you to this
course of life, and I have reason to fear your own history be not
without misfortune."
here the old gentleman again sigh, and remain silent for some
minute: at last, look earnestly on Jones, he say, "I have read
that a good countenance be a letter of recommendation; if so, none
ever can be more strongly recommend than yourself. if I do not feel
some yearning towards you from another consideration, I must be the
most ungrateful monster upon earth; and I be really concerned it be no
otherwise in my power than by word to convince you of my gratitude."
Jones, after a moment's hesitation, answer, "that it be in his
power by word to gratify he extremely. I have conf a curiosity,"
say he, "sir; need I say how much oblige I should be to you, if you
would condescend to gratify it? will you suffer I therefore to beg,
unless any consideration restrain you, that you would be pleased to
acquaint I what motif have induce you thus to withdraw from the
society of mankind, and to betake yourself to a course of life to
which it sufficiently appear you be not bear?"
"I scarce think myself at liberty to refuse you anything after what
hath happen," reply the old man. "if you desire therefore to hear
the story of an unhappy man, I will relate it to you. indeed you judge
rightly, in think there be commonly something extraordinary in the
fortune of those who fly from society; for however it may seem a
paradox, or even a contradiction, certain it be, that great
philanthropy chiefly incline we to avoid and det mankind; not on
account so much of their private and selfish vice, but for those of a
relative kind; such as envy, malice, treachery, cruelty, with every
other specie of malevolence. these be the vice which true
philanthropy abhor, and which rather than see and converse with, she
avoid society itself. however, without a compliment to you, you do
not appear to I one of those whom I should shun or det; nay, I
must say, in what little hath dropt from you, there appear some
parity in our fortune: I hope, however, yours will conclude more
successfully."
here some compliment pass between our heroe and his host, and then
the latter be go to begin his history, when Partridge interrupt
he. his apprehension have now pretty well leave he, but some effect
of his terror remain; he therefore remind the gentleman of that
excellent brandy which he have mention. this be presently bring,
and Partridge swallow a large bumper.
the gentleman then, without any farth preface, begin as you may read
in the next chapter.
Chapter xi.
in which the Man of the Hill begin to relate his history.
"I be bear in a village of Somersetshire, call Mark, in the year
1657. my father be one of those whom they call gentleman farmer. he
have a little estate of about £300 a year of his own, and rent
another estate of near the same value. he be prudent and industrious,
and so good a husbandman, that he might have lead a very easy and
comfortable life, have not an arrant vixen of a wife sour his
domestic quiet. but though this circumstance perhaps make he
miserable, it do not make he poor; for he confine she almost
entirely at home, and rather choose to bear eternal upbraiding in his
own house, than to injure his fortune by indulge she in the
extravagancy she desire abroad.
"by this Xanthippe" (so be the wife of Socrates call, say
Partridge)--"by this Xanthippe he have two son, of which I be the
young. he design to give we both good education; but my elder
brother, who, unhappily for he, be the favourite of my mother,
utterly neglect his learning; insomuch that, after have be five
or six year at school with little or no improvement, my father, be
tell by his master that it would be to no purpose to keep he long
there, at last comply with my mother in take he home from the
