hand of that tyrant, as she call his master; though indeed he give
the lad much less correction than his idleness deserve, but much
more, it seem, than the young gentleman like, who constantly
complain to his mother of his severe treatment, and she as
constantly give he a hearing."
"yes, yes," cry Partridge, "I have see such mother; I have be
abuse myself by they, and very unjustly; such parent deserve
correction as much as their child."
Jones chid the pedagogue for his interruption, and then the stranger
proceed.
"my brother now, at the age of fifteen, bade adieu to all learning,
and to everything else but to his dog and gun; with which latter he
become so expert, that, though perhaps you may think it incredible, he
could not only hit a standing mark with great certainty, but hath
actually shoot a crow as it be fly in the air. he be likewise
excellent at find a hare sitting, and be soon repute one of the
good sportsman in the country; a reputation which both he and his
mother enjoy as much as if he have be think the fine scholar.
"the situation of my brother make I at first think my lot the hard,
in be continue at school: but I soon change my opinion; for as I
advance pretty fast in learn, my labour become easy, and my
exercise so delightful, that holiday be my most unpleasant time;
for my mother, who never love I, now apprehend that I have the
great share of my father's affection, and finding, or at least
thinking, that I be more take notice of by some gentleman of
learning, and particularly by the parson of the parish, than my
brother, she now hat my sight, and make home so disagreeable to I,
that what be call by school-boy Black Monday, be to I the whitest
in the whole year.
"have at length go through the school at Taunton, I be thence
remove to Exeter College in Oxford, where I remain four year; at
the end of which an accident take I off entirely from my study; and
hence, I may truly date the rise of all which happen to I
afterwards in life.
"there be at the same college with myself one Sir George Gresham, a
young fellow who be intitle to a very considerable fortune, which he
be not, by the will of his father, to come into full possession of
till he arrive at the age of twenty-five. however, the liberality of
his guardian give he little cause to regret the abundant caution of
his father; for they allow he five hundred pound a year while he
remain at the university, where he keep his horse and his whore,
and live as wicked and as profligate a life as he could have do have
he be never so entirely master of his fortune; for besides the five
hundred a year which he receive from his guardian, he find mean to
spend a thousand more. he be above the age of twenty-one, and have no
difficulty in gain what credit he please.
"this young fellow, among many other tolerable bad quality, have one
very diabolical. he have a great delight in destroy and ruin the
youth of inferior fortune, by draw they into expense which they
could not afford so well as himself; and the well, and worthy, and
soberer any young man be, the great pleasure and triumph have he in
his destruction. thus act the character which be record of the
devil, and go about seek whom he might devour.
"it be my misfortune to fall into an acquaintance and intimacy with
this gentleman. my reputation of diligence in my study make I a
desirable object of his mischievous intention; and my own inclination
make it sufficiently easy for he to effect his purpose; for though I
have apply myself with much industry to book, in which I take great
delight, there be other pleasure in which I be capable of take
much great; for I be high-mettle, have a violent flow of animal
spirit, be a little ambitious, and extremely amorous.
"I have not long contract an intimacy with Sir George before I become
a partaker of all his pleasure; and when I be once enter on that
scene, neither my inclination nor my spirit would suffer I to play an
under part. I be second to none of the company in any act of
debauchery; nay, I soon distinguish myself so notably in all riot
and disorder, that my name generally stand first in the roll of
delinquent; and instead of be lament as the unfortunate pupil of
Sir George, I be now accuse as the person who have mislead and
debauch that hopeful young gentleman; for though he be the
ringleader and promoter of all the mischief, he be never so
consider. I fall at last under the censure of the vice-chancellor,
and very narrowly escape expulsion.
"you will easily believe, sir, that such a life as I be now describe
must be incompatible with my further progress in learn; and that in
proportion as I addict myself more and more to loose pleasure, I
must grow more and more remiss in application to my study. this be
truly the consequence; but this be not all. my expense now greatly
exceed not only my former income, but those addition which I
extort from my poor generous father, on pretence of sum be
necessary for prepare for my approach degree of batchelor of
art. these demand, however, grow at last so frequent and exorbitant,
that my father by slow degree open his ear to the account which
he receive from many quarter of my present behaviour, and which my
mother fail not to echo very faithfully and loudly; add, `ay,
this be the fine gentleman, the scholar who doth so much honour to his
family, and be to be the making of it. I think what all this
learning would come to. he be to be the ruin of we all, I find, after
his eld brother hath be deny necessary for his sake, to
perfect his education forsooth, for which he be to pay we such
interest: I think what the interest would come to,' with much more
of the same kind; but I have, I believe, satisfy you with this
taste.
"my father, therefore, begin now to return remonstrance instead of
money to my demand, which bring my affair perhaps a little soon
to a crisis; but have he remit I his whole income, you will imagine
it could have suffice a very short time to support one who keep pace
with the expense of Sir George Gresham.
"it be more than possible that the distress I be now in for money,
and the impracticability of go on in this manner, might have
restore I at once to my sens and to my study, have I open my
eye before I become involved in debt from which I see no hope of
ever extricate myself. this be indeed the great art of Sir George,
and by which he accomplish the ruin of many, whom he afterwards
laugh at as fool and coxcomb, for vie, as he call it, with a
man of his fortune. to bring this about, he would now and then advance
a little money himself, in order to support the credit of the
unfortunate youth with other people; till, by mean of that very
credit, he be irretrievably undo.
"my mind be by these mean grow as desperate as my fortune, there
be scarce a wickedness which I do not meditate, in order for my
relief. self-murder itself become the subject of my serious
deliberation; and I have certainly resolve on it, have not a more
shameful, though perhaps less sinful, think expel it from my
head."--Here he hesitate a moment, and then cry out, "I protest, so
many year have not wash away the shame of this act, and I shall
blush while I relate it." Jones desire he to pass over anything that
might give he pain in the relation; but Partridge eagerly cry out,
"oh, pray, sir, let we hear this; I have rather hear this than all the
rest; as I hope to be save, I will never mention a word of it." Jones
be go to rebuke he, but the stranger prevent it by proceed
thus: "I have a chum, a very prudent, frugal young lad, who, though he
have no very large allowance, have by his parsimony heap up upwards of
forty guineas, which I know he keep in his escritore. I take therefore
an opportunity of purloin his key from his breech-pocket, while
he be asleep, and thus make myself master of all his riches: after
which I again convey his key into his pocket, and counterfeit
sleep--though I never once close my eye, lie in bed till after he
arise and go to prayer--an exercise to which I have long be
unaccustomed.
"timorous thief, by extreme caution, often subject themselves to
discovery, which those of a bold kind escape. thus it happen to
I; for have I boldly break open his escritore, I have, perhaps, escape
even his suspicion; but as it be plain that the person who rob he
have possess himself of his key, he have no doubt, when he first
miss his money, but that his chum be certainly the thief. now as he
be of a fearful disposition, and much my inferior in strength, and I
believe in courage, he do not dare to confront I with my guilt, for
fear of bad bodily consequence which might happen to he. he
repair therefore immediately to the vice-chancellor, and upon
swear to the robbery, and to the circumstance of it, very easily
obtain a warrant against one who have now so bad a character through
the whole university.
"luckily for I, I lay out of the college the next evening; for that
day I attend a young lady in a chaise to Witney, where we staid all
night, and in our return, the next morning, to Oxford, I meet one of my
crony, who acquaint I with sufficient news concern myself to
make I turn my horse another way."
"pray, sir, do he mention anything of the warrant?" say Partridge.
but Jones beg the gentleman to proceed without regard any
impertinent question; which he do as follows:--
"have now abandon all thought of return to Oxford, the next
thing which offer itself be a journey to London. I impart this
intention to my female companion, who at first remonstrate against
it; but upon produce my wealth, she immediately consent. we then
strike across the country, into the great Cirencester road, and make
such haste, that we spend the next evening, save one, in London.
"when you consider the place where I now be, and the company with
whom I be, you will, I fancy, conceive that a very short time bring
I to an end of that sum of which I have so iniquitously possess
myself.
"I be now reduce to a much high degree of distress than before:
the necessary of life begin to be number among my want; and what
make my case still the more grievous be, that my paramour, of whom I
be now grow immoderately fond, share the same distress with
myself. to see a woman you love in distress; to be unable to relieve
she, and at the same time to reflect that you have bring she into
this situation, be perhaps a curse of which no imagination can
represent the horror to those who have not feel it."--"i believe it
from my soul," cry Jones, "and I pity you from the bottom of my
heart:" he then take two or three disorderly turn about the room, and
at last beg pardon, and fling himself into his chair, cry, "I
thank Heaven, I have escape that!"
"this circumstance," continue the gentleman, "so severely aggravate
the horror of my present situation, that they become absolutely
intolerable. I could with less pain endure the rag in my own
natural unsatisfie appetite, even hunger or thirst, than I could
submit to leave ungratified the most whimsical desire of a woman on
whom I so extravagantly doate, that, though I know she have be the
mistress of half my acquaintance, I firmly intend to marry she. but
the good creature be unwilling to consent to an action which the
world might think so much to my disadvantage. and as, possibly, she
compassionate the daily anxiety which she must have perceive I
suffer on her account, she resolve to put an end to my distress. she
soon, indeed, find mean to relieve I from my troublesome and
perplexed situation; for while I be distract with various
invention to supply she with pleasure, she very kindly--betray I
to one of her former lover at Oxford, by whose care and diligence I
be immediately apprehend and commit to gaol.
"here I first begin seriously to reflect on the miscarriage of my
former life; on the error I have be guilty of; on the misfortune
which I have bring on myself; and on the grief which I must have
occasion to one of the good of father. when I add to all these
the perfidy of my mistress, such be the horror of my mind, that life,
instead of be long desirable, grow the object of my abhorrence;
and I could have gladly embrace death as my dear friend, if it have
offer itself to my choice unattended by shame.
"the time of the assize soon come, and I be remove by habeas corpus
to Oxford, where I expect certain conviction and condemnation; but,
to my great surprize, none appear against I, and I be, at the end
of the session, discharge for want of prosecution. in short, my chum
have leave Oxford, and whether from indolence, or from what other motive
I be ignorant, have decline concern himself any far in the
affair."
"perhaps," cry Partridge, "he do not care to have your blood upon
his hand; and he be in the right on't. if any person be to be
hang upon my evidence, I should never be able to lie alone
afterwards, for fear of see his ghost."
"I shall shortly doubt, Partridge," say Jones, "whether thou art more
brave or wise."--"you may laugh at I, sir, if you please," answer
Partridge; "but if you will hear a very short story which I can tell,
and which be most certainly true, perhaps you may change your opinion.
in the parish where I be born--" here Jones would have silence he;
but the stranger intercede that he might be permit to tell his
story, and in the meantime promise to recollect the remainder of his
own.
Partridge then proceed thus: "in the parish where I be bear, there
live a farmer whose name be Bridle, and he have a son name Francis,
a good hopeful young fellow: I be at the grammar-school with he,
where I remember he be get into Ovid's Epistles, and he could
construe you three line together sometimes without look into a
dictionary. besides all this, he be a very good lad, never miss
church o' Sundays, and be reckon one of the good psalm-singer in
the whole parish. he would indeed now and then take a cup too much,
and that be the only fault he have."--"Well, but come to the ghost,"
cry Jones. "never fear, sir; I shall come to he soon enough,"
answer Partridge. "you must know, then, that farmer Bridle lose a
mare, a sorrel one, to the good of my remembrance; and so it fall out
that this young Francis shortly afterward be at a fair at Hindon,
and as I think it be on--, I cannot remember the day; and be as he
be, what should he happen to meet but a man upon his father's mare.
Frank call out presently, stop thief; and it be in the middle of
the fair, it be impossible, you know, for the man to make his escape.
so they apprehend he and carry he before the justice: I remember
it be Justice Willoughby, of Noyle, a very worthy good gentleman; and
he commit he to prison, and bind Frank in a recognisance, I think
they call it--a hard word compound of _re_ and _cognosco_; but it
differ in its meaning from the use of the simple, as many other
compound do. well, at last down come my Lord Justice Page to hold the
assize; and so the fellow be have up, and Frank be have up for a
witness. to be sure, I shall never forget the face of the judge, when
he begin to ask he what he have to say against the prisoner. he make
poor Frank tremble and shake in his shoe. `well you, fellow,' say my
lord, `what have you to say? donot stand hum and hawing, but speak
out.' but, however, he soon turn altogether as civil to Frank, and
begin to thunder at the fellow; and when he ask he if he have
anything to say for himself, the fellow say, he have find the horse.
`ay!' answer the judge, `thou art a lucky fellow: I have travel
the circuit these forty year, and never find a horse in my life: but
Iwill tell thee what, friend, thou wast more lucky than thou didst know
of; for thou didst not only find a horse, but a halter too, I promise
thee.' to be sure, I shall never forget the word. upon which everybody
fall a laughing, as how could they help it? nay, and twenty other
jest he make, which I cannot remember now. there be something about
his skill in horse-flesh which make all the folk laugh. to be
certain, the judge must have be a very brave man, as well as a man
of much learning. it be indeed charming sport to hear trial upon life
and death. one thing I own I think a little hard, that the
prisoner's counsel be not suffer to speak for he, though he
desire only to be hear one very short word, but my lord would not
hearken to he, though he suffer a counsellor to talk against he
for above half-an-hour. I think it hard, I own, that there should be
so many of they; my lord, and the court, and the jury, and the
counsellor, and the witness, all upon one poor man, and he too in
chain. well, the fellow be hang, as to be sure it could be no
otherwise, and poor Frank could never be easy about it. he never be
in the dark alone, but he fancy he see the fellow's spirit."--"Well,
and be this thy story?" cry Jones. "no, no," answer Partridge. "o
Lord have mercy upon I! I be just now come to the matter; for one
night, come from the alehouse, in a long, narrow, dark lane, there
he run directly up against he; and the spirit be all in white, and
fall upon Frank; and Frank, who be a sturdy lad, fall upon the spirit
again, and there they have a tussel together, and poor Frank be
dreadfully beat: indeed he make a shift at last to crawl home; but
what with the beating, and what with the fright, he lie ill above a
fortnight; and all this be most certainly true, and the whole parish
will bear witness to it."
the stranger smile at this story, and Jones burst into a loud fit of
laughter; upon which Partridge cry, "ay, you may laugh, sir; and so
do some other, particularly a squire, who be think to be no well
than an atheist; who, forsooth, because there be a calf with a white
face find dead in the same lane the next morning, would fain have it
that the battle be between Frank and that, as if a calf would set
upon a man. besides, Frank tell I he know it to be a spirit, and
could swear to he in any court in Christendom; and he have not drank
above a quart or two or such a matter of liquor, at the time. Lud have
mercy upon we, and keep we all from dip our hand in blood, I
say!"
"well, sir," say Jones to the stranger, "Mr Partridge hath finish
his story, and I hope will give you no future interruption, if you
will be so kind to proceed." he then resume his narration; but as he
hath take breath for a while, we think proper to give it to our
reader, and shall therefore put an end to this chapter.
Chapter xii.
in which the Man of the Hill continue his history.
"I have now regain my liberty," say the stranger; "but I have lose my
reputation; for there be a wide difference between the case of a man
who be barely acquit of a crime in a court of justice, and of he
who be acquit in his own heart, and in the opinion of the people. I
be conscious of my guilt, and ashame to look any one in the face; so
resolve to leave Oxford the next morning, before the daylight
discover I to the eye of any beholder.
"when I have get clear of the city, it first enter into my head to
return home to my father, and endeavour to obtain his forgiveness; but
as I have no reason to doubt his knowledge of all which have past, and
as I be well assured of his great aversion to all act of dishonesty,
I could entertain no hope of be receive by he, especially since
I be too certain of all the good office in the power of my mother;
nay, have my father's pardon be as sure, as I conceive his
resentment to be, I yet question whether I could have have the
assurance to behold he, or whether I could, upon any term, have
submit to live and converse with those who, I be convince, know
I to have be guilty of so base an action.
"I hasten therefore back to London, the good retirement of either
grief or shame, unless for person of a very public character; for
here you have the advantage of solitude without its disadvantage,
since you may be alone and in company at the same time; and while you
walk or sit unobserved, noise, hurry, and a constant succession of
object, entertain the mind, and prevent the spirit from prey on
themselves, or rather on grief or shame, which be the most
unwholesome diet in the world; and on which (though there be many who
never taste either but in public) there be some who can feed very
plentifully and very fatally when alone.
"but as there be scarce any human good without its concomitant evil,
so there be people who find an inconvenience in this unobserving
temper of mankind; I mean person who have no money; for as you be
not put out of countenance, so neither be you cloathe or feed by
those who do not know you. and a man may be as easily starve in
leadenhall-market as in the desert of Arabia.
"it be at present my fortune to be destitute of that great evil, as
it be apprehend to be by several writer, who I suppose be
overburthene with it, namely, money."--"With submission, sir," say
Partridge, "I do not remember any writer who have call it
_malorum_; but _irritamenta malorum_. _Effodiuntur ope, irritamenta
malorum_"--"Well, sir," continue the stranger, "whether it be an
evil, or only the cause of evil, I be entirely void of it, and at the
same time of friend, and, as I think, of acquaintance; when one
evening, as I be pass through the Inner Temple, very hungry, and
very miserable, I hear a voice on a sudden hail I with great
familiarity by my christian name; and upon turn about, I presently
recollect the person who so salute I to have be my
fellow-collegiate; one who have leave the university above a year, and
long before any of my misfortune have befall I. this gentleman,
whose name be Watson, shake I heartily by the hand; and express
great joy at meet I, propose our immediately drink a bottle
together. I first decline the proposal, and pretend business, but
as he be very earnest and pressing, hunger at last overcame my pride,
and I fairly confess to he I have no money in my pocket; yet not
without frame a lie for an excuse, and impute it to my have
change my breech that morning. Mr Watson answer, `I think,
Jack, you and I have be too old acquaintance for you to mention such
a matter.' he then take I by the arm, and be pull I along; but I
give he very little trouble, for my own inclination pull I much
strong than he could do.
"we then go into the Friars, which you know be the scene of all
mirth and jollity. here, when we arrive at the tavern, Mr Watson
apply himself to the drawer only, without take the least notice of
the cook; for he have no suspicion but that I have din long since.
however, as the case be really otherwise, I forge another falsehood,
and tell my companion I have be at the further end of the city on
business of consequence, and have snapt up a mutton-chop in haste; so
that I be again hungry, and wish he would add a beef-steak to his
bottle."--"some people," cry Partridge, "ought to have good
memory; or do you find just money enough in your breech to pay
for the mutton-chop?"--"your observation be right," answer the
stranger, "and I believe such blunder be inseparable from all
deal in untruth.--but to proceed--I begin now to feel myself
extremely happy. the meat and wine soon revive my spirit to a high
pitch, and I enjoy much pleasure in the conversation of my old
acquaintance, the rather as I think he entirely ignorant of what
have happen at the university since his leave it.
"but he do not suffer I to remain long in this agreeable delusion;
for take a bumper in one hand, and hold I by the other, `here,
my boy,' cry he, `herebe wish you joy of your be so honourably
acquit of that affair lay to your charge.' I be thunderstruck
with confusion at those word, which Watson observe, proceed thus:
`Nay, never be ashamed, man; thou hast be acquit, and no one now
dare call thee guilty; but, prithee, do tell I, who be thy friend--I
hope thou didst really rob he? for rat I if it be not a meritorious
action to strip such a sneaking, pitiful rascal; and instead of the
two hundred guinea, I wish you have take as many thousand. come,
come, my boy, donot be shy of confess to I: you be not now
bring before one of the pimp. D--n I if I donot honour you for it;
for, as I hope for salvation, I would have make no manner of scruple
of do the same thing.'
"this declaration a little relieved my abashment; and as wine have now
somewhat open my heart, I very freely acknowledge the robbery, but
acquaint he that he have be misinformed as to the sum take, which
be little more than a fifth part of what he have mention.
"`I be sorry for it with all my heart,' quoth he, `and I wish thee
well success another time. though, if you will take my advice, you
shall have no occasion to run any such risque. here,' say he, take
some dice out of his pocket, `herebe the stuff. here be the
implement; here be the little doctor which cure the distemper of
the purse. follow but my counsel, and I will show you a way to empty
the pocket of a queer cull without any danger of the nubbe cheat.'"
"nubbe cheat!" cry Partridge: "pray, sir, what be that?"
"why that, sir," say the stranger, "be a cannot phrase for the gallow;
for as gamester differ little from highwayman in their moral, so do
they very much resemble they in their language.
"we have now each drank our bottle, when Mr Watson say, the board be
sit, and that he must attend, earnestly press I at the same
time to go with he and try my fortune. I answer he know that be at
present out of my power, as I have inform he of the emptiness of my
pocket. to say the truth, I doubt not from his many strong
expression of friendship, but that he would offer to lend I a small
sum for that purpose, but he answer, `never mind that, man; e'en
boldly run a levant' [Partridge be go to inquire the meaning of
that word, but Jones stop his mouth]: `but be circumspect as to the
man. I will tip you the proper person, which may be necessary, as you
do not know the town, nor can distinguish a rum cull from a queer
one."
"the bill be now bring, when Watson pay his share, and be
depart. I remind he, not without blushing, of my have no
money. he answer, `that signify nothing; score it behind the door,
or make a bold brush and take no notice.--or--stay,' say he; `I will
go down-stair first, and then do you take up my money, and score the
whole reckoning at the bar, and I will wait for you at the corner.' I
express some dislike at this, and hint my expectation that he
would have deposit the whole; but he swear he have not another
sixpence in his pocket.
"he then go down, and I be prevail on to take up the money and
follow he, which I do close enough to hear he tell the drawer the
reckoning be upon the table. the drawer past by I up-stair; but I
make such haste into the street, that I hear nothing of his
disappointment, nor do I mention a syllable at the bar, accord to
my instruction.
"we now go directly to the gaming-table, where Mr Watson, to my
surprize, pull out a large sum of money and place it before he, as
do many other; all of they, no doubt, consider their own heap as
so many decoy bird, which be to intice and draw over the heap of
their neighbour.
"here it would be tedious to relate all the freak which Fortune, or
rather the dice, play in this her temple. mountain of gold be in
a few moment reduce to nothing at one part of the table, and rise as
suddenly in another. the rich grow in a moment poor, and the poor as
suddenly become rich; so that it seem a philosopher could nowhere
have so well instruct his pupil in the contempt of rich, at least
he could nowhere have well inculcate the incertainty of their
duration.
"for my own part, after have considerably improve my small estate,
I at last entirely demolish it. Mr Watson too, after much variety of
luck, rise from the table in some heat, and declare he have lose a
cool hundred, and would play no long. then come up to I, he ask
I to return with he to the tavern; but I positively refuse, say,
I would not bring myself a second time into such a dilemma, and
especially as he have lose all his money and be now in my own
condition. `Pooh!' say he, `I have just borrow a couple of guineas
of a friend, and one of they be at your service.' he immediately put
one of they into my hand, and I no long resist his inclination.
"I be at first a little shocked at return to the same house whence
we have depart in so unhandsome a manner; but when the drawer, with
very civil address, tell we, `he believe we have forget to pay our
reckoning,' I become perfectly easy, and very readily give he a
guinea, bid he pay himself, and acquiesce in the unjust charge which
have be lay on my memory.
"Mr Watson now bespoke the most extravagant supper he could well think
of; and though he have content himself with simple claret before,
nothing now but the most precious Burgundy would serve his purpose.
"our company be soon encrease by the addition of several gentleman
from the gaming-table; most of whom, as I afterwards find, come not
to the tavern to drink, but in the way of business; for the true
gamester pretend to be ill, and refuse their glass, while they
ply heartily two young fellow, who be to be afterwards pillage,
as indeed they be without mercy. of this plunder I have the good
fortune to be a sharer, though I be not yet let into the secret.
"there be one remarkable accident attend this tavern play; for the
money by degree totally disappear; so that though at the beginning
the table be half cover with gold, yet before the play end, which
it do not till the next day, be Sunday, at noon, there be scarce
a single guinea to be see on the table; and this be the stranger as
every person present, except myself, declare he have lose; and what
be become of the money, unless the devil himself carry it away, be
difficult to determine."
"most certainly he do," say Partridge, "for evil spirit can carry
away anything without be see, though there be never so many folk
in the room; and I should not have be surprize if he have carry
away all the company of a set of wicked wretch, who be at play in
sermon time. and I could tell you a true story, if I would, where the
devil take a man out of bed from another man's wife, and carry he
away through the keyhole of the door. Ihave see the very house where
it be do, and nobody hath live in it these thirty year."
Though Jones be a little offend by the impertinence of Partridge,
he could not however avoid smile at his simplicity. the stranger do
the same, and then proceed with his story, as will be see in the
next chapter.
Chapter xiii.
in which the forego story be far continued.
"my fellow-collegiate have now enter I in a new scene of life. I
soon become acquaint with the whole fraternity of sharper, and be
let into their secret; I mean, into the knowledge of those gross
cheat which be proper to impose upon the raw and unexperienced; for
there be some trick of a fine kind, which be know only to a few
of the gang, who be at the head of their profession; a degree of
honour beyond my expectation; for drink, to which I be immoderately
addicted, and the natural warmth of my passion, prevent I from
arrive at any great success in an art which require as much
coolness as the most austere school of philosophy.
"Mr Watson, with whom I now live in the close amity, have unluckily
the former fail to a very great excess; so that instead of make a
fortune by his profession, as some other do, he be alternately rich
and poor, and be often oblige to surrender to his cool friend,
over a bottle which they never taste, that plunder that he have take
from cull at the public table.
"however, we both make a shift to pick up an uncomfortable livelihood;
and for two year I continue of the calling; during which time I
taste all the variety of fortune, sometimes flourish in
affluence, and at other be oblige to struggle with almost
incredible difficulty. to-day wallowing in luxury, and to-morrow
reduce to the coarse and most homely fare. my fine clothe be
often on my back in the evening, and at the pawn-shop the next
morning.
"one night, as I be return pennyless from the gaming-table, I
observe a very great disturbance, and a large mob gather together
in the street. as I be in no danger from pickpocket, I venture into
the croud, where upon enquiry I find that a man have be rob and
very ill use by some ruffian. the wounded man appear very bloody,
and seem scarce able to support himself on his leg. as I have not
therefore be deprive of my humanity by my present life and
conversation, though they have leave I very little of either honesty or
shame, I immediately offer my assistance to the unhappy person, who
thankfully accept it, and, put himself under my conduct, beg
I to convey he to some tavern, where he might send for a surgeon,
be, as he say, faint with loss of blood. he seem indeed highly
pleased at find one who appear in the dress of a gentleman; for
as to all the rest of the company present, their outside be such that
he could not wisely place any confidence in they.
"I take the poor man by the arm, and lead he to the tavern where we
keep our rendezvous, as it happen to be the near at hand. a
surgeon happen luckily to be in the house, immediately attend,
and apply himself to dress his wound, which I have the pleasure
to hear be not likely to be mortal.
"the surgeon have very expeditiously and dextrously finish his
business, begin to enquire in what part of the town the wound man
lodge; who answer, `that he be come to town that very morning;
that his horse be at an inn in Piccadilly, and that he have no other
lodging, and very little or no acquaintance in town.'
"this surgeon, whose name I have forget, though I remember it begin
with an r, have the first character in his profession, and be
serjeant-surgeon to the king. he have moreover many good quality, and
be a very generous good-natured man, and ready to do any service to
his fellow-creature. he offer his patient the use of his chariot to
carry he to his inn, and at the same time whisper in his ear, `that
if he want any money, he would furnish he.'
"the poor man be not now capable of return thank for this
generous offer; for have have his eye for some time stedfastly on
I, he throw himself back in his chair, cry, `oh, my son! my son!'
and then faint away.
"many of the people present imagine this accident have happen
through his loss of blood; but I, who at the same time begin to
recollect the feature of my father, be now confirm in my
suspicion, and satisfy that it be he himself who appear before
I. I presently run to he, raise he in my arm, and kiss his cold
lip with the utmost eagerness. here I must draw a curtain over a
scene which I cannot describe; for though I do not lose my being, as
my father for a while do, my sens be however so overpowered with
affright and surprize, that I be a stranger to what pass during some
minute, and indeed till my father have again recover from his swoon,
and I find myself in his arm, both tenderly embrace each other,
while the tear trickle a-pace down the cheek of each of we.
"Most of those present seem affect by this scene, which we, who
might be consider as the actor in it, be desirous of remove
from the eye of all spectator as fast as we could; my father
therefore accept the kind offer of the surgeon's chariot, and I
attend he in it to his inn.
"when we be alone together, he gently upbraid I with have
neglect to write to he during so long a time, but entirely omit
the mention of that crime which have occasion it. he then inform I
of my mother's death, and insist on my return home with he,
say, `that he have long suffer the great anxiety on my account;
that he know not whether he have most fear my death or wish it,
since he have so many more dreadful apprehension for I. at last, he
say, a neighbouring gentleman, who have just recover a son from the
same place, inform he where I be; and that to reclaim I from this
course of life be the sole cause of his journey to London.' he
thank Heaven he have succeed so far as to find I out by mean of
an accident which have like to have prove fatal to he; and have the
pleasure to think he partly owe his preservation to my humanity, with
which he profest himself to be more delighted than he should have be
with my filial piety, if I have know that the object of all my care
be my own father.
"Vice have not so deprave my heart as to excite in it an insensibility
of so much paternal affection, though so unworthily bestow. I
presently promise to obey his command in my return home with he, as
soon as he be able to travel, which indeed he be in a very few day,
by the assistance of that excellent surgeon who have undertake his
cure.
"the day precede my father's journey (before which time I scarce
ever leave he), I go to take my leave of some of my most intimate
acquaintance, particularly of Mr Watson, who dissuade I from bury
myself, as he call it, out of a simple compliance with the fond
desire of a foolish old fellow. such sollicitation, however, have no
effect, and I once more see my own home. my father now greatly
sollicite I to think of marriage; but my inclination be utterly
averse to any such thought. I have taste of love already, and perhaps
you know the extravagant excess of that most tender and most violent
passion."--Here the old gentleman pause, and look earnestly at
Jones; whose countenance, within a minute's space, display the
extremity of both red and white. upon which the old man, without
make any observation, renew his narrative.
"be now provide with all the necessary of life, I betook myself
once again to study, and that with a more inordinate application than
I have ever do formerly. the book which now employ my time solely
be those, as well antient as modern, which treat of true philosophy,
a word which be by many thought to be the subject only of farce and
ridicule. I now read over the work of Aristotle and Plato, with the
rest of those inestimable treasure which antient Greece have
bequeath to the world.
"these author, though they instruct I in no science by which man
may promise to themselves to acquire the least rich or worldly
power, teach I, however, the art of despise the high
acquisition of both. they elevate the mind, and steel and harden it
against the capricious invasion of fortune. they not only instruct in
the knowledge of Wisdom, but confirm man in her habit, and
demonstrate plainly, that this must be our guide, if we propose ever
to arrive at the great worldly happiness, or to defend ourselves,
with any tolerable security, against the misery which everywhere
surround and invest we.
"to this I add another study, compare to which, all the philosophy
teach by the wise heathen be little well than a dream, and be
indeed as full of vanity as the silly jester ever pleased to
represent it. this be that divine wisdom which be alone to be find in
the Holy Scriptures; for they impart to we the knowledge and assurance
of thing much more worthy our attention than all which this world can
offer to our acceptance; of thing which Heaven itself hath
condescend to reveal to we, and to the small knowledge of which
the high human wit unassisted could never ascend. I begin now to
think all the time I have spend with the good heathen writer be
little more than labour lose: for, however pleasant and delightful
their lesson may be, or however adequate to the right regulation of
our conduct with respect to this world only; yet, when compare with
the glory reveal in Scripture, their high document will appear
as trifling, and of as little consequence, as the rule by which
child regulate their childish little game and pastime. true it be,
that philosophy make we wise, but Christianity make we well man.
philosophy elevate and steel the mind, Christianity soften and
sweeten it. the former make we the object of human admiration, the
latter of Divine love. that insure we a temporal, but this an eternal
happiness.--But I be afraid I tire you with my rhapsody."
"not at all," cry Partridge; "Lud forbid we should be tired with
good thing!"
"I have spend," continue the stranger, "about four year in the most
delightful manner to myself, totally give up to contemplation, and
entirely unembarrassed with the affair of the world, when I lose the
good of father, and one whom I so entirely love, that my grief at
his loss exceed all description. I now abandon my book, and give
myself up for a whole month to the effect of melancholy and despair.
Time, however, the good physician of the mind, at length bring I
relief."--"Ay, ay; _Tempus edax rerum_" say partridge.--"i then,"
continue the strange, "betook myself again to my former study,
which I may say perfect my cure; for philosophy and religion may be
call the exercise of the mind, and when this be disorder, they
be as wholesome as exercise can be to a distempered body. they do
indeed produce similar effect with exercise; for they strengthen and
confirm the mind, till man become, in the noble strain of horace--
_Fortis, et in seipso totus tere atque rotundus,
Externi ne quid valeat per laeve morari;
in quem manca ruit semper Fortuna._"[*]
[*] firm in himself, who on himself rely,
polish'd and round, who run his proper course
and break misfortune with superior force.--mr francis.
here Jones smile at some conceit which intrude itself into his
imagination; but the stranger, I believe, perceive it not, and
proceed thus:--
"my circumstance be now greatly alter by the death of that good
of man; for my brother, who be now become master of the house,
differ so widely from I in his inclination, and our pursuit in
life have be so very various, that we be the bad of company to
each other: but what make our live together still more disagreeable,
be the little harmony which could subsist between the few who
resort to I, and the numerous train of sportsman who often attend
my brother from the field to the table; for such fellow, besides the
noise and nonsense with which they persecute the ear of sober man,
endeavour always to attack they with affront and contempt. this be so
much the case, that neither I myself, nor my friend, could ever sit
down to a meal with they without be treat with derision, because
we be unacquainted with the phrase of sportsman. for man of true
learning, and almost universal knowledge, always compassionate the
ignorance of other; but fellow who excel in some little, low,
contemptible art, be always certain to despise those who be
unacquainted with that art.
"in short, we soon separate, and I go, by the advice of a
physician, to drink the Bath water; for my violent affliction, add
to a sedentary life, have throw I into a kind of paralytic disorder,
for which those water be account an almost _certain_ cure. the
second day after my arrival, as I be walk by the river, the sun
shine so intensely hot (though it be early in the year), that I
retire to the shelter of some willow, and sit down by the river
side. here I have not be seat long before I hear a person on the
other side of the willow sigh and bemoan himself bitterly. on a
sudden, have utter a most impious oath, he cry, `I be resolve
to bear it no long,' and directly throw himself into the water. I
immediately start, and run towards the place, call at the same
time as loudly as I could for assistance. an angler happen luckily
to be a-fishing a little below I, though some very high sedge have hide
he from my sight. he immediately come up, and both of we together,
not without some hazard of our life, draw the body to the shore. at
first we perceive no sign of life remain; but have hold the body
up by the heel (for we soon have assistance enough), it discharge a
vast quantity of water at the mouth, and at length begin to discover
some symptom of breathing, and a little afterwards to move both its
hand and its leg.
"an apothecary, who happen to be present among other, advise that
the body, which seem now to have pretty well empty itself of
water, and which begin to have many convulsive motion, should be
directly take up, and carry into a warm bed. this be accordingly
perform, the apothecary and myself attend.
"as we be go towards an inn, for we know not the man's lodging,
luckily a woman meet we, who, after some violent screaming, tell we
that the gentleman lodge at her house.
"when I have see the man safely deposit there, I leave he to the
care of the apothecary; who, I suppose, use all the right method
with he, for the next morning I hear he have perfectly recover his
sens.
"I then go to visit he, intend to search out, as well as I
could, the cause of his have attempt so desperate an act, and to
prevent, as far as I be able, his pursue such wicked intention for
the future. I be no soon admit into his chamber, than we both
instantly know each other; for who should this person be but my good
friend Mr Watson! here I will not trouble you with what past at our
first interview; for I would avoid prolixity as much as
possible."--"Pray let we hear all," cry Partridge; "I want mightily
to know what bring he to Bath."
"you shall hear everything material," answer the stranger; and then
proceed to relate what we shall proceed to write, after we have
give a short breathing time to both ourselves and the reader.
Chapter xiv.
in which the Man of the Hill conclude his history.
"Mr Watson," continue the strange, "very freely acquaint I, that
the unhappy situation of his circumstance, occasion by a tide of
ill luck, have in a manner force he to a resolution of destroy
himself.
"I now begin to argue very seriously with he, in opposition to this
heathenish, or indeed diabolical, principle of the lawfulness of
self-murder; and say everything which occur to I on the subject;
but, to my great concern, it seem to have very little effect on he.
he seem not at all to repent of what he have do, and give I reason
to fear he would soon make a second attempt of the like horrible kind.
"when I have finish my discourse, instead of endeavour to answer
my argument, he look I stedfastly in the face, and with a smile
say, `you be strangely alter, my good friend, since I remember
you. I question whether any of our bishop could make a well
argument against suicide than you have entertain I with; but unless
you can find somebody who will lend I a cool hundred, I must either
hang, or drown, or starve; and, in my opinion, the last death be the
most terrible of the three.'
"I answer he very gravely that I be indeed alter since I have
see he last. that I have find leisure to look into my folly and to
repent of they. I then advise he to pursue the same step; and at
last conclude with an assurance that I myself would lend he a
hundred pound, if it would be of any service to his affair, and he
would not put it into the power of a die to deprive he of it.
"Mr Watson, who seem almost compose in slumber by the former part
of my discourse, be rouse by the latter. he seize my hand eagerly,
give I a thousand thank, and declare I be a friend indeed; add
that he hop I have a well opinion of he than to imagine he have
profit so little by experience, as to put any confidence in those
damned dice which have so often deceive he. `no, no,' cry he; `let
I but once handsomely be set up again, and if ever Fortune make a
break merchant of I afterwards, I will forgive she.'
"I very well understand the language of set up, and break
merchant. I therefore say to he, with a very grave face, Mr Watson,
you must endeavour to find out some business or employment, by which
you may procure yourself a livelihood; and I promise you, could I see
any probability of be repay hereafter, I would advance a much
large sum than what you have mention, to equip you in any fair and
honourable call; but as to gaming, besides the baseness and
wickedness of make it a profession, you be really, to my own
knowledge, unfit for it, and it will end in your certain ruin.
"`why now, thatbe strange,' answer he; `neither you, nor any of my
friend, would ever allow I to know anything of the matter, and yet I
believe I _be_ as good a hand at every game as any of you all; and I
heartily wish I be to play with you only for your whole fortune: I
should desire no well sport, and I would let you name your game into
the bargain: but come, my dear boy, have you the hundred in your
pocket?"
"I answer I have only a bill for £50, which I deliver he, and
promise to bring he the rest next morning; and after give he a
little more advice, take my leave.
"I be indeed well than my word; for I return to he that very
afternoon. when I enter the room, I find he sit up in his bed
at card with a notorious gamester. this sight, you will imagine,
shock I not a little; to which I may add the mortification of
see my bill deliver by he to his antagonist, and thirty guineas
only give in exchange for it.
"the other gamester presently quit the room, and then Watson
declare he be ashamed to see I; `but,' say he, `I find luck run
so damnably against I, that I will resolve to leave off play for
ever. I have think of the kind proposal you make I ever since, and
I promise you there shall be no fault in I, if I do not put it in
execution.'
"though I have no great faith in his promise, I produce he the
remainder of the hundred in consequence of my own; for which he give
I a note, which be all I ever expect to see in return for my
money.
"we be prevent from any further discourse at present by the
arrival of the apothecary; who, with much joy in his countenance, and
without even ask his patient how he do, proclaim there be great
news arrive in a letter to himself, which he say would shortly be
public, `that the Duke of Monmouth be land in the west with a vast
army of Dutch; and that another vast fleet hover over the coast of
Norfolk, and be to make a descent there, in order to favour the
duke's enterprize with a diversion on that side.'
"this apothecary be one of the great politician of his time. he
be more delighted with the most paultry packet, than with the good
patient, and the high joy he be capable of, he receive from
have a piece of news in his possession an hour or two soon than
any other person in the town. his advice, however, be seldom
authentic; for he would swallow almost anything as a truth--a humour
which many make use of to impose upon he.
"thus it happen with what he at present communicate; for it be
know within a short time afterwards that the duke be really land,
but that his army consist only of a few attendant; and as to the
diversion in Norfolk, it be entirely false.
"the apothecary staid no long in the room than while he acquaint
we with his news; and then, without say a syllable to his patient
on any other subject, depart to spread his advice all over the
town.
"event of this nature in the public be generally apt to eclipse all
private concern. our discourse therefore now become entirely
political.[*] for my own part, I have be for some time very seriously
affect with the danger to which the protestant religion be so
visibly expose under a popish prince, and think the apprehension of
it alone sufficient to justify that insurrection; for no real security
can ever be find against the persecuting spirit of Popery, when arm
with power, except the deprive it of that power, as woeful
experience presently show. you know how King James behave after
get the well of this attempt; how little he value either his
royal word, or coronation oath, or the liberty and right of his
people. but all have not the sense to foresee this at first; and
therefore the Duke of Monmouth be weakly support; yet all could
feel when the evil come upon they; and therefore all unite, at last,
to drive out that king, against whose exclusion a great party among we
have so warmly contend during the reign of his brother, and for whom
they now fight with such zeal and affection."
"what you say," interrupt Jones, "be very true; and it have often
strike I, as the most wonderful thing I ever read of in history, that
so soon after this convincing experience which bring our whole
nation to join so unanimously in expel King James, for the
preservation of our religion and liberty, there should be a party
among we mad enough to desire the place his family again on the
throne." "you be not in earnest!" answer the old man; "there can be
no such party. as bad an opinion as I have of mankind, I cannot
believe they infatuate to such a degree. there may be some hot-head
Papists lead by their priest to engage in this desperate cause, and
think it a holy war; but that protestant, that be member of the
Church of England, should be such apostate, such _felos de se_, I
cannot believe it; no, no, young man, unacquainted as I be with what
have past in the world for these last thirty year, I cannot be so
impose upon as to credit so foolish a tale; but I see you have a mind
to sport with my ignorance."--"can it be possible," reply Jones,
"that you have live so much out of the world as not to know that
during that time there have be two rebellion in favour of the son
of King James, one of which be now actually rag in the very heart
of the kingdom." at these word the old gentleman start up, and in a
most solemn tone of voice, conjure Jones by his Maker to tell he if
what he say be really true; which the other as solemnly affirm,
he walk several turn about the room in a profound silence, then
cry, then laugh, and at last fall down on his knee, and bless
God, in a loud thanksgive prayer, for have deliver he from all
society with human nature, which could be capable of such monstrous
extravagance. after which, be remind by Jones that he have break
off his story, he resume it again in this manner:--
"as mankind, in the day I be speak of, be not yet arrive at
that pitch of madness which I find they be capable of now, and which,
to be sure, I have only escape by live alone, and at a distance
from the contagion, there be a considerable rise in favour of
Monmouth; and my principle strongly incline I to take the same
part, I determine to join he; and Mr Watson, from different motif
concur in the same resolution (for the spirit of a gamester will
carry a man as far upon such an occasion as the spirit of patriotism),
we soon provide ourselves with all necessary, and go to the duke
at Bridgewater.
"the unfortunate event of this enterprize, you be, I conclude, as
well acquainted with as myself. I escape, together with Mr Watson,
from the battle at Sedgemore, in which action I receive a slight
wound. we ride near forty mile together on the Exeter road, and then
abandon our horse, scramble as well as we could through the
field and bye-road, till we arrive at a little wild hut on a
common, where a poor old woman take all the care of we she could, and
dress my wound with salve, which quickly heal it."
"pray, sir, where be the wound?" say Partridge. the stranger
satisfy he it be in his arm, and then continue his narrative.
"here, sir," say he, "Mr Watson leave I the next morning, in order,
as he pretend, to get we some provision from the town of Collumpton;
but--can I relate it, or can you believe it?--this Mr Watson, this
friend, this base, barbarous, treacherous villain, betray I to a
party of horse belong to King James, and at his return deliver I
into their hand.
"the soldier, be six in number, have now seize I, and be
conduct I to Taunton gaol; but neither my present situation, nor
the apprehension of what might happen to I, be half so irksome to
my mind as the company of my false friend, who, have surrender
himself, be likewise consider as a prisoner, though he be well
treat, as be to make his peace at my expense. he at first
endeavour to excuse his treachery; but when he receive nothing but
scorn and upbraid from I, he soon change his note, abuse I as
the most atrocious and malicious rebel, and lay all his own guilt to
my charge, who, as he declare, have solicit, and even threaten
he, to make he take up arm against his gracious as well as lawful
sovereign.
"this false evidence (for in reality he have be much the forwarder of
the two) sting I to the quick, and raise an indignation scarce
conceivable by those who have not feel it. however, fortune at length
take pity on I; for as we be get a little beyond Wellington, in a
narrow lane, my guard receive a false alarm, that near fifty of the
enemy be at hand; upon which they shift for themselves, and leave
I and my betrayer to do the same. that villain immediately run from
I, and I be glad he do, or I should have certainly endeavour,
though I have no arm, to have execute vengeance on his baseness.
"I be now once more at liberty; and immediately withdraw from the
highway into the field, I travel on, scarce know which way I
go, and make it my chief care to avoid all public road and all
town--nay, even the most homely house; for I imagine every human
creature whom I see desirous of betray I.
"at last, after ramble several day about the country, during which
the field afford I the same bed and the same food which nature
bestow on our savage brother of the creation, I at length arrive at
this place, where the solitude and wildness of the country invite I
to fix my abode. the first person with whom I take up my habitation
be the mother of this old woman, with whom I remain conceal till
the news of the glorious revolution put an end to all my apprehension
of danger, and give I an opportunity of once more visit my own
home, and of enquire a little into my affair, which I soon settle
as agreeably to my brother as to myself; have resign everything to
he, for which he pay I the sum of a thousand pound, and settle on
I an annuity for life.
"his behaviour in this last instance, as in all other, be selfish
and ungenerous. I could not look on he as my friend, nor indeed do
he desire that I should; so I presently take my leave of he, as well
as of my other acquaintance; and from that day to this, my history be
little well than a blank."
"and be it possible, sir," say Jones, "that you can have reside here
from that day to this?"--"O no, sir," answer the gentleman; "I have
be a great traveller, and there be few part of Europe with which I
be not acquaint." "I have not, sir," cry Jones, "the assurance to
ask it of you now; indeed it would be cruel, after so much breath as
you have already spend: but you will give I leave to wish for some
further opportunity of hear the excellent observation which a man
of your sense and knowledge of the world must have make in so long a
course of travel."--"Indeed, young gentleman," answer the stranger,
"I will endeavour to satisfy your curiosity on this head likewise, as
far as I be able." Jones attempt fresh apology, but be prevent;
and while he and Partridge sit with greedy and impatient ear, the
stranger proceed as in the next chapter.
[*] _the rest of this paragraph and the two follow paragraph
in the first edition be as follow_:
"for my own part, I have be for some time very seriously affect
with the danger to which the protestant religion be so visibly
expose, that nothing but the immediate interposition of Providence
seem capable of preserve it; for King James have indeed declare
war against the protestant cause. he have bring known papist into
the army and attempt to bring they into the Church and into the
University. popish priest swarm through the nation, appear
publicly in their habit, and boast that they should shortly walk
in procession through the street. our own clergy be forbid to
preach against popery, and bishop be order to supend those who
do; and to do the business at once an illegal ecclesiastical
commission be erect, little inferior to an inquisition, of which,
probably, it be intend to be the ringleader. thus, as our duty to
the king can never be call more than our second duty, he have
discharge we from this by make it incompatible with our
preserve the first, which be surely to heaven. besides this, he
have dissolve his subject from their allegiance by break his
Coronation Oath, to which their allegiance be annex; for he have
imprison bishop because they would not give up their religion,
and turn out judge because they would not absolutely surrender
the law into his hand; nay, he seize this himself, and when he
claim a dispense power, he declare himself, in fact, as
absolute as any tyrant ever be or can be. I have recapitulate
these matter in full lest some of they should have be omit in
history; and I think nothing less than such provocation as I have
here mention, nothing less than certain and imminent danger to
their religion and liberty, can justify or even mitigate the
dreadful sin of rebellion in any people."
"I promise you, sir," say Jones, "all these fact, and more, I have
read in history, but I will tell you a fact which be not yet
record and of which I suppose you be ignorant. there be actually
now a rebellion on foot in this kingdom in favour of the son of that
very King James, a profess papist, more bigoted, if possible, than
his father, and this carry on by Protestants against a king who
hath never in one single instance make the least invasion on our
liberty."
"prodigious indeed!" answer the stranger. "you tell I what would
be incredible of a nation which do not deserve the character that
Virgil give of a woman, _varium et mutabile semper_. surely this be
to be unworthy of the care which Providence seem to have take of
we in the preservation of our religion against the powerful design
and constant machination of Popery, a preservation so strange and
unaccountable that I almost think we may appeal to it as to a
miracle for the proof of its holiness. prodigious indeed! a
protestant rebellion in favour of a popish prince! the folly of
mankind be as wonderful as their knavery--but to conclude my story:
I resolve to take arm in defence of my country, of my religion,
and my liberty, and Mr. Watson join in the same resolution. we
soon provide ourselves with an necessary and join the Duke at
Bridgewater."
"the unfortunate event of this enterprise you be perhaps well
acquaint with than myself. I escape together with Mr. Watson from
the battle at Sedgemore,...
Chapter xv.
a brief history of Europe; and a curious discourse between Mr Jones
and the Man of the Hill.
"in Italy the landlord be very silent. in France they be more
talkative, but yet civil. in Germany and Holland they be generally
very impertinent. and as for their honesty, I believe it be pretty
equal in all those country. the _laquais à louange_ be sure to lose
no opportunity of cheat you; and as for the postilion, I think
they be pretty much alike all the world over. these, sir, be the
observation on man which I make in my travel; for these be the
only man I ever converse with. my design, when I go abroad, be to
divert myself by see the wondrous variety of prospect, beast,
bird, fish, insect, and vegetable, with which God have be
pleased to enrich the several part of this globe; a variety which, as
it must give great pleasure to a contemplative beholder, so doth it
admirably display the power, and wisdom, and goodness of the Creator.
indeed, to say the truth, there be but one work in his whole creation
that doth he any dishonour, and with that I have long since avoid
hold any conversation."
"you will pardon I," cry Jones; "but I have always imagine that
there be in this very work you mention as great variety as in all the
rest; for, besides the difference of inclination, custom and climate
have, I be tell, introduce the utmost diversity into human nature."
"very little indeed," answer the other: "those who travel in order
to acquaint themselves with the different manner of man might spare
themselves much pain by go to a carnival at Venice; for there they
will see at once all which they can discover in the several court of
Europe. the same hypocrisy, the same fraud; in short, the same folly
and vice dress in different habit. in Spain, these be equip
with much gravity; and in Italy, with vast splendor. in France, a
knave be dress like a fop; and in the northern country, like a
sloven. but human nature be everywhere the same, everywhere the object
of detestation and scorn.
"as for my own part, I past through all these nation as you perhaps
may have do through a croud at a shew-jostling to get by they,
hold my nose with one hand, and defend my pocket with the
other, without speak a word to any of they, while I be press on
to see what I want to see; which, however entertain it might be
in itself, scarce make I amend for the trouble the company give I."
"do not you find some of the nation among which you travel less
troublesome to you than other?" say Jones. "o yes," reply the old
man: "the Turks be much more tolerable to I than the Christians;
for they be man of profound taciturnity, and never disturb a stranger
with question. now and then indeed they bestow a short curse upon
he, or spit in his face as he walk the street, but then they have
do with he; and a man may live an age in their country without
hear a dozen word from they. but of all the people I ever see,
heaven defend I from the French! with their damned prate and
civility, and do the honour of their nation to stranger (as they
be pleased to call it), but indeed set forth their own vanity;
they be so troublesome, that I have infinitely rather pass my life
with the Hottentots than set my foot in Paris again. they be a nasty
people, but their nastiness be mostly without; whereas, in France, and
some other nation that I willnot name, it be all within, and make they
stink much more to my reason than that of Hottentots do to my nose.
"thus, sir, I have end the history of my life; for as to all that
series of year during which I have live retire here, it afford no
variety to entertain you, and may be almost consider as one
day.[*] the retirement have be so compleat, that I could hardly have
enjoy a more absolute solitude in the desert of the Thebais than
here in the midst of this populous kingdom. as I have no estate, I be
plague with no tenant or steward: my annuity be pay I pretty
regularly, as indeed it ought to be; for it be much less than what I
might have expect in return for what I give up. visit I admit none;
and the old woman who keep my house know that her place entirely
depend upon she save I all the trouble of buy the thing that I
want, keep off all sollicitation or business from I, and hold
her tongue whenever I be within hearing. as my walk be all by night,
I be pretty secure in this wild unfrequented place from meet any
company. some few person I have meet by chance, and send they home
heartily fright, as from the oddness of my dress and figure they
take I for a ghost or a hobgoblin. but what have happen to-night
show that even here I cannot be safe from the villany of man; for
without your assistance I have not only be rob, but very probably
murder."
[*] the rest of this paragraph be omit in the third edition
Jones thank the stranger for the trouble he have take in relate
his story, and then express some wonder how he could possibly endure
a life of such solitude; "in which," say he, "you may well complain
of the want of variety. indeed I be astonish how you have fill up,
or rather kill, so much of your time."
"I be not at all surprize," answer the other, "that to one whose
affection and thought be fix on the world my hour should appear
to have want employment in this place: but there be one single act,
for which the whole life of man be infinitely too short: what time can
suffice for the contemplation and worship of that glorious, immortal,
and eternal being, among the work of whose stupendous creation not
only this globe, but even those numberless luminary which we may
here behold spangle all the sky, though they should many of they be
sun light different system of world, may possibly appear but as
a few atom oppose to the whole earth which we inhabit? can a man who
by divine meditation be admit as it be into the conversation of
this ineffable, incomprehensible Majesty, think day, or year, or
age, too long for the continuance of so ravish an honour? shall
the trifling amusement, the palling pleasure, the silly business of
the world, roll away our hour too swiftly from we; and shall the pace
of time seem sluggish to a mind exercise in study so high, so
important, and so glorious? as no time be sufficient, so no place be
improper, for this great concern. on what object can we cast our eye
which may not inspire we with idea of his power, of his wisdom, and
of his goodness? it be not necessary that the rise sun should dart
his fiery glory over the eastern horizon; nor that the boisterous
wind should rush from their cavern, and shake the lofty forest; nor
that the open cloud should pour their deluge on the plain: it be
not necessary, I say, that any of these should proclaim his majesty:
there be not an insect, not a vegetable, of so low an order in the
creation as not to be honour with bear mark of the attribute of
its great creator; mark not only of his power, but of his wisdom and
goodness. man alone, the king of this globe, the last and great
work of the Supreme Being, below the sun; man alone hath basely
dishonour his own nature; and by dishonesty, cruelty, ingratitude,
and treachery, hath call his maker's goodness in question, by
puzzle we to account how a benevolent being should form so foolish
and so vile an animal. yet this be the being from whose conversation
you think, I suppose, that I have be unfortunately restrain, and
without whose blessed society, life, in your opinion, must be tedious
and insipid."
"in the former part of what you say," reply Jones, "I most heartily
and readily concur; but I believe, as well as hope, that the
abhorrence which you express for mankind in the conclusion, be much
too general. indeed, you here fall into an error, which in my little
experience I have observe to be a very common one, by take the
character of mankind from the bad and base among they; whereas,
indeed, as an excellent writer observe, nothing should be esteem as
characteristical of a specie, but what be to be find among the good
and most perfect individual of that specie. this error, I believe,
be generally commit by those who from want of proper caution in the
choice of their friend and acquaintance, have suffer injury from
bad and worthless man; two or three instance of which be very
unjustly charge on all human nature."
"I think I have experience enough of it," answer the other: "my first
mistress and my first friend betray I in the basest manner, and in
matter which threaten to be of the bad of consequence--even to
bring I to a shameful death."
"but you will pardon I," cry Jones, "if I desire you to reflect who
that mistress and who that friend be. what well, my good sir,
could be expect in love derive from the stew, or in friendship
first produce and nourish at the gaming-table? to take the
character of woman from the former instance, or of man from the
latter, would be as unjust as to assert that air be a nauseous and
unwholesome element, because we find it so in a jake. I have live
but a short time in the world, and yet have know man worthy of the
high friendship, and woman of the high love."
"alas! young man," answer the stranger, "you have live, you
confess, but a very short time in the world: I be somewhat old than
you when I be of the same opinion."
"you might have remain so still," reply Jones, "if you have not
be unfortunate, I will venture to say incautious, in the placing
your affection. if there be, indeed, much more wickedness in the
world than there be, it would not prove such general assertion
against human nature, since much of this arrive by mere accident, and
many a man who commit evil be not totally bad and corrupt in his
heart. in truth, none seem to have any title to assert human nature to
be necessarily and universally evil, but those whose own mind afford
they one instance of this natural depravity; which be not, I be
convince, your case."
"and such," say the stranger, "will be always the most backward to
assert any such thing. knave will no more endeavour to persuade we of
the baseness of mankind, than a highwayman will inform you that there
be thief on the road. this would, indeed, be a method to put you on
your guard, and to defeat their own purpose. for which reason, though
knave, as I remember, be very apt to abuse particular person, yet
they never cast any reflection on human nature in general." the old
gentleman speak this so warmly, that as Jones despair of make a
convert, and be unwilling to offend, he return no answer.
the day now begin to send forth its first stream of light, when Jones
make an apology to the stranger for have staid so long, and perhaps
detain he from his rest. the stranger answer, "he never want
rest less than at present; for that day and night be indifferent
season to he; and that he commonly make use of the former for the
time of his repose and of the latter for his walk and lucubration.
however," say he, "it be now a most lovely morning, and if you can
bear any long to be without your own rest or food, I will gladly
entertain you with the sight of some very fine prospect which I
believe you have not yet see."
Jones very readily embrace this offer, and they immediately set
forward together from the cottage. as for Partridge, he have fall
into a profound repose just as the stranger have finish his story;
for his curiosity be satisfied, and the subsequent discourse be not
forcible enough in its operation to conjure down the charm of sleep.
Jones therefore leave he to enjoy his nap; and as the reader may
perhaps be at this season glad of the same favour, we will here put an
end to the eighth book of our history.
BOOK IX.
contain TWELVE HOURS.
Chapter i.
of those who lawfully may, and of those who may not, write such
history as this.
among other good us for which I have think proper to institute
these several introductory chapter, I have consider they as a kind
of mark or stamp, which may hereafter enable a very indifferent reader
to distinguish what be true and genuine in this historic kind of
writing, from what be false and counterfeit. indeed, it seem likely
that some such mark may shortly become necessary, since the favourable
reception which two or three author have lately procure for their
work of this nature from the public, will probably serve as an
encouragement to many other to undertake the like. thus a swarm of
foolish novel and monstrous romance will be produce, either to the
great impoverishing of bookseller, or to the great loss of time and
depravation of moral in the reader; nay, often to the spreading of
scandal and calumny, and to the prejudice of the character of many
worthy and honest people.
I question not but the ingenious author of the Spectator be
principally induce to prefix Greek and latin motto to every paper,
from the same consideration of guard against the pursuit of those
scribbler, who have no talent of a writer but what be teach by
the writing-master, be yet nowise afraid nor ashamed to assume the
same title with the great genius, than their good brother in the
fable be of bray in the lion's skin.
by the device therefore of his motto, it become impracticable for any
man to presume to imitate the Spectators, without understanding at
least one sentence in the learn language. in the same manner I have
now secure myself from the imitation of those who be utterly
incapable of any degree of reflection, and whose learning be not equal
to an essay.
I would not be here understand to insinuate, that the great merit
of such historical production can ever lie in these introductory
chapter; but, in fact, those part which contain mere narrative only,
afford much more encouragement to the pen of an imitator, than those
which be compose of observation and reflection. here I mean such
imitator as Rowe be of Shakespear, or as Horace hint some of the
Romans be of Cato, by bare foot and sour face.
to invent good story, and to tell they well, be possibly very rare
talent, and yet I have observe few person who have scruple to aim
at both: and if we examine the romance and novel with which the
world abound, I think we may fairly conclude, that most of the
author would not have attempt to show their tooth (if the
expression may be allow I) in any other way of writing; nor could
indeed have string together a dozen sentence on any other subject
whatever.
_Scribimus indocti doctique passim_,[*]
[*] --Each desperate blockhead dare to write:
Verse be the trade of every live wight.--francis.
may be more truly say of the historian and biographer, than of any
other specie of writing; for all the art and science (even
criticism itself) require some little degree of learn and
knowledge. Poetry, indeed, may perhaps be think an exception; but
then it demand number, or something like number: whereas, to the
composition of novel and romance, nothing be necessary but paper,
pen, and ink, with the manual capacity of use they. this, I
conceive, their production show to be the opinion of the author
themselves: and this must be the opinion of their reader, if indeed
there be any such.
hence we be to derive that universal contempt which the world, who
always denominate the whole from the majority, have cast on all
historical writer who do not draw their material from record. and
it be the apprehension of this contempt that hath make we so
cautiously avoid the term romance, a name with which we might
otherwise have be well enough content. though, as we have good
authority for all our character, no less indeed than the vast
authentic doomsday-book of nature, as be elsewhere hint, our labour
have sufficient title to the name of history. certainly they deserve
some distinction from those work, which one of the witty of man
regard only as proceed from a _pruritus_, or indeed rather from a
looseness of the brain.
but besides the dishonour which be thus cast on one of the most useful
as well as entertaining of all kind of writing, there be just reason
to apprehend, that by encourage such author we shall propagate much
dishonour of another kind; I mean to the character of many good and
valuable member of society; for the dull writer, no more than the
dull companion, be always inoffensive. they have both enough of
language to be indecent and abusive. and surely if the opinion just
above cite be true, we cannot wonder that work so nastily derive
should be nasty themselves, or have a tendency to make other so.
to prevent therefore, for the future, such intemperate abuse of
leisure, of letter, and of the liberty of the press, especially as
the world seem at present to be more than usually threaten with
they, I shall here venture to mention some qualification, every one
of which be in a pretty high degree necessary to this order of
historian.
the first be, genius, without a full vein of which no study, say
Horace, can avail we. by genius I would understand that power or
rather those power of the mind, which be capable of penetrate into
all thing within our reach and knowledge, and of distinguish their
essential difference. these be no other than invention and judgment;
and they be both call by the collective name of genius, as they be
of those gift of nature which we bring with we into the world.
concern each of which many seem to have fall into very great
error; for by invention, I believe, be generally understand a
creative faculty, which would indeed prove most romance writer to
have the high pretension to it; whereas by invention be really
mean no more (and so the word signify) than discovery, or find
out; or to explain it at large, a quick and sagacious penetration into
the true essence of all the object of our contemplation. this, I
think, can rarely exist without the concomitancy of judgment; for how
we can be say to have discover the true essence of two thing,
without discern their difference, seem to I hard to conceive. now
this last be the undisputed province of judgment, and yet some few man
of wit have agree with all the dull fellow in the world in
represent these two to have be seldom or never the property of
one and the same person.
but though they should be so, they be not sufficient for our purpose,
without a good share of learning; for which I could again cite the
authority of Horace, and of many other, if any be necessary to prove
