that tool be of no service to a workman, when they be not sharpen
by art, or when he want rule to direct he in his work, or hath no
matter to work upon. all these us be supply by learn; for
nature can only furnish we with capacity; or, as I have choose to
illustrate it, with the tool of our profession; learning must fit
they for use, must direct they in it, and, lastly, must contribute
part at least of the material. a competent knowledge of history and
of the belle-lettre be here absolutely necessary; and without this
share of knowledge at least, to affect the character of an historian,
be as vain as to endeavour at build a house without timber or
mortar, or brick or stone. Homer and Milton, who, though they add
the ornament of number to their work, be both historian of our
order, be master of all the learning of their time.
again, there be another sort of knowledge, beyond the power of
learn to bestow, and this be to be have by conversation. so
necessary be this to the understanding the character of man, that
none be more ignorant of they than those learn pedant whose life
have be entirely consume in college, and among book; for however
exquisitely human nature may have be describe by writer, the true
practical system can be learn only in the world. indeed the like
happen in every other kind of knowledge. neither physic nor law be
to be practically know from book. Nay, the farmer, the planter, the
gardener, must perfect by experience what he hath acquire the
rudiment of by read. how accurately soever the ingenious Mr Miller
may have describe the plant, he himself would advise his disciple to
see it in the garden. as we must perceive, that after the nice
stroke of a Shakespear or a Jonson, of a Wycherly or an Otway, some
touch of nature will escape the reader, which the judicious action
of a Garrick, of a Cibber, or a Clive,[*] can convey to he; so, on the
real stage, the character show himself in a strong and bold light
than he can be describe. and if this be the case in those fine and
nervous description which great author themselves have take from
life, how much more strongly will it hold when the writer himself
take his line not from nature, but from book? such character be
only the faint copy of a copy, and can have neither the justness nor
spirit of an original.
[*] there be a peculiar propriety in mention this great actor,
and these two most justly celebrate actress, in this place, as
they have all form themselves on the study of nature only, and not
on the imitation of their predecessor. hence they have be able to
excel all who have go before they; a degree of merit which the
servile herd of imitator can never possibly arrive at.
now this conversation in our historian must be universal, that be,
with all rank and degree of man; for the knowledge of what be call
high life will not instruct he in low; nor, _e converso_, will his
be acquaint with the inferior part of mankind teach he the
manner of the superior. and though it may be think that the
knowledge of either may sufficiently enable he to describe at least
that in which he hath be conversant, yet he will even here fall
greatly short of perfection; for the folly of either rank do in
reality illustrate each other. for instance, the affectation of high
life appear more glaring and ridiculous from the simplicity of the
low; and again, the rudeness and barbarity of this latter, strike
with much strong idea of absurdity, when contrast with, and
oppose to, the politeness which controul the former. besides, to say
the truth, the manner of our historian will be improve by both these
conversation; for in the one he will easily find example of
plainness, honesty, and sincerity; in the other of refinement,
elegance, and a liberality of spirit; which last quality I myself have
scarce ever see in man of low birth and education.
nor will all the quality I have hitherto give my historian avail
he, unless he have what be generally mean by a good heart, and be
capable of feeling. the author who will make I weep, say Horace,
must first weep himself. in reality, no man can paint a distress well
which he doth not feel while he be paint it; nor do I doubt, but
that the most pathetic and affect scene have be writ with tear.
in the same manner it be with the ridiculous. I be convinced I never
make my reader laugh heartily but where I have laugh before he;
unless it should happen at any time, that instead of laugh with I
he should be incline to laugh at I. perhaps this may have be the
case at some passage in this chapter, from which apprehension I will
here put an end to it.
Chapter ii.
contain a very surprizing adventure indeed, which Mr Jones meet with
in his walk with the Man of the Hill.
Aurora now first open her casement, _anglice_ the day begin to
break, when Jones walk forth in company with the stranger, and
mount Mazard Hill; of which they have no soon gain the summit
than one of the most noble prospect in the world present itself to
their view, and which we would likewise present to the reader, but for
two reason: first, we despair of make those who have see this
prospect admire our description; secondly, we very much doubt whether
those who have not see it would understand it.
Jones stand for some minute fix in one posture, and direct his
eye towards the south; upon which the old gentleman ask, what he
be look at with so much attention? "alas! sir," answer he with a
sigh, "I be endeavour to trace out my own journey hither. good
heaven! what a distance be Gloucester from we! what a vast track of
land must be between I and my own home!"--"Ay, ay, young gentleman,"
cry the other, "and by your sighing, from what you love well than
your own home, or I be mistaken. I perceive now the object of your
contemplation be not within your sight, and yet I fancy you have a
pleasure in look that way." Jones answer with a smile, "I find,
old friend, you have not yet forget the sensation of your youth. I
own my thought be employ as you have guess."
they now walk to that part of the hill which look to the
north-west, and which hang over a vast and extensive wood. here they
be no soon arrive than they hear at a distance the most violent
scream of a woman, proceed from the wood below they. Jones
listen a moment, and then, without say a word to his companion
(for indeed the occasion seem sufficiently press), run, or rather
slid, down the hill, and, without the least apprehension or concern
for his own safety, make directly to the thicket, whence the sound have
issue.
he have not enter far into the wood before he behold a most shocking
sight indeed, a woman stript half naked, under the hand of a ruffian,
who have put his garter round her neck, and be endeavour to draw
she up to a tree. Jones ask no question at this interval, but fall
instantly upon the villain, and make such good use of his trusty oaken
stick that he lay he sprawl on the ground before he could defend
himself, indeed almost before he know he be attack; nor do he
cease the prosecution of his blow till the woman herself beg he
to forbear, say, she believe he have sufficiently do his
business.
the poor wretch then fall upon her knee to Jones, and give he a
thousand thank for her deliverance. he presently lift she up, and
tell she he be highly pleased with the extraordinary accident which
have send he thither for her relief, where it be so improbable she
should find any; add, that Heaven seem to have design he as
the happy instrument of her protection. "Nay," answer she, "I could
almost conceive you to be some good angel; and, to say the truth, you
look more like an angel than a man in my eye." indeed he be a
charming figure; and if a very fine person, and a most comely set of
feature, adorn with youth, health, strength, freshness, spirit, and
good-nature, can make a man resemble an angel, he certainly have that
resemblance.
the redeem captive have not altogether so much of the human-angelic
specie: she seem to be at least of the middle age, nor have her face
much appearance of beauty; but her cloath be tear from all the
upper part of her body, her breast, which be well form and
extremely white, attract the eye of her deliverer, and for a few
moment they stand silent, and gaze at each other; till the ruffian
on the ground begin to move, Jones take the garter which have be
intend for another purpose, and bind both his hand behind he. and
now, on contemplate his face, he discover, greatly to his
surprize, and perhaps not a little to his satisfaction, this very
person to be no other than ensign Northerton. nor have the ensign
forget his former antagonist, whom he know the moment he come to
himself. his surprize be equal to that of Jones; but I conceive his
pleasure be rather less on this occasion.
Jones help Northerton upon his leg, and then look he stedfastly
in the face, "I fancy, sir," say he, "you do not expect to meet I
any more in this world, and I confess I have as little expectation to
find you here. however, fortune, I see, hath bring we once more
together, and hath give I satisfaction for the injury I have
receive, even without my own knowledge."
"it be very much like a man of honour, indeed," answer Northerton,
"to take satisfaction by knock a man down behind his back. neither
be I capable of give you satisfaction here, as I have no sword; but
if you dare behave like a gentleman, let we go where I can furnish
myself with one, and I will do by you as a man of honour ought."
"Doth it become such a villain as you be," cry Jones, "to
contaminate the name of honour by assume it? but I shall waste no
time in discourse with you. justice require satisfaction of you now,
and shall have it." then turn to the woman, he ask she, if she
be near her home; or if not, whether she be acquaint with any
house in the neighbourhood, where she might procure herself some
decent cloath, in order to proceed to a justice of the peace.
she answer she be an entire stranger in that part of the world.
Jones then recollect himself, say, he have a friend near who would
direct they; indeed, he wonder at his not follow; but, in fact,
the good Man of the Hill, when our heroe depart, sit himself down on
the brow, where, though he have a gun in his hand, he with great
patience and unconcern have attend the issue.
Jones then step without the wood, perceive the old man sit as
we have just describe he; he presently exert his utmost agility,
and with surprizing expedition ascend the hill.
the old man advise he to carry the woman to Upton, which, he say,
be the near town, and there he would be sure of furnish she
with all manner of conveniencie. Jones have receive his direction
to the place, take his leave of the Man of the Hill, and, desire he
to direct Partridge the same way, return hastily to the wood.
our heroe, at his departure to make this enquiry of his friend, have
consider, that as the ruffian's hand be tie behind he, he be
incapable of execute any wicked purpose on the poor woman. besides,
he know he should not be beyond the reach of her voice, and could
return soon enough to prevent any mischief. he have moreover declare
to the villain, that if he attempt the least insult, he would be
himself immediately the executioner of vengeance on he. but Jones
unluckily forget, that though the hand of Northerton be tie, his
leg be at liberty; nor do he lay the least injunction on the
prisoner that he should not make what use of these he please.
Northerton therefore have give no parole of that kind, think he
might without any breach of honour depart; not be oblige, as he
imagine, by any rule, to wait for a formal discharge. he therefore
take up his leg, which be at liberty, and walk off through the
wood, which favour his retreat; nor do the woman, whose eye be
perhaps rather turn toward her deliverer, once think of his escape,
or give herself any concern or trouble to prevent it.
Jones therefore, at his return, find the woman alone. he would have
spend some time in search for Northerton, but she would not permit
he; earnestly entreat that he would accompany she to the town
whither they have be direct. "as to the fellow's escape," say she,
"it give I no uneasiness; for philosophy and Christianity both
preach up forgiveness of injury. but for you, sir, I be concern at
the trouble I give you; nay, indeed, my nakedness may well make I
ashamed to look you in the face; and if it be not for the sake of
your protection, I should wish to go alone."
Jones offer she his coat; but, I know not for what reason, she
absolutely refuse the most earnest solicitation to accept it. he
then beg she to forget both the cause of her confusion. "with
regard to the former," say he, "I have do no more than my duty in
protect you; and as for the latter, I will entirely remove it, by
walk before you all the way; for I would not have my eye offend
you, and I could not answer for my power of resist the attractive
charm of so much beauty."
thus our heroe and the redeem lady walk in the same manner as
Orpheus and Eurydice march heretofore; but though I cannot believe
that Jones be designedly tempt by his fair one to look behind he,
yet as she frequently want his assistance to help she over stile,
and have besides many trip and other accident, he be often oblige
to turn about. however, he have well fortune than what attend poor
Orpheus, for he bring his companion, or rather follower, safe into
the famous town of Upton.
Chapter iii.
the arrival of Mr Jones with his lady at the inn; with a very full
description of the battle of Upton.
though the reader, we doubt not, be very eager to know who this lady
be, and how she fall into the hand of Mr Northerton, we must beg he
to suspend his curiosity for a short time, as we be oblige, for some
very good reason which hereafter perhaps he may guess, to delay his
satisfaction a little long.
Mr Jones and his fair companion no soon enter the town, than they
go directly to that inn which in their eye present the fairest
appearance to the street. here Jones, have order a servant to show
a room above stair, be ascend, when the dishevel fair, hastily
follow, be lay hold on by the master of the house, who cry,
"Heyday, where be that beggar wench go? stay below stair, I desire
you." but Jones at that instant thunder from above, "let the lady
come up," in so authoritative a voice, that the good man instantly
withdraw his hand, and the lady make the good of her way to the
chamber.
here Jones wish her joy of her safe arrival, and then depart, in
order, as he promise, to send the landlady up with some cloath. the
poor woman thank he heartily for all his kindness, and say, she
hop she should see he again soon, to thank he a thousand time
more. during this short conversation, she cover her white bosom as
well as she could possibly with her arm; for Jones could not avoid
steal a sly peep or two, though he take all imaginable care to
avoid give any offence.
our traveller have happen to take up their residence at a house of
exceed good repute, whither irish lady of strict virtue, and many
northern lass of the same predicament, be accustom to resort in
their way to Bath. the landlady therefore would by no mean have
admit any conversation of a disreputable kind to pass under her
roof. indeed, so foul and contagious be all such proceeding, that
they contaminate the very innocent scene where they be commit,
and give the name of a bad house, or of a house of ill repute, to all
those where they be suffer to be carry on.
not that I would intimate that such strict chastity as be preserve
in the temple of Vesta can possibly be maintain at a public inn. my
good landlady do not hope for such a blessing, nor would any of the
lady I have speak of, or indeed any other of the most rigid note,
have expect or insist on any such thing. but to exclude all vulgar
concubinage, and to drive all whore in rag from within the wall, be
within the power of every one. this my landlady very strictly adhere
to, and this her virtuous guest, who do not travel in rag, would
very reasonably have expect of she.
now it require no very blameable degree of suspicion to imagine that
Mr Jones and his ragged companion have certain purpose in their
intention, which, though tolerate in some christian country,
connive at in other, and practise in all, be however as expressly
forbid as murder, or any other horrid vice, by that religion which
be universally believe in those country. the landlady, therefore,
have no soon receive an intimation of the entrance of the above-say
person than she begin to meditate the most expeditious mean for
their expulsion. in order to this, she have provide herself with a
long and deadly instrument, with which, in time of peace, the
chambermaid be willnot to demolish the labour of the industrious
spider. in vulgar phrase, she have take up the broomstick, and be
just about to sally from the kitchen, when Jones accost she with a
demand of a gown and other vestment, to cover the half-naked woman
upstairs.
nothing can be more provoking to the human temper, nor more dangerous
to that cardinal virtue, patience, than solicitation of extraordinary
office of kindness on behalf of those very person with whom we be
highly incensed. for this reason Shakespear hath artfully introduce
his Desdemona soliciting favour for Cassio of her husband, as the
mean of inflame, not only his jealousy, but his rage, to the
high pitch of madness; and we find the unfortunate Moor less able
to command his passion on this occasion, than even when he behold his
value present to his wife in the hand of his suppose rival. in
fact, we regard these effort as insult on our understanding, and to
such the pride of man be very difficultly bring to submit.
my landlady, though a very good-temper woman, have, I suppose, some
of this pride in her composition, for Jones have scarce end his
request, when she fall upon he with a certain weapon, which, though
it be neither long, nor sharp, nor hard, nor indeed threaten from its
appearance with either death or wound, hath be however hold in great
dread and abhorrence by many wise man--nay, by many brave one;
insomuch, that some who have dare to look into the mouth of a loaded
cannon, have not dare to look into a mouth where this weapon be
brandish; and rather than run the hazard of its execution, have
content themselves with make a most pitiful and sneak figure in
the eye of all their acquaintance.
to confess the truth, I be afraid Mr Jones be one of these; for
though he be attack and violently belabour with the aforesaid
weapon, he could not be provoke to make any resistance; but in a most
cowardly manner apply, with many entreaty, to his antagonist to
desist from pursue she blow; in plain English, he only beg she
with the utmost earnestness to hear he; but before he could obtain
his request, my landlord himself enter into the fray, and embrace
that side of the cause which seem to stand very little in need of
assistance.
there be a sort of hero who be suppose to be determine in their
chusing or avoid a conflict by the character and behaviour of the
person whom they be to engage. these be say to know their man, and
Jones, I believe, know his woman; for though he have be so submissive
to she, he be no soon attack by her husband, than he demonstrate
an immediate spirit of resentment, and enjoin he silence under a
very severe penalty; no less than that, I think, of be convert
into fuel for his own fire.
the husband, with great indignation, but with a mixture of pity,
answer, "you must pray first to be make able. I believe I be a
well man than yourself; ay, every way, that I be;" and presently
proceed to discharge half-a-dozen whore at the lady above stair,
the last of which have scarce issue from his lip, when a swing
blow from the cudgel that Jones carry in his hand assault he over
the shoulder.
it be a question whether the landlord or the landlady be the most
expeditious in return this blow. my landlord, whose hand be
empty, fall to with his fist, and the good wife, uplift her broom
and aim at the head of Jones, have probably put an immediate end to
the fray, and to Jones likewise, have not the descent of this broom
be prevent--not by the miraculous intervention of any heathen
deity, but by a very natural though fortunate accident, viz., by the
arrival of Partridge; who enter the house at that instant (for fear
have cause he to run every step from the hill), and who, see the
danger which threaten his master or companion (which you chuse to
call he), prevent so sad a catastrophe, by catch hold of the
landlady's arm, as it be brandish aloft in the air.
the landlady soon perceive the impediment which prevent her blow;
and be unable to rescue her arm from the hand of Partridge, she
let fall the broom; and then leave Jones to the discipline of her
husband, she fall with the utmost fury on that poor fellow, who have
already give some intimation of himself, by cry, "Zounds! do you
intend to kill my friend?"
Partridge, though not much addict to battle, would not however stand
still when his friend be attack; nor be he much displeased with
that part of the combat which fall to his share; he therefore return
my landlady's blow as soon as he receive they: and now the fight be
obstinately maintain on all part, and it seem doubtful to which
side Fortune would incline, when the naked lady, who have listen at
the top of the stair to the dialogue which precede the engagement,
descend suddenly from above, and without weigh the unfair
inequality of two to one, fall upon the poor woman who be box with
Partridge; nor do that great champion desist, but rather redouble
his fury, when he find fresh succour be arrive to his assistance.
victory must now have fall to the side of the traveller (for the
brave troop must yield to number) have not Susan the chambermaid
come luckily to support her mistress. this Susan be as two-hand a
wench (accord to the phrase) as any in the country, and would, I
believe, have beat the famed Thalestris herself, or any of her subject
Amazons; for her form be robust and man-like, and every way make for
such encounter. as her hand and arm be form to give blow with
great mischief to an enemy, so be her face as well contrive to
receive blow without any great injury to herself, her nose be
already flat to her face; her lip be so large, that no swelling
could be perceive in they, and moreover they be so hard, that a
fist could hardly make any impression on they. lastly, her cheek-bone
stand out, as if nature have intend they for two bastion to defend
her eye in those encounter for which she seem so well calculate,
and to which she be most wonderfully well inclined.
this fair creature enter the field of battle, immediately file to
that wing where her mistress maintain so unequal a fight with one of
either sex. here she presently challenge Partridge to single combat.
he accept the challenge, and a most desperate fight begin between
they.
now the dog of war be let loose, begin to lick their bloody lip;
now victory, with golden wing, hang hover in the air; now Fortune,
take her scale from her shelf, begin to weigh the fate of Tom
Jones, his female companion, and Partridge, against the landlord, his
wife, and maid; all which hang in exact balance before she; when a
good-natured accident put suddenly an end to the bloody fray, with
which half of the combatant have already sufficiently feast. this
accident be the arrival of a coach and four; upon which my landlord
and landlady immediately desist from fight, and at their entreaty
obtain the same favour of their antagonist: but Susan be not so
kind to Partridge; for that Amazonian fair have overthrow and
bestrid her enemy, be now cuff he lustily with both her hand,
without any regard to his request of a cessation of arm, or to those
loud exclamation of murder which he roar forth.
no soon, however, have Jones quit the landlord, than he fly to
the rescue of his defeat companion, from whom he with much
difficulty draw off the enrage chambermaid: but Partridge be not
immediately sensible of his deliverance, for he still lie flat on the
floor, guard his face with his hand; nor do he cease roar till
Jones have force he to look up, and to perceive that the battle be
at an end.
the landlord, who have no visible hurt, and the landlady, hide she
well-scratch face with her handkerchief, run both hastily to the
door to attend the coach, from which a young lady and she maid now
alight. these the landlady presently usher into that room where Mr
Jones have at first deposit his fair prize, as it be the good
apartment in the house. hither they be oblige to pass through the
field of battle, which they do with the utmost haste, cover their
face with their handkerchief, as desirous to avoid the notice of any
one. indeed their caution be quite unnecessary; for the poor
unfortunate Helen, the fatal cause of all the bloodshed, be entirely
take up in endeavour to conceal her own face, and Jones be no
less occupy in rescue Partridge from the fury of Susan; which
be happily effect, the poor fellow immediately depart to the
pump to wash his face, and to stop that bloody torrent which Susan have
plentifully set a-flow from his nostril.
chapter iv.
in which the arrival of a man of war put a final end to hostility,
and cause the conclusion of a firm and last peace between all
party.
a serjeant and a file of musqueteer, with a deserter in their
custody, arrive about this time. the serjeant presently enquire for
the principal magistrate of the town, and be inform by my landlord,
that he himself be vest in that office. he then demand his
billet, together with a mug of beer, and complain it be cold,
spread himself before the kitchen fire.
Mr Jones be at this time comfort the poor distressed lady, who sit
down at a table in the kitchen, and lean her head upon her arm, be
bemoan she misfortune; but lest my fair reader should be in pain
concern a particular circumstance, I think proper here to acquaint
they, that before she have quit the room above stair, she have so
well cover herself with a pillowbeer which she there find, that she
regard to decency be not in the least violate by the presence of so
many man as be now in the room.
one of the soldier now go up to the serjeant, and whisper
something in his ear; upon which he stedfastly fix his eye on the
lady, and have look at she for near a minute, he come up to she,
say, "I ask pardon, madam; but I be certain I be not deceive; you
can be no other person than Captain Waters's lady?"
the poor woman, who in her present distress have very little regard
the face of any person present, no soon look at the serjeant than
she presently recollect he, and call he by his name, answer,
"that she be indeed the unhappy person he imagine she to be;" but
add, "I wonder any one should know I in this disguise." to which
the serjeant reply, "he be very much surprize to see her ladyship
in such a dress, and be afraid some accident have happen to
she."--"An accident hath happen to I, indeed," say she, "and I be
highly obliged to this gentleman" (point to Jones) "that it be not
a fatal one, or that I be now live to mention it."--"Whatever the
gentleman hath do," cry the serjeant, "I be sure the captain will
make he amend for it; and if I can be of any service, your ladyship
may command I, and I shall think myself very happy to have it in my
power to serve your ladyship; and so indeed may any one, for I know
the captain will well reward they for it."
the landlady, who hear from the stair all that past between the
serjeant and Mrs Waters, come hastily down, and run directly up to
she, begin to ask pardon for the offence she have commit, beg
that all might be impute to ignorance of her quality: for, "Lud!
madam," say she, "how should I have imagine that a lady of your
fashion would appear in such a dress? I be sure, madam, if I have once
suspect that your ladyship be your ladyship, I would soon have
burn my tongue out, than have say what I have say; and I hope your
ladyship will accept of a gown, till you can get your own cloath."
"Prithee, woman," say Mrs Waters, "cease your impertinence: how can
you imagine I should concern myself about anything which come from
the lip of such low creature as yourself? but I be surprize at your
assurance in thinking, after what be past, that I will condescend to
put on any of your dirty thing. I would have you know, creature, I
have a spirit above that."
here Jones interfere, and beg Mrs Waters to forgive the landlady,
and to accept her gown: "for I must confess," cry he, "our
appearance be a little suspicious when first we come in; and I be
well assure all this good woman do be, as she profess, out of
regard to the reputation of her house."
"yes, upon my truly be it," say she: "the gentleman speak very much
like a gentleman, and I see very plainly be so; and to be certain the
house be well known to be a house of as good reputation as any on the
road, and though I say it, be frequent by gentry of the good
quality, both Irish and English. I defy anybody to say black be my
eye, for that matter. and, as I be say, if I have know your
ladyship to be your ladyship, I would as soon have burn my finger as
have affront your ladyship; but truly where gentry come and spend
their money, I be not willing that they should be scandalize by a set
of poor shabby vermin, that, wherever they go, leave more lice than
money behind they; such folk never raise my compassion, for to be
certain it be foolish to have any for they; and if our justice do as
they ought, they would be all whipt out of the kingdom, for to be
certain it be what be most fitting for they. but as for your ladyship,
I be heartily sorry your ladyship hath have a misfortune, and if your
ladyship will do I the honour to wear my cloath till you can get
some of your ladyship's own, to be certain the good I have be at your
ladyship's service."
whether cold, shame, or the persuasion of Mr Jones prevail most on
Mrs Waters, I will not determine, but she suffer herself to be
pacify by this speech of my landlady, and retire with that good
woman, in order to apparel herself in a decent manner.
my landlord be likewise begin his oration to Jones, but be
presently interrupt by that generous youth, who shake he heartily
by the hand, and assure he of entire forgiveness, say, "if you
be satisfied, my worthy friend, I promise you I be;" and indeed, in
one sense, the landlord have the well reason to be satisfy; for he
have receive a bellyfull of drubbing, whereas Jones have scarce feel a
single blow.
Partridge, who have be all this time wash his bloody nose at the
pump, return into the kitchen at the instant when his master and the
landlord be shake hand with each other. as he be of a peaceable
disposition, he be pleased with those symptom of reconciliation; and
though his face bear some mark of Susan's fist, and many more of her
nail, he rather choose to be content with his fortune in the last
battle than to endeavour at better it in another.
the heroic Susan be likewise well content with her victory, though
it have cost she a black eye, which Partridge have give she at the
first onset. between these two, therefore, a league be strike, and
those hand which have be the instrument of war become now the
mediator of peace.
matter be thus restore to a perfect calm; at which the serjeant,
though it may seem so contrary to the principle of his profession,
testify his approbation. "why now, thatbe friendly," say he; "d--n
I, I hate to see two people bear ill-will to one another after they
have have a tussel. the only way when friend quarrel be to see it out
fairly in a friendly manner, as a man may call it, either with a fist,
or sword, or pistol, accord as they like, and then let it be all
over; for my own part, d--n I if ever I love my friend well than
when I be fight with he! to bear malice be more like a Frenchman
than an Englishman."
he then propose a libation as a necessary part of the ceremony at all
treaty of this kind. perhaps the reader may here conclude that he
be well verse in antient history; but this, though highly probable,
as he cite no authority to support the custom, I will not affirm with
any confidence. most likely indeed it be, that he found his opinion
on very good authority, since he confirm it with many violent oath.
Jones no soon hear the proposal than, immediately agree with the
learn serjeant, he order a bowl, or rather a large mug, fill
with the liquor use on these occasion, to be bring in, and then
begin the ceremony himself. he place his right hand in that of the
landlord, and, seize the bowl with his left, utter the usual
word, and then make his libation. after which, the same be observe
by all present. indeed, there be very little need of be particular
in describe the whole form, as it differ so little from those
libation of which so much be record in antient author and their
modern transcriber. the principal difference lie in two instance;
for, first, the present company pour the liquor only down their
throat; and, secondly, the serjeant, who officiate as priest, drank
the last; but he preserve, I believe, the antient form, in swallow
much the large draught of the whole company, and in be the only
person present who contribute nothing towards the libation besides
his good office in assist at the performance.
the good people now range themselves round the kitchen fire, where
good humour seem to maintain an absolute dominion; and Partridge not
only forget his shameful defeat, but convert hunger into thirst, and
soon become extremely facetious. we must however quit this agreeable
assembly for a while, and attend Mr Jones to Mrs Waters's apartment,
where the dinner which he have bespoke be now on the table. indeed, it
take no long time in prepare, have be all dr three day
before, and require nothing more from the cook than to warm it over
again.
Chapter v.
an apology for all hero who have good stomach, with a description
of a battle of the amorous kind.
hero, notwithstanding the high idea which, by the mean of
flatterer, they may entertain of themselves, or the world may
conceive of they, have certainly more of mortal than divine about
they. however elevate their mind may be, their body at least
(which be much the major part of most) be liable to the bad
infirmity, and subject to the vilest office of human nature. among
these latter, the act of eating, which hath by several wise man be
consider as extremely mean and derogatory from the philosophic
dignity, must be in some measure perform by the great prince,
heroe, or philosopher upon earth; nay, sometimes Nature hath be so
frolicsome as to exact of these dignified character a much more
exorbitant share of this office than she hath oblige those of the
low order to perform.
to say the truth, as no know inhabitant of this globe be really more
than man, so none need be ashamed of submit to what the
necessity of man demand; but when those great personage I have just
mention condescend to aim at confine such low office to
themselves--as when, by hoarding or destroy, they seem desirous to
prevent any other from eat--then they surely become very low and
despicable.
now, after this short preface, we think it no disparagement to our
heroe to mention the immoderate ardour with which he lay about he at
this season. indeed, it may be doubt whether Ulysses, who by the way
seem to have have the good stomach of all the hero in that eat
poem of the Odyssey, ever make a well meal. three pound at least of
that flesh which formerly have contribute to the composition of an ox
be now honour with become part of the individual Mr Jones.
this particular we think ourselves oblige to mention, as it may
account for our heroe's temporary neglect of his fair companion, who
eat but very little, and be indeed employ in consideration of a
very different nature, which pass unobserve by Jones, till he have
entirely satisfied that appetite which a fast of twenty-four hour have
procure he; but his dinner be no soon end than his attention to
other matter revive; with these matter therefore we shall now
proceed to acquaint the reader.
Mr Jones, of whose personal accomplishment we have hitherto say very
little, be, in reality, one of the handsome young fellow in the
world. his face, besides be the picture of health, have in it the
most apparent mark of sweetness and good-nature. these quality be
indeed so characteristical in his countenance, that, while the spirit
and sensibility in his eye, though they must have be perceive by
an accurate observer, might have escape the notice of the less
discerning, so strongly be this good-nature paint in his look, that
it be remark by almost every one who see he.
it be, perhaps, as much owe to this as to a very fine complexion
that his face have a delicacy in it almost inexpressible, and which
might have give he an air rather too effeminate, have it not be
join to a most masculine person and mien: which latter have as much
in they of the Hercules as the former have of the Adonis. he be
besides active, genteel, gay, and good-humour; and have a flow of
animal spirit which enliven every conversation where he be
present.
when the reader hath duly reflect on these many charm which all
center in our heroe, and consider at the same time the fresh
obligation which Mrs Waters have to he, it will be a mark more of
prudery than candour to entertain a bad opinion of she because she
conceive a very good opinion of he.
but, whatever censure may be pass upon she, it be my business to
relate matter of fact with veracity. Mrs Waters have, in truth, not
only a good opinion of our heroe, but a very great affection for he.
to speak out boldly at once, she be in love, accord to the present
universally-receive sense of that phrase, by which love be apply
indiscriminately to the desirable object of all our passion,
appetite, and sens, and be understand to be that preference which
we give to one kind of food rather than to another.
but though the love to these several object may possibly be one and
the same in all case, its operation however must be allow to be
different; for, how much soever we may be in love with an excellent
surloin of beef, or bottle of Burgundy; with a damask rise, or Cremona
fiddle; yet do we never smile, nor ogle, nor dress, nor flatter, nor
endeavour by any other art or trick to gain the affection of the
say beef, &c. Sigh indeed we sometimes may; but it be generally in
the absence, not in the presence, of the beloved object. for otherwise
we might possibly complain of their ingratitude and deafness, with the
same reason as Pasiphae doth of her bull, whom she endeavour to
engage by all the coquetry practise with good success in the
drawing-room on the much more sensible as well as tender heart of the
fine gentleman there.
the contrary happen in that love which operate between person of
the same specie, but of different sex. here we be no soon in
love than it become our principal care to engage the affection of the
object belove. for what other purpose indeed be our youth instruct
in all the art of render themselves agreeable? if it be not with
a view to this love, I question whether any of those trade which deal
in set off and adorn the human person would procure a
livelihood. Nay, those great polisher of our manner, who be by some
thought to teach what principally distinguish we from the brute
creation, even dancing-masters themselves, might possibly find no
place in society. in short, all the grace which young lady and
young gentleman too learn from other, and the many improvement
which, by the help of a look-glass, they add of their own, be in
reality those very _spicula et face amoris_ so often mention by
Ovid; or, as they be sometimes call in our own language, the whole
artillery of love.
now Mrs Waters and our heroe have no soon sit down together than the
former begin to play this artillery upon the latter. but here, as we
be about to attempt a description hitherto unassayed either in prose
or verse, we think proper to invoke the assistance of certain aërial
being, who will, we doubt not, come kindly to our aid on this
occasion.
"say then, ye Graces! you that inhabit the heavenly mansion of
Seraphina's countenance; for you be truly divine, be always in her
presence, and well know all the art of charming; say, what be the
weapon now use to captivate the heart of Mr Jones."
"first, from two lovely blue eye, whose bright orb flash lightning
at their discharge, fly forth two pointed ogle; but, happily for our
heroe, hit only a vast piece of beef which he be then convey into
his plate, and harmless spend their force. the fair warrior perceive
their miscarriage, and immediately from her fair bosom draw forth a
deadly sigh. a sigh which none could have hear unmove, and which be
sufficient at once to have sweep off a dozen beau; so soft, so sweet,
so tender, that the insinuate air must have find its subtle way to
the heart of our heroe, have it not luckily be drive from his ear
by the coarse bubble of some bottled ale, which at that time he be
pour forth. many other weapon do she assay; but the god of eat
(if there be any such deity, for I do not confidently assert it)
preserve his votary; or perhaps it may not be _dignu vindice nodus_,
and the present security of Jones may be account for by natural
mean; for as love frequently preserve from the attack of hunger, so
may hunger possibly, in some case, defend we against love.
"the fair one, enrage at her frequent disappointment, determine on
a short cessation of arm. which interval she employ in make ready
every engine of amorous warfare for the renewing of the attack when
dinner should be over.
"no soon then be the cloth remove than she again begin her
operation. first, have plant her right eye sidewise against Mr
Jones, she shoot from its corner a most penetrate glance; which,
though great part of its force be spend before it reach our heroe,
do not vent itself absolutely without effect. this the fair one
perceiving, hastily withdraw her eye, and level they downwards, as
if she be concerned for what she have do; though by this mean she
design only to draw he from his guard, and indeed to open his eye,
through which she intend to surprize his heart. and now, gently
lift up those two bright orb which have already begin to make an
impression on poor Jones, she discharge a volley of small charm at
once from her whole countenance in a smile. not a smile of mirth, nor
of joy; but a smile of affection, which most lady have always ready
at their command, and which serve they to show at once their
good-humour, their pretty dimple, and their white tooth.
"this smile our heroe receive full in his eye, and be immediately
stagger with its force. he then begin to see the design of the
enemy, and indeed to feel their success. a parley now be set on foot
between the party; during which the artful fair so slily and
imperceptibly carry on her attack, that she have almost subdue the
heart of our heroe before she again repair to act of hostility. to
confess the truth, I be afraid Mr Jones maintain a kind of dutch
defence, and treacherously deliver up the garrison, without duly
weigh his allegiance to the fair Sophia. in short, no soon have
the amorous parley end and the lady have unmask the royal battery,
by carelessly let her handkerchief drop from her neck, than the
heart of Mr Jones be entirely take, and the fair conqueror enjoy
the usual fruit of her victory."
here the Graces think proper to end their description, and here we
think proper to end the chapter.
Chapter vi.
a friendly conversation in the kitchen, which have a very common,
though not very friendly, conclusion.
while our lover be entertain themselves in the manner which be
partly describe in the forego chapter, they be likewise
furnish out an entertainment for their good friend in the kitchen.
and this in a double sense, by afford they matter for their
conversation, and, at the same time, drink to enliven their spirit.
there be now assemble round the kitchen fire, besides my landlord
and landlady, who occasionally go backward and forward, Mr
Partridge, the serjeant, and the coachman who drive the young lady and
she maid.
Partridge have acquaint the company with what he have learn from
the Man of the Hill concern the situation in which Mrs Waters have
be find by Jones, the serjeant proceed to that part of her
history which be know to he. he say she be the wife of Mr Waters,
who be a captain in their regiment, and have often be with he at
quarter. "some folk," say he, "use indeed to doubt whether they
be lawfully married in a church or no. but, for my part, thatbe no
business of mine: I must own, if I be put to my corporal oath, I
believe she be little well than one of we; and I fancy the captain
may go to heaven when the sun shin upon a rainy day. but if he do,
that be neither here nor there; for he willnot want company. and the
lady, to give the devil his due, be a very good sort of lady, and
love the cloth, and be always desirous to do strict justice to it;
for she hath beg off many a poor soldier, and, by her good-will,
would never have any of they punish. but yet, to be sure, ensign
Northerton and she be very well acquainted together at our last
quarter; that be the very right and truth of the matter. but the
captain he know nothing about it; and as long as there be enough for
he too, what do it signify? he love she not a bit the bad, and I
be certain would run any man through the body that be to abuse she;
therefore I willnot abuse she, for my part. I only repeat what other
folk say; and, to be certain, what everybody say, there must be some
truth in."--"Ay, ay, a great deal of truth, I warrant you," cry
Partridge; "_Veritas odium parit_"--"all a parcel of scandalous
stuff," answer the mistress of the house. "I be sure, now she be
drest, she look like a very good sort of lady, and she behave
herself like one; for she give I a guinea for the use of my
cloath."--"a very good lady indeed!" cry the landlord; "and if you
have not be a little too hasty, you would not have quarrel with
she as you do at first."--"you need mention that with my truly!"
answer she: "if it have not be for your nonsense, nothing have
happen. you must be meddle with what do not belong to you, and
throw in your fool's discourse."--"Well, well," answer he; "whatbe
past cannot be mend, so therebe an end of the matter."--"Yes," cry
she, "for this once; but will it be mend ever the more hereafter?
this be not the first time I have suffer for your numscull's pate. I
wish you would always hold your tongue in the house, and meddle only
in matter without door, which concern you. donot you remember what
happen about seven year ago?"--"Nay, my dear," return he, "donot
rip up old story. come, come, allbe well, and I be sorry for what I
have do." the landlady be go to reply, but be prevent by the
peace-make serjeant, sorely to the displeasure of Partridge, who be
a great lover of what be call fun, and a great promoter of those
harmless quarrel which tend rather to the production of comical than
tragical incident.
the serjeant ask Partridge whither he and his master be travel?
"none of your magister," answer Partridge; "I be no man's servant, I
assure you; for, though I have have misfortune in the world, I write
gentleman after my name; and, as poor and simple as I may appear now, I
have teach grammar-school in my time; _se hei mihi! non sum quod
fui_."--"no offence, I hope, sir," say the serjeant; "where, then, if
I may venture to be so bold, may you and your friend be
travelling?"--"you have now denominate we right," say Partridge.
"_amici sumus._ and I promise you my friend be one of the great
gentleman in the kingdom" (at which word both landlord and landlady
prick up their ear). "he be the heir of Squire Allworthy."--"what,
the squire who doth so much good all over the country?" cry my
landlady. "even he," answer Partridge.--"Then I warrant," say she,
"hewill have a swing great estate hereafter."--"most certainly,"
answer Partridge.--"Well," reply the landlady, "I think the first
moment I see he he look like a good sort of gentleman; but my
husband here, to be sure, be wise than anybody."--"I own, my dear,"
cry he, "it be a mistake."--"a mistake, indeed!" answer she; "but
when do you ever know I to make such mistakes?"--"but how come it,
sir," cry the landlord, "that such a great gentleman walk about the
country afoot?"--"i donot know," return Partridge; "great gentleman
have humour sometimes. he hath now a dozen horse and servant at
Gloucester; and nothing would serve he, but last night, it be very
hot weather, he must cool himself with a walk to yon high hill, whither
I likewise walk with he to bear he company; but if ever you catch
I there again: for I be never so frightened in all my life. we meet
with the strange man there."--"I'll be hang," cry the landlord,
"if it be not the Man of the Hill, as they call he; if indeed he be a
man; but I know several people who believe it be the devil that live
there."--"Nay, nay, like enough," say Partridge; "and now you put I
in the head of it, I verily and sincerely believe it be the devil,
though I could not perceive his cloven foot: but perhaps he might have
the power give he to hide that, since evil spirit can appear in what
shape they please."--"And pray, sir," say the serjeant, "no offence,
I hope; but pray what sort of a gentleman be the devil? for I have
hear some of our officer say there be no such person; and that it be
only a trick of the parson, to prevent their being break; for, if it
be publickly know that there be no devil, the parson would be of no
more use than we be in time of peace."--"those officer," say
Partridge, "be very great scholar, I suppose."--"Not much of
schollard neither," answer the serjeant; "they have not half your
learning, sir, I believe; and, to be sure, I think there must be a
devil, notwithstanding what they say, though one of they be a
captain; for methought, think I to myself, if there be no devil, how
can wicked people be send to he? and I have read all that upon a
book."--"some of your officer," quoth the landlord, "will find there
be a devil, to their shame, I believe. I donot question but hewill pay
off some old score upon my account. here be one quarter upon I
half a year, who have the conscience to take up one of my good bed,
though he hardly spend a shilling a day in the house, and suffer his
man to roast cabbage at the kitchen fire, because I would not give
they a dinner on a Sunday. every good Christian must desire there
should be a devil for the punishment of such wretch."--"Harkee,
landlord," say the serjeant, "donot abuse the cloth, for I willnot take
it."--"D--n the cloth!" answer the landlord, "I have suffer enough
by they."--"Bear witness, gentleman," say the serjeant, "he curse the
king, and thatbe high treason."--"I curse the king! you villain," say
the landlord. "yes, you do," cry the serjeant; "you curse the
cloth, and thatbe curse the king. itbe all one and the same; for
every man who curse the cloth would curse the king if he durst; so for
matter o' that, itbe all one and the same thing."--"excuse I there, Mr
Serjeant," quoth Partridge, "thatbe a _non sequitur_."--"none of your
outlandish linguo," answer the serjeant, leap from his seat; "I
will not sit still and hear the cloth abuse."--"you mistake I,
friend," cry Partridge. "I do not mean to abuse the cloth; I only
say your conclusion be a _non sequitur_.[*]"--"You
be another," cry the serjeant," an you come to that. no more a
_sequitur_ than yourself. you be a pack of rascal, and Iwill prove it;
for I will fight the good man of you all for twenty pound." this
challenge effectually silence Partridge, whose stomach for drubbing
do not so soon return after the hearty meal which he have lately be
treat with; but the coachman, whose bone be less sore, and whose
appetite for fighting be somewhat sharp, do not so easily brook the
affront, of which he conceive some part at least fall to his share. he
start therefore from his seat, and, advance to the serjeant, swore
he look on himself to be as good a man as any in the army, and
offer to box for a guinea. the military man accept the combat, but
refuse the wager; upon which both immediately stript and engage, till
the driver of horse be so well maul by the leader of man, that he
be oblige to exhaust his small remainder of breath in beg for
quarter.
[*] this word, which the serjeant unhappily mistake for an affront,
be a term in logic, and mean that the conclusion do not follow
from the premise.
the young lady be now desirous to depart, and have give order for
her coach to be prepare; but all in vain, for the coachman be
disabled from perform his office for that evening. an antient
heathen would perhaps have impute this disability to the god of
drink, no less than to the god of war; for, in reality, both the
combatant have sacrifice as well to the former deity as to the
latter. to speak plainly, they be both dead drunk, nor be Partridge
in a much well situation. as for my landlord, drinking be his
trade; and the liquor have no more effect on he than it have on any
other vessel in his house.
the mistress of the inn, be summon to attend Mr Jones and his
companion at their tea, give a full relation of the latter part of the
foregoing scene; and at the same time express great concern for the
young lady, "who," she say, "be under the utmost uneasiness at be
prevent from pursue her journey. she be a sweet pretty creature,"
add she, "and I be certain I have see her face before. I fancy she
be in love, and run away from her friend. who know but some
young gentleman or other may be expect she, with a heart as heavy
as her own?"
Jones fetch a heavy sigh at those word; of which, though Mrs Waters
observe it, she take no notice while the landlady continue in the
room; but, after the departure of that good woman, she could not
forbear give our heroe certain hint on her suspect some very
dangerous rival in his affection. the aukward behaviour of Mr Jones
on this occasion convince she of the truth, without his give she a
direct answer to any of her question; but she be not nice enough in
her amour to be greatly concerned at the discovery. the beauty of
Jones highly charm her eye; but as she could not see his heart, she
give herself no concern about it. she could feast heartily at the
table of love, without reflect that some other already have be, or
hereafter might be, feast with the same repast. a sentiment which,
if it deal but little in refinement, deal, however, much in
substance; and be less capricious, and perhaps less ill-natured and
selfish, than the desire of those female who can be content enough
to abstain from the possession of their lover, provide they be
sufficiently satisfied that no one else possess they.
Chapter vii.
contain a full account of Mrs Waters, and by what mean she come
into that distressful situation from which she be rescue by Jones.
though Nature hath by no mean mix up an equal share either of
curiosity or vanity in every human composition, there be perhaps no
individual to whom she hath not allot such a proportion of both as
require much art, and pain too, to subdue and keep under;--a
conquest, however, absolutely necessary to every one who would in any
degree deserve the character of wisdom or good breeding.
as Jones, therefore, might very justly be call a well-breed man, he
have stifle all that curiosity which the extraordinary manner in which
he have find Mrs Waters must be suppose to have occasion. he have,
indeed, at first throw out some few hint to the lady; but, when he
perceive she industriously avoid any explanation, he be content
to remain in ignorance, the rather as he be not without suspicion
that there be some circumstance which must have raise her blush,
have she relate the whole truth.
now since it be possible that some of our reader may not so easily
acquiesce under the same ignorance, and as we be very desirous to
satisfy they all, we have take uncommon pain to inform ourselves of
the real fact, with the relation of which we shall conclude this book.
this lady, then, have live some year with one Captain Waters, who be
a captain in the same regiment to which Mr Northerton belong. she
past for that gentleman's wife, and go by his name; and yet, as the
serjeant say, there be some doubt concern the reality of their
marriage, which we shall not at present take upon we to resolve.
Mrs Waters, I be sorry to say it, have for some time contract an
intimacy with the above-mention ensign, which do no great credit to
her reputation. that she have a remarkable fondness for that young
fellow be most certain; but whether she indulge this to any very
criminal length be not so extremely clear, unless we will suppose
that woman never grant every favour to a man but one, without grant
he that one also.
the division of the regiment to which Captain Waters belong have two
day precede the march of that company to which Mr Northerton be the
ensign; so that the former have reach Worcester the very day after
the unfortunate re-encounter between Jones and Northerton which we
have before record.
now, it have be agree between Mrs Waters and the captain that she
would accompany he in his march as far as Worcester, where they be
to take their leave of each other, and she be thence to return to
Bath, where she be to stay till the end of the winter's campaign
against the rebel.
with this agreement Mr Northerton be make acquainted. to say the
truth, the lady have make he an assignation at this very place, and
promise to stay at Worcester till his division come thither; with
what view, and for what purpose, must be leave to the reader's
divination; for, though we be oblige to relate fact, we be not
oblige to do a violence to our nature by any comment to the
disadvantage of the lovely part of the creation.
Northerton no soon obtain a release from his captivity, as we have
see, than he haste away to overtake Mrs Waters; which, as he be a
very active nimble fellow, he do at the last-mention city, some few
hour after Captain Waters have leave she. at his first arrival he make
no scruple of acquaint she with the unfortunate accident; which he
make appear very unfortunate indeed, for he totally extract every
particle of what could be call fault, at least in a court of honour,
though he leave some circumstance which might be questionable in a
court of law.
woman, to their glory be it speak, be more generally capable of that
violent and apparently disintereste passion of love, which seek only
the good of its object, than man. Mrs Waters, therefore, be no soon
apprize of the danger to which her lover be expose, than she lose
every consideration besides that of his safety; and this be a
matter equally agreeable to the gentleman, it become the immediate
subject of debate between they.
after much consultation on this matter, it be at length agree that
the ensign should go across the country to Hereford, whence he might
find some conveyance to one of the sea-port in Wales, and thence
might make his escape abroad. in all which expedition Mrs Waters
declare she would bear he company; and for which she be able to
furnish he with money, a very material article to Mr Northerton, she
have then in her pocket three bank-note to the amount of £90,
besides some cash, and a diamond ring of pretty considerable value on
her finger. all which she, with the utmost confidence, reveal to
this wicked man, little suspecting she should by these mean inspire
he with a design of rob she. now, as they must, by take horse
from Worcester, have furnish any pursuer with the mean of
hereafter discover their route, the ensign propose, and the lady
presently agree, to make their first stage on foot; for which purpose
the hardness of the frost be very seasonable.
the main part of the lady's baggage be already at Bath, and she have
nothing with she at present besides a very small quantity of linen,
which the gallant undertake to carry in his own pocket. all thing,
therefore, be settle in the evening, they arise early the next
morning, and at five o'clock depart from Worcester, it be then
above two hour before day, but the moon, which be then at the full,
give they all the light she be capable of affording.
Mrs Waters be not of that delicate race of woman who be oblige to
the invention of vehicle for the capacity of remove themselves from
one place to another, and with whom consequently a coach be reckon
among the necessary of life. her limb be indeed full of strength
and agility, and, as her mind be no less animate with spirit, she
be perfectly able to keep pace with her nimble lover.
have travel on for some mile in a high road, which Northerton
say he be inform lead to Hereford, they come at the break of day to
the side of a large wood, where he suddenly stop, and, affect to
meditate a moment with himself, express some apprehension from
travel any long in so public a way. upon which he easily
persuade his fair companion to strike with he into a path which
seem to lead directly through the wood, and which at length bring
they both to the bottom of Mazard Hill.
whether the execrable scheme which he now attempt to execute be the
effect of previous deliberation, or whether it now first come into his
head, I cannot determine. but be arrive in this lonely place,
where it be very improbable he should meet with any interruption, he
suddenly slip his garter from his leg, and, lay violent hand on
the poor woman, endeavour to perpetrate that dreadful and detestable
fact which we have before commemorate, and which the providential
appearance of Jones do so fortunately prevent.
Happy be it for Mrs Waters that she be not of the weak order of
female; for no soon do she perceive, by his tie a knot in his
garter, and by his declaration, what his hellish intention be,
than she stand stoutly to her defence, and so strongly struggle with
her enemy, scream all the while for assistance, that she delay
the execution of the villain's purpose several minute, by which mean
Mr Jones come to her relief at that very instant when her strength
fail and she be totally overpower, and deliver she from the
ruffian's hand, with no other loss than that of her cloath, which
be tear from her back, and of the diamond ring, which during the
contention either drop from her finger, or be wrench from it by
Northerton.
thus, reader, we have give thee the fruit of a very painful enquiry
which for thy satisfaction we have make into this matter. and here we
have open to thee a scene of folly as well as villany, which we
could scarce have believe a human creature capable of be guilty
of, have we not remember that this fellow be at that time firmly
persuade that he have already commit a murder, and have forfeit
his life to the law. as he conclude therefore that his only safety
lie in flight, he think the possessing himself of this poor woman's
money and ring would make he amend for the additional burthen he be
to lay on his conscience.
and here, reader, we must strictly caution thee that thou dost not
take any occasion, from the misbehaviour of such a wretch as this, to
reflect on so worthy and honourable a body of man as be the officer
of our army in general. thou wilt be pleased to consider that this
fellow, as we have already inform thee, have neither the birth nor
education of a gentleman, nor be a proper person to be enrol among
the number of such. if, therefore, his baseness can justly reflect on
any besides himself, it must be only on those who give he his
commission.
BOOK X.
in which the history go FORWARD about twelve hour.
Chapter i.
contain instruction very necessary to be peruse by modern
critic.
Reader, it be impossible we should know what sort of person thou wilt
be; for, perhaps, thou may'st be as learn in human nature as
Shakespear himself be, and, perhaps, thou may'st be no wise than
some of his editor. now, lest this latter should be the case, we
think proper, before we go any far together, to give thee a few
wholesome admonition; that thou may'st not as grossly misunderstand
and misrepresent we, as some of the said editor have misunderstood
and misrepresent their author.
first, then, we warn thee not too hastily to condemn any of the
incident in this our history as impertinent and foreign to our main
design, because thou dost not immediately conceive in what manner such
incident may conduce to that design. this work may, indeed, be
consider as a great creation of our own; and for a little reptile of
a critic to presume to find fault with any of its part, without
know the manner in which the whole be connect, and before he
come to the final catastrophe, be a most presumptuous absurdity. the
allusion and metaphor we have here make use of, we must acknowledge to
be infinitely too great for our occasion; but there be, indeed, no
other, which be at all adequate to express the difference between an
author of the first rate and a critic of the low.
another caution we would give thee, my good reptile, be, that thou
dost not find out too near a resemblance between certain character
here introduce; as, for instance, between the landlady who appear in
the seventh book and she in the ninth. thou art to know, friend, that
there be certain characteristic in which most individual of every
profession and occupation agree. to be able to preserve these
characteristic, and at the same time to diversify their operation,
be one talent of a good writer. again, to mark the nice distinction
between two person actuate by the same vice or folly be another;
and, as this last talent be find in very few writer, so be the true
discernment of it find in as few reader; though, I believe, the
observation of this form a very principal pleasure in those who be
capable of the discovery; every person, for instance, can distinguish
between Sir Epicure Mammon and Sir Fopling Flutter; but to note the
difference between Sir Fopling Flutter and Sir Courtly Nice require a
more exquisite judgment: for want of which, vulgar spectator of play
very often do great injustice in the theatre; where I have sometimes
know a poet in danger of be convict as a thief, upon much bad
evidence than the resemblance of hand hath be hold to be in the
law. in reality, I apprehend every amorous widow on the stage would
run the hazard of be condemn as a servile imitation of Dido, but
that happily very few of our play-house critic understand enough of
Latin to read Virgil.
in the next place, we must admonish thee, my worthy friend (for,
perhaps, thy heart may be well than thy head), not to condemn a
character as a bad one, because it be not perfectly a good one. if
thou dost delight in these model of perfection, there be book enow
write to gratify thy taste; but, as we have not, in the course of
our conversation, ever happen to meet with any such person, we have
not choose to introduce any such here. to say the truth, I a little
question whether mere man ever arrive at this consummate degree of
excellence, as well as whether there hath ever exist a monster bad
enough to verify that
_----nulla virtute redemptum
a vitiis_----[*]
[*] whose vice be not allay with a single virtue
in Juvenal; nor do I, indeed, conceive the good purpose serve by
insert character of such angelic perfection, or such diabolical
depravity, in any work of invention; since, from contemplate either,
the mind of man be more likely to be overwhelm with sorrow and shame
than to draw any good us from such pattern; for in the former
instance he may be both concerned and ashamed to see a pattern of
excellence in his nature, which he may reasonably despair of ever
arrive at; and in contemplate the latter he may be no less
affect with those uneasy sensation, at see the nature of which
he be a partaker degrade into so odious and detestable a creature.
in fact, if there be enough of goodness in a character to engage the
admiration and affection of a well-dispose mind, though there should
appear some of those little blemish _quas humana parum cavit
natura_, they will raise our compassion rather than our abhorrence.
indeed, nothing can be of more moral use than the imperfection which
be see in example of this kind; since such form a kind of surprize,
more apt to affect and dwell upon our mind than the fault of very
vicious and wicked person. the foible and vice of man, in whom
there be great mixture of good, become more glaring object from the
virtue which contrast they and shew their deformity; and when we find
such vice attend with their evil consequence to our favourite
character, we be not only teach to shun they for our own sake, but
to hate they for the mischief they have already bring on those we
love.
and now, my friend, have give you these few admonition, we will,
if you please, once more set forward with our history.
Chapter ii.
contain the arrival of an irish gentleman, with very extraordinary
adventure which ensue at the inn.
now the little trembling hare, which the dread of all her numerous
enemy, and chiefly of that cunning, cruel, carnivorous animal, man,
have confine all the day to her lurking-place, sport wantonly o'er
the lawn; now on some hollow tree the owl, shrill chorister of the
night, hoot forth note which might charm the ear of some modern
connoisseur in music; now, in the imagination of the half-drunk
clown, as he stagger through the churchyard, or rather charnelyard,
to his home, fear paint the bloody hobgoblin; now thief and
ruffian be awake, and honest watchman fast asleep; in plain English,
it be now midnight; and the company at the inn, as well those who
have be already mention in this history, as some other who
arrive in the evening, be all in bed. only Susan Chambermaid be
now stir, she be oblige to wash the kitchen before she retire
to the arm of the fond expect hostler.
in this posture be affair at the inn when a gentleman arrive there
post. he immediately alight from his horse, and, come up to Susan,
enquire of she, in a very abrupt and confused manner, be almost
out of breath with eagerness, whether there be any lady in the house?
the hour of night, and the behaviour of the man, who star very
wildly all the time, a little surprized Susan, so that she hesitate
before she make any answer; upon which the gentleman, with redouble
eagerness, beg she to give he a true information, say, he have
lose his wife, and be come in pursuit of she. "upon my shoul," cry
he, "I have be near catch she already in two or three place, if
I have not find she go just as I come up with she. if she be in the
house, do carry I up in the dark and show she to I; and if she be
go away before I, do tell I which way I shall go after she to meet
she, and, upon my shoul, I will make you the rich poor woman in the
nation." he then pull out a handful of guineas, a sight which would
have bribe person of much great consequence than this poor wench
to much bad purpose.
Susan, from the account she have receive of Mrs Waters, make not the
least doubt but that she be the very identical stray whom the right
owner pursue. as she conclude, therefore, with great appearance of
reason, that she never could get money in an honester way than by
restore a wife to her husband, she make no scruple of assure the
gentleman that the lady he want be then in the house; and be
presently afterwards prevail upon (by very liberal promise, and
some earnest pay into her hand) to conduct he to the bedchamber of
Mrs Waters.
it hath be a custom long establish in the polite world, and that
upon very solid and substantial reason, that a husband shall never
enter his wife's apartment without first knock at the door. the
many excellent us of this custom need scarce be hint to a reader
who hath any knowledge of the world; for by this mean the lady hath
time to adjust herself, or to remove any disagreeable object out of
the way; for there be some situation in which nice and delicate
woman would not be discover by their husband.
to say the truth, there be several ceremony institute among the
polished part of mankind, which, though they may, to coarser
judgment, appear as matter of mere form, be find to have much of
substance in they, by the more discerning; and lucky would it have
be have the custom above mention be observe by our gentleman in
the present instance. Knock, indeed, he do at the door, but not with
one of those gentle rap which be usual on such occasion. on the
contrary, when he find the door lock, he fly at it with such
violence, that the lock immediately give way, the door burst open, and
he fall headlong into the room.
he have no soon recover his leg than forth from the bed, upon his
leg likewise, appear--with shame and sorrow be we oblige to
proceed--our heroe himself, who, with a menacing voice, demand of
the gentleman who he be, and what he mean by dare to burst open
his chamber in that outrageous manner.
the gentleman at first thought he have commit a mistake, and be
go to ask pardon and retreat, when, on a sudden, as the moon shine
very bright, he cast his eye on stay, gown, petticoat, cap,
ribbon, stocking, garter, shoe, clog, &c., all which lie in a
disorder manner on the floor. all these, operate on the natural
jealousy of his temper, so enrage he, that he lose all power of
speech; and, without return any answer to Jones, he endeavour to
approach the bed.
Jones immediately interpose, a fierce contention arise, which soon
proceed to blow on both side. and now Mrs Waters (for we must
confess she be in the same bed), be, I suppose, awaken from her
sleep, and see two man fight in her bedchamber, begin to scream
in the most violent manner, cry out murder! robbery! and more
frequently rape! which last, some, perhaps, may wonder she should
mention, who do not consider that these word of exclamation be use
by lady in a fright, as fa, la, la, ra, da, &c., be in music, only
as the vehicle of sound, and without any fix idea.
next to the lady's chamber be deposit the body of an irish
gentleman who arrive too late at the inn to have be mention
before. this gentleman be one of those whom the irish call a
calabalaro, or cavalier. he be a young brother of a good family,
and, have no fortune at home, be oblige to look abroad in order to
get one; for which purpose he be proceed to the Bath, to try his
luck with card and the woman.
this young fellow lie in bed read one of Mrs Behn's novel; for he
have be instruct by a friend that he would find no more effectual
method of recommend himself to the lady than the improve his
understanding, and fill his mind with good literature. he no
soon, therefore, hear the violent uproar in the next room, than he
leap from his bolster, and, take his sword in one hand, and the
candle which burn by he in the other, he go directly to Mrs
Waters's chamber.
if the sight of another man in his shirt at first add some shock to
the decency of the lady, it make she presently amend by considerably
abate her fear; for no soon have the calabalaro enter the room
than he cry out, "Mr Fitzpatrick, what the devil be the maning of
this?" upon which the other immediately answer, "o, Mr Maclachlan! I
be rejoice you be here.--this villain hath debauch my wife, and be
get into bed with she."--"What wife?" cry Maclachlan; "do not I know
Mrs Fitzpatrick very well, and donot I see that the lady, whom the
gentleman who stand here in his shirt be lie in bed with, be none
of she?"
Fitzpatrick, now perceive, as well by the glimpse he have of the
lady, as by her voice, which might have be distinguish at a
great distance than he now stand from she, that he have make a very
unfortunate mistake, begin to ask many pardon of the lady; and then,
turn to Jones, he say, "I would have you take notice I do not ask
your pardon, for you have bate I; for which I be resolve to have
your blood in the morning."
Jones treat this menace with much contempt; and Mr Maclachlan
answer, "indeed, Mr Fitzpatrick, you may be ashamed of your own
self, to disturb people at this time of night; if all the people in
the inn be not asleep, you would have awaken they as you have I.
the gentleman have serve you very rightly. upon my conscience, though
I have no wife, if you have treat she so, I would have cut your
throat."
Jones be so confound with his fear for his lady's reputation, that
he know neither what to say or do; but the invention of woman be, as
hath be observe, much ready than that of man. she recollect
that there be a communication between her chamber and that of Mr
Jones; rely, therefore, on his honour and her own assurance, she
answer, "I know not what you mean, villain! I be wife to none of
you. help! rape! murder! rape!"--and now, the landlady come into the
room, Mrs Waters fall upon she with the utmost virulence, say, "she
think herself in a sober inn, and not in a bawdy-house; but that a
set of villain have break into her room, with an intent upon she
honour, if not upon her life; and both, she say, be equally dear to
she."
the landlady now begin to roar as loudly as the poor woman in bed have
do before. she cry, "she be undo, and that the reputation of
her house, which be never blow upon before, be utterly destroy."
then, turn to the man, she cry, "what, in the devil's name, be
the reason of all this disturbance in the lady's room?" fitzpatrick,
hang down his head, repeat, "that he have commit a mistake, for
which he heartily ask pardon," and then retire with his countryman.
Jones, who be too ingenious to have miss the hint give he by his
fair one, boldly assert, "that he have run to her assistance upon
hear the door break open, with what design he could not conceive,
unless of rob the lady; which, if they intend, he say, he have
the good fortune to prevent." "I never have a robbery commit in my
house since I have keep it," cry the landlady; "I would have you to
know, sir, I harbour no highwayman here; I scorn the word, thof I say
it. none but honest, good gentlefolk, be welcome to my house; and, I
thank good luck, I have always have enow of such customer; indeed as
many as I could entertain. here hath be my lord--," and then she
repeat over a catalogue of name and title, many of which we might,
perhaps, be guilty of a breach of privilege by insert.
Jones, after much patience, at length interrupt she, by make an
apology to Mrs Waters, for have appear before she in his shirt,
assure she "that nothing but a concern for her safety could have
prevail on he to do it." the reader may inform himself of her
answer, and, indeed, of her whole behaviour to the end of the scene,
by consider the situation which she affect, it be that of a
modest lady, who be awaken out of her sleep by three strange man in
her chamber. this be the part which she undertake to perform; and,
indeed, she execute it so well, that none of our theatrical actress
could exceed she, in any of their performance, either on or off the
stage.
and hence, I think, we may very fairly draw an argument, to prove how
extremely natural virtue be to the fair sex; for, though there be not,
perhaps, one in ten thousand who be capable of make a good actress,
and even among these we rarely see two who be equally able to
personate the same character, yet this of virtue they can all
admirably well put on; and as well those individual who have it not,
as those who possess it, can all act it to the utmost degree of
perfection.
when the man be all depart, Mrs Waters, recover from her fear,
recover likewise from her anger, and speak in much gentler accent
to the landlady, who do not so readily quit her concern for the
reputation of the house, in favour of which she begin again to number
the many great person who have sleep under her roof; but the lady
stopt her short, and have absolutely acquit she of have have any
share in the past disturbance, beg to be leave to her repose, which,
she say, she hop to enjoy unmoleste during the remainder of the
night. upon which the landlady, after much civility and many
courtsie, take she leave.
Chapter iii.
