a dialogue between the landlady and Susan the chamber-maid, proper to
be read by all inn-keeper and their servant; with the arrival, and
affable behaviour of a beautiful young lady; which may teach person
of condition how they may acquire the love of the whole world.
the landlady, remember that Susan have be the only person out of
bed when the door be burst open, resort presently to she, to
enquire into the first occasion of the disturbance, as well as who the
strange gentleman be, and when and how he arrive.
Susan relate the whole story which the reader know already, vary
the truth only in some circumstance, as she see convenient, and
totally conceal the money which she have receive. but whereas her
mistress have, in the preface to her enquiry, speak much in compassion
for the fright which the lady have be in concern any intended
depredation on her virtue, Susan could not help endeavour to quiet
the concern which her mistress seem to be under on that account, by
swear heartily she see Jones leap out from her bed.
the landlady fall into a violent rage at these word. "a likely story,
truly," cry she, "that a woman should cry out, and endeavour to
expose herself, if that be the case! I desire to know what well
proof any lady can give of her virtue than she cry out, which, I
believe, twenty people can witness for she she do? I beg, madam, you
would spread no such scandal of any of my guest; for it will not only
reflect on they, but upon the house; and I be sure no vagabond, nor
wicked beggarly people, come here."
"well," say Susan, "then I must not believe my own eye." "no,
indeed, must you not always," answer her mistress; "I would not have
believe my own eye against such good gentlefolk. I have not have a
well supper order this half-year than they order last night; and
so easy and good-humour be they, that they find no fault with my
Worcestershire perry, which I sell they for champagne; and to be sure
it be as well taste and as wholesome as the good champagne in the
kingdom, otherwise I would scorn to give it them; and they drink I two
bottle. no, no, I will never believe any harm of such sober good sort
of people."
Susan be thus silence, her mistress proceed to other matter.
"and so you tell I," continue she, "that the strange gentleman come
post, and there be a footman without with the horse; why, then, he be
certainly some of your great gentlefolk too. why do not you ask he
whether hewould have any supper? I think he be in the other gentleman's
room; go up and ask whether he call. perhaps hewill order something
when he find anybody stir in the house to dress it. now donot
commit any of your usual blunder, by tell he the fire's out, and
the fowl alive. and if he should order mutton, donot blab out that we
have none. the butcher, I know, kill a sheep just before I go to
bed, and he never refuse to cut it up warm when I desire it. go,
remember therebe all sort of mutton and fowl; go, open the door
with, Gentlemen, d'ye call? and if they say nothing, ask what his
honour will be pleased to have for supper? donot forget his honour.
go; if you donot mind all these matter well, youwill never come to
anything."
Susan depart, and soon return with an account that the two
gentleman be get both into the same bed. "two gentleman," say the
landlady, "in the same bed! thatbe impossible; they be two arrant
scrub, I warrant they; and I believe young squire Allworthy guess
right, that the fellow intend to rob her ladyship; for, if he have
break open the lady's door with any of the wicked design of a
gentleman, he would never have sneak away to another room to save
the expense of a supper and a bed to himself. they be certainly
thief, and their search after a wife be nothing but a pretence."
in these censure my landlady do Mr Fitzpatrick great injustice; for
he be really bear a gentleman, though not worth a groat; and though,
perhaps, he have some few blemish in his heart as well as in his
head, yet be a sneaking or a niggardly fellow be not one of they.
in reality, he be so generous a man, that, whereas he have receive a
very handsome fortune with his wife, he have now spend every penny of
it, except some little pittance which be settle upon she; and, in
order to possess himself of this, he have use she with such cruelty,
that, together with his jealousy, which be of the bitter kind, it
have force the poor woman to run away from he.
this gentleman then be well tired with his long journey from
Chester in one day, with which, and some good dry blow he have
receive in the scuffle, his bone be so sore, that, add to the
soreness of his mind, it have quite deprive he of any appetite for
eating. and be now so violently disappointed in the woman whom, at
the maid's instance, he have mistake for his wife, it never once
enter into his head that she might nevertheless be in the house,
though he have err in the first person he have attack. he therefore
yield to the dissuasion of his friend from search any far
after she that night, and accept the kind offer of part of his bed.
the footman and post-boy be in a different disposition. they be
more ready to order than the landlady be to provide; however, after
be pretty well satisfied by they of the real truth of the case, and
that Mr Fitzpatrick be no thief, she be at length prevail on to
set some cold meat before they, which they be devour with great
greediness, when Partridge come into the kitchen. he have be first
awake by the hurry which we have before see; and while he be
endeavour to compose himself again on his pillow, a screech-owl have
give he such a serenade at his window, that he leap in a most
horrible affright from his bed, and, huddle on his cloath with
great expedition, run down to the protection of the company, whom he
hear talk below in the kitchen.
his arrival detain my landlady from return to her rest; for she
be just about to leave the other two guest to the care of Susan; but
the friend of young Squire Allworthy be not to be so neglect,
especially as he call for a pint of wine to be mull. she
immediately obey, by put the same quantity of perry to the fire;
for this readily answer to the name of every kind of wine.
the irish footman be retire to bed, and the post-boy be go to
follow; but Partridge invite he to stay and partake of his wine,
which the lad very thankfully accept. the schoolmaster be indeed
afraid to return to bed by himself; and as he do not know how soon he
might lose the company of my landlady, he be resolve to secure that
of the boy, in whose presence he apprehend no danger from the devil
or any of his adherent.
and now arrive another post-boy at the gate; upon which Susan, be
order out, return, introduce two young woman in rid habit,
one of which be so very richly laced, that Partridge and the post-boy
instantly start from their chair, and my landlady fall to her
courtsie, and her ladyship, with great eagerness.
the lady in the rich habit say, with a smile of great condescension,
"if you will give I leave, madam, I will warm myself a few minute at
your kitchen fire, for it be really very cold; but I must insist on
disturb no one from his seat." this be speak on account of
Partridge, who have retreat to the other end of the room, strike with
the utmost awe and astonishment at the splendor of the lady's dress.
indeed, she have a much well title to respect than this; for she be
one of the most beautiful creature in the world.
the lady earnestly desire Partridge to return to his seat; but could
not prevail. she then pull off her glove, and display to the fire
two hand, which have every property of snow in they, except that of
melt. her companion, who be indeed her maid, likewise pull off
her glove, and discover what bear an exact resemblance, in cold and
colour, to a piece of frozen beef.
"I wish, madam," quoth the latter, "your ladyship would not think of
go any far to-night. I be terribly afraid your ladyship will
not be able to bear the fatigue."
"why sure," cry the landlady, "her ladyship's honour can never
intend it. o, bless I! far to-night, indeed! let I beseech your
ladyship not to think on't----But, to be sure, your ladyship cannot.
what will your honour be pleased to have for supper? I have mutton of
all kind, and some nice chicken."
"I think, madam," say the lady, "it would be rather breakfast than
supper; but I cannot eat anything; and, if I stay, shall only lie down
for an hour or two. however, if you please, madam, you may get I a
little sack whey, make very small and thin."
"yes, madam," cry the mistress of the house, "I have some excellent
white wine."--"you have no sack, then?" say the lady. "yes, an't
please your honour, I have; I may challenge the country for that--but
let I beg your ladyship to eat something."
"upon my word, I cannot eat a morsel," answer the lady; "and I shall
be much oblige to you if you will please to get my apartment ready as
soon as possible; for I be resolve to be on horseback again in three
hour."
"why, Susan," cry the landlady, "be there a fire light yet in the
wild-goose? I be sorry, madam, all my good room be full. several
people of the first quality be now in bed. herebe a great young
squire, and many other great gentlefolk of quality." Susan answer,
"that the irish gentleman be get into the wild-goose."
"be ever anything like it?" say the mistress; "why the devil would
you not keep some of the good room for the quality, when you know
scarce a day pass without some call here?----if they be
gentleman, I be certain, when they know it be for her ladyship, they
will get up again."
"not upon my account," say the lady; "I will have no person disturb
for I. if you have a room that be commonly decent, it will serve I
very well, though it be never so plain. I beg, madam, you will not
give yourself so much trouble on my account." "o, madam!" cry the
other, "I have several very good room for that matter, but none good
enough for your honour's ladyship. however, as you be so
condescend to take up with the good I have, do, Susan, get a fire
in the Rose this minute. will your ladyship be pleased to go up now,
or stay till the fire be light?" "I think I have sufficiently warm
myself," answer the lady; "so, if you please, I will go now; I be
afraid I have keep people, and particularly that gentleman (meaning
Partridge), too long in the cold already. indeed, I cannot bear to
think of keep any person from the fire this dreadful weather."--she
then depart with her maid, the landlady march with two light
candle before she.
when that good woman return, the conversation in the kitchen be all
upon the charm of the young lady. there be indeed in perfect beauty a
power which none almost can withstand; for my landlady, though she be
not pleased at the negative give to the supper, declare she have
never see so lovely a creature. Partridge run out into the most
extravagant encomium on her face, though he could not refrain from
pay some compliment to the gold lace on her habit; the post-boy
sing forth the praise of her goodness, which be likewise echo by
the other post-boy, who be now come in. "shebe a true good lady, I
warrant she," say he; "for she hath mercy upon dumb creature; for
she ask I every now and tan upon the journey, if I do not think
she should hurt the horse by rid too fast? and when she come in
she charge I to give they as much corn as ever they would eat."
such charm be there in affability, and so sure be it to attract the
praise of all kind of people. it may indeed be compare to the
celebrate Mrs Hussey.[*] it be equally sure to set off every female
perfection to the high advantage, and to palliate and conceal every
defect. a short reflection, which we could not forbear make in this
place, where my reader hath see the loveliness of an affable
deportment; and truth will now oblige we to contrast it, by show
the reverse.
[*] a celebrated mantua-maker in the Strand, famous for set off
the shape of woman.
chapter iv.
contain infallible nostrum for procure universal disesteem and
hatred.
the lady have no soon lay herself on her pillow than the
waiting-woman return to the kitchen to regale with some of those
dainty which her mistress have refuse.
the company, at her entrance, shew she the same respect which they
have before pay to her mistress, by rise; but she forget to imitate
she, by desire they to sit down again. indeed, it be scarce
possible they should have do so, for she place her chair in such a
posture as to occupy almost the whole fire. she then order a chicken
to be broil that instant, declare, if it be not ready in a
quarter of an hour, she would not stay for it. now, though the say
chicken be then at roost in the stable, and require the several
ceremony of catch, killing, and pick, before it be bring to
the gridiron, my landlady would nevertheless have undertake to do all
within the time; but the guest, be unfortunately admit behind
the scene, must have be witness to the _fourberie_; the poor woman
be therefore oblige to confess that she have none in the house; "but,
madam," say she, "I can get any kind of mutton in an instant from the
butcher's."
"do you think, then," answer the waiting-gentlewoman, "that I have
the stomach of a horse, to eat mutton at this time of night? sure you
people that keep inn imagine your better be like yourself.
indeed, I expect to get nothing at this wretched place. I wonder my
lady would stop at it. I suppose none but tradesman and grasier ever
call here." the landlady fire at this indignity offer to her house;
however, she suppress her temper, and content herself with say,
"very good quality frequent it, she thank heaven!" "donot tell
I," cry the other, "of quality! I believe I know more of people of
quality than such as you.--But, prithee, without trouble I with any
of your impertinence, do tell I what I can have for supper; for,
though I cannot eat horse-flesh, I be really hungry." "why, truly,
madam," answer the landlady, "you could not take I again at such a
disadvantage; for I must confess I have nothing in the house, unless a
cold piece of beef, which indeed a gentleman's footman and the
post-boy have almost clear to the bone." "Woman," say Mrs Abigail
(so for shortness we will call she), "I entreat you not to make I
sick. if I have fast a month, I could not eat what have be touch
by the finger of such fellow. be there nothing neat or decent to be
have in this horrid place?" "what think you of some egg and bacon,
madam?" say the landlady. "be your egg new laid? be you certain
they be lay to-day? and let I have the bacon cut very nice and
thin; for I cannot endure anything thatbe gross.--prithee try if you
can do a little tolerably for once, and donot think you have a
farmer's wife, or some of those creature, in the house."--The
landlady begin then to handle her knife; but the other stopt she,
say, "good woman, I must insist upon your first wash your hand;
for I be extremely nice, and have be always use from my cradle to
have everything in the most elegant manner."
the landlady, who govern herself with much difficulty, begin now the
necessary preparation; for as to Susan, she be utterly reject, and
with such disdain, that the poor wench be as hard put to it to
restrain her hand from violence as her mistress have be to hold she
tongue. this indeed Susan do not entirely; for, though she literally
keep it within her tooth, yet there it mutter many "marry-come-up,
as good flesh and blood as yourself;" with other such indignant
phrase.
while the supper be prepare, Mrs Abigail begin to lament she have
not order a fire in the parlour; but, she say, that be now too
late. "however," say she, "I have novelty to recommend a kitchen; for
I do not believe I ever eat in one before." then, turn to the
post-boy, she ask they, "why they be not in the stable with their
horse? if I must eat my hard fare here, madam," cry she to the
landlady, "I beg the kitchen may be keep clear, that I may not be
surround with all the blackguard in town: as for you, sir," say
she to Partridge, "you look somewhat like a gentleman, and may sit
still if you please; I donot desire to disturb anybody but mob."
"yes, yes, madam," cry Partridge, "I be a gentleman, I do assure
you, and I be not so easily to be disturb. _Non semper vox casualis
est verbo nominativus_." this Latin she take to be some affront, and
answer, "you may be a gentleman, sir; but you donot show yourself as
one to talk Latin to a woman." Partridge make a gentle reply, and
conclude with more Latin; upon which she toss up her nose, and
content herself by abuse he with the name of a great scholar.
the supper be now on the table, Mrs Abigail eat very heartily for
so delicate a person; and, while a second course of the same be by
her order prepare, she say, "and so, madam, you tell I your house
be frequent by people of great quality?"
the landlady answer in the affirmative, say, "there be a great
many very good quality and gentlefolk in it now. therebe young Squire
Allworthy, as that gentleman there know."
"and pray who be this young gentleman of quality, this young squire
Allworthy?" say Abigail.
"who should he be," answer Partridge, "but the son and heir of the
great Squire Allworthy, of Somersetshire!"
"upon my word," say she, "you tell I strange news; for I know Mr
Allworthy of Somersetshire very well, and I know he hath no son
alive."
the landlady prick up her ear at this, and Partridge look a
little confound. however, after a short hesitation, he answer,
"indeed, madam, it be true, everybody doth not know he to be squire
Allworthy's son; for he be never married to his mother; but his son
he certainly be, and will be his heir too, as certainly as his name be
Jones." at that word, Abigail let drop the bacon which she be
convey to her mouth, and cry out, "you surprize I, sir! be it
possible Mr Jones should be now in the house?" "_Quare non?_" answer
Partridge, "it be possible, and it be certain."
Abigail now make haste to finish the remainder of her meal, and then
repair back to her mistress, when the conversation pass which may
be read in the next chapter.
Chapter v.
show who the amiable lady, and her unamiable maid, be.
as in the month of June, the damask rise, which chance hath plant
among the lily, with their candid hue mix his vermilion; or as
some playsome heifer in the pleasant month of May diffuse she
odoriferous breath over the flowery meadow; or as, in the blooming
month of April, the gentle, constant dove, perch on some fair bough,
sit meditate on her mate; so, look a hundred charm and
breathing as many sweet, her thought be fix on her Tommy, with
a heart as good and innocent as her face be beautiful, Sophia (for it
be she herself) lie recline her lovely head on her hand, when she
maid enter the room, and, run directly to the bed, cry,
"Madam--madam--who doth your ladyship think be in the house?" Sophia,
start up, cry, "I hope my father hath not overtake we." "no,
madam, it be one worth a hundred father; Mr Jones himself be here at
this very instant." "Mr Jones!" say Sophia, "it be impossible! I
cannot be so fortunate." her maid aver the fact, and be presently
detach by her mistress to order he to be call; for she say she
be resolve to see he immediately.
Mrs Honour have no soon leave the kitchen in the manner we have before
see than the landlady fall severely upon she. the poor woman have
indeed be load her heart with foul language for some time, and
now it scour out of her mouth, as filth doth from a mud-cart, when
the board which confine it be remove. Partridge likewise shovel
in his share of calumny, and (what may surprize the reader) not only
bespatter the maid, but attempt to sully the lily-white character
of Sophia herself. "never a barrel the well herring," cry he,
"_Noscitur a socio_, be a true saying. it must be confess, indeed,
that the lady in the fine garment be the civiller of the two; but I
warrant neither of they be a bit well than they should be. a couple
of Bath trull, Iwill answer for they; your quality donot ride about at
this time o' night without servant." "sbodlikin, and thatbe true,"
cry the landlady, "you have certainly hit upon the very matter; for
quality donot come into a house without bespeak a supper, whether
they eat or no."
while they be thus discourse, Mrs Honour return and discharge
her commission, by bid the landlady immediately wake Mr Jones, and
tell he a lady want to speak with he. the landlady refer she to
Partridge, say, "he be the squire's friend: but, for her part, she
never call man-folk, especially gentleman," and then walk
sullenly out of the kitchen. Honour apply herself to Partridge; but
he refuse, "for my friend," cry he, "go to bed very late, and he
would be very angry to be disturb so soon." Mrs Honour insist
still to have he call, say, "she be sure, instead of be
angry, that he would be to the high degree delight when he know
the occasion." "another time, perhaps, he might," cry Partridge;
"but _non omnia possumus omne_. one woman be enough at once for a
reasonable man." "what do you mean by one woman, fellow?" cry
Honour. "none of your fellow," answer Partridge. he then proceed
to inform she plainly that Jones be in bed with a wench, and make use
of an expression too indelicate to be here insert; which so enrage
Mrs Honour, that she call he jackanape, and return in a violent
hurry to her mistress, whom she acquaint with the success of her
errand, and with the account she have receive; which, if possible, she
exaggerate, be as angry with Jones as if he have pronounce all the
word that come from the mouth of Partridge. she discharge a torrent
of abuse on the master, and advise her mistress to quit all thought
of a man who have never show himself deserve of she. she then rip
up the story of Molly Seagrim, and give the most malicious turn to his
formerly quit Sophia herself; which, I must confess, the present
incident not a little countenance.
the spirit of Sophia be too much dissipate by concern to enable
she to stop the torrent of she maid. at last, however, she interrupt
she, say, "I never can believe this; some villain hath belie he.
you say you have it from his friend; but surely it be not the office of
a friend to betray such secret." "I suppose," cry Honour, "the
fellow be his pimp; for I never see so ill-look a villain. besides,
such profligate rake as Mr Jones be never ashamed of these matter."
to say the truth, this behaviour of Partridge be a little
inexcusable; but he have not sleep off the effect of the dose which he
swallow the evening before; which have, in the morning, receive the
addition of above a pint of wine, or indeed rather of malt spirit;
for the perry be by no mean pure. now, that part of his head which
Nature design for the reservoir of drink be very shallow, a small
quantity of liquor overflow it, and open the sluice of his heart;
so that all the secret there deposit run out. these sluice be
indeed, naturally, very ill-secure. to give the well-natured turn we
can to his disposition, he be a very honest man; for, as he be the
most inquisitive of mortal, and eternally pry into the secret of
other, so he very faithfully pay they by communicate, in return,
everything within his knowledge.
while Sophia, torment with anxiety, know not what to believe, nor
what resolution to take, Susan arrive with the sack-whey. Mrs Honour
immediately advise her mistress, in a whisper, to pump this wench,
who probably could inform she of the truth. Sophia approve it, and
begin as follow: "come hither, child; now answer I truly what I be
go to ask you, and I promise you I will very well reward you. be
there a young gentleman in this house, a handsome young gentleman,
that----." here Sophia blush and be confound. "a young
gentleman," cry Honour, "that come hither in company with that saucy
rascal who be now in the kitchen?" Susan answer, "there be."--"Do
you know anything of any lady?" continue Sophia, "any lady? I donot
ask you whether she be handsome or no; perhaps she be not; thatbe
nothing to the purpose; but do you know of any lady?" "La, madam,"
cry Honour, "you will make a very bad examiner. Hark'ee, child,"
say she, "be not that very young gentleman now in bed with some nasty
trull or other?" here Susan smile, and be silent. "answer the
question, child," say Sophia, "and herebe a guinea for you."--"A
guinea! madam," cry Susan; "la, whatbe a guinea? if my mistress
should know it I shall certainly lose my place that very instant."
"herebe another for you," say Sophia, "and I promise you faithfully
your mistress shall never know it." Susan, after a very short
hesitation, take the money, and tell the whole story, conclude with
say, "if you have any great curiosity, madam, I can steal softly
into his room, and see whether he be in his own bed or no." she
accordingly do this by Sophia's desire, and return with an answer
in the negative.
Sophia now tremble and turn pale. Mrs Honour beg she to be
comfort, and not to think any more of so worthless a fellow. "why
there," say Susan, "I hope, madam, your ladyship willnot be offend;
but pray, madam, be not your ladyship's name Madam Sophia Western?"
"how be it possible you should know I?" answer Sophia. "why that
man, that the gentlewoman speak of, who be in the kitchen, tell about
you last night. but I hope your ladyship be not angry with I."
"indeed, child," say she, "I be not; pray tell I all, and I promise
you Iwill reward you." "why, madam," continue Susan, "that man tell we
all in the kitchen that Madam Sophia Western--indeed I donot know how
to bring it out."--Here she stopt, till, have receive encouragement
from Sophia, and be vehemently press by Mrs Honour, she proceed
thus:--"He tell we, madam, though to be sure it be all a lie, that
your ladyship be die for love of the young squire, and that he be
go to the war to get rid of you. I think to myself then he be a
false-hearted wretch; but, now, to see such a fine, rich, beautiful
lady as you be, forsake for such an ordinary woman; for to be sure so
she be, and another man's wife into the bargain. it be such a strange
unnatural thing, in a manner."
Sophia give she a third guinea, and, tell she she would certainly
be her friend if she mention nothing of what have pass, nor
inform any one who she be, dismiss the girl, with order to the
post-boy to get the horse ready immediately.
be now leave alone with she maid, she tell her trusty waiting-woman,
"that she never be more easy than at present. I be now convince,"
say she, "he be not only a villain, but a low despicable wretch. I
can forgive all rather than his expose my name in so barbarous a
manner. that render he the object of my contempt. yes, Honour, I be
now easy; I be indeed; I be very easy;" and then she burst into a
violent flood of tear.
after a short interval spend by Sophia, chiefly in cry, and
assure she maid that she be perfectly easy, Susan arrive with an
account that the horse be ready, when a very extraordinary thought
suggest itself to our young heroine, by which Mr Jones would be
acquaint with she have be at the inn, in a way which, if any
spark of affection for she remain in he, would be at least some
punishment for his fault.
the reader will be pleased to remember a little muff, which hath have
the honour of be more than once remember already in this history.
this muff, ever since the departure of Mr Jones, have be the constant
companion of Sophia by day, and her bedfellow by night; and this muff
she have at this very instant upon her arm; whence she take it off with
great indignation, and, have writ her name with her pencil upon a
piece of paper which she pin to it, she bribe the maid to convey
it into the empty bed of Mr Jones, in which, if he do not find it,
she charge she to take some method of convey it before his eye in
the morning.
then, have pay for what Mrs Honour have eat, in which bill be
include an account for what she herself might have eat, she mount
her horse, and, once more assure her companion that she be
perfectly easy, continue her journey.
Chapter vi.
Containing, among other thing, the ingenuity of Partridge, the
madness of Jones, and the folly of Fitzpatrick.
it be now past five in the morning, and other company begin to rise
and come to the kitchen, among whom be the serjeant and the
coachman, who, be thoroughly reconcile, make a libation, or, in
the english phrase, drink a hearty cup together.
in this drinking nothing more remarkable happen than the behaviour
of Partridge, who, when the serjeant drink a health to King George,
repeat only the word king; nor could he be bring to utter more;
for though he be go to fight against his own cause, yet he could
not be prevail upon to drink against it.
Mr Jones, be now return to his own bed (but from whence he
return we must beg to be excuse from relate), summon Partridge
from this agreeable company, who, after a ceremonious preface, have
obtain leave to offer his advice, deliver himself as follows:--
"it be, sir, an old saying, and a true one, that a wise man may
sometimes learn counsel from a fool; I wish, therefore, I might be so
bold as to offer you my advice, which be to return home again, and
leave these _horrida bella_, these bloody war, to fellow who be
content to swallow gunpowder, because they have nothing else to eat.
now, everybody know your honour want for nothing at home; when
thatbe the case, why should any man travel abroad?"
"Partridge," cry Jones, "thou art certainly a coward; I wish,
therefore, thou wouldst return home thyself, and trouble I no more."
"I ask your honour's pardon," cry Partridge; "I speak on your
account more than my own; for as to I, Heaven know my circumstance
be bad enough, and I be so far from be afraid, that I value a
pistol, or a blunderbuss, or any such thing, no more than a pop-gun.
every man must die once, and what signify the manner how? besides,
perhaps I may come off with the loss only of an arm or a leg. I assure
you, sir, I be never less afraid in my life; and so, if your honour
be resolve to go on, I be resolve to follow you. but, in that case,
I wish I might give my opinion. to be sure, it be a scandalous way of
travel, for a great gentleman like you to walk afoot. now here be
two or three good horse in the stable, which the landlord will
certainly make no scruple of trust you with; but, if he should, I
can easily contrive to take they; and, let the bad come to the
bad, the king would certainly pardon you, as you be go to fight
in his cause."
now, as the honesty of Partridge be equal to his understanding, and
both deal only in small matter, he would never have attempt a
roguery of this kind, have he not imagine it altogether safe; for he
be one of those who have more consideration of the gallow than of
the fitness of thing; but, in reality, he think he might have
commit this felony without any danger; for, besides that he doubt
not but the name of Mr Allworthy would sufficiently quiet the
landlord, he conceive they should be altogether safe, whatever turn
affair might take; as Jones, he imagine, would have friend enough
on one side, and as his friend would as well secure he on the other.
when Mr Jones find that Partridge be in earnest in this proposal, he
very severely rebuke he, and that in such bitter term, that the
other attempt to laugh it off, and presently turn the discourse to
other matter; say, he believe they be then in a bawdy house,
and that he have with much ado prevent two wench from disturb
his honour in the middle of the night. "Heyday!" say he, "I believe
they get into your chamber whether I would or no; for here lie the
muff of one of they on the ground." indeed, as Jones return to his
bed in the dark, he have never perceive the muff on the quilt, and, in
leap into his bed, he have tumble it on the floor. this Partridge
now take up, and be go to put into his pocket, when Jones desire
to see it. the muff be so very remarkable, that our heroe might
possibly have recollect it without the information annex. but his
memory be not put to that hard office; for at the same instant he see
and read the word Sophia Western upon the paper which be pin to
it. his look now grow frantic in a moment, and he eagerly cry out,
"oh Heavens! how come this muff here?" "I know no more than your
honour," cry Partridge; "but I see it upon the arm of one of the
woman who would have disturb you, if I would have suffer they."
"where be they?" cry Jones, jump out of bed, and lay hold of
his cloath. "many mile off, I believe, by this time," say
Partridge. and now Jones, upon further enquiry, be sufficiently
assure that the bearer of this muff be no other than the lovely
Sophia herself.
the behaviour of Jones on this occasion, his thought, his look, his
word, his action, be such as beggar all description. after many
bitter execration on Partridge, and not few on himself, he order
the poor fellow, who be frightened out of his wit, to run down and
hire he horse at any rate; and a very few minute afterwards, have
shuffle on his clothe, he hasten down-stair to execute the order
himself, which he have just before give.
but before we proceed to what pass on his arrival in the kitchen, it
will be necessary to recur to what have there happen since Partridge
have first leave it on his master's summon.
the serjeant be just march off with his party, when the two irish
gentleman arise, and come downstairs; both complain that they have
be so often wake by the noise in the inn, that they have never once
be able to close their eye all night.
the coach which have bring the young lady and her maid, and which,
perhaps, the reader may have hitherto conclude be her own, be,
indeed, a return coach belong to Mr King, of Bath, one of the
worthy and honest man that ever deal in horse-flesh, and whose
coach we heartily recommend to all our reader who travel that road.
by which mean they may, perhaps, have the pleasure of rid in the
very coach, and be drive by the very coachman, that be record in
this history.
the coachman, have but two passenger, and hear Mr Maclachlan be
go to Bath, offer to carry he thither at a very moderate price.
he be induce to this by the report of the hostler, who say that the
horse which Mr Maclachlan have hire from Worcester would be much more
pleased with return to his friend there than to prosecute a long
journey; for that the say horse be rather a two-legged than a
four-legged animal.
Mr Maclachlan immediately close with the proposal of the coachman,
and, at the same time, persuade his friend Fitzpatrick to accept of
the fourth place in the coach. this conveyance the soreness of his
bone make more agreeable to he than a horse; and, be well assure
of meet with his wife at Bath, he think a little delay would be
of no consequence.
Maclachlan, who be much the sharp man of the two, no soon hear
that this lady come from Chester, with the other circumstance which
he learn from the hostler, than it come into his head that she might
possibly be his friend's wife; and presently acquaint he with this
suspicion, which have never once occur to Fitzpatrick himself. to
say the truth, he be one of those composition which nature make up
in too great a hurry, and forget to put any brain into their head.
now it happen to this sort of man, as to bad hound, who never hit
off a fault themselves; but no soon doth a dog of sagacity open his
mouth than they immediately do the same, and, without the guidance of
any scent, run directly forwards as fast as they be able. in the same
manner, the very moment Mr Maclachlan have mention his apprehension,
Mr Fitzpatrick instantly concur, and fly directly up-stair, to
surprize his wife, before he know where she be; and unluckily (as
Fortune love to play trick with those gentleman who put themselves
entirely under her conduct) run his head against several door and
post to no purpose. much kinder be she to I, when she suggest
that simile of the hound, just before insert; since the poor wife
may, on these occasion, be so justly compare to a hunted hare. like
that little wretched animal, she prick up her ear to listen after
the voice of her pursuer; like she, fly away tremble when she
hear it; and, like she, be generally overtake and destroy in the
end.
this be not however the case at present; for after a long fruitless
search, Mr Fitzpatrick return to the kitchen, where, as if this have
be a real chace, enter a gentleman hallow as hunter do when
the hound be at a fault. he be just alight from his horse, and
have many attendant at his heel.
here, reader, it may be necessary to acquaint thee with some matter,
which, if thou dost know already, thou art wise than I take thee to
be. and this information thou shalt receive in the next chapter.
Chapter vii.
in which be conclude the adventure that happen at the inn at
Upton.
in the first place, then, this gentleman just arrive be no other
person than Squire Western himself, who be come hither in pursuit of
his daughter; and, have he fortunately be two hour early, he have
not only find she, but his niece into the bargain; for such be the
wife of Mr Fitzpatrick, who have run away with she five year before,
out of the custody of that sage lady, Madam Western.
now this lady have depart from the inn much about the same time with
Sophia; for, have be wake by the voice of her husband, she have
send up for the landlady, and be by she apprized of the matter, have
bribe the good woman, at an extravagant price, to furnish she with
horse for her escape. such prevalence have money in this family; and
though the mistress would have turn away her maid for a corrupt
hussy, if she have know as much as the reader, yet she be no more
proof against corruption herself than poor Susan have be.
Mr Western and his nephew be not know to one another; nor indeed
would the former have take any notice of the latter if he have know
he; for, this be a steal match, and consequently an unnatural one
in the opinion of the good squire, he have, from the time of she
commit it, abandon the poor young creature, who be then no more
than eighteen, as a monster, and have never since suffer she to be
name in his presence.
the kitchen be now a scene of universal confusion, Western enquiring
after his daughter, and Fitzpatrick as eagerly after his wife, when
Jones enter the room, unfortunately have Sophia's muff in his
hand.
as soon as Western see Jones, he set up the same holla as be use by
sportsman when their game be in view. he then immediately run up and
lay hold of Jones, cry, "we have get the dog fox, I warrant the
bitch be not far off." the jargon which follow for some minute,
where many speak different thing at the same time, as it would be
very difficult to describe, so would it be no less unpleasant to read.
Jones have, at length, shake Mr Western off, and some of the
company have interfere between they, our heroe protest his
innocence as to know anything of the lady; when Parson Supple
step up, and say, "it be folly to deny it; for why, the mark of
guilt be in thy hand. I will myself asseverate and bind it by an
oath, that the muff thou bearest in thy hand belongeth unto Madam
Sophia; for I have frequently observe she, of later day, to bear it
about she." "my daughter's muff!" cry the squire in a rage. "hath he
get my daughter's muff? bear witness the good be find upon he.
Iwill have he before a justice of peace this instant. where be my
daughter, villain?" "Sir," say Jones, "I beg you would be pacify.
the muff, I acknowledge, be the young lady's; but, upon my honour, I
have never see she." at these word Western lose all patience, and
grow inarticulate with rage.
some of the servant have acquaint Fitzpatrick who Mr Western be.
the good Irishman, therefore, think he have now an opportunity to do
an act of service to his uncle, and by that mean might possibly
obtain his favour, stept up to Jones, and cry out, "upon my
conscience, sir, you may be ashamed of deny your have see the
gentleman's daughter before my face, when you know I find you there
upon the bed together." then, turn to Western, he offer to
conduct he immediately to the room where his daughter be; which
offer be accept, he, the squire, the parson, and some other,
ascend directly to Mrs Waters's chamber, which they enter with no
less violence than Mr Fitzpatrick have do before.
the poor lady start from her sleep with as much amazement as terror,
and behold at her bedside a figure which might very well be suppose
to have escape out of Bedlam. such wildness and confusion be in the
look of Mr Western; who no soon see the lady than he start back,
shew sufficiently by his manner, before he speak, that this be not
the person seek after.
so much more tenderly do woman value their reputation than their
person, that, though the latter seem now in more danger than
before, yet, as the former be secure, the lady scream not with such
violence as she have do on the other occasion. however, she no soon
find herself alone than she abandon all thought of further repose;
and, as she have sufficient reason to be dissatisfied with her present
lodging, she dress herself with all possible expedition.
Mr Western now proceed to search the whole house, but to as little
purpose as he have disturb poor Mrs Waters. he then return
disconsolate into the kitchen, where he find Jones in the custody of
his servant.
this violent uproar have raise all the people in the house, though it
be yet scarcely daylight. among these be a grave gentleman, who have
the honour to be in the commission of the peace for the county of
Worcester. of which Mr Western be no soon informed than he offer
to lay his complaint before he. the justice decline execute his
office, as he say he have no clerk present, nor no book about justice
business; and that he could not carry all the law in his head about
steal away daughter, and such sort of thing.
here Mr Fitzpatrick offer to lend he his assistance, inform the
company that he have be himself breed to the law. (and indeed he have
serve three year as clerk to an attorney in the north of Ireland,
when, chuse a genteeler walk in life, he quit his master, come
over to England, and set up that business which require no
apprenticeship, namely, that of a gentleman, in which he have
succeed, as hath be already partly mention.)
Mr Fitzpatrick declare that the law concern daughter be out of
the present case; that steal a muff be undoubtedly felony, and the
good be find upon the person, be sufficient evidence of the
fact.
the magistrate, upon the encouragement of so learn a coadjutor, and
upon the violent intercession of the squire, be at length prevail
upon to seat himself in the chair of justice, where be place, upon
view the muff which Jones still hold in his hand, and upon the
parson's swear it to be the property of Mr Western, he desire Mr
Fitzpatrick to draw up a commitment, which he say he would sign.
Jones now desire to be hear, which be at last, with difficulty,
grant he. he then produce the evidence of Mr Partridge, as to the
find it; but, what be still more, Susan depose that Sophia
herself have deliver the muff to she, and have order she to convey
it into the chamber where Mr Jones have find it.
whether a natural love of justice, or the extraordinary comeliness of
Jones, have work on Susan to make the discovery, I will not
determine; but such be the effect of her evidence, that the
magistrate, throw himself back in his chair, declare that the
matter be now altogether as clear on the side of the prisoner as it
have before be against he: with which the parson concur, say,
the Lord forbid he should be instrumental in commit an innocent
person to durance. the justice then arise, acquit the prisoner, and
break up the court.
Mr Western now give every one present a hearty curse, and, immediately
order his horse, depart in pursuit of his daughter, without
take the least notice of his nephew Fitzpatrick, or return any
answer to his claim of kindred, notwithstanding all the obligation he
have just receive from that gentleman. in the violence, moreover, of
his hurry, and of his passion, he luckily forget to demand the muff of
Jones: I say luckily; for he would have die on the spot rather than
have part with it.
Jones likewise, with his friend Partridge, set forward the moment he
have pay his reckoning, in quest of his lovely Sophia, whom he now
resolve never more to abandon the pursuit of. nor could he bring
himself even to take leave of Mrs Waters; of whom he detest the very
thought, as she have be, though not designedly, the occasion of his
miss the happy interview with Sophia, to whom he now vow
eternal constancy.
as for Mrs Waters, she take the opportunity of the coach which be
go to Bath; for which place she set out in company with the two
irish gentleman, the landlady kindly lend she her cloath; in
return for which she be content only to receive about double their
value, as a recompence for the loan. upon the road she be perfectly
reconcile to Mr Fitzpatrick, who be a very handsome fellow, and
indeed do all she could to console he in the absence of his wife.
thus end the many odd adventure which Mr Jones encounter at his
inn at Upton, where they talk, to this day, of the beauty and lovely
behaviour of the charming Sophia, by the name of the Somersetshire
angel.
Chapter viii.
in which the history go backward.
before we proceed any far in our history, it may be proper to look
a little back, in order to account for the extraordinary appearance of
Sophia and her father at the inn at Upton.
the reader may be pleased to remember that, in the ninth chapter of
the seventh book of our history, we leave Sophia, after a long debate
between love and duty, decide the cause, as it usually, I believe,
happen, in favour of the former.
this debate have arise, as we have there show, from a visit which her
father have just before make she, in order to force her consent to a
marriage with Blifil; and which he have understand to be fully imply
in her acknowledgment "that she neither must nor could refuse any
absolute command of his."
now from this visit the squire retire to his evening potation,
overjoy at the success he have gain with his daughter; and, as he
be of a social disposition, and willing to have partaker in his
happiness, the beer be order to flow very liberally into the
kitchen; so that before eleven in the evening there be not a single
person sober in the house except only Mrs Western herself and the
charming Sophia.
early in the morning a messenger be despatch to summon Mr Blifil;
for, though the squire imagine that young gentleman have be much
less acquainted than he really be with the former aversion of his
daughter, as he have not, however, yet receive her consent, he long
impatiently to communicate it to he, not doubt but that the
intend bride herself would confirm it with her lip. as to the
wedding, it have the evening before be fix, by the male party, to
be celebrate on the next morning save one.
Breakfast be now set forth in the parlour, where Mr Blifil attend,
and where the squire and his sister likewise be assemble; and now
Sophia be order to be call.
O, Shakespear! have I thy pen! O, Hogarth! have I thy pencil! then would
I draw the picture of the poor serve-man, who, with pale
countenance, star eye, chatter tooth, faultere tongue, and
trembling limb,
(e'en such a man, so faint, so spiritless,
so dull, so dead in look, so woe-begone,
Drew Priam's curtain in the dead of night,
and would have tell he, half his Troy be burn'd)
enter the room, and declare--that Madam Sophia be not to be find.
"not to be find!" cry the squire, start from his chair; "zound
and d--nation! blood and fury! where, when, how, what--not to be
find! where?"
"La! brother," say Mrs Western, with true political coldness, "you
be always throw yourself into such violent passion for nothing.
my niece, I suppose, be only walk out into the garden. I protest you
be grow so unreasonable, that it be impossible to live in the house
with you."
"Nay, nay," answer the squire, return as suddenly to himself, as
he have go from himself; "if that be all the matter, it signify not
much; but, upon my soul, my mind misgave I when the fellow say she
be not to be find." he then give order for the bell to be ring in
the garden, and sit himself contentedly down.
no two thing could be more the reverse of each other than be the
brother and sister in most instance; particularly in this, that as
the brother never foresaw anything at a distance, but be most
sagacious in immediately see everything the moment it have happen;
so the sister eternally foresee at a distance, but be not so
quick-sighted to object before her eye. of both these the reader may
have observe example: and, indeed, both their several talent be
excessive; for, as the sister often foresaw what never come to pass,
so the brother often see much more than be actually the truth.
this be not however the case at present. the same report be bring
from the garden as before have be bring from the chamber, that
Madam Sophia be not to be find.
the squire himself now sally forth, and begin to roar forth the name
of Sophia as loudly, and in as hoarse a voice, as whilome do Hercules
that of Hylas; and, as the poet tell we that the whole shore echo
back the name of that beautiful youth, so do the house, the garden,
and all the neighbouring field resound nothing but the name of
Sophia, in the hoarse voice of the man, and in the shrill pipe of
the woman; while echo seem so pleased to repeat the beloved sound,
that, if there be really such a person, I believe Ovid hath belie she
sex.
nothing reign for a long time but confusion; till at last the
squire, have sufficiently spend his breath, return to the parlour,
where he find Mrs Western and Mr Blifil, and throw himself, with the
utmost dejection in his countenance, into a great chair.
here Mrs Western begin to apply the follow consolation:
"Brother, I be sorry for what hath happen; and that my niece should
have behave herself in a manner so unbecome her family; but it be
all your own doing, and you have nobody to thank but yourself. you
know she hath be educate always in a manner directly contrary to my
advice, and now you see the consequence. have I not a thousand time
argue with you about give my niece her own will? but you know I
never could prevail upon you; and when I have take so much pain to
eradicate her headstrong opinion, and to rectify your error in
policy, you know she be take out of my hand; so that I have nothing
to answer for. have I be trust entirely with the care of her
education, no such accident as this have ever befall you; so that you
must comfort yourself by think it be all your own doing; and,
indeed, what else could be expect from such indulgence?"
"Zounds! sister," answer he, "you be enough to make one mad. have I
indulge she? have I give she she will?----it be no long ago than
last night that I threaten, if she disobey I, to confine she to
her chamber upon bread and water as long as she lived.----you would
provoke the patience of Job."
"do ever mortal hear the like?" reply she. "Brother, if I have not
the patience of fifty job, you would make I forget all decency and
decorum. why would you interfere? do I not beg you, do I not intreat
you, to leave the whole conduct to I? you have defeat all the
operation of the campaign by one false step. would any man in his
sens have provoke a daughter by such threat as these? how often
have I tell you that english woman be not to be treat like
ciracessian[*] slave. we have the protection of the world; we be to
be win by gentle mean only, and not to be hector, and bully, and
beat into compliance. I thank Heaven no Salique law govern here.
Brother, you have a roughness in your manner which no woman but myself
would bear. I do not wonder my niece be frightened and terrify into
take this measure; and, to speak honestly, I think my niece will be
justify to the world for what she hath do. I repeat it to you
again, brother, you must comfort yourself by rememb're that it be
all your own fault. how often have I advised--" here western rise
hastily from his chair, and, vent two or three horrid imprecation,
run out of the room.
[*] possibly Circassian.
when he be depart, his sister express more bitterness (if
possible) against he than she have do while he be present; for the
truth of which she appeal to Mr Blifil, who, with great complacence,
acquiesce entirely in all she say; but excuse all the fault of Mr
Western, "as they must be consider," he say, "to have proceed
from the too inordinate fondness of a father, which must be allow
the name of an amiable weakness." "so much the more inexcuseable,"
answer the lady; "for whom doth he ruin by his fondness but his own
child?" to which Blifil immediately agree.
Mrs Western then begin to express great confusion on the account of Mr
Blifil, and of the usage which he have receive from a family to which
he intend so much honour. on this subject she treat the folly of
her niece with great severity; but conclude with throw the whole
on her brother, who, she say, be inexcuseable to have proceed so
far without well assurance of his daughter's consent: "but he be
(say she) always of a violent, headstrong temper; and I can scarce
forgive myself for all the advice I have throw away upon he."
after much of this kind of conversation, which, perhaps, would not
greatly entertain the reader, be it here particularly related, Mr
Blifil take his leave and return home, not highly pleased with his
disappointment: which, however, the philosophy which he have acquire
from Square, and the religion infuse into he by Thwackum, together
with somewhat else, teach he to bear rather well than more
passionate lover bear these kind of evil.
chapter ix.
the escape of Sophia.
it be now time to look after Sophia; whom the reader, if he love she
half so well as I do, will rejoice to find escape from the clutch
of her passionate father, and from those of her dispassionate lover.
twelve time do the iron register of time beat on the sonorous
bell-metal, summon the ghost to rise and walk their nightly
round.----In plainer language, it be twelve o'clock, and all the
family, as we have say, lie bury in drink and sleep, except only
Mrs Western, who be deeply engage in read a political pamphlet,
and except our heroine, who now softly steal down-stair, and, have
unbarred and unlocked one of the house-door, sally forth, and
hasten to the place of appointment.
notwithstanding the many pretty art which lady sometimes practise,
to display their fear on every little occasion (almost as many as the
other sex use to conceal theirs), certainly there be a degree of
courage which not only become a woman, but be often necessary to
enable she to discharge her duty. it be, indeed, the idea of
fierceness, and not of bravery, which destroy the female character;
for who can read the story of the justly celebrated Arria without
conceiving as high an opinion of her gentleness and tenderness as of
her fortitude? at the same time, perhaps, many a woman who shriek at
a mouse, or a rat, may be capable of poison a husband; or, what be
bad, of drive he to poison himself.
Sophia, with all the gentleness which a woman can have, have all the
spirit which she ought to have. when, therefore, she come to the place
of appointment, and, instead of meet she maid, as be agree, see a
man ride directly up to she, she neither scream out nor faint
away: not that her pulse then beat with its usual regularity; for she
be, at first, under some surprize and apprehension: but these be
relieve almost as soon as raise, when the man, pull off his hat,
ask she, in a very submissive manner, "if her ladyship do not
expect to meet another lady?" and then proceed to inform she that he
be send to conduct she to that lady.
Sophia could have no possible suspicion of any falsehood in this
account: she therefore mount resolutely behind the fellow, who
convey she safe to a town about five mile distant, where she have
the satisfaction of find the good Mrs Honour: for, as the soul of
the waiting-woman be wrapt up in those very habiliment which use to
enwrap her body, she could by no mean bring herself to trust they out
of her sight. upon these, therefore, she keep guard in person, while
she detach the aforesaid fellow after her mistress, have give he
all proper instruction.
they now debate what course to take, in order to avoid the pursuit of
Mr Western, who they know would send after they in a few hour. the
London road have such charm for Honour, that she be desirous of go
on directly; allege that, as Sophia could not be miss till eight
or nine the next morning, her pursuer would not be able to overtake
she, even though they know which way she have go. but Sophia have too
much at stake to venture anything to chance; nor do she dare trust
too much to her tender limb, in a contest which be to be decide
only by swiftness. she resolve, therefore, to travel across the
country, for at least twenty or thirty mile, and then to take the
direct road to London. so, have hire horse to go twenty mile one
way, when she intend to go twenty mile the other, she set forward
with the same guide behind whom she have ride from her father's
house; the guide have now take up behind he, in the room of
Sophia, a much heavy, as well as much less lovely burden; be,
indeed, a huge portmanteau, well stuff with those outside ornament,
by mean of which the fair Honour hop to gain many conquest, and,
finally, to make her fortune in London city.
when they have go about two hundred pace from the inn on the London
road, Sophia ride up to the guide, and, with a voice much full of
honey than be ever that of Plato, though his mouth be suppose to
have be a bee-hive, beg he to take the first turning which lead
towards Bristol.
Reader, I be not superstitious, nor any great believer of modern
miracle. I do not, therefore, deliver the following as a certain
truth; for, indeed, I can scarce credit it myself: but the fidelity of
an historian oblige I to relate what hath be confidently assert.
the horse, then, on which the guide ride, be report to have be so
charm by Sophia's voice, that he make a full stop, and express an
unwillingness to proceed any far.
perhaps, however, the fact may be true, and less miraculous than it
hath be represent; since the natural cause seem adequate to the
effect: for, as the guide at that moment desist from a constant
application of his armed right heel (for, like hudibra, he wear but
one spur), it be more than possible that this omission alone might
occasion the beast to stop, especially as this be very frequent with
he at other time.
but if the voice of Sophia have really an effect on the horse, it have
very little on the rider. he answer somewhat surlily, "that measter
have order he to go a different way, and that he should lose his
place if he go any other than that he be order."
Sophia, find all her persuasion have no effect, begin now to add
irresistible charm to her voice; charm which, accord to the
proverb, make the old mare trot, instead of stand still; charm!
to which modern age have attribute all that irresistible force which
the antient impute to perfect oratory. in a word, she promise she
would reward he to his utmost expectation.
the lad be not totally deaf to these promise; but he dislike their
be indefinite; for, though perhaps he have never hear that word,
yet that, in fact, be his objection. he say, "Gentlevolks do not
consider the case of poor volk; that he have like to have be turn
away the other day, for rid about the country with a gentleman from
Squire Allworthy's, who do not reward he as he should have do."
"with whom?" say Sophia eagerly. "with a gentleman from Squire
Allworthy's," repeat the lad; "the squire's son, I think they call
'un."--"Whither? which way do he go?" say Sophia.--"Why, a little o'
one side o' Bristol, about twenty mile off," answer the
lad.--"guide I," say Sophia, "to the same place, and Iwill give thee
a guinea, or two, if one be not sufficient."--"To be certain," say
the boy, "it be honestly worth two, when your ladyship consider what
a risk I run; but, however, if your ladyship will promise I the two
guineas, Iwill e'en venture: to be certain it be a sinful thing to ride
about my measter's horse; but one comfort be, I can only be turn
away, and two guineas will partly make I amend."
the bargain be thus strike, the lad turn aside into the Bristol
road, and Sophia set forward in pursuit of Jones, highly contrary to
the remonstrance of Mrs Honour, who have much more desire to see London
than to see Mr Jones: for indeed she be not his friend with her
mistress, as he have be guilty of some neglect in certain pecuniary
civility, which be by custom due to the waiting-gentlewoman in all
love affair, and more especially in those of a clandestine kind. this
we impute rather to the carelessness of his temper than to any want of
generosity; but perhaps she derive it from the latter motive. certain
it be that she hat he very bitterly on that account, and resolve to
take every opportunity of injure he with her mistress. it be
therefore highly unlucky for she, that she have go to the very same
town and inn whence Jones have start, and still more unlucky be she
in have stumble on the same guide, and on this accidental discovery
which Sophia have make.
our traveller arrive at Hambrook[*] at the break of day, where
Honour be against she will charge to enquire the route which Mr
Jones have take. of this, indeed, the guide himself could have
inform they; but Sophia, I know not for what reason, never ask he
the question.
[*] this be the village where Jones meet the Quaker.
when Mrs Honour have make her report from the landlord, Sophia, with
much difficulty, procure some indifferent horse, which bring she
to the inn where Jones have be confine rather by the misfortune of
meeting with a surgeon than by have meet with a broken head.
here Honour, be again charge with a commission of enquiry, have no
soon apply herself to the landlady, and have describe the person
of Mr Jones, than that sagacious woman begin, in the vulgar phrase, to
smell a rat. when Sophia therefore enter the room, instead of
answer the maid, the landlady, address herself to the mistress,
begin the follow speech: "good lack-a-day! why there now, who would
have think it? I protest the lovely couple that ever eye behold.
I-fackins, madam, it be no wonder the squire run on so about your
ladyship. he tell I indeed you be the fine lady in the world, and
to be sure so you be. mercy on he, poor heart! I bepitie he, so I
do, when he use to hug his pillow, and call it his dear Madam
Sophia. I do all I could to dissuade he from go to the war: I
tell he there be man enow that be good for nothing else but to be
kill, that have not the love of such fine lady." "sure," say
Sophia, "the good woman be distract." "no, no," cry the landlady,
"I be not distract. what, doth your ladyship think I donot know
then? I assure you he tell I all." "what saucy fellow," cry Honour,
"tell you anything of my lady?" "no saucy fellow," answer the
landlady, "but the young gentleman you enquire after, and a very
pretty young gentleman he be, and he love Madam Sophia Western to the
bottom of his soul." "he love my lady! Iwould have you to know, woman,
she be meat for his master."--"Nay, Honour," say Sophia, interrupt
she, "donot be angry with the good woman; she intend no harm." "no,
marry, donot I," answer the landlady, embolden by the soft accent
of Sophia; and then launch into a long narrative too tedious to be
here set down, in which some passage dropt that give a little offence
to Sophia, and much more to her waiting-woman, who hence take occasion
to abuse poor Jones to her mistress the moment they be alone
together, say, "that he must be a very pitiful fellow, and could
have no love for a lady, whose name he would thus prostitute in an
ale-house."
Sophia do not see his behaviour in so very disadvantageous a light,
and be perhaps more pleased with the violent rapture of his love
(which the landlady exaggerate as much as she have do every other
circumstance) than she be offend with the rest; and indeed she
impute the whole to the extravagance, or rather ebullience, of his
passion, and to the openness of his heart.
this incident, however, be afterwards revive in her mind, and
place in the most odious colour by Honour, serve to heighten and
give credit to those unlucky occurrence at Upton, and assist the
waiting-woman in her endeavour to make her mistress depart from that
inn without see Jones.
the landlady find Sophia intend to stay no long than till she
horse be ready, and that without either eat or drinking, soon
withdraw; when Honour begin to take her mistress to task (for indeed
she use great freedom), and after a long harangue, in which she
remind she of her intention to go to London, and give frequent hint
of the impropriety of pursue a young fellow, she at last conclude
with this serious exhortation: "for heaven's sake, madam, consider
what you be about, and whither you be go."
this advice to a lady who have already ride near forty mile, and in no
very agreeable season, may seem foolish enough. it may be suppose she
have well consider and resolve this already; nay, Mrs Honour, by the
hint she throw out, seem to think so; and this I doubt not be the
opinion of many reader, who have, I make no doubt, be long since
well convinced of the purpose of our heroine, and have heartily
condemn she for it as a wanton baggage.
but in reality this be not the case. Sophia have be lately so
distract between hope and fear, her duty and love to her father, her
hatred to Blifil, her compassion, and (why should we not confess the
truth?) her love for Jones; which last the behaviour of her father, of
her aunt, of every one else, and more particularly of Jones himself,
have blow into a flame, that her mind be in that confused state which
may be truly say to make we ignorant of what we do, or whither we go,
or rather, indeed, indifferent as to the consequence of either.
the prudent and sage advice of she maid produce, however, some cool
reflection; and she at length determine to go to Gloucester, and
thence to proceed directly to London.
but, unluckily, a few mile before she enter that town, she meet the
hack-attorney, who, as be before mention, have din there with Mr
Jones. this fellow, be well know to Mrs Honour, stopt and speak to
she; of which Sophia at that time take little notice, more than to
enquire who he be.
but, have have a more particular account from Honour of this man
afterwards at Gloucester, and hearing of the great expedition he
usually make in travelling, for which (as hath be before observe)
he be particularly famous; recollect, likewise, that she have
overhear Mrs Honour inform he that they be go to Gloucester,
she begin to fear lest her father might, by this fellow's mean, be
able to trace she to that city; wherefore, if she should there strike
into the London road, she apprehend he would certainly be able to
overtake she. she therefore alter her resolution; and, have hire
horse to go a week's journey a way which she do not intend to
travel, she again set forward after a light refreshment, contrary to
the desire and earnest entreaty of she maid, and to the no less
vehement remonstrance of Mrs Whitefield, who, from good breeding, or
perhaps from good nature (for the poor young lady appear much
fatigue), press she very heartily to stay that evening at
Gloucester.
have refresh herself only with some tea, and with lie about two
hour on the bed, while her horse be get ready, she resolutely
leave Mrs Whitefield's about eleven at night, and, strike directly
into the Worcester road, within less than four hour arrive at that
very inn where we last see she.
have thus trace our heroine very particularly back from her
departure, till her arrival at Upton, we shall in a very few word
bring her father to the same place; who, have receive the first
scent from the post-boy, who conduct his daughter to Hambrook, very
easily trace she afterwards to Gloucester; whence he pursue she to
Upton, as he have learn Mr Jones have take that route (for Partridge,
to use the squire's expression, leave everywhere a strong scent behind
he), and he doubt not in the least but Sophia travel, or, as he
phrase it, run, the same way. he use indeed a very coarse
expression, which need not be here insert; as fox-hunter, who alone
will understand it, will easily suggest it to themselves.
BOOK XI.
contain about three day.
Chapter i.
a crust for the critic.
in our last initial chapter we may be suppose to have treat that
formidable set of man who be call critic with more freedom than
become we; since they exact, and indeed generally receive, great
condescension from author. we shall in this, therefore, give the
reason of our conduct to this august body; and here we shall,
perhaps, place they in a light in which they have not hitherto be
see.
this word critic be of greek derivation, and signify judgment. hence
I presume some person who have not understand the original, and have
see the english translation of the primitive, have conclude that it
mean judgment in the legal sense, in which it be frequently use as
equivalent to condemnation.
I be the rather inclined to be of that opinion, as the great number
of critic hath of late year be find amongst the lawyer. many of
these gentleman, from despair, perhaps, of ever rise to the bench in
Westminster-hall, have place themselves on the bench at the
playhouse, where they have exert their judicial capacity, and have
give judgment, _i.e._, condemn without mercy.
the gentleman would, perhaps, be well enough please, if we be to
leave they thus compare to one of the most important and honourable
office in the commonwealth, and, if we intend to apply to their
favour, we would do so; but, as we design to deal very sincerely and
plainly too with they, we must remind they of another officer of
justice of a much low rank; to whom, as they not only pronounce, but
execute, their own judgment, they bear likewise some remote
resemblance.
but in reality there be another light, in which these modern critic
may, with great justice and propriety, be see; and this be that of a
common slanderer. if a person who prys into the character of other,
with no other design but to discover their fault, and to publish they
to the world, deserve the title of a slanderer of the reputation of
man, why should not a critic, who read with the same malevolent view,
be as properly stile the slanderer of the reputation of book?
Vice hath not, I believe, a more abject slave; society produce not a
more odious vermin; nor can the devil receive a guest more worthy of
he, nor possibly more welcome to he, than a slanderer. the world, I
be afraid, regard not this monster with half the abhorrence which he
deserve; and I be more afraid to assign the reason of this criminal
lenity show towards he; yet it be certain that the thief look
innocent in the comparison; nay, the murderer himself can seldom stand
in competition with his guilt: for slander be a more cruel weapon than
a sword, as the wound which the former give be always incurable.
one method, indeed, there be of killing, and that the basest and most
execrable of all, which bear an exact analogy to the vice here
disclaim against, and that be poison: a means of revenge so base,
and yet so horrible, that it be once wisely distinguish by our law
from all other murder, in the peculiar severity of the punishment.
besides the dreadful mischief do by slander, and the baseness of
the mean by which they be effect, there be other circumstance
that highly aggravate its atrocious quality; for it often proceed
from no provocation, and seldom promise itself any reward, unless
some black and infernal mind may propose a reward in the thought of
have procure the ruin and misery of another.
Shakespear hath nobly touch this vice, when he says--
"who steal my purse steal trash; 't be something, nothing;
'twa mine, 'tis his, and hath be slave to thousand:
but he that filch from I my good name
Robs I of that which not ENRICHES HIM,
but makes I POOR indeed."
with all this my good reader will doubtless agree; but much of it will
probably seem too severe, when apply to the slanderer of book. but
let it here be consider that both proceed from the same wicked
disposition of mind, and be alike void of the excuse of temptation.
nor shall we conclude the injury do this way to be very slight, when
we consider a book as the author's offspring, and indeed as the child
of his brain.
the reader who hath suffer his muse to continue hitherto in a virgin
state can have but a very inadequate idea of this kind of paternal
fondness. to such we may parody the tender exclamation of Macduff,
"alas! Thou hast write no book." but the author whose muse hath
bring forth will feel the pathetic strain, perhaps will accompany I
with tear (especially if his darling be already no more), while I
mention the uneasiness with which the big muse bear about her burden,
the painful labour with which she produce it, and, lastly, the care,
the fondness, with which the tender father nourish his favourite,
till it be bring to maturity, and produce into the world.
nor be there any paternal fondness which seem less to savour of
absolute instinct, and which may so well be reconcile to worldly
wisdom, as this. these child may most truly be call the rich of
their father; and many of they have with true filial piety feed their
parent in his old age: so that not only the affection, but the
interest, of the author may be highly injure by these slanderer,
whose poisonous breath bring his book to an untimely end.
lastly, the slander of a book be, in truth, the slander of the author:
for, as no one can call another bastard, without call the mother a
whore, so neither can any one give the name of sad stuff, horrid
nonsense, &c., to a book, without call the author a blockhead;
which, though in a moral sense it be a preferable appellation to that
of villain, be perhaps rather more injurious to his worldly interest.
now, however ludicrous all this may appear to some, other, I doubt
not, will feel and acknowledge the truth of it; nay, may, perhaps,
think I have not treat the subject with decent solemnity; but surely
a man may speak truth with a smile countenance. in reality, to
depreciate a book maliciously, or even wantonly, be at least a very
ill-natured office; and a morose snarl critic may, I believe, be
suspect to be a bad man.
I will therefore endeavour, in the remain part of this chapter, to
explain the mark of this character, and to show what criticism I here
intend to obviate: for I can never be understand, unless by the very
person here mean, to insinuate that there be no proper judge of
writing, or to endeavour to exclude from the commonwealth of
literature any of those noble critic to whose labour the learned
world be so greatly indebted. such be Aristotle, Horace, and
Longinus, among the antient, Dacier and Bossu among the French, and
some perhaps among we; who have certainly be duly authorise to
execute at least a judicial authority _in foro literario_.
but without ascertain all the proper qualification of a critic,
which I have touch on elsewhere, I think I may very boldly object to
the censure of any one past upon work which he hath not himself
read. such censurer as these, whether they speak from their own guess
or suspicion, or from the report and opinion of other, may properly
be say to slander the reputation of the book they condemn.
such may likewise be suspect of deserve this character, who,
without assign any particular fault, condemn the whole in general
defamatory term; such as vile, dull, d--d stuff, &c., and
particularly by the use of the monosyllable low; a word which become
the mouth of no critic who be not right honourable.
again, though there may be some fault justly assign in the work,
yet, if those be not in the most essential part, or if they be
compensate by great beauty, it will savour rather of the malice
of a slanderer than of the judgment of a true critic to pass a severe
sentence upon the whole, merely on account of some vicious part. this
be directly contrary to the sentiment of Horace:
_verum ubi plura nitent in carmine, non ego paucis
Offendor maculi, quas aut incuria fudit,
Aut humana parum cavit natura----_
but where the beauty, more in number, shine,
I be not angry, when a casual line
(that with some trivial fault unequal flow)
a careless hand or human frailty shows.--mr FRANCIS.
for, as Martial say, _Aliter non fit, Avite, liber_. no book can be
otherwise compose. all beauty of character, as well as of
countenance, and indeed of everything human, be to be try in this
manner. Cruel indeed would it be if such a work as this history, which
hath employ some thousand of hour in the composing, should be
liable to be condemn, because some particular chapter, or perhaps
chapter, may be obnoxious to very just and sensible objection. and
yet nothing be more common than the most rigorous sentence upon book
support by such objection, which, if they be rightly take (and
that they be not always), do by no mean go to the merit of the
whole. in the theatre especially, a single expression which doth not
coincide with the taste of the audience, or with any individual critic
of that audience, be sure to be hiss; and one scene which should be
disapprove would hazard the whole piece. to write within such severe
rule as these be as impossible as to live up to some splenetic
opinion: and if we judge accord to the sentiment of some critic,
and of some Christians, no author will be save in this world, and no
man in the next.
Chapter ii.
the adventure which Sophia meet with after she leave Upton.
our history, just before it be oblige to turn about and travel
backwards, have mention the departure of Sophia and she maid from the
inn; we shall now therefore pursue the step of that lovely creature,
and leave her unworthy lover a little long to bemoan his ill-luck,
or rather his ill-conduct.
Sophia have direct her guide to travel through bye-road, across
the country, they now pass the Severn, and have scarce get a mile
from the inn, when the young lady, look behind she, see several
horse come after on full speed. this greatly alarm her fear, and
she call to the guide to put on as fast as possible.
he immediately obey she, and away they ride a full gallop. but the
fast they go, the fast be they follow; and as the horse
behind be somewhat swifter than those before, so the former be at
length overtaken. a happy circumstance for poor Sophia; whose fear,
join to her fatigue, have almost overpower her spirit; but she be
now instantly relieve by a female voice, that greet she in the
soft manner, and with the utmost civility. this greet Sophia, as
soon as she could recover her breath, with like civility, and with the
high satisfaction to herself, return.
the traveller who join Sophia, and who have give she such terror,
consist, like her own company, of two female and a guide. the two
party proceed three full mile together before any one offer
again to open their mouth; when our heroine, have pretty well get
the well of her fear (but yet be somewhat surprize that the
other still continue to attend she, as she pursue no great road, and
have already pass through several turning), accost the strange
lady in a most obliging tone, and say, "she be very happy to find
they be both travel the same way." the other, who, like a ghost,
only want to be speak to, readily answer, "that the happiness be
entirely her; that she be a perfect stranger in that country, and
be so overjoyed at meet a companion of her own sex, that she have
perhaps be guilty of an impertinence, which require great apology,
in keep pace with she." More civility pass between these two
lady; for Mrs Honour have now give place to the fine habit of the
stranger, and have fall into the rear. but, though Sophia have great
curiosity to know why the other lady continue to travel on through
