the same bye-road with herself, nay, though this give she some
uneasiness, yet fear, or modesty, or some other consideration,
restrain she from ask the question.
the strange lady now labour under a difficulty which appear almost
below the dignity of history to mention. her bonnet have be blow
from her head not less than five time within the last mile; nor could
she come at any ribbon or handkerchief to tie it under her chin. when
Sophia be inform of this, she immediately supply she with a
handkerchief for this purpose; which while she be pull from her
pocket, she perhaps too much neglect the management of her horse,
for the beast, now unluckily make a false step, fall upon his
fore-leg, and throw his fair rider from his back.
Though Sophia come head foremost to the ground, she happily receive
not the least damage: and the same circumstance which have perhaps
contribute to her fall now preserve she from confusion; for the lane
which they be then pass be narrow, and very much overgrown with
tree, so that the moon could here afford very little light, and be
moreover, at present, so obscure in a cloud, that it be almost
perfectly dark. by these mean the young lady's modesty, which be
extremely delicate, escape as free from injury as her limb, and she
be once more reinstate in her saddle, have receive no other harm
than a little fright by her fall.
daylight at length appear in its full lustre; and now the two
lady, who be rid over a common side by side, look stedfastly
at each other, at the same moment both their eye become fix; both
their horse stopt, and, both speak together, with equal joy
pronounce, the one the name of Sophia, the other that of Harriet.
this unexpected encounter surprize the lady much more than I
believe it will the sagacious reader, who must have imagine that the
strange lady could be no other than Mrs Fitzpatrick, the cousin of
Miss Western, whom we before mention to have sally from the inn a
few minute after she.
so great be the surprize and joy which these two cousin conceive at
this meeting (for they have formerly be most intimate acquaintance
and friend, and have long live together with their aunt Western),
that it be impossible to recount half the congratulation which pass
between they, before either ask a very natural question of the
other, namely, whither she be go?
this at last, however, come first from Mrs Fitzpatrick; but, easy and
natural as the question may seem, Sophia find it difficult to give it
a very ready and certain answer. she beg her cousin therefore to
suspend all curiosity till they arrive at some inn, "which I
suppose," say she, "can hardly be far distant; and, believe I,
Harriet, I suspend as much curiosity on my side; for, indeed, I
believe our astonishment be pretty equal."
the conversation which pass between these lady on the road be, I
apprehend, little worth relate; and less certainly be that between
the two waiting-woman; for they likewise begin to pay their
compliment to each other. as for the guide, they be debar from
the pleasure of discourse, the one be place in the van, and the
other oblige to bring up the rear.
in this posture they travel many hour, till they come into a wide
and well-beat road, which, as they turn to the right, soon bring
they to a very fair promising inn, where they all alight: but so
fatigue be Sophia, that as she have sit her horse during the last
five or six mile with great difficulty, so be she now incapable of
dismount from he without assistance. this the landlord, who have
hold of her horse, presently perceive, offer to lift she in his
arm from her saddle; and she too readily accept the tender of his
service. indeed fortune seem to have resolve to put Sophia to the
blush that day, and the second malicious attempt succeed well than
the first; for my landlord have no soon receive the young lady in
his arm, than his foot, which the gout have lately very severely
handle, give way, and down he tumble; but, at the same time, with no
less dexterity than gallantry, contrive to throw himself under his
charming burden, so that he alone receive any bruise from the fall;
for the great injury which happen to Sophia be a violent shock
give to her modesty by an immoderate grin, which, at she rise from
the ground, she observe in the countenance of most of the
bye-stander. this make her suspect what have really happen, and what
we shall not here relate for the indulgence of those reader who be
capable of laugh at the offence give to a young lady's delicacy.
accident of this kind we have never regard in a comical light; nor
will we scruple to say that he must have a very inadequate idea of the
modesty of a beautiful young woman, who would wish to sacrifice it to
so paltry a satisfaction as can arise from laughter.
this fright and shock, join to the violent fatigue which both her
mind and body have undergo, almost overcome the excellent
constitution of Sophia, and she have scarce strength sufficient to
totter into the inn, lean on the arm of she maid. here she be no
soon seat than she call for a glass of water; but Mrs Honour,
very judiciously, in my opinion, change it into a glass of wine.
Mrs Fitzpatrick, hear from Mrs Honour that Sophia have not be in
bed during the two last night, and observe she to look very pale
and wan with her fatigue, earnestly entreat she to refresh herself
with some sleep. she be yet a stranger to her history, or her
apprehension; but, have she know both, she would have give the same
advice; for rest be visibly necessary for she; and their long journey
through bye-road so entirely remove all danger of pursuit, that she
be herself perfectly easy on that account.
Sophia be easily prevail on to follow the counsel of her friend,
which be heartily second by her maid. Mrs Fitzpatrick likewise
offer to bear her cousin company, which Sophia, with much
complacence, accept.
the mistress be no soon in bed than the maid prepare to follow she
example. she begin to make many apology to her sister Abigail for
leave she alone in so horrid a place as an inn; but the other stopt
her short, be as well inclined to a nap as herself, and desire the
honour of be her bedfellow. Sophia's maid agree to give she a
share of her bed, but put in her claim to all the honour. so, after
many courtsie and compliment, to bed together go the
waiting-woman, as their mistress have do before they.
it be usual with my landlord (as indeed it be with the whole
fraternity) to enquire particularly of all coachman, footman,
postboy, and other, into the name of all his guest; what their
estate be, and where it lay. it cannot therefore be wonder at that
the many particular circumstance which attend our traveller, and
especially their retire all to sleep at so extraordinary and unusual
an hour as ten in the morning, should excite his curiosity. as soon,
therefore, as the guide enter the kitchen, he begin to examine who
the lady be, and whence they come; but the guide, though they
faithfully relate all they know, give he very little satisfaction.
on the contrary, they rather enflame his curiosity than extinguish
it.
this landlord have the character, among all his neighbour, of be a
very sagacious fellow. he be think to see far and deep into
thing than any man in the parish, the parson himself not excepted.
perhaps his look have contribute not a little to procure he this
reputation; for there be in this something wonderfully wise and
significant, especially when he have a pipe in his mouth; which,
indeed, he seldom be without. his behaviour, likewise, greatly
assist in promote the opinion of his wisdom. in his deportment he
be solemn, if not sullen; and when he speak, which be seldom, he
always deliver himself in a slow voice; and, though his sentence
be short, they be still interrupt with many hum and ha's, ay
ays, and other expletive: so that, though he accompany his word
with certain explanatory gesture, such as shake or nod the
head, or point with his fore-finger, he generally leave his hearer
to understand more than he express; nay, he commonly give they a
hint that he know much more than he think proper to disclose. this
last circumstance alone may, indeed, very well account for his
character of wisdom; since man be strangely inclined to worship what
they do not understand. a grand secret, upon which several imposer on
mankind have totally rely for the success of their fraud.
this polite person, now take his wife aside, ask she "what she
think of the lady lately arrive?" "think of they?" say the wife,
"why, what should I think of they?" "I know," answer he, "what I
think. the guide tell strange story. one pretend to be come from
Gloucester, and the other from Upton; and neither of they, for what I
can find, can tell whither they be go. but what people ever travel
across the country from Upton hither, especially to London? and one of
the maid-servant, before she alight from her horse, ask if this
be not the London road? now I have put all these circumstance
together, and whom do you think I have find they out to be?" "Nay,"
answer she, "you know I never pretend to guess at your
discovery."----"It be a good girl," reply he, chuck she under
the chin; "I must own you have always submit to my knowledge of
these matter. why, then, depend upon it; mind what I say--depend upon
it, they be certainly some of the rebel lady, who, they say, travel
with the young Chevalier; and have take a roundabout way to escape
the duke's army."
"Husband," quoth the wife, "you have certainly hit it; for one of they
be dress as fine as any princess; and, to be sure, she look for all
the world like one.----but yet, when I consider one thing"----"when
you consider," cry the landlord contemptuously----"Come, pray letus
hear what you consider."----"why, it be," answer the wife, "that she
be too humble to be any very great lady: for, while our Betty be
warm the bed, she call she nothing but child, and my dear, and
sweetheart; and, when Betty offer to pull off her shoe and
stocking, she would not suffer she, say, she would not give she
the trouble."
"Pugh!" answer the husband, "that be nothing. dost think, because
you have see some great lady rude and uncivil to person below
they, that none of they know how to behave themselves when they come
before their inferior? I think I know people of fashion when I see
they--I think I do. do not she call for a glass of water when she
come in? another sort of woman would have call for a dram; you know
they would. if she be not a woman of very great quality, sell I for a
fool; and, I believe, those who buy I will have a bad bargain. now,
would a woman of her quality travel without a footman, unless upon
some such extraordinary occasion?" "Nay, to be sure, husband," cry
she, "you know these matter well than I, or most folk." "I think I
do know something," say he. "to be sure," answer the wife, "the
poor little heart look so piteous, when she sit down in the chair, I
protest I could not help have a compassion for she almost as much as
if she have be a poor body. but whatbe to be do, husband? if an she
be a rebel, I suppose you intend to betray she up to the court. well,
shebe a sweet-temper, good-humour lady, be she what she will, and
I shall hardly refrain from cry when I hear she be hang or
behead." "Pooh!" answer the husband.----"But, as to whatbe to be
do, it be not so easy a matter to determine. I hope, before she go
away, we shall have the news of a battle; for, if the Chevalier should
get the well, she may gain we interest at court, and make our
fortune without betray she." "why, thatbe true," reply the wife;
"and I heartily hope she will have it in her power. certainly shebe a
sweet good lady; it would go horribly against I to have she come to
any harm." "Pooh!" cry the landlord, "woman be always so
tender-hearted. why, you would not harbour rebel, would you?" "no,
certainly," answer the wife; "and as for betray she, come what
will on't, nobody can blame we. it be what anybody would do in our
case."
while our politic landlord, who have not, we see, undeservedly the
reputation of great wisdom among his neighbour, be engage in
debate this matter with himself (for he pay little attention to the
opinion of his wife), news arrive that the rebel have give the duke
the slip, and have get a day's march towards London; and soon after
arrive a famous Jacobite squire, who, with great joy in his
countenance, shake the landlord by the hand, say, "allbe our own,
boy, ten thousand honest frenchman be land in Suffolk. Old England
for ever! ten thousand French, my brave lad! I be go to tap away
directly."
this news determine the opinion of the wise man, and he resolve to
make his court to the young lady when she arise; for he have now (he
say) discover that she be no other than Madam Jenny Cameron
herself.
Chapter iii.
a very short chapter, in which however be a sun, a moon, a star, and
an angel.
the sun (for he keep very good hour at this time of the year) have
be some time retire to rest when Sophia arise greatly refresh by
her sleep; which, short as it be, nothing but her extreme fatigue
could have occasion; for, though she have tell she maid, and perhaps
herself too, that she be perfectly easy when she leave Upton, yet it
be certain her mind be a little affected with that malady which be
attend with all the restless symptom of a fever, and be perhaps the
very distemper which physician mean (if they mean anything) by the
fever on the spirit.
Mrs Fitzpatrick likewise leave her bed at the same time; and, have
summon she maid, immediately dress herself. she be really a very
pretty woman, and, have she be in any other company but that of
Sophia, might have be think beautiful; but when Mrs Honour of her
own accord attend (for her mistress would not suffer she to be
waked), and have equip our heroine, the charm of Mrs Fitzpatrick,
who have perform the office of the morning-star, and have precede
great glory, share the fate of that star, and be totally
eclipse the moment those glory shine forth.
perhaps Sophia never look more beautiful than she do at this
instant. we ought not, therefore, to condemn the maid of the inn for
her hyperbole, who, when she descend, after have light the fire,
declare, and ratify it with an oath, that if ever there be an
angel upon earth, she be now above-stair.
Sophia have acquaint her cousin with her design to go to London; and
Mrs Fitzpatrick have agree to accompany she; for the arrival of her
husband at Upton have put an end to her design of go to Bath, or to
her aunt western. they have therefore no soon finish their tea than
Sophia propose to set out, the moon then shin extremely bright,
and as for the frost she defy it; nor have she any of those
apprehension which many young lady would have feel at travel by
night; for she have, as we have before observe, some little degree of
natural courage; and this, her present sensation, which border
somewhat on despair, greatly encrease. besides, as she have already
travel twice with safety by the light of the moon, she be the
well embolden to trust to it a third time.
the disposition of Mrs Fitzpatrick be more timorous; for, though the
great terror have conquer the less, and the presence of her
husband have drive she away at so unseasonable an hour from Upton,
yet, be now arrive at a place where she think herself safe from
his pursuit, these less terror of I know not what operate so
strongly, that she earnestly entreat her cousin to stay till the
next morning, and not expose herself to the danger of travel by
night.
Sophia, who be yield to an excess, when she could neither laugh
nor reason her cousin out of these apprehension, at last give way to
they. perhaps, indeed, have she know of her father's arrival at Upton,
it might have be more difficult to have persuade she; for as to
Jones, she have, I be afraid, no great horror at the thought of be
overtake by he; nay, to confess the truth, I believe she rather
wish than fear it; though I might honestly enough have conceal
this wish from the reader, as it be one of those secret spontaneous
emotion of the soul to which the reason be often a stranger.
when our young lady have determine to remain all that evening in
their inn they be attend by the landlady, who desire to know what
their ladyship would be pleased to eat. such charm be there in the
voice, in the manner, and in the affable deportment of Sophia, that
she ravish the landlady to the high degree; and that good woman,
conclude that she have attend Jenny Cameron, become in a moment a
stanch Jacobite, and wish heartily well to the young Pretender's
cause, from the great sweetness and affability with which she have be
treat by his suppose mistress.
the two cousin begin now to impart to each other their reciprocal
curiosity to know what extraordinary accident on both side
occasion this so strange and unexpected meeting. at last Mrs
Fitzpatrick, have obtain of Sophia a promise of communicate
likewise in her turn, begin to relate what the reader, if he be
desirous to know her history, may read in the ensue chapter.
chapter iv.
the history of Mrs Fitzpatrick.
Mrs Fitzpatrick, after a silence of a few moment, fetch a deep
sigh, thus begin:
"it be natural to the unhappy to feel a secret concern in recollect
those period of their life which have be most delightful to they.
the remembrance of past pleasure affect we with a kind of tender
grief, like what we suffer for depart friend; and the idea of both
may be say to haunt our imagination.
"for this reason, I never reflect without sorrow on those day (the
happy far of my life) which we spend together when both be under
the care of my aunt Western. alas! why be Miss Graveairs and Miss
Giddy no more? you remember, I be sure, when we know each other by no
other name. indeed, you give the latter appellation with too much
cause. I have since experience how much I deserve it. you, my
Sophia, be always my superior in everything, and I heartily hope you
will be so in your fortune. I shall never forget the wise and matronly
advice you once give I, when I lament be disappoint of a ball,
though you could not be then fourteen year old.----o my Sophy, how
bl must have be my situation, when I could think such a
disappointment a misfortune; and when indeed it be the great I have
ever know!"
"and yet, my dear Harriet," answer Sophia, "it be then a serious
matter with you. comfort yourself therefore with thinking, that
whatever you now lament may hereafter appear as trifle and
contemptible as a ball would at this time."
"alas, my Sophia," reply the other lady, "you yourself will think
otherwise of my present situation; for greatly must that tender heart
be alter if my misfortune do not draw many a sigh, nay, many a
tear, from you. the knowledge of this should perhaps deter I from
relate what I be convinced will so much affect you." here Mrs
Fitzpatrick stopt, till, at the repeat entreaty of Sophia, she
thus proceed:
"though you must have hear much of my marriage; yet, as matter may
probably have be misrepresent, I will set out from the very
commencement of my unfortunate acquaintance with my present husband;
which be at Bath, soon after you leave my aunt, and return home to
your father.
"among the gay young fellow who be at this season at Bath, Mr
Fitzpatrick be one. he be handsome, _dégagé,_ extremely gallant, and
in his dress exceed most other. in short, my dear, if you be
unluckily to see he now, I could describe he no well than by
tell you he be the very reverse of everything which he be: for he
hath rusticate himself so long, that he be become an absolute wild
Irishman. but to proceed in my story: the qualification which he then
possess so well recommend he, that, though the people of quality
at that time live separate from the rest of the company, and exclude
they from all their party, Mr Fitzpatrick find mean to gain
admittance. it be perhaps no easy matter to avoid he; for he
require very little or no invitation; and as, be handsome and
genteel, he find it no very difficult matter to ingratiate himself
with the lady, so, he have frequently draw his sword, the man do
not care publickly to affront he. have it not be for some such
reason, I believe he would have be soon expel by his own sex; for
surely he have no strict title to be prefer to the english gentry;
nor do they seem inclined to show he any extraordinary favour. they
all abuse he behind his back, which might probably proceed from
envy; for by the woman he be well receive, and very particularly
distinguish by they.
"my aunt, though no person of quality herself, as she have always live
about the court, be enrol in that party; for, by whatever mean
you get into the polite circle, when you be once there, it be
sufficient merit for you that you be there. this observation, young
as you be, you could scarce avoid make from my aunt, who be free,
or reserve, with all people, just as they have more or less of this
merit.
"and this merit, I believe, it be, which principally recommend Mr
Fitzpatrick to her favour. in which he so well succeed, that he be
always one of her private party. nor be he backward in return
such distinction; for he soon grow so very particular in his behaviour
to she, that the scandal club first begin to take notice of it, and
the well-dispose person make a match between they. for my own
part, I confess, I make no doubt but that his design be strictly
honourable, as the phrase be; that be, to rob a lady of her fortune by
way of marriage. my aunt be, I conceive, neither young enough nor
handsome enough to attract much wicked inclination; but she have
matrimonial charm in great abundance.
"I be the more confirmed in this opinion from the extraordinary
respect which he show to myself from the first moment of our
acquaintance. this I understand as an attempt to lessen, if possible,
that disinclination which my interest might be suppose to give I
towards the match; and I know not but in some measure it have that
effect; for, as I be well content with my own fortune, and of all
people the least a slave to interested view, so I could not be
violently the enemy of a man with whose behaviour to I I be greatly
please; and the more so, as I be the only object of such respect;
for he behave at the same time to many woman of quality without any
respect at all.
"agreeable as this be to I, he soon change it into another kind of
behaviour, which be perhaps more so. he now put on much softness and
tenderness, and languish and sigh abundantly. at time, indeed,
whether from art or nature I will not determine, he give his usual
loose to gaiety and mirth; but this be always in general company, and
with other woman; for even in a country-dance, when he be not my
partner, he become grave, and put on the soft look imaginable the
moment he approach I. indeed he be in all thing so very
particular towards I, that I must have be blind not to have
discover it. and, and, and----" "and you be more pleased still, my
dear Harriet," cry Sophia; "you need not be ashamed," add she,
sigh; "for sure there be irresistible charm in tenderness, which
too many man be able to affect." "true," answer her cousin; "man,
who in all other instance want common sense, be very Machiavels in
the art of love. I wish I do not know an instance.--well, scandal
now begin to be as busy with I as it have before be with my aunt;
and some good lady do not scruple to affirm that Mr Fitzpatrick have
an intrigue with we both.
"but, what may seem astonishing, my aunt never see, nor in the least
seem to suspect, that which be visible enough, I believe, from both
our behaviour. one would indeed think that love quite put out the
eye of an old woman. in fact, they so greedily swallow the address
which be make to they, that, like an outrageous glutton, they be not
at leisure to observe what pass amongst other at the same table.
this I have observe in more case than my own; and this be so
strongly verify by my aunt, that, though she often find we together
at her return from the pump, the least cant word of his, pretend
impatience at her absence, effectually smother all suspicion. one
artifice succeed with she to admiration. this be his treat I
like a little child, and never call I by any other name in her
presence but that of pretty miss. this indeed do he some disservice
with your humble servant; but I soon see through it, especially as in
her absence he behave to I, as I have say, in a different manner.
however, if I be not greatly disoblige by a conduct of which I have
discover the design, I smart very severely for it; for my aunt
really conceive I to be what her lover (as she think he) call
I, and treat I in all respect as a perfect infant. to say the
truth, I wonder she have not insist on my again wear
lead-string.
"at last, my lover (for so he be) think proper, in a most solemn
manner, to disclose a secret which I have know long before. he now
place all the love which he have pretend to my aunt to my account.
he lament, in very pathetic term, the encouragement she have give
he, and make a high merit of the tedious hour in which he have
undergo she conversation.--what shall I tell you, my dear
Sophia?--Then I will confess the truth. I be pleased with my man. I
be pleased with my conquest. to rival my aunt delight I; to rival
so many other woman charm I. in short, I be afraid I do not behave
as I should do, even upon the very first declaration--I wish I do not
almost give he positive encouragement before we part.
"the Bath now talk loudly--I might almost say, roar against I.
several young woman affect to shun my acquaintance, not so much,
perhaps, from any real suspicion, as from a desire of banish I
from a company in which I too much engross their favourite man. and
here I cannot omit express my gratitude to the kindness intend I
by Mr Nash, who take I one day aside, and give I advice, which if I
have follow, I have be a happy woman. `child,' say he, `I be sorry
to see the familiarity which subsist between you and a fellow who be
altogether unworthy of you, and I be afraid will prove your ruin. as
for your old stinking aunt, if it be to be no injury to you and my
pretty Sophy Western (I assure you I repeat his word), I should be
heartily glad that the fellow be in possession of all that belong to
she. I never advise old woman: for, if they take it into their head
to go to the devil, it be no more possible than worth while to keep
they from he. innocence and youth and beauty be worthy a well
fate, and I would save they from his clutch. let I advise you
therefore, dear child, never suffer this fellow to be particular with
you again.' many more thing he say to I, which I have now
forget, and indeed I attend very little to they at the time; for
inclination contradict all he say; and, besides, I could not be
persuade that woman of quality would condescend to familiarity with
such a person as he describe.
"but I be afraid, my dear, I shall tire you with a detail of so many
minute circumstance. to be concise, therefore, imagine I marry;
imagine I with my husband, at the foot of my aunt; and then imagine
the mad woman in Bedlam, in a rave fit, and your imagination
will suggest to you no more than what really happen.
"the very next day my aunt leave the place, partly to avoid see Mr
Fitzpatrick or myself, and as much perhaps to avoid see any one
else; for, though I be tell she hath since deny everything stoutly,
I believe she be then a little confound at her disappointment.
since that time, I have write to her many letter, but never could
obtain an answer, which I must own sit somewhat the heavy, as she
herself be, though undesignedly, the occasion of all my suffering:
for, have it not be under the colour of pay his address to she,
Mr Fitzpatrick would never have find sufficient opportunity to have
engage my heart, which, in other circumstance, I still flatter
myself would not have be an easy conquest to such a person. indeed,
I believe I should not have err so grossly in my choice if I have
rely on my own judgment; but I trust totally to the opinion of
other, and very foolishly take the merit of a man for grant whom I
see so universally well receive by the woman. what be the reason, my
dear, that we, who have understanding equal to the wise and
great of the other sex, so often make choice of the silly
fellow for companion and favourite? it raise my indignation to the
high pitch to reflect on the number of woman of sense who have
be undo by fool." here she pause a moment; but, Sophia make no
answer, she proceed as in the next chapter.
Chapter v.
in which the history of Mrs Fitzpatrick be continue.
"we remain at Bath no long than a fortnight after our wedding; for
as to any reconciliation with my aunt, there be no hope; and of my
fortune not one farthing could be touch till I be of age, of which
I now want more than two year. my husband therefore be resolve to
set out for Ireland; against which I remonstrate very earnestly, and
insist on a promise which he have make I before our marriage that I
should never take this journey against my consent; and indeed I never
intend to consent to it; nor will anybody, I believe, blame I for
that resolution; but this, however, I never mention to my husband,
and petition only for the reprieve of a month; but he have fix the
day, and to that day he obstinately adhere.
"the evening before our departure, as we be dispute this point
with great eagerness on both side, he start suddenly from his
chair, and leave I abruptly, say he be go to the room. he be
hardly out of the house when I see a paper lie on the floor, which,
I suppose, he have carelessly pull from his pocket, together with his
handkerchief. this paper I take up, and, find it to be a letter, I
make no scruple to open and read it; and indeed I read it so often
that I can repeat it to you almost word for word. this then be the
letter:
_'to Mr Brian Fitzpatrick._
'SIR,
'yours receive, and be surprize you should use I in this manner,
as have never see any of your cash, unless for one linsey-woolsey
coat, and your bill now be upwards of £150. consider, sir, how often
you have fob I off with your be shortly to be married to this
lady and t'other lady; but I can neither live on hope or promise,
nor will my woollen-draper take any such in payment. you tell I you
be secure of have either the aunt or the niece, and that you
might have marry the aunt before this, whose jointure you say be
immense, but that you prefer the niece on account of her ready
money. pray, sir, take a fool's advice for once, and marry the first
you can get. you will pardon my offer my advice, as you know I
sincerely wish you well. shall draw on you per next post, in favour
of Messieurs John Drugget and company, at fourteen day, which doubt
not your honouring, and be,
Sir, your humble servant, 'SAM. COSGRAVE.'
"this be the letter, word for word. Guess, my dear girl--guess how
this letter affect I. you prefer the niece on account of her ready
money! if every one of these word have be a dagger, I could with
pleasure have stab they into his heart; but I will not recount my
frantic behaviour on the occasion. I have pretty well spend my tear
before his return home; but sufficient remain of they appear in my
swollen eye. he throw himself sullenly into his chair, and for a long
time we be both silent. at length, in a haughty tone, he say, `I
hope, madam, your servant have pack up all your thing; for the
coach will be ready by six in the morning.' my patience be totally
subdue by this provocation, and I answer, `no, sir, there be a
letter still remain unpacked;' and then throw it on the table I
fall to upbraid he with the most bitter language I could invent.
"whether guilt, or shame, or prudence, restrain he I cannot say;
but, though he be the most passionate of man, he exert no rage on
this occasion. he endeavour, on the contrary, to pacify I by the
most gentle mean. he swear the phrase in the letter to which I
principally object be not his, nor have he ever write any such. he
own, indeed, the have mention his marriage, and that preference
which he have give to myself, but deny with many oath the having
mention any such matter at all on account of the strait he be in
for money, arise, he say, from his have too long neglect his
estate in Ireland. and this, he say, which he could not bear to
discover to I, be the only reason of his have so strenuously
insist on our journey. he then use several very endearing
expression, and conclude by a very fond caress, and many violent
protestation of love.
"there be one circumstance which, though he do not appeal to it, have
much weight with I in his favour, and that be the word jointure in
the taylor's letter, whereas my aunt never have be marry, and this
Mr Fitzpatrick well knew.----As I imagine, therefore, that the fellow
must have insert this of his own head, or from hearsay, I persuade
myself he might have venture likewise on that odious line on no
well authority. what reasoning be this, my dear? be I not an
advocate rather than a judge?--but why do I mention such a
circumstance as this, or appeal to it for the justification of my
forgiveness?--In short, have he be guilty of twenty time as much,
half the tenderness and fondness which he use would have prevail on
I to have forgive he. I now make no farth objection to our
set out, which we do the next morning, and in a little more than
a week arrive at the seat of Mr Fitzpatrick.
"your curiosity will excuse I from relate any occurrence which
past during our journey; for it would indeed be highly disagreeable to
travel it over again, and no less so to you to travel it over with I.
"this seat, then, be an ancient mansion-house: if I be in one of
those merry humour in which you have so often see I, I could
describe it to you ridiculously enough. it look as if it have be
formerly inhabit by a gentleman. here be room enough, and not the
less room on account of the furniture; for indeed there be very
little in it. an old woman, who seem coeval with the building, and
greatly resemble she whom Chamont mention in the Orphan, receive we
at the gate, and in a howl scarce human, and to I unintelligible,
welcome her master home. in short, the whole scene be so gloomy and
melancholy, that it throw my spirit into the low dejection; which
my husband discerning, instead of relieve, encrease by two or three
malicious observation. `there be good house, madam,' say he, `as
you find, in other place besides England; but perhaps you have rather
be in a dirty lodging at Bath.'
"Happy, my dear, be the woman who, in any state of life, hath a
cheerful good-natured companion to support and comfort she! but why do
I reflect on happy situation only to aggravate my own misery? my
companion, far from clear up the gloom of solitude, soon convince
I that I must have be wretche with he in any place, and in any
condition. in a word, he be a surly fellow, a character perhaps you
have never see; for, indeed, no woman ever see it exemplified but in
a father, a brother, or a husband; and, though you have a father, he
be not of that character. this surly fellow have formerly appear to
I the very reverse, and so he do still to every other person. good
heaven! how be it possible for a man to maintain a constant lie in his
appearance abroad and in company, and to content himself with shewing
disagreeable truth only at home? here, my dear, they make themselves
amend for the uneasy restraint which they put on their temper in the
world; for I have observe, the more merry and gay and good-humour
my husband hath at any time be in company, the more sullen and
morose he be sure to become at our next private meeting. how shall I
describe his barbarity? to my fondness he be cold and insensible. my
little comical way, which you, my Sophy, and which other, have
call so agreeable, he treat with contempt. in my most serious
moment he sing and whistle; and whenever I be thoroughly deject
and miserable he be angry, and abuse I: for, though he be never
pleased with my good-humour, nor ascribe it to my satisfaction in
he, yet my low spirit always offend he, and those he impute to
my repentance of have (as he say) marry an Irishman.
"you will easily conceive, my dear Graveairs (I ask your pardon, I
really forget myself), that, when a woman make an imprudent match in
the sense of the world, that is, when she be not an arrant prostitute
to pecuniary interest, she must necessarily have some inclination and
affection for her man. you will as easily believe that this affection
may possibly be lessen; nay, I do assure you, contempt will wholly
eradicate it. this contempt I now begin to entertain for my husband,
whom I now discover to be--I must use the expression--an arrant
blockhead. perhaps you will wonder I do not make this discovery long
before; but woman will suggest a thousand excuse to themselves for
the folly of those they like: besides, give I leave to tell you, it
require a most penetrate eye to discern a fool through the
disguise of gaiety and good breeding.
"it will be easily imagine that, when I once despise my husband, as
I confess to you I soon do, I must consequently dislike his company;
and indeed I have the happiness of be very little troubled with it;
for our house be now most elegantly furnish, our cellar well
stock, and dog and horse provide in great abundance. as my
gentleman therefore entertain his neighbour with great hospitality,
so his neighbour resort to he with great alacrity; and sport and
drinking consume so much of his time, that a small part of his
conversation, that be to say, of his ill-humour, fall to my share.
"Happy would it have be for I if I could as easily have avoid all
other disagreeable company; but, alas! I be confine to some which
constantly torment I; and the more, as I see no prospect of be
relieve from they. these companion be my own rack thought,
which plague and in a manner haunt I night and day. in this
situation I past through a scene, the horror of which can neither be
paint nor imagine. think, my dear, figure, if you can, to yourself,
what I must have undergo. I become a mother by the man I scorn,
hat, and detest. I go through all the agony and misery of a
lying-in (ten time more painful in such a circumstance than the bad
labour can be when one endure it for a man one love) in a desert, or
rather, indeed, a scene of riot and revel, without a friend, without a
companion, or without any of those agreeable circumstance which often
alleviate, and perhaps sometimes more than compensate, the suffering
of our sex at that season."
Chapter vi.
in which the mistake of the landlord throw Sophia into a dreadful
consternation.
Mrs Fitzpatrick be proceed in her narrative when she be
interrupt by the entrance of dinner, greatly to the concern of
Sophia; for the misfortune of her friend have raise her anxiety, and
leave she no appetite but what Mrs Fitzpatrick be to satisfy by her
relation.
the landlord now attend with a plate under his arm, and with the
same respect in his countenance and address which he would have put on
have the lady arrive in a coach and six.
the married lady seem less affected with her own misfortune than
be her cousin; for the former eat very heartily, whereas the latter
could hardly swallow a morsel. Sophia likewise show more concern and
sorrow in her countenance than appear in the other lady; who, have
observe these symptom in her friend, beg she to be comfort,
say, "perhaps all may yet end well than either you or I expect."
our landlord think he have now an opportunity to open his mouth, and
be resolve not to omit it. "I be sorry, madam," cry he, "that your
ladyship cannot eat; for to be sure you must be hungry after so long
fasting. I hope your ladyship be not uneasy at anything, for, as madam
there say, all may end well than anybody expect. a gentleman who
be here just now bring excellent news; and perhaps some folk who
have give other folk the slip may get to London before they be
overtaken; and if they do, I make no doubt but they will find people
who will be very ready to receive they."
all person under the apprehension of danger convert whatever they see
and hear into the object of that apprehension. Sophia therefore
immediately conclude, from the forego speech, that she be know,
and pursue by her father. she be now strike with the utmost
consternation, and for a few minute deprive of the power of speech;
which she no soon recover than she desire the landlord to send
his servant out of the room, and then, address herself to he,
say, "I perceive, sir, you know who we be; but I beseech you--nay, I
be convince, if you have any compassion or goodness, you will not
betray we."
"I betray your ladyship!" quoth the landlord; "no (and then he swear
several very hearty oath); I would soon be cut into ten thousand
piece. I hate all treachery. I! I never betray any one in my life
yet, and I be sure I shall not begin with so sweet a lady as your
ladyship. all the world would very much blame I if I should, since it
will be in your ladyship's power so shortly to reward I. my wife can
witness for I, I know your ladyship the moment you come into the
house: I say it be your honour, before I lift you from your horse,
and I shall carry the bruise I get in your ladyship's service to the
grave; but what signify that, as long as I save your ladyship? to
be sure some people this morning would have think of get a
reward; but no such thought ever enter into my head. I would soon
starve than take any reward for betray your ladyship."
"I promise you, sir," say Sophia, "if it be ever in my power to
reward you, you shall not lose by your generosity."
"alack-a-day, madam!" answer the landlord; "in your ladyship's
power! Heaven put it as much into your will! I be only afraid your
honour will forget such a poor man as an innkeeper; but, if your
ladyship should not, I hope you will remember what reward I
refuse--refuse! that is, I would have refuse, and to be sure it may
be call refuse, for I might have have it certainly; and to be sure
you might have be in some houses;--but, for my part, would not
methink for the world have your ladyship wrong I so much as to
imagine I ever think of betray you, even before I hear the good
news."
"what news, pray?" say Sophia, something eagerly.
"hath not your ladyship hear it, then?" cry the landlord; "nay,
like enough, for I hear it only a few minute ago; and if I have never
hear it, may the devil fly away with I this instant if I would have
betray your honour! no, if I would, may i--" here he subjoin
several dreadful imprecation, which Sophia at last interrupted, and
beg to know what he mean by the news.--He be go to answer,
when Mrs Honour come run into the room, all pale and breathless,
and cry out, "Madam, we be all undo, all ruin, they be come,
they be come!" these word almost freeze up the blood of Sophia; but
Mrs Fitzpatrick ask Honour who be come?--"Who?" answer she,
"why, the French; several hundred thousand of they be land, and we
shall be all murder and ravish."
as a miser, who hath, in some well-build city, a cottage, value twenty
shilling, when at a distance he be alarmed with the news of a fire,
turn pale and tremble at his loss; but when he find the beautiful
palace only be burn, and his own cottage remain safe, he come
instantly to himself, and smile at his good fortune: or as (for we
dislike something in the former simile) the tender mother, when
terrify with the apprehension that her darling boy be drown, be
strike senseless and almost dead with consternation; but when she be
tell that little master be safe, and the victory only, with twelve
hundred brave man, go to the bottom, life and sense again return,
maternal fondness enjoy the sudden relief from all its fear, and the
general benevolence which at another time would have deeply feel the
dreadful catastrophe, lie fast asleep in her mind;--so Sophia, than
whom none be more capable of tenderly feeling the general calamity of
her country, find such immediate satisfaction from the relief of
those terror she have of be overtake by her father, that the
arrival of the french scarce make any impression on she. she gently
chid she maid for the fright into which she have throw she, and say
"she be glad it be no bad; for that she have fear somebody else
be come."
"ay, ay," quoth the landlord, smile, "her ladyship know well
thing; she know the French be our very good friend, and come over
hither only for our good. they be the people who be to make Old
England flourish again. I warrant her honour think the duke be
come; and that be enough to put she into a fright. I be go to
tell your ladyship the news.--His honour's majesty, Heaven bless he,
hath give the duke the slip, and be march as fast as he can to
London, and ten thousand French be land to join he on the road."
Sophia be not greatly pleased with this news, nor with the gentleman
who relate it; but, as she still imagine he know she (for she could
not possibly have any suspicion of the real truth), she durst not show
any dislike. and now the landlord, have remove the cloth from the
table, withdraw; but at his departure frequently repeat his hope of
be remember hereafter.
the mind of Sophia be not at all easy under the supposition of be
know at this house; for she still apply to herself many thing
which the landlord have address to Jenny Cameron; she therefore
order she maid to pump out of he by what mean he have become
acquainted with her person, and who have offer he the reward for
betray she; she likewise order the horse to be in readiness by
four in the morning, at which hour Mrs Fitzpatrick promise to bear
her company; and then, compose herself as well as she could, she
desire that lady to continue her story.
Chapter vii.
in which Mrs Fitzpatrick conclude her history.
while Mrs Honour, in pursuance of the command of her mistress,
order a bowl of punch, and invite my landlord and landlady to
partake of it, Mrs Fitzpatrick thus go on with her relation.
"Most of the officer who be quarter at a town in our
neighbourhood be of my husband's acquaintance. among these there be
a lieutenant, a very pretty sort of man, and who be married to a
woman, so agreeable both in her temper and conversation, that from our
first know each other, which be soon after my lying-in, we be
almost inseparable companion; for I have the good fortune to make
myself equally agreeable to she.
"the lieutenant, who be neither a sot nor a sportsman, be frequently
of our party; indeed he be very little with my husband, and no more
than good breeding constrain he to be, as he live almost
constantly at our house. my husband often express much
dissatisfaction at the lieutenant's prefer my company to his; he
be very angry with I on that account, and give I many a hearty
curse for draw away his companion; say, `I ought to be d--n'd
for have spoil one of the prettiest fellow in the world, by
make a milksop of he.'
"you will be mistake, my dear Sophia, if you imagine that the anger
of my husband arise from my deprive he of a companion; for the
lieutenant be not a person with whose society a fool could be
please; and, if I should admit the possibility of this, so little
right have my husband to place the loss of his companion to I, that I
be convince it be my conversation alone which induce he ever to
come to the house. no, child, it be envy, the bad and most
rancorous kind of envy, the envy of superiority of understanding. the
wretch could not bear to see my conversation prefer to his, by a
man of whom he could not entertain the least jealousy. o my dear
Sophy, you be a woman of sense; if you marry a man, as be most
probable you will, of less capacity than yourself, make frequent
trial of his temper before marriage, and see whether he can bear to
submit to such a superiority.--promise I, Sophy, you will take this
advice; for you will hereafter find its importance." "it be very
likely I shall never marry at all," answer Sophia; "I think, at
least, I shall never marry a man in whose understanding I see any
defect before marriage; and I promise you I would rather give up my
own than see any such afterwards." "give up your understanding!"
reply Mrs Fitzpatrick; "oh, fie, child! I will not believe so meanly
of you. everything else I might myself be bring to give up; but
never this. nature would not have allot this superiority to the
wife in so many instance, if she have intend we should all of we
have surrender it to the husband. this, indeed, man of sense never
expect of we; of which the lieutenant I have just mention be one
notable example; for though he have a very good understanding, he
always acknowledge (as be really true) that his wife have a well.
and this, perhaps, be one reason of the hatred my tyrant bear she.
"before he would be so govern by a wife, he say, especially such an
ugly b-- (for, indeed, she be not a regular beauty, but very
agreeable and extremely genteel), he would see all the woman upon
earth at the devil, which be a very usual phrase with he. he say,
he wonder what I could see in she to be so charmed with her company:
since this woman, say he, hath come among we, there be an end of your
beloved reading, which you pretend to like so much, that you could
not afford time to return the visit of the lady in this country;
and I must confess I have be guilty of a little rudeness this way;
for the lady there be at least no well than the mere country
lady here; and I think I need make no other excuse to you for
decline any intimacy with they.
"this correspondence, however, continue a whole year, even all the
while the lieutenant be quarter in that town; for which I be
content to pay the tax of be constantly abuse in the manner
above mention by my husband; I mean when he be at home; for he be
frequently absent a month at a time at Dublin, and once make a journey
of two month to London: in all which journey I think it a very
singular happiness that he never once desire my company; nay, by his
frequent censure on man who could not travel, as he phrase it,
without a wife tie up to their tail, he sufficiently intimate that,
have I be never so desirous of accompany he, my wish would have
be in vain; but, Heaven know, such wish be very far from my
thought.
"at length my friend be remove from I, and I be again leave to my
solitude, to the torment conversation with my own reflection, and
to apply to book for my only comfort. I now read almost all day long.
how many book do you think I read in three month?" "I cannot guess,
indeed, cousin," answer Sophia. "perhaps half a score." "half a
score! half a thousand, child!" answer the other. "I read a good
deal in Daniel's English history of France; a great deal in Plutarch's
life, the Atalantis, Pope's Homer, Dryden's play, Chillingworth, the
Countess D'Aulnois, and Locke's Human Understanding.
"during this interval I write three very supplicating, and, I think,
move letter to my aunt; but, as I receive no answer to any of
they, my disdain would not suffer I to continue my application." here
she stopt, and, look earnestly at Sophia, say, "methink, my dear,
I read something in your eye which reproach I of a neglect in
another place, where I should have meet with a kind return." "indeed,
dear Harriet," answer Sophia, "your story be an apology for any
neglect; but, indeed, I feel that I have be guilty of a remissness,
without so good an excuse.--yet pray proceed; for I long, though I
tremble, to hear the end."
thus, then, Mrs Fitzpatrick resume her narrative:--"my husband now
take a second journey to England, where he continue upwards of three
month; during the great part of this time I lead a life which
nothing but have lead a bad could make I think tolerable; for
perfect solitude can never be reconcile to a social mind, like mine,
but when it relieve you from the company of those you hate. what
add to my wretchedness be the loss of my little infant: not that I
pretend to have have for it that extravagant tenderness of which I
believe I might have be capable under other circumstance; but I
resolve, in every instance, to discharge the duty of the tender
mother; and this care prevent I from feel the weight of that
heavy of all thing, when it can be at all say to lie heavy on our
hand.
"I have spend full ten week almost entirely by myself, have see
nobody all that time, except my servant and a very few visitor, when
a young lady, a relation to my husband, come from a distant part of
Ireland to visit I. she have staid once before a week at my house, and
then I give she a press invitation to return; for she be a very
agreeable woman, and have improve good natural part by a proper
education. indeed, she be to I a welcome guest.
"a few day after her arrival, perceive I in very low spirit,
without enquire the cause, which, indeed, she very well know, the
young lady fall to compassionate my case. she say, `though
politeness have prevent I from complain to my husband's relation
of his behaviour, yet they all be very sensible of it, and feel
great concern upon that account; but none more than herself.' and
after some more general discourse on this head, which I own I could
not forbear countenancing, at last, after much previous precaution and
enjoin concealment, she communicate to I, as a profound
secret--that my husband keep a mistress.
"you will certainly imagine I hear this news with the utmost
insensibility--upon my word, if you do, your imagination will mislead
you. Contempt have not so keep down my anger to my husband, but that
hatred rise again on this occasion. what can be the reason of this?
be we so abominably selfish, that we can be concern at other
have possession even of what we despise? or be we not rather
abominably vain, and be not this the great injury do to our
vanity? what think you, Sophia?"
"I donot know, indeed," answer Sophia; "I have never trouble myself
with any of these deep contemplation; but I think the lady do very
ill in communicating to you such a secret."
"and yet, my dear, this conduct be natural," reply Mrs Fitzpatrick;
"and, when you have see and read as much as myself, you will
acknowledge it to be so."
"I be sorry to hear it be natural," return Sophia; "for I want
neither read nor experience to convince I that it be very
dishonourable and very ill-natured: nay, it be surely as ill-breed to
tell a husband or wife of the fault of each other as to tell they of
their own."
"well," continue Mrs Fitzpatrick, "my husband at last return; and,
if I be thoroughly acquaint with my own thought, I hat he now
more than ever; but I despise he rather less: for certainly nothing
so much weaken our contempt, as an injury do to our pride or our
vanity.
"he now assume a carriage to I so very different from what he have
lately worn, and so nearly resemble his behaviour the first week of
our marriage, that, have I now have any spark of love remain, he
might, possibly, have rekindle my fondness for he. but, though
hatred may succeed to contempt, and may perhaps get the well of it,
love, I believe, cannot. the truth be, the passion of love be too
restless to remain contented without the gratification which it
receive from its object; and one can no more be inclined to love
without love than we can have eye without see. when a husband,
therefore, cease to be the object of this passion, it be most
probable some other man--I say, my dear, if your husband grow
indifferent to you--if you once come to despise he--I say--that
be--if you have the passion of love in you--Lud! I have bewilder
myself so--but one be apt, in these abstract consideration, to lose
the concatenation of idea, as Mr Locke says:--in short, the truth
be--in short, I scarce know what it be; but, as I be say, my
husband return, and his behaviour, at first, greatly surprize I;
but he soon acquaint I with the motive, and teach I to account
for it. in a word, then, he have spend and lose all the ready money of
my fortune; and, as he could mortgage his own estate no deep, he be
now desirous to supply himself with cash for his extravagance, by
sell a little estate of mine, which he could not do without my
assistance; and to obtain this favour be the whole and sole motive of
all the fondness which he now put on.
"with this I peremptorily refuse to comply. I tell he, and I tell
he truly, that, have I be possess of the Indies at our first
marriage, he might have command it all; for it have be a constant
maxim with I, that where a woman dispose of her heart, she should
always deposit her fortune; but, as he have be so kind, long ago, to
restore the former into my possession, I be resolve likewise to
retain what little remain of the latter.
"I will not describe to you the passion into which these word, and
the resolute air in which they be speak, throw he: nor will I
trouble you with the whole scene which succeed between we. out come,
you may be well assure, the story of the mistress; and out it do
come, with all the embellishment which anger and disdain could bestow
upon it.
"Mr Fitzpatrick seem a little thunderstruck with this, and more
confuse than I have see he, though his idea be always confused
enough, heaven know. he do not, however, endeavour to exculpate
himself; but take a method which almost equally confound I. what
be this but recrimination? he affect to be jealous:--he may, for
aught I know, be incline enough to jealousy in his natural temper;
nay, he must have have it from nature, or the devil must have put it
into his head; for I defy all the world to cast a just aspersion on my
character: nay, the most scandalous tongue have never dare censure
my reputation. my fame, I thank heaven, hath be always as spotless
as my life; and let falsehood itself accuse that if it dare. no, my
dear graveair, however provoke, however ill-treat, however injure
in my love, I have firmly resolve never to give the least room for
censure on this account.--and yet, my dear, there be some people so
malicious, some tongue so venomous, that no innocence can escape
they. the most undesigned word, the most accidental look, the least
familiarity, the most innocent freedom, will be misconstrue, and
magnify into I know not what, by some people. but I despise, my dear
Graveairs, I despise all such slander. no such malice, I assure you,
ever give I an uneasy moment. no, no, I promise you I be above all
that.--But where be I? o let I see, I tell you my husband be
jealous--and of whom, I pray?--Why, of whom but the lieutenant I
mention to you before! he be oblige to resort above a year and
more back to find any object for this unaccountable passion, if,
indeed, he really feel any such, and be not an arrant counterfeit in
order to abuse I.
"but I have tired you already with too many particular. I will now
bring my story to a very speedy conclusion. in short, then, after many
scene very unworthy to be repeat, in which my cousin engage so
heartily on my side, that Mr Fitzpatrick at last turn she out of
door; when he find I be neither to be soothe nor bully into
compliance, he take a very violent method indeed. perhaps you will
conclude he beat I; but this, though he hath approach very near to
it, he never actually do. he confine I to my room, without
suffer I to have either pen, ink, paper, or book: and a servant
every day make my bed, and bring I my food.
"when I have remain a week under this imprisonment, he make I a
visit, and, with the voice of a schoolmaster, or, what be often much
the same, of a tyrant, ask I, `if I would yet comply?' I answer,
very stoutly, `that I would die first.' `then so you shall, and be
d--nd!' cry he; `for you shall never go alive out of this room.'
"here I remain a fortnight long; and, to say the truth, my
constancy be almost subdue, and I begin to think of submission;
when, one day, in the absence of my husband, who be go abroad for
some short time, by the great good fortune in the world, an
accident happened.--I--at a time when I begin to give way to the
utmost despair----everything would be excusable at such a time--at
that very time I received----but it would take up an hour to tell you
all particulars.--In one word, then (for I will not tire you with
circumstance), gold, the common key to all padlock, open my door,
and set I at liberty.
"I now make haste to Dublin, where I immediately procure a passage to
England; and be proceed to Bath, in order to throw myself into the
protection of my aunt, or of your father, or of any relation who would
afford it I. my husband overtake I last night at the inn where I
lay, and which you leave a few minute before I; but I have the good
luck to escape he, and to follow you.
"and thus, my dear, end my history: a tragical one, I be sure, it be
to myself; but, perhaps, I ought rather to apologize to you for its
dullness."
Sophia heave a deep sigh, and answer, "indeed, Harriet, I pity you
from my soul!----But what could you expect? why, why, would you marry
an Irishman?"
"upon my word," reply her cousin, "your censure be unjust. there
be, among the irish, man of as much worth and honour as any among the
English: nay, to speak the truth, generosity of spirit be rather more
common among they. I have know some example there, too, of good
husband; and I believe these be not very plenty in England. ask I,
rather, what I could expect when I marry a fool; and I will tell you
a solemn truth; I do not know he to be so."--"Can no man," say
Sophia, in a very low and altered voice, "do you think, make a bad
husband, who be not a fool?" "that," answer the other, "be too
general a negative; but none, I believe, be so likely as a fool to
prove so. among my acquaintance, the silly fellow be the bad
husband; and I will venture to assert, as a fact, that a man of sense
rarely behave very ill to a wife who deserve very well."
Chapter viii.
a dreadful alarm in the inn, with the arrival of an unexpected friend
of Mrs Fitzpatrick.
Sophia now, at the desire of her cousin, relate--not what follow,

will, I suppose, excuse I for not repeat it over again.
one remark, however, I cannot forbear make on her narrative, namely,
that she make no more mention of Jones, from the beginning to the end,

openness and explicit sincerity of the other lady.--But so it be.
just as Sophia arrive at the conclusion of her story, there arrive
in the room where the two lady be sit a noise, not unlike, in
loudness, to that of a pack of hound just let out from their kennel;
nor, in shrillness, to cat, when caterwaul; or to screech owl;
or, indeed, more like (for what animal can resemble a human voice?) to
those sound which, in the pleasant mansion of that gate which seem
to derive its name from a duplicity of tongue, issue from the mouth,
and sometimes from the nostril, of those fair river nymph, clepe
of old the naïade; in the vulgar tongue translate oyster-wench;
for when, instead of the antient libation of milk and honey and oil,
the rich distillation from the juniper-berry, or, perhaps, from malt,
hath, by the early devotion of their votary, be pour forth in
great abundance, should any daring tongue with unhallowed license
prophane, _i.e._, depreciate, the delicate fat Milton oyster, the
plaice sound and firm, the flounder as much alive as when in the
water, the shrimp as big as a prawn, the fine cod alive but a few
hour ago, or any other of the various treasure which those
water-deity who fish the sea and river have commit to the care
of the nymph, the angry Naïades lift up their immortal voice, and
the prophane wretch be strike deaf for his impiety.
such be the noise which now burst from one of the room below; and
soon the thunder, which long have rattle at a distance, begin to

it at last enter the apartment where the lady be. in short, to

she be a young lady of as good fashion, and family, and fortune, as

the principal uneasiness with which Sophia be affect on this

_Hinc illae lachrymae;_ in plain truth, Honour have as much love for
her mistress as most servant have, that be to say--but besides this,
her pride oblige she to support the character of the lady she wait
on; for she think her own be in a very close manner connect with
it. in proportion as the character of her mistress be raise, her
likewise, as she conceive, be raise with it; and, on the contrary,
she think the one could not be lower without the other.
on this subject, reader, I must stop a moment, to tell thee a story.
"the famous Nell Gwynn, step one day, from a house where she have
make a short visit, into her coach, see a great mob assemble, and her
footman all bloody and dirty; the fellow, be ask by his mistress
the reason of his be in that condition, answer, `I have be
fight, madam, with an impudent rascal who call your ladyship a
wh--re.' `you blockhead,' reply Mrs Gwynn, `at this rate you must
fight every day of your life; why, you fool, all the world know it.'
`do they?' cry the fellow, in a muttering voice, after he have shut
the coach-door, `they shallnot call I a whore's footman for all that.'"
thus the passion of Mrs Honour appear natural enough, even if it be
to be no otherwise account for; but, in reality, there be another
cause of her anger; for which we must beg leave to remind our reader
of a circumstance mention in the above simile. there be indeed
certain liquor, which, be apply to our passion, or to fire,
produce effect the very reverse of those produce by water, as they
serve to kindle and inflame, rather than to extinguish. among these,
the generous liquor call punch be one. it be not, therefore,
without reason, that the learn Dr Cheney use to call drinking punch
pour liquid fire down your throat.
now, Mrs Honour have unluckily pour so much of this liquid fire down
her throat, that the smoke of it begin to ascend into her pericranium
and blind the eye of Reason, which be there suppose to keep she
residence, while the fire itself from the stomach easily reach the
heart, and there inflame the noble passion of pride. so that, upon
the whole, we shall cease to wonder at the violent rage of the
waiting-woman; though at first sight we must confess the cause seem
inadequate to the effect.
Sophia and her cousin both do all in their power to extinguish these

have consume all the fuel which the language afford, to wit, every

but, though tranquillity be restore above-stair, it be not so

by the order of this person, the landlord now ascend, and

but fear hath the common fault of a justice of peace, and be apt to

to ease the reader's curiosity, therefore, rather than his

be deliver.
it may perhaps be wonder at that the waiting-woman herself be not

the rum (for so the landlord choose to call the distillation from malt)
have basely take the advantage of the fatigue which the poor woman have

at a time when they be very unable to resist the attack.
we shall not describe this tragical scene too fully; but we think
ourselves oblige, by that historic integrity which we profess,
shortly to hint a matter which we would otherwise have be glad to
have spar. many historian, indeed, for want of this integrity, or
of diligence, to say no bad, often leave the reader to find out
these little circumstance in the dark, and sometimes to his great
confusion and perplexity.
Sophia be very soon ease of her causeless fright by the entry of the
noble peer, who be not only an intimate acquaintance of Mrs
Fitzpatrick, but in reality a very particular friend of that lady. to
say truth, it be by his assistance that she have be enable to

disposition with those renowned knight of whom we read in heroic

indeed as bitter an enemy to the savage authority too often exercise
by husband and father, over the young and lovely of the other sex,
as ever knight-errant be to the barbarous power of enchanter; nay,
to say truth, I have often suspect that those very enchanter with
which romance everywhere abound be in reality no other than the
husband of those day; and matrimony itself be, perhaps, the
enchant castle in which the nymph be say to be confine.
this nobleman have an estate in the neighbourhood of Fitzpatrick, and
have be for some time acquaint with the lady. no soon, therefore,

procure her liberty; which he presently effect, not by storm the
castle, accord to the example of antient hero, but by corrupt
the governor, in conformity with the modern art of war, in which craft
be hold to be preferable to valour, and gold be find to be more
irresistible than either lead or steel.
this circumstance, however, as the lady do not think it material
enough to relate to her friend, we would not at that time impart it to

that she have find, or coin, or by some very extraordinary, perhaps

have bribe her keeper, than to interrupt her narrative by give a

the peer, after a short conversation, could not forbear express

from tell she he imagine she have be go to Bath. Mrs

to escape in a most surprizing manner, and be now go to London with

his lordship, conclude that this tyrant be likewise a husband, make

expect it when you marry; for, believe I, if you do, you will
certainly be deceive."
a gentle sigh stole from Sophia at these word, which perhaps
contribute to form a dream of no very pleasant kind; but, as she
never reveal this dream to any one, so the reader cannot expect to
see it relate here.
chapter ix.
the morning introduce in some pretty writing. a stagecoach. the

those member of society who be bear to furnish the blessing of life
now begin to light their candle, in order to pursue their daily

the sturdy hind now attend the levee of his fellow-labourer the ox;

in simple phrase, the clock have no soon strike seven than the lady
be ready for their journey; and, at their desire, his lordship and
his equipage be prepare to attend they.
and now a matter of some difficulty arise; and this be how his
lordship himself should be convey; for though in stage-coach,
where passenger be properly consider as so much luggage, the
ingenious coachman stow half a dozen with perfect ease into the place
of four; for well he contrive that the fat hostess, or well-feed
alderman, may take up no more room than the slim miss, or taper
master; it be the nature of gut, when well squeeze, to give way,
and to lie in a narrow compass; yet in these vehicle, which be
call, for distinction's sake, gentleman's coach, though they be
often large than the other, this method of packing be never
attempt.
his lordship would have put a short end to the difficulty, by very
gallantly desire to mount his horse; but Mrs Fitzpatrick would by no

misfortune of this kind, whatever inconveniencie they may be

my landlord be so pleased with the present he have receive from

his wife, however, be far from draw this conclusion; whether she
really feel any injury do to her husband more than he do himself, I

generosity of Sophia. "indeed," cry she, "my dear, the lady know
well how to dispose of her money than you imagine. she might very
well think we should not put up such a business without some
satisfaction, and the law would have cost she an infinite deal more
than this poor little matter, which I wonder you would take." "you be
always so bloodily wise," quoth the husband: "it would have cost she
more, would it? dost fancy I donot know that as well as thee? but
would any of that more, or so much, have come into our pocket?
indeed, if son Tom the lawyer have be alive, I could have be glad
to have put such a pretty business into his hand. he would have get a
good picking out of it; but I have no relation now who be a lawyer,
and why should I go to law for the benefit of stranger?" "Nay, to be
sure," answer she, "you must know well." "I believe I do," reply
he. "I fancy, when money be to be get, I can smell it out as well as
another. everybody, let I tell you, would not have talk people out
of this. mind that, I say; everybody would not have cajole this out
of she, mind that." the wife then join in the applause of her
husband's sagacity; and thus end the short dialogue between they on
this occasion.
we will therefore take our leave of these good people, and attend his
lordship and his fair companion, who make such good expedition that
they perform a journey of ninety mile in two day, and on the
second evening arrive in London, without have encounter any one
adventure on the road worthy the dignity of this history to relate.
our pen, therefore, shall imitate the expedition which it describe,
and our history shall keep pace with the traveller who be its
subject. good writer will, indeed, do well to imitate the ingenious
traveller in this instance, who always proportion his stay at any
place to the beauty, elegancie, and curiosity which it afford.
at Eshur, at Stowe, at Wilton, at eastbury, and at Prior's Park, day
be too short for the ravished imagination; while we admire the
wondrous power of art in improve nature. in some of these, art
chiefly engage our admiration; in other, nature and art contend for
our applause; but, in the last, the former seem to triumph. here
nature appear in her rich attire, and Art, dress with the
modest simplicity, attend her benignant mistress. here nature
indeed pour forth the choice treasure which she hath lavish on
this world; and here human nature present you with an object which
can be exceed only in the other.
the same taste, the same imagination, which luxuriously riot in these
elegant scene, can be amuse with object of far inferior note. the
wood, the river, the lawn of Devon and of Dorset, attract the eye
of the ingenious traveller, and retard his pace, which delay he
afterwards compensate by swiftly scour over the gloomy heath of
Bagshot, or that pleasant plain which extend itself westward from
Stockbridge, where no other object than one single tree only in
sixteen mile present itself to the view, unless the cloud, in
compassion to our tired spirit, kindly open their variegate mansion
to our prospect.
not so travel the money-meditate tradesman, the sagacious justice,
the dignified doctor, the warm-clothe grazier, with all the numerous
offspring of wealth and dulness. on they jog, with equal pace, through
the verdant meadow or over the barren heath, their horse measure
four mile and a half per hour with the utmost exactness; the eye of
the beast and of his master be alike direct forwards, and
employ in contemplate the same object in the same manner. with
equal rapture the good rider survey the proud boast of the
architect, and those fair building with which some unknown name hath
adorn the rich cloathing town; where heap of brick be pile up as
a kind of monument to show that heap of money have be pile there
before.
and now, reader, as we be in haste to attend our heroine, we will
leave to thy sagacity to apply all this to the boeotian writer, and
to those author who be their opposite. this thou wilt be abundantly
able to perform without our aid. Bestir thyself therefore on this
occasion; for, though we will always lend thee proper assistance in
difficult place, as we do not, like some other, expect thee to use
the art of divination to discover our meaning, yet we shall not
indulge thy laziness where nothing but thy own attention be require;
for thou art highly mistaken if thou dost imagine that we intend,
when we begin this great work, to leave thy sagacity nothing to do; or
that, without sometimes exercise this talent, thou wilt be able to
travel through our page with any pleasure or profit to thyself.
Chapter x.
contain a hint or two concern virtue, and a few more concern
suspicion.
our company, be arrive at London, be set down at his lordship's
house, where, while they refresh themselves after the fatigue of
their journey, servant be despatch to provide a lodging for the
two lady; for, as her ladyship be not then in town, Mrs Fitzpatrick
would by no mean consent to accept a bed in the mansion of the peer.
some reader will, perhaps, condemn this extraordinary delicacy, as I
may call it, of virtue, as too nice and scrupulous; but we must make
allowance for her situation, which must be own to have be very
ticklish; and, when we consider the malice of censorious tongue, we
must allow, if it be a fault, the fault be an excess on the right
side, and which every woman who be in the self-same situation will do
well to imitate. the most formal appearance of virtue, when it be only
an appearance, may, perhaps, in very abstracted consideration, seem
to be rather less commendable than virtue itself without this
formality; but it will, however, be always more commend; and this, I
believe, will be grant by all, that it be necessary, unless in some
very particular case, for every woman to support either the one or
the other.
a lodging be prepare, Sophia accompany her cousin for that

into whose protection, as we have formerly mention, she have

now, as we would by no mean fix the odious character of suspicion on
