reader touch suspicion in general.
of this there have always appear to I to be two degree. the first
of these I chuse to derive from the heart, as the extreme velocity of
its discernment seem to denote some previous inward impulse, and the
rather as this superlative degree often form its own object; see
what be not, and always more than really exist. this be that
quick-sighted penetration whose hawk's eye no symptom of evil can
escape; which observe not only upon the action, but upon the word
and look, of man; and, as it proceed from the heart of the observer,
so it dive into the heart of the observe, and there espy evil, as
it be, in the first embryo; nay, sometimes before it can be say to
be conceive. an admirable faculty, if it be infallible; but, as this
degree of perfection be not even claim by more than one mortal being;
so from the fallibility of such acute discernment have arise many sad
mischief and most grievous heart-ache to innocence and virtue. I
cannot help, therefore, regard this vast quick-sightedness into evil
as a vicious excess, and as a very pernicious evil in itself. and I be
the more inclined to this opinion, as I be afraid it always proceed
from a bad heart, for the reason I have above mention, and for one
more, namely, because I never know it the property of a good one. now,
from this degree of suspicion I entirely and absolutely acquit Sophia.
a second degree of this quality seem to arise from the head. this be,
indeed, no other than the faculty of see what be before your eye,
and of draw conclusion from what you see. the former of these be
unavoidable by those who have any eye, and the latter be perhaps no
less certain and necessary a consequence of our have any brain.
this be altogether as bitter an enemy to guilt as the former be to
innocence: nor can I see it in an unamiable light, even though,
through human fallibility, it should be sometimes mistake. for
instance, if a husband should accidentally surprize his wife in the
lap or in the embrace of some of those pretty young gentleman who
profess the art of cuckold-making, I should not highly, I think, blame
he for conclude something more than what he see, from the
familiarity which he really have see, and which we be at least
favourable enough to when we call they innocent freedom. the reader
will easily suggest great plenty of instance to himself; I shall add
but one more, which, however unchristian it may be think by some, I
cannot help esteem to be strictly justifiable; and this be a
suspicion that a man be capable of do what he hath do already,
and that it be possible for one who hath be a villain once to act
the same part again. and, to confess the truth, of this degree of
suspicion I believe Sophia be guilty. from this degree of suspicion
she have, in fact, conceive an opinion that her cousin be really not
well than she should be.
the case, it seem, be this: Mrs Fitzpatrick wisely consider that
the virtue of a young lady be, in the world, in the same situation
with a poor hare, which be certain, whenever it venture abroad, to
meet its enemy; for it can hardly meet any other. no soon
therefore be she determined to take the first opportunity of quit
the protection of her husband, than she resolve to cast herself under
the protection of some other man; and whom could she so properly
choose to be her guardian as a person of quality, of fortune, of
honour; and who, besides a gallant disposition which incline man to
knight-errantry, that is, to be the champion of lady in distress,
have often declare a violent attachment to herself, and have already
give she all the instance of it in his power?
but, as the law hath foolishly omit this office of vice-husband, or
guardian to an eloped lady, and as malice be apt to denominate he by
a more disagreeable appellation, it be conclude that his lordship
should perform all such kind office to the lady in secret, and
without publickly assume the character of her protector. Nay, to
prevent any other person from see he in this light, it be agree
that the lady should proceed directly to Bath, and that his lordship
should first go to London, and thence should go down to that place by
the advice of his physician.
now all this Sophia very plainly understand, not from the lip or
behaviour of Mrs Fitzpatrick, but from the peer, who be infinitely
less expert at retain a secret than be the good lady; and perhaps
the exact secrecy which Mrs Fitzpatrick have observe on this head in
her narrative serve not a little to heighten those suspicion which
be now rise in the mind of her cousin.
Sophia very easily find out the lady she seek; for indeed there be
not a chairman in town to whom her house be not perfectly well know;
and, as she receive, in return of her first message, a most pressing
invitation, she immediately accept it. Mrs Fitzpatrick, indeed, do
not desire her cousin to stay with she with more earnestness than
civility require. whether she have discern and resent the
suspicion above-mention, or from what other motive it arise, I
cannot say; but certain it be, she be full as desirous of part
with Sophia as Sophia herself could be of go.
the young lady, when she come to take leave of her cousin, could not
avoid give she a short hint of advice. she beg she, for heaven's
sake, to take care of herself, and to consider in how dangerous a
situation she stand; add, she hop some method would be find of
reconcile she to her husband. "you must remember, my dear," say
she, "the maxim which my aunt western hath so often repeat to we
both; that whenever the matrimonial alliance be break, and war
declare between husband and wife, she can hardly make a
disadvantageous peace for herself on any condition. these be my
aunt's very word, and she hath have a great deal of experience in the
world." Mrs Fitzpatrick answer, with a contemptuous smile, "never
fear I, child, take care of yourself; for you be young than I. I
will come and visit you in a few day; but, dear Sophy, let I give
you one piece of advice: leave the character of Graveairs in the
country, for, believe I, it will sit very awkwardly upon you in this
town."
thus the two cousin part, and Sophia repair directly to Lady
Bellaston, where she find a most hearty, as well as a most polite,
welcome. the lady have take a great fancy to she when she have see she
formerly with her aunt western. she be indeed extremely glad to see
she, and be no soon acquaint with the reason which induce she
to leave the squire and to fly to London than she highly applaud her
sense and resolution; and after express the high satisfaction in
the opinion which Sophia have declare she entertain of her ladyship,
by chuse her house for an asylum, she promise she all the
protection which it be in her power to give.
as we have now bring Sophia into safe hand, the reader will, I
apprehend, be content to deposit she there a while, and to look a
little after other personage, and particularly poor Jones, whom we
have leave long enough to do penance for his past offence, which, as
be the nature of vice, bring sufficient punishment upon he
themselves.
BOOK XII.
contain the same individual time with the FORMER.
Chapter i.
show what be to be deem plagiarism in a modern author, and what
be to be consider as lawful prize.
the learned reader must have observe that in the course of this
mighty work, I have often translate passage out of the good antient
author, without quote the original, or without take the least
notice of the book from whence they be borrow.
this conduct in writing be place in a very proper light by the
ingenious Abbé Bannier, in his preface to his mythology, a work of
great erudition and of equal judgment. "it will be easy," say he,
"for the reader to observe that I have frequently have great regard
to he than to my own reputation: for an author certainly pay he a
considerable compliment, when, for his sake, he suppress learn
quotation that come in his way, and which would have cost he but the
bare trouble of transcribe."
to fill up a work with these scrap may, indeed, be consider as a
downright cheat on the learn world, who be by such mean impose
upon to buy a second time, in fragment and by retail, what they have
already in gross, if not in their memory, upon their shelf; and it
be still more cruel upon the illiterate, who be draw in to pay for
what be of no manner of use to they. a writer who intermix great
quantity of Greek and Latin with his work, deal by the lady and
fine gentleman in the same paultry manner with which they be treat
by the auctioneer, who often endeavour so to confound and mix up
their lot, that, in order to purchase the commodity you want, you be
oblige at the same time to purchase that which will do you no
service.
and yet, as there be no conduct so fair and disinterested but that it
may be misunderstood by ignorance, and misrepresent by malice, I
have be sometimes tempt to preserve my own reputation at the
expense of my reader, and to transcribe the original, or at least to
quote chapter and verse, whenever I have make use either of the
thought or expression of another. I be, indeed, in some doubt that I
have often suffer by the contrary method; and that, by suppress
the original author's name, I have be rather suspect of plagiarism
than repute to act from the amiable motive assign by that justly
celebrated Frenchman.
now, to obviate all such imputation for the future, I do here confess
and justify the fact. the antient may be consider as a rich common,
where every person who hath the small tenement in Parnassus hath a
free right to fatten his muse. or, to place it in a clear light, we
modern be to the antient what the poor be to the rich. by the poor
here I mean that large and venerable body which, in English, we call
the mob. now, whoever hath have the honour to be admit to any degree
of intimacy with this mob, must well know that it be one of their
establish maxim to plunder and pillage their rich neighbour
without any reluctance; and that this be hold to be neither sin nor
shame among they. and so constantly do they abide and act by this
maxim, that, in every parish almost in the kingdom, there be a kind of
confederacy ever carry on against a certain person of opulence
call the squire, whose property be consider as free-booty by all
his poor neighbour; who, as they conclude that there be no manner of
guilt in such depredation, look upon it as a point of honour and
moral obligation to conceal, and to preserve each other from
punishment on all such occasion.
in like manner be the antient, such as Homer, Virgil, Horace,
Cicero, and the rest, to be esteem among us writer, as so many
wealthy squire, from whom we, the poor of Parnassus, claim an
immemorial custom of take whatever we can come at. this liberty I
demand, and this I be as ready to allow again to my poor neighbour in
their turn. all I profess, and all I require of my brother, be to
maintain the same strict honesty among ourselves which the mob show to
one another. to steal from one another be indeed highly criminal and
indecent; for this may be strictly stile defraud the poor
(sometimes perhaps those who be poor than ourselves), or, to set it
under the most opprobrious colour, rob the spittal.
since, therefore, upon the strict examination, my own conscience
cannot lay any such pitiful theft to my charge, I be content to
plead guilty to the former accusation; nor shall I ever scruple to
take to myself any passage which I shall find in an antient author to
my purpose, without set down the name of the author from whence it
be take. Nay, I absolutely claim a property in all such sentiment
the moment they be transcribe into my writing, and I expect all
reader henceforward to regard they as purely and entirely my own.
this claim, however, I desire to be allow I only on condition that
I preserve strict honesty towards my poor brother, from whom, if ever
I borrow any of that little of which they be possess, I shall never
fail to put their mark upon it, that it may be at all time ready to
be restore to the right owner.
the omission of this be highly blameable in one Mr Moore, who, have
formerly borrow some line of Pope and company, take the liberty to
transcribe six of they into his play of the Rival Modes. Mr Pope,
however, very luckily find they in the said play, and, lay violent
hand on his own property, transfer it back again into his own
work; and, for a further punishment, imprison the say Moore in the
loathsome dungeon of the Dunciad, where his unhappy memory now
remain, and eternally will remain, as a proper punishment for such
his unjust dealing in the poetical trade.
Chapter ii.
in which, though the squire doth not find his daughter, something be
find which put an end to his pursuit.
the history now return to the inn at Upton, whence we shall first
trace the footstep of Squire Western; for, as he will soon arrive at
an end of his journey, we shall have then full leisure to attend our
heroe.
the reader may be pleased to remember that the say squire depart
from the inn in great fury, and in that fury he pursue his daughter.
the hostler have inform he that she have cross the Severn, he
likewise past that river with his equipage, and ride full speed,
vow the utmost vengeance against poor Sophia, if he should but
overtake she.
he have not go far before he arrive at a crossway. here he call a
short council of war, in which, after hear different opinion, he
at last give the direction of his pursuit to fortune, and strike
directly into the Worcester road.
in this road he proceed about two mile, when he begin to bemoan
himself most bitterly, frequently cry out, "what pity be it! sure
never be so unlucky a dog as myself!" and then burst forth a volley
of oath and execration.
the parson attempt to administer comfort to he on this occasion.
"sorrow not, sir," say he, "like those without hope. Howbeit we have
not yet be able to overtake young madam, we may account it some good
fortune that we have hitherto trace her course aright. peradventure
she will soon be fatigate with her journey, and will tarry in some
inn, in order to renovate her corporeal function; and in that case,
in all moral certainty, you will very briefly be _compos voti_."
"Pogh! d--n the slut!" answer the squire, "I be lament the loss
of so fine a morning for hunting. it be confound hard to lose one of
the well scenting day, in all appearance, which hath be this
season, and especially after so long a frost."
whether Fortune, who now and then show some compassion in her
wanton trick, might not take pity of the squire; and, as she have
determine not to let he overtake his daughter, might not resolve to
make he amend some other way, I will not assert; but he have hardly
utter the word just before commemorate, and two or three oath at
their heel, when a pack of hound begin to open their melodious
throat at a small distance from they, which the squire's horse and
his rider both perceiving, both immediately prick up their ear, and
the squire, cry, "shebe go, shebe go! damn I if she be not
go!" instantly clap spur to the beast, who little need it,
have indeed the same inclination with his master; and now the whole
company, cross into a corn-field, ride directly towards the hound,
with much hallowing and whooping, while the poor parson, blessing
himself, bring up the rear.
thus fable report that the fair Grimalkin, whom Venus, at the desire
of a passionate lover, convert from a cat into a fine woman, no
soon perceive a mouse than, mindful of her former sport, and still
retain her pristine nature, she leap from the bed of her husband
to pursue the little animal.
what be we to understand by this? not that the bride be displeased
with the embrace of her amorous bridegroom; for, though some have
remark that cat be subject to ingratitude, yet woman and cat too
will be pleased and purr on certain occasion. the truth be, as the
sagacious Sir Roger L'Estrange observe, in his deep reflection,
that, "if we shut Nature out at the door, she will come in at the
window; and that puss, though a madam, will be a mouser still." in the
same manner we be not to arraign the squire of any want of love for
his daughter; for in reality he have a great deal; we be only to
consider that he be a squire and a sportsman, and then we may apply
the fable to he, and the judicious reflection likewise.
the hound run very hard, as it be call, and the squire pursue over
hedge and ditch, with all his usual vociferation and alacrity, and
with all his usual pleasure; nor do the thought of Sophia ever once
intrude themselves to allay the satisfaction he enjoy in the chace,
which, he say, be one of the fine he ever see, and which he swear
be very well worth go fifty mile for. as the squire forget his
daughter, the servant, we may easily believe, forget their mistress;
and the parson, after have express much astonishment, in Latin, to
himself, at length likewise abandon all farth thought of the
young lady, and, jog on at a distance behind, begin to meditate a
portion of doctrine for the ensue Sunday.
the squire who own the hound be highly pleased with the arrival of
his brother squire and sportsman; for all man approve merit in their
own way, and no man be more expert in the field than Mr Western, nor
do any other well know how to encourage the dog with his voice,
and to animate the hunt with his holla.
sportsman, in the warmth of a chace, be too much engage to attend to
any manner of ceremony, nay, even to the office of humanity: for, if
any of they meet with an accident by tumble into a ditch, or into a
river, the rest pass on regardless, and generally leave he to his
fate: during this time, therefore, the two squire, though often close
to each other, interchange not a single word. the master of the hunt,
however, often see and approve the great judgment of the stranger in
draw the dog when they be at a fault, and hence conceive a very
high opinion of his understanding, as the number of his attendant
inspire no small reverence to his quality. as soon, therefore, as the
sport be end by the death of the little animal which have occasion
it, the two squire meet, and in all squire-like greeting salute each
other.
the conversation be entertain enough, and what we may perhaps
relate in an appendix, or on some other occasion; but as it nowise
concern this history, we cannot prevail on ourselves to give it a
place here. it conclude with a second chace, and that with an
invitation to dinner. this be accept, be follow by a hearty
bout of drinking, which end in as hearty a nap on the part of squire
Western.
our squire be by no mean a match either for his host, or for parson
Supple, at his cup that evening; for which the violent fatigue of
mind as well as body that he have undergo, may very well account,
without the least derogation from his honour. he be indeed, accord
to the vulgar phrase, whistle drunk; for before he have swallow the
third bottle, he become so entirely overpowered that though he be not
carry off to bed till long after, the parson consider he as
absent, and have acquaint the other squire with all relate to
Sophia, he obtain his promise of second those argument which he
intend to urge the next morning for Mr Western's return.
no soon, therefore, have the good squire shake off his evening, and
begin to call for his morning draught, and to summon his horse in
order to renew his pursuit, than Mr Supple begin his dissuasive,
which the host so strongly seconded, that they at length prevail,
and Mr Western agree to return home; be principally move by one
argument, viz., that he know not which way to go, and might probably
be rid far from his daughter instead of towards she. he then
take leave of his brother sportsman, and express great joy that the
frost be break (which might perhaps be no small motive to his
hastening home), set forwards, or rather backwards, for Somersetshire;
but not before he have first despatch part of his retinue in quest of
his daughter, after whom he likewise send a volley of the most bitter
execration which he could invent.
Chapter iii.
the departure of Jones from Upton, with what pass between he and
Partridge on the road.
at length we be once more come to our heroe; and, to say truth, we
have be oblige to part with he so long, that, consider the
condition in which we leave he, I apprehend many of our reader have
conclude we intend to abandon he for ever; he be at present in
that situation in which prudent people usually desist from enquire
any far after their friend, lest they should be shock by
hear such friend have hang themselves.
but, in reality, if we have not all the virtue, I will boldly say,
neither have we all the vice of a prudent character; and though it be
not easy to conceive circumstance much more miserable than those of
poor Jones at present, we shall return to he, and attend upon he
with the same diligence as if he be wanton in the bright beam
of fortune.
Mr Jones, then, and his companion Partridge, leave the inn a few
minute after the departure of squire Western, and pursue the same
road on foot, for the hostler tell they that no horse be by any
mean to be at that time procure at Upton. on they march with heavy
heart; for though their disquiet proceed from very different
reason, yet displeased they be both; and if Jones sigh bitterly,
Partridge grunt altogether as sadly at every step.
when they come to the cross-road where the squire have stopt to take
counsel, Jones stopt likewise, and turn to Partridge, ask his
opinion which track they should pursue. "ah, sir," answer Partridge,
"I wish your honour would follow my advice." "why should I not?"
reply Jones; "for it be now indifferent to I whither I go, or what
become of I." "my advice, then," say Partridge, "be, that you
immediately face about and return home; for who that hath such a home
to return to as your honour, would travel thus about the country like
a vagabond? I ask pardon, _se vox ea sola reperta est_."
"alas!" cry Jones, "I have no home to return to;--but if my friend,
my father, would receive I, could I bear the country from which
Sophia be fly? Cruel Sophia! Cruel! no; let I blame myself!--no;
let I blame thee. D--nation seize thee--fool--blockhead! thou hast
undo I, and I will tear thy soul from thy body."--At which word he
lay violent hand on the collar of poor Partridge, and shake he more
heartily than an ague-fit, or his own fear have ever do before.
Partridge fall tremble on his knee, and beg for mercy, vow he
have mean no harm--when Jones, after star wildly on he for a
moment, quit his hold, and discharge a rage on himself, that, have
it fall on the other, would certainly have put an end to his being,
which indeed the very apprehension of it have almost effect.
we would bestow some pain here in minutely describe all the mad
prank which Jones play on this occasion, could we be well assure
that the reader would take the same pain in peruse they; but as we
be apprehensive that, after all the labour which we should employ in
paint this scene, the say reader would be very apt to skip it
entirely over, we have save ourselves that trouble. to say the truth,
we have, from this reason alone, often do great violence to the
luxuriance of our genius, and have leave many excellent description
out of our work, which would otherwise have be in it. and this
suspicion, to be honest, arise, as be generally the case, from our
own wicked heart; for we have, ourselves, be very often most
horridly give to jumping, as we have run through the page of
voluminous historian.
suffice it then simply to say, that Jones, after have play the
part of a madman for many minute, come, by degree, to himself; which
no soon happen, than, turn to Partridge, he very earnestly
beg his pardon for the attack he have make on he in the violence of
his passion; but conclude, by desire he never to mention his
return again; for he be resolve never to see that country any more.
Partridge easily forgave, and faithfully promise to obey the
injunction now lay upon he. and then Jones very briskly cry out,
"since it be absolutely impossible for I to pursue any far the
step of my angel--I will pursue those of glory. come on, my brave
lad, now for the army:--it be a glorious cause, and I would willingly
sacrifice my life in it, even though it be worth my preserving." and
so say, he immediately strike into the different road from that
which the squire have take, and, by mere chance, pursue the very same
through which Sophia have before pass.
our traveller now march a full mile, without speak a syllable to
each other, though Jones, indeed, mutter many thing to himself. as
to Partridge, he be profoundly silent; for he be not, perhaps,
perfectly recover from his former fright; besides, he have
apprehension of provoke his friend to a second fit of wrath,
especially as he now begin to entertain a conceit, which may not,
perhaps, create any great wonder in the reader. in short, he begin now
to suspect that Jones be absolutely out of his sens.
at length, Jones, be weary of soliloquy, address himself to his
companion, and blame he for his taciturnity; for which the poor man
very honestly account, from his fear of give offence. and now this
fear be pretty well remove, by the most absolute promise of
indemnity, Partridge again take the bridle from his tongue; which,
perhaps, rejoice no less at regain its liberty, than a young colt,
when the bridle be slipt from his neck, and he be turn loose into
the pasture.
as Partridge be inhibit from that topic which would have first
suggest itself, he fall upon that which be next uppermost in his
mind, namely, the Man of the Hill. "certainly, sir," say he, "that
could never be a man, who dress himself and live after such a
strange manner, and so unlike other folk. besides, his diet, as the
old woman tell I, be chiefly upon herb, which be a fitter food for a
horse than a Christian: nay, landlord at Upton say that the
neighbour thereabout have very fearful notion about he. it run
strangely in my head that it must have be some spirit, who, perhaps,
might be send to forewarn we: and who know but all that matter which
he tell we, of his go to fight, and of his be take prisoner,
and of the great danger he be in of be hang, might be intend
as a warning to we, consider what we be go about? besides, I
dream of nothing all last night but of fight; and methought the
blood run out of my nose, as liquor out of a tap. indeed, sir,
_infandum, regina, jubes renovare dolorem_."
"Thy story, Partridge," answer Jones, "be almost as ill apply as
thy Latin. nothing can be more likely to happen than death to man who
go into battle. perhaps we shall both fall in it--and what then?"
"what then?" reply Partridge; "why then there be an end of we, be
there not? when I be go, all be over with I. what matter the cause
to I, or who get the victory, if I be kill? I shall never enjoy
any advantage from it. what be all the ringing of bell, and
bonfire, to one that be six foot under ground? there will be an end
of poor Partridge." "and an end of poor Partridge," cry Jones,
"there must be, one time or other. if you love Latin, I will repeat
you some fine line out of Horace, which would inspire courage into a
coward.
`_Dulce et decorum est pro patria mori
Mors et fugacem persequitur virum
Nec parcit imbellis juventae
Poplitibus, timidoque tergo._'"
"I wish you would construe they," cry Partridge; "for Horace be a
hard author, and I cannot understand as you repeat they."
"I will repeat you a bad imitation, or rather paraphrase, of my own,"
say Jones; "for I be but an indifferent poet:
`who would not die in his dear country's cause? since, if base fear
his dastard step withdraw, from death he cannot fly:--One common
grave Receives, at last, the coward and the brave.'"
"thatbe very certain," cry Partridge. "ay, sure, _mor omnibus
communis:_ but there be a great difference between die in one's bed
a great many year hence, like a good Christian, with all our friend
cry about we, and be shoot to-day or to-morrow, like a mad dog;
or, perhaps, hack in twenty piece with the sword, and that too
before we have repent of all our sin. o Lord, have mercy upon we!
to be sure the soldier be a wicked kind of people. I never love to
have anything to do with they. I could hardly bring myself ever to
look upon they as Christians. there be nothing but curse and
swearing among they. I wish your honour would repent: I heartily wish
you would repent before it be too late; and not think of go among
them.--Evil communication corrupt good manner. that be my principal
reason. for as for that matter, I be no more afraid than another man,
not I; as to matter of that. I know all human flesh must die; but yet
a man may live many year, for all that. why, I be a middle-aged man
now, and yet I may live a great number of year. I have read of
several who have live to be above a hundred, and some a great deal
above a hundred. not that I hope, I mean that I promise myself, to
live to any such age as that, neither.--but if it be only to eighty or
ninety. Heaven be praise, that be a great way off yet; and I be not
afraid of die then, no more than another man; but, surely, to tempt
death before a man's time be come seem to I downright wickedness and
presumption. besides, if it be to do any good indeed; but, let the
cause be what it will, what mighty matter of good can two people do?
and, for my part, I understand nothing of it. I never fire off a gun
above ten time in my life; and then it be not charge with bullet.
and for the sword, I never learn to fence, and know nothing of the
matter. and then there be those cannon, which certainly it must be
think the high presumption to go in the way of; and nobody but a
madman--I ask pardon; upon my soul I mean no harm; I beg I may not
throw your honour into another passion."
"be under no apprehension, Partridge," cry Jones; "I be now so well
convinced of thy cowardice, that thou couldst not provoke I on any
account." "your honour," answer he, "may call I coward, or anything
else you please. if love to sleep in a whole skin make a man a
coward, _non immune ab illis malis sumus_. I never read in my grammar
that a man cannot be a good man without fight. _vir bonus est quis?
Qui consulta patrum, qui lege juraque servat_. not a word of
fight; and I be sure the scripture be so much against it, that a
man shall never persuade I he be a good Christian while he shed
christian blood."
Chapter iv.
the adventure of a beggar-man.
just as Partridge have utter that good and pious doctrine, with which
the last chapter conclude, they arrive at another cross-way, when a
lame fellow in rag ask they for alm; upon which Partridge give he
a severe rebuke, say, "every parish ought to keep their own poor."
Jones then fall a-laugh, and ask Partridge, "if he be not
ashamed, with so much charity in his mouth, to have no charity in his
heart. your religion," say he, "serve you only for an excuse for
your fault, but be no incentive to your virtue. can any man who be
really a Christian abstain from relieve one of his brother in such
a miserable condition?" and at the same time, put his hand in his
pocket, he give the poor object a shilling.
"Master," cry the fellow, after thank he, "I have a curious
thing here in my pocket, which I find about two mile off, if your
worship will please to buy it. I should not venture to pull it out to
every one; but, as you be so good a gentleman, and so kind to the
poor, you willnot suspect a man of be a thief only because he be
poor." he then pull out a little gilt pocket-book, and deliver it
into the hand of Jones.
Jones presently open it, and (guess, reader, what he feel) see in
the first page the word Sophia Western, write by her own fair hand.
he no soon read the name than he pr it close to his lip; nor
could he avoid fall into some very frantic rapture,
notwithstanding his company; but, perhaps, these very rapture make
he forget he be not alone.
while Jones be kiss and mumble the book, as if he have an
excellent brown buttered crust in his mouth or as if he have really
be a book-worm, or an author who have nothing to eat but his own
work, a piece of paper fall from its leaf to the ground, which
Partridge take up, and deliver to Jones, who presently perceive it
to be a bank-bill. it be, indeed, the very bill which Western have
give his daughter the night before her departure; and a Jew would
have jump to purchase it at five shilling less than £100.
the eye of Partridge sparkle at this news, which Jones now
proclaim aloud; and so do (though with somewhat a different aspect)
those of the poor fellow who have find the book; and who (I hope from
a principle of honesty) have never open it: but we should not deal
honestly by the reader if we omit to inform he of a circumstance
which may be here a little material, viz. that the fellow could not
read.
Jones, who have feel nothing but pure joy and transport from the
find the book, be affect with a mixture of concern at this new
discovery; for his imagination instantly suggest to he that the
owner of the bill might possibly want it before he should be able to
convey it to she. he then acquaint the finder that he know the lady
to whom the book belong, and would endeavour to find she out as soon
as possible, and return it she.
the pocket-book be a late present from Mrs Western to her niece; it
have cost five-and-twenty shilling, have be buy of a celebrated
toyman; but the real value of the silver which it contain in its
clasp be about eighteen-pence; and that price the said toyman, as it
be altogether as good as when it first issue from his shop, would
now have give for it. a prudent person would, however, have take
proper advantage of the ignorance of this fellow, and would not have
offer more than a shilling, or perhaps sixpence, for it; nay, some
perhaps would have give nothing, and leave the fellow to his action of
trover, which some learn serjeant may doubt whether he could, under
these circumstance, have maintain.
Jones, on the contrary, whose character be on the outside of
generosity, and may perhaps not very unjustly have be suspect of
extravagance, without any hesitation give a guinea in exchange for the
book. the poor man, who have not for a long time before be possess
of so much treasure, give Mr Jones a thousand thank, and discover
little less of transport in his muscle than Jones have before show
when he have first read the name of Sophia Western.
the fellow very readily agree to attend our traveller to the place
where he have find the pocket-book. together, therefore, they
proceed directly thither; but not so fast as Mr Jones desire; for
his guide unfortunately happen to be lame, and could not possibly
travel fast than a mile an hour. as this place, therefore, be at
above three mile' distance, though the fellow have say otherwise, the
reader need not be acquaint how long they be in walk it.
Jones open the book a hundred time during their walk, kiss it as
often, talk much to himself, and very little to his companion. at
all which the guide expr some sign of astonishment to Partridge;
who more than once shake his head, and cry, poor gentleman! _orandum
est ut sit men sana in corpore sano._
at length they arrive at the very spot where Sophia unhappily dropt
the pocket-book, and where the fellow have as happily find it. here
Jones offer to take leave of his guide, and to improve his pace; but
the fellow, in whom that violent surprize and joy which the first
receipt of the guinea have occasion be now considerably abate, and
who have now have sufficient time to recollect himself, put on a
discontent look, and, scratch his head, say, "he hop his
worship would give he something more. your worship," say he, "will,
I hope, take it into your consideration that if I have not be honest
I might have keep the whole." and, indeed, this the reader must
confess to have be true. "if the paper there," say he, "be worth
£100, I be sure the finding it deserve more than a guinea. besides,
suppose your worship should never see the lady, nor give it she--and,
though your worship look and talk very much like a gentleman, yet I
have only your worship's bare word; and, certainly, if the right owner
ben't to be find, it all belong to the first finder. I hope your
worship will consider of all these matter: I be but a poor man, and
therefore donot desire to have all; but it be but reasonable I should
have my share. your worship look like a good man, and, I hope, will
consider my honesty; for I might have keep every farthing, and nobody
ever the wiser." "I promise thee, upon my honour," cry Jones, "that
I know the right owner, and will restore it she." "Nay, your worship,"
answer the fellow, "may do as you please as to that; if you will but
give I my share, that is, one-half of the money, your honour may keep
the rest yourself if you please;" and conclude with swearing, by a
very vehement oath, "that he would never mention a syllable of it to
any man live."
"Lookee, friend," cry Jones, "the right owner shall certainly have
again all that she lose; and as for any farth gratuity, I really
cannot give it you at present; but let I know your name, and where
you live, and it be more than possible you may hereafter have further
reason to rejoice at this morning's adventure."
"I donot know what you mean by venture," cry the fellow; "it seem I
must venture whether you will return the lady her money or no; but I
hope your worship will consider--" "come, come," say Partridge, "tell
his honour your name, and where you may be find; I warrant you will
never repent have put the money into his hand." the fellow, see
no hope of recover the possession of the pocket-book, at last
comply in give in his name and place of abode, which Jones writ
upon a piece of paper with the pencil of Sophia; and then, place the
paper in the same page where she have writ her name, he cry out,
"there, friend, you be the happy man alive; I have join your
name to that of an angel." "I donot know anything about angel,"
answer the fellow; "but I wish you would give I a little more
money, or else return I the pocket-book." Partridge now wax wrath:
he call the poor cripple by several vile and opprobrious name, and
be absolutely proceed to beat he, but Jones would not suffer any
such thing: and now, tell the fellow he would certainly find some
opportunity of serve he, Mr Jones depart as fast as his heel
would carry he; and Partridge, into whom the thought of the hundred
pound have infuse new spirit, follow his leader; while the man, who
be oblige to stay behind, fall to curse they both, as well as his
parent; "for have they," say he, "send I to charity-school to learn
to write and read and cast account, I should have know the value of
these matter as well as other people."
Chapter v.
contain more adventure which Mr Jones and his companion meet on the
road.
our traveller now walk so fast, that they have very little time or
breath for conversation; Jones meditate all the way on Sophia, and
Partridge on the bank-bill, which, though it give he some pleasure,
cause he at the same time to repine at fortune, which, in all his
walk, have never give he such an opportunity of show his honesty.
they have proceed above three mile, when Partridge, be unable any
long to keep up with Jones, call to he, and beg he a little
to slacken his pace: with this he be the more ready to comply, as he
have for some time lose the footstep of the horse, which the thaw have
enable he to trace for several mile, and he be now upon a wide
common, where be several road.
he here therefore stopt to consider which of these road he should
pursue; when on a sudden they hear the noise of a drum, that seem
at no great distance. this sound presently alarm the fear of
Partridge, and he cry out, "Lord have mercy upon we all; they be
certainly a coming!" "who be come?" cry Jones; for fear have long
since give place to soft idea in his mind; and since his adventure
with the lame man, he have be totally intent on pursue Sophia,
without entertain one thought of an enemy. "who?" cry Partridge,
"why, the rebel: but why should I call they rebel? they may be very
honest gentleman, for anything I know to the contrary. the devil take
he that affront they, I say; I be sure, if they have nothing to say
to I, I will have nothing to say to they, but in a civil way. for
Heaven's sake, sir, donot affront they if they should come, and
perhaps they may do we no harm; but would it not be the wise way to
creep into some of yonder bush, till they be go by? what can two
unarmed man do perhaps against fifty thousand? certainly nobody but a
madman; I hope your honour be not offend; but certainly no man who
hath _mens sana in corpore sano_----" here Jones interrupt this
torrent of eloquence, which fear have inspire, say, "that by the
drum he perceive they be near some town." he then make directly
towards the place whence the noise proceed, bid Partridge "take
courage, for that he would lead he into no danger;" and add, "it
be impossible the rebel should be so near."
Partridge be a little comfort with this last assurance; and though
he would more gladly have go the contrary way, he follow his
leader, his heart beat time, but not after the manner of hero, to
the music of the drum, which cease not till they have traverse the
common, and be come into a narrow lane.
and now Partridge, who keep even pace with Jones, discover something
paint fly in the air, a very few yard before he, which fancy
to be the colour of the enemy, he fall a bellowing, "oh Lord, sir,
here they be; there be the crown and coffin. oh Lord! I never see
anything so terrible; and we be within gun-shot of they already."
Jones no soon look up, than he plainly perceive what it be which
Partridge have thus mistake. "Partridge," say he, "I fancy you will
be able to engage this whole army yourself; for by the colour I guess
what the drum be which we hear before, and which beat up for
recruit to a puppet-show."
"a puppet-show!" answer Partridge, with most eager transport. "and
be it really no more than that? I love a puppet-show of all the
pastime upon earth. do, good sir, let we tarry and see it. besides, I
be quite famished to death; for it be now almost dark, and I have not
eat a morsel since three o'clock in the morning."
they now arrive at an inn, or indeed an ale-house, where Jones be
prevail upon to stop, the rather as he have no long any assurance
of be in the road he desire. they walk both directly into the
kitchen, where Jones begin to enquire if no lady have pass that way
in the morning, and Partridge as eagerly examine into the state of
their provision; and indeed his enquiry meet with the well success;
for Jones could not hear news of Sophia; but Partridge, to his great
satisfaction, find good reason to expect very shortly the agreeable
sight of an excellent smoaking dish of egg and bacon.
in strong and healthy constitution love hath a very different effect
from what it cause in the puny part of the specie. in the latter it
generally destroy all that appetite which tend towards the
conservation of the individual; but in the former, though it often
induce forgetfulness, and a neglect of food, as well as of everything
else; yet place a good piece of well-powder buttock before a hungry
lover, and he seldom fail very handsomely to play his part. thus it
happen in the present case; for though Jones perhaps want a
prompter, and might have travel much far, have he be alone,
with an empty stomach; yet no soon do he sit down to the bacon and
egg, than he fall to as heartily and voraciously as Partridge
himself.
before our traveller have finish their dinner, night come on, and as
the moon be now past the full, it be extremely dark. partridge
therefore prevail on Jones to stay and see the puppet-show, which
be just go to begin, and to which they be very eagerly invite
by the master of the say show, who declare that his figure be the
fine which the world have ever produce, and that they have give
great satisfaction to all the quality in every town in England.
the puppet-show be perform with great regularity and decency. it
be call the fine and serious part of the Provoked Husband; and it
be indeed a very grave and solemn entertainment, without any low wit
or humour, or jest; or, to do it no more than justice, without
anything which could provoke a laugh. the audience be all highly
pleased. a grave matron tell the master she would bring her two
daughter the next night, as he do not show any stuff; and an
attorney's clerk and an exciseman both declare, that the character
of Lord and Lady Townley be well preserve, and highly in nature.
Partridge likewise concur with this opinion.
the master be so highly elated with these encomium, that he could
not refrain from add some more of his own. he say, "the present
age be not improve in anything so much as in their puppet-show;
which, by throw out Punch and his wife Joan, and such idle
trumpery, be at last bring to be a rational entertainment. I
remember," say he, "when I first take to the business, there be a
great deal of low stuff that do very well to make folk laugh; but
be never calculate to improve the moral of young people, which
certainly ought to be principally aim at in every puppet-show: for
why may not good and instructive lesson be convey this way, as well
as any other? my figure be as big as the life, and they represent
the life in every particular; and I question not but people rise from
my little drama as much improve as they do from the great." "I would
by no mean degrade the ingenuity of your profession," answer Jones,
"but I should have be glad to have see my old acquaintance master
Punch, for all that; and so far from improve, I think, by leave
out he and his merry wife Joan, you have spoil your puppet-show."
the dancer of wire conceive an immediate and high contempt for
Jones, from these word. and with much disdain in his countenance, he
reply, "very probably, sir, that may be your opinion; but I have the
satisfaction to know the good judge differ from you, and it be
impossible to please every taste. I confess, indeed, some of the
quality at Bath, two or three year ago, want mightily to bring
Punch again upon the stage. I believe I lose some money for not
agree to it; but let other do as they will; a little matter shall
never bribe I to degrade my own profession, nor will I ever willingly
consent to the spoil the decency and regularity of my stage, by
introduce any such low stuff upon it."
"right, friend," cry the clerk, "you be very right. always avoid
what be low. there be several of my acquaintance in London, who be
resolve to drive everything which be low from the stage." "nothing
can be more proper," cry the exciseman, pull his pipe from his
mouth. "I remember," add he, "(for I then live with my lord) I be
in the footman's gallery, the night when this play of the Provoked
Husband be act first. there be a great deal of low stuff in it
about a country gentleman come up to town to stand for parliament-man;
and there they bring a parcel of his servant upon the stage, his
coachman I remember particularly; but the gentleman in our gallery
could not bear anything so low, and they damn it. I observe, friend,
you have leave all that matter out, and you be to be commend for
it."
"Nay, gentleman," cry Jones, "I can never maintain my opinion
against so many; indeed, if the generality of his audience dislike
he, the learn gentleman who conduct the show might have do very
right in dismiss Punch from his service."
the master of the show then begin a second harangue, and say much of
the great force of example, and how much the inferior part of mankind
would be deter from vice, by observe how odious it be in their
superior; when he be unluckily interrupt by an incident, which,
though perhaps we might have omit it at another time, we cannot
help relate at present, but not in this chapter.
Chapter vi.
from which it may be infer that the good thing be liable to be
misunderstood and misinterpret.
a violent uproar now arise in the entry, where my landlady be well
cuff she maid both with her fist and tongue. she have indeed miss
the wench from her employment, and, after a little search, have find
she on the puppet-show stage in company with the Merry Andrew, and in
a situation not very proper to be describe.
Though Grace (for that be her name) have forfeit all title to
modesty; yet have she not impudence enough to deny a fact in which she
be actually surprize; she, therefore, take another turn, and
attempt to mitigate the offence. "why do you beat I in this manner,
mistress?" cry the wench. "if you donot like my doing, you may turn
I away. if I be a w--e" (for the other have liberally bestow that
appellation on she), "my better be so as well as I. what be the
fine lady in the puppet-show just now? I suppose she do not lie all
night out from her husband for nothing."
the landlady now burst into the kitchen, and fall foul on both her
husband and the poor puppet-mover. "here, husband," say she, "you see
the consequence of harbour these people in your house. if one doth
draw a little drink the more for they, one be hardly make amend for
the litter they make; and then to have one's house make a bawdy-house
of by such lousy vermin. in short, I desire you would be go
to-morrow morning; for I will tolerate no more such doing. it be only
the way to teach our servant idleness and nonsense; for to be sure
nothing well can be learn by such idle show as these. I remember
when puppet-show be make of good scripture story, as Jephthah's
Rash Vow, and such good thing, and when wicked people be carry
away by the devil. there be some sense in those matter; but as the
parson tell we last Sunday, nobody believe in the devil now-a-day;
and here you bring about a parcel of puppet drest up like lord and
lady, only to turn the head of poor country wench; and when their
head be once turn topsy-turvy, no wonder everything else be so."
Virgil, I think, tell we, that when the mob be assemble in a
riotous and tumultuous manner, and all sort of missile weapon fly
about, if a man of gravity and authority appear amongst they, the
tumult be presently appease, and the mob, which when collect into
one body, may be well compare to an ass, erect their long ear at the
grave man's discourse.
on the contrary, when a set of grave man and philosopher be
dispute; when wisdom herself may in a manner be consider as
present, and administer argument to the disputant; should a
tumult arise among the mob, or should one scold, who be herself equal
in noise to a mighty mob, appear among the say philosopher; their
dispute cease in a moment, wisdom no long perform her ministerial
office, and the attention of every one be immediately attract by the
scold alone.
thus the uproar aforesaid, and the arrival of the landlady, silence
the master of the puppet-show, and put a speedy and final end to that
grave and solemn harangue, of which we have give the reader a
sufficient taste already. nothing indeed could have happen so very
inopportune as this accident; the most wanton malice of fortune could
not have contrive such another stratagem to confound the poor fellow,
while he be so triumphantly descant on the good moral inculcate
by his exhibition. his mouth be now as effectually stopt, as that of
quack must be, if, in the midst of a declamation on the great virtue
of his pill and powder, the corpse of one of his martyr should be
bring forth, and deposit before the stage, as a testimony of his
skill.
instead, therefore, of answer my landlady, the puppet-show man run
out to punish his Merry Andrew; and now the moon begin to put
forth her silver light, as the poet call it (though she look at
that time more like a piece of copper), Jones call for his
reckoning, and order Partridge, whom my landlady have just awake
from a profound nap, to prepare for his journey; but Partridge, have
lately carry two point, as my reader hath see before, be
embolden to attempt a third, which be to prevail with Jones to take
up a lodging that evening in the house where he then be. he
introduce this with an affected surprize at the intention which Mr
Jones declare of remove; and, after urge many excellent argument
against it, he at last insist strongly that it could be to no manner
of purpose whatever; for that, unless Jones know which way the lady
be go, every step he take might very possibly lead he the far
from she; "for you find, sir," say he, "by all the people in the
house, that she be not go this way. how much well, therefore,
would it be to stay till the morning, when we may expect to meet with
somebody to enquire of?"
this last argument have indeed some effect on Jones, and while he be
weigh it the landlord throw all the rhetoric of which he be master
into the same scale. "sure, sir," say he, "your servant give you
most excellent advice; for who would travel by night at this time of
the year?" he then begin in the usual stile to trumpet forth the
excellent accommodation which his house afford; and my landlady
likewise open on the occasion----but, not to detain the reader with
what be common to every host and hostess, it be sufficient to tell he
Jones be at last prevail on to stay and refresh himself with a few
hour' rest, which indeed he very much want; for he have hardly shut
his eye since he have leave the inn where the accident of the break
head have happen.
as soon as Jones have take a resolution to proceed no far that
night, he presently retire to rest, with his two bedfellow, the
pocket-book and the muff; but Partridge, who at several time have
refresh himself with several nap, be more inclined to eat than
to sleep, and more to drink than to either.
and now the storm which Grace have raise be at an end, and my
landlady be again reconcile to the puppet-man, who on his side
forgave the indecent reflection which the good woman in her passion
have cast on his performance, a face of perfect peace and tranquillity
reign in the kitchen; where sit assemble round the fire the
landlord and landlady of the house, the master of the puppet-show, the
attorney's clerk, the exciseman, and the ingenious Mr Partridge; in
which company past the agreeable conversation which will be find in
the next chapter.
Chapter vii.
contain a remark or two of our own and many more of the good
company assemble in the kitchen.
though the pride of Partridge do not submit to acknowledge himself a
servant, yet he condescend in most particular to imitate the
manner of that rank. one instance of this be, his greatly magnify
the fortune of his companion, as he call Jones: such be a general
custom with all servant among stranger, as none of they would
willingly be think the attendant on a beggar: for, the high the
situation of the master be, the high consequently be that of the man
in his own opinion; the truth of which observation appear from the
behaviour of all the footman of the nobility.
but, though title and fortune communicate a splendor all around they,
and the footman of man of quality and of estate think themselves
entitle to a part of that respect which be pay to the quality and
estate of their master, it be clearly otherwise with regard to virtue
and understanding. these advantage be strictly personal, and swallow
themselves all the respect which be pay to they. to say the truth,
this be so very little, that they cannot well afford to let any other
partake with they. as these therefore reflect no honour on the
domestic, so neither be he at all dishonour by the most deplorable
want of both in his master. indeed it be otherwise in the want of what
be call virtue in a mistress, the consequence of which we have
before see: for in this dishonour there be a kind of contagion,
which, like that of poverty, communicate itself to all who approach
it.
now for these reason we be not to wonder that servant (I mean among
the man only) should have so great regard for the reputation of the
wealth of their master, and little or none at all for their character
in other point, and that, though they would be ashamed to be the
footman of a beggar, they be not so to attend upon a rogue or a
blockhead; and do consequently make no scruple to spread the fame of
the iniquity and folly of their say master as far as possible,
and this often with great humour and merriment. in reality, a footman
be often a wit as well as a beau, at the expence of the gentleman
whose livery he wear.
After Partridge, therefore, have enlarge greatly on the vast fortune
to which Mr Jones be heir, he very freely communicate an
apprehension, which he have begin to conceive the day before, and for
which, as we hint at that very time, the behaviour of Jones seem
to have furnish a sufficient foundation. in short, he be now pretty
well confirm in an opinion that his master be out of his wit, with
which opinion he very bluntly acquaint the good company round the
fire.
with this sentiment the puppet-show man immediately coincide. "I
own," say he, "the gentleman surprize I very much, when he talk
so absurdly about puppet-show. it be indeed hardly to be conceive
that any man in his sens should be so much mistaken; what you say
now account very well for all his monstrous notion. poor gentleman!
I be heartily concerned for he; indeed he hath a strange wildness
about his eye, which I take notice of before, though I do not
mention it."
the landlord agree with this last assertion, and likewise claim the
sagacity of have observe it. "and certainly," add he, "it must be
so; for no one but a madman would have think of leave so good a
house to ramble about the country at that time of night."
the exciseman, pull his pipe from his mouth, say, "he think the
gentleman look and talk a little wildly;" and then turn to
Partridge, "if he be a madman," say he, "he should not be suffer to
travel thus about the country; for possibly he may do some mischief.
it be a pity he be not secure and send home to his relation."
now some conceit of this kind be likewise lurk in the mind of
Partridge; for, as he be now persuade that Jones have run away from
Mr Allworthy, he promise himself the high reward if he could by
any mean convey he back. but fear of Jones, of whose fierceness and
strength he have see, and indeed feel, some instance, have however
represent any such scheme as impossible to be execute, and have
discourage he from apply himself to form any regular plan for the
purpose. but no soon do he hear the sentiment of the exciseman
than he embrace that opportunity of declare his own, and express
a hearty wish that such a matter could be bring about.
"could be bring about!" say the exciseman: "why, there be nothing
easy."
"ah! sir," answer Partridge, "you donot know what a devil of a
fellow he be. he can take I up with one hand, and throw I out at
window; and he would, too, if he do but imagine--"
"Pogh!" say the exciseman, "I believe I be as good a man as he.
besides, here be five of we."
"I donot know what five," cry the landlady, "my husband shall have
nothing to do in it. nor shall any violent hand be lay upon anybody
in my house. the young gentleman be as pretty a young gentleman as
ever I see in my life, and I believe he be no more mad than any of we.
what do you tell of his have a wild look with his eye? they be the
prettiest eye I ever see, and he hath the prettiest look with they;
and a very modest civil young man he be. I be sure I have bepitie he
heartily ever since the gentleman there in the corner tell we he be
crost in love. certainly that be enough to make any man, especially
such a sweet young gentleman as he be, to look a little otherwise than
he do before. Lady, indeed! what the devil would the lady have well
than such a handsome man with a great estate? I suppose she be one of
your quality folk, one of your townly lady that we see last night
in the puppet-show, who donot know what they would be at."
the attorney's clerk likewise declare he would have no concern in the
business without the advice of counsel. "suppose," say he, "an action
of false imprisonment should be bring against we, what defence could
we make? who know what may be sufficient evidence of madness to a
jury? but I only speak upon my own account; for it donot look well for
a lawyer to be concern in these matter, unless it be as a lawyer.
jury be always less favourable to we than to other people. I donot
therefore dissuade you, Mr Thomson (to the exciseman), nor the
gentleman, nor anybody else."
the exciseman shake his head at this speech, and the puppet-show man
say, "Madness be sometimes a difficult matter for a jury to decide:
for I remember," say he, "I be once present at a tryal of madness,
where twenty witness swear that the person be as mad as a March
hare; and twenty other, that he be as much in his sens as any man
in england.--and indeed it be the opinion of most people, that it be
only a trick of his relation to rob the poor man of his right."
"very likely!" cry the landlady. "I myself know a poor gentleman who
be keep in a mad-house all his life by his family, and they enjoy
his estate, but it do they no good; for though the law give it they,
it be the right of another."
"Pogh!" cry the clerk, with great contempt, "who hath any right but
what the law give they? if the law give I the good estate in the
country, I should never trouble myself much who have the right."
"if it be so," say Partridge, "_Felix quem faciunt aliena pericula
cautum."_
my landlord, who have be call out by the arrival of a horseman at
the gate, now return into the kitchen, and with an affrighted
countenance cry out, "what do you think, gentleman? the rebel have
give the duke the slip, and be get almost to London. it be certainly
true, for a man on horseback just now tell I so."
"I be glad of it with all my heart," cry Partridge; "then there will
be no fighting in these part."
"I be glad," cry the clerk, "for a well reason; for I would always
have right take place."
"ay, but," answer the landlord, "I have hear some people say this
man hath no right."
"I will prove the contrary in a moment," cry the clerk: "if my
father die seize of a right; do you mind I, seize of a right, I
say; doth not that right descend to his son; and doth not one right
descend as well as another?"
"but how can he have any right to make we papishe?" say the
landlord.
"never fear that," cry Partridge. "as to the matter of right, the
gentleman there hath prove it as clear as the sun; and as to the
matter of religion, it be quite out of the case. the papist
themselves donot expect any such thing. a popish priest, whom I know
very well, and who be a very honest man, tell I upon his word and
honour they have no such design."
"and another priest, of my acquaintance," say the landlady, "hath
tell I the same thing; but my husband be always so afraid of
papishe. I know a great many papishe that be very honest sort of
people, and spend their money very freely; and it be always a maxim
with I, that one man's money be as good as another's."
"very true, mistress," say the puppet-show man, "I donot care what
religion come; provide the Presbyterians be not uppermost; for they
be enemy to puppet-show."
"and so you would sacrifice your religion to your interest," cry the
exciseman; "and be desirous to see popery bring in, be you?"
"not I, truly," answer the other; "I hate popery as much as any man;
but yet it be a comfort to one, that one should be able to live under
it, which I could not do among Presbyterians. to be sure, every man
value his livelihood first; that must be grant; and I warrant, if
you would confess the truth, you be more afraid of lose your place
than anything else; but never fear, friend, there will be an excise
under another government as well as under this."
"why, certainly," reply the exciseman, "I should be a very ill man
if I do not honour the king, whose bread I eat. that be no more than
natural, as a man may say: for what signify it to I that there
would be an excise-office under another government, since my friend
would be out, and I could expect no well than to follow they? no,
no, friend, I shall never be bubble out of my religion in hope only
of keep my place under another government; for I should certainly
be no well, and very probably might be bad."
"why, that be what I say," cry the landlord, "whenever folk say who
know what may happen! odsook! should not I be a blockhead to lend my
money to I know not who, because mayhap he may return it again? I be
sure it be safe in my own bureau, and there I will keep it."
the attorney's clerk have take a great fancy to the sagacity of
Partridge. whether this proceed from the great discernment which the
former have into man, as well as thing, or whether it arise from the
sympathy between their mind; for they be both truly Jacobites in
principle; they now shake hand heartily, and drink bumper of strong
beer to health which we think proper to bury in oblivion.
these health be afterwards pledge by all present, and even by my
landlord himself, though reluctantly; but he could not withstand the
menace of the clerk, who swear he would never set his foot within his
house again, if he refuse. the bumper which be swallow on this
occasion soon put an end to the conversation. here, therefore, we will
put an end to the chapter.
Chapter viii.
in which fortune seem to have be in a well humour with Jones than
we have hitherto see she.
as there be no wholesomer, so perhaps there be few strong, sleep
potion than fatigue. of this Jones might be say to have take a very
large dose, and it operate very forcibly upon he. he have already
sleep nine hour, and might perhaps have sleep long, have he not be
awaken by a most violent noise at his chamber-door, where the sound
of many heavy blow be accompany with many exclamation of murder.
Jones presently leapt from his bed, where he find the master of the
puppet-show belabour the back and rib of his poor Merry-Andrew,
without either mercy or moderation.
Jones instantly interpose on behalf of the suffer party, and
pin the insulting conqueror up to the wall: for the puppet-show man
be no more able to contend with Jones than the poor party-colour
jester have be to contend with this puppet-man.
but though the Merry-Andrew be a little fellow, and not very strong,
he have nevertheless some choler about he. he therefore no soon
find himself deliver from the enemy, than he begin to attack he
with the only weapon at which he be his equal. from this he first
discharge a volley of general abusive word, and thence proceed to
some particular accusations--"d--n your bl--d, you rascal," say he,
"I have not only support you (for to I you owe all the money you
get), but I have save you from the gallow. do you not want to rob
the lady of her fine riding-habit, no long ago than yesterday, in
the back-lane here? can you deny that you wish to have she alone in
a wood to strip she--to strip one of the prettiest lady that ever
be see in the world? and here you have fall upon I, and have
almost murder I, for do no harm to a girl as willing as myself,
only because she like I well than you."
Jones no soon hear this than he quit the master, lay on he
at the same time the most violent injunction of forbearance from any
further insult on the Merry-Andrew; and then take the poor wretch
with he into his own apartment, he soon learn tiding of his
Sophia, whom the fellow, as he be attend his master with his drum
the day before, have see pass by. he easily prevail with the lad to
show he the exact place, and then have summon Partridge, he
depart with the utmost expedition.
it be almost eight of the clock before all matter could be get ready
for his departure: for Partridge be not in any haste, nor could the
reckoning be presently adjust; and when both these be settle and
over, Jones would not quit the place before he have perfectly
reconcile all difference between the master and the man.
when this be happily accomplish, he set forwards, and be by the
trusty Merry-Andrew conduct to the spot by which Sophia have past;
and then have handsomely reward his conductor, he again push on
with the utmost eagerness, be highly delighted with the
extraordinary manner in which he receive his intelligence. of this
Partridge be no soon acquaint, than he, with great earnestness,
begin to prophesy, and assure Jones that he would certainly have good
success in the end: for, he say, "two such accident could never have
happen to direct he after his mistress, if Providence have not
design to bring they together at last." and this be the first time
that Jones lend any attention to the superstitious doctrine of his
companion.
they have not go above two mile when a violent storm of rain
overtake they; and, as they happen to be at the same time in sight
of an ale-house, Partridge, with much earnest entreaty, prevail with
Jones to enter, and weather the storm. hunger be an enemy (if indeed
it may be call one) which partake more of the English than of the
french disposition; for, though you subdue this never so often, it
will always rally again in time; and so it do with Partridge, who be
no soon arrive within the kitchen, than he begin to ask the same
question which he have ask the night before. the consequence of this
be an excellent cold chine be produce upon the table, upon which
not only Partridge, but Jones himself, make a very hearty breakfast,
though the latter begin to grow again uneasy, as the people of the
house could give he no fresh information concern Sophia.
their meal be over, Jones be again prepare to sally,
notwithstanding the violence of the storm still continue; but
Partridge beg heartily for another mug; and at last cast his
eye on a lad at the fire, who have enter into the kitchen, and who
at that instant be look as earnestly at he, he turn suddenly to
Jones, and cry, "Master, give I your hand, a single mug shallnot
serve the turn this bout. why, herebe more news of Madam Sophia come
to town. the boy there stand by the fire be the very lad that ride
before she. I can swear to my own plaister on his face."--"heaven
bless you, sir," cry the boy, "it be your own plaister sure enough;
I shall have always reason to remember your goodness; for it hath
almost cure I."
at these word Jones start from his chair, and, bid the boy
follow he immediately, depart from the kitchen into a private
apartment; for, so delicate be he with regard to Sophia, that he
never willingly mention her name in the presence of many people;
and, though he have, as it be, from the overflowings of his heart,
give Sophia as a toast among the officer, where he think it be
impossible she should be know; yet, even there, the reader may
remember how difficultly he be prevail upon to mention her surname.
Hard therefore be it, and perhaps, in the opinion of many sagacious
reader, very absurd and monstrous, that he should principally owe his
present misfortune to the suppose want of that delicacy with which he
so abound; for, in reality, Sophia be much more offended at the
freedom which she think (and not without good reason) he have take
with her name and character, than at any freedom, in which, under his
present circumstance, he have indulge himself with the person of
another woman; and to say truth, I believe Honour could never have
prevail on she to leave Upton without her see Jones, have it not
be for those two strong instance of a levity in his behaviour, so
void of respect, and indeed so highly inconsistent with any degree of
love and tenderness in great and delicate mind.
but so matter fall out, and so I must relate they; and if any reader
be shock at their appear unnatural, I cannot help it. I must
remind such person that I be not write a system, but a history, and
I be not oblige to reconcile every matter to the received notion
concern truth and nature. but if this be never so easy to do,
perhaps it might be more prudent in I to avoid it. for instance, as
the fact at present before we now stand, without any comment of mine
upon it, though it may at first sight offend some reader, yet, upon
more mature consideration, it must please all; for wise and good man
may consider what happen to Jones at Upton as a just punishment for
his wickedness with regard to woman, of which it be indeed the
immediate consequence; and silly and bad person may comfort
themselves in their vice by flatter their own heart that the
character of man be rather owe to accident than to virtue. now,
perhaps the reflection which we should be here incline to draw would
alike contradict both these conclusion, and would show that these
incident contribute only to confirm the great, useful, and uncommon
doctrine, which it be the purpose of this whole work to inculcate, and
which we must not fill up our page by frequently repeat, as an
ordinary parson fill his sermon by repeat his text at the end of
every paragraph.
we be content that it must appear, however unhappily Sophia have
err in her opinion of Jones, she have sufficient reason for her
opinion; since, I believe, every other young lady would, in her
situation, have err in the same manner. Nay, have she follow she
lover at this very time, and have enter this very alehouse the moment
he be depart from it, she would have find the landlord as well
acquaint with her name and person as the wench at Upton have appear
to be. for while Jones be examine his boy in whisper in an inner
room, Partridge, who have no such delicacy in his disposition, be in
the kitchen very openly catechise the other guide who have attend
Mrs Fitzpatrick; by which mean the landlord, whose ear be open on
all such occasion, become perfectly well acquainted with the tumble
of Sophia from her horse, &c., with the mistake concern Jenny
Cameron, with the many consequence of the punch, and, in short, with
almost everything which have happen at the inn whence we despatch
our lady in a coach-and-six when we last take our leaf of they.
chapter ix.
contain little more than a few odd observation.
Jones have be absent a full half-hour, when he return into the

what be to pay. and now the concern which Partridge feel at be
oblige to quit the warm chimney-corner, and a cup of excellent

far on foot, for Jones, by golden argument, have prevail with
the boy to attend he back to the inn whither he have before conduct

other guide would wait for he at the alehouse; because, as the
landlord at Upton be an intimate acquaintance of the landlord at
Gloucester, it might some time or other come to the ear of the latter
that his horse have be let to more than one person; and so the boy
might be bring to account for money which he wisely intend to put
in his own pocket.
we be oblige to mention this circumstance, trifle as it may seem,
since it retard Mr Jones a considerable time in his setting out; for
the honesty of this latter boy be somewhat high--that be, somewhat
high-price, and would indeed have cost Jones very dear, have not
Partridge, who, as we have say, be a very cunning fellow, artfully
throw in half-a-crown to be spend at that very alehouse, while the
