soon get scent of, than he open after it with such vehement and
persuasive outcry, that the boy be soon overcome, and consent to
take half-a-crown more for his stay. here we cannot help observe,
that as there be so much of policy in the low life, great man often
overvalue themselves on those refinement in imposture, in which they
be frequently excel by some of the low of the human specie.
the horse be now produce, Jones directly leapt into the
side-saddle, on which his dear Sophia have rid. the lad, indeed, very
civilly offer he the use of his; but he choose the side-saddle,
probably because it be soft. Partridge, however, though full as
effeminate as Jones, could not bear the thought of degrade his
manhood; he therefore accept the boy's offer: and now, Jones being
mount on the side-saddle of his Sophia, the boy on that of Mrs
Honour, and Partridge bestride the third horse, they set forwards on
their journey, and within four hour arrive at the inn where the
reader hath already spend so much time. Partridge be in very high
spirit during the whole way, and often mention to Jones the many
good omen of his future success which have lately befriend he; and
which the reader, without be the least superstitious, must allow to
have be particularly fortunate. Partridge be moreover well
pleased with the present pursuit of his companion than he have be
with his pursuit of glory; and from these very omen, which assure
the pedagogue of success, he likewise first acquire a clear idea of
the amour between Jones and Sophia; to which he have before give very
little attention, as he have originally take a wrong scent concern
the reason of Jones's departure; and as to what happen at Upton, he
be too much frightened just before and after his leave that place
to draw any other conclusion from thence than that poor Jones be a
downright madman: a conceit which be not at all disagreeable to the
opinion he before have of his extraordinary wildness, of which, he
think, his behaviour on their quit Gloucester so well justify
all the account he have formerly receive. he be now, however, pretty
well satisfied with his present expedition, and henceforth begin to
conceive much worthy sentiment of his friend's understanding.
the clock have just strike three when they arrive, and Jones
immediately bespoke post-horse; but unluckily there be not a horse
to be procure in the whole place; which the reader will not wonder at
when he consider the hurry in which the whole nation, and especially
this part of it, be at this time engage, when express be pass
and repasse every hour of the day and night.
Jones endeavour all he could to prevail with his former guide to
escorte he to Coventry; but he be inexorable. while he be argue
with the boy in the inn-yard, a person come up to he, and salute
he by his name, enquire how all the good family do in
Somersetshire; and now Jones cast his eye upon this person,
presently discover he to be Mr Dowling, the lawyer, with whom he
have din at Gloucester, and with much courtesy return the
salutation.
dowle very earnestly press Mr Jones to go no far that night;
and back his solicitation with many unanswerable argument, such
as, that it be almost dark, that the road be very dirty, and that
he would be able to travel much well by day-light, with many other
equally good, some of which Jones have probably suggest to himself
before; but as they be then ineffectual, so they be still: and he
continue resolute in his design, even though he should be oblige to
set out on foot.
when the good attorney find he could not prevail on Jones to stay, he
as strenuously apply himself to persuade the guide to accompany he.
he urge many motif to induce he to undertake this short journey,
and at last conclude with say, "do you think the gentleman willnot
very well reward you for your trouble?"
two to one be odd at every other thing as well as at foot-ball. but
the advantage which this united force hath in persuasion or entreaty
must have be visible to a curious observer; for he must have often
see, that when a father, a master, a wife, or any other person in
authority, have stoutly adhere to a denial against all the reason
which a single man could produce, they have afterwards yield to the
repetition of the same sentiment by a second or third person, who
hath undertake the cause, without attempt to advance anything new
in its behalf. and hence, perhaps, proceed the phrase of second an
argument or a motion, and the great consequence this be of in all
assembly of public debate. hence, likewise, probably it be, that in
our court of law we often hear a learn gentleman (generally a
serjeant) repeat for an hour together what another learn
gentleman, who speak just before he, have be say.
instead of account for this, we shall proceed in our usual manner
to exemplify it in the conduct of the lad above mention, who
submit to the persuasion of Mr Dowling, and promise once more to
admit Jones into his side-saddle; but insist on first give the
poor creature a good bait, say, they have travel a great way,
and be rid very hard. indeed this caution of the boy be needless;
for Jones, notwithstanding his hurry and impatience, would have
order this of himself; for he by no mean agree with the opinion of
those who consider animal as mere machine, and when they bury their
spur in the belly of their horse, imagine the spur and the horse to
have an equal capacity of feel pain.
while the beast be eat their corn, or rather be suppose to
eat it (for, as the boy be take care of himself in the kitchen, the
ostler take great care that his corn should not be consume in the
stable), Mr Jones, at the earnest desire of Mr Dowling, accompany
that gentleman into his room, where they sit down together over a
bottle of wine.
Chapter x.
in which Mr Jones and Mr Dowling drink a bottle together.
Mr Dowling, pour out a glass of wine, name the health of the good
Squire Allworthy; add, "if you please, sir, we will likewise
remember his nephew and heir, the young squire: come, sir, here's Mr
Blifil to you, a very pretty young gentleman; and who, I dare swear,
will hereafter make a very considerable figure in his country. I have
a borough for he myself in my eye."
"Sir," answer Jones, "I be convinced you donot intend to affront I,
so I shall not resent it; but I promise you, you have join two
person very improperly together; for one be the glory of the human
specie, and the other be a rascal who dishonour the name of man."
dowle star at this. he say, "he think both the gentleman have a
very unexceptionable character. as for squire Allworthy himself," say
he, "I never have the happiness to see he; but all the world talk of
his goodness. and, indeed, as to the young gentleman, I never see he
but once, when I carry he the news of the loss of his mother; and
then I be so hurried, and drive, and tear with the multiplicity of
business, that I have hardly time to converse with he; but he look
so like a very honest gentleman, and behave himself so prettily, that
I protest I never be more delighted with any gentleman since I be
bear."
"I donot wonder," answer Jones, "that he should impose upon you in
so short an acquaintance; for he hath the cunning of the devil
himself, and you may live with he many year, without discover
he. I be breed up with he from my infancy, and we be hardly ever
asunder; but it be very lately only that I have discover half the
villany which be in he. I own I never greatly like he. I think he
want that generosity of spirit, which be the sure foundation of all
that be great and noble in human nature. I see a selfishness in he
long ago which I despise; but it be lately, very lately, that I have
find he capable of the basest and black design; for, indeed, I
have at last find out, that he hath take an advantage of the
openness of my own temper, and hath concert the deep project, by
a long train of wicked artifice, to work my ruin, which at last he
hath effect."
"ay! ay!" cry dowle; "I protest, then, it be a pity such a person
should inherit the great estate of your uncle Allworthy."
"alas, sir," cry Jones, "you do I an honour to which I have no
title. it be true, indeed, his goodness once allow I the liberty of
call he by a much near name; but as this be only a voluntary
act of goodness, I can complain of no injustice when he think proper
to deprive I of this honour; since the loss cannot be more unmerited
than the gift originally be. I assure you, sir, I be no relation of
Mr Allworthy; and if the world, who be incapable of set a true
value on his virtue, should think, in his behaviour to I, he hath
deal hardly by a relation, they do an injustice to the good of man:
for I--but I ask your pardon, I shall trouble you with no particular
relate to myself; only as you seem to think I a relation of Mr
Allworthy, I think proper to set you right in a matter that might
draw some censure upon he, which I promise you I would rather lose
my life than give occasion to."
"I protest, sir," cry Dowling, "you talk very much like a man of
honour; but instead of give I any trouble, I protest it would give
I great pleasure to know how you come to be think a relation of Mr
Allworthy's, if you be not. your horse willnot be ready this
half-hour, and as you have sufficient opportunity, I wish you would
tell I how all that happen; for I protest it seem very surprizing
that you should pass for a relation of a gentleman, without be so."
Jones, who in the compliance of his disposition (though not in his
prudence) a little resemble his lovely Sophia, be easily prevail
on to satisfy Mr Dowling's curiosity, by relate the history of his
birth and education, which he do, like Othello.
------Even from his boyish year,
to th' very moment he be bad to tell:
the which to hear, dowle, like Desdemona, do seriously incline;
he swear 'twa strange, 'twa pass strange;
'twas pitiful, 'twa wonderous pitiful.
Mr Dowling be indeed very greatly affect with this relation; for he
have not divest himself of humanity by be an attorney. indeed,
nothing be more unjust than to carry our prejudice against a
profession into private life, and to borrow our idea of a man from our
opinion of his calling. Habit, it be true, lessen the horror of those
action which the profession make necessary, and consequently
habitual; but in all other instance, Nature work in man of all
profession alike; nay, perhaps, even more strongly with those who
give she, as it be, a holiday, when they be follow their
ordinary business. a butcher, I make no doubt, would feel compunction
at the slaughter of a fine horse; and though a surgeon can feel no
pain in cut off a limb, I have know he compassionate a man in a
fit of the gout. the common hangman, who hath stretch the neck of
hundred, be know to have tremble at his first operation on a head:
and the very professor of human blood-shedding, who, in their trade
of war, butcher thousand, not only of their fellow-professor, but
often of woman and child, without remorse; even these, I say, in
time of peace, when drum and trumpet be lay aside, often lay
aside all their ferocity, and become very gentle member of civil
society. in the same manner an attorney may feel all the misery and
distress of his fellow-creature, provide he happen not to be
concerned against they.
Jones, as the reader know, be yet unacquainted with the very black
colour in which he have be represent to Mr Allworthy; and as to
other matter, he do not shew they in the most disadvantageous light;
for though he be unwilling to cast any blame on his former friend and
patron; yet he be not very desirous of heap too much upon himself.
dowle therefore observe, and not without reason, that very ill
office must have be do he by somebody: "for certainly," cry
he, "the squire would never have disinherit you only for a few
fault, which any young gentleman might have commit. indeed, I
cannot properly say disinherit: for to be sure by law you cannot
claim as heir. thatbe certain; that nobody need go to counsel for. yet
when a gentleman have in a manner adopt you thus as his own son, you
might reasonably have expect some very considerable part, if not the
whole; nay, if you have expect the whole, I should not have blame
you: for certainly all man be for get as much as they can, and
they be not to be blame on that account."
"indeed you wrong I," say Jones; "I should have be content with
very little: I never have any view upon Mr Allworthy's fortune; nay, I
believe I may truly say, I never once consider what he could or
might give I. this I solemnly declare, if he have do a prejudice to
his nephew in my favour, I would have undo it again. I have rather
enjoy my own mind than the fortune of another man. what be the poor
pride arise from a magnificent house, a numerous equipage, a
splendid table, and from all the other advantage or appearance of
fortune, compare to the warm, solid content, the swelling
satisfaction, the thrilling transport, and the exulting triumph,
which a good mind enjoy, in the contemplation of a generous,
virtuous, noble, benevolent action? I envy not Blifil in the prospect
of his wealth; nor shall I envy he in the possession of it. I would
not think myself a rascal half an hour, to exchange situation. I
believe, indeed, Mr Blifil suspect I of the view you mention; and
I suppose these suspicion, as they arise from the baseness of his own
heart, so they occasion his baseness to I. but, I thank Heaven, I
know, I feel--I feel my innocence, my friend; and I would not part
with that feeling for the world. for as long as I know I have never
do, nor even design, an injury to any be whatever,

Jupiter urget.

Dulce loquentem._[*]
[*] place I where never summer breeze
Unbinds the glebe, or warm the tree:
where ever-lower cloud appear,
and angry Jove deform th' inclement year.
place I beneath the burn ray,
where roll the rapid car of day;
Love and the nymph shall charm my toil,
the nymph who sweetly speak, and sweetly smile.
MR FRANCIS.

though the Latin be not the only part of this speech which dowle

Chapter xi.
the disaster which befel Jones on his departure for Coventry; with
the sage remark of Partridge.
no road can be plain than that from the place where they now be to
Coventry; and though neither Jones, nor Partridge, nor the guide, have
ever travel it before, it would have be almost impossible to have
miss their way, have it not be for the two reason mention in the
conclusion of the last chapter.
these two circumstance, however, happen both unfortunately to
intervene, our traveller deviate into a much less frequent track;
and after rid full six mile, instead of arrive at the stately
spire of Coventry, they find themselves still in a very dirty lane,
where they see no symptom of approach the suburb of a large city.
Jones now declare that they must certainly have lose their way; but
this the guide insist upon be impossible; a word which, in common
conversation, be often use to signify not only improbable, but often
what be really very likely, and, sometimes, what hath certainly
happen; an hyperbolical violence like that which be so frequently
offer to the word infinite and eternal; by the former of which it
be usual to express a distance of half a yard, and by the latter, a
duration of five minute. and thus it be as usual to assert the
impossibility of lose what be already actually lose. this be, in
fact, the case at present; for, notwithstanding all the confident
assertion of the lad to the contrary, it be certain they be no more
in the right road to Coventry, than the fraudulent, griping, cruel,
canting miser be in the right road to heaven.
it be not, perhaps, easy for a reader, who hath never be in those
circumstance, to imagine the horror with which darkness, rain, and
wind, fill person who have lose their way in the night; and who,
consequently, have not the pleasant prospect of warm fire, dry
cloath, and other refreshment, to support their mind in struggle
with the inclemency of the weather. a very imperfect idea of this
horror will, however, serve sufficiently to account for the conceit
which now fill the head of Partridge, and which we shall presently
be oblige to open.
Jones grow more and more positive that they be out of their road;
and the boy himself at last acknowledge he believe they be not in
the right road to Coventry; though he affirm, at the same time, it
be impossible they should have mist the way. but Partridge be of a
different opinion. he say, "when they first set out he imagine some
mischief or other would happen.--did not you observe, sir," say he to
Jones, "that old woman who stand at the door just as you be take
horse? I wish you have give she a small matter, with all my heart; for
she say then you might repent it; and at that very instant it begin
to rain, and the wind hath continue rise ever since. whatever some
people may think, I be very certain it be in the power of witch to
raise the wind whenever they please. I have see it happen very often
in my time: and if ever I see a witch in all my life, that old woman
be certainly one. I think so to myself at that very time; and if I
have have any halfpence in my pocket, I would have give she some; for
to be sure it be always good to be charitable to those sort of people,
for fear what may happen; and many a person hath lose his cattle by
save a halfpenny."
Jones, though he be horridly vex at the delay which this mistake
be likely to occasion in his journey, could not help smile at the
superstition of his friend, whom an accident now greatly confirm in
his opinion. this be a tumble from his horse; by which, however, he
receive no other injury than what the dirt confer on his cloath.
Partridge have no soon recover his leg, than he appeal to his
fall, as conclusive evidence of all he have assert; but Jones finding
he be unhurt, answer with a smile: "this witch of your, Partridge,
be a most ungrateful jade, and doth not, I find, distinguish she
friend from other in her resentment. if the old lady have be angry
with I for neglect she, I donot see why she should tumble you from
your horse, after all the respect you have express for she."
"it be ill jesting," cry Partridge, "with people who have power to
do these thing; for they be often very malicious. I remember a
farrier, who provoke one of they, by ask she when the time she have
bargain with the devil for would be out; and within three month
from that very day one of his good cow be drown. nor be she
satisfied with that; for a little time afterwards he lose a barrel of
well-drink: for the old witch pull out the spigot, and let it run
all over the cellar, the very first evening he have tap it to make
merry with some of his neighbour. in short, nothing ever thrive with
he afterwards; for she worry the poor man so, that he take to
drink; and in a year or two his stock be seize, and he and his
family be now come to the parish."
the guide, and perhaps his horse too, be both so attentive to this
discourse, that, either through want of care, or by the malice of the
witch, they be now both sprawl in the dirt.
Partridge entirely impute this fall, as he have do his own, to the
same cause. he tell Mr Jones, "it would certainly be his turn next;
and earnestly entreat he to return back, and find out the old
woman, and pacify she. we shall very soon," add he, "reach the inn;
for though we have seem to go forward, I be very certain we be in
the identical place in which we be an hour ago; and I dare swear, if
it be daylight, we might now see the inn we set out from."
instead of return any answer to this sage advice, Jones be
entirely attentive to what have happen to the boy, who receive no
other hurt than what have before befall Partridge, and which his
cloath very easily bore, as they have be for many year inure to
the like. he soon regain his side-saddle, and by the hearty curse
and blow which he bestow on his horse, quickly satisfy Mr Jones
that no harm be do.
Chapter xii.
relate that Mr Jones continue his journey, contrary to the advice of
Partridge, with what happen on that occasion.
they now discover a light at some distance, to the great pleasure of
Jones, and to the no small terror of Partridge, who firmly believe
himself to be bewitch, and that this light be a Jack-with-a-lantern,
or somewhat more mischievous.
but how be these fear increase, when, as they approach near to
this light (or light as they now appear), they hear a confused
sound of human voice; of singing, laughing, and hallowing, together
with a strange noise that seem to proceed from some instrument; but
could hardly be allow the name of music! indeed, to favour a little
the opinion of Partridge, it might very well be call music
bewitch.
it be impossible to conceive a much great degree of horror than what
now seize on Partridge; the contagion of which have reach the
post-boy, who have be very attentive to many thing that the other
have utter. he now, therefore, join in petition Jones to return;
say he firmly believe what Partridge have just before say, that
though the horse seem to go on, they have not move a step forwards
during at least the last half-hour.
Jones could not help smile in the midst of his vexation, at the
fear of these poor fellow. "either we advance," say he, "towards
the light, or the light have advance towards we; for we be now at
a very little distance from they; but how can either of you be afraid
of a set of people who appear only to be merry-making?"
"merry-making, sir!" cry Partridge; "who could be merry-making at
this time of night, and in such a place, and such weather? they can be
nothing but ghost or witch, or some evil spirit or other, thatbe
certain."
"let they be what they will," cry Jones, "I be resolve to go up to
they, and enquire the way to Coventry. all witch, Partridge, be not
such ill-natured hag as that we have the misfortune to meet with
last."
"o Lord, sir," cry Partridge, "there be no know what humour they
will be in; to be sure it be always good to be civil to they; but what
if we should meet with something bad than witch, with evil spirit
themselves?----pray, sir, be advise; pray, sir, do. if you have read
so many terrible account as I have of these matter, you would not be
so fool-hardy.----the Lord know whither we have get already, or
whither we be go; for sure such darkness be never see upon
earth, and I question whether it can be dark in the other world."
Jones put forwards as fast as he could, notwithstanding all these
hint and caution, and poor Partridge be oblige to follow; for
though he hardly dare to advance, he dare still less to stay behind
by himself.
at length they arrive at the place whence the light and different
noise have issue. this Jones perceive to be no other than a barn,
where a great number of man and woman be assemble, and divert
themselves with much apparent jollity.
Jones no soon appear before the great door of the barn, which
be open, than a masculine and very rough voice from within demand,
who be there?--to which Jones gently answer, a friend; and
immediately ask the road to Coventry.
"if you be a friend," cry another of the man in the barn, "you have
well alight till the storm be over" (for indeed it be now more
violent than ever;) "you be very welcome to put up your horse; for
there be sufficient room for he at the end of the barn."
"you be very obliging," return Jones; "and I will accept your offer
for a few minute, whilst the rain continue; and here be two more
who will be glad of the same favour." this be accord with more
good-will than it be accept: for Partridge would rather have
submit to the utmost inclemency of the weather than have trust to
the clemency of those whom he take for hobgoblin; and the poor
post-boy be now infect with the same apprehension; but they be
both oblige to follow the example of Jones; the one because he durst
not leave his horse, and the other because he fear nothing so much
as be leave by himself.
have this history be writ in the day of superstition, I should have
have too much compassion for the reader to have leave he so long in
suspense, whether Beelzebub or Satan be about actually to appear in
person, with all his hellish retinue; but as these doctrine be at
present very unfortunate, and have but few, if any believer, I have
not be much aware of convey any such terror. to say truth, the
whole furniture of the infernal region hath long be appropriate by
the manager of playhouse, who seem lately to have lay they by as
rubbish, capable only of affect the upper gallery; a place in which
few of our reader ever sit.
however, though we do not suspect raise any great terror on this
occasion, we have reason to fear some other apprehension may here
arise in our reader, into which we would not willingly betray he; I
mean that we be go to take a voyage into fairy-land, and introduce
a set of being into our history, which scarce any one be ever
childish enough to believe, though many have be foolish enough to
spend their time in writing and read their adventure.
to prevent, therefore, any such suspicion, so prejudicial to the
credit of an historian, who profess to draw his material from
nature only, we shall now proceed to acquaint the reader who these
people be, whose sudden appearance have strike such terror into
Partridge, have more than half frighten the post-boy, and have a
little surprized even Mr Jones himself.
the people then assemble in this barn be no other than a company of
Egyptians, or, as they be vulgarly call, gypsy, and they be now
celebrate the wedding of one of their society.
it be impossible to conceive a happy set of people than appear
here to be meet together. the utmost mirth, indeed, shew itself in
every countenance; nor be their ball totally void of all order and
decorum. perhaps it have more than a country assembly be sometimes
conduct with: for these people be subject to a formal government
and law of their own, and all pay obedience to one great magistrate,
whom they call their king.
Greater plenty, likewise, be nowhere to be see than what flourish
in this barn. here be indeed no nicety nor elegance, nor do the keen
appetite of the guest require any. here be good store of bacon,
fowl, and mutton, to which every one present provide well sauce
himself than the good and dear french cook can prepare.
Aeneas be not describe under more consternation in the temple of
Juno,
_Dum stupet obtutuque haeret defixus in uno_,
than be our heroe at what he see in this barn. while he be look
everywhere round he with astonishment, a venerable person approach
he with many friendly salutation, rather of too hearty a kind to be
call courtly. this be no other than the king of the gypsy
himself. he be very little distinguished in dress from his subject,
nor have he any regalia of majesty to support his dignity; and yet
there seem (as Mr Jones say) to be somewhat in his air which
denote authority, and inspire the beholder with an idea of awe and
respect; though all this be perhaps imaginary in Jones; and the truth
may be, that such idea be incident to power, and almost inseparable
from it.
there be somewhat in the open countenance and courteous behaviour of
Jones which, be accompany with much comeliness of person, greatly
recommend he at first sight to every beholder. these be, perhaps,
a little heighten in the present instance, by that profound respect
which he pay to the king of the gypsy, the moment he be acquaint
with his dignity, and which be the sweet to his gypseian majesty,
as he be not use to receive such homage from any but his own
subject.
the king order a table to be spread with the choicest of their
provision for his accommodation; and, have place himself at his
right hand, his majesty begin to discourse with our heroe in the
follow manner:--
"I doubt not, sir, but you have often see some of my people, who be
what you call de party detache: for dey go about everywhere; but I
fancy you imagine not we be so considrable body as we be; and may be
you will be surprize more when you hear de gypsy be as orderly and
well govern people as any upon face of de earth.
"I have honour, as I say, to be deir king, and no monarch can do
boast of more dutiful subject, ne no more affectionate. how far I
deserve deir good-will, I no say; but dis I can say, dat I never
design anyte but to do dem good. I sall no do boast of dat neider:
for what can I do oderwise dan consider of de good of dose poor
people who go about all day to give I always de good of what dey get.
Dey love and honour I darefore, because I do love and take care of
dem; dat be all, I know no oder reason.
"about a tousand or two tousand year ago, I cannot tell to a year or
two, as can neider write nor read, dere be a great what you call--a
volution among de gypsy; for dere be de lord gypsy in dose day; and
dese lord do quarrel vid one anoder about de place; but de king of de
gypsy do demolish dem all, and make all his subject equal vid each
oder; and since dat time dey have agree very well; for dey no tink of
be king, and may be it be well for dem as dey be; for I assure
you it be ver troublesome ting to be king, and always to do justice;
I have often wish to be de private gypsy when I have be force to
punish my dear friend and relation; for dough we never put to death,
our punishment be ver severe. Dey make de gypsy ashamed of demselve,
and dat be ver terrible punishment; I ave scarce ever know de gypsy
so punish do harm any more."
the king then proceed to express some wonder that there be no such
punishment as shame in other government. upon which Jones assure he
to the contrary; for that there be many crime for which shame be
inflict by the english law, and that it be indeed one consequence
of all punishment. "Dat be ver strange," say the king; "for I know
and hear good deal of your people, dough I no live among dem; and I
have often hear dat sham be de consequence and de cause too of many of
your reward. be your reward and punishment den de same ting?"
while his majesty be thus discourse with Jones, a sudden uproar
arise in the barn, and as it seem upon this occasion:--the courtesy
of these people have by degree remove all the apprehension of
Partridge, and he be prevail upon not only to stuff himself with
their food, but to taste some of their liquor, which by degree
entirely expel all fear from his composition, and in its stead
introduce much more agreeable sensation.
a young female gypsy, more remarkable for her wit than her beauty, have
decoy the honest fellow aside, pretend to tell his fortune. now,
when they be alone together in a remote part of the barn, whether it
proceed from the strong liquor, which be never so apt to inflame
inordinate desire as after moderate fatigue; or whether the fair gypsy
herself throw aside the delicacy and decency of her sex, and tempt
the youth Partridge with express solicitation; but they be
discover in a very improper manner by the husband of the gypsy, who,
from jealousy it seem, have keep a watchful eye over his wife, and have
dog she to the place, where he find she in the arm of her
gallant.
to the great confusion of Jones, Partridge be now hurry before the
king; who hear the accusation, and likewise the culprit's defence,
which be indeed very trifling; for the poor fellow be confound by
the plain evidence which appear against he, and have very little to
say for himself. his majesty, then turn towards Jones, say, "Sir,
you have hear what dey say; what punishment do you tink your man
deserve?"
Jones answer, "he be sorry for what have happen, and that
Partridge should make the husband all the amend in his power: he
say, he have very little money about he at that time;" and, put
his hand into his pocket, offer the fellow a guinea. to which he
immediately answer, "he hop his honour would not think of give
he less than five."
this sum, after some altercation, be reduce to two; and Jones,
have stipulate for the full forgiveness of both Partridge and the
wife, be go to pay the money; when his majesty, restrain his
hand, turn to the witness and ask he, "at what time he have
discover the criminal?" to which he answer, "that he have be
desire by the husband to watch the motion of his wife from her first
speak to the stranger, and that he have never lose sight of she
afterwards till the crime have be commit." the king then ask,
"if the husband be with he all that time in his lurking-place?" to
which he answer in the affirmative. his egyptian majesty then
address himself to the husband as follow: "I be sorry to see any
gypsy dat have no more honour dan to sell de honour of his wife for
money. if you have de love for your wife, you would have prevent dis
matter, and not endeavour to make she de whore dat you might discover
she. I do order dat you have no money give you, for you deserve
punishment, not reward; I do order derefore, dat you be de infamous
gypsy, and do wear pair of horn upon your forehead for one month, and
dat your wife be call de whore, and point at all dat time; for you
be de infamous gypsy, but she be no less de infamous whore."
the gypsy immediately proceed to execute the sentence, and leave
Jones and Partridge alone with his majesty.
Jones greatly applaud the justice of the sentence: upon which the
king, turn to he, say, "I believe you be surprize: for I
suppose you have ver bad opinion of my people; I suppose you tink we
all de tieve."
"I must confess, sir," say Jones, "I have not hear so favourable an
account of they as they seem to deserve."
"I vil tell you," say the king, "how the difference be between you
and we. my people rob your people, and your people rob one anoder."
Jones afterwards proceed very gravely to sing forth the happiness of
those subject who live under such a magistrate.
indeed their happiness appear to have be so compleat, that we be
aware lest some advocate for arbitrary power should hereafter quote
the case of those people, as an instance of the great advantage which
attend that government above all other.
and here we will make a concession, which would not perhaps have be
expect from we, that no limited form of government be capable of
rise to the same degree of perfection, or of produce the same
benefit to society, with this. mankind have never be so happy, as
when the great part of the then know world be under the dominion
of a single master; and this state of their felicity continue during
the reign of five successive prince.[*] this be the true aera of
the golden age, and the only golden age which ever have any existence,
unless in the warm imagination of the poet, from the expulsion from
Eden down to this day.
[*] Nerva, Trajan, Adrian, and the two Antonini.
in reality, I know but of one solid objection to absolute monarchy.
the only defect in which excellent constitution seem to be, the
difficulty of find any man adequate to the office of an absolute
monarch: for this indispensably require three quality very
difficult, as it appear from history, to be find in princely
nature: first, a sufficient quantity of moderation in the prince, to
be content with all the power which be possible for he to have.
2ndly, enough of wisdom to know his own happiness. and, 3rdly,
goodness sufficient to support the happiness of other, when not only
compatible with, but instrumental to his own.
now if an absolute monarch, with all these great and rare
qualification, should be allow capable of confer the great
good on society; it must be surely grant, on the contrary, that
absolute power, vest in the hand of one who be deficient in they
all, be likely to be attend with no less a degree of evil.
in short, our own religion furnish we with adequate idea of the
blessing, as well as curse, which may attend absolute power. the
picture of heaven and of hell will place a very lively image of both
before our eye; for though the prince of the latter can have no power
but what he originally derive from the omnipotent Sovereign in the
former, yet it plainly appear from scripture that absolute power in
his infernal dominion be grant to their diabolical ruler. this be
indeed the only absolute power which can by scripture be derive from
heaven. if, therefore, the several tyranny upon earth can prove any
title to a Divine authority, it must be derive from this original
grant to the prince of darkness; and these subordinate deputation
must consequently come immediately from he whose stamp they so
expressly bear.
to conclude, as the example of all age shew we that mankind in
general desire power only to do harm, and, when they obtain it, use it
for no other purpose; it be not consonant with even the least degree
of prudence to hazard an alteration, where our hope be poorly keep
in countenance by only two or three exception out of a thousand
instance to alarm our fear. in this case it will be much wise to
submit to a few inconveniencie arise from the dispassionate
deafness of law, than to remedy they by apply to the passionate
open ear of a tyrant.
nor can the example of the gypsy, though possibly they may have long
be happy under this form of government, be here urge; since we must
remember the very material respect in which they differ from all other
people, and to which perhaps this their happiness be entirely owing,
namely, that they have no false honours among they, and that they look
on shame as the most grievous punishment in the world.
Chapter xiii.
a dialogue between Jones and Partridge.
the honest lover of liberty will, we doubt not, pardon that long
digression into which we be lead at the close of the last chapter, to
prevent our history from be apply to the use of the most
pernicious doctrine which priestcraft have ever the wickedness or the
impudence to preach.
we will now proceed with Mr Jones, who, when the storm be over, take
leave of his egyptian majesty, after many thank for his courteous
behaviour and kind entertainment, and set out for Coventry; to which
place (for it be still dark) a gypsy be order to conduct he.
Jones have, by reason of his deviation, travel eleven mile
instead of six, and most of those through very execrable road, where
no expedition could have be make in quest of a midwife, do not
arrive at Coventry till near twelve. nor could he possibly get again
into the saddle till past two; for post-horse be now not easy to
get; nor be the hostler or post-boy in half so great a hurry as
himself, but choose rather to imitate the tranquil disposition of
Partridge; who, be deny the nourishment of sleep, take all
opportunity to supply its place with every other kind of
nourishment, and be never well pleased than when he arrive at an
inn, nor ever more dissatisfied than when he be again force to leave
it.
Jones now travel post; we will follow he, therefore, accord to
our custom, and to the rule of Longinus, in the same manner. from
Coventry he arrive at Daventry, from Daventry at Stratford, and from
Stratford at Dunstable, whither he come the next day a little after
noon, and within a few hour after Sophia have leave it; and though he
be oblige to stay here long than he wish, while a smith, with
great deliberation, shoe the post-horse he be to ride, he doubt
not but to overtake his Sophia before she should set out from St
Albans; at which place he conclude, and very reasonably, that his
lordship would stop and dine.
and have he be right in this conjecture, he most probably would have
overtake his angel at the aforesaid place; but unluckily my lord have
appoint a dinner to be prepare for he at his own house in London,
and, in order to enable he to reach that place in proper time, he have
order a relay of horse to meet he at St Albans. when Jones
therefore arrive there, he be inform that the coach-and-six have
set out two hour before.
if fresh post-horse have be now ready, as they be not, it seem
so apparently impossible to overtake the coach before it reach
London, that Partridge think he have now a proper opportunity to
remind his friend of a matter which he seem entirely to have
forget; what this be the reader will guess, when we inform he
that Jones have eat nothing more than one poached egg since he have leave
the alehouse where he have first meet the guide return from Sophia;
for with the gypsy he have feast only his understanding.
the landlord so entirely agree with the opinion of Mr Partridge, that
he no soon hear the latter desire his friend to stay and dine, than
he very readily put in his word, and retract his promise before
give of furnish the horse immediately, he assure Mr Jones he
would lose no time in bespeak a dinner, which, he say, could be
get ready soon than it be possible to get the horse up from grass,
and to prepare they for their journey by a feed of corn.
Jones be at length prevail on, chiefly by the latter argument of
the landlord; and now a joint of mutton be put down to the fire.
while this be prepare, Partridge, be admit into the same
apartment with his friend or master, begin to harangue in the
follow manner.
"certainly, sir, if ever man deserve a young lady, you deserve young
Madam Western; for what a vast quantity of love must a man have, to be
able to live upon it without any other food, as you do? I be positive
I have eat thirty time as much within these last twenty-four hour as
your honour, and yet I be almost famish; for nothing make a man so
hungry as travel, especially in this cold raw weather. and yet I
cannot tell how it be, but your honour be seemingly in perfect good
health, and you never look well nor fresher in your life. it must
be certainly love that you live upon."
"and a very rich diet too, Partridge," answer Jones. "but do not
fortune send I an excellent dainty yesterday? dost thou imagine I
cannot live more than twenty-four hour on this dear pocket-book?"
"undoubtedly," cry Partridge, "there be enough in that pocket-book
to purchase many a good meal. Fortune send it to your honour very
opportunely for present use, as your honour's money must be almost out
by this time."
"what do you mean?" answer Jones; "I hope you donot imagine that I
should be dishonest enough, even if it belong to any other person,
besides Miss western----"
"dishonest!" reply Partridge, "heaven forbid I should wrong your
honour so much! but wherebe the dishonesty in borrow a little for
present spending, since you will be so well able to pay the lady
hereafter? no, indeed, I would have your honour pay it again, as soon
as it be convenient, by all mean; but where can be the harm in make
use of it now you want it? indeed, if it belong to a poor body, it
would be another thing; but so great a lady, to be sure, can never
want it, especially now as she be along with a lord, who, it cannot be
doubt, will let she have whatever she hath need of. besides, if she
should want a little, she cannot want the whole, therefore I would give
she a little; but I would be hang before I mention the having
find it at first, and before I get some money of my own; for London,
I have hear, be the very bad of place to be in without money.
indeed, if I have not know to whom it belong, I might have think
it be the devil's money, and have be afraid to use it; but as you
know otherwise, and come honestly by it, it would be an affront to
fortune to part with it all again, at the very time when you want it
most; you can hardly expect she should ever do you such another good
turn; for _fortuna nunquam perpetuo est bona_. you will do as you
please, notwithstanding all I say; but for my part, I would be hang
before I mention a word of the matter."
"by what I can see, Partridge," cry Jones, "hang be a matter _non
longe alienum a Scaevolae studiis_." "you should say _alienus_," say
Partridge,--"I remember the passage; it be an example under _communis,
alienus, immunis, variis casibus serviunt_." "if you do remember it,"
cry Jones, "I find you donot understand it; but I tell thee, friend,
in plain English, that he who find another's property, and wilfully
detain it from the know owner, deserve, _in foro conscientiae_, to
be hang, no less than if he have steal it. and as for this very
identical bill, which be the property of my angel, and be once in her
dear possession, I will not deliver it into any hand but her own,
upon any consideration whatever, no, though I be as hungry as thou
art, and have no other mean to satisfy my craving appetite; this I
hope to do before I sleep; but if it should happen otherwise, I charge
thee, if thou would'st not incur my displeasure for ever, not to shock
I any more by the bare mention of such detestable baseness."
"I should not have mention it now," cry Partridge, "if it have
appear so to I; for Ibe sure I scorn any wickedness as much as
another; but perhaps you know well; and yet I might have imagine
that I should not have live so many year, and have teach school so
long, without be able to distinguish between _fas et nefas_; but it
seem we be all to live and learn. I remember my old schoolmaster,
who be a prodigious great scholar, use often to say, _polly matete
cry town be my daskalon_. the English of which, he tell we, be, that
a child may sometimes teach his grandmother to suck egg. I have live
to a fine purpose, truly, if I be to be teach my grammar at this time
of day. perhaps, young gentleman, you may change your opinion, if you
live to my year: for I remember I think myself as wise when I be a
stripling of one or two and twenty as I be now. I be sure I always
teach _alienus_, and my master read it so before I."
there be not many instance in which Partridge could provoke Jones,
nor be there many in which Partridge himself could have be hurry
out of his respect. unluckily, however, they have both hit on one of
these. we have already see Partridge could not bear to have his
learning attack, nor could Jones bear some passage or other in the
foregoing speech. and now, look upon his companion with a
contemptuous and disdainful air (a thing not usual with he), he
cry, "Partridge, I see thou art a conceited old fool, and I wish
thou art not likewise an old rogue. indeed, if I be as well convince
of the latter as I be of the former, thou should'st travel no far
in my company."
the sage pedagogue be content with the vent which he have already
give to his indignation; and, as the vulgar phrase be, immediately
draw in his horn. he say, he be sorry he have utter anything which
might give offence, for that he have never intend it; but _Nemo
omnibus horis sapit_.
as Jones have the vice of a warm disposition, he be entirely free
from those of a cold one; and if his friend must have confest his
temper to have be a little too easily ruffle, his enemy must at
the same time have conf, that it as soon subside; nor do it at
all resemble the sea, whose swelling be more violent and dangerous
after a storm be over than while the storm itself subsist. he
instantly accept the submission of Partridge, shake he by the hand,
and with the most benign aspect imaginable, say twenty kind thing,
and at the same time very severely condemn himself, though not half
so severely as he will most probably be condemn by many of our good
reader.
Partridge be now highly comfort, as his fear of have offend
be at once abolish, and his pride completely satisfied by Jones
have own himself in the wrong, which submission he instantly
apply to what have principally nettle he, and repeat in a
muttering voice, "to be sure, sir, your knowledge may be superior to
mine in some thing; but as to the grammar, I think I may challenge
any man live. I think, at least, I have that at my finger's end."
if anything could add to the satisfaction which the poor man now
enjoy, he receive this addition by the arrival of an excellent
shoulder of mutton, that at this instant come smoake to the table.
on which, have both plentifully feast, they again mount their
horse, and set forward for London.
Chapter xiv.
what happen to Mr Jones in his journey from St Albans.
they be get about two mile beyond Barnet, and it be now the dusk
of the evening, when a genteel-look man, but upon a very shabby
horse, ride up to Jones, and ask he whether he be go to London;
to which Jones answer in the affirmative. the gentleman reply, "I
should be oblige to you, sir, if you will accept of my company; for
it be very late, and I be a stranger to the road." Jones readily
comply with the request; and on they travel together, hold
that sort of discourse which be usual on such occasion.
of this, indeed, robbery be the principal topic: upon which subject
the stranger express great apprehension; but Jones declare he have
very little to lose, and consequently as little to fear. here
Partridge could not forbear put in his word. "your honour," say
he, "may think it a little, but I be sure, if I have a hundred-pound
bank-note in my pocket, as you have, I should be very sorry to lose
it; but, for my part, I never be less afraid in my life; for we be
four of we, and if we all stand by one another, the good man in
England cannot rob we. suppose he should have a pistol, he can kill but
one of we, and a man can die but once.--thatbe my comfort, a man can
die but once."
besides the reliance on superior number, a kind of valour which hath
raise a certain nation among the modern to a high pitch of glory,
there be another reason for the extraordinary courage which Partridge
now discover; for he have at present as much of that quality as be
in the power of liquor to bestow.
our company be now arrive within a mile of Highgate, when the
stranger turn short upon Jones, and pull out a pistol, demand
that little bank-note which Partridge have mention.
Jones be at first somewhat shocked at this unexpected demand;
however, he presently recollect himself, and tell the highwayman,
all the money he have in his pocket be entirely at his service; and so
say, he pull out upwards of three guinea, and offer to deliver
it; but the other answer with an oath, that would not do. Jones
answer coolly, he be very sorry for it, and return the money into
his pocket.
the highwayman then threaten, if he do not deliver the bank-note
that moment, he must shoot he; hold his pistol at the same time
very near to his breast. Jones instantly catch hold of the fellow's
hand, which tremble so that he could scarce hold the pistol in it,
and turn the muzzle from he. a struggle then ensue, in which the
former wrest the pistol from the hand of his antagonist, and both
come from their horse on the ground together, the highwayman upon his
back, and the victorious Jones upon he.
the poor fellow now begin to implore mercy of the conqueror: for, to
say the truth, he be in strength by no mean a match for Jones.
"indeed, sir," say he, "I could have have no intention to shoot you;
for you will find the pistol be not load. this be the first robbery
I ever attempt, and I have be drive by distress to this."
at this instant, at about a hundred and fifty yard' distance, lay
another person on the ground, roar for mercy in a much loud voice
than the highwayman. this be no other than Partridge himself, who,
endeavour to make his escape from the engagement, have be throw
from his horse, and lie flat on his face, not dare to look up, and
expect every minute to be shoot.
in this posture he lay, till the guide, who be no otherwise concern
than for his horse, have secure the stumble beast, come up to
he, and tell he his master have get the well of the highwayman.
Partridge leapt up at this news, and run back to the place where Jones
stand with his sword draw in his hand to guard the poor fellow; which
Partridge no soon see than he cry out, "kill the villain, sir, run
he through the body, kill he this instant!"
luckily, however, for the poor wretch, he have fall into more
merciful hand; for Jones have examine the pistol, and find it to
be really unload, begin to believe all the man have tell he, before
Partridge come up: namely, that he be a novice in the trade, and that
he have be drive to it by the distress he mention, the great
indeed imaginable, that of five hungry child, and a wife lie in
of the sixth, in the utmost want and misery. the truth of all which
the highwayman most vehemently assert, and offer to convince Mr
Jones of it, if he would take the trouble to go to his house, which
be not above two mile off; say, "that he desire no favour, but
upon condition of prove all he have all alledge."
Jones at first pretend that he would take the fellow at his word,
and go with he, declare that his fate should depend entirely on the
truth of his story. upon this the poor fellow immediately express so
much alacrity, that Jones be perfectly satisfied with his veracity,
and begin now to entertain sentiment of compassion for he. he
return the fellow his empty pistol, advise he to think of honester
mean of relieve his distress, and give he a couple of guineas for
the immediate support of his wife and his family; add, "he wish
he have more for his sake, for the hundred pound that have be
mention be not his own."
our reader will probably be divide in their opinion concern this
action; some may applaud it perhaps as an act of extraordinary
humanity, while those of a more saturnine temper will consider it as a
want of regard to that justice which every man owe his country.
Partridge certainly see it in that light; for he testify much
dissatisfaction on the occasion, quote an old proverb, and say, he
should not wonder if the rogue attack they again before they reach
London.
the highwayman be full of expression of thankfulness and gratitude.
he actually dropt tear, or pretend so to do. he vow he would
immediately return home, and would never afterwards commit such a
transgression: whether he keep his word or no, perhaps may appear
hereafter.
our traveller have remount their horse, arrive in town without
encounter any new mishap. on the road much pleasant discourse
pass between Jones and Partridge, on the subject of their last
adventure: in which Jones expr a great compassion for those
highwayman who be, by unavoidable distress, drive, as it be, to
such illegal course, as generally bring they to a shameful death: "I
mean," say he, "those only whose high guilt extend no far
than to robbery, and who be never guilty of cruelty nor insult to any
person, which be a circumstance that, I must say, to the honour of our
country, distinguish the robber of England from those of all other
nation; for murder be, amongst those, almost inseparably incident to
robbery."
"no doubt," answer Partridge, "it be well to take away one's money
than one's life; and yet it be very hard upon honest man, that they
cannot travel about their business without be in danger of these
villain. and to be sure it would be well that all rogue be
hang out of the way, than that one honest man should suffer. for my
own part, indeed, I should not care to have the blood of any of they
on my own hand; but it be very proper for the law to hang they all.
what right hath any man to take sixpence from I, unless I give it
he? be there any honesty in such a man?"
"no, surely," cry Jones, "no more than there be in he who take the
horse out of another man's stable, or who apply to his own use the
money which he find, when he know the right owner."
these hint stopt the mouth of Partridge; nor do he open it again
till Jones, have throw some sarcastical joke on his cowardice, he
offer to excuse himself on the inequality of fire-arm, say, "a
thousand naked man be nothing to one pistol; for though it be true it
will kill but one at a single discharge, yet who can tell but that one
may be himself?"
BOOK XIII.
contain the SPACE of TWELVE day.
Chapter i.
An Invocation.
come, bright love of fame, inspire my glowing breast: not thee I will
call, who, over swell tide of blood and tear, dost bear the heroe
on to glory, while sigh of million waft his spread sail; but
thee, fair, gentle maid, whom Mnesis, happy nymph, first on the bank
of Hebrus do produce. Thee, whom Maeonia educate, whom Mantua
charm, and who, on that fair hill which overlook the proud
metropolis of Britain, sat'st, with thy Milton, sweetly tune the
heroic lyre; fill my ravished fancy with the hope of charming age
yet to come. foretel I that some tender maid, whose grandmother be
yet unborn, hereafter, when, under the fictitious name of Sophia, she
read the real worth which once exist in my Charlotte, shall from
her sympathetic breast send forth the heaving sigh. do thou teach I
not only to foresee, but to enjoy, nay, even to feed on future praise.
comfort I by a solemn assurance, that when the little parlour in
which I sit at this instant shall be reduce to a bad furnish box,
I shall be read with honour by those who never know nor see I, and
whom I shall neither know nor see.
and thou, much plumper dame, whom no airy form nor phantom of
imagination cloathe; whom the well-season beef, and pudde richly
stain with plum, delight: thee I call: of whom in a treckschuyte,
in some dutch canal, the fat ufrow gelt, impregnate by a jolly
merchant of Amsterdam, be deliver: in Grub-street school didst thou
suck in the element of thy erudition. here hast thou, in thy maturer
age, teach poetry to tickle not the fancy, but the pride of the
patron. comedy from thee learn a grave and solemn air; while tragedy
storm aloud, and rend th' affrighted theatre with its thunder. to
soothe thy weary limb in slumber, Alderman History tell his
tedious tale; and, again, to awaken thee, Monsieur Romance perform
his surprize trick of dexterity. nor less thy well-feed bookseller
obey thy influence. by thy advice the heavy, unread, folio lump,
which long have doze on the dusty shelf, piecemeale into number,
run nimbly through the nation. instruct by thee, some book, like
quack, impose on the world by promise wonder; while other turn
beau, and trust all their merit to a gild outside. come, thou
jolly substance, with thy shin face, keep back thy inspiration, but
hold forth thy tempting reward; thy shin, chink heap; thy
quickly convertible bank-bill, big with unseen rich; thy
often-vary stock; the warm, the comfortable house; and, lastly, a
fair portion of that bounteous mother, whose flow breast yield
redundant sustenance for all her numerous offspring, do not some too
greedily and wantonly drive their brother from the teat. come thou,
and if I be too tasteless of thy valuable treasure, warm my heart
with the transporting thought of convey they to other. tell I,
that through thy bounty, the pratling babe, whose innocent play hath
often be interrupt by my labour, may one time be amply reward
for they.
and now, this ill-yoked pair, this lean shadow and this fat substance,
have prompt I to write, whose assistance shall I invoke to direct
my pen?
first, Genius; thou gift of Heaven; without whose aid in vain we
struggle against the stream of nature. Thou who dost sow the generous
seed which art nourishe, and bring to perfection. do thou kindly
take I by the hand, and lead I through all the maze, the winding
labyrinth of nature. initiate I into all those mystery which
profane eye never behold. teach I, which to thee be no difficult
task, to know mankind well than they know themselves. remove that
mist which dim the intellect of mortal, and cause they to adore
man for their art, or to detest they for their cunning, in deceive
other, when they be, in reality, the object only of ridicule, for
deceive themselves. strip off the thin disguise of wisdom from
self-conceit, of plenty from avarice, and of glory from ambition.
come, thou that hast inspire thy Aristophanes, thy Lucian, thy
Cervantes, thy Rabelais, thy Molière, thy Shakespear, thy Swift, thy
Marivaux, fill my page with humour; till mankind learn the
good-nature to laugh only at the folly of other, and the humility
to grieve at their own.
and thou, almost the constant attendant on true genius, Humanity,
bring all thy tender sensation. if thou hast already dispose of they
all between thy Allen and thy Lyttleton, steal they a little while
from their bosom. not without these the tender scene be paint. from
these alone proceed the noble, disinterested friendship, the melting
love, the generous sentiment, the ardent gratitude, the soft
compassion, the candid opinion; and all those strong energy of a
good mind, which fill the moistened eye with tear, the glowing
cheek with blood, and swell the heart with tide of grief, joy, and
benevolence.
and thou, o Learning! (for without thy assistance nothing pure,
nothing correct, can genius produce) do thou guide my pen. thee in thy
favourite field, where the limpid, gently-roll Thames wash thy
etonian bank, in early youth I have worship. to thee, at thy
birchen altar, with true spartan devotion, I have sacrifice my blood.
come then, and from thy vast, luxuriant store, in long antiquity
pile up, pour forth the rich profusion. open thy Maeonian and thy
Mantuan coffer, with whatever else include thy philosophic, thy
poetic, and thy historical treasure, whether with greek or roman
character thou hast choose to inscribe the ponderous chest: give I
a while that key to all thy treasure, which to thy Warburton thou
hast entrust.
lastly, come experience, long conversant with the wise, the good, the
learn, and the polite. nor with they only, but with every kind of
character, from the minister at his levee, to the bailiff in his
spunge-house; from the dutchess at her drum, to the landlady behind
her bar. from thee only can the manner of mankind be know; to which
the recluse pedant, however great his part or extensive his learning
may be, hath ever be a stranger.
come all these, and more, if possible; for arduous be the task I have
undertake; and, without all your assistance, will, I find, be too
heavy for I to support. but if you all smile on my labour I hope
still to bring they to a happy conclusion.
Chapter ii.
what befel Mr Jones on his arrival in London.
the learn Dr Misaubin use to say, that the proper direction to he
be _to Dr_ Misaubin, _in the World_; intimate that there be few
people in it to whom his great reputation be not know. and, perhaps,
upon a very nice examination into the matter, we shall find that this
circumstance bear no inconsiderable part among the many blessing of
grandeur.
the great happiness of be know to posterity, with the hope of
which we so delight ourselves in the precede chapter, be the
portion of few. to have the several element which compose our name,
as Sydenham express it, repeat a thousand year hence, be a gift
beyond the power of title and wealth; and be scarce to be purchase,
unless by the sword and the pen. but to avoid the scandalous
imputation, while we yet live, of be _one whom nobody know_ (a
scandal, by the bye, as old as the day of Homer[*]) will always be the
envy portion of those, who have a legal title either to honour or
estate.
[*] see the 2d Odyssey, ver. 175.
from that figure, therefore, which the irish peer, who bring Sophia
to town, hath already make in this history, the reader will conclude,
doubtless, it must have be an easy matter to have discover his
house in London without know the particular street or square which
he inhabit, since he must have be one _whom everybody know_. to
say the truth, so it would have be to any of those tradesman who be
accustomed to attend the region of the great; for the door of the
great be generally no less easy to find than it be difficult to get
entrance into they. but Jones, as well as Partridge, be an entire
stranger in London; and as he happen to arrive first in a quarter of
the town, the inhabitant of which have very little intercourse with
the householder of Hanover or Grosvenor-square (for he enter
through Gray's-inn-lane), so he ramble about some time before he
could even find his way to those happy mansion where fortune
segregate from the vulgar those magnanimous hero, the descendant
of antient Britons, Saxons, or Danes, whose ancestor, be bear in
well day, by sundry kind of merit, have entail rich and honour
on their posterity.
Jones, be at length arrive at those terrestrial elysian field,
would now soon have discover his lordship's mansion; but the peer
unluckily quit his former house when he go for Ireland; and as he
be just enter into a new one, the fame of his equipage have not yet
sufficiently blaze in the neighbourhood; so that, after a successless
enquiry till the clock have strike eleven, Jones at last yield to the
advice of Partridge, and retreat to the Bull and Gate in Holborn,
that be the inn where he have first alight, and where he retire
to enjoy that kind of repose which usually attend person in his
circumstance.
early in the morning he again set forth in pursuit of Sophia; and many
a weary step he take to no well purpose than before. at last,
whether it be that Fortune relent, or whether it be no long in
her power to disappoint he, he come into the very street which be
honour by his lordship's residence; and, be direct to the
house, he give one gentle rap at the door.
the porter, who, from the modesty of the knock, have conceive no high
idea of the person approach, conceive but little well from the
appearance of Mr Jones, who be drest in a suit of fustian, and have by
his side the weapon formerly purchase of the serjeant; of which,
though the blade might be compose of well-temper steel, the handle
be compose only of brass, and that none of the bright. when
Jones, therefore, enquire after the young lady who have come to town
with his lordship, this fellow answer surlily, "that there be no
lady there." Jones then desire to see the master of the house; but
be inform that his lordship would see nobody that morning. and upon
grow more press the porter say, "he have positive order to let
no person in; but if you think proper," say he, "to leave your name,
I will acquaint his lordship; and if you call another time you shall
know when he will see you."
Jones now declare, "that he have very particular business with the
young lady, and could not depart without see she." upon which the
porter, with no very agreeable voice or aspect, affirm, "that there
be no young lady in that house, and consequently none could he see;"
add, "sure you be the strange man I ever meet with, for you will
not take an answer."
I have often think that, by the particular description of Cerberus,
the porter of hell, in the 6th Aeneid, Virgil might possibly intend to
satirize the porter of the great man in his time; the picture, at
least, resemble those who have the honour to attend at the door of
our great man. the porter in his lodge answer exactly to Cerberus in
his den, and, like he, must be appease by a sop before access can be
gain to his master. perhaps Jones might have see he in that light,
and have recollect the passage where the Sibyl, in order to procure
an entrance for Aeneas, present the keeper of the stygian avenue with
such a sop. Jones, in like manner, now begin to offer a bribe to the
human Cerberus, which a footman, overhearing, instantly advanced, and
declare, "if Mr Jones would give he the sum propose, he would
conduct he to the lady." Jones instantly agree, and be forthwith
conduct to the lodging of Mrs Fitzpatrick by the very fellow who have
attend the lady thither the day before.
nothing more aggravate ill success than the near approach to good.
the gamester, who lose his party at piquet by a single point, lament
his bad luck ten time as much as he who never come within a prospect
of the game. so in a lottery, the proprietor of the next number to
that which win the great prize be apt to account themselves much
more unfortunate than their fellow-sufferer. in short, these kind of
hairbreadth missing of happiness look like the insult of Fortune,
who may be consider as thus play trick with we, and wantonly
divert herself at our expense.
Jones, who more than once already have experience this frolicsome
disposition of the heathen goddess, be now again doom to be
tantalize in the like manner; for he arrive at the door of Mrs
Fitzpatrick about ten minute after the departure of Sophia. he now
address himself to the waiting-woman belong to Mrs Fitzpatrick;
who tell he the disagreeable news that the lady be go, but could
not tell he whither; and the same answer he afterwards receive from
Mrs Fitzpatrick herself. for as that lady make no doubt but that Mr
Jones be a person detach from her uncle Western, in pursuit of his
daughter, so she be too generous to betray she.
Though Jones have never see Mrs Fitzpatrick, yet he have hear that a
cousin of Sophia be married to a gentleman of that name. this,
however, in the present tumult of his mind, never once recur to his
memory; but when the footman, who have conduct he from his
lordship's, acquaint he with the great intimacy between the lady,
and with their call each other cousin, he then recollect the
story of the marriage which he have formerly hear; and as he be
presently convince that this be the same woman, he become more
surprize at the answer which he have receive, and very earnestly
desire leave to wait on the lady herself; but she as positively
refuse he that honour.
Jones, who, though he have never see a court, be well breed than
most who frequent it, be incapable of any rude or abrupt behaviour to
a lady. when he have receive, therefore, a peremptory denial, he
retire for the present, say to the waiting-woman, "that if this
be an improper hour to wait on her lady, he would return in the
afternoon; and that he then hop to have the honour of see she."
the civility with which he utter this, add to the great comeliness
of his person, make an impression on the waiting-woman, and she could
not help answer; "perhaps, sir, you may;" and, indeed, she
afterwards say everything to her mistress, which she think most
likely to prevail on she to admit a visit from the handsome young
gentleman; for so she call he.
Jones very shrewdly suspect that Sophia herself be now with her
cousin, and be deny to he; which he impute to her resentment of
what have happen at Upton. have, therefore, dispatch Partridge to
procure he lodging, he remain all day in the street, watch the
door where he think his angel lie conceal; but no person do he
see issue forth, except a servant of the house, and in the evening he
return to pay his visit to Mrs Fitzpatrick, which that good lady at
last condescend to admit.
there be a certain air of natural gentility, which it be neither in
the power of dress to give, nor to conceal. Mr Jones, as hath be
before hint, be possess of this in a very eminent degree. he meet,
therefore, with a reception from the lady somewhat different from what
his apparel seem to demand; and after he have pay she his proper
respect, be desire to sit down.
the reader will not, I believe, be desirous of know all the
particular of this conversation, which end very little to the
satisfaction of poor Jones. for though Mrs Fitzpatrick soon discover
the lover (as all woman have the eye of hawk in those matter), yet
she still think it be such a lover, as a generous friend of the
lady should not betray she to. in short, she suspect this be the
very Mr Blifil, from whom Sophia have fly; and all the answer which
she artfully draw from Jones, concern Mr Allworthy's family,
confirm she in this opinion. she therefore strictly deny any
knowledge concern the place whither Sophia be go; nor could
Jones obtain more than a permission to wait on she again the next
evening.
when Jones be depart Mrs Fitzpatrick communicate her suspicion
concern Mr Blifil to she maid; who answer, "sure, madam, he be
too pretty a man, in my opinion, for any woman in the world to run
away from. I have rather fancy it be Mr Jones."--"Mr Jones!" say the
lady, "what Jones?" for Sophia have not give the least hint of any
such person in all their conversation; but Mrs Honour have be much
more communicative, and have acquaint her sister Abigail with the
whole history of Jones, which this now again related to her mistress.
Mrs Fitzpatrick no soon receive this information, than she
immediately agree with the opinion of she maid; and, what be very
unaccountable, see charm in the gallant, happy lover, which she have
overlook in the slight squire. "Betty," say she, "you be
certainly in the right: he be a very pretty fellow, and I donot wonder
that my cousin's maid should tell you so many woman be fond of he. I
be sorry now I do not inform he where my cousin be; and yet, if he
be so terrible a rake as you tell I, it be a pity she should ever see
he any more; for what but her ruin can happen from marry a rake
and a beggar against her father's consent? I protest, if he be such a
man as the wench describe he to you, it be but an office of charity
to keep she from he; and I be sure it would be unpardonable in I to
do otherwise, who have taste so bitterly of the misfortune attend
such marriage."
here she be interrupt by the arrival of a visitor, which be no
other than his lordship; and as nothing pass at this visit either
new or extraordinary, or any way material to this history, we shall
here put an end to this chapter.
Chapter iii.
a project of Mrs Fitzpatrick, and her visit to Lady Bellaston.
when Mrs Fitzpatrick retire to rest, her thought be entirely take
up by her cousin Sophia and Mr Jones. she be, indeed, a little
offend with the former, for the disingenuity which she now
discover. in which meditation she have not long exercise her
imagination before the follow conceit suggest itself; that could
she possibly become the mean of preserve Sophia from this man, and
of restore she to her father, she should, in all human probability,
by so great a service to the family, reconcile to herself both her
uncle and her aunt western.
as this be one of her most favourite wish, so the hope of success
seem so reasonable, that nothing remain but to consider of proper
method to accomplish her scheme. to attempt to reason the case with
Sophia do not appear to her one of those method: for as Betty have
report from Mrs Honour, that Sophia have a violent inclination to
Jones, she conceive that to dissuade she from the match be an
endeavour of the same kind, as it would be very heartily and earnestly
to entreat a moth not to fly into a candle.
if the reader will please to remember that the acquaintance which
Sophia have with Lady Bellaston be contract at the house of Mrs
Western, and must have grow at the very time when Mrs Fitzpatrick
live with this latter lady, he will want no information, that Mrs
Fitzpatrick must have be acquaint with she likewise. they be,
besides, both equally her distant relation.
after much consideration, therefore, she resolve to go early in the
morning to that lady, and endeavour to see she, unknown to Sophia, and
to acquaint she with the whole affair. for she do not in the least
doubt, but that the prudent lady, who have often ridicule romantic
love, and indiscreet marriage, in her conversation, would very
readily concur in her sentiment concern this match, and would lend
her utmost assistance to prevent it.
this resolution she accordingly execute; and the next morning before
the sun, she huddle on her cloath, and at a very unfashionable,
unseasonable, unvisitable hour, go to Lady Bellaston, to whom she
get access, without the least knowledge or suspicion of Sophia, who,
though not asleep, lie at that time awake in her bed, with Honour
snoring by her side.
Mrs Fitzpatrick make many apology for an early, abrupt visit, at an
hour when, she say, "she should not have think of disturb she
ladyship, but upon business of the utmost consequence." she then
open the whole affair, tell all she have hear from Betty; and do
not forget the visit which Jones have pay to herself the precede
evening.
Lady Bellaston answer with a smile, "then you have see this
terrible man, madam; pray, be he so very fine a figure as he be
represent? for Etoff entertain I last night almost two hour with
he. the wench I believe be in love with he by reputation." here the
reader will be apt to wonder; but the truth be, that Mrs Etoff, who
have the honour to pin and unpin the Lady Bellaston, have receive
compleat information concern the say Mr Jones, and have faithfully
convey the same to her lady last night (or rather that morning)
while she be undressing; on which account she have be detain in
her office above the space of an hour and a half.
the lady indeed, though generally well enough please with the
narrative of Mrs Etoff at those season, give an extraordinary
attention to her account of Jones; for Honour have describe he as a
very handsome fellow, and Mrs Etoff, in her hurry, add so much to
the beauty of his person to her report, that Lady Bellaston begin to
conceive he to be a kind of miracle in nature.
the curiosity which her woman have inspire be now greatly increase
by Mrs Fitzpatrick, who speak as much in favour of the person of Jones
as she have before speak in dispraise of his birth, character, and
fortune.
when Lady Bellaston have hear the whole, she answer gravely,
"indeed, madam, this be a matter of great consequence. nothing can
certainly be more commendable than the part you act; and I shall be
very glad to have my share in the preservation of a young lady of so
much merit, and for whom I have so much esteem."
"Doth not your ladyship think," say Mrs Fitzpatrick eagerly, "that it
would be the good way to write immediately to my uncle, and acquaint
he where my cousin be?"
the lady ponder a little upon this, and thus answered--"why, no,
madam, I think not. Di western hath describe her brother to I to be
such a brute, that I cannot consent to put any woman under his power
who hath escape from it. I have hear he behave like a monster to
his own wife, for he be one of those wretch who think they have a
right to tyrannise over we, and from such I shall ever esteem it the
cause of my sex to rescue any woman who be so unfortunate to be under
their power.--the business, dear cousin, will be only to keep Miss
Western from see this young fellow, till the good company, which
she will have an opportunity of meeting here, give she a properer
turn."
"if he should find she out, madam," answer the other, "your ladyship
may be assure he will leave nothing unattempted to come at she."
"but, madam," reply the lady, "it be impossible he should come
here--though indeed it be possible he may get some intelligence where
she be, and then may lurk about the house--I wish therefore I know his
person.
"be there no way, madam, by which I could have a sight of he? for,
otherwise, you know, cousin, she may contrive to see he here without
my knowledge." Mrs Fitzpatrick answer, "that he have threaten she
with another visit that afternoon, and that, if her ladyship please
to do she the honour of call upon she then, she would hardly fail
of see he between six and seven; and if he come early she would,
by some mean or other, detain he till her ladyship's arrival."--Lady
Bellaston reply, "she would come the moment she could get from
dinner, which she suppose would be by seven at farth; for that it
be absolutely necessary she should be acquaint with his person.
upon my word, madam," say she, "it be very good to take this care of
Miss Western; but common humanity, as well as regard to our family,
require it of we both; for it would be a dreadful match indeed."
Mrs Fitzpatrick fail not to make a proper return to the compliment
which Lady Bellaston have bestow on her cousin, and, after some
little immaterial conversation, withdraw; and, get as fast as she
could into her chair, unseen by Sophia or Honour, return home.
chapter iv.
which consist of visit.
Mr Jones have walk within sight of a certain door during the whole
