the long in the whole year. at length, the clock have strike
five, he return to Mrs Fitzpatrick, who, though it be a full hour
early than the decent time of visit, receive he very civilly;
but still persist in her ignorance concern Sophia.
Jones, in ask for his angel, have drop the word cousin, upon
which Mrs Fitzpatrick say, "then, sir, you know we be relate: and,
as we be, you will permit I the right of enquire into the
particular of your business with my cousin." here Jones hesitate a
good while, and at last answer, "he have a considerable sum of money
of her in his hand, which he desire to deliver to she." he then
produce the pocket-book, and acquaint Mrs Fitzpatrick with the
content, and with the method in which they come into his hand. he
have scarce finish his story, when a most violent noise shake the
whole house. to attempt to describe this noise to those who have hear
it would be in vain; and to aim at give any idea of it to those who
have never hear the like, would be still more vain: for it may be
truly said--
_--Non acuta
Sic geminant Corybantes aera._
the priest of Cybele do not so rattle their sound brass.
in short, a footman knock, or rather thunder, at the door. Jones
be a little surprized at the sound, have never hear it before; but
Mrs Fitzpatrick very calmly say, that, as some company be come,
she could not make he any answer now; but if he please to stay till
they be go, she intimate she have something to say to he.
the door of the room now fly open, and, after push in her hoop
sideways before she, enter Lady Bellaston, who have first make a
very low courtesy to Mrs Fitzpatrick, and as low a one to Mr Jones,
be usher to the upper end of the room.
we mention these minute matter for the sake of some country lady of
our acquaintance, who think it contrary to the rule of modesty to
bend their knee to a man.
the company be hardly well settle, before the arrival of the peer
lately mention, cause a fresh disturbance, and a repetition of
ceremonial.
these be over, the conversation begin to be (as the phrase be)
extremely brilliant. however, as nothing past in it which can be
think material to this history, or, indeed, very material in itself,
I shall omit the relation; the rather, as I have know some very fine
polite conversation grow extremely dull, when transcribe into book,
or repeat on the stage. indeed, this mental repast be a dainty, of
which those who be exclude from polite assembly must be content
to remain as ignorant as they must of the several dainty of french
cookery, which be serve only at the table of the great. to say the
truth, as neither of these be adapt to every taste, they might both
be often throw away on the vulgar.
Poor Jones be rather a spectator of this elegant scene, than an actor
in it; for though, in the short interval before the peer's arrival,
Lady Bellaston first, and afterwards Mrs Fitzpatrick, have address
some of their discourse to he; yet no soon be the noble lord
enter, than he engross the whole attention of the two lady to
himself; and as he take no more notice of Jones than if no such person
have be present, unless by now and then star at he, the lady
follow his example.
the company have now staid so long, that Mrs Fitzpatrick plainly
perceive they all design to stay out each other. she therefore
resolve to rid herself of Jones, he be the visitant to whom she
think the least ceremony be due. take therefore an opportunity of
a cessation of chat, she address herself gravely to he, and say,
"Sir, I shall not possibly be able to give you an answer to-night as
to that business; but if you please to leave word where I may send to
you to-morrow---"
Jones have natural, but not artificial good-breeding. instead therefore
of communicate the secret of his lodging to a servant, he
acquaint the lady herself with it particularly, and soon after very
ceremoniously withdraw.
he be no soon go than the great personage, who have take no
notice of he present, begin to take much notice of he in his
absence; but if the reader hath already excuse we from relate the
more brilliant part of this conversation, he will surely be very ready
to excuse the repetition of what may be call vulgar abuse; though,
perhaps, it may be material to our history to mention an observation
of Lady Bellaston, who take her leave in a few minute after he, and
then say to Mrs Fitzpatrick, at her departure, "I be satisfied on the
account of my cousin; she can be in no danger from this fellow."
our history shall follow the example of Lady Bellaston, and take leave
of the present company, which be now reduce to two person; between
whom, as nothing pass, which in the least concern we or our reader,
we shall not suffer ourselves to be divert by it from matter which
must seem of more consequence to all those who be at all interested
in the affair of our heroe.
Chapter v.
an adventure which happen to Mr Jones at his lodging, with some

the next morning, as early as it be decent, Jones attend at Mrs

backwards and forwards in the street from break of day; and if she have

to receive, and not only now, but to five several visit which he make
she that day.
to be plain with the reader, the noble peer have from some reason or
other, perhaps from a regard for the lady's honour, insist that she
should not see Mr Jones, whom he look on as a scrub, any more; and
the lady have comply in make that promise to which we now see she
so strictly adhere.
but as our gentle reader may possibly have a well opinion of the
young gentleman than her ladyship, and may even have some concern,

Mr Jones, then, have often hear Mr Allworthy mention the gentlewoman

as Jones likewise know, live in Bond-street, be the widow of a

daughter, and of a compleat set of manuscript sermon.
of these two daughter, Nancy, the elder, be now arrive at the age

Hither Jones have despatch Partridge, and in this house he be
provide with a room for himself in the second floor, and with one for

the first floor be inhabit by one of those young gentleman, who, in
the last age, be call man of wit and pleasure about town, and
properly enough; for as man be usually denominate from their
business or profession, so pleasure may be say to have be the only
business or profession of those gentleman to whom fortune have make all
useful occupation unnecessary. playhouse, coffeehouse, and tavern
be the scene of their rendezvous. wit and humour be the
entertainment of their loose hour, and love be the business of
their more serious moment. Wine and the mus conspire to kindle the
bright flame in their breast; nor do they only admire, but some
be able to celebrate the beauty they admire, and all to judge of
the merit of such composition.
such, therefore, be properly call the man of wit and pleasure; but
I question whether the same appellation may, with the same propriety,
be give to those young gentleman of our time, who have the same
ambition to be distinguish for part. wit certainly they have
nothing to do with. to give they their due, they soar a step high
than their predecessor, and may be call man of wisdom and vertù
(take heed you do not read virtue). thus at an age when the gentleman
above mention employ their time in toast the charm of a woman,
or in make sonnet in her praise; in give their opinion of a play
at the theatre, or of a poem at Will's or Button's; these gentleman
be consider the method to bribe a corporation, or meditating
speech for the House of Commons, or rather for the magazine. but
the science of gaming be that which above all other employ their
thought. these be the study of their graver hour, while for their
amusement they have the vast circle of connoisseurship, painting,
music, statuary, and natural philosophy, or rather _unnatural_, which
deal in the wonderful, and know nothing of Nature, except her
monster and imperfection.
when Jones have spend the whole day in vain enquiry after Mrs
Fitzpatrick, he return at last disconsolate to his apartment. here,
while he be vent his grief in private, he hear a violent uproar
below-stair; and soon after a female voice beg he for heaven's

occasion to help the distressed, immediately run down-stair; when
step into the dining-room, whence all the noise issue, he behold
the young gentleman of wisdom and vertù just before mention, pin
close to the wall by his footman, and a young woman stand by,
wring her hand, and cry out, "he will be murder! he will be
murder!" and, indeed, the poor gentleman seem in some danger of
be choake, when Jones fly hastily to his assistance, and rescue
he, just as he be breathe his last, from the unmerciful clutch
of the enemy.
though the fellow have receive several kick and cuff from the little
gentleman, who have more spirit than strength, he have make it a kind of
scruple of conscience to strike his master, and would have content
himself with only choake he; but towards Jones he bear no such
respect; he no soon therefore find himself a little roughly handle
by his new antagonist, than he give he one of those punch in the
gut which, though the spectator at Broughton's amphitheatre have
such exquisite delight in see they, convey but very little pleasure
in the feeling.
the lusty youth have no soon receive this blow, than he meditate a
most grateful return; and now ensue a combat between Jones and the

able to contend with Jones than his master have before be to contend
with he.
and now, Fortune, accord to her usual custom, reverse the face of

his seasonable assistance; he receive likewise the hearty thank of

eld daughter of the house.
the footman, have now recover his leg, shake his head at Jones,
and, with a sagacious look, cried--"O d--n I, Iwill have nothing more
to do with you; you have be upon the stage, or Ibe d--nably
mistaken." and indeed we may forgive this his suspicion; for such be
the agility and strength of our heroe, that he be, perhaps, a match
for one of the first-rate boxer, and could, with great ease, have
beat all the muffled[*] graduate of Mr Broughton's school.
[*] lest posterity should be puzzle by this epithet, I think proper
to explain it by an advertisement which be publish Feb. 1, 1747.
N.B.--Mr Broughton propose, with proper assistance, to open an
academy at his house in the Haymarket, for the instruction of those
who be willing to be initiate in the mystery of boxing: where the
whole theory and practice of that truly british art, with all the
various stop, blow, cross-buttock, &c., incident to combatant,
will be fully teach and explain; and that person of quality and
distinction may not be deter from enter into _a course of
those lecture_, they will be give with the utmost tenderness and
regard to the delicacy of the frame and constitution of the pupil,
for which reason muffle be provide, that will effectually secure
they from the inconveniency of black eye, broken jaw, and bloody
nose.
the master, foam with wrath, order his man immediately to strip,
to which the latter very readily agree, on condition of receive his
wage. this condition be presently comply with, and the fellow be
discharge.
and now the young gentleman, whose name be Nightingale, very

when the bottle and glass be on the table the gentleman begin to

"I hope, sir," say he to Jones, "you will not from this accident
conclude, that I make a custom of strike my servant, for I assure
you this be the first time I have be guilty of it in my remembrance,
and I have pass by many provoking fault in this very fellow, before
he could provoke I to it; but when you hear what hath happen this

of the cloth at whist by my fire;--and my Hoyle, sir--my good Hoyle,
which cost I a guinea, lie open on the table, with a quantity of
porter spill on one of the most material leaf of the whole book.
this, you will allow, be provoke; but I say nothing till the rest
of the honest company be go, and then give the fellow a gentle
rebuke, who, instead of express any concern, make I a pert answer,
`that servant must have their diversion as well as other people;
that he be sorry for the accident which have happen to the book, but
that several of his acquaintance have buy the same for a shilling,
and that I might stop as much in his wage, if I please.' I now give

to---in short, he impute my early come home to----In short, he cast
a reflection----He mention the name of a young lady, in a manner--in

Jones answer, "that he believe no person live would blame he;
for my part," say he, "I confess I should, on the last-mention
provocation, have do the same thing."
our company have not sit long before they be join by the mother and

even he put on as much constrain mirth as possible. indeed, half his
natural flow of animal spirit, join to the sweetness of his temper,
be sufficient to make a most amiable companion; and notwithstanding
the heaviness of his heart, so agreeable do he make himself on the

earnestly desire his further acquaintance. Miss Nancy be well
pleased with he; and the widow, quite charm with her new lodger,
invite he, with the other, next morning to breakfast.
Jones on his part be no less satisfied. as for Miss Nancy, though a
very little creature, she be extremely pretty, and the widow have all
the charm which can adorn a woman near fifty. as she be one of the
most innocent creature in the world, so she be one of the most
chearful. she never think, nor speak, nor wish any ill, and have
constantly that desire of pleasing, which may be call the happy
of all desire in this, that it scarce ever fail of attain its
end, when not disgrace by affectation. in short, though her power
be very small, she be in her heart one of the warm friend. she
have be a most affectionate wife, and be a most fond and tender
mother. as our history doth not, like a newspaper, give great
character to people who never be hear of before, nor will ever be
hear of again, the reader may hence conclude, that this excellent
woman will hereafter appear to be of some importance in our history.
nor be Jones a little pleased with the young gentleman himself, whose

expression of the high disinterestedness in the affair of love. on
which subject the young gentleman deliver himself in a language
which might have very well become an arcadian shepherd of old, and
which appear very extraordinary when proceed from the lip of a
modern fine gentleman; but he be only one by imitation, and mean by
nature for a much well character.
Chapter vi.
what arrive while the company be at breakfast, with some hint

our company bring together in the morning the same good inclination

afflict he, and his countenance, as well as his behaviour, in

indication of a disorder mind.
the discourse turn at present, as before, on love; and Mr

call romantic, but which wise and sober woman generally regard in a
well light. Mrs Miller (for so the mistress of the house be call)

this compliment be so apparently direct to Jones, that we should

prattler? she hath not speak twenty word this week."
here their conversation be interrupt by the entrance of a

will have the happiness of meeting at the masquerade."
Jones have not a sufficient degree of vanity to entertain any such
flattering imagination; nor do Mrs Miller herself give much assent to

follows:--
to MR JONES.
the queen of the fairy send you this;
use she favour not amiss.
Mrs Miller and Miss Nancy now both agree with Mr Nightingale; nay,
Jones himself be almost persuade to be of the same opinion. and as
no other lady but Mrs Fitzpatrick, he think, know his lodging, he
begin to flatter himself with some hope, that it come from she, and
that he might possibly see his Sophia. these hope have surely very
little foundation; but as the conduct of Mrs Fitzpatrick, in not
see he accord to her promise, and in quit her lodging, have
be very odd and unaccountable, he conceive some faint hope, that
she (of whom he have formerly hear a very whimsical character) might
possibly intend to do he that service in a strange manner, which she
decline do by more ordinary method. to say the truth, as nothing
certain could be conclude from so odd and uncommon an incident, he
have the great latitude to draw what imaginary conclusion from it he
please. as his temper therefore be naturally sanguine, he indulge
it on this occasion, and his imagination work up a thousand
conceit, to favour and support his expectation of meet his dear
Sophia in the evening.
Reader, if thou hast any good wish towards I, I will fully repay
they by wish thee to be possess of this sanguine disposition of
mind; since, after have read much and consider long on that
subject of happiness which hath employ so many great pen, I be
almost inclined to fix it in the possession of this temper; which put
we, in a manner, out of the reach of Fortune, and make we happy
without her assistance. indeed, the sensation of pleasure it give
be much more constant as well as much keener, than those which that
blind lady bestow; nature have wisely contrive, that some satiety
and languor should be annex to all our real enjoyment, lest we
should be so take up by they, as to be stopt from further pursuit. I
make no manner of doubt but that, in this light, we may see the
imaginary future chancellor just call to the bar, the archbishop in
crape, and the prime minister at the tail of an opposition, more truly
happy than those who be invest with all the power and profit of
those respective office.
Mr Jones have now determine to go to the masquerade that evening,
Mr Nightingale offer to conduct he thither. the young gentleman, at
the same time, offer ticket to Miss Nancy and her mother; but the
good woman would not accept they. she say, "she do not conceive the
harm which some people imagine in a masquerade; but that such
extravagant diversion be proper only for person of quality and
fortune, and not for young woman who be to get their living, and
could, at good, hope to be married to a good tradesman."----"a
tradesman!" cry Nightingale, "you shallnot undervalue my Nancy. there
be not a nobleman upon earth above her merit." "o fie! Mr
Nightingale," answer Mrs Miller, "you must not fill the girl's head
with such fancy: but if it be her good luck" (say the mother with
a simper) "to find a gentleman of your generous way of thinking, I
hope she would make a well return to his generosity than to give she
mind up to extravagant pleasure. indeed, where young lady bring
great fortune themselves, they have some right to insist on spend
what be their own; and on that account I have hear the gentleman say,
a man have sometimes a well bargain with a poor wife, than with a
rich one.----but let my daughter marry whom they will, I shall
endeavour to make they blessing to their husbands:----I beg,
therefore, I may hear of no more masquerade. Nancy be, I be certain,
too good a girl to desire to go; for she must remember when you
carry she thither last year, it almost turn her head; and she do
not return to herself, or to her needle, in a month afterwards."
though a gentle sigh, which steal from the bosom of Nancy, seem to
argue some secret disapprobation of these sentiment, she do not dare
openly to oppose they. for as this good woman have all the tenderness,
so she have preserve all the authority of a parent; and as her
indulgence to the desire of her child be restrain only by she
fear for their safety and future welfare, so she never suffer those
command which proceed from such fear to be either disobey or
dispute. and this the young gentleman, who have lodge two year in
the house, know so well, that he presently acquiesce in the refusal.
Mr Nightingale, who grow every minute fonder of Jones, be very
desirous of his company that day to dinner at the tavern, where he
offer to introduce he to some of his acquaintance; but Jones beg
to be excuse, "as his cloath," he say, "be not yet come to town."
to confess the truth, Mr Jones be now in a situation, which sometimes
happen to be the case of young gentleman of much well figure than
himself. in short, he have not one penny in his pocket; a situation in
much great credit among the antient philosopher than among the
modern wise man who live in Lombard-street, or those who frequent
White's chocolate-house. and, perhaps, the great honour which those
philosopher have ascribe to an empty pocket may be one of the
reason of that high contempt in which they be hold in the aforesaid
street and chocolate-house.
now if the antient opinion, that man might live very comfortably on
virtue only, be, as the modern wise man just above-mention pretend
to have discover, a notorious error; no less false be, I apprehend,
that position of some writer of romance, that a man can live
altogether on love; for however delicious repast this may afford to
some of our sens or appetite, it be most certain it can afford none
to other. those, therefore, who have place too great a confidence in
such writer, have experience their error when it be too late; and
have find that love be no more capable of allay hunger, than a
rise be capable of delight the ear, or a violin of gratify the
smell.
Notwithstanding, therefore, all the delicacy which love have set
before he, namely, the hope of see Sophia at the masquerade; on
which, however ill-found his imagination might be, he have
voluptuously feast during the whole day, the evening no soon come
than Mr Jones begin to languish for some food of a gross kind.
Partridge discover this by intuition, and take the occasion to give
some oblique hint concern the bank-bill; and, when these be
reject with disdain, he collect courage enough once more to
mention a return to Mr Allworthy.
"Partridge," cry Jones, "you cannot see my fortune in a more
desperate light than I see it myself; and I begin heartily to repent
that I suffer you to leave a place where you be settle, and to
follow I. however, I insist now on your return home; and for the
expense and trouble which you have so kindly put yourself to on my
account, all the cloath I leave behind in your care I desire you would
take as your own. I be sorry I can make you no other acknowledgment."
he speak these word with so pathetic an accent, that Partridge, among
whose vice ill-nature or hardness of heart be not number, burst
into tear; and after swear he would not quit he in his distress,
he begin with the most earnest entreaty to urge his return home.
"for heaven's sake, sir," say he, "do but consider; what can your
honour do?--how be it possible you can live in this town without
money? do what you will, sir, or go wherever you please, I be resolve
not to desert you. but pray, sir, consider--do pray, sir, for your own
sake, take it into your consideration; and Ibe sure," say he, "that
your own good sense will bid you return home."
"how often shall I tell thee," answer Jones, "that I have no home to
return to? have I any hope that Mr Allworthy's door would be open to
receive I, I want no distress to urge I--nay, there be no other
cause upon earth, which could detain I a moment from fly to his
presence; but, alas! that I be for ever banish from. his last word
be--o, Partridge, they still ring in my ear--his last word be,
when he give I a sum of money--what it be I know not, but
considerable Ibe sure it be--his last word were--`i be resolve from
this day forward, on no account to converse with you any more.'"
here passion stopt the mouth of Jones, as surprize for a moment do
that of Partridge; but he soon recover the use of speech, and after
a short preface, in which he declare he have no inquisitiveness in his
temper, enquire what Jones mean by a considerable sum--he know not
how much--and what be become of the money.
in both these point he now receive full satisfaction; on which he
be proceed to comment, when he be interrupt by a message from
Mr Nightingale, who desire his master's company in his apartment.
when the two gentleman be both attire for the masquerade, and Mr
Nightingale have give order for chair to be send for, a circumstance
of distress occur to Jones, which will appear very ridiculous to
many of my reader. this be how to procure a shilling; but if such
reader will reflect a little on what they have themselves feel from
the want of a thousand pound, or, perhaps, of ten or twenty, to
execute a favourite scheme, they will have a perfect idea of what Mr
Jones feel on this occasion. for this sum, therefore, he apply to
Partridge, which be the first he have permit he to advance, and
be the last he intend that poor fellow should advance in his
service. to say the truth, Partridge have lately make no offer of this
kind. whether it be that he desire to see the bank-bill break in
upon, or that distress should prevail on Jones to return home, or from
what other motive it proceed, I will not determine.
Chapter vii.
contain the whole humour of a masquerade.
our cavalier now arrive at that temple, where Heydegger, the great
Arbiter Deliciarum, the great high-priest of pleasure, preside; and,
like other heathen priest, impose on his votary by the pretended
presence of the deity, when in reality no such deity be there.
Mr Nightingale, have take a turn or two with his companion, soon
leave he, and walk off with a female, say, "now you be here,
sir, you must beat about for your own game."
Jones begin to entertain strong hope that his Sophia be present; and
these hope give he more spirit than the light, the music, and the
company; though these be pretty strong antidote against the spleen.
he now accost every woman he see, whose stature, shape, or air, bore
any resemblance to his angel. to all of whom he endeavour to say
something smart, in order to engage an answer, by which he might
discover that voice which he think it impossible he should mistake.
some of these answer by a question, in a squeaking voice, do you
know I? much the great number say, I donot know you, sir, and
nothing more. some call he an impertinent fellow; some make he no
answer at all; some say, indeed I donot know your voice, and I shall
have nothing to say to you; and many give he as kind answer as he
could wish, but not in the voice he desire to hear.
whilst he be talk with one of these last (who be in the habit of
a shepherdess) a lady in a domino come up to he, and slap he on
the shoulder, whisper he, at the same time, in the ear, "if you
talk any long with that trollop, I will acquaint Miss Western."
Jones no soon hear that name, than, immediately quit his former
companion, he apply to the domino, beg and entreat she to
show he the lady she have mention, if she be then in the room.
the mask walk hastily to the upper end of the innermost apartment
before she speak; and then, instead of answer he, sit down, and
declare she be tired. Jones sit down by she, and still persist in
his entreaty; at last the lady coldly answer, "I imagine Mr Jones
have be a more discerning lover, than to suffer any disguise to
conceal his mistress from he." "be she here, then, madam?" reply
Jones, with some vehemence. upon which the lady cried--"Hush, sir, you
will be observe. I promise you, upon my honour, Miss Western be not
here."
Jones, now take the mask by the hand, fall to entreat she in the
most earnest manner, to acquaint he where he might find Sophia; and
when he could obtain no direct answer, he begin to upbraid she gently
for have disappoint he the day before; and conclude, say,
"indeed, my good fairy queen, I know your majesty very well,
notwithstanding the affect disguise of your voice. indeed, Mrs
Fitzpatrick, it be a little cruel to divert yourself at the expense of
my torment."
the mask answer, "though you have so ingeniously discover I, I
must still speak in the same voice, lest I should be know by other.
and do you think, good sir, that I have no great regard for my
cousin, than to assist in carry on an affair between you two, which
must end in her ruin, as well as your own? besides, I promise you, my
cousin be not mad enough to consent to her own destruction, if you be
so much her enemy as to tempt she to it."
"alas, madam!" say Jones, "you little know my heart, when you call I
an enemy of Sophia."
"and yet to ruin any one," cry the other, "you will allow, be the
act of an enemy; and when by the same act you must knowingly and
certainly bring ruin on yourself, be it not folly or madness, as well
as guilt? now, sir, my cousin hath very little more than her father
will please to give she; very little for one of her fashion--you know
he, and you know your own situation."
Jones vow he have no such design on Sophia, "that he would rather
suffer the most violent of death than sacrifice her interest to his
desire." he say, "he know how unworthy he be of she, every way,
that he have long ago resolve to quit all such aspire thought, but
that some strange accident have make he desirous to see she once
more, when he promise he would take leave of she for ever. no,
madam," conclude he, "my love be not of that base kind which seek
its own satisfaction at the expense of what be most dear to its
object. I would sacrifice everything to the possession of my Sophia,
but Sophia herself."
though the reader may have already conceive no very sublime idea of
the virtue of the lady in the mask; and though possibly she may
hereafter appear not to deserve one of the first character of her
sex; yet, it be certain, these generous sentiment make a strong
impression upon she, and greatly add to the affection she have before
conceive for our young heroe.
the lady now, after silence of a few moment, say, "she do not see
his pretension to Sophia so much in the light of presumption, as of
imprudence. young fellow," say she, "can never have too aspiring
thought. I love ambition in a young man, and I would have you
cultivate it as much as possible. perhaps you may succeed with those
who be infinitely superior in fortune; nay, I be convinced there be
women----but donot you think I a strange creature, Mr Jones, to be
thus give advice to a man with whom I be so little acquainted, and
one with whose behaviour to I I have so little reason to be please?"
here Jones begin to apologize, and to hope he have not offend in
anything he have say of her cousin.--to which the mask answer, "and
be you so little verse in the sex, to imagine you can well affront a
lady more than by entertain she with your passion for another
woman? if the fairy queen have conceive no well opinion of your
gallantry, she would scarce have appoint you to meet she at the
masquerade."
Jones have never less inclination to an amour than at present; but
gallantry to the lady be among his principle of honour; and he
hold it as much incumbent on he to accept a challenge to love, as if
it have be a challenge to fight. Nay, his very love to Sophia make it
necessary for he to keep well with the lady, as he make no doubt but
she be capable of bring he into the presence of the other.
he begin therefore to make a very warm answer to her last speech, when
a mask, in the character of an old woman, join they. this mask be
one of those lady who go to a masquerade only to vent ill-nature, by
tell people rude truth, and by endeavour, as the phrase be, to
spoil as much sport as they be able. this good lady, therefore,
have observe Jones, and his friend, whom she well know, in close
consultation together in a corner of the room, conclude she could
nowhere satisfy her spleen well than by interrupt they. she
attack they, therefore, and soon drive they from their retirement;
nor be she content with this, but pursue they to every place which
they shift to avoid she; till Mr Nightingale, see the distress of
his friend, at last relieve he, and engage the old woman in another
pursuit.
while Jones and his mask be walk together about the room, to rid
themselves of the teazer, he observe his lady speak to several mask,
with the same freedom of acquaintance as if they have be bareface.
he could not help express his surprize at this; say, "sure,
madam, you must have infinite discernment, to know people in all
disguise." to which the lady answer, "you cannot conceive anything
more insipid and childish than a masquerade to the people of fashion,
who in general know one another as well here as when they meet in an
assembly or a drawing-room; nor will any woman of condition converse
with a person with whom she be not acquaint. in short, the
generality of person whom you see here may more properly be say to
kill time in this place than in any other; and generally retire from
hence more tired than from the long sermon. to say the truth, I
begin to be in that situation myself; and if I have any faculty at
guess, you be not much well pleased. I protest it would be
almost charity in I to go home for your sake." "I know but one
charity equal to it," cry Jones, "and that be to suffer I to wait
on you home." "sure," answer the lady, "you have a strange opinion
of I, to imagine, that upon such an acquaintance, I would let you
into my door at this time of night. I fancy you impute the friendship
I have show my cousin to some other motive. Confess honestly; donot
you consider this contrived interview as little well than a
downright assignation? be you use, Mr Jones, to make these sudden
conquest?" "I be not use, madam," say Jones, "to submit to such
sudden conquest; but as you have take my heart by surprize, the rest
of my body hath a right to follow; so you must pardon I if I resolve
to attend you wherever you go." he accompany these word with some
proper action; upon which the lady, after a gentle rebuke, and say
their familiarity would be observe, tell he, "she be go to sup
with an acquaintance, whither she hop he would not follow she; for
if you should," say she, "I shall be think an unaccountable
creature, though my friend indeed be not censorious: yet I hope you
willnot follow I; I protest I shall not know what to say if you do."
the lady presently after quit the masquerade, and Jones,
notwithstanding the severe prohibition he have receive, presume to
attend she. he be now reduce to the same dilemma we have mention
before, namely, the want of a shilling, and could not relieve it by
borrowing as before. he therefore walk boldly on after the chair in
which his lady rode, pursue by a grand huzza, from all the chairman
present, who wisely take the good care they can to discountenance all
walk afoot by their better. luckily, however, the gentry who
attend at the Opera-house be too busy to quit their station, and as
the lateness of the hour prevent he from meet many of their
brother in the street, he proceed without molestation, in a dress,
which, at another season, would have certainly raise a mob at his
heel.
the lady be set down in a street not far from Hanover-square, where
the door be presently open, she be carry in, and the
gentleman, without any ceremony, walk in after she.
Jones and his companion be now together in a very well-furnish and
well-warm room; when the female, still speak in her masquerade
voice, say she be surprize at her friend, who must absolutely have
forget her appointment; at which, after vent much resentment, she
suddenly expr some apprehension from Jones, and ask he what the
world would think of their have be alone together in a house at
that time of night? but instead of a direct answer to so important a
question, Jones begin to be very importunate with the lady to unmask;
and at length have prevail, there appear not Mrs Fitzpatrick,
but the Lady Bellaston herself.
it would be tedious to give the particular conversation, which
consist of very common and ordinary occurrence, and which last
from two till six o'clock in the morning. it be sufficient to mention
all of it that be anywise material to this history. and this be a
promise that the lady would endeavour to find out Sophia, and in a few
day bring he to an interview with she, on condition that he would
then take his leave of she. when this be thoroughly settle, and a
second meeting in the evening appoint at the same place, they
separate; the lady return to her house, and Jones to his lodging.
Chapter viii.
contain a scene of distress, which will appear very extraordinary
to most of our reader.
Jones have refresh himself with a few hour' sleep, summon
Partridge to his presence; and deliver he a bank-note of fifty
pound, order he to go and change it. Partridge receive this with
sparkle eye, though, when he come to reflect far, it raise in
he some suspicion not very advantageous to the honour of his master:
to these the dreadful idea he have of the masquerade, the disguise in
which his master have go out and return, and his have be abroad
all night, contribute. in plain language, the only way he could
possibly find to account for the possession of this note, be by
robbery: and, to confess the truth, the reader, unless he should
suspect it be owe to the generosity of Lady Bellaston, can hardly
imagine any other.
to clear, therefore, the honour of Mr Jones, and to do justice to the
liberality of the lady, he have really receive this present from she,
who, though she do not give much into the hackney charity of the
age, such as building hospital, &c., be not, however, entirely void
of that Christian virtue; and conceive (very rightly I think) that a
young fellow of merit, without a shilling in the world, be no
improper object of this virtue.
Mr Jones and Mr Nightingale have be invite to dine this day with Mrs
Miller. at the appoint hour, therefore, the two young gentleman,
with the two girl, attend in the parlour, where they wait from
three till almost five before the good woman appear. she have be
out of town to visit a relation, of whom, at her return, she give the
follow account.
"I hope, gentleman, you will pardon my making you wait; I be sure if
you know the occasion--I have be to see a cousin of mine, about six
mile off, who now lie in.--it should be a warning to all person
(say she, look at her daughter) how they marry indiscreetly.
there be no happiness in this world without a competency. o nancy! how
shall I describe the wretched condition in which I find your poor
cousin? she hath scarce lain in a week, and there be she, this
dreadful weather, in a cold room, without any curtain to her bed, and
not a bushel of coal in her house to supply she with fire; her second
son, that sweet little fellow, lie ill of a quinzy in the same bed
with his mother; for there be no other bed in the house. poor little
Tommy! I believe, Nancy, you will never see your favourite any more;
for he be really very ill. the rest of the child be in pretty good
health: but molly, I be afraid, will do herself an injury: she be but
thirteen year old, Mr Nightingale, and yet, in my life, I never see a
well nurse: she tend both her mother and her brother; and, what be
wonderful in a creature so young, she show all the chearfulness in
the world to her mother; and yet I see she--I see the poor child, Mr
Nightingale, turn about, and privately wipe the tear from her eye."
here Mrs Miller be prevent, by her own tear, from go on, and
there be not, I believe, a person present who do not accompany she
in they; at length she a little recover herself, and proceed thus:
"in all this distress the mother support her spirit in a surprizing
manner. the danger of her son sit heavy upon she, and yet she
endeavour as much as possible to conceal even this concern, on her
husband's account. her grief, however, sometimes get the well of
all her endeavour; for she be always extravagantly fond of this boy,
and a most sensible, sweet-temper creature it be. I protest I be
never more affected in my life than when I hear the little wretch,
who be hardly yet seven year old, while his mother be wet he
with her tear, beg she to be comfort. `indeed, mamma,' cry the
child, `I shallnot die; God Almighty, Ibe sure, willnot take Tommy away;
let heaven be ever so fine a place, I have rather stay here and starve
with you and my papa than go to it.' pardon I, gentleman, I cannot
help it" (say she, wipe her eye), "such sensibility and affection
in a child.--And yet, perhaps, he be least the object of pity; for a
day or two will, most probably, place he beyond the reach of all
human evil. the father be, indeed, most worthy of compassion. poor
man, his countenance be the very picture of horror, and he look like
one rather dead than alive. oh heaven! what a scene do I behold at
my first come into the room! the good creature be lie behind the
bolster, support at once both his child and his wife. he have
nothing on but a thin waistcoat; for his coat be spread over the bed,
to supply the want of blankets.--when he rise up at my entrance, I
scarce know he. as comely a man, Mr Jones, within this fortnight, as
you ever beheld; Mr Nightingale hath see he. his eye sunk, his face
pale, with a long beard. his body shiver with cold, and wear with
hunger too; for my cousin say she can hardly prevail upon he to
eat.--He tell I himself in a whisper--he tell I--I cannot repeat
it--he say he could not bear to eat the bread his child want.
and yet, can you believe it, gentleman? in all this misery his wife
have as good caudle as if she lie in the midst of the great
affluence; I taste it, and I scarce ever taste better.--the mean of
procure she this, he say, he believe be send he by an angel from
heaven. I know not what he mean; for I have not spirit enough to ask
a single question.
"this be a love-match, as they call it, on both side; that be, a
match between two beggar. I must, indeed, say, I never see a fonder
couple; but what be their fondness good for, but to torment each
other?" "indeed, mamma," cry Nancy, "I have always look on my
cousin Anderson" (for that be her name) "as one of the happy of
woman." "I be sure," say Mrs Miller, "the case at present be much
otherwise; for any one might have discern that the tender
consideration of each other's suffering make the most intolerable
part of their calamity, both to the husband and wife. compare to
which, hunger and cold, as they affect their own person only, be
scarce evil. Nay, the very child, the young, which be not two
year old, excepted, feel in the same manner; for they be a most
love family, and, if they have but a bare competency, would be the
happy people in the world." "I never see the least sign of misery
at her house," reply Nancy; "I be sure my heart bleed for what you
now tell I."--"O child," answer the mother, "she hath always
endeavour to make the good of everything. they have always be in
great distress; but, indeed, this absolute ruin hath be bring upon
they by other. the poor man be bail for the villain his brother; and
about a week ago, the very day before her lie-in, their good be
all carry away, and sell by an execution. he send a letter to I of
it by one of the bailiff, which the villain never delivered.--what
must he think of my suffering a week to pass before he hear of I?"
it be not with dry eye that Jones hear this narrative; when it be
end he take Mrs Miller apart with he into another room, and,
deliver she his purse, in which be the sum of £50, desire she to
send as much of it as she think proper to these poor people. the
look which Mrs Miller give Jones, on this occasion, be not easy to be
describe. she burst into a kind of agony of transport, and cry
out--"good heaven! be there such a man in the world?"--But
recollect herself, she say, "indeed I know one such; but can there
be another?" "I hope, madam," cry Jones, "there be many who have
common humanity; for to relieve such distress in our fellow-creature,
can hardly be call more." Mrs Miller then take ten guinea, which
be the utmost he could prevail with she to accept, and say, "she
would find some mean of convey they early the next morning;"
add, "that she have herself do some little matter for the poor
people, and have not leave they in quite so much misery as she find
they."
they then return to the parlour, where Nightingale express much
concern at the dreadful situation of these wretch, whom indeed he
know; for he have see they more than once at Mrs Miller's. he
inveigh against the folly of make oneself liable for the debt of
other; vent many bitter execration against the brother; and
conclude with wish something could be do for the unfortunate
family. "suppose, madam," say he, "you should recommend they to Mr
Allworthy? or what think you of a collection? I will give they a
guinea with all my heart."
Mrs Miller make no answer; and Nancy, to whom her mother have whisper
the generosity of Jones, turn pale upon the occasion; though, if
either of they be angry with Nightingale, it be surely without
reason. for the liberality of Jones, if he have know it, be not an
example which he have any obligation to follow; and there be thousand
who would not have contribute a single halfpenny, as indeed he do
not in effect, for he make no tender of anything; and therefore, as
the other think proper to make no demand, he keep his money in his
pocket.
I have, in truth, observe, and shall never have a well opportunity
than at present to communicate my observation, that the world be in
general divide into two opinion concern charity, which be the
very reverse of each other. one party seem to hold, that all act of
this kind be to be esteem as voluntary gift, and, however little
you give (if indeed no more than your good wish), you acquire a
great degree of merit in so do. other, on the contrary, appear to
be as firmly persuade, that beneficence be a positive duty, and that
whenever the rich fall greatly short of their ability in relieve the
distress of the poor, their pitiful largess be so far from be
meritorious, that they have only perform their duty by half, and
be in some sense more contemptible than those who have entirely
neglect it.
to reconcile these different opinion be not in my power. I shall only
add, that the giver be generally of the former sentiment, and the
receiver be almost universally inclined to the latter.
Chapter ix.
which treat of matter of a very different kind from those in the
precede chapter.
in the evening Jones meet his lady again, and a long conversation again
ensue between they: but as it consist only of the same ordinary
occurrence as before, we shall avoid mention particular, which we
despair of render agreeable to the reader; unless he be one whose
devotion to the fair sex, like that of the papist to their saint,
want to be raise by the help of picture. but I be so far from
desire to exhibit such picture to the public, that I would wish to
draw a curtain over those that have be lately set forth in certain
french novel; very bungling copy of which have be present we
here under the name of translation.
Jones grow still more and more impatient to see Sophia; and find,
after repeat interview with Lady Bellaston, no likelihood of
obtain this by her mean (for, on the contrary, the lady begin to
treat even the mention of the name of Sophia with resentment), he
resolve to try some other method. he make no doubt but that Lady
Bellaston know where his angel be, so he think it most likely that
some of her servant should be acquaint with the same secret.
Partridge therefore be employ to get acquaint with those
servant, in order to fish this secret out of they.
few situation can be imagine more uneasy than that to which his poor
master be at present reduce; for besides the difficulty he meet
with in discover Sophia, besides the fear he have of have
disoblige she, and the assurance he have receive from Lady Bellaston
of the resolution which Sophia have take against he, and of she
have purposely conceal herself from he, which he have sufficient
reason to believe might be true; he have still a difficulty to combat
which it be not in the power of his mistress to remove, however kind
her inclination might have be. this be the exposing of she to be
disinherit of all her father's estate, the almost inevitable
consequence of their come together without a consent, which he have
no hope of ever obtain.
add to all these the many obligation which Lady Bellaston, whose
violent fondness we can no long conceal, have heap upon he; so
that by her mean he be now become one of the well-dress man about
town; and be not only relieve from those ridiculous distress we
have before mention, but be actually raise to a state of affluence
beyond what he have ever know.
now, though there be many gentleman who very well reconcile it to
their conscience to possess themselves of the whole fortune of a
woman, without make she any kind of return; yet to a mind, the
proprietor of which doth not deserve to be hang, nothing be, I
believe, more irksome than to support love with gratitude only;
especially where inclination pull the heart a contrary way. such be
the unhappy case of Jones; for though the virtuous love he bear to
Sophia, and which leave very little affection for any other woman, have
be entirely out of the question, he could never have be able to
have make any adequate return to the generous passion of this lady,
who have indeed be once an object of desire, but be now enter at
least into the autumn of life, though she wear all the gaiety of
youth, both in her dress and manner; nay, she contrive still to
maintain the rose in her cheek; but these, like flower force out
of season by art, have none of that lively bloom freshness with
which nature, at the proper time, bedeck her own production. she
have, besides, a certain imperfection, which render some flower,
though very beautiful to the eye, very improper to be place in a
wilderness of sweet, and what above all other be most disagreeable
to the breath of love.
Though Jones see all these discouragement on the one side, he feel
his obligation full as strongly on the other; nor do he less plainly
discern the ardent passion whence those obligation proceed, the
extreme violence of which if he fail to equal, he well know the lady
would think he ungrateful; and, what be bad, he would have think
himself so. he know the tacit consideration upon which all her favour
be confer; and as his necessity oblige he to accept they, so
his honour, he conclude, force he to pay the price. this therefore
he resolve to do, whatever misery it cost he, and to devote himself
to she, from that great principle of justice, by which the law of
some country oblige a debtor, who be no otherwise capable of
discharge his debt, to become the slave of his creditor.
while he be meditate on these matter, he receive the follow
note from the lady:--
"a very foolish, but a very perverse accident hath happen since
our last meeting, which make it improper I should see you any more
at the usual place. I will, if possible, contrive some other place
by to-morrow. in the meantime, adieu."
this disappointment, perhaps, the reader may conclude be not very
great; but if it be, he be quickly relieve; for in less than an
hour afterwards another note be bring he from the same hand, which
contain as follows:--
"I have alter my mind since I write; a change which, if you be no
strange to the tender of all passion, you will not wonder at. I
be now resolve to see you this evening at my own house, whatever
may be the consequence. come to I exactly at seven; I dine abroad,
but will be at home by that time. a day, I find, to those that
sincerely love, seem long than I imagine.
"if you should accidentally be a few moment before I, bid they
show you into the drawing-room."
to confess the truth, Jones be less pleased with this last epistle
than he have be with the former, as he be prevent by it from
comply with the earnest entreaty of Mr Nightingale, with whom he
have now contract much intimacy and friendship. these entreaty be
to go with that young gentleman and his company to a new play, which
be to be act that evening, and which a very large party have agree
to damn, from some dislike they have take to the author, who be a
friend to one of Mr Nightingale's acquaintance. and this sort of fun,
our heroe, we be ashamed to confess, would willingly have prefer
to the above kind appointment; but his honour get the well of his
inclination.
before we attend he to this intended interview with the lady, we
think proper to account for both the precede note, as the reader
may possibly be not a little surprized at the imprudence of Lady
Bellaston, in bring her lover to the very house where her rival be
lodge.
first, then, the mistress of the house where these lover have hitherto
meet, and who have be for some year a pensioner to that lady, be now
become a methodist, and have that very morning wait upon her
ladyship, and after rebuke she very severely for her past life, have
positively declare that she would, on no account, be instrumental in
carry on any of her affair for the future.
the hurry of spirit into which this accident throw the lady make she
despair of possibly find any other convenience to meet Jones that
evening; but as she begin a little to recover from her uneasiness at
the disappointment, she set her thought to work, when luckily it come
into her head to propose to Sophia to go to the play, which be
immediately consent to, and a proper lady provide for her
companion. Mrs Honour be likewise despatch with Mrs Etoff on the
same errand of pleasure; and thus her own house be leave free for the
safe reception of Mr Jones, with whom she promise herself two or
three hour of uninterrupted conversation after her return from the
place where she din, which be at a friend's house in a pretty
distant part of the town, near her old place of assignation, where she
have engage herself before she be well apprized of the revolution
that have happen in the mind and moral of her late confidante.
Chapter x.
a chapter which, though short, may draw tear from some eye.
Mr Jones be just dressed to wait on Lady Bellaston, when Mrs Miller
rap at his door; and, be admit, very earnestly desire his
company below-stair, to drink tea in the parlour.
upon his entrance into the room, she presently introduce a person to
he, say, "this, sir, be my cousin, who hath be so greatly
beholden to your goodness, for which he beg to return you his
sincere thank."
the man have scarce enter upon that speech which Mrs Miller have so
kindly preface, when both Jones and he, look stedfastly at each
other, show at once the utmost token of surprize. the voice of the
latter begin instantly to faulter; and, instead of finish his
speech, he sink down into a chair, cry, "it be so, I be convince
it be so!"
"bless I! whatbe the meaning of this?" cry Mrs Miller; "you be not
ill, I hope, cousin? some water, a dram this instant."
"be not fright, madam," cry Jones, "I have almost as much need of
a dram as your cousin. we be equally surprize at this unexpected
meeting. your cousin be an acquaintance of mine, Mrs Miller."
"an acquaintance!" cry the man.--"Oh, heaven!"
"ay, an acquaintance," repeat Jones, "and an honour acquaintance
too. when I do not love and honour the man who dare venture
everything to preserve his wife and child from instant destruction,
may I have a friend capable of disown I in adversity!"
"oh, you be an excellent young man," cry Mrs Miller:--"Yes, indeed,
poor creature! he hath venture everything.--if he have not have one of
the good of constitution, it must have kill he."
"Cousin," cry the man, who have now pretty well recover himself,
"this be the angel from heaven whom I mean. this be he to whom,
before I see you, I owe the preservation of my Peggy. he it be to
whose generosity every comfort, every support which I have procure
for she, be owe. he be, indeed, the worthy, bravest, noblest; of
all human being. o cousin, I have obligation to this gentleman of
such a nature!"
"mention nothing of obligation," cry Jones eagerly; "not a word, I
insist upon it, not a word" (meaning, I suppose, that he would not
have he betray the affair of the robbery to any person). "if, by the
trifle you have receive from I, I have preserve a whole family,
sure pleasure be never buy so cheap."
"oh, sir!" cry the man, "I wish you could this instant see my house.
if any person have ever a right to the pleasure you mention, I be
convince it be yourself. my cousin tell I she acquaint you with
the distress in which she find we. that, sir, be all greatly remove,
and chiefly by your goodness.----My child have now a bed to lie
on----and they have----they have----eternal blessing reward you for
it!----they have bread to eat. my little boy be recover; my wife be
out of danger, and I be happy. all, all owe to you, sir, and to my
cousin here, one of the good of woman. indeed, sir, I must see you at
my house.--indeed my wife must see you, and thank you.--My child
too must express their gratitude.----indeed, sir, they be not without
a sense of their obligation; but what be my feeling when I reflect to
whom I owe that they be now capable of express their
gratitude.----oh, sir, the little heart which you have warm have now
be cold as ice without your assistance."
here Jones attempt to prevent the poor man from proceeding; but
indeed the overflowing of his own heart would of itself have stop
his word. and now Mrs Miller likewise begin to pour forth
thanksgiving, as well in her own name, as in that of her cousin, and
conclude with say, "she doubt not but such goodness would meet a
glorious reward."
Jones answer, "he have be sufficiently reward already. your
cousin's account, madam," say he, "hath give I a sensation more
pleasing than I have ever know. he must be a wretch who be unmove at
hear such a story; how transporting then must be the thought of
have happily act a part in this scene! if there be man who cannot
feel the delight of give happiness to other, I sincerely pity they,
as they be incapable of taste what be, in my opinion, a great
honour, a high interest, and a sweet pleasure than the ambitious,
the avaricious, or the voluptuous man can ever obtain."
the hour of appointment be now come, Jones be force to take a
hasty leave, but not before he have heartily shake his friend by the
hand, and desire to see he again as soon as possible; promise that
he would himself take the first opportunity of visit he at his own
house. he then stept into his chair, and proceed to Lady
Bellaston's, greatly exult in the happiness which he have procure
to this poor family; nor could he forbear reflect, without horror,
on the dreadful consequence which must have attend they, have he
listen rather to the voice of strict justice than to that of mercy,
when he be attack on the high road.
Mrs Miller sing forth the praise of Jones during the whole evening,
in which Mr Anderson, while he stay, so passionately accompany
she, that he be often on the very point of mention the
circumstance of the robbery. however, he luckily recollect himself,
and avoid an indiscretion which would have be so much the great,
as he know Mrs Miller to be extremely strict and nice in her
principle. he be likewise well apprized of the loquacity of this
lady; and yet such be his gratitude, that it have almost get the
well both of discretion and shame, and make he publish that which
would have defame his own character, rather than omit any
circumstance which might do the full honour to his benefactor.
Chapter xi.
in which the reader will be surprize.
Mr Jones be rather early than the time appoint, and early than
the lady; whose arrival be hinder, not only by the distance of the
place where she din, but by some other cross accident very
vexatious to one in her situation of mind. he be accordingly show
into the drawing-room, where he have not be many minute before the
door open, and in came----no other than Sophia herself, who have leave
the play before the end of the first act; for this, as we have already
say, be, a new play, at which two large party meet, the one to
damn, and the other to applaud, a violent uproar, and an engagement
between the two party, have so terrify our heroine, that she be
glad to put herself under the protection of a young gentleman who
safely convey she to her chair.
as Lady Bellaston have acquaint she that she should not be at home
till late, Sophia, expect to find no one in the room, come hastily
in, and go directly to a glass which almost front she, without
once look towards the upper end of the room, where the statue of
Jones now stand motionless.---In this glass it be, after
contemplate her own lovely face, that she first discover the say
statue; when, instantly turn about, she perceive the reality of
the vision: upon which she give a violent scream, and scarce preserve
herself from faint, till Jones be able to move to she, and support
she in his arm.
to paint the look or thought of either of these lover, be beyond my
power. as their sensation, from their mutual silence, may be judge
to have be too big for their own utterance, it cannot be suppose
that I should be able to express they: and the misfortune be, that few
of my reader have be enough in love to feel by their own heart
what past at this time in theirs.
after a short pause, Jones, with faultere accent, said--"i see,
madam, you be surprize."--"surprize!" answer she; "oh heaven!
indeed, I be surprize. I almost doubt whether you be the person you
seem."--"Indeed," cry he, "my Sophia, pardon I, madam, for this
once call you so, I be that very wretched Jones, whom fortune,
after so many disappointment, hath, at last, kindly conduct to you.
oh! my Sophia, do you know the thousand torment I have suffer in
this long, fruitless pursuit."--"pursuit of whom?" say Sophia, a
little recollect herself, and assume a reserve air.--"Can you be
so cruel to ask that question?" cry Jones; "need I say, of you?" "of
I!" answer Sophia: "Hath Mr Jones, then, any such important
business with me?"--"To some, madam," cry Jones, "this might seem an
important business" (give she the pocket-book). "I hope, madam, you
will find it of the same value as when it be lose." Sophia take the
pocket-book, and be go to speak, when he interrupt she
thus:--"let we not, I beseech you, lose one of these precious moment
which fortune hath so kindly send we. o, my Sophia! I have business of
a much superior kind. thus, on my knee, let I ask your pardon."--"My
pardon!" cry she; "sure, sir, after what be past, you cannot expect,
after what I have hear."--"I scarce know what I say," answer Jones.
"by heaven! I scarce wish you should pardon I. o my Sophia!
henceforth never cast away a thought on such a wretch as I be. if any
remembrance of I should ever intrude to give a moment's uneasiness to
that tender bosom, think of my unworthiness; and let the remembrance
of what pass at Upton blot I for ever from your mind."
Sophia stand tremble all this while. her face be white than snow,
and her heart be throb through her stay. but, at the mention of
Upton, a blush arise in her cheek, and her eye, which before she have
scarce lift up, be turn upon Jones with a glance of disdain. he
understand this silent reproach, and reply to it thus: "o my Sophia!
my only love! you cannot hate or despise I more for what happen
there than I do myself; but yet do I the justice to think that my
heart be never unfaithful to you. that have no share in the folly I
be guilty of; it be even then unalterably your. though I despair
of possess you, nay, almost of ever see you more, I doate still
on your charming idea, and could seriously love no other woman. but if
my heart have not be engage, she, into whose company I accidently
fall at that curse place, be not an object of serious love. believe
I, my angel, I never have see she from that day to this; and never
intend or desire to see she again." Sophia, in her heart, be very
glad to hear this; but force into her face an air of more coldness
than she have yet assume, "why," say she, "Mr Jones, do you take the
trouble to make a defence where you be not accuse? if I think it
worth while to accuse you, I have a charge of unpardonable nature
indeed."--"what be it, for heaven's sake?" answer Jones, tremble
and pale, expect to hear of his amour with Lady Bellaston. "oh,"
say she, "how be it possible! can everything noble and everything
base be lodge together in the same bosom?" Lady Bellaston, and the
ignominious circumstance of have be keep, rise again in his mind,
and stopt his mouth from any reply. "could I have expect," proceed
Sophia, "such treatment from you? Nay, from any gentleman, from any
man of honour? to have my name traduce in public; in inn, among the
mean vulgar! to have any little favour that my unguarded heart may
have too lightly betray I to grant, boast of there! nay, even to
hear that you have be force to fly from my love!"
nothing could equal Jones's surprize at these word of Sophia; but
yet, not be guilty, he be much less embarrassed how to defend
himself than if she have touch that tender string at which his
conscience have be alarm. by some examination he presently find,
that she suppose he guilty of so shocking an outrage against his
love, and her reputation, be entirely owe to Partridge's talk at
the inn before landlord and servant; for Sophia confess to he it
be from they that she receive her intelligence. he have no very great
difficulty to make she believe that he be entirely innocent of an
offence so foreign to his character; but she have a great deal to
hinder he from go instantly home, and put Partridge to death,
which he more than once swear he would do. this point be clear
up, they soon find themselves so well pleased with each other, that
Jones quite forget he have begin the conversation with conjure she to
give up all thought of he; and she be in a temper to have give ear
to a petition of a very different nature; for before they be aware
they have both go so far, that he let fall some word that sound
like a proposal of marriage. to which she reply, "that, do not her
duty to her father forbid she to follow her own inclination, ruin
with he would be more welcome to she than the most affluent fortune
with another man." at the mention of the word ruin, he start, let
drop her hand, which he have hold for some time, and strike his
breast with his own, cry out, "oh, Sophia! can I then ruin thee? no;
by heaven, no! I never will act so base a part. Dearest Sophia,
whatever it cost I, I will renounce you; I will give you up; I will
tear all such hope from my heart as be inconsistent with your real
good. my love I will ever retain, but it shall be in silence; it shall
be at a distance from you; it shall be in some foreign land; from
whence no voice, no sigh of my despair, shall ever reach and disturb
your ear. and when I be dead"--He would have go on, but be stopt
by a flood of tear which Sophia let fall in his bosom, upon which she
lean, without be able to speak one word. he kiss they off,
which, for some moment, she allow he to do without any resistance;
but then recollect herself, gently withdraw out of his arm; and,
to turn the discourse from a subject too tender, and which she find
she could not support, bethink herself to ask he a question she
never have time to put to he before, "how he come into that room?" he
begin to stammer, and would, in all probability, have raise her
suspicion by the answer he be go to give, when, at once, the door
open, and in come Lady Bellaston.
have advance a few step, and see Jones and Sophia together, she
suddenly stopt; when, after a pause of a few moment, recollect
herself with admirable presence of mind, she say--though with
sufficient indication of surprize both in voice and countenance--"i
thought, Miss Western, you have be at the play?"
Though Sophia have no opportunity of learning of Jones by what mean he
have discover she, yet, as she have not the least suspicion of the
real truth, or that Jones and Lady Bellaston be acquaint, so she
be very little confound; and the less, as the lady have, in all
their conversation on the subject, entirely take her side against
her father. with very little hesitation, therefore, she go through
the whole story of what have happen at the play-house, and the cause
of her hasty return.
the length of this narrative give Lady Bellaston an opportunity of
rally her spirit, and of consider in what manner to act. and as
the behaviour of Sophia give her hope that Jones have not betray
she, she put on an air of good humour, and say, "I should not have
break in so abruptly upon you, Miss Western, if I have know you have
company."
Lady Bellaston fix her eye on Sophia whilst she speak these word.
to which that poor young lady, have her face overspread with blush
and confusion, answer, in a stammering voice, "I be sure, madam, I
shall always think the honour of your ladyship's company----" "I hope,
at least," cry Lady Bellaston, "I interrupt no business."--"No,
madam," answer Sophia, "our business be at an end. your ladyship
may be pleased to remember I have often mention the loss of my
pocket-book, which this gentleman, have very luckily find, be so
kind to return it to I with the bill in it."
Jones, ever since the arrival of Lady Bellaston, have be ready to
sink with fear. he sit kick his heel, play with his finger,
and look more like a fool, if it be possible, than a young booby
squire, when he be first introduce into a polite assembly. he begin,
however, now to recover himself; and take a hint from the behaviour
of Lady Bellaston, who he see do not intend to claim any acquaintance
with he, he resolve as entirely to affect the stranger on his part.
he say, "ever since he have the pocket-book in his possession, he have
use great diligence in enquire out the lady whose name be writ in
it; but never till that day could be so fortunate to discover she."
Sophia have indeed mention the loss of her pocket-book to Lady
Bellaston; but as Jones, for some reason or other, have never once
hint to she that it be in his possession, she believe not one
syllable of what Sophia now say, and wonderfully admire the extreme
quickness of the young lady in invent such an excuse. the reason of
Sophia's leave the playhouse meet with no well credit; and though
she could not account for the meeting between these two lover, she
be firmly persuade it be not accidental.
with an affected smile, therefore, she say, "indeed, Miss Western,
you have have very good luck in recover your money. not only as it
fall into the hand of a gentleman of honour, but as he happen to
discover to whom it belong. I think you would not consent to have it
advertised.--it be great good fortune, sir, that you find out to
whom the note belong."
"oh, madam," cry Jones, "it be enclose in a pocket-book, in which
the young lady's name be write."
"that be very fortunate, indeed," cry the lady:--"And it be no
less so, that you hear Miss Western be at my house; for she be very
little known."
Jones have at length perfectly recover his spirit; and as he
conceive he have now an opportunity of satisfy Sophia as to the
question she have ask he just before Lady Bellaston come in, he
proceed thus: "why, madam," answer he, "it be by the lucky
chance imaginable I make this discovery. I be mention what I have
find, and the name of the owner, the other night to a lady at the
masquerade, who tell I she believe she know where I might see Miss
Western; and if I would come to her house the next morning she would
inform I, I go accord to her appointment, but she be not at
home; nor could I ever meet with she till this morning, when she
direct I to your ladyship's house. I come accordingly, and do
myself the honour to ask for your ladyship; and upon my saying that I
have very particular business, a servant show I into this room;
where I have not be long before the young lady return from the
play."
upon his mention the masquerade, he look very slily at Lady
Bellaston, without any fear of be remark by Sophia; for she be
visibly too much confound to make any observation. this hint a
little alarmed the lady, and she be silent; when Jones, who see the
agitation of Sophia's mind, resolve to take the only method of
relieve she, which be by retire; but, before he do this, he
say, "I believe, madam, it be customary to give some reward on these
occasions;--i must insist on a very high one for my honesty;--it be,
madam, no less than the honour of be permit to pay another visit
here."
"Sir," reply the lady, "I make no doubt that you be a gentleman,
and my door be never shut to people of fashion."
Jones then, after proper ceremonial, depart, highly to his own
satisfaction, and no less to that of Sophia; who be terribly alarmed
lest Lady Bellaston should discover what she know already but too
well.
upon the stair Jones meet his old acquaintance, Mrs Honour, who,
notwithstanding all she have say against he, be now so well breed to
behave with great civility. this meeting prove indeed a lucky
circumstance, as he communicate to she the house where he lodge,
with which Sophia be unacquainted.
Chapter xii.
in which the thirteenth book be conclude.
the elegant Lord Shaftesbury somewhere object to tell too much
truth: by which it may be fairly infer, that, in some case, to lie
be not only excusable but commendable.
and surely there be no person who may so properly challenge a right
to this commendable deviation from truth, as young woman in the affair
of love; for which they may plead precept, education, and above all,
the sanction, nay, I may say the necessity of custom, by which they
be restrain, not from submit to the honest impulse of nature
(for that would be a foolish prohibition), but from own they.
we be not, therefore, ashamed to say, that our heroine now pursue
the dictate of the above-mention right honourable philosopher. as
she be perfectly satisfied then, that Lady Bellaston be ignorant of
the person of Jones, so she determine to keep she in that ignorance,
though at the expense of a little fib.
Jones have not be long go, before Lady Bellaston cry, "upon my
word, a good pretty young fellow; I wonder who he be; for I donot
remember ever to have see his face before."
"nor I neither, madam," cry Sophia. "I must say he behave very
handsomely in relation to my note."
"yes; and he be a very handsome fellow," say the lady: "donot you
think so?"
"I do not take much notice of he," answer Sophia, "but I think
he seem rather awkward, and ungenteel than otherwise."
"you be extremely right," cry Lady Bellaston: "you may see, by his
manner, that he hath not keep good company. Nay, notwithstanding his
return your note, and refuse the reward, I almost question
whether he be a gentleman.----I have always observe there be a
something in person well bear, which other can never acquire.----i
think I will give order not to be at home to he."
"nay, sure, madam," answer Sophia, "one cannot suspect after what he
hath done;--beside, if your ladyship observe he, there be an
elegance in his discourse, a delicacy, a prettiness of expression
that, that----"
"I confess," say Lady Bellaston, "the fellow hath words----and
indeed, Sophia, you must forgive I, indeed you must."
"I forgive your ladyship!" say Sophia.
"yes, indeed you must," answer she, laugh; "for I have a horrible
suspicion when I first come into the room----i vow you must forgive
it; but I suspect it be Mr Jones himself."
"do your ladyship, indeed?" cry Sophia, blushing, and affect a
laugh.
"yes, I vow I do," answer she. "I cannot imagine what put it into my
head: for, give the fellow his due, he be genteely drest; which, I
think, dear Sophy, be not commonly the case with your friend."
"this raillery," cry Sophia, "be a little cruel, Lady Bellaston,
after my promise to your ladyship."
"not at all, child," say the lady;----"It would have be cruel
before; but after you have promise I never to marry without your
father's consent, in which you know be imply your give up Jones,
sure you can bear a little raillery on a passion which be pardonable
enough in a young girl in the country, and of which you tell I you
have so entirely get the well. what must I think, my dear Sophy, if
you cannot bear a little ridicule even on his dress? I shall begin to
fear you be very far go indeed; and almost question whether you
have deal ingenuously with I."
"indeed, madam," cry Sophia, "your ladyship mistake I, if you
imagine I have any concern on his account."
"on his account!" answer the lady: "you must have mistake I; I
go no far than his dress;----for I would not injure your taste
by any other comparison--I donot imagine, my dear Sophy, if your Mr
Jones have be such a fellow as this--"
"I think," say Sophia, "your ladyship have allow he to be
handsome"----
"Whom, pray?" cry the lady hastily.
"Mr Jones," answer sophia;--and immediately recollect herself,
"Mr Jones!--no, no; I ask your pardon;--i mean the gentleman who be
just now here."
"O Sophy! Sophy!" cry the lady; "this Mr Jones, I be afraid, still
run in your head."
"then, upon my honour, madam," say Sophia, "Mr Jones be as entirely
indifferent to I, as the gentleman who just now leave we."
"upon my honour," say Lady Bellaston, "I believe it. forgive I,
therefore, a little innocent raillery; but I promise you I will never
mention his name any more."
and now the two lady separate, infinitely more to the delight of
Sophia than of Lady Bellaston, who would willingly have torment she
rival a little long, have not business of more importance call she
away. as for Sophia, her mind be not perfectly easy under this first
practice of deceit; upon which, when she retire to her chamber, she
reflect with the high uneasiness and conscious shame. nor could
the peculiar hardship of her situation, and the necessity of the case,
at all reconcile her mind to her conduct; for the frame of her mind
be too delicate to bear the thought of have be guilty of a
falsehood, however qualified by circumstance. nor do this thought
once suffer she to close her eye during the whole succeeding night.
BOOK XIV.
contain two day.
Chapter i.
an essay to prove that an author will write the well for have some
knowledge of the subject on which he write.
as several gentleman in these time, by the wonderful force of genius
only, without the least assistance of learning, perhaps, without be
well able to read, have make a considerable figure in the republic of
letter; the modern critic, I be tell, have lately begin to assert,
that all kind of learning be entirely useless to a writer; and,
indeed, no other than a kind of fetter on the natural sprightliness
and activity of the imagination, which be thus weigh down, and
prevent from soar to those high flight which otherwise it would
be able to reach.
this doctrine, I be afraid, be at present carry much too far: for
why should write differ so much from all other art? the nimbleness
of a dancing-master be not at all prejudice by be teach to move;
nor doth any mechanic, I believe, exercise his tool the bad by
have learn to use they. for my own part, I cannot conceive that
Homer or Virgil would have writ with more fire, if instead of be
master of all the learning of their time, they have be as ignorant
as most of the author of the present age. nor do I believe that all
the imagination, fire, and judgment of Pitt, could have produce those
oration that have make the senate of England, in these our time, a
rival in eloquence to Greece and Rome, if he have not be so well read
in the writing of Demosthenes and Cicero, as to have transfer
their whole spirit into his speech, and, with their spirit, their
knowledge too.
I would not here be understand to insist on the same fund of learn
in any of my brother, as Cicero persuade we be necessary to the
composition of an orator. on the contrary, very little reading be, I
conceive, necessary to the poet, less to the critic, and the least of
all to the politician. for the first, perhaps, Byshe's Art of Poetry,
and a few of our modern poet, may suffice; for the second, a moderate
heap of play; and, for the last, an indifferent collection of
political journal.
to say the truth, I require no more than that a man should have some
little knowledge of the subject on which he treat, accord to the
old maxim of law, _Quam quisque nôrit artem in eâ se exerceat_. with
this alone a writer may sometimes do tolerably well; and, indeed,
without this, all the other learning in the world will stand he in
little stead.
for instance, let we suppose that Homer and Virgil, Aristotle and
