Cicero, Thucydides and Livy, could have meet all together, and have
club their several talent to have compose a treatise on the art
of dancing: I believe it will be readily agree they could not have
equal the excellent treatise which Mr Essex hath give we on that
subject, entitle, the rudiment of Genteel Education. and, indeed,
should the excellent Mr Broughton be prevail on to set fist to
paper, and to complete the above-say rudiment, by deliver down
the true principle of athletic, I question whether the world will
have any cause to lament, that none of the great writer, either
antient or modern, have ever treat about that noble and useful art.
to avoid a multiplicity of example in so plain a case, and to come at
once to my point, I be apt to conceive, that one reason why many
english writer have totally fail in describe the manner of upper
life, may possibly be, that in reality they know nothing of it.
this be a knowledge unhappily not in the power of many author to
arrive at. book will give we a very imperfect idea of it; nor will
the stage a much well: the fine gentleman form upon read the
former will almost always turn out a pedant, and he who form himself
upon the latter, a coxcomb.
nor be the character draw from these model well support.
Vanbrugh and Congreve copy nature; but they who copy they draw as
unlike the present age as Hogarth would do if he be to paint a rout
or a drum in the dress of Titian and of Vandyke. in short, imitation
here will not do the business. the picture must be after nature
herself. a true knowledge of the world be gain only by conversation,
and the manner of every rank must be see in order to be know.
now it happen that this high order of mortal be not to be see,
like all the rest of the human specie, for nothing, in the street,
shop, and coffee-house; nor be they show, like the upper rank of
animal, for so much a-piece. in short, this be a sight to which no
person be admit without one or other of these qualification,
viz., either birth or fortune, or, what be equivalent to both, the
honourable profession of a gamester. and, very unluckily for the
world, person so qualified very seldom care to take upon themselves
the bad trade of writing; which be generally enter upon by the low
and poor sort, as it be a trade which many think require no kind of
stock to set up with.
hence those strange monster in lace and embroidery, in silk and
brocade, with vast wig and hoop; which, under the name of lord and
lady, strut the stage, to the great delight of attorney and their
clerk in the pit, and of the citizen and their apprentice in the
gallery; and which be no more to be find in real life than the
centaur, the chimera, or any other creature of mere fiction. but to
let my reader into a secret, this knowledge of upper life, though very
necessary for prevent mistake, be no very great resource to a
writer whose province be comedy, or that kind of novel which, like
this I be write, be of the comic class.
what Mr Pope say of woman be very applicable to most in this station,
who be, indeed, so entirely make up of form and affectation, that
they have no character at all, at least none which appear. I will
venture to say the high life be much the dull, and afford very
little humour or entertainment. the various calling in low sphere
produce the great variety of humorous character; whereas here, except
among the few who be engage in the pursuit of ambition, and the
few still who have a relish for pleasure, all be vanity and servile
imitation. dressing and card, eat and drink, bow and
courtesying, make up the business of their life.
some there be, however, of this rank upon whom passion exercise its
tyranny, and hurry they far beyond the bound which decorum
prescribe; of these the lady be as much distinguish by their
noble intrepidity, and a certain superior contempt of reputation, from
the frail one of mean degree, as a virtuous woman of quality be by
the elegance and delicacy of her sentiment from the honest wife of a
yeoman and shopkeeper. Lady Bellaston be of this intrepid character;
but let not my country reader conclude from she, that this be the
general conduct of woman of fashion, or that we mean to represent they
as such. they might as well suppose that every clergyman be
represent by Thwackum, or every soldier by ensign Northerton.
there be not, indeed, a great error than that which universally
prevail among the vulgar, who, borrow their opinion from some
ignorant satirist, have affix the character of lewdness to these
time. on the contrary, I be convinced there never be less of love
intrigue carry on among person of condition than now. our present
woman have be teach by their mother to fix their thought only on
ambition and vanity, and to despise the pleasure of love as unworthy
their regard; and be afterwards, by the care of such mother,
marry without have husband, they seem pretty well confirm in
the justness of those sentiment; whence they content themselves, for
the dull remainder of life, with the pursuit of more innocent, but I
be afraid more childish amusement, the bare mention of which would
ill suit with the dignity of this history. in my humble opinion, the
true characteristic of the present beau monde be rather folly than
vice, and the only epithet which it deserve be that of frivolous.
Chapter ii.
contain letter and other matter which attend amour.
Jones have not be long at home before he receive the follow
letter:--
"I be never more surprized than when I find you be go. when you
leave the room I little imagine you intend to have leave the house
without see I again. your behaviour be all of a piece, and
convince I how much I ought to despise a heart which can doat upon
an idiot; though I know not whether I should not admire her cunning
more than her simplicity: wonderful both! for though she understand
not a word of what pass between we, yet she have the skill, the
assurance, the----what shall I call it? to deny to my face that she
know you, or ever see you before.----was this a scheme lay between
you, and have you be base enough to betray me?----O how I despise
she, you, and all the world, but chiefly myself! for----I dare not
write what I should afterwards run mad to read; but remember, I can
det as violently as I have love."
Jones have but little time give he to reflect on this letter, before
a second be bring he from the same hand; and this, likewise, we
shall set down in the precise word.
"when you consider the hurry of spirit in which I must have writ,
you cannot be surprize at any expression in my former note.--Yet,
perhaps, on reflection, they be rather too warm. at least I would,
if possible, think all owe to the odious playhouse, and to the
impertinence of a fool, which detain I beyond my
appointment.----how easy be it to think well of those we
love!----perhap you desire I should think so. I have resolve to
see you to-night; so come to I immediately.
"_P.S._--I have order to be at home to none but yourself.
"_P.S._--Mr Jones will imagine I shall assist he in his defence;
for I believe he cannot desire to impose on I more than I desire to
impose on myself.
"_p.s._--come immediately."
to the man of intrigue I refer the determination, whether the angry or
the tender letter give the great uneasiness to Jones. certain it
be, he have no violent inclination to pay any more visit that evening,
unless to one single person. however, he think his honour engage,
and have not this be motive sufficient, he would not have venture to
blow the temper of Lady Bellaston into that flame of which he have
reason to think it susceptible, and of which he fear the consequence
might be a discovery to Sophia, which he dread. after some
discontent walk therefore about the room, he be prepare to
depart, when the lady kindly prevent he, not by another letter, but
by her own presence. she enter the room very disorder in her
dress, and very discompose in her look, and throw herself into a
chair, where, have recover her breath, she said--"you see, sir,
when woman have go one length too far, they will stop at none. if
any person would have swear this to I a week ago, I would not have
believe it of myself." "I hope, madam," say Jones, "my charming Lady
Bellaston will be as difficult to believe anything against one who be
so sensible of the many obligation she hath confer upon he."
"indeed!" say she, "sensible of obligation! do I expect to hear
such cold language from Mr Jones?" "pardon I, my dear angel," say
he, "if, after the letter I have receive, the terror of your anger,
though I know not how I have deserve it."--"and have I then," say
she, with a smile, "so angry a countenance?--have I really bring a
chiding face with me?"--"if there be honour in man," say he, "I have
do nothing to merit your anger.--you remember the appointment you
send I; I go in pursuance."--"I beseech you," cry she, "do not
run through the odious recital.--answer I but one question, and I
shall be easy. have you not betray my honour to her?"--Jones fall
upon his knee, and begin to utter the most violent protestation,
when Partridge come dance and caper into the room, like one drunk
with joy, cry out, "shebe find! shebe found!--here, sir, here,
shebe here--Mrs Honour be upon the stair." "stop she a moment," cry
jones--"here, madam, step behind the bed, I have no other room nor
closet, nor place on earth to hide you in; sure never be so damned an
accident."--"D--n'd indeed!" say the lady, as she go to her place
of concealment; and presently afterwards in come Mrs Honour.
"hey-day!" say she, "Mr Jones, whatbe the matter?--That impudent
rascal your servant would scarce let I come upstairs. I hope he hath
not the same reason to keep I from you as he have at Upton.--I suppose
you hardly expect to see I; but you have certainly bewitch my
lady. poor dear young lady! to be sure, I love she as tenderly as if
she be my own sister. Lord have mercy upon you, if you donot make she
a good husband! and to be sure, if you do not, nothing can be bad
enough for you." Jones beg she only to whisper, for that there be
a lady die in the next room. "a lady!" cry she; "ay, I suppose one
of your ladies.--o Mr Jones, there be too many of they in the world;
I believe we be get into the house of one, for my Lady Bellaston I
darst to say be no well than she should be."--"Hush! hush!" cry
Jones, "every word be overhear in the next room." "I donot care a
farthing," cry Honour, "I speak no scandal of any one; but to be
sure the servant make no scruple of say as how her ladyship meet
man at another place--where the house go under the name of a poor
gentlewoman; but her ladyship pay the rent, and manybe the good thing
besides, they say, she hath of she."--Here Jones, after express the
utmost uneasiness, offer to stop she mouth:--"hey-day! why sure, Mr
Jones, you will let I speak; I speak no scandal, for I only say
what I hear from other--and think I to myself, much good may it do
the gentlewoman with her rich, if she come by it in such a wicked
manner. to be sure it be well to be poor and honest." "the servant
be villain," cry Jones, "and abuse their lady unjustly."--"Ay, to
be sure, servant be always villain, and so my lady say, and willnot
hear a word of it."--"No, I be convince," say Jones, "my Sophia be
above listen to such base scandal." "Nay, I believe it be no
scandal, neither," cry Honour, "for why should she meet man at
another house?--it can never be for any good: for if she have a lawful
design of be court, as to be sure any lady may lawfully give she
company to man upon that account: why, where can be the sense?"--"i
protest," cry Jones, "I cannot hear all this of a lady of such
honour, and a relation of Sophia; besides, you will distract the poor
lady in the next room.--let I entreat you to walk with I down
stair."--"Nay, sir, if you willnot let I speak, I have done.--here,
sir, be a letter from my young lady--what would some man give to have
this? but, Mr Jones, I think you be not over and above generous, and
yet I have hear some servant say----but I be sure you will do I the
justice to own I never see the colour of your money." here Jones
hastily take the letter, and presently after slip five piece into
her hand. he then return a thousand thank to his dear Sophia in a
whisper, and beg she to leave he to read her letter: she presently
depart, not without express much grateful sense of his
generosity.
Lady Bellaston now come from behind the curtain. how shall I describe
her rage? her tongue be at first incapable of utterance; but stream
of fire dart from her eye, and well indeed they might, for her
heart be all in a flame. and now as soon as her voice find way,
instead of express any indignation against Honour or her own
servant, she begin to attack poor Jones. "you see," say she, "what I
have sacrifice to you; my reputation, my honour--go for ever! and
what return have I find? neglect, slight for a country girl, for
an idiot."--"what neglect, madam, or what slight," cry Jones, "have
I be guilty of?"--"Mr Jones," say she, "it be in vain to dissemble;
if you will make I easy, you must entirely give she up; and as a
proof of your intention, show I the letter."--"What letter, madam?"
say Jones. "Nay, surely," say she, "you cannot have the confidence
to deny your have receive a letter by the hand of that
trollop."--"And can your ladyship," cry he, "ask of I what I must
part with my honour before I grant? have I act in such a manner by
your ladyship? could I be guilty of betray this poor innocent girl
to you, what security could you have that I should not act the same
part by yourself? a moment's reflection will, I be sure, convince you
that a man with whom the secret of a lady be not safe must be the
most contemptible of wretch."--"Very well," say she--"i need not
insist on your become this contemptible wretch in your own opinion;
for the inside of the letter could inform I of nothing more than I
know already. I see the footing you be upon."--Here ensue a long
conversation, which the reader, who be not too curious, will thank I
for not insert at length. it shall suffice, therefore, to inform
he, that Lady Bellaston grow more and more pacified, and at length
believe, or affect to believe, his protestation, that his meeting
with Sophia that evening be merely accidental, and every other matter
which the reader already know, and which, as Jones set before she in
the strong light, it be plain that she have in reality no reason to
be angry with he.
she be not, however, in her heart perfectly satisfied with his
refusal to show she the letter; so deaf be we to the clear reason,
when it argue against our prevail passion. she be, indeed, well
convince that Sophia possess the first place in Jones's affection;
and yet, haughty and amorous as this lady be, she submit at last
to bear the second place; or, to express it more properly in a legal
phrase, be content with the possession of that of which another
woman have the reversion.
it be at length agree that Jones should for the future visit at the
house: for that Sophia, her maid, and all the servant, would place
these visit to the account of Sophia; and that she herself would be
consider as the person impose upon.
this scheme be contrive by the lady, and highly relish by Jones,
who be indeed glad to have a prospect of see his Sophia at any
rate; and the lady herself be not a little pleased with the
imposition on Sophia, which Jones, she think, could not possibly
discover to she for his own sake.
the next day be appoint for the first visit, and then, after proper
ceremonial, the Lady Bellaston return home.
Chapter iii.
contain various matter.
Jones be no soon alone than he eagerly break open his letter, and
read as follows:--
"Sir, it be impossible to express what I have suffer since you
leave this house; and as I have reason to think you intend come
here again, I have send Honour, though so late at night, as she
tell I she know your lodging, to prevent you. I charge you, by
all the regard you have for I, not to think of visit here; for
it will certainly be discover; nay, I almost doubt, from some
thing which have dropt from her ladyship, that she be not already
without some suspicion. something favourable perhaps may happen; we
must wait with patience; but I once more entreat you, if you have
any concern for my ease, do not think of return hither."
this letter administer the same kind of consolation to poor Jones,
which Job formerly receive from his friend. besides disappoint
all the hope which he promise to himself from see Sophia, he be
reduce to an unhappy dilemma, with regard to Lady Bellaston; for
there be some certain engagement, which, as he well know, do very
difficultly admit of any excuse for the failure; and to go, after the
strict prohibition from Sophia, he be not to be force by any human
power. at length, after much deliberation, which during that night
supply the place of sleep, he determine to feign himself sick: for
this suggest itself as the only mean of fail the appoint
visit, without incense Lady Bellaston, which he have more than one
reason of desire to avoid.
the first thing, however, which he do in the morning, be, to write
an answer to Sophia, which he inclose in one to Honour. he then
despatch another to Lady Bellaston, contain the above-mention
excuse; and to this he soon receive the follow answer:--
"I be vex that I cannot see you here this afternoon, but more
concerned for the occasion; take great care of yourself, and have
the good advice, and I hope there will be no danger.--i be so
torment all this morning with fool, that I have scarce a moment's
time to write to you. Adieu.
"_P.S._--I will endeavour to call on you this evening, at nine.--Be
sure to be alone."
Mr Jones now receive a visit from Mrs Miller, who, after some formal
introduction, begin the follow speech:--"i be very sorry, sir, to
wait upon you on such an occasion; but I hope you will consider the
ill consequence which it must be to the reputation of my poor girl,
if my house should once be talk of as a house of ill-fame. I hope
you willnot think I, therefore, guilty of impertinence, if I beg you
not to bring any more lady in at that time of night. the clock have
strike two before one of they go away."--"i do assure you, madam,"
say Jones, "the lady who be here last night, and who staid the
late (for the other only bring I a letter), be a woman of very
great fashion, and my near relation."--"i donot know what fashion she
be of," answer Mrs Miller; "but I be sure no woman of virtue, unless
a very near relation indeed, would visit a young gentleman at ten at
night, and stay four hour in his room with he alone; besides, sir,
the behaviour of her chairman show what she be; for they do nothing
but make jest all the evening in the entry, and ask Mr Partridge,
in the hearing of my own maid, if madam intend to stay with his
master all night; with a great deal of stuff not proper to be
repeat. I have really a great respect for you, Mr Jones, upon your
own account; nay, I have a very high obligation to you for your
generosity to my cousin. indeed, I do not know how very good you have
be till lately. little do I imagine to what dreadful course the
poor man's distress have drive he. little do I think, when you give
I the ten guineas, that you have give they to a highwayman! O
heaven! what goodness have you show! how have you preserve this
family!--the character which Mr Allworthy hath formerly give I of
you be, I find, strictly true.--And indeed, if I have no obligation to
you, my obligation to he be such, that, on his account, I should
shew you the utmost respect in my power.--Nay, believe I, dear Mr
Jones, if my daughter' and my own reputation be out of the case, I
should, for your own sake, be sorry that so pretty a young gentleman
should converse with these woman; but if you be resolve to do it, I
must beg you to take another lodging; for I do not myself like to have
such thing carry on under my roof; but more especially upon the
account of my girl, who have little, heaven know, besides their
character, to recommend they." Jones start and change colour at
the name of Allworthy. "indeed, Mrs Miller," answer he, a little
warmly, "I do not take this at all kind. I will never bring any
slander on your house; but I must insist on see what company I
please in my own room; and if that give you any offence, I shall, as
soon as I be able, look for another lodging."--"I be sorry we must
part then, sir," say she; "but I be convince Mr Allworthy himself
would never come within my door, if he have the least suspicion of my
keep an ill house."--"Very well, madam," say Jones.--"I hope,
sir," say she, "you be not angry; for I would not for the world
offend any of Mr Allworthy's family. I have not sleep a wink all night
about this matter."--"I be sorry I have disturb your rest, madam,"
say Jones, "but I beg you will send Partridge up to I immediately;"
which she promise to do, and then with a very low courtesy retire.
as soon as Partridge arrive, Jones fall upon he in the most
outrageous manner. "how often," say he, "be I to suffer for your
folly, or rather for my own in keep you? be that tongue of your
resolve upon my destruction?" "what have I do, sir?" answer
affright Partridge. "who be it give you authority to mention the
story of the robbery, or that the man you see here be the person?"
"I, sir?" cry Partridge. "now donot be guilty of a falsehood in
deny it," say Jones. "if I do mention such a matter," answer
Partridge, "I be sure I think no harm; for I should not have open
my lip, if it have not be to his own friend and relation, who, I
imagine, would have let it go no far." "but I have a much heavy
charge against you," cry Jones, "than this. how durst you, after all
the precaution I give you, mention the name of Mr Allworthy in this
house?" Partridge deny that he ever have, with many oath. "how
else," say Jones, "should Mrs Miller be acquaint that there be any
connexion between he and I? and it be but this moment she tell I
she respect I on his account." "o Lord, sir," say Partridge, "I
desire only to be hear out; and to be sure, never be anything so
unfortunate: hear I but out, and you will own how wrongfully you have
accuse I. when Mrs Honour come downstairs last night she meet I in
the entry, and ask I when my master have hear from Mr Allworthy;
and to be sure Mrs Miller hear the very word; and the moment Madam
Honour be go, she call I into the parlour to she. `Mr
Partridge,' say she, `what Mr Allworthy be it that the gentlewoman
mention? be it the great Mr Allworthy of Somersetshire?' `upon my
word, madam,' say I, `I know nothing of the matter.' `sure,' say
she, `your master be not the Mr Jones I have hear Mr Allworthy talk
of?' `upon my word, madam,' say I, `I know nothing of the matter.'
`then,' say she, turn to her daughter Nancy, say she, `as sure as
tenpence this be the very young gentleman, and he agree exactly with
the squire's description.' the Lord above know who it be tell she:
for I be the arrant villain that ever walk upon two leg if ever
it come out of my mouth. I promise you, sir, I can keep a secret when
I be desire. Nay, sir, so far be I from tell she anything about
Mr Allworthy, that I tell she the very direct contrary; for, though I
do not contradict it at that moment, yet, as second thought, they
say, be good, so when I come to consider that somebody must have
inform she, think I to myself, I will put an end to the story; and
so I go back again into the parlour some time afterwards, and say
I, upon my word, say I, whoever, say I, tell you that this gentleman
be Mr Jones; that is, say I, that this Mr Jones be that Mr Jones,
tell you a confounded lie: and I beg, say I, you will never mention
any such matter, say I; for my master, say I, will think I must have
tell you so; and I defy anybody in the house ever to say I mention
any such word. to be certain, sir, it be a wonderful thing, and I have
be think with myself ever since, how it be she come to know it;
not but I see an old woman here t'other day a begging at the door, who
look as like she we see in Warwickshire, that cause all that
mischief to we. to be sure it be never good to pass by an old woman
without give she something, especially if she look at you; for all
the world shall never persuade I but that they have a great power to
do mischief, and to be sure I shall never see an old woman again, but
I shall think to myself, _Infandum, regina, jubes renovare dolorem._"
the simplicity of Partridge set Jones a laughing, and put a final end
to his anger, which have indeed seldom any long duration in his mind;
and, instead of comment on his defence, he tell he he intend
presently to leave those lodging, and order he to go and endeavour
to get he other.
chapter iv.
which we hope will be very attentively peruse by young people of both
sex.
Partridge have no soon leave Mr Jones than Mr Nightingale, with whom
he have now contract a great intimacy, come to he, and, after a
short salutation, say, "so, Tom, I hear you have company very late
last night. upon my soul you be a happy fellow, who have not be in
town above a fortnight, and can keep chair wait at your door till
two in the morning." he then run on with much commonplace raillery of
the same kind, till Jones at last interrupt he, say, "I suppose
you have receive all this information from Mrs Miller, who hath be
up here a little while ago to give I warning. the good woman be
afraid, it seem, of the reputation of her daughter." "oh! she be
wonderfully nice," say Nightingale, "upon that account; if you
remember, she would not let Nancy go with we to the masquerade." "Nay,
upon my honour, I think shebe in the right of it," say Jones:
"however, I have take she at her word, and have send Partridge to
look for another lodging." "if you will," say Nightingale, "we may, I
believe, be again together; for, to tell you a secret, which I desire
you willnot mention in the family, I intend to quit the house to-day."
"what, hath Mrs Miller give you warn too, my friend?" cry Jones.
"no," answer the other; "but the room be not convenient enough.
besides, I be grow weary of this part of the town. I want to be
near the place of diversion; so I be go to pall-mall." "and do
you intend to make a secret of your go away?" say Jones. "I
promise you," answer Nightingale, "I donot intend to bilk my
lodging; but I have a private reason for not take a formal leave."
"not so private," answer Jones; "I promise you, I have see it ever
since the second day of my come to the house. here will be some wet
eye on your departure. poor Nancy, I pity she, faith! indeed, Jack,
you have play the fool with that girl. you have give she a longing,
which I be afraid nothing will ever cure she of." Nightingale
answer, "what the devil would you have I do? would you have I
marry she to cure she?" "no," answer Jones, "I would not have have
you make love to she, as you have often do in my presence. I have
be astonish at the blindness of her mother in never see it."
"Pugh, see it!" cry Nightingale. "what, the devil should she see?"
"why, see," say Jones, "that you have make her daughter distractedly
in love with you. the poor girl cannot conceal it a moment; her eye
be never off from you, and she always colour every time you come
into the room. indeed, I pity she heartily; for she seem to be one of
the well-natured and honest of human creature." "and so," answer
Nightingale, "accord to your doctrine, one must not amuse oneself
by any common gallantry with woman, for fear they should fall in
love with we." "indeed, Jack," say Jones, "you wilfully misunderstand
I; I do not fancy woman be so apt to fall in love; but you have go
far beyond common gallantry." "what, do you suppose," say
Nightingale, "that we have be a-bed together?" "no, upon my honour,"
answer Jones, very seriously, "I do not suppose so ill of you; nay,
I will go far, I do not imagine you have lay a regular
premeditate scheme for the destruction of the quiet of a poor little
creature, or have even foresee the consequence: for I be sure thou
art a very good-natured fellow; and such a one can never be guilty of
a cruelty of that kind; but at the same time you have please your own
vanity, without consider that this poor girl be make a sacrifice
to it; and while you have have no design but of amuse an idle hour,
you have actually give her reason to flatter herself that you have the
most serious design in her favour. Prithee, Jack, answer I honestly;
to what have tend all those elegant and luscious description of
happiness arise from violent and mutual fondness? all those warm
profession of tenderness, and generous disinterested love? do you
imagine she would not apply they? or, speak ingenuously, do not you
intend she should?" "upon my soul, Tom," cry Nightingale, "I do not
think this be in thee. thou wilt make an admirable parson. so I
suppose you would not go to bed to Nancy now, if she would let you?"
"no," cry Jones, "may I be d--n'd if I would." "Tom, Tom," answer
Nightingale, "last night; remember last night----
when every eye be closed, and the pale moon,
and silent star, shine conscious of the theft."
"Lookee, Mr Nightingale," say Jones, "I be no canting hypocrite, nor
do I pretend to the gift of chastity, more than my neighbour. I have
be guilty with woman, I own it; but be not conscious that I have
ever injure any.--nor would I, to procure pleasure to myself, be
knowingly the cause of misery to any human being."
"well, well," say Nightingale, "I believe you, and I be convinced you
acquit I of any such thing."
"I do, from my heart," answer Jones, "of have debauch the girl,
but not from have gain her affection."
"if I have," say Nightingale, "I be sorry for it; but time and
absence will soon wear off such impression. it be a receipt I must
take myself; for, to confess the truth to you--I never like any girl
half so much in my whole life; but I must let you into the whole
secret, Tom. my father hath provide a match for I with a woman I
never see; and she be now come to town, in order for I to make my
address to she."
at these word Jones burst into a loud fit of laughter; when
Nightingale cried--"nay, prithee, donot turn I into ridicule. the
devil take I if I be not half mad about this matter! my poor Nancy!
oh! Jones, Jones, I wish I have a fortune in my own possession."
"I heartily wish you have," cry Jones; "for, if this be the case, I
sincerely pity you both; but surely you donot intend to go away
without take your leave of she?"
"I would not," answer Nightingale, "undergo the pain of taking
leave, for ten thousand pound; besides, I be convince, instead of
answer any good purpose, it would only serve to inflame my poor
Nancy the more. I beg, therefore, you would not mention a word of it
to-day, and in the evening, or to-morrow morning, I intend to depart."
Jones promise he would not; and say, upon reflection, he think, as
he have determine and be oblige to leave she, he take the most
prudent method. he then tell Nightingale he should be very glad to
lodge in the same house with he; and it be accordingly agree
between they, that Nightingale should procure he either the ground
floor, or the two pair of stair; for the young gentleman himself be
to occupy that which be between they.
this Nightingale, of whom we shall be presently oblige to say a
little more, be in the ordinary transaction of life a man of strict
honour, and, what be more rare among young gentleman of the town, one
of strict honesty too; yet in affair of love he be somewhat loose in
his moral; not that he be even here as void of principle as
gentleman sometimes be, and oftener affect to be; but it be certain
he have be guilty of some indefensible treachery to woman, and have,
in a certain mystery, call make love, practise many deceit,
which, if he have use in trade, he would have be count the
great villain upon earth.
but as the world, I know not well for what reason, agree to see this
treachery in a well light, he be so far from be ashamed of his
iniquity of this kind, that he glory in they, and would often
boast of his skill in gain of woman, and his triumph over their
heart, for which he have before this time receive some rebuke from
Jones, who always expr great bitterness against any misbehaviour to
the fair part of the specie, who, if consider, he say, as they
ought to be, in the light of the dear friend, be to be
cultivate, honour, and caress with the utmost love and
tenderness; but, if regard as enemy, be a conquest of which a
man ought rather to be ashamed than to value himself upon it.
Chapter v.
a short account of the history of Mrs Miller.
Jones this day eat a pretty good dinner for a sick man, that be to
say, the large half of a shoulder of mutton. in the afternoon he
receive an invitation from Mrs Miller to drink tea; for that good
woman, have learn, either by mean of Partridge, or by some other
mean natural or supernatural, that he have a connexion with Mr
Allworthy, could not endure the thought of part with he in an
angry manner.
Jones accept the invitation; and no soon be the tea-kettle
remove, and the girl send out of the room, than the widow, without
much preface, begin as follow: "well, there be very surprizing
thing happen in this world; but certainly it be a wonderful business
that I should have a relation of Mr Allworthy in my house, and never
know anything of the matter. alas! sir, you little imagine what a
friend that good of gentleman hath be to I and mine. yes, sir, I be
not ashamed to own it; it be owe to his goodness that I do not long
since perish for want, and leave my poor little wretch, two
destitute, helpless, friendless orphan, to the care, or rather to the
cruelty, of the world.
"you must know, sir, though I be now reduce to get my living by
let lodging, I be bear and breed a gentlewoman. my father be an
officer of the army, and die in a considerable rank: but he live up
to his pay; and, as that expire with he, his family, at his death,
become beggar. we be three sister. one of we have the good luck to
die soon after of the small-pox; a lady be so kind as to take the
second out of charity, as she say, to wait upon she. the mother of
this lady have be a servant to my grand-mother; and, have inherit
a vast fortune from her father, which he have get by pawnbroke, be
married to a gentleman of great estate and fashion. she use my sister
so barbarously, often upbraid she with her birth and poverty,
call she in derision a gentlewoman, that I believe she at length
break the heart of the poor girl. in short, she likewise die within a
twelvemonth after my father. Fortune think proper to provide well
for I, and within a month from his decease I be married to a
clergyman, who have be my lover a long time before, and who have be
very ill use by my father on that account: for though my poor father
could not give any of we a shilling, yet he breed we up as delicately,
consider we, and would have have we consider ourselves, as highly as
if we have be the rich heiress. but my dear husband forget all
this usage, and the moment we be become fatherless he immediately
renew his address to I so warmly, that I, who always like, and
now more than ever esteem he, soon comply. five year do I live
in a state of perfect happiness with that good of man, till at
last--oh! cruel! cruel fortune, that ever separate we, that deprive
I of the kind of husband and my poor girl of the tender
parent.--O my poor girl! you never know the blessing which ye
lost.--I be ashamed, Mr Jones, of this womanish weakness; but I shall
never mention he without tear." "I ought rather, madam," say Jones,
"to be ashamed that I do not accompany you." "well, sir," continue
she, "I be now leave a second time in a much bad condition than
before; besides the terrible affliction I be to encounter, I have now
two child to provide for; and be, if possible, more pennyless than
ever; when that great, that good, that glorious man, Mr Allworthy, who
have some little acquaintance with my husband, accidentally hear of my
distress, and immediately writ this letter to I. here, sir, here it
be; I put it into my pocket to shew it you. this be the letter, sir; I
must and will read it to you.
"'Madam,
"'I heartily condole with you on your late grievous loss, which your
own good sense, and the excellent lesson you must have learn from
the worthy of man, will well enable you to bear than any advice
which I be capable of give. nor have I any doubt that you, whom I
have hear to be the tender of mother, will suffer any
immoderate indulgence of grief to prevent you from discharge your
duty to those poor infant, who now alone stand in need of your
tenderness.
"`however, as you must be suppose at present to be incapable of
much worldly consideration, you will pardon my have order a
person to wait on you, and to pay you twenty guineas, which I beg
you will accept till I have the pleasure of see you, and believe
I to be, madam, &c.'
"this letter, sir, I receive within a fortnight after the irreparable
loss I have mention; and within a fortnight afterwards, Mr
Allworthy--the blessed Mr Allworthy, come to pay I a visit, when he
place I in the house where you now see I, give I a large sum of
money to furnish it, and settle an annuity of £50 a-year upon I,
which I have constantly receive ever since. Judge, then, Mr Jones, in
what regard I must hold a benefactor, to whom I owe the preservation
of my life, and of those dear child, for whose sake alone my life
be valuable. do not, therefore, think I impertinent, Mr Jones (since
I must esteem one for whom I know Mr Allworthy hath so much value), if
I beg you not to converse with these wicked woman. you be a young
gentleman, and do not know half their artful wile. do not be angry
with I, sir, for what I say upon account of my house; you must be
sensible it would be the ruin of my poor dear girl. besides, sir, you
cannot but be acquaint that Mr Allworthy himself would never forgive
my conniving at such matter, and particularly with you."
"upon my word, madam," say Jones, "you need make no farth apology;
nor do I in the least take anything ill you have say; but give I
leave, as no one can have more value than myself for Mr Allworthy, to
deliver you from one mistake, which, perhaps, would not be altogether
for his honour; I do assure you, I be no relation of his."
"alas! sir," answer she, "I know you be not, I know very well who
you be; for Mr Allworthy hath tell I all; but I do assure you, have
you be twenty time his son, he could not have express more regard
for you than he hath often express in my presence. you need not be
ashamed, sir, of what you be; I promise you no good person will
esteem you the less on that account. no, Mr Jones, the word
`dishonourable birth' be nonsense, as my dear, dear husband use to
say, unless the word `dishonourable' be apply to the parent; for
the child can derive no real dishonour from an act of which they
be intirely innocent."
here Jones heave a deep sigh, and then say, "since I perceive,
madam, you really do know I, and Mr Allworthy hath think proper to
mention my name to you; and since you have be so explicit with I as
to your own affair, I will acquaint you with some more circumstance
concern myself." and these Mrs Miller have express great desire
and curiosity to hear, he begin and relate to she his whole history,
without once mention the name of Sophia.
there be a kind of sympathy in honest mind, by mean of which they
give an easy credit to each other. Mrs Miller believe all which Jones
tell she to be true, and expr much pity and concern for he. she
be begin to comment on the story, but Jones interrupt she; for,
as the hour of assignation now draw nigh, he begin to stipulate for a
second interview with the lady that evening, which he promise should
be the last at her house; swearing, at the same time, that she be one
of great distinction, and that nothing but what be intirely innocent
be to pass between they; and I do firmly believe he intend to keep
his word.
Mrs Miller be at length prevail on, and Jones depart to his
chamber, where he sit alone till twelve o'clock, but no Lady Bellaston
appear.
as we have say that this lady have a great affection for Jones, and as
it must have appear that she really have so, the reader may perhaps
wonder at the first failure of her appointment, as she apprehend he
to be confine by sickness, a season when friendship seem most to
require such visit. this behaviour, therefore, in the lady, may, by
some, be condemn as unnatural; but that be not our fault; for our
business be only to record truth.
Chapter vi.
contain a scene which we doubt not will affect all our reader.
Mr Jones close not his eye during all the former part of the night;
not owe to any uneasiness which he conceive at be disappoint
by Lady Bellaston; nor be Sophia herself, though most of his waking
hour be justly to be charge to her account, the present cause of
dispel his slumber. in fact, poor Jones be one of the
well-natured fellow alive, and have all that weakness which be call
compassion, and which distinguish this imperfect character from that
noble firmness of mind, which roll a man, as it be, within himself,
and like a polished bowl, enable he to run through the world without
be once stop by the calamity which happen to other. he could
not help, therefore, compassionate the situation of poor Nancy,
whose love for Mr Nightingale seem to he so apparent, that he be
astonish at the blindness of her mother, who have more than once, the
precede evening, remark to he the great change in the temper of
her daughter, "who from be," she say, "one of the lively,
merry girl in the world, be, on a sudden, become all gloom and
melancholy."
Sleep, however, at length get the well of all resistance; and now,
as if he have already be a deity, as the antient imagine, and an
offend one too, he seem to enjoy his dear-buy conquest.--to
speak simply, and without any metaphor, Mr Jones sleep till eleven the
next morning, and would, perhaps, have continue in the same quiet
situation much long, have not a violent uproar awaken he.
Partridge be now summon, who, be ask what be the matter,
answer, "that there be a dreadful hurricane below-stair; that
Miss Nancy be in fit; and that the other sister, and the mother,
be both cry and lament over she." Jones express much
concern at this news; which Partridge endeavour to relieve, by
say, with a smile, "he fancy the young lady be in no danger of
death; for that Susan" (which be the name of the maid) "have give
he to understand, it be nothing more than a common affair. in
short," say he, "Miss Nancy hath have a mind to be as wise as her
mother; thatbe all; she be a little hungry, it seem, and so sit
down to dinner before grace be say; and so there be a child come
for the Foundling Hospital."----"prithee, leave thy stupid jesting,"
cry Jones. "be the misery of these poor wretch a subject of
mirth? go immediately to Mrs Miller, and tell she I beg leave--stay,
you will make some blunder; I will go myself; for she desire I to
breakfast with she." he then rise and dress himself as fast as he
could; and while he be dress, Partridge, notwithstanding many
severe rebuke, could not avoid throw forth certain piece of
brutality, commonly call jest, on this occasion. Jones be no
soon dressed than he walk downstairs, and knock at the door,
be presently admit by the maid, into the outward parlour, which
be as empty of company as it be of any apparatus for eat. Mrs
Miller be in the inner room with her daughter, whence the maid
presently bring a message to Mr Jones, "that her mistress hop he
would excuse the disappointment, but an accident have happen, which
make it impossible for she to have the pleasure of his company at
breakfast that day; and beg his pardon for not send he up
notice soon." Jones desire, "she would give herself no trouble
about anything so trifle as his disappointment; that he be
heartily sorry for the occasion; and that if he could be of any
service to she, she might command he."
he have scarce speak these word, when Mrs Miller, who hear they all,
suddenly throw open the door, and come out to he, in a flood of
tear, say, "o Mr Jones! you be certainly one of the good young man
alive. I give you a thousand thank for your kind offer of your
service; but, alas! sir, it be out of your power to preserve my poor
girl.--O my child! my child! she be undo, she be ruin for ever!"
"I hope, madam," say Jones, "no villain"----"O Mr Jones!" say she,
"that villain who yesterday leave my lodging, hath betray my poor
girl; hath destroy her.--I know you be a man of honour. you have a
good--a noble heart, Mr Jones. the action to which I have be myself
a witness, could proceed from no other. I will tell you all: nay,
indeed, it be impossible, after what hath happen, to keep it a
secret. that Nightingale, that barbarous villain, hath undo my
daughter. she be--she be--oh! Mr Jones, my girl be with child by he;
and in that condition he hath desert she. here! here, sir, be his
cruel letter: read it, Mr Jones, and tell I if such another monster
life."
the letter be as follow:
"DEAR NANCY,
"as I find it impossible to mention to you what, I be afraid, will
be no less shocking to you, than it be to I, I have take this
method to inform you, that my father insist upon my immediately
pay my address to a young lady of fortune, whom he hath
provide for my--I need not write the detest word. your own good
understanding will make you sensible, how entirely I be oblige to
an obedience, by which I shall be for ever exclude from your dear
arm. the fondness of your mother may encourage you to trust she
with the unhappy consequence of our love, which may be easily keep a
secret from the world, and for which I will take care to provide, as
I will for you. I wish you may feel less on this account than I have
suffer; but summon all your fortitude to your assistance, and
forgive and forget the man, whom nothing but the prospect of certain
ruin could have force to write this letter. I bid you forget I, I
mean only as a lover; but the good of friend you shall ever find in
your faithful, though unhappy,
"J. N."
when Jones have read this letter, they both stand silent during a
minute, look at each other; at last he begin thus: "I cannot
express, madam, how much I be shock at what I have read; yet let I
beg you, in one particular, to take the writer's advice. consider the
reputation of your daughter."----"It be go, it be lose, Mr Jones,"
cry she, "as well as her innocence. she receive the letter in a
room full of company, and immediately swoon away upon open it,
the content be know to every one present. but the loss of her
reputation, bad as it be, be not the bad; I shall lose my child; she
hath attempt twice to destroy herself already; and though she hath
be hitherto prevent, vow she will not outlive it; nor could I
myself outlive any accident of that nature.--what then will become of
my little Betsy, a helpless infant orphan? and the poor little wretch
will, I believe, break her heart at the misery with which she see
her sister and myself distract, while she be ignorant of the cause.
o 'tis the most sensible, and good-natured little thing! the
barbarous, cruel----hath destroy we all. o my poor child! be this
the reward of all my care? be this the fruit of all my prospect?
have I so chearfully undergo all the labour and duty of a mother?
have I be so tender of their infancy, so careful of their education?
have I be toil so many year, deny myself even the
convenience of life, to provide some little sustenance for they, to
lose one or both in such a manner?" "indeed, madam," say Jones, with
tear in his eye, "I pity you from my soul."--"O! Mr Jones," answer
she, "even you, though I know the goodness of your heart, can have no
idea of what I feel. the good, the kind, the most dutiful of
child! o my poor Nancy, the darling of my soul! the delight of my
eye! the pride of my heart! too much, indeed, my pride; for to those
foolish, ambitious hope, arise from her beauty, I owe her ruin.
alas! I see with pleasure the liking which this young man have for she.
I think it an honourable affection; and flatter my foolish vanity
with the thought of see she married to one so much her superior.
and a thousand time in my presence, nay, often in your, he hath
endeavour to soothe and encourage these hope by the most generous
expression of disinterested love, which he hath always direct to my
poor girl, and which I, as well as she, believe to be real. could I
have believe that these be only snare lay to betray the innocence
of my child, and for the ruin of we all?"--At these word little Betsy
come run into the room, cry, "dear mamma, for heaven's sake
come to my sister; for she be in another fit, and my cousin cannot hold
she." Mrs Miller immediately obey the summon; but first order
Betsy to stay with Mr Jones, and beg he to entertain she a few
minute, say, in the most pathetic voice, "good heaven! let I
preserve one of my child at least."
Jones, in compliance with this request, do all he could to comfort
the little girl, though he be, in reality, himself very highly
affect with Mrs Miller's story. he tell she "her sister would be
soon very well again; that by take on in that manner she would not
only make her sister bad, but make her mother ill too." "indeed,
sir," say she, "I would not do anything to hurt they for the world. I
would burst my heart rather than they should see I cry.--but my poor
sister cannot see I cry.--I am afraid she will never be able to see I
cry any more. indeed, I cannot part with she; indeed, I can't.--and
then poor mamma too, what will become of her?--She say she will die
too, and leave I: but I be resolve I willnot be leave behind." "and be
you not afraid to die, my little Betsy?" say Jones. "yes," answer
she, "I be always afraid to die; because I must have leave my mamma,
and my sister; but I be not afraid of go anywhere with those I
love."
Jones be so pleased with this answer, that he eagerly kiss the
child; and soon after Mrs Miller return, say, "she thank heaven
Nancy be now come to herself. and now, Betsy," say she, "you may go
in, for your sister be well, and long to see you." she then turn
to Jones, and begin to renew her apology for have disappoint he
of his breakfast.
"I hope, madam," say Jones, "I shall have a more exquisite repast
than any you could have provide for I. this, I assure you, will be
the case, if I can do any service to this little family of love. but
whatever success may attend my endeavour, I be resolve to attempt
it. I be very much deceive in Mr Nightingale, if, notwithstanding
what hath happen, he hath not much goodness of heart at the bottom,
as well as a very violent affection for your daughter. if this be the
case, I think the picture which I shall lay before he will affect
he. Endeavour, madam, to comfort yourself, and Miss Nancy, as well as
you can. I will go instantly in quest of Mr Nightingale; and I hope to
bring you good news."
Mrs Miller fall upon her knee and invoke all the blessing of heaven
upon Mr Jones; to which she afterwards add the most passionate
expression of gratitude. he then depart to find Mr Nightingale, and
the good woman return to comfort her daughter, who be somewhat
cheare at what her mother tell she; and both join in resound the
praise of Mr Jones.
Chapter vii.
the interview between Mr Jones and Mr Nightingale.
the good or evil we confer on other very often, I believe, recoil on
ourselves. for as man of a benign disposition enjoy their own act of
beneficence equally with those to whom they be do, so there be
scarce any nature so entirely diabolical, as to be capable of do
injury, without pay themselves some pang for the ruin which they
bring on their fellow-creature.
Mr Nightingale, at least, be not such a person. on the contrary,
Jones find he in his new lodging, sit melancholy by the fire,
and silently lament the unhappy situation in which he have place
poor Nancy. he no soon see his friend appear than he arise hastily
to meet he; and after much congratulation say, "nothing could be
more opportune than this kind visit; for I be never more in the
spleen in my life."
"I be sorry," answer Jones, "that I bring news very unlikely to
relieve you: nay, what I be convinced must, of all other, shock you
the most. however, it be necessary you should know it. without further
preface, then, I come to you, Mr Nightingale, from a worthy family,
which you have involve in misery and ruin." Mr Nightingale change
colour at these word; but Jones, without regard it, proceed, in
the lively manner, to paint the tragical story with which the
reader be acquaint in the last chapter.
Nightingale never once interrupt the narration, though he discover
violent emotion at many part of it. but when it be conclude, after
fetch a deep sigh, he say, "what you tell I, my friend, affect
I in the tender manner. sure there never be so cursed an accident
as the poor girl's betray my letter. her reputation might otherwise
have be safe, and the affair might have remain a profound secret;
and then the girl might have go off never the bad; for many such
thing happen in this town: and if the husband should suspect a
little, when it be too late, it will be his wise conduct to conceal
his suspicion both from his wife and the world."
"indeed, my friend," answer Jones, "this could not have be the
case with your poor Nancy. you have so entirely gain her affection,
that it be the loss of you, and not of her reputation, which afflict
she, and will end in the destruction of she and her family." "Nay, for
that matter, I promise you," cry Nightingale, "she hath my
affection so absolutely, that my wife, whoever she be to be, will
have very little share in they." "and be it possible then," say
Jones, "you can think of desert she?" "why, what can I do?"
answer the other. "ask Miss Nancy," reply Jones warmly. "in the
condition to which you have reduce she, I sincerely think she ought
to determine what reparation you shall make she. her interest alone,
and not your, ought to be your sole consideration. but if you ask I
what you shall do, what can you do less," cry Jones, "than fulfil
the expectation of her family, and her own? Nay, I sincerely tell
you, they be mine too, ever since I first see you together. you will
pardon I if I presume on the friendship you have favour I with,
move as I be with compassion for those poor creature. but your own
heart will well suggest to you, whether you have never intend, by
your conduct, to persuade the mother, as well as the daughter, into an
opinion, that you design honourably: and if so, though there may
have be no direct promise of marriage in the case, I will leave to
your own good understanding, how far you be bind to proceed."
"Nay, I must not only confess what you have hint," say Nightingale;
"but I be afraid even that very promise you mention I have give."
"and can you, after own that," say Jones, "hesitate a moment?"
"consider, my friend," answer the other; "I know you be a man of
honour, and would advise no one to act contrary to its rule; if there
be no other objection, can I, after this publication of she
disgrace, think of such an alliance with honour?" "undoubtedly,"
reply Jones, "and the very good and true honour, which be
goodness, require it of you. as you mention a scruple of this kind,
you will give I leave to examine it. can you with honour be guilty of
have under false pretence deceive a young woman and her family,
and of have by these mean treacherously rob she of her
innocence? can you, with honour, be the knowing, the wilful occasion,
nay, the artful contriver of the ruin of a human being? can you, with
honour, destroy the fame, the peace, nay, probably, both the life and
soul too, of this creature? can honour bear the thought, that this
creature be a tender, helpless, defenceless, young woman? a young
woman, who love, who doat on you, who die for you; who hath place
the utmost confidence in your promise; and to that confidence hath
sacrifice everything which be dear to she? can honour support such
contemplation as these a moment?"
"common sense, indeed," say Nightingale, "warrant all you say; but
yet you well know the opinion of the world be so contrary to it, that,
be I to marry a whore, though my own, I should be ashamed of ever
show my face again."
"fie upon it, Mr Nightingale!" say Jones, "do not call she by so
ungenerous a name: when you promise to marry she she become your
wife; and she hath sin more against prudence than virtue. and what
be this world which you would be ashamed to face but the vile, the
foolish, and the profligate? forgive I if I say such a shame must
proceed from false modesty, which always attend false honour as its
shadow.--But I be well assured there be not a man of real sense and
goodness in the world who would not honour and applaud the action.
but, admit no other would, would not your own heart, my friend,
applaud it? and do not the warm, rapturous sensation, which we feel
from the consciousness of an honest, noble, generous, benevolent
action, convey more delight to the mind than the undeserved praise of
million? set the alternative fairly before your eye. on the one
side, see this poor, unhappy, tender, believe girl, in the arm of
her wretched mother, breathe she last. hear her break heart in
agony, sigh out your name; and lamenting, rather than accuse,
the cruelty which weigh she down to destruction. paint to your
imagination the circumstance of her fond despairing parent, drive to
madness, or, perhaps, to death, by the loss of her lovely daughter.
view the poor, helpless, orphan infant; and when your mind hath dwelt
a moment only on such idea, consider yourself as the cause of all the
ruin of this poor, little, worthy, defenceless family. on the other
side, consider yourself as relieve they from their temporary
suffering. think with what joy, with what transport that lovely
creature will fly to your arm. see her blood return to her pale
cheek, her fire to her languid eye, and rapture to her tortured
breast. consider the exultation of her mother, the happiness of all.
think of this little family make by one act of yours completely happy.
think of this alternative, and sure I be mistaken in my friend if it
require any long deliberation whether he will sink these wretch
down for ever, or, by one generous, noble resolution, raise they all
from the brink of misery and despair to the high pitch of human
happiness. add to this but one consideration more; the consideration
that it be your duty so to do--that the misery from which you will
relieve these poor people be the misery which you yourself have
wilfully bring upon they."
"o, my dear friend!" cry Nightingale, "I want not your eloquence
to rouse I. I pity poor Nancy from my soul, and would willingly give
anything in my power that no familiarity have ever pass between we.
Nay, believe I, I have many struggle with my passion before I could
prevail with myself to write that cruel letter, which hath cause all
the misery in that unhappy family. if I have no inclination to consult
but my own, I would marry she to-morrow morning: I would, by heaven!
but you will easily imagine how impossible it would be to prevail on
my father to consent to such a match; besides, he hath provide
another for I; and to-morrow, by his express command, I be to wait on
the lady."
"I have not the honour to know your father," say Jones; "but, suppose
he could be persuade, would you yourself consent to the only mean of
preserve these poor people?" "as eagerly as I would pursue my
happiness," answer Nightingale: "for I never shall find it in any
other woman.--O, my dear friend! could you imagine what I have feel
within these twelve hour for my poor girl, I be convinced she would
not engross all your pity. passion lead I only to she; and, if I have
any foolish scruple of honour, you have fully satisfy they: could
my father be induce to comply with my desire, nothing would be
want to compleat my own happiness or that of my Nancy."
"then I be resolve to undertake it," say Jones. "you must not be
angry with I, in whatever light it may be necessary to set this
affair, which, you may depend on it, could not otherwise be long hid
from he: for thing of this nature make a quick progress when once
they get abroad, as this unhappily hath already. besides, should any
fatal accident follow, as upon my soul I be afraid will, unless
immediately prevent, the public would ring of your name in a manner
which, if your father hath common humanity, must offend he. if you
will therefore tell I where I may find the old gentleman, I will not
lose a moment in the business; which, while I pursue, you cannot do a
more generous action than by pay a visit to the poor girl. you will
find I have not exaggerate in the account I have give of the
wretchedness of the family."
Nightingale immediately consent to the proposal; and now, have
acquaint Jones with his father's lodging, and the coffee-house where
he would most probably find he, he hesitate a moment, and then say,
"my dear Tom, you be go to undertake an impossibility. if you know
my father you would never think of obtain his consent.----Stay,
there be one way--suppose you tell he I be already marry, it might
be easy to reconcile he to the fact after it be do; and, upon my
honour, I be so affected with what you have say, and I love my Nancy
so passionately, I almost wish it be do, whatever might be the
consequence."
Jones greatly approve the hint, and promise to pursue it. they then
separate, Nightingale to visit his Nancy, and Jones in quest of the
old gentleman.
Chapter viii.
what pass between Jones and old Mr Nightingale; with the arrival of
a person not yet mention in this history.
notwithstanding the sentiment of the roman satirist, which deny the
divinity of fortune, and the opinion of Seneca to the same purpose;
Cicero, who be, I believe, a wise man than either of they, expressly
hold the contrary; and certain it be, there be some incident in
life so very strange and unaccountable, that it seem to require more
than human skill and foresight in produce they.
of this kind be what now happen to Jones, who find Mr Nightingale
the elder in so critical a minute, that Fortune, if she be really
worthy all the worship she receive at Rome, could not have contrive
such another. in short, the old gentleman, and the father of the young
lady whom he intend for his son, have be hard at it for many hour;
and the latter be just now go, and have leave the former delight
with the thought that he have succeed in a long contention, which
have be between the two father of the future bride and bridegroom;
in which both endeavour to overreach the other, and, as it not
rarely happen in such case, both have retreat fully satisfied of
have obtain the victory.
this gentleman, whom Mr Jones now visit, be what they call a man of
the world; that be to say, a man who direct his conduct in this world
as one who, be fully persuade there be no other, be resolve to
make the most of this. in his early year he have be breed to trade;
but, have acquire a very good fortune, he have lately decline his
business; or, to speak more properly, have change it from deal in
good, to deal only in money, of which he have always a plentiful
fund at command, and of which he know very well how to make a very
plentiful advantage, sometimes of the necessity of private man, and
sometimes of those of the public. he have indeed converse so entirely
with money, that it may be almost doubted whether he imagine there
be any other thing really exist in the world; this at least may be
certainly aver, that he firmly believe nothing else to have any
real value.
the reader will, I fancy, allow that Fortune could not have cull out
a more improper person for Mr Jones to attack with any probability of
success; nor could the whimsical lady have direct this attack at a
more unseasonable time.
as money then be always uppermost in this gentleman's thought, so
the moment he see a stranger within his door it immediately occur
to his imagination, that such stranger be either come to bring he
money, or to fetch it from he. and accord as one or other of these
thought prevail, he conceive a favourable or unfavourable idea of
the person who approach he.
Unluckily for Jones, the latter of these be the ascendant at present;
for as a young gentleman have visit he the day before, with a bill
from his son for a play debt, he apprehend, at the first sight of
Jones, that he be come on such another errand. Jones therefore have no
soon tell he that he be come on his son's account than the old
gentleman, be confirm in his suspicion, burst forth into an
exclamation, "that he would lose his labour." "be it then possible,
sir," answer Jones, "that you can guess my business?" "if I do guess
it," reply the other, "I repeat again to you, you will lose your
labour. what, I suppose you be one of those spark who lead my son
into all those scene of riot and debauchery, which will be his
destruction? but I shall pay no more of his bill, I promise you. I
expect he will quit all such company for the future. if I have imagine
otherwise, I should not have provide a wife for he; for I would be
instrumental in the ruin of nobody." "how, sir," say Jones, "and be
this lady of your providing?" "pray, sir," answer the old gentleman,
"how come it to be any concern of yours?"--"Nay, dear sir," reply
Jones, "be not offend that I interest myself in what regard your
son's happiness, for whom I have so great an honour and value. it be
upon that very account I come to wait upon you. I cannot express the
satisfaction you have give I by what you say; for I do assure you
your son be a person for whom I have the high honour.--Nay, sir, it
be not easy to express the esteem I have for you; who could be so
generous, so good, so kind, so indulgent to provide such a match for
your son; a woman, who, I dare swear, will make he one of the
happy man upon earth."
there be scarce anything which so happily introduce man to our good
liking, as have conceive some alarm at their first appearance; when
once those apprehension begin to vanish we soon forget the fear
which they occasion, and look on ourselves as indebted for our
present ease to those very person who at first raise our fear.
thus it happen to Nightingale, who no soon find that Jones have no
demand on he, as he suspect, than he begin to be pleased with his
presence. "pray, good sir," say he, "be pleased to sit down. I do not
remember to have ever have the pleasure of see you before; but if
you be a friend of my son, and have anything to say concern this
young lady, I shall be glad to hear you. as to she make he happy,
it will be his own fault if she doth not. I have discharge my duty,
in take care of the main article. she will bring he a fortune
capable of make any reasonable, prudent, sober man, happy."
"undoubtedly," cry Jones, "for she be in herself a fortune; so
beautiful, so genteel, so sweet-temper, and so well-educate; she be
indeed a most accomplished young lady; sing admirably well, and hath
a most delicate hand at the harpsichord." "I do not know any of these
matter," answer the old gentleman, "for I never see the lady: but I
do not like she the bad for what you tell I; and I be the well
pleased with her father for not lay any stress on these
qualification in our bargain. I shall always think it a proof of his
understanding. a silly fellow would have bring in these article as
an addition to her fortune; but, to give he his due, he never
mention any such matter; though to be sure they be no
disparagement to a woman." "I do assure you, sir," cry Jones, "she
hath they all in the most eminent degree: for my part, I own I be
afraid you might have be a little backward, a little less inclined
to the match; for your son tell I you have never see the lady;
therefore I come, sir, in that case, to entreat you, to conjure you,
as you value the happiness of your son, not to be averse to his match
with a woman who hath not only all the good quality I have
mention, but many more."--"If that be your business, sir," say the
old gentleman, "we be both oblige to you; and you may be perfectly
easy; for I give you my word I be very well satisfied with her
fortune." "Sir," answer Jones, "I honour you every moment more and
more. to be so easily satisfied, so very moderate on that account, be
a proof of the soundness of your understanding, as well as the
nobleness of your mind."----"not so very moderate, young gentleman,
not so very moderate," answer the father.--"still more and more
noble," reply Jones; "and give I leave to add, sensible: for sure
it be little less than madness to consider money as the sole
foundation of happiness. such a woman as this with her little, her
nothing of a fortune"--"i find," cry the old gentleman, "you have a
pretty just opinion of money, my friend, or else you be well
acquaint with the person of the lady than with her circumstance.
why, pray, what fortune do you imagine this lady to have?" "what
fortune?" cry Jones, "why, too contemptible a one to be name for
your son."--"Well, well, well," say the other, "perhaps he might have
do well."--"That I deny," say Jones, "for she be one of the good
of woman."--"Ay, ay, but in point of fortune I mean," answer the
other. "and yet, as to that now, how much do you imagine your friend
be to have?"--"how much?" cry Jones, "how much? why, at the utmost,
perhaps £200." "do you mean to banter I, young gentleman?" say the
father, a little angry. "no, upon my soul," answer Jones, "I be in
earnest: nay, I believe I have go to the utmost farthing. if I do
the lady an injury, I ask her pardon." "indeed you do," cry the
father; "I be certain she hath fifty time that sum, and she shall
produce fifty to that before I consent that she shall marry my son."
"Nay," say Jones, "it be too late to talk of consent now; if she have
not fifty farthing your son be marry."--"My son marry!" answer
the old gentleman, with surprize. "Nay," say Jones, "I think you
be unacquainted with it." "my son marry to Miss Harris!" answer
he again. "to Miss Harris!" say Jones; "no, sir; to Miss Nancy
Miller, the daughter of Mrs Miller, at whose house he lodge; a young
lady, who, though her mother be reduce to let lodgings--"--"Are you
bantering, or be you in earnest?" cry the father, with a most
solemn voice. "indeed, sir," answer Jones, "I scorn the character of
a banterer. I come to you in most serious earnest, imagine, as I
find true, that your son have never dare acquaint you with a match so
much inferior to he in point of fortune, though the reputation of the
lady will suffer it no long to remain a secret."
while the father stand like one strike suddenly dumb at this news, a
gentleman come into the room, and salute he by the name of brother.
but though these two be in consanguinity so nearly related, they
be in their disposition almost the opposite to each other. the
brother who now arrive have likewise be breed to trade, in which he
no soon see himself worth £6000 than he purchase a small estate
with the great part of it, and retire into the country; where he
marry the daughter of an unbeneficed clergyman; a young lady, who,
though she have neither beauty nor fortune, have recommend herself to
his choice entirely by her good humour, of which she possess a very
large share.
with this woman he have, during twenty-five year, live a life more
resemble the model which certain poet ascribe to the golden age,
than any of those pattern which be furnish by the present time.
by she he have four child, but none of they arrive at maturity,
except only one daughter, whom, in vulgar language, he and his wife
have spoil; that is, have educate with the utmost tenderness and
fondness, which she return to such a degree, that she have actually
refuse a very extraordinary match with a gentleman a little turn of
forty, because she could not bring herself to part with her parent.
the young lady whom Mr Nightingale have intend for his son be a near
neighbour of his brother, and an acquaintance of his niece; and in
reality it be upon the account of his project match that he be now
come to town; not, indeed, to forward, but to dissuade his brother
from a purpose which he conceive would inevitably ruin his nephew;
for he foresaw no other event from a union with Miss Harris,
notwithstanding the largeness of her fortune, as neither her person
nor mind seem to he to promise any kind of matrimonial felicity:
for she be very tall, very thin, very ugly, very affected, very
silly, and very ill-natured.
his brother, therefore, no soon mention the marriage of his nephew
with Miss Miller, than he expr the utmost satisfaction; and when
the father have very bitterly revile his son, and pronounce sentence
of beggary upon he, the uncle begin in the following manner:
"if you be a little cool, brother, I would ask you whether you love
your son for his sake or for your own. you would answer, I suppose,
and so I suppose you think, for his sake; and doubtless it be his
happiness which you intend in the marriage you propose for he.
"now, brother, to prescribe rule of happiness to other hath always
appear to I very absurd, and to insist on do this, very
tyrannical. it be a vulgar error, I know; but it be, nevertheless, an
error. and if this be absurd in other thing, it be mostly so in the
affair of marriage, the happiness of which depend entirely on the
affection which subsist between the party.
"I have therefore always think it unreasonable in parent to desire
to chuse for their child on this occasion; since to force affection
be an impossible attempt; nay, so much doth love abhor force, that I
know not whether, through an unfortunate but uncurable perverseness in
our nature, it may not be even impatient of persuasion.
"it be, however, true that, though a parent will not, I think, wisely
prescribe, he ought to be consult on this occasion; and, in
strictness, perhaps, should at least have a negative voice. my nephew,
therefore, I own, in marrying, without ask your advice, hath be
guilty of a fault. but, honestly speak, brother, have you not a
little promote this fault? have not your frequent declaration on
this subject give he a moral certainty of your refusal, where there
be any deficiency in point of fortune? Nay, doth not your present
anger arise solely from that deficiency? and if he hath fail in his
duty here, do you not as much exceed that authority when you
absolutely bargain with he for a woman, without his knowledge, whom
you yourself never see, and whom, if you have see and know as well as
I, it must have be madness in you to have ever think of bring
she into your family?
"still I own my nephew in a fault; but surely it be not an
unpardonable fault. he hath act indeed without your consent, in a
matter in which he ought to have ask it, but it be in a matter in
which his interest be principally concerned; you yourself must and
will acknowledge that you consult his interest only, and if he
unfortunately differed from you, and hath be mistaken in his notion
of happiness, will you, brother, if you love your son, carry he still
wide from the point? will you increase the ill consequence of his
simple choice? will you endeavour to make an event certain misery to
he, which may accidentally prove so? in a word, brother, because he
hath put it out of your power to make his circumstance as affluent as
you would, will you distress they as much as you can?"
by the force of the true catholic faith St Anthony win upon the
fish. Orpheus and Amphion go a little far, and by the charm
of music enchant thing merely inanimate. wonderful, both! but
neither history nor fable have ever yet venture to record an instance
of any one, who, by force of argument and reason, hath triumph over
habitual avarice.
Mr Nightingale, the father, instead of attempt to answer his
brother, content himself with only observe, that they have always
differ in their sentiment concern the education of their
child. "I wish," say he, "brother, you would have confine your
care to your own daughter, and never have trouble yourself with my
son, who hath, I believe, as little profit by your precept, as by
your example." for young Nightingale be his uncle's godson, and have
live more with he than with his father. so that the uncle have often
declare he love his nephew almost equally with his own child.
Jones fall into rapture with this good gentleman; and when, after
much persuasion, they find the father grow still more and more
irritated, instead of appeased, Jones conduct the uncle to his
nephew at the house of Mrs Miller.
Chapter ix.
contain strange matter.
at his return to his lodging, Jones find the situation of affair
greatly alter from what they have be in at his departure. the
mother, the two daughter, and young Mr Nightingale, be now sit down
to supper together, when the uncle be, at his own desire, introduce
without any ceremony into the company, to all of whom he be well
know; for he have several time visit his nephew at that house.
the old gentleman immediately walk up to Miss Nancy, salute and
wish her joy, as he do afterwards the mother and the other sister;
and lastly, he pay the proper compliment to his nephew, with the
same good humour and courtesy, as if his nephew have marry his equal
or superior in fortune, with all the previous requisite first
perform.
Miss Nancy and her suppose husband both turn pale, and look
rather foolish than otherwise upon the occasion; but Mrs Miller take
the first opportunity of withdraw; and, have send for Jones into
the dining-room, she throw herself at his foot, and in a most
passionate flood of tear, call he her good angel, the preserver of
her poor little family, with many other respectful and endear
appellation, and make he every acknowledgment which the high
benefit can extract from the most grateful heart.
after the first gust of her passion be a little over, which she
declare, if she have not vent, would have burst she, she proceed
to inform Mr Jones that all matter be settle between Mr
Nightingale and her daughter, and that they be to be marry the
next morning; at which Mr Jones have express much pleasure, the
poor woman fall again into a fit of joy and thanksgiving, which he at
length with difficulty silence, and prevail on she to return with
he back to the company, whom they find in the same good humour in
which they have leave they.
this little society now past two or three very agreeable hour
together, in which the uncle, who be a very great lover of his
bottle, have so well ply his nephew, that this latter, though not
drunk, begin to be somewhat fluster; and now Mr Nightingale, take
the old gentleman with he upstairs into the apartment he have lately
occupy, unbosomed himself as follows:--
"as you have be always the good and kind of uncle to I, and as
you have show such unparalleled goodness in forgive this match,
which to be sure may be think a little improvident, I should never
forgive myself if I attempt to deceive you in anything." he then
confess the truth, and open the whole affair.
"how, Jack?" say the old gentleman, "and be you really then not
married to this young woman?" "no, upon my honour," answer
Nightingale, "I have tell you the simple truth." "my dear boy," cry
the uncle, kiss he, "I be heartily glad to hear it. I never be
well pleased in my life. if you have be marry I should have
assist you as much as be in my power to have make the good of a bad
matter; but there be a great difference between consider a thing
which be already do and irrecoverable, and that which be yet to do.
let your reason have fair play, Jack, and you will see this match in
so foolish and preposterous a light, that there will be no need of any
dissuasive argument." "how, sir?" reply young Nightingale, "be
there this difference between have already do an act, and be in
