honour engage to do it?" "Pugh!" say the uncle, "honour be a
creature of the world's making, and the world hath the power of a
creator over it, and may govern and direct it as they please. now you
well know how trivial these breach of contract be think; even the
gross make but the wonder and conversation of a day. be there a man
who afterwards will be more backward in give you his sister, or
daughter? or be there any sister or daughter who would be more
backward to receive you? Honour be not concerned in these
engagement." "pardon I, dear sir," cry Nightingale, "I can never
think so; and not only honour, but conscience and humanity, be
concerned. I be well satisfied, that, be I now to disappoint the
young creature, her death would be the consequence, and I should look
upon myself as her murderer; nay, as her murderer by the cruell of
all method, by break her heart." "break her heart, indeed! no, no,
Jack," cry the uncle, "the heart of woman be not so soon break;
they be tough, boy, they be tough." "but, sir," answer
Nightingale, "my own affection be engage, and I never could be
happy with any other woman. how often have I hear you say, that
child should be always suffer to chuse for themselves, and that
you would let my cousin Harriet do so?" "why, ay," reply the old
gentleman, "so I would have they; but then I would have they chuse
wisely.--Indeed, Jack, you must and shall leave the girl."----"Indeed,
uncle," cry the other, "I must and will have she." "you will, young
gentleman;" say the uncle; "I do not expect such a word from you. I
should not wonder if you have use such language to your father, who
hath always treat you like a dog, and keep you at the distance which
a tyrant preserve over his subject; but I, who have live with you
upon an equal footing, might surely expect well usage: but I know
how to account for it all: it be all owe to your preposterous
education, in which I have have too little share. there be my daughter,
now, whom I have bring up as my friend, never doth anything without
my advice, nor ever refuse to take it when I give it she." "you have
never yet give her advice in an affair of this kind," say
Nightingale; "for I be greatly mistaken in my cousin, if she would be
very ready to obey even your most positive command in abandon she
inclination." "donot abuse my girl," answer the old gentleman with
some emotion; "donot abuse my Harriet. I have bring she up to have
no inclination contrary to my own. by suffer she to do whatever
she please, I have enure she to a habit of be pleased to do
whatever I like." "Pardon, I, sir," say Nightingale, "I have not the
least design to reflect on my cousin, for whom I have the great
esteem; and indeed I be convinced you will never put she to so severe
a tryal, or lie such hard command on she as you would do on me.--But,
dear sir, let we return to the company; for they will begin to be
uneasy at our long absence. I must beg one favour of my dear uncle,
which be that he would not say anything to shock the poor girl or her
mother." "oh! you need not fear I," answer he, "I understand myself
too well to affront woman; so I will readily grant you that favour;
and in return I must expect another of you." "there be but few of
your command, sir," say Nightingale, "which I shall not very
chearfully obey." "Nay, sir, I ask nothing," say the uncle, "but the
honour of your company home to my lodging, that I may reason the case
a little more fully with you; for I would, if possible, have the
satisfaction of preserve my family, notwithstanding the headstrong
folly of my brother, who, in his own opinion, be the wise man in the
world."
Nightingale, who well know his uncle to be as headstrong as his
father, submit to attend he home, and then they both return back
into the room, where the old gentleman promise to carry himself with
the same decorum which he have before maintain.
Chapter x.
a short chapter, which conclude the book.
the long absence of the uncle and nephew have occasion some disquiet
in the mind of all whom they have leave behind they; and the more, as,
during the precede dialogue, the uncle have more than once elevate
his voice, so as to be hear downstair; which, though they could not
distinguish what he say, have cause some evil foreboding in Nancy and
her mother, and, indeed, even in Jones himself.
when the good company, therefore, again assemble, there be a visible
alteration in all their face; and the good-humour which, at their
last meeting, universally shine forth in every countenance, be now
change into a much less agreeable aspect. it be a change, indeed,
common enough to the weather in this climate, from sunshine to cloud,
from June to December.
this alteration be not, however, greatly remark by any present; for
as they be all now endeavour to conceal their own thought, and
to act a part, they become all too busily engage in the scene to be
spectator of it. thus neither the uncle nor nephew see any symptom
of suspicion in the mother or daughter; nor do the mother or daughter
remark the overact complacence of the old man, nor the counterfeit
satisfaction which grin in the feature of the young one.
something like this, I believe, frequently happen, where the whole
attention of two friend be engage in the part which each be to
act, in order to impose on the other, neither see nor suspect the
art practise against himself; and thus the thrust of both (to borrow
no improper metaphor on the occasion) alike take place.
from the same reason it be no unusual thing for both party to be
overreach in a bargain, though the one must be always the great
loser; as be he who sell a blind horse, and receive a bad note in
payment.
our company in about half an hour break up, and the uncle carry off
his nephew; but not before the latter have assure Miss Nancy, in a
whisper, that he would attend she early in the morning, and fulfil all
his engagement.
Jones, who be the least concerned in this scene, see the most. he do
indeed suspect the very fact; for, besides observe the great
alteration in the behaviour of the uncle, the distance he assume, and
his overstrained civility to Miss Nancy; the carry off a bridegroom
from his bride at that time of night be so extraordinary a proceeding
that it could be account for only by imagine that young
Nightingale have reveal the whole truth, which the apparent openness
of his temper, and his be fluster with liquor, make too probable.
while he be reason with himself, whether he should acquaint these
poor people with his suspicion, the maid of the house inform he
that a gentlewoman desire to speak with him.----He go immediately
out, and, take the candle from the maid, usher his visitant
upstairs, who, in the person of Mrs Honour, acquaint he with such
dreadful news concern his Sophia, that he immediately lose all
consideration for every other person; and his whole stock of
compassion be entirely swallow up in reflection on his own misery,
and on that of his unfortunate angel.
what this dreadful matter be, the reader will be inform, after we
have first relate the many precede step which produce it, and
those will be the subject of the follow book.
BOOK XV.
in which the history advances about two day.
Chapter i.
too short to need a preface.
there be a set of religious, or rather moral writer, who teach that
virtue be the certain road to happiness, and vice to misery, in this
world. a very wholesome and comfortable doctrine, and to which we have
but one objection, namely, that it be not true.
indeed, if by virtue these writer mean the exercise of those cardinal
virtue, which like good housewife stay at home, and mind only the
business of their own family, I shall very readily concede the point;
for so surely do all these contribute and lead to happiness, that I
could almost wish, in violation of all the antient and modern sage,
to call they rather by the name of wisdom, than by that of virtue;
for, with regard to this life, no system, I conceive, be ever wise
than that of the antient Epicureans, who hold this wisdom to
constitute the chief good; nor foolisher than that of their opposite,
those modern epicure, who place all felicity in the abundant
gratification of every sensual appetite.
but if by virtue be mean (as I almost think it ought) a certain
relative quality, which be always busy itself without-door, and
seem as much interested in pursue the good of other as its own; I
cannot so easily agree that this be the sure way to human happiness;
because I be afraid we must then include poverty and contempt, with
all the mischief which backbiting, envy, and ingratitude, can bring
on mankind, in our idea of happiness; nay, sometimes perhaps we shall
be oblige to wait upon the say happiness to a jail; since many by
the above virtue have bring themselves thither.
I have not now leisure to enter upon so large a field of speculation,
as here seem open upon I; my design be to wipe off a doctrine
that lie in my way; since, while Mr Jones be act the most virtuous
part imaginable in labour to preserve his fellow-creature from
destruction, the devil, or some other evil spirit, one perhaps
cloathe in human flesh, be hard at work to make he completely
miserable in the ruin of his Sophia.
this therefore would seem an exception to the above rule, if indeed it
be a rule; but as we have in our voyage through life see so many
other exception to it, we chuse to dispute the doctrine on which it
be found, which we donot apprehend to be Christian, which we be
convince be not true, and which be indeed destructive of one of the
noble argument that reason alone can furnish for the belief of
immortality.
but as the reader's curiosity (if he hath any) must be now awake, and
hungry, we shall provide to feed it as fast as we can.
Chapter ii.
in which be open a very black design against Sophia.
I remember a wise old gentleman who use to say, "when child be
do nothing, they be do mischief." I will not enlarge this
quaint say to the most beautiful part of the creation in general;
but so far I may be allow, that when the effect of female jealousy
do not appear openly in their proper colour of rage and fury, we may
suspect that mischievous passion to be at work privately, and
attempt to undermine, what it doth not attack above-ground.
this be exemplified in the conduct of Lady Bellaston, who, under all
the smile which she wear in her countenance, conceal much
indignation against Sophia; and as she plainly see that this young
lady stand between she and the full indulgence of her desire, she
resolve to get rid of she by some mean or other; nor be it long
before a very favourable opportunity of accomplish this present
itself to she.
the reader may be pleased to remember, that when Sophia be throw
into that consternation at the playhouse, by the wit and humour of a
set of young gentleman who call themselves the town, we inform he,
that she have put herself under the protection of a young nobleman, who
have very safely conduct she to her chair.
this nobleman, who frequently visit Lady Bellaston, have more than
once see Sophia there, since her arrival in town, and have conceive a
very great liking to she; which like, as beauty never look more
amiable than in distress, Sophia have in this fright so encreased, that
he might now, without any great impropriety, be say to be actually in
love with she.
it may easily be believe, that he would not suffer so handsome an
occasion of improve his acquaintance with the beloved object as now
offer itself to elapse, when even good breeding alone might have
prompt he to pay she a visit.
the next morning therefore, after this accident, he wait on Sophia,
with the usual compliment, and hop that she have receive no harm
from her last night's adventure.
as love, like fire, when once thoroughly kindle, be soon blow into a
flame, Sophia in a very short time compleate her conquest. time now
fly away unperceived, and the noble lord have be two hour in
company with the lady, before it enter into his head that he have
make too long a visit. though this circumstance alone would have
alarm Sophia, who be somewhat more a mistress of computation at
present; she have indeed much more pregnant evidence from the eye of
her lover of what past within his bosom; nay, though he do not make
any open declaration of his passion, yet many of his expression be
rather too warm, and too tender, to have be impute to complacence,
even in the age when such complacence be in fashion; the very reverse
of which be well known to be the reign mode at present.
Lady Bellaston have be apprize of his lordship's visit at his first
arrival; and the length of it very well satisfied she, that thing
go as she wish, and as indeed she have suspect the second time
she see this young couple together. this business, she rightly I think
conclude, that she should by no mean forward by mix in the
company while they be together; she therefore order her servant,
that when my lord be go, they should tell he she desire to speak
with he; and employ the intermediate time in meditate how good to
accomplish a scheme, which she make no doubt but his lordship would
very readily embrace the execution of.
Lord Fellamar (for that be the title of this young nobleman) be no
soon introduce to her ladyship, than she attack he in the
following strain: "bless I, my lord, be you here yet? I think my
servant have make a mistake, and let you go away; and I want to see
you about an affair of some importance."----"Indeed, Lady Bellaston,"
say he, "I donot wonder you be astonish at the length of my
visit; for I have staid above two hour, and I do not think I have
staid above half-a-one."----"what be I to conclude from thence, my
lord?" say she. "the company must be very agreeable which can make
time slide away so very deceitfully."----"Upon my honour," say he,
"the most agreeable I ever see. pray tell I, Lady Bellaston, who be
this blaze star which you have produce among we all of a
sudden?"----"what blaze star, my lord?" say she, affect a
surprize. "I mean," say he, "the lady I see here the other day, whom
I have last night in my arm at the playhouse, and to whom I have be
make that unreasonable visit."----"O, my cousin western!" say she;
"why, that blaze star, my lord, be the daughter of a country booby
squire, and hath be in town about a fortnight, for the first
time."----"Upon my soul," say he, "I should swear she have be breed
up in a court; for besides her beauty, I never see anything so
genteel, so sensible, so polite."----"O brave!" cry the lady, "my
cousin hath you, I find."----"Upon my honour," answer he, "I wish
she have; for I be in love with she to distraction."----"Nay, my
lord," say she, "it be not wish yourself very ill neither, for
she be a very great fortune: I assure you she be an only child, and
her father's estate be a good £3000 a-year." "then I can assure you,
madam," answer the lord, "I think she the good match in England."
"indeed, my lord," reply she, "if you like she, I heartily wish you
have she." "if you think so kindly of I, madam," say he, "as she be
a relation of your, will you do I the honour to propose it to her
father?" "and be you really then in earnest?" cry the lady, with
an affected gravity. "I hope, madam," answer he, "you have a well
opinion of I, than to imagine I would jest with your ladyship in an
affair of this kind." "indeed, then," say the lady, "I will most
readily propose your lordship to her father; and I can, I believe,
assure you of his joyful acceptance of the proposal; but there be a
bar, which I be almost ashamed to mention; and yet it be one you will
never be able to conquer. you have a rival, my lord, and a rival who,
though I blush to name he, neither you, nor all the world, will ever
be able to conquer." "upon my word, Lady Bellaston," cry he, "you
have strike a damp to my heart, which hath almost deprive I of
be." "Fie, my lord," say she, "I should rather hope I have strike
fire into you. a lover, and talk of damp in your heart! I rather
imagine you would have ask your rival's name, that you might have
immediately enter the list with he." "I promise you, madam,"
answer he, "there be very few thing I would not undertake for
your charming cousin; but pray, who be this happy man?"--"why, he
be," say she, "what I be sorry to say most happy man with we be,
one of the low fellow in the world. he be a beggar, a bastard, a
foundling, a fellow in mean circumstance than one of your
lordship's footman." "and be it possible," cry he, "that a young
creature with such perfection should think of bestow herself so
unworthily?" "alas! my lord," answer she, "consider the
country--the bane of all young woman be the country. there they learn
a set of romantic notion of love, and I know not what folly, which
this town and good company can scarce eradicate in a whole winter."
"indeed, madam," reply my lord, "your cousin be of too immense a
value to be throw away; such ruin as this must be prevent."
"alas!" cry she, "my lord, how can it be prevent? the family have
already do all in their power; but the girl be, I think,
intoxicate, and nothing less than ruin will content she. and to deal
more openly with you, I expect every day to hear she be run away with
he." "what you tell I, Lady Bellaston," answer his lordship,
"affect I most tenderly, and only raise my compassion, instead of
lessen my adoration of your cousin. some mean must be find to
preserve so inestimable a jewel. hath your ladyship endeavour to
reason with she?" here the lady affect a laugh, and cry, "my dear
lord, sure you know we well than to talk of reason a young woman
out of her inclination? these inestimable jewel be as deaf as the
jewel they wear: time, my lord, time be the only medicine to cure
their folly; but this be a medicine which I be certain she will not
take; nay, I live in hourly horror on her account. in short, nothing
but violent method will do." "what be to be do?" cry my lord;
"what method be to be taken?--is there any method upon earth?--oh!
Lady Bellaston! there be nothing which I would not undertake for such
a reward."----"I really know not," answer the lady, after a pause;
and then pause again, she cry out--"upon my soul, I be at my
wit's end on this girl's account.--if she can be preserve, something
must be do immediately; and, as I say, nothing but violent method
will do.----if your lordship hath really this attachment to my cousin
(and to do her justice, except in this silly inclination, of which
she will soon see her folly, she be every way deserve), I think
there may be one way, indeed, it be a very disagreeable one, and what
I be almost afraid to think of.--It require a great spirit, I
promise you." "I be not conscious, madam," say he, "of any defect
there; nor be I, I hope, suspect of any such. it must be an
egregious defect indeed, which could make I backward on this
occasion." "Nay, my lord," answer she, "I be so far from doubt
you, I be much more inclined to doubt my own courage; for I must run
a monstrous risque. in short, I must place such a confidence in your
honour as a wise woman will scarce ever place in a man on any
consideration." in this point likewise my lord very well satisfy
she; for his reputation be extremely clear, and common fame do he
no more than justice, in speak well of he. "well, then," say
she, "my lord,--I--I vow, I cannot bear the apprehension of it.--no,
it must not be.----at least every other method shall be try. can
you get rid of your engagement, and dine here to-day? your lordship
will have an opportunity of see a little more of Miss Western.--I
promise you we have no time to lose. here will be nobody but Lady
Betty, and Miss Eagle, and Colonel Hampsted, and Tom Edwards; they
will all go soon--and I shall be at home to nobody. then your
lordship may be a little more explicit. Nay, I will contrive some
method to convince you of her attachment to this fellow." my lord
make proper compliment, accept the invitation, and then they
part to dress, it be now past three in the morning, or to reckon
by the old style, in the afternoon.
Chapter iii.
a further explanation of the foregoing design.
though the reader may have long since conclude Lady Bellaston to be a
member (and no inconsiderable one) of the great world; she be in
reality a very considerable member of the little world; by which
appellation be distinguish a very worthy and honourable society
which not long since flourish in this kingdom.
among other good principle upon which this society be found, there
be one very remarkable; for, as it be a rule of an honourable club
of hero, who assemble at the close of the late war, that all the
member should every day fight once at least; so 'twa in this, that
every member should, within the twenty-four hour, tell at least one
merry fib, which be to be propagate by all the brother and
sisterhood.
many idle story be tell about this society, which from a certain
quality may be, perhaps not unjustly, suppose to have come from the
society themselves. as, that the devil be the president; and that he
sit in person in an elbow-chair at the upper end of the table; but,
upon very strict enquiry, I find there be not the least truth in any
of those tale, and that the assembly consist in reality of a set of
very good sort of people, and the fib which they propagate be of a
harmless kind, and tend only to produce mirth and good humour.
Edwards be likewise a member of this comical society. to he
therefore Lady Bellaston apply as a proper instrument for her
purpose, and furnish he with a fib, which he be to vent whenever
the lady give he her cue; and this be not to be till the evening,
when all the company but Lord Fellamar and himself be go, and
while they be engage in a rubber at whist.
to this time then, which be between seven and eight in the evening,
we will convey our reader; when Lady Bellaston, Lord Fellamar, Miss
Western, and Tom, be engage at whist, and in the last game of
their rubber, Tom receive his cue from Lady Bellaston, which be, "I
protest, Tom, you be grow intolerable lately; you use to tell we
all the news of the town, and now you know no more of the world than
if you live out of it."
Mr Edwards then begin as follow: "the fault be not mine, madam: it
lie in the dulness of the age, that doth nothing worth talk
of.----O la! though now I think on't there hath a terrible accident
befall poor Colonel Wilcox.----Poor ned.----you know he, my lord,
everybody know he; faith! I be very much concerned for he."
"what be it, pray?" say Lady Bellaston.
"why, he hath kill a man this morning in a duel, thatbe all."
his lordship, who be not in the secret, ask gravely, whom he have
kill? to which Edwards answer, "a young fellow we none of we know;
a Somersetshire lad just come to town, one Jones his name be; a near
relation of one Mr Allworthy, of whom your lordship I believe hath
hear. I see the lad lie dead in a coffee-house.--Upon my soul, he be
one of the fine corps I ever see in my life!"
Sophia, who have just begin to deal as Tom have mention that a man be
kill, stopt her hand, and listen with attention (for all story
of that kind affect she), but no soon have he arrive at the latter
part of the story than she begin to deal again; and have deal three
card to one, and seven to another, and ten to a third, at last dropt
the rest from her hand, and fall back in her chair.
the company behave as usually on these occasion. the usual
disturbance ensue, the usual assistance be summon, and Sophia at
last, as it be usual, return again to life, and be soon after, at
her earnest desire, lead to her own apartment; where, at my lord's
request, Lady Bellaston acquaint she with the truth, attempt to
carry it off as a jest of her own, and comfort she with repeat
assurance, that neither his lordship nor Tom, though she have teach
he the story, be in the true secret of the affair.
there be no farth evidence necessary to convince Lord Fellamar how
justly the case have be represent to he by Lady Bellaston; and
now, at her return into the room, a scheme be lay between these two
noble person, which, though it appear in no very heinous light to
his lordship (as he faithfully promise, and faithfully resolve too,
to make the lady all the subsequent amend in his power by marriage),
yet many of our reader, we doubt not, will see with just detestation.
the next evening at seven be appoint for the fatal purpose, when
Lady Bellaston undertake that Sophia should be alone, and his lordship
should be introduce to she. the whole family be to be regulate for
the purpose, most of the servant despatch out of the house; and for
Mrs Honour, who, to prevent suspicion, be to be leave with her
mistress till his lordship's arrival, Lady Bellaston herself be to
engage she in an apartment as distant as possible from the scene of
the intended mischief, and out of the hearing of Sophia.
matter be thus agree on, his lordship take his leave, and her
ladyship retire to rest, highly pleased with a project, of which she
have no reason to doubt the success, and which promise so effectually
to remove Sophia from be any further obstruction to she amour with
Jones, by a mean of which she should never appear to be guilty, even
if the fact appear to the world; but this she make no doubt of
prevent by huddle up a marriage, to which she think the
ravish Sophia would easily be bring to consent, and at which all
the rest of her family would rejoice.
but affair be not in so quiet a situation in the bosom of the other
conspirator; his mind be tost in all the distract anxiety so nobly
describe by Shakespear--
"between the acting of a dreadful thing,
and the first motion, all the interim be
like a phantasma, or a hideous dream;
the genius and the mortal instrument
be then in council; and the state of man,
like to a little kingdom, suffer then
the nature of an insurrection."----
though the violence of his passion have make he eagerly embrace the
first hint of this design, especially as it come from a relation of
the lady, yet when that friend to reflection, a pillow, have place the
action itself in all its natural black colour before his eye, with
all the consequence which must, and those which might probably attend
it, his resolution begin to abate, or rather indeed to go over to the
other side; and after a long conflict, which last a whole night,
between honour and appetite, the former at length prevail, and he
determine to wait on Lady Bellaston, and to relinquish the design.
Lady Bellaston be in bed, though very late in the morning, and Sophia
sit by her bed-side, when the servant acquaint she that Lord
Fellamar be below in the parlour; upon which her ladyship desire he
to stay, and that she would see he presently; but the servant be no
soon depart than poor Sophia begin to intreat her cousin not to
encourage the visit of that odious lord (so she call he, though a
little unjustly) upon her account. "I see his design," say she; "for
he make downright love to I yesterday morning; but as I be resolve
never to admit it, I beg your ladyship not to leave we alone together
any more, and to order the servant that, if he enquire for I, I may
be always deny to he."
"La! child," say Lady Bellaston, "you country girl have nothing but
sweetheart in your head; you fancy every man who be civil to you be
make love. he be one of the most gallant young fellow about town,
and I be convince mean no more than a little gallantry. make love to
you indeed! I wish with all my heart he would, and you must be an
arrant mad woman to refuse he."
"but as I shall certainly be that mad woman," cry Sophia, "I hope
his visit shall not be intrude upon I."
"o child!" say Lady Bellaston, "you need not be so fearful; if you
resolve to run away with that Jones, I know no person who can hinder
you."
"upon my honour, madam," cry Sophia, "your ladyship injure I. I
will never run away with any man; nor will I ever marry contrary to my
father's inclination."
"well, Miss Western," say the lady, "if you be not in a humour to
see company this morning, you may retire to your own apartment; for I
be not frightened at his lordship, and must send for he up into my
dressing-room."
Sophia thank her ladyship, and withdraw; and presently afterwards
Fellamar be admit upstairs.
chapter iv.
by which it will appear how dangerous an advocate a lady be when she
apply her eloquence to an ill purpose.
when Lady Bellaston hear the young lord's scruple, she treat they
with the same disdain with which one of those sage of the law, call
newgate solicitor, treat the qualm of conscience in a young
witness. "my dear lord," say she, "you certainly want a cordial. I
must send to Lady Edgely for one of her good dram. fie upon it! have
more resolution. be you frighten by the word rape? or be you
apprehensive----? well! if the story of Helen be modern, I should
think it unnatural. I mean the behaviour of Paris, not the fondness of
the lady; for all woman love a man of spirit. there be another story
of the Sabine lady--and that too, I thank heaven, be very antient.
your lordship, perhaps, will admire my reading; but I think Mr Hook
tell we, they make tolerable good wife afterwards. I fancy few of my
married acquaintance be ravish by their husband." "Nay, dear Lady
Bellaston," cry he, "donot ridicule I in this manner." "why, my
good lord," answer she, "do you think any woman in England would not
laugh at you in her heart, whatever prudery she might wear in her
countenance?----you force I to use a strange kind of language, and to
betray my sex most abominably; but I be content with know my
intention be good, and that I be endeavour to serve my cousin;
for I think you will make she a husband notwithstanding this; or, upon
my soul, I would not even persuade she to fle herself away upon an
empty title. she should not upbraid I hereafter with have lose a
man of spirit; for that his enemy allow this poor young fellow to
be."
let those who have have the satisfaction of hear reflection of this
kind from a wife or a mistress, declare whether they be at all
sweeten by come from a female tongue. certain it be, they sink
deeply into his lordship than anything which demosthene or Cicero
could have say on the occasion.
Lady Bellaston, perceive she have fire the young lord's pride, begin
now, like a true orator, to rouse other passion to its assistance.
"my lord," say she, in a graver voice, "you will be pleased to
remember, you mention this matter to I first; for I would not
appear to you in the light of one who be endeavour to put off my
cousin upon you. Fourscore thousand pound do not stand in need of an
advocate to recommend they." "nor doth Miss Western," say he,
"require any recommendation from her fortune; for, in my opinion, no
woman ever have half her charm." "yes, yes, my lord," reply the
lady, look in the glass, "there have be woman with more than half
her charm, I assure you; not that I need lessen she on that account:
she be a most delicious girl, thatbe certain; and within these few
hour she will be in the arm of one, who surely doth not deserve she,
though I will give he his due, I believe he be truly a man of
spirit."
"I hope so, madam," say my lord; "though I must own he doth not
deserve she; for, unless heaven or your ladyship disappoint I, she
shall within that time be in mine."
"well speak, my lord," answer the lady; "I promise you no
disappointment shall happen from my side; and within this week I be
convince I shall call your lordship my cousin in public."
the remainder of this scene consist entirely of rapture, excuse,
and compliment, very pleasant to have hear from the party; but
rather dull when relate at second hand. here, therefore, we shall put
an end to this dialogue, and hasten to the fatal hour when everything
be prepare for the destruction of poor Sophia.
but this be the most tragical matter in our whole history, we shall
treat it in a chapter by itself.
Chapter v.
contain some matter which may affect, and other which may
surprize, the reader.
the clock have now strike seven, and poor Sophia, alone and melancholy,
sit read a tragedy. it be the Fatal Marriage; and she be now come
to that part where the poor distr Isabella dispose of she
wedding-ring.
here the book dropt from her hand, and a shower of tear run down into
her bosom. in this situation she have continue a minute, when the door
open, and in come Lord Fellamar. Sophia start from her chair at
his entrance; and his lordship advance forwards, and make a low
bow, say, "I be afraid, Miss Western, I break in upon you abruptly."
"indeed, my lord," say she, "I must own myself a little surprized at
this unexpected visit." "if this visit be unexpected, madam," answer
Lord Fellamar, "my eye must have be very faithless interpreter of
my heart, when last I have the honour of see you; for surely you
could not otherwise have hop to detain my heart in your possession,
without receive a visit from its owner." Sophia, confuse as she
be, answer this bombast (and very properly I think) with a look of
inconceivable disdain. my lord then make another and a long speech
of the same sort. upon which Sophia, trembling, say, "be I really to
conceive your lordship to be out of your sens? sure, my lord, there
be no other excuse for such behaviour." "I be, indeed, madam, in the
situation you suppose," cry his lordship; "and sure you will pardon
the effect of a frenzy which you yourself have occasion; for love
hath so totally deprive I of reason, that I be scarce accountable
for any of my action." "upon my word, my lord," say Sophia, "I
neither understand your word nor your behaviour." "suffer I then,
madam," cry he, "at your foot to explain both, by lay open my
soul to you, and declare that I doat on you to the high degree of
distraction. o most adorable, most divine creature! what language can
express the sentiment of my heart?" "I do assure you, my lord," say
Sophia, "I shall not stay to hear any more of this." "do not," cry
he, "think of leave I thus cruelly; could you know half the
torment which I feel, that tender bosom must pity what those eye
have cause." then fetch a deep sigh, and lay hold of her hand,
he run on for some minute in a strain which would be little more
pleasing to the reader than it be to the lady; and at last conclude
with a declaration, "that if he be master of the world, he would lay
it at her foot." Sophia then, forcibly pull away her hand from his,
answer with much spirit, "I promise you, sir, your world and its
master I should spurn from I with equal contempt." she then offer
to go; and Lord Fellamar, again lay hold of her hand, say, "Pardon
I, my beloved angel, freedom which nothing but despair could have
tempt I to take.----believe I, could I have have any hope that my
title and fortune, neither of they inconsiderable, unless when
compare with your worth, would have be accept, I have, in the
humblest manner, present they to your acceptance.----but I cannot
lose you.--By heaven, I will soon part with my soul!--You be, you
must, you shall be only mine." "my lord," say she, "I intreat you to
desist from a vain pursuit; for, upon my honour, I will never hear you
on this subject. let go my hand, my lord; for I be resolve to go from
you this moment; nor will I ever see you more." "then, madam," cry
his lordship, "I must make the good use of this moment; for I cannot
live, nor will I live without you."----"what do you mean, my lord?"
say Sophia; "I will raise the family." "I have no fear, madam,"
answer he, "but of lose you, and that I be resolve to prevent,
the only way which despair point to I."--He then catch she in his
arm: upon which she scream so loud, that she must have alarm some
one to her assistance, have not Lady Bellaston take care to remove all
ear.
but a more lucky circumstance happen for poor Sophia; another noise
now break forth, which almost drown her cry; for now the whole
house ring with, "where be she? D--n I, Iwill unkennel she this
instant. show I her chamber, I say. where be my daughter? I know
shebe in the house, and Iwill see she if shebe above-ground. show I
where she be."--At which last word the door fly open, and in come
squire Western, with his parson and a set of myrmidon at his heel.
how miserable must have be the condition of poor Sophia, when the
enrage voice of her father be welcome to her ear! welcome indeed it
be, and luckily do he come; for it be the only accident upon earth
which could have preserve the peace of her mind from be for ever
destroy.
Sophia, notwithstanding her fright, presently know her father's voice;
and his lordship, notwithstanding his passion, know the voice of

relinquish his prey, have only disorder her handkerchief, and with
his rude lip commit violence on her lovely neck.
if the reader's imagination doth not assist I, I shall never be able

his lordship sit down near she, with the bag of his wig hang over

as to squire Western, he happen at this time to be overtake by an
enemy, which very frequently pursue, and seldom fail to overtake,
most of the country gentleman in this kingdom. he be, literally
speak, drunk; which circumstance, together with his natural

of the young lady. I stand assure, if you will forgive she, she will
repent she of all past offence, and return unto her duty."
the strength of the parson's arm have at first be of more service

Shat ha un! d--n I, shat ha un! why dost unt answer? be ever such a

"let I intreat you, sir, to be a little more moderate," say the

"power of mine a--," answer the squire. "you take her part then,
you do? a pretty parson, truly, to side with an undutiful child! yes,
yes, I will gee you a living with a pox. Iwill gee un to the devil
soon."
"I humbly crave your pardon," say the parson; "I assure your worship
I mean no such matter."
my Lady Bellaston now enter the room, and come up to the squire, who

provide for she."

advantage."
this be a wilful mistake in Lady Bellaston, for she well know whom Mr

as Lord Fellamar be very well assure that he be mean by Lady

his lordship be no soon go, than Lady Bellaston, come up to Mr
Western, say, "bless I, sir, what have you do? you know not whom
you have affront; he be a nobleman of the first rank and fortune,
and yesterday make proposal to your daughter; and such as I be sure
you must accept with the high pleasure."
"answer for yourself, lady cousin," say the squire, "I will have
nothing to do with any of your lord. my daughter shall have an honest
country gentleman; I have pitch upon one for she--and she shall ha'
un.--i be sorry for the trouble she hath give your ladyship with all
my heart." Lady Bellaston make a civil speech upon the word trouble;
to which the squire answered--"Why, thatbe kind--and I would do as
much for your ladyship. to be sure relation should do for one
another. so I wish your ladyship a good night.--come, madam, you must
go along with I by fair mean, or Iwill have you carry down to the
coach."
Sophia say she would attend he without force; but beg to go in a
chair, for she say she should not be able to ride any other way.
"Prithee," cry the squire, "wout unt persuade I canst not ride in a
coach, wouldst? thatbe a pretty thing surely! no, no, Iwill never let
thee out of my sight any more till art marry, that I promise thee."
Sophia tell he, she see he be resolve to break her heart. "o break
thy heart and be d--n'd," quoth he, "if a good husband will break it.
I donot value a brass varden, not a halfpenny, of any undutiful b--
upon earth." he then take violent hold of her hand; upon which the
parson once more interfered, beg he to use gentle method. at
that the squire thunder out a curse, and bid the parson hold his
tongue, say, "At'nt in pulpit now? when art a get up there I never
mind what dost say; but I willnot be priest-ride, nor teach how to
behave myself by thee. I wish your ladyship a good-night. come along,
Sophy; be a good girl, and all shall be well. Shat ha' un, d--n I,
shat ha' un!"
Mrs Honour appear below-stair, and with a low curtesy to the squire
offer to attend her mistress; but he push she away, say, "hold,
madam, hold, you come no more near my house." "and will you take my
maid away from I?" say Sophia. "yes, indeed, madam, will I," cry
the squire: "you need not fear be without a servant; I will get you
another maid, and a well maid than this, who, Iwould lay five pound to
a crown, be no more a maid than my grannum. no, no, Sophy, she shall
contrive no more escape, I promise you." he then pack up his
daughter and the parson into the hackney coach, after which he mount
himself, and order it to drive to his lodging. in the way thither
he suffer Sophia to be quiet, and entertain himself with read a
lecture to the parson on good manner, and a proper behaviour to his
better.
it be possible he might not so easily have carry off his daughter
from Lady Bellaston, have that good lady desire to have detain she;
but, in reality, she be not a little pleased with the confinement
into which Sophia be go; and as her project with Lord Fellamar have
fail of success, she be well content that other violent method
be now go to be use in favour of another man.
Chapter vi.
by what mean the squire come to discover his daughter.
though the reader, in many history, be oblige to digest much more
unaccountable appearance than this of Mr Western, without any
satisfaction at all; yet, as we dearly love to oblige he whenever it
be in our power, we shall now proceed to shew by what method the
squire discover where his daughter be.
in the third chapter, then, of the precede book, we give a hint (for
it be not our custom to unfold at any time more than be necessary for
the occasion) that Mrs Fitzpatrick, who be very desirous of
reconcile her uncle and aunt Western, think she have a probable
opportunity, by the service of preserve Sophia from commit the
same crime which have draw on herself the anger of her family. after
much deliberation, therefore, she resolve to inform her aunt Western
where her cousin be, and accordingly she writ the follow letter,
which we shall give the reader at length, for more reason than one.
"HONOURED MADAM,
"the occasion of my writing this will perhaps make a letter of mine
agreeable to my dear aunt, for the sake of one of her niece, though
I have little reason to hope it will be so on the account of
another.
"without more apology, as I be come to throw my unhappy self at
your foot, I meet, by the strange accident in the world, my cousin
Sophy, whose history you be well acquaint with than myself,
though, alas! I know infinitely too much; enough indeed to satisfy
I, that unless she be immediately prevent, she be in danger of
run into the same fatal mischief, which, by foolishly and
ignorantly refuse your most wise and prudent advice, I have
unfortunately bring on myself.
"in short, I have see the man, nay, I be most part of yesterday in
his company, and a charming young fellow I promise you he be. by
what accident he come acquaint with I be too tedious to tell you
now; but I have this morning change my lodging to avoid he, lest
he should by my mean discover my cousin; for he doth not yet know
where she be, and it be adviseable he should not, till my uncle hath
secure her.----no time therefore be to be lose; and I need only
inform you, that she be now with Lady Bellaston, whom I have see,
and who hath, I find, a design of conceal she from her family.
you know, madam, she be a strange woman; but nothing could misbecome
I more than to presume to give any hint to one of your great
understanding and great knowledge of the world, besides barely
inform you of the matter of fact.
"I hope, madam, the care which I have shew on this occasion for the
good of my family will recommend I again to the favour of a lady
who hath always exert so much zeal for the honour and true
interest of we all; and that it may be a means of restore I to
your friendship, which hath make so great a part of my former, and
be so necessary to my future happiness.
"I be,
with the utmost respect,
honoured madam,
your most dutiful obliged niece,
and most obedient humble
servant,
HARRIET FITZPATRICK."
Mrs Western be now at her brother's house, where she have reside ever
since the flight of Sophia, in order to administer comfort to the poor
squire in his affliction. of this comfort, which she dole out to he
in daily portion, we have formerly give a speciman.
she be now stand with she back to the fire, and, with a pinch of
snuff in her hand, be deal forth this daily allowance of comfort
to the squire, while he smoake his afternoon pipe, when she receive
the above letter; which she have no soon read than she deliver it
to he, say, "there, sir, there be an account of your lose sheep.
Fortune hath again restore she to you, and if you will be govern by
my advice, it be possible you may yet preserve she."
the squire have no soon read the letter than he leap from his
chair, throw his pipe into the fire, and give a loud huzza for joy. he
then summon his servant, call for his boot, and order the
Chevalier and several other horse to be saddle, and that parson
Supple should be immediately send for. have do this, he turn to
his sister, catch she in his arm, and give she a close embrace,
say, "Zounds! you donot seem pleased; one would imagine you be
sorry I have find the girl."
"Brother," answer she, "the deep politician, who see to the
bottom, discover often a very different aspect of affair, from what
swim on the surface. it be true, indeed, thing do look rather less
desperate than they do formerly in Holland, when Lewis the Fourteenth
be at the gate of Amsterdam; but there be a delicacy require in
this matter, which you will pardon I, brother, if I suspect you want.
there be a decorum to be use with a woman of figure, such as Lady
Bellaston, brother, which require a knowledge of the world, superior,
I be afraid, to your."
"Sister," cry the squire, "I know you have no opinion of my part;
but Iwill shew you on this occasion who be a fool. Knowledge, quotha! I
have not be in the country so long without have some knowledge of
warrant and the law of the land. I know I may take my own wherever I
can find it. shew I my own daughter, and if I donot know how to come
at she, Iwill suffer you to call I a fool as long as I live. there be
justice of peace in London, as well as in other place."
"I protest," cry she, "you make I tremble for the event of this
matter, which, if you will proceed by my advice, you may bring to so
good an issue. do you really imagine, brother, that the house of a
woman of figure be to be attack by warrant and brutal justice of
the peace? I will inform you how to proceed. as soon as you arrive in
town, and have get yourself into a decent dress (for indeed, brother,
you have none at present fit to appear in), you must send your
compliment to Lady Bellaston, and desire leave to wait on she. when
you be admit to her presence, as you certainly will be, and have
tell she your story, and have make proper use of my name (for I think
you just know one another only by sight, though you be relation), I
be confident she will withdraw her protection from my niece, who hath
certainly impose upon she. this be the only method.--justice of
peace, indeed! do you imagine any such event can arrive to a woman of
figure in a civilised nation?"
"D--n their figure," cry the squire; "a pretty civilised nation,
truly, where woman be above the law. and what must I stand send a
parcel of compliment to a confounded whore, that keep away a
daughter from her own natural father? I tell you, sister, I be not so
ignorant as you think me----I know you would have woman above the law,
but it be all a lye; I hear his lordship say at size, that no one be
above the law. but this of yours be Hanover law, I suppose."
"Mr Western," say she, "I think you daily improve in ignorance.----I
protest you be grow an arrant bear."
"no more a bear than yourself, sister Western," say the
squire.--"Pox! you may talk of your civility an you will, I be sure
you never shew any to I. I be no bear, no, nor no dog neither, though
I know somebody, that be something that begin with a b; but pox! I
will show you I have get more good manner than some folk."
"Mr Western," answer the lady, "you may say what you please, _je
vous mesprise de tout mon coeur._ I shall not therefore be
angry.----beside, as my cousin, with that odious irish name, justly
say, I have that regard for the honour and true interest of my
family, and that concern for my niece, who be a part of it, that I
have resolve to go to town myself upon this occasion; for indeed,
indeed, brother, you be not a fit minister to be employ at a polite
court.--Greenland--Greenland should always be the scene of the
tramontane negociation."
"I thank Heaven," cry the squire, "I donot understand you now. you
be get to your hanoverian linguo. however, Iwill shew you I scorn to
be behind-hand in civility with you; and as you be not angry for what
I have say, so I be not angry for what you have say. indeed I have
always think it a folly for relation to quarrel; and if they do now
and then give a hasty word, why, people should give and take; for my
part, I never bear malice; and I take it very kind of you to go up to
London; for I never be there but twice in my life, and then I do not
stay above a fortnight at a time, and to be sure I cannot be expect
to know much of the street and the folk in that time. I never deny
that you know'd all these matter well than i. for I to dispute
that would be all as one as for you to dispute the management of a
pack of dog, or the find a hare sitting, with I."--"Which I
promise you," say she, "I never will."--"Well, and I promise you,"
return he, "that I never will dispute the t'other."
here then a league be strike (to borrow a phrase from the lady)
between the contend party; and now the parson arrive, and the
horse be ready, the squire depart, have promise his sister to
follow her advice, and she prepare to follow he the next day.
but have communicate these matter to the parson on the road, they
both agree that the prescribed formality might very well be
dispense with; and the squire, have change his mind, proceed in
the manner we have already see.
Chapter vii.
in which various misfortune befel poor Jones.
affair be in the aforesaid situation when Mrs Honour arrive at Mrs
Miller's, and call Jones out from the company, as we have before
see, with whom, when she find herself alone, she begin as follows:--
"O, my dear sir! how shall I get spirit to tell you; you be undo,
sir, and my poor lady's undone, and I be undo." "hath anything
happen to Sophia?" cry Jones, star like a madman. "all that be
bad," cry Honour: "oh, I shall never get such another lady! oh that
I should ever live to see this day!" at these word Jones turn pale
as ash, tremble, and stammer; but Honour go on--"o! Mr Jones, I
have lose my lady for ever." "how? what! for Heaven's sake, tell I.
o, my dear Sophia!" "you may well call she so," say Honour; "she be
the dear lady to I. I shall never have such another
place."----"D--n your place!" cry Jones; "where be--what--what be
become of my Sophia?" "ay, to be sure," cry she, "servant may be
d--n'd. it signify nothing what become of they, though they be
turn away, and ruin ever so much. to be sure they be not flesh
and blood like other people. no, to be sure, it signify nothing what
become of they." "if you have any pity, any compassion," cry Jones,
"I beg you will instantly tell I what hath happen to Sophia?" "to
be sure, I have more pity for you than you have for I," answer
Honour; "I donot d--n you because you have lose the sweetest lady in
the world. to be sure you be worthy to be pity, and I be worthy to
be pity too: for, to be sure, if ever there be a good mistress----"
"what hath happen?" cry Jones, in almost a raving fit.
"what?--what?" say Honour: "why, the bad that could have happen
both for you and for me.--her father be come to town, and hath carry
she away from we both." here Jones fall on his knee in thanksgive
that it be no bad. "no bad!" repeat Honour; "what could be
bad for either of we? he carry she off, swear she should marry
Mr Blifil; thatbe for your comfort; and, for poor I, I be turn out
of door." "indeed, Mrs Honour," answer Jones, "you frighten I
out of my wit. I imagine some most dreadful sudden accident have
happen to Sophia; something, compare to which, even see she
married to Blifil would be a trifle; but while there be life there be
hope, my dear Honour. woman in this land of liberty, cannot be
marry by actual brutal force." "to be sure, sir," say she, "thatbe
true. there may be some hope for you; but alack-a-day! what hope be
there for poor I? and to be sure, sir, you must be sensible I suffer
all this upon your account. all the quarrel the squire hath to I be
for take your part, as I have do, against Mr Blifil." "indeed, Mrs
Honour," answer he, "I be sensible of my obligation to you, and
will leave nothing in my power undo to make you amend." "alas!
sir," say she, "what can make a servant amend for the loss of one
place but the get another altogether as good?" "do not despair,
Mrs Honour," say Jones, "I hope to reinstate you again in the same."
"alack-a-day, sir," say she, "how can I flatter myself with such
hope when I know it be a thing impossible? for the squire be so set
against I: and yet, if you should ever have my lady, as to be sure I
now hop heartily you will; for you be a generous, good-natured
gentleman; and I be sure you love she, and to be sure she love you
as dearly as her own soul; it be a matter in vain to deny it; because
as why, everybody, that be in the least acquainted with my lady, must
see it; for, poor dear lady, she cannot dissemble: and if two people
who love one another a'n't happy, why who should be so? happiness
donot always depend upon what people have; besides, my lady have enough
for both. to be sure, therefore, as one may say, it would be all the
pity in the world to keep two such lovier asunder; nay, I be
convince, for my part, you will meet together at last; for, if it be
to be, there be no prevent it. if a marriage be make in heaven, all
the justice of peace upon earth cannot break it off. to be sure I
wish that parson Supple have but a little more spirit, to tell the
squire of his wickedness in endeavour to force his daughter
contrary to her liking; but then his whole dependance be on the
squire; and so the poor gentleman, though he be a very religious good
sort of man, and talk of the badness of such doing behind the
squirebe back, yet he dare not say his soul be his own to his face.
to be sure I never see he make so bold as just now; I be afeard the
squire would have strike he. I would not have your honour be
melancholy, sir, nor despair; thing may go well, as long as you be
sure of my lady, and that I be certain you may be; for she never will
be bring to consent to marry any other man. indeed I be terribly
afeare the squire will do she a mischief in his passion, for he be a
prodigious passionate gentleman; and I be afeare too the poor lady
will be bring to break her heart, for she be as tender-hearted as a
chicken. it be pity, methink, she have not a little of my courage. if
I be in love with a young man, and my father offer to lock I up,
Iwould tear his eye out but Iwould come at he; but then therebe a great
fortune in the case, which it be in her father's power either to give
she or not; that, to be sure, may make some difference."
whether Jones give strict attention to all the foregoing harangue, or
whether it be for want of any vacancy in the discourse, I cannot
determine; but he never once attempt to answer, nor do she once
stop till Partridge come run into the room, and inform he that
the great lady be upon the stair.
nothing could equal the dilemma to which Jones be now reduce. Honour
know nothing of any acquaintance that subsist between he and Lady
Bellaston, and she be almost the last person in the world to whom he
would have communicate it. in this hurry and distress, he take (as be
common enough) the bad course, and, instead of expose she to the
lady, which would have be of little consequence, he choose to expose
the lady to she; he therefore resolve to hide Honour, whom he have but
just time to convey behind the bed, and to draw the curtain.
the hurry in which Jones have be all day engage on account of his
poor landlady and her family, the terror occasion by Mrs Honour,
and the confusion into which he be throw by the sudden arrival of
Lady Bellaston, have altogether drive former thought out of his head;
so that it never once occur to his memory to act the part of a sick
man; which, indeed, neither the gaiety of his dress, nor the freshness
of his countenance, would have at all support.
he receive her ladyship therefore rather agreeably to her desire
than to her expectation, with all the good humour he could muster in
his countenance, and without any real or affected appearance of the
least disorder.
Lady Bellaston no soon enter the room, than she squat herself
down on the bed: "so, my dear Jones," say she, "you find nothing can
detain I long from you. perhaps I ought to be angry with you, that I
have neither see nor hear from you all day; for I perceive your
distemper would have suffer you to come abroad: nay, I suppose you
have not sit in your chamber all day drest up like a fine lady to see
company after a lying-in; but, however, donot think I intend to scold
you; for I never will give you an excuse for the cold behaviour of a
husband, by put on the ill-humour of a wife."
"Nay, Lady Bellaston," say Jones, "I be sure your ladyship will not
upbraid I with neglect of duty, when I only wait for order. who,
my dear creature, hath reason to complain? who miss an appointment
last night, and leave an unhappy man to expect, and wish, and sigh, and
languish?"
"do not mention it, my dear Mr Jones," cry she. "if you know the
occasion, you would pity I. in short, it be impossible to conceive
what woman of condition be oblige to suffer from the impertinence of
fool, in order to keep up the farce of the world. I be glad, however,
all your languishing and wish have do you no harm; for you never
look well in your life. upon my faith! Jones, you might at this
instant sit for the picture of Adonis."
there be certain word of provocation which man of honour hold can
properly be answer only by a blow. among lover possibly there may
be some expression which can be answer only by a kiss. now the
compliment which Lady Bellaston now make Jones seem to be of this
kind, especially as it be attend with a look, in which the lady
convey more soft idea than it be possible to express with her
tongue.
Jones be certainly at this instant in one of the most disagreeable
and distressed situation imaginable; for, to carry on the comparison
we make use of before, though the provocation be give by the lady,
Jones could not receive satisfaction, nor so much as offer to ask it,
in the presence of a third person; second in this kind of duel not
be accord to the law of arm. as this objection do not occur to
Lady Bellaston, who be ignorant of any other woman be there but
herself, she wait some time in great astonishment for an answer from
Jones, who, conscious of the ridiculous figure he make, stand at a
distance, and, not dare to give the proper answer, give none at all.
nothing can be imagine more comic, nor yet more tragical, than this
scene would have be if it have last much long. the lady have
already change colour two or three time; have get up from the bed and
sit down again, while Jones be wish the ground to sink under he,
or the house to fall on his head, when an odd accident free he from
an embarrassment out of which neither the eloquence of a Cicero, nor
the politic of a Machiavel, could have deliver he, without utter
disgrace.
this be no other than the arrival of young Nightingale, dead drunk;
or rather in that state of drunkenness which deprive man of the use
of their reason without deprive they of the use of their limb.
Mrs Miller and her daughter be in bed, and Partridge be smoake
his pipe by the kitchen fire; so that he arrive at Mr Jones's
chamber-door without any interruption. this he burst open, and be
enter without any ceremony, when Jones start from his seat and
run to oppose he, which he do so effectually, that Nightingale never
come far enough within the door to see who be sit on the bed.
Nightingale have in reality mistaken Jones's apartment for that in
which himself have lodge; he therefore strongly insist on come in,
often swear that he would not be keep from his own bed. Jones,
however, prevail over he, and deliver he into the hand of
Partridge, whom the noise on the stair soon summon to his master's
assistance.
and now Jones be unwillingly oblige to return to his own apartment,
where at the very instant of his entrance he hear Lady Bellaston
vent an exclamation, though not a very loud one; and at the same
time see she fling herself into a chair in a vast agitation, which
in a lady of a tender constitution would have be an hysteric fit.
in reality the lady, frighten with the struggle between the two man,
of which she do not know what would be the issue, as she hear
Nightingale swear many oath he would come to his own bed, attempt
to retire to her know place of hiding, which to her great confusion
she find already occupy by another.
"be this usage to be bear, Mr Jones?" cry the lady.--"bas of
men?----what wretch be this to whom you have expose I?" "Wretch!"
cry Honour, burst in a violent rage from her place of
concealment--"Marry come up!----wretch forsooth?----as poor a wretch
as I be, I be honest; this be more than some folk who be rich can
say."
Jones, instead of apply himself directly to take off the edge of
Mrs Honour's resentment, as a more experienced gallant would have
do, fall to curse his star, and lament himself as the most
unfortunate man in the world; and presently after, address himself
to Lady Bellaston, he fall to some very absurd protestation of
innocence. by this time the lady, have recover the use of her
reason, which she have as ready as any woman in the world, especially
on such occasion, calmly reply: "Sir, you need make no apology, I
see now who the person be; I do not at first know Mrs Honour: but now
I do, I can suspect nothing wrong between she and you; and I be sure
she be a woman of too good sense to put any wrong construction upon
my visit to you; I have be always her friend, and it may be in my
power to be much more hereafter."
Mrs Honour be altogether as placable as she be passionate. hearing,
therefore, Lady Bellaston assume the soft tone, she likewise soften
hers.----"I'm sure, madam," say she, "I have be always ready to
acknowledge your ladyship's friendship to I; sure I never have so
good a friend as your ladyship----and to be sure, now I see it be your
ladyship that I speak to, I could almost bite my tongue off for very
mad.--i construction upon your ladyship--to be sure it doth not
become a servant as I be to think about such a great lady--I mean I
be a servant: for indeed I be nobody's servant now, the more
miserable wretch be me.--I have lose the good mistress----" here
Honour think fit to produce a shower of tears.--"don't cry, child,"
say the good lady; "way perhaps may be find to make you amend.
come to I to-morrow morning." she then take up her fan which lie on
the ground, and without even look at Jones walk very majestically
out of the room; there be a kind of dignity in the impudence of
woman of quality, which their inferior vainly aspire to attain to in
circumstance of this nature.
Jones follow her downstair, often offer she his hand, which she
absolutely refuse he, and get into her chair without take any
notice of he as he stand bow before she.
at his return upstairs, a long dialogue past between he and Mrs
Honour, while she be adjust herself after the discomposure she have
undergo. the subject of this be his infidelity to her young lady;
on which she enlarge with great bitterness; but Jones at last find
mean to reconcile she, and not only so, but to obtain a promise of
most inviolable secrecy, and that she would the next morning endeavour
to find out Sophia, and bring he a further account of the proceeding
of the squire.
thus end this unfortunate adventure to the satisfaction only of Mrs
Honour; for a secret (as some of my reader will perhaps acknowledge
from experience) be often a very valuable possession: and that not
only to those who faithfully keep it, but sometimes to such as whisper
it about till it come to the ear of every one except the ignorant
person who pay for the suppose concealing of what be publickly
know.
Chapter viii.
short and sweet.
notwithstanding all the obligation she have receive from Jones, Mrs
Miller could not forbear in the morning some gentle remonstrance for
the hurricane which have happen the precede night in his chamber.
these be, however, so gentle and so friendly, profess, and indeed
truly, to aim at nothing more than the real good of Mr Jones himself,
that he, far from be offend, thankfully receive the admonition
of the good woman, express much concern for what have past, excuse
it as well as he could, and promise never more to bring the same
disturbance into the house.
but though Mrs Miller do not refrain from a short expostulation in

indeed to perform the office of a father to Miss Nancy, and to give

a wife in so imprudent a manner.
and here perhaps it may be proper to account for the escape which this
young gentleman have make from his uncle, and for his appearance in the

now when the uncle have arrive at his lodging with his nephew, partly
to indulge his own inclination (for he dearly love his bottle), and
partly to disqualify his nephew from the immediate execution of his
purpose, he order wine to be set on the table; with which he so
briskly ply the young gentleman, that this latter, who, though not
much use to drinking, do not detest it so as to be guilty of
disobedience or want of complacence by refuse, be soon completely
finish.
just as the uncle have obtain this victory, and be prepare a bed

entirely disconcert and shock he, that he in a moment lose all
consideration for his nephew, and his whole mind become entirely take
up with his own concern.
this sudden and afflict news be no less than that his daughter have
take the opportunity of almost the first moment of his absence, and

her father could have have but one objection, namely, that he be worth
nothing, yet she have never think proper to communicate she amour

never be once suspect by any, till now that it be consummate.
Old Mr Nightingale no soon receive this account, than in the utmost

the uncle thus depart, when the servant come to attend the nephew to

tender he, insist on a chair be call; with this the servant,
who have receive no strict order to the contrary, readily comply;
and, thus be conduct back to the house of Mrs Miller, he have
stagger up to Mr Jones's chamber, as hath be before recount.
this bar of the uncle be now remove (though young Nightingale know
not as yet in what manner), and all party be quickly ready, the
mother, Mr Jones, Mr Nightingale, and his love, stept into a
hackney-coach, which convey they to doctor' Commons; where Miss
Nancy be, in vulgar language, soon make an honest woman, and the poor
mother become, in the pure sense of the word, one of the happy of
all human being.
and now Mr Jones, have see his good office to that poor woman and
her family bring to a happy conclusion, begin to apply himself to
his own concern; but here, lest many of my reader should censure his
folly for thus trouble himself with the affair of other, and lest
some few should think he act more disinterestedly than indeed he
do, we think proper to assure our reader, that he be so far from
be unconcerned in this matter, that he have indeed a very
considerable interest in bring it to that final consummation.
to explain this seem paradox at once, he be one who could truly
say with he in Terence, _homo sum: humani nihil a me alienum puto_.
he be never an indifferent spectator of the misery or happiness of
any one; and he feel either the one or the other in great proportion
as he himself contribute to either. he could not, therefore, be the
instrument of raise a whole family from the low state of
wretchedness to the high pitch of joy without convey great
felicity to himself; more perhaps than worldly man often purchase to
themselves by undergo the most severe labour, and often by wad
through the deep iniquity.
those reader who be of the same complexion with he will perhaps
think this short chapter contain abundance of matter; while other
may probably wish, short as it be, that it have be totally spar as
impertinent to the main design, which I suppose they conclude be to
bring Mr Jones to the gallow, or, if possible, to a more deplorable
catastrophe.
chapter ix.
contain love-letter of several sort.
Mr Jones, at his return home, find the following letter lie on his
table, which he luckily open in the order they be send.
LETTER I.
"surely I be under some strange infatuation; I cannot keep my
resolution a moment, however strongly make or justly found. last
night I resolve never to see you more; this morning I be willing to
hear if you can, as you say, clear up this affair. and yet I know
that to be impossible. I have say everything to myself which you
can invent.----perhap not. perhaps your invention be strong. come
to I, therefore, the moment you receive this. if you can forge an
excuse I almost promise you to believe it. betrayed too----i will
think no more.----come to I directly.----This be the third letter I
have writ, the two former be burnt----I be almost inclined to burn
this too----i wish I may preserve my senses.----come to I
presently."
LETTER II.
"if you ever expect to be forgive, or even suffer within my
door, come to I this instant."
LETTER III.
"I now find you be not at home when my note come to your lodging.
the moment you receive this let I see you;--I shall not stir out;
nor shall anybody be let in but yourself. sure nothing can detain
you long."
Jones have just read over these three billet when Mr Nightingale come
into the room. "well, Tom," say he, "any news from Lady Bellaston,
after last night's adventure?" (for it be now no secret to any one in
that house who the lady be). "the Lady Bellaston?" answer Jones
very gravely.----"Nay, dear Tom," cry Nightingale, "donot be so
reserve to your friend. though I be too drunk to see her last
night, I see she at the masquerade. do you think I be ignorant who the
queen of the fairy be?" "and do you really then know the lady at
the masquerade?" say Jones. "yes, upon my soul, do I," say
Nightingale, "and have give you twenty hint of it since, though you
seem always so tender on that point, that I would not speak plainly.
I fancy, my friend, by your extreme nicety in this matter, you be not
so well acquainted with the character of the lady as with her person.
donot be angry, Tom, but upon my honour, you be not the first young
fellow she hath debauch. her reputation be in no danger, believe
I."
Though Jones have no reason to imagine the lady to have be of the
vestal kind when his amour begin; yet, as he be thoroughly ignorant
of the town, and have very little acquaintance in it, he have no
knowledge of that character which be vulgarly call a demirep; that
be to say, a woman who intrigue with every man she like, under the
name and appearance of virtue; and who, though some over-nice lady
will not be see with she, be visit (as they term it) by the whole
town, in short, whom everybody know to be what nobody call she.
when he find, therefore, that Nightingale be perfectly acquaint
with his intrigue, and begin to suspect that so scrupulous a delicacy
as he have hitherto observe be not quite necessary on the occasion,
he give a latitude to his friend's tongue, and desire he to speak
plainly what he know, or have ever hear of the lady.
Nightingale, who, in many other instance, be rather too effeminate
in his disposition, have a pretty strong inclination to tittle-tattle.
he have no soon, therefore, receive a full liberty of speak from
Jones, than he enter upon a long narrative concern the lady;
which, as it contain many particular highly to her dishonour, we
have too great a tenderness for all woman of condition to repeat. we
would cautiously avoid give an opportunity to the future
commentator on our work, of make any malicious application and of
force we to be, against our will, the author of scandal, which never
enter into our head.
Jones, have very attentively hear all that Nightingale have to say,
fetch a deep sigh; which the other, observe, cry, "Heyday! why,
thou art not in love, I hope! have I imagine my story would have
affect you, I promise you should never have hear they." "o my dear
friend!" cry Jones, "I be so entangle with this woman, that I know
not how to extricate myself. in love, indeed! no, my friend, but I be
under obligation to she, and very great one. since you know so much,
I will be very explicit with you. it be owing, perhaps, solely to she,
that I have not, before this, want a bit of bread. how can I
possibly desert such a woman? and yet I must desert she, or be guilty
of the black treachery to one who deserve infinitely well of I
than she can; a woman, my Nightingale, for whom I have a passion which
few can have an idea of. I be half distracted with doubt how to act."
"and be this other, pray, an honourable mistress?" cry Nightingale.
"honourable!" answer Jones; "no breath ever yet durst sully her
reputation. the sweetest air be not pure, the limpid stream not
clear, than her honour. she be all over, both in mind and body,
consummate perfection. she be the most beautiful creature in the
universe: and yet she be mistress of such noble elevated quality,
that, though she be never from my thought, I scarce ever think of her
beauty but when I see it."--"And can you, my good friend," cry
Nightingale, "with such an engagement as this upon your hand,
hesitate a moment about quit such a--" "hold," say Jones, "no
more abuse of she: I det the thought of ingratitude." "Pooh!"
answer the other, "you be not the first upon whom she hath
confer obligation of this kind. she be remarkably liberal where
she like; though, let I tell you, her favour be so prudently
bestow, that they should rather raise a man's vanity than his
gratitude." in short, Nightingale proceed so far on this head, and
tell his friend so many story of the lady, which he swear to the
truth of, that he entirely remove all esteem for she from the breast
of Jones; and his gratitude be lessen in proportion. indeed, he
begin to look on all the favour he have receive rather as wage than
benefit, which depreciate not only she, but himself too in his own
conceit, and put he quite out of humour with both. from this disgust,
his mind, by a natural transition, turn towards Sophia; her virtue,
her purity, her love to he, her suffering on his account, fill all
his thought, and make his commerce with Lady Bellaston appear still
more odious. the result of all be, that, though his turn himself
out of her service, in which light he now see his affair with she,
would be the loss of his bread; yet he determine to quit she, if he
could but find a handsome pretence: which be communicate to his
friend, Nightingale consider a little, and then say, "I have it, my
boy! I have find out a sure method; propose marriage to she, and I
would venture hang upon the success." "marriage?" cry Jones. "ay,
propose marriage," answer Nightingale, "and she will declare off in
a moment. I know a young fellow whom she keep formerly, who make the
offer to she in earnest, and be presently turn off for his pain."
Jones declare he could not venture the experiment. "perhaps," say
he, "she may be less shocked at this proposal from one man than from
another. and if she should take I at my word, where be I then?
catch, in my own trap, and undo for ever." "no;" answer
Nightingale, "not if I can give you an expedient by which you may at
any time get out of the trap."----"What expedient can that be?"
reply Jones. "this," answer Nightingale. "the young fellow I
mention, who be one of the most intimate acquaintance I have in the
world, be so angry with she for some ill office she hath since do
he, that I be sure he would, without any difficulty, give you a sight
of her letter; upon which you may decently break with she; and
declare off before the knot be tye, if she should really be willing
to tie it, which I be convinced she will not."
after some hesitation, Jones, upon the strength of this assurance,
consent; but, as he swear he want the confidence to propose the
matter to her face, he write the following letter, which Nightingale
dictated:--
"MADAM,
"I be extremely concerned, that, by an unfortunate engagement
abroad, I should have miss receive the honour of your ladyship's
command the moment they come; and the delay which I must now suffer
of vindicate myself to your ladyship greatly add to this
misfortune. o, Lady Bellaston! what a terror have I be in for fear
your reputation should be expose by these perverse accident! there
be one only way to secure it. I need not name what that be. only
permit I to say, that as your honour be as dear to I as my own, so
my sole ambition be to have the glory of lay my liberty at your
foot; and believe I when I assure you, I can never be make
completely happy without you generously bestow on I a legal right
of call you mine for ever.--I am,
madam,
with most profound respect,
your ladyship's most oblige,
obedient, humble servant,
THOMAS JONES."
to this she presently return the follow answer:
"SIR,
"when I read over your serious epistle, I could, from its coldness
and formality, have swear that you already have the legal right you
mention; nay, that we have for many year compose that monstrous
animal a husband and wife. do you really then imagine I a fool? or
do you fancy yourself capable of so entirely persuade I out of my
sens, that I should deliver my whole fortune into your power, in
order to enable you to support your pleasure at my expense? be
these the proof of love which I expect? be this the return for--?
but I scorn to upbraid you, and be in great admiration of your
profound respect.
"P.S. I be prevent from revising:----perhap I have say more than
I meant.----come to I at eight this evening."
Jones, by the advice of his privy-council, reply:
"MADAM,
"it be impossible to express how much I be shock at the suspicion
you entertain of I. can Lady Bellaston have confer favour on a
man whom she could believe capable of so base a design? or can she
treat the most solemn tie of love with contempt? can you imagine,
madam, that if the violence of my passion, in an unguarded moment,
overcome the tenderness which I have for your honour, I would think
of indulge myself in the continuance of an intercourse which could
not possibly escape long the notice of the world; and which, when
discover, must prove so fatal to your reputation? if such be your
opinion of I, I must pray for a sudden opportunity of return
those pecuniary obligation, which I have be so unfortunate to
receive at your hand; and for those of a more tender kind, I shall
ever remain, &c." and so conclude in the very word with which he
have conclude the former letter.
the lady answer as follow:
"I see you be a villain! and I despise you from my soul. if you
come here I shall not be at home."
Though Jones be well satisfied with his deliverance from a thraldom
which those who have ever experience it will, I apprehend, allow to
be none of the light, he be not, however, perfectly easy in his
mind. there be in this scheme too much of fallacy to satisfy one who
utterly detest every specie of falshood or dishonesty: nor would
he, indeed, have submit to put it in practice, have he not be
involve in a distressful situation, where he be oblige to be guilty
of some dishonour, either to the one lady or the other; and surely the
