reader will allow, that every good principle, as well as love, plead
strongly in favour of Sophia.
Nightingale highly exult in the success of his stratagem, upon which
he receive many thank and much applause from his friend. he
answer, "Dear Tom, we have confer very different obligation on
each other. to I you owe the regain your liberty; to you I owe the
loss of mine. but if you be as happy in the one instance as I be in
the other, I promise you we be the two happy fellow in England."
the two gentleman be now summon down to dinner, where Mrs Miller,
who perform herself the office of cook, have exert her good talent
to celebrate the wedding of her daughter. this joyful circumstance she
ascribe principally to the friendly behaviour of Jones, her whole
soul be fire with gratitude towards he, and all her look, word,
and action, be so busied in express it, that her daughter, and
even her new son-in-law, be very little object of her
consideration.
Dinner be just end when Mrs Miller receive a letter; but as we
have have letter enow in this chapter, we shall communicate its
content in our next.
Chapter x.
consist partly of fact, and partly of observation upon they.
the letter then which arrive at the end of the precede chapter be
from Mr Allworthy, and the purport of it be, his intention to come
immediately to town, with his nephew Blifil, and a desire to be
accommodate with his usual lodging, which be the first floor for
himself, and the second for his nephew.
the chearfulness which have before display itself in the countenance
of the poor woman be a little clouded on this occasion. this news do
indeed a good deal disconcert she. to requite so disintereste a match
with her daughter, by presently turn her new son-in-law out of
door, appear to her very unjustifiable on the one hand; and on the
other, she could scarce bear the thought of make any excuse to Mr
Allworthy, after all the obligation receive from he, for deprive
he of lodging which be indeed strictly his due; for that
gentleman, in confer all his numberless benefit on other, act
by a rule diametrically opposite to what be practise by most generous
people. he contrive, on all occasion, to hide his beneficence, not
only from the world, but even from the object of it. he constantly
use the word Lend and Pay, instead of Give; and by every other
method he could invent, always lessen with his tongue the favour he
confer, while he be heap they with both his hand. when he
settle the annuity of £50 a year therefore on Mrs Miller, he tell
she, "it be in consideration of always have her first-floor when he
be in town (which he scarce ever intend to be), but that she might
let it at any other time, for that he would always send she a month's
warning." he be now, however, hurry to town so suddenly, that he
have no opportunity of give such notice; and this hurry probably
prevent he, when he write for his lodging, add, if they be
then empty; for he would most certainly have be well satisfied to
have relinquish they, on a less sufficient excuse than what Mrs
Miller could now have make.
but there be a sort of person, who, as Prior excellently well
remark, direct their conduct by something
beyond the fix'd and settle rule
of vice and virtue in the school,
beyond the letter of the law.
to these it be so far from be sufficient that their defence would
acquit they at the Old Bailey, that they be not even content,
though conscience, the severest of all judge, should discharge they.
nothing short of the fair and honourable will satisfy the delicacy of
their mind; and if any of their action fall short of this mark, they
mope and pine, be as uneasy and restless as a murderer, who be afraid
of a ghost, or of the hangman.
Mrs Miller be one of these. she could not conceal her uneasiness at
this letter; with the content of which she have no soon acquaint
the company, and give some hint of her distress, than Jones, her
good angel, presently relieve her anxiety. "as for myself, madam,"
say he, "my lodging be at your service at a moment's warning; and Mr
Nightingale, I be sure, as he cannot yet prepare a house fit to
receive his lady, will consent to return to his new lodging, whither
Mrs Nightingale will certainly consent to go." with which proposal
both husband and wife instantly agree.
the reader will easily believe, that the cheek of Mrs Miller begin
again to glow with additional gratitude to Jones; but, perhaps, it may
be more difficult to persuade he, that Mr Jones have in his last
speech call her daughter Mrs Nightingale (it be the first time
that agreeable sound have ever reach her ear), give the fond mother
more satisfaction, and warm her heart more towards Jones, than his
have dissipate her present anxiety.
the next day be then appoint for the removal of the new-married
couple, and of Mr Jones, who be likewise to be provide for in the
same house with his friend. and now the serenity of the company be
again restore, and they past the day in the utmost chearfulness, all
except Jones, who, though he outwardly accompany the rest in their
mirth, feel many a bitter pang on the account of his Sophia, which
be not a little heighten by the news of Mr Blifil's come to town
(for he clearly see the intention of his journey); and what greatly
aggravate his concern be, that Mrs Honour, who have promise to
inquire after Sophia, and to make her report to he early the next
evening, have disappoint he.
in the situation that he and his mistress be in at this time, there
be scarce any ground for he to hope that he should hear any good
news; yet he be as impatient to see Mrs Honour as if he have expect
she would bring he a letter with an assignation in it from Sophia,
and bear the disappointment as ill. whether this impatience arise from
that natural weakness of the human mind, which make it desirous to
know the bad, and render uncertainty the most intolerable of pain;
or whether he still flatter himself with some secret hope, we will
not determine. but that it might be the last, whoever have love cannot
but know. for of all the power exercise by this passion over our
mind, one of the most wonderful be that of support hope in the
midst of despair. difficulty, improbability, nay, impossibility,
be quite overlook by it; so that to any man extremely in love, may
be apply what Addison say of Caesar,
"the Alps, and Pyrenaeans, sink before he!"
yet it be equally true, that the same passion will sometimes make
mountain of molehill, and produce despair in the midst of hope; but
these cold fit last not long in good constitution. which temper
Jones be now in, we leave the reader to guess, have no exact
information about it; but this be certain, that he have spend two hour
in expectation, when, be unable any long to conceal his
uneasiness, he retire to his room; where his anxiety have almost make
he frantick, when the following letter be bring he from Mrs
Honour, with which we shall present the reader _verbatim et
literatim._
"SIR,
"I shud sartenly haf kale on you a cordin too mi prommiss haddunt
itt bin that hur lashipp prevent mee; for to bee sur, Sir, you nose
very well that evere persun must luk furst at ome, and sartenly such
anuther offar mite not have ever hapne, so as I shud ave bin justly
to blam, have I not excepted of it when her lashipp be so veri kind
as to offar to mak mee hur one uman without mi ever askin any such
thing, to be sur shee be win of thee good ladi in thee wurld, and
pepil who sase to the kontrari must bee veri wiket pepil in thare
hart. to bee sur if ever I ave sad any thing of that kine it as bin
thru ignoren, and I be hartili sorri for it. I nose your onur to be
a genteelman of more onur and onesty, if I ever say ani such thing,
to repete it to hurt a pore servant that as alwais add thee grat
respect in thee wurld for ure onur. to be sur win shud kepe won
tung within won tooth, for no boddi nose what may hapen; and to bee
sur if ani boddi ad tolde mee yesterday, that I shud haf bin in so
gud a plase to day, I shud not haf beleeve it; for to be sur I
never be a dremd of any such thing, nor shud I ever have soft after
ani other bodi's plase; but as her lashipp wass so kine of her one a
cord too give it mee without askin, to be sur Mrs Etoff herself, nor
no other boddi can blam mee for exceptin such a thing when it fal
in mi waye. I beg ure Onur not to menshion ani thing of what I haf
sad, for I wish ure Onur all thee gud luk in the wurld; and I donot
cuestion butt thatt u will haf Madam Sofia in the end; butt ass to
miself ure onur nose I kant bee of ani farder sarvis to u in that
matar, nou bein under thee cumand off anuther parson, and nott mi
one mistress, I begg ure Onur to say nothing of what past, and
belive I to be, sir, ure Onur's umble servant to cumand till deth,
"HONOUR BLACKMORE."
Various be the conjecture which Jones entertain on this step of
Lady Bellaston; who, in reality, have little farth design than to
secure within her own house the repository of a secret, which she
choose should make no farth progress than it have make already; but
mostly, she desire to keep it from the ear of Sophia; for though
that young lady be almost the only one who would never have repeat
it again, her ladyship could not persuade herself of this; since, as
she now hat poor Sophia with most implacable hatred, she conceive a
reciprocal hatred to herself to be lodge in the tender breast of our
heroine, where no such passion have ever yet find an entrance.
while Jones be terrify himself with the apprehension of a thousand
dreadful machination, and deep political design, which he imagine
to be at the bottom of the promotion of Honour, Fortune, who hitherto
seem to have be an utter enemy to his match with Sophia, try a
new method to put a final end to it, by throw a temptation in his
way, which in his present desperate situation it seem unlikely he
should be able to resist.
Chapter xi.
contain curious, but not unprecedented matter.
there be a lady, one Mrs Hunt, who have often see Jones at the house
where he lodge, be intimately acquaint with the woman there, and
indeed a very great friend to Mrs Miller. her age be about thirty,
for she own six-and-twenty; her face and person very good, only
incline a little too much to be fat. she have be marry young by
her relation to an old Turkey merchant, who, have get a great
fortune, have leave off trade. with he she live without reproach, but
not without pain, in a state of great self-denial, for about twelve
year; and her virtue be reward by his dying and leave she very
rich. the first year of her widowhood be just at an end, and she have
past it in a good deal of retirement, see only a few particular
friend, and divide her time between her devotion and novel, of
which she be always extremely fond. very good health, a very warm
constitution, and a good deal of religion, make it absolutely
necessary for she to marry again; and she resolve to please herself
in her second husband, as she have do her friend in the first. from
she the follow billet be bring to jones:--
"SIR,
"from the first day I see you, I doubt my eye have tell you too
plainly that you be not indifferent to I; but neither my tongue
nor my hand should have ever avow it, have not the lady of the
family where you be lodge give I such a character of you, and
tell I such proof of your virtue and goodness, as convince I you
be not only the most agreeable, but the most worthy of man. I have
also the satisfaction to hear from they, that neither my person,
understanding, or character, be disagreeable to you. I have a
fortune sufficient to make we both happy, but which cannot make I
so without you. in thus dispose of myself, I know I shall incur
the censure of the world; but if I do not love you more than I fear
the world, I should not be worthy of you. one only difficulty stop
I: I be inform you be engage in a commerce of gallantry with a
woman of fashion. if you think it worth while to sacrifice that to
the possession of I, I be your; if not, forget my weakness, and
let this remain an eternal secret between you and
"ARABELLA HUNT."
at the reading of this, Jones be put into a violent flutter. his
fortune be then at a very low ebb, the source be stopt from which
hitherto he have be supply. of all he have receive from Lady
Bellaston, not above five guineas remain; and that very morning he
have be dun by a tradesman for twice that sum. his honourable
mistress be in the hand of her father, and he have scarce any hope
ever to get she out of they again. to be subsist at her expense,
from that little fortune she have independent of her father, go much
against the delicacy both of his pride and his love. this lady's
fortune would have be exceed convenient to he, and he could have
no objection to she in any respect. on the contrary, he like she as
well as he do any woman except Sophia. but to abandon Sophia, and
marry another, that be impossible; he could not think of it upon any
account, yet why should he not, since it be plain she could not be
his? would it not be kind to she, than to continue she long
engage in a hopeless passion for he? ought he not to do so in
friendship to she? this notion prevail some moment, and he have
almost determined to be false to she from a high point of honour: but
that refinement be not able to stand very long against the voice of
nature, which cry in his heart that such friendship be treason to
love. at last he call for pen, ink, and paper, and writ as follow
to Mrs Hunt:--
"MADAM,
"it would be but a poor return to the favour you have do I to
sacrifice any gallantry to the possession of you, and I would
certainly do it, though I be not disengage, as at present I be,
from any affair of that kind. but I should not be the honest man you
think I, if I do not tell you that my affection be engage to
another, who be a woman of virtue, and one that I never can leave,
though it be probable I shall never possess she. God forbid that, in
return of your kindness to I, I should do you such an injury as to
give you my hand when I cannot give my heart. no; I have much rather
starve than be guilty of that. even though my mistress be marry
to another, I would not marry you unless my heart have entirely
efface all impression of she. be assure that your secret be not
more safe in your own breast, than in that of your most oblige, and
grateful humble servant,
"T. JONES."
when our heroe have finish and send this letter, he go to his
scrutore, take out Miss Western's muff, kiss it several time, and
then strut some turn about his room, with more satisfaction of
mind than ever any Irishman feel in carry off a fortune of fifty
thousand pound.
Chapter xii.
a discovery make by Partridge.
while Jones be exult in the consciousness of his integrity,
Partridge come caper into the room, as be his custom when he
bring, or fancy he bring, any good tiding. he have be
despatch that morning by his master, with order to endeavour, by
the servant of Lady Bellaston, or by any other mean, to discover
whither Sophia have be convey; and he now return, and with a
joyful countenance tell our heroe that he have find the lose bird. "I
have see, sir," say he, "Black George, the gamekeeper, who be one of
the servant whom the squire hath bring with he to town. I know he
presently, though I have not see he these several year; but you
know, sir, he be a very remarkable man, or, to use a purer phrase, he
hath a most remarkable beard, the large and black I ever see. it
be some time, however, before Black George could recollect I."
"well, but what be your good news?" cry Jones; "what do you know of
my Sophia?" "you shall know presently, sir," answer Partridge, "I be
come to it as fast as I can. you be so impatient, sir, you would
come at the infinitive mood before you can get to the imperative. as I
be say, sir, it be some time before he recollect my
face."--"Confound your face!" cry Jones, "what of my Sophia?" "Nay,
sir," answer Partridge, "I know nothing more of Madam Sophia than
what I be go to tell you; and I should have tell you all before
this if you have not interrupt I; but if you look so angry at I you
will frighten all of it out of my head, or, to use a purer phrase, out
of my memory. I never see you look so angry since the day we leave
Upton, which I shall remember if I be to live a thousand
year."--"Well, pray go on your own way," say Jones: "you be
resolve to make I mad I find." "not for the world," answer
Partridge, "I have suffer enough for that already; which, as I say,
I shall bear in my remembrance the long day I have to live." "well,
but Black George?" cry Jones. "well, sir, as I be say, it be a
long time before he could recollect I; for, indeed, I be very much
alter since I see he. _Non sum quali eram._ I have have trouble in
the world, and nothing alter a man so much as grief. I have hear it
will change the colour of a man's hair in a night. however, at last,
know I he do, thatbe sure enough; for we be both of an age, and
be at the same charity school. George be a great dunce, but no
matter for that; all man do not thrive in the world accord to their
learning. I be sure I have reason to say so; but it will be all one a
thousand year hence. well, sir, where be I?--O--well, we no soon
know each other, than, after many hearty shake by the hand, we agree
to go to an alehouse and take a pot, and by good luck the beer be
some of the good I have meet with since I have be in town. now, sir,
I be come to the point; for no soon do I name you, and tell he
that you and I come to town together, and have live together ever
since, than he call for another pot, and swear he would drink to
your health; and indeed he drink your health so heartily that I be
overjoyed to see there be so much gratitude leave in the world; and
after we have empty that pot I say I would buy my pot too, and so we
drink another to your health; and then I make haste home to tell you
the news."
"what news?" cry Jones, "you have not mention a word of my
Sophia!" "bless I! I have like to have forget that. indeed, we
mention a great deal about young Madam Western, and George tell I
all; that Mr Blifil be come to town in order to be married to she.
he have well make haste then, say I, or somebody will have she before
he come; and, indeed, say I, Mr Seagrim, it be a thousand pity
somebody should not have she; for he certainly love she above all the
woman in the world. I would have both you and she know, that it be not
for her fortune he follow she; for I can assure you, as to matter of
that, there be another lady, one of much great quality and fortune
than she can pretend to, who be so fond of somebody that she come
after he day and night."
here Jones fall into a passion with Partridge, for have, as he say,
betray he; but the poor fellow answer, he have mention no name:
"besides, sir," say he, "I can assure you George be sincerely your
friend, and wish Mr Blifil at the devil more than once; nay, he say
he would do anything in his power upon earth to serve you; and so I be
convince he will. betray you, indeed! why, I question whether you
have a well friend than George upon earth, except myself, or one
that would go far to serve you."
"well," say Jones, a little pacify, "you say this fellow, who, I
believe, indeed, be enough incline to be my friend, live in the same
house with Sophia?"
"in the same house!" answer Partridge; "why, sir, he be one of the
servant of the family, and very well drest I promise you he be; if it
be not for his black beard you would hardly know he."
"one service then at least he may do I," say Jones: "sure he can
certainly convey a letter to my Sophia."
"you have hit the nail _ad unguem_" cry Partridge; "how come I not
to think of it? I will engage he shall do it upon the very first
mention."
"well, then," say Jones, "do you leave I at present, and I will
write a letter, which you shall deliver to he to-morrow morning; for
I suppose you know where to find he."
"o yes, sir," answer Partridge, "I shall certainly find he again;
there be no fear of that. the liquor be too good for he to stay away
long. I make no doubt but he will be there every day he stay in
town."
"so you donot know the street then where my Sophia be lodge?" cry
Jones.
"indeed, sir, I do," say Partridge.
"what be the name of the street?" cry Jones.
"the name, sir? why, here, sir, just by," answer Partridge, "not
above a street or two off. I donot, indeed, know the very name; for,
as he never tell I, if I have ask, you know, it might have put some
suspicion into his head. no, no, sir, let I alone for that. I be too
cunning for that, I promise you."
"thou art most wonderfully cunning, indeed," reply Jones; "however,
I will write to my charmer, since I believe you will be cunning enough
to find he to-morrow at the alehouse."
and now, have dismiss the sagacious Partridge, Mr Jones sit
himself down to write, in which employment we shall leave he for a
time. and here we put an end to the fifteenth book.
BOOK XVI.
contain the SPACE of five day.
Chapter i.
of prologue.
I have hear of a dramatic writer who use to say, he would rather
write a play than a prologue; in like manner, I think, I can with less
pain write one of the book of this history than the prefatory
chapter to each of they.
to say the truth, I believe many a hearty curse hath be devoted on
the head of that author who first institute the method of prefix
to his play that portion of matter which be call the prologue; and
which at first be part of the piece itself, but of latter year hath
have usually so little connexion with the drama before which it stand,
that the prologue to one play might as well serve for any other. those
indeed of more modern date, seem all to be write on the same three
topic, viz., an abuse of the taste of the town, a condemnation of all
contemporary author, and an eulogium on the performance just about to
be represent. the sentiment in all these be very little varied,
nor be it possible they should; and indeed I have often wonder at
the great invention of author, who have be capable of find such
various phrase to express the same thing.
in like manner I apprehend, some future historian (if any one shall do
I the honour of imitate my manner) will, after much scratch his
pate, bestow some good wish on my memory, for have first
establish these several initial chapter; most of which, like modern
prologue, may as properly be prefix to any other book in this
history as to that which they introduce, or indeed to any other
history as to this.
but however author may suffer by either of these invention, the
reader will find sufficient emolument in the one as the spectator hath
long find in the other.
first, it be well know that the prologue serve the critic for an
opportunity to try his faculty of hiss, and to tune his cat-call to
the good advantage; by which mean, I have know those musical
instrument so well prepare, that they have be able to play in full
concert at the first rise of the curtain.
the same advantage may be draw from these chapter, in which the
critic will be always sure of meet with something that may serve as
a whetstone to his noble spirit; so that he may fall with a more
hungry appetite for censure on the history itself. and here his
sagacity must make it needless to observe how artfully these chapter
be calculate for that excellent purpose; for in these we have always
take care to intersperse somewhat of the sour or acid kind, in order
to sharpen and stimulate the say spirit of criticism.
again, the indolent reader, as well as spectator, find great
advantage from both these; for, as they be not oblige either to see
the one or read the other, and both the play and the book be thus
protract, by the former they have a quarter of an hour long
allow they to sit at dinner, and by the latter they have the
advantage of begin to read at the fourth or fifth page instead of
the first, a matter by no mean of trivial consequence to person who
read book with no other view than to say they have read they, a more
general motive to reading than be commonly imagine; and from which
not only law book, and good book, but the page of Homer and Virgil,
of Swift and Cervantes, have be often turn over.
many other be the emolument which arise from both these, but they
be for the most part so obvious, that we shall not at present stay to
enumerate they; especially since it occur to we that the principal
merit of both the prologue and the preface be that they be short.
Chapter ii.
a whimsical adventure which befel the squire, with the distressed
situation of Sophia.
we must now convey the reader to Mr Western's lodging, which be in
Piccadilly, where he be place by the recommendation of the landlord
at the Hercules Pillars at Hyde Park Corner; for at the inn, which be
the first he see on his arrival in town, he place his horse, and in
those lodging, which be the first he hear of, he deposit
himself.
here, when Sophia alight from the hackney-coach, which bring she
from the house of Lady Bellaston, she desire to retire to the
apartment provide for she; to which her father very readily agree,
and whither he attend she himself. a short dialogue, neither very
material nor pleasant to relate minutely, then pass between they, in
which he press she vehemently to give her consent to the marriage
with Blifil, who, as he acquaint she, be to be in town in a few
day; but, instead of comply, she give a more peremptory and
resolute refusal than she have ever do before. this so incense she
father, that after many bitter vow, that he would force she to have
he whether she would or no, he depart from she with many hard word
and curse, lock the door, and put the key into his pocket.
while Sophia be leave with no other company than what attend the
close state prisoner, namely, fire and candle, the squire sit down
to regale himself over a bottle of wine, with his parson and the
landlord of the Hercules Pillars, who, as the squire say, would make
an excellent third man, and could inform they of the news of the town,
and how affair go; for to be sure, say he, he know a great deal,
since the horse of many of the quality stand at his house.
in this agreeable society Mr Western past that evening and great part
of the succeed day, during which period nothing happen of
sufficient consequence to find a place in this history. all this time
Sophia past by herself; for her father swear she should never come out
of her chamber alive, unless she first consent to marry Blifil; nor
do he ever suffer the door to be unlocked, unless to convey her food,
on which occasion he always attend himself.
the second morning after his arrival, while he and the parson be at
breakfast together on a toast and tankard, he be inform that a
gentleman be below to wait on he.
"a gentleman!" quoth the squire, "who the devil can he be? do, doctor,
go down and see who 'tis. Mr Blifil can hardly be come to town
yet.--Go down, do, and know what his business be."
the doctor return with an account that it be a very well-dr man,
and by the ribbon in his hat he take he for an officer of the army;
that he say he have some particular business, which he could deliver
to none but Mr Western himself.
"an officer!" cry the squire; "what can any such fellow have to do
with I? if he want an order for baggage-waggon, I be no justice of
peace here, nor can I grant a warrant.--let un come up then, if he
must speak to I."
a very genteel man now enter the room; who, have make his
compliment to the squire, and desire the favour of be alone with
he, deliver himself as follows:--
"Sir, I come to wait upon you by the command of my Lord Fellamar; but
with a very different message from what I suppose you expect, after
what past the other night."
"my lord who?" cry the squire; "I never hear the name o'un."
"his lordship," say the gentleman, "be willing to impute everything
to the effect of liquor, and the most trifling acknowledgment of that
kind will set everything right; for as he hath the most violent
attachment to your daughter, you, sir, be the last person upon earth
from whom he would resent an affront; and happy be it for you both
that he hath give such public demonstration of his courage as to be
able to put up an affair of this kind without danger of any imputation
on his honour. all he desire, therefore, be, that you will before I
make some acknowledgment; the slight in the world will be
sufficient; and he intend this afternoon to pay his respect to you,
in order to obtain your leave of visit the young lady on the
footing of a lover."
"I donot understand much of what you say, sir," say the squire; "but
I suppose, by what you talk about my daughter, that this be the lord
which my cousin, Lady Bellaston, mention to I, and say something
about his court my daughter. if so be that how that be the
case--you may give my service to his lordship, and tell un the girl be
dispose of already."
"perhaps, sir," say the gentleman, "you be not sufficiently apprize
of the greatness of this offer. I believe such a person, title, and
fortune would be nowhere refuse."
"Lookee, sir," answer the squire; "to be very plain, my daughter be
bespoke already; but if she be not, I would not marry she to a lord
upon any account; I hate all lord; they be a parcel of courtier and
Hanoverians, and I will have nothing to do with they."
"well, sir," say the gentleman, "if that be your resolution, the
message I be to deliver to you be that my lord desire the favour of
your company this morning in Hyde Park."
"you may tell my lord," answer the squire, "that I be busy and
cannot come. I have enough to look after at home, and cannot stir
abroad on any account."
"I be sure, sir," quoth the other, "you be too much a gentleman to
send such a message; you will not, I be convince, have it say of
you, that, after have affront a noble peer, you refuse he
satisfaction. his lordship would have be willing, from his great
regard to the young lady, to have make up matter in another way; but
unless he be to look on you as a father, his honour will not suffer
his put up such an indignity as you must be sensible you offer
he."
"I offer he!" cry the squire; "it be a d--n'd lie! I never
offer he anything."
upon these word the gentleman return a very short verbal rebuke,
and this he accompany at the same time with some manual
remonstrance, which no soon reach the ear of Mr Western, than
that worthy squire begin to caper very briskly about the room,
bellowing at the same time with all his might, as if desirous to
summon a great number of spectator to behold his agility.
the parson, who have leave great part of the tankard unfinishe, be not
retire far; he immediately attend therefore on the squire's
vociferation, crying, "bless I! sir, whatbe the matter?"--"matter!"
quoth the squire, "herebe a highwayman, I believe, who want to rob
and murder I--for he hath fall upon I with that stick there in his
hand, when I wish I may be d--n'd if I gid un the least provocation."
"how, sir," say the captain, "do you not tell I I lye?"
"no, as I hope to be save," answer the squire, "--I believe I might
say, 'twas a lie that I have offer any affront to my lord--but I
never say the word, `you lie.'--I understand myself well, and you
might have understand yourself well than to fall upon a naked man.
if I have a stick in my hand, you would not have dare strike I. Iwould
have knock thy lantern jaw about thy ear. come down into yard this
minute, and Iwill take a bout with thee at single stick for a break
head, that I will; or I will go into naked room and box thee for a
belly-full. at unt half a man, at unt, Ibe sure."
the captain, with some indignation, reply, "I see, sir, you be
below my notice, and I shall inform his lordship you be below his. I
be sorry I have dirty my finger with you." at which word he
withdraw, the parson interpose to prevent the squire from stop
he, in which he easily prevail, as the other, though he make some
effort for the purpose, do not seem very violently bent on success.
however, when the captain be depart, the squire send many curse
and some menace after he; but as these do not set out from his lip
till the officer be at the bottom of the stair, and grow loud and
louder as he be more and more remote, they do not reach his ear, or
at least do not retard his departure.
Poor Sophia, however, who, in her prison, hear all her father's
outcry from first to last, begin now first to thunder with her foot,
and afterwards to scream as loudly as the old gentleman himself have
do before, though in a much sweet voice. these scream soon
silence the squire, and turn all his consideration towards his
daughter, whom he love so tenderly, that the least apprehension of
any harm happen to she, throw he presently into agony; for,
except in that single instance in which the whole future happiness of
her life be concern, she be sovereign mistress of his
inclination.
have end his rage against the captain, with swear he would take
the law of he, the squire now mount upstairs to Sophia, whom, as
soon as he have unlock and open the door, he find all pale and
breathless. the moment, however, that she see her father, she
collect all her spirit, and, catch he hold by the hand, she
cry passionately, "o my dear sir, I be almost frightened to death! I
hope to heaven no harm hath happen to you." "no, no," cry the
squire, "no great harm. the rascal hath not hurt I much, but rat I
if I donot ha the la o' un." "pray, dear sir," say she, "tell I
whatbe the matter; who be it that hath insult you?" "I donot know
the name o' un," answer western; "some officer fellow, I suppose,
that we be to pay for beat we; but Iwill make he pay this bout, if
the rascal hath get anything, which I suppose he hath not. for thof he
be drest out so vine, I question whether he have get a voot of land in
the world." "but, dear sir," cry she, "what be the occasion of your
quarrel?" "what should it be, Sophy," answer the squire, "but about
you, Sophy? all my misfortune be about you; you will be the death of
your poor father at last. herebe a varlet of a lord, the Lord know
who, forsooth! who hath a taan a liking to you, and because I would
not gi un my consent, he send I a kallenge. come, do be a good girl,
Sophy, and put an end to all your father's trouble; come, do consent
to ha un; he will be in town within this day or two; do but promise I
to marry un as soon as he come, and you will make I the happy man
in the world, and I will make you the happy woman; you shall have
the fine cloath in London, and the fine jewel, and a coach and
six at your command. I promise Allworthy already to give up half my
estate--od rabbet it! I should hardly stick at give up the whole."
"will my papa be so kind," say she, "as to hear I speak?"--"why wout
ask, Sophy?" cry he, "when dost know I have rather hear thy voice
than the musick of the good pack of dog in england.--hear thee, my
dear little girl! I hope I shall hear thee as long as I live; for if I
be ever to lose that pleasure, I would not gee a brass varden to live
a moment long. indeed, Sophy, you do not know how I love you, indeed
you donot, or you never could have run away and leave your poor father,
who hath no other joy, no other comfort upon earth, but his little
Sophy." at these word the tear stand in his eye; and Sophia (with
the tear stream from her) answer, "indeed, my dear papa, I know
you have love I tenderly, and heaven be my witness how sincerely I
have return your affection; nor could anything but an apprehension
of be force into the arm of this man have drive I to run from a
father whom I love so passionately, that I would, with pleasure,
sacrifice my life to his happiness; nay, I have endeavour to reason
myself into do more, and have almost work up a resolution to
endure the most miserable of all life, to comply with your
inclination. it be that resolution alone to which I could not force
my mind; nor can I ever." here the squire begin to look wild, and the
foam appear at his lip, which Sophia, observing, beg to be hear
out, and then proceed: "if my father's life, his health, or any real
happiness of his be at stake, here stand your resolved daughter; may
heaven blast I if there be a misery I would not suffer to preserve
you!--no, that most detest, most loathsome of all lot would I
embrace. I would give my hand to Blifil for your sake."--"I tell thee,
it will preserve I," answer the father; "it will give I health,
happiness, life, everything.--upon my soul I shall die if dost refuse
I; I shall break my heart, I shall, upon my soul."--"Is it possible,"
say she, "you can have such a desire to make I miserable?"--"i tell
thee noa," answer he loudly, "d--n I if there be a thing upon earth
I would not do to see thee happy."--"And will not my dear papa allow
I to have the least knowledge of what will make I so? if it be true
that happiness consist in opinion, what must be my condition, when I
shall think myself the most miserable of all the wretch upon earth?"
"well think yourself so," say he, "than know it by be marry to
a poor bastardly vagabond." "if it will content you, sir," say
Sophia, "I will give you the most solemn promise never to marry he,
nor any other, while my papa live, without his consent. let I
dedicate my whole life to your service; let I be again your poor
Sophy, and my whole business and pleasure be, as it hath be, to
please and divert you." "Lookee, Sophy," answer the squire, "I be
not to be chouse in this manner. your aunt Western would then have
reason to think I the fool she doth. no, no, Sophy, Iwould have you to
know I have a get more wisdom, and know more of the world, than to
take the word of a woman in a matter where a man be concern." "how,
sir, have I deserve this want of confidence?" say she; "have I ever
break a single promise to you? or have I ever be find guilty of a
falsehood from my cradle?" "Lookee, Sophy," cry he; "thatbe neither
here nor there. I be determine upon this match, and have he you
shall, d--n I if shat unt. D--n I if shat unt, though dost hang
thyself the next morning." at repeat which word he clinch his
fist, knit his brow, bit his lip, and thunder so loud, that the
poor afflict, terrified Sophia sunk tremble into her chair, and,
have not a flood of tear come immediately to her relief, perhaps bad
have follow.
western beheld the deplorable condition of his daughter with no more
contrition or remorse than the turnkey of Newgate feel at view the
agony of a tender wife, when take she last farewel of she
condemn husband; or rather he look down on she with the same
emotion which arise in an honest fair tradesman, who see his debtor
drag to prison for £10, which, though a just debt, the wretch be
wickedly unable to pay. or, to hit the case still more nearly, he feel
the same compunction with a bawd, when some poor innocent, whom she
hath ensnare into her hand, fall into fit at the first proposal of
what be call see company. indeed this resemblance would be exact,
be it not that the bawd hath an interest in what she doth, and the
father, though perhaps he may blindly think otherwise, can, in
reality, have none in urge his daughter to almost an equal
prostitution.
in this condition he leave his poor Sophia, and, depart with a very
vulgar observation on the effect of tear, he lock the room, and
return to the parson, who say everything he durst in behalf of the
young lady, which, though perhaps it be not quite so much as his duty
require, yet be it sufficient to throw the squire into a violent
rage, and into many indecent reflection on the whole body of the
clergy, which we have too great an honour for that sacred function to
commit to paper.
Chapter iii.
what happen to Sophia during her confinement.
the landlady of the house where the squire lodge have begin very early
to entertain a strange opinion of her guest. however, as she be
inform that the squire be a man of vast fortune, and as she have
take care to exact a very extraordinary price for her room, she do
not think proper to give any offence; for, though she be not without
some concern for the confinement of poor Sophia, of whose great
sweetness of temper and affability the maid of the house have make so
favourable a report, which be confirm by all the squire's servant,
yet she have much more concern for her own interest than to provoke
one, whom, as she say, she perceive to be a very hastish kind of a
gentleman.
Though Sophia eat but little, yet she be regularly serve with her
meal; indeed, I believe, if she have like any one rarity, that the
squire, however angry, would have spar neither pain nor cost to
have procure it for she; since, however strange it may appear to some
of my reader, he really doate on his daughter, and to give she any
kind of pleasure be the high satisfaction of his life.
the dinner-hour being arrive, Black George carry she up a pullet,
the squire himself (for he have swear not to part with the key)
attend the door. as George deposit the dish, some compliment
pass between he and Sophia (for he have not see she since she leave
the country, and she treat every servant with more respect than some
person shew to those who be in a very slight degree their
inferior). Sophia would have have he take the pullet back, say,
she could not eat; but George beg she to try, and particularly
recommend to she the egg, of which he say it be full.
all this time the squire be wait at the door; but George be a
great favourite with his master, as his employment be in concern of
the high nature, namely, about the game, and be accustom to take
many liberty. he have officiously carry up the dinner, be, as he
say, very desirous to see his young lady; he make therefore no
scruple of keep his master stand above ten minute, while
civility be pass between he and Sophia, for which he receive
only a good-humour rebuke at the door when he return.
the egg of pullet, partridge, pheasant, &c., be, as George well
know, the most favourite dainty of Sophia. it be therefore no
wonder that he, who be a very good-natured fellow, should take care
to supply she with this kind of delicacy, at a time when all the
servant in the house be afraid she would be starve; for she have
scarce swallow a single morsel in the last forty hour.
though vexation hath not the same effect on all person as it usually
hath on a widow, whose appetite it often render sharp than it can
be render by the air on Bansted down, or Salisbury Plain; yet the
sublime grief, notwithstanding what some people may say to the
contrary, will eat at last. and Sophia, herself, after some little
consideration, begin to dissect the fowl, which she find to be as
full of egg as George have report it.
but, if she be pleased with these, it contain something which would
have delight the Royal Society much more; for if a fowl with three
leg be so invaluable a curiosity, when perhaps time hath produce a
thousand such, at what price shall we esteem a bird which so totally
contradict all the law of animal oeconomy, as to contain a letter in
its belly? Ovid tell we of a flower into which Hyacinthus be
metamorphose, that bear letter on its leaf, which Virgil
recommend as a miracle to the Royal Society of his day; but no age
nor nation hath ever record a bird with a letter in its maw.
but though a miracle of this kind might have engage all the
_Académies des Sciences_ in Europe, and perhaps in a fruitless
enquiry; yet the reader, by barely recollect the last dialogue
which pass between Messieurs Jones and Partridge, will be very
easily satisfied from whence this letter come, and how it find its
passage into the fowl.
Sophia, notwithstanding her long fast, and notwithstanding her
favourite dish be there before she, no soon see the letter than she
immediately snatch it up, tear it open, and read as follows:--
"MADAM,
"be I not sensible to whom I have the honour of writing, I should
endeavour, however difficult, to paint the horror of my mind at the
account bring I by Mrs Honour; but as tenderness alone can have
any true idea of the pang which tenderness be capable of feeling,
so can this most amiable quality, which my Sophia possess in the
most eminent degree, sufficiently inform she what her Jones must
have suffer on this melancholy occasion. be there a circumstance
in the world which can heighten my agony, when I hear of any
misfortune which hath befall you? surely there be one only, and
with that I be accurse. it be, my Sophia, the dreadful
consideration that I be myself the wretched cause. perhaps I here do
myself too much honour, but none will envy I an honour which cost
I so extremely dear. pardon I this presumption, and pardon I a
great still, if I ask you, whether my advice, my assistance, my
presence, my absence, my death, or my torture can bring you any
relief? can the most perfect admiration, the most watchful
observance, the most ardent love, the most melting tenderness, the
most resign submission to your will, make you amend for what you
be to sacrifice to my happiness? if they can, fly, my lovely angel,
to those arm which be ever open to receive and protect you; and to
which, whether you bring yourself alone, or the rich of the world
with you, be, in my opinion, an alternative not worth regard. if,
on the contrary, wisdom shall predominate, and, on the most mature
reflection, inform you, that the sacrifice be too great; and if
there be no way leave to reconcile your father, and restore the peace
of your dear mind, but by abandon I, I conjure you drive I for
ever from your thought, exert your resolution, and let no
compassion for my suffering bear the least weight in that tender
bosom. believe I, madam, I so sincerely love you well than
myself, that my great and principal end be your happiness. my first
wish (why would not fortune indulge I in it?) be, and pardon I if
I say, still be, to see you every moment the happy of woman; my
second wish be, to hear you be so; but no misery on earth can equal
mine, while I think you owe an uneasy moment to he who be,
Madam,
in every sense, and to every purpose,
your devoted,
THOMAS JONES."
what Sophia say, or do, or think, upon this letter, how often she
read it, or whether more than once, shall all be leave to our reader's
imagination. the answer to it he may perhaps see hereafter, but not at
present: for this reason, among other, that she do not now write
any, and that for several good cause, one of which be this, she have
no paper, pen, nor ink.
in the evening, while Sophia be meditate on the letter she have
receive, or on something else, a violent noise from below disturb
her meditation. this noise be no other than a round bout at
altercation between two person. one of the combatant, by his voice,
she immediately distinguish to be her father; but she do not so
soon discover the shrill pipe to belong to the organ of her aunt
Western, who be just arrive in town, where have, by mean of one
of her servant, who stopt at the Hercules Pillars, learn where she
brother lodge, she drive directly to his lodging.
we shall therefore take our leave at present of Sophia, and, with our
usual good-breeding, attend her ladyship.
chapter iv.
in which Sophia be deliver from her confinement.
the squire and the parson (for the landlord be now otherwise engage)
be smoake their pipe together, when the arrival of the lady be
first signify. the squire no soon hear her name, than he
immediately run down to usher her upstairs; for he be a great
observer of such ceremonial, especially to his sister, of whom he
stand more in awe than of any other human creature, though he never
would own this, nor do he perhaps know it himself.
Mrs Western, on her arrival in the dining-room, have fling herself
into a chair, begin thus to harangue: "well, surely, no one ever have
such an intolerable journey. I think the road, since so many turnpike
act, be grow bad than ever. La, brother, how could you get into
this odious place? no person of condition, I dare swear, ever set foot
here before." "I donot know," cry the squire, "I think they do well
enough; it be landlord recommend they. I think, as he know most
of the quality, he could well shew I where to get among um." "well,
and wherebe my niece?" say the lady; "have you be to wait upon Lady
Bellaston yet?" "ay, ay," cry the squire, "your niece be safe
enough; she be upstairs in chamber." "how!" answer the lady, "be my
niece in this house, and do she not know of my be here?" "no,
nobody can well get to she," say the squire, "for she be under lock
and key. I have she safe; I vetche she from my lady cousin the first
night I come to town, and I have take care o' she ever since; she be
as secure as a fox in a bag, I promise you." "Good heaven!" return
Mrs Western, "what do I hear? I think what a fine piece of work
would be the consequence of my consent to your come to town
yourself; nay, it be indeed your own headstrong will, nor can I
charge myself with have ever consent to it. do not you promise
I, brother, that you would take none of these headstrong measure?
be it not by these headstrong measure that you force my niece to
run away from you in the country? have you a mind to oblige she to
take such another step?" "Z--ds and the devil!" cry the squire,
dash his pipe on the ground; "do ever mortal hear the like? when I
expect you would have commend I for all I have do, to be fall
upon in this manner!" "how, brother!" say the lady, "have I ever
give you the least reason to imagine I should commend you for lock
up your daughter? have I not often tell you that woman in a free
country be not to be treat with such arbitrary power? we be as
free as the man, and I heartily wish I could not say we deserve that
freedom well. if you expect I should stay a moment long in this
wretched house, or that I should ever own you again as my relation, or
that I should ever trouble myself again with the affair of your
family, I insist upon it that my niece be set at liberty this
instant." this she speak with so command an air, stand with she
back to the fire, with one hand behind she, and a pinch of snuff in
the other, that I question whether Thalestris, at the head of her
Amazons, ever make a more tremendous figure. it be no wonder,
therefore, that the poor squire be not proof against the awe which
she inspire. "there," he cry, throw down the key, "there it be,
do whatever you please. I intend only to have keep she up till
Blifil come to town, which cannot be long; and now if any harm happen
in the mean time, remember who be to be blame for it."
"I will answer it with my life," cry Mrs Western, "but I shall not
intermeddle at all, unless upon one condition, and that be, that you
will commit the whole entirely to my care, without take any one
measure yourself, unless I shall eventually appoint you to act. if you
ratify these preliminary, brother, I yet will endeavour to preserve
the honour of your family; if not, I shall continue in a neutral
state."
"I pray you, good sir," say the parson, "permit yourself this once to
be admonish by her ladyship: peradventure, by commune with young
Madam Sophia, she will effect more than you have be able to
perpetrate by more rigorous measure."
"what, dost thee open upon I?" cry the squire: "if thee dost begin
to babble, I shall whip thee in presently."
"Fie, brother," answer the lady, "be this language to a clergyman?
Mr Supple be a man of sense, and give you the good advice; and the
whole world, I believe, will concur in his opinion; but I must tell
you I expect an immediate answer to my categorical proposal. either
cede your daughter to my disposal, or take she wholly to your own
surprizing discretion, and then I here, before Mr Supple, evacuate the
garrison, and renounce you and your family for ever."
"I pray you let I be a mediator," cry the parson, "let I
supplicate you."
"why, there lie the key on the table," cry the squire. "she may
take un up, if she please: who hinder she?"
"no, brother," answer the lady, "I insist on the formality of its
be deliver I, with a full ratification of all the concession
stipulate."
"why then I will deliver it to you.--There 'tis," cry the squire. "I
be sure, sister, you cannot accuse I of ever deny to trust my
daughter to you. she hath a-live wi' you a whole year and muore to a
time, without my ever zeee she."
"and it would have be happy for she," answer the lady, "if she have
always live with I. nothing of this kind would have happen under
my eye."
"ay, certainly," cry he, "I only be to blame."
"why, you be to blame, brother," answer she. "I have be often
oblige to tell you so, and shall always be oblige to tell you so.
however, I hope you will now amend, and gather so much experience from
past error, as not to defeat my wise machination by your blunder.
indeed, brother, you be not qualified for these negociation. all
your whole scheme of politic be wrong. I once more, therefore,
insist, that you do not intermeddle. remember only what be past."----
"Z--ds and bl--d, sister," cry the squire, "what would you have I
say? you be enough to provoke the devil."
"there, now," say she, "just accord to the old custom. I see,
brother, there be no talk to you. I will appeal to Mr Supple, who
be a man of sense, if I say anything which could put any human
creature into a passion; but you be so wrongheade every way."
"let I beg you, madam," say the parson, "not to irritate his
worship."
"irritate he?" say the lady; "sure, you be as great a fool as
himself. well, brother, since you have promise not to interfere, I
will once more undertake the management of my niece. Lord have mercy
upon all affair which be under the direction of man! the head of
one woman be worth a thousand of your." and now have summon a
servant to show she to Sophia, she depart, bear the key with she.
she be no soon go, than the squire (have first shut the door)
ejaculate twenty bitch, and as many hearty curse against she, not
spar himself for have ever thought of her estate; but add, "now
one hath be a slave so long, it would be pity to lose it at last,
for want of hold out a little long. the bitch cannot live for
ever, and I know I be down for it upon the will."
the parson greatly commend this resolution: and now the squire
have order in another bottle, which be his usual method when
anything either pleased or vex he, do, by drink plentifully of
this medicinal julap, so totally wash away his choler, that his temper
be become perfectly placid and serene, when Mrs Western return with
Sophia into the room. the young lady have on her hat and capuchin, and
the aunt acquaint Mr Western, "that she intend to take her niece
with she to her own lodging; for, indeed, brother," say she, "these
room be not fit to receive a christian soul in."
"very well, madam," quoth western, "whatever you please. the girl can
never be in well hand than your; and the parson here can do I the
justice to say, that I have say fifty time behind your back, that
you be one of the most sensible woman in the world."
"to this," cry the parson, "I be ready to bear testimony."
"Nay, brother," say Mrs Western, "I have always, Ibe sure, give you
as favourable a character. you must own you have a little too much
hastiness in your temper; but when you will allow yourself time to
reflect I never know a man more reasonable."
"why then, sister, if you think so," say the squire, "herebe your
good health with all my heart. I be a little passionate sometimes, but
I scorn to bear any malice. Sophy, do you be a good girl, and do
everything your aunt order you."
"I have not the least doubt of she," answer Mrs Western. "she hath
have already an example before her eye in the behaviour of that wretch
her cousin Harriet, who ruin herself by neglect my advice. O
brother, what think you? you be hardly go out of hearing, when you
set out for London, when who should arrive but that impudent fellow
with the odious irish name--that Fitzpatrick. he break in abruptly
upon I without notice, or I would not have see he. he run on a
long, unintelligible story about his wife, to which he force I to
give he a hearing; but I make he very little answer, and deliver
he the letter from his wife, which I bid he answer himself. I
suppose the wretch will endeavour to find we out, but I beg you will
not see she, for I be determined I will not."
"I zee she!" answer the squire; "you need not fear I. Iwill ge no
encouragement to such undutiful wench. it be well for the fellow,
her husband, I be not at huome. od rabbit it, he should have take a
dance thru the horse-pond, I promise un. you zee, Sophy, what
undutifulness bring volk to. you have an example in your own
family."
"Brother," cry the aunt, "you need not shock my niece by such odious
repetition. why will you not leave everything entirely to I?" "well,
well, I wull, I wull," say the squire.
and now Mrs Western, luckily for Sophia, put an end to the
conversation by order chair to be call. I say luckily, for have
it continue much long, fresh matter of dissension would, most
probably, have arise between the brother and sister; between whom
education and sex make the only difference; for both be equally
violent and equally positive: they have both a vast affection for
Sophia, and both a sovereign contempt for each other.
Chapter v.
in which Jones receive a letter from Sophia, and go to a play with
Mrs Miller and Partridge.
the arrival of Black George in town, and the good office which that
grateful fellow have promise to do for his old benefactor, greatly
comfort Jones in the midst of all the anxiety and uneasiness which
he have suffer on the account of Sophia; from whom, by the mean of
the say George, he receive the follow answer to his letter, which
Sophia, to whom the use of pen, ink, and paper be restore with her
liberty, write the very evening when she depart from her
confinement:
"Sir,
"as I do not doubt your sincerity in what you write, you will be
pleased to hear that some of my affliction be at an end, by the
arrival of my aunt Western, with whom I be at present, and with whom
I enjoy all the liberty I can desire. one promise my aunt hath
insist on my making, which be, that I will not see or converse
with any person without her knowledge and consent. this promise I
have most solemnly give, and shall most inviolably keep: and though
she hath not expressly forbid I writing, yet that must be an
omission from forgetfulness; or this, perhaps, be include in the
word converse. however, as I cannot but consider this as a breach
of her generous confidence in my honour, you cannot expect that I
shall, after this, continue to write myself or to receive letter,
without her knowledge. a promise be with I a very sacred thing, and
to be extend to everything understand from it, as well as to what
be express by it; and this consideration may, perhaps, on
reflection, afford you some comfort. but why should I mention a
comfort to you of this kind; for though there be one thing in which
I can never comply with the good of father, yet be I firmly
resolve never to act in defiance of he, or to take any step of
consequence without his consent. a firm persuasion of this must
teach you to divert your thought from what fortune hath (perhaps)
make impossible. this your own interest persuade you. this may
reconcile, I hope, Mr Allworthy to you; and if it will, you have my
injunction to pursue it. accident have lay some obligation on
I, and your good intention probably more. Fortune may, perhaps, be
some time kind to we both than at present. believe this, that I
shall always think of you as I think you deserve, and be,
Sir,
your obliged humble servant,
Sophia Western.
"I charge you write to I no more--at present at least; and accept
this, which be now of no service to I, which I know you must want,
and think you owe the trifle only to that fortune by which you find
it."[*]
[*] meaning, perhaps, the bank-bill for £100.
a child who hath just learn his letter would have spell this letter
out in less time than Jones take in read it. the sensation it
occasion be a mixture of joy and grief; somewhat like what divide
the mind of a good man when he peruse the will of his deceased
friend, in which a large legacy, which his distress make the more
welcome, be bequeath to he. upon the whole, however, he be more
pleased than displeased; and, indeed, the reader may probably wonder
that he be displeased at all; but the reader be not quite so much in
love as be poor Jones; and love be a disease which, though it may, in
some instance, resemble a consumption (which it sometimes cause), in
other proceed in direct opposition to it, and particularly in this,
that it never flatter itself, or see any one symptom in a favourable
light.
one thing give he complete satisfaction, which be, that his mistress
have regain her liberty, and be now with a lady where she might at
least assure herself of a decent treatment. another comfortable
circumstance be the reference which she make to her promise of never
marry any other man; for however disintereste he might imagine his
passion, and notwithstanding all the generous overture make in his
letter, I very much question whether he could have hear a more
afflict piece of news than that Sophia be married to another,
though the match have be never so great, and never so likely to end
in make she completely happy. that refined degree of platonic
affection which be absolutely detach from the flesh, and be, indeed,
entirely and purely spiritual, be a gift confine to the female part
of the creation; many of whom I have hear declare (and, doubtless,
with great truth), that they would, with the utmost readiness, resign
a lover to a rival, when such resignation be prove to be necessary
for the temporal interest of such lover. hence, therefore, I conclude
that this affection be in nature, though I cannot pretend to say I
have ever see an instance of it.
Mr Jones have spend three hour in read and kiss the aforesaid
letter, and be, at last, in a state of good spirit, from the
last-mention consideration, he agree to carry an appointment,
which he have before make, into execution. this be, to attend Mrs
Miller, and her young daughter, into the gallery at the play-house,
and to admit Mr Partridge as one of the company. for as Jones have
really that taste for humour which many affect, he expect to enjoy
much entertainment in the criticism of Partridge, from whom he
expect the simple dictate of nature, unimproved, indeed, but
likewise unadulterated, by art.
in the first row then of the first gallery do Mr Jones, Mrs Miller,
her young daughter, and Partridge, take their place. partridge
immediately declare it be the fine place he have ever be in. when
the first music be play, he say, "it be a wonder how so many
fiddler could play at one time, without put one another out."
while the fellow be light the upper candle, he cry out to Mrs
Miller, "look, look, madam, the very picture of the man in the end of
the common-prayer book before the gunpowder-treason service." nor
could he help observe, with a sigh, when all the candle be
light, "that here be candle enough burn in one night, to keep an
honest poor family for a whole twelvemonth."
as soon as the play, which be Hamlet, Prince of Denmark, begin,
Partridge be all attention, nor do he break silence till the
entrance of the ghost; upon which he ask Jones, "what man that be
in the strange dress; something," say he, "like what I have see in a
picture. sure it be not armour, be it?" Jones answer, "that be the
ghost." to which Partridge reply with a smile, "persuade I to that,
sir, if you can. though I cannot say I ever actually see a ghost in my
life, yet I be certain I should know one, if I see he, well than
that come to. no, no, sir, ghost donot appear in such dress as
that, neither." in this mistake, which cause much laughter in the
neighbourhood of Partridge, he be suffer to continue, till the
scene between the ghost and Hamlet, when Partridge give that credit to
Mr Garrick, which he have deny to Jones, and fall into so violent a
trembling, that his knee knock against each other. Jones ask he
what be the matter, and whether he be afraid of the warrior upon the
stage? "o la! sir," say he, "I perceive now it be what you tell I. I
be not afraid of anything; for I know it be but a play. and if it be
really a ghost, it could do one no harm at such a distance, and in so
much company; and yet if I be frightened, I be not the only person."
"why, who," cry Jones, "dost thou take to be such a coward here
besides thyself?" "Nay, you may call I coward if you will; but if
that little man there upon the stage be not frightened, I never see
any man frighten in my life. ay, ay: go along with you: ay, to be
sure! whobe fool then? will you? Lud have mercy upon such
fool-hardiness!--whatever happen, it be good enough for
you.----follow you? Iwould follow the devil as soon. Nay, perhaps it be
the devil----for they say he can put on what likeness he pleases.--oh!
here he be again.----No far! no, you have go far enough already;
far than Iwould have go for all the king's dominion." Jones
offer to speak, but Partridge cry "hush, hush! dear sir, donot you
hear he?" and during the whole speech of the ghost, he sit with his
eye fix partly on the ghost and partly on Hamlet, and with his
mouth open; the same passion which succeed each other in Hamlet,
succeed likewise in he.
when the scene be over Jones say, "why, Partridge, you exceed my
expectation. you enjoy the play more than I conceive possible."
"Nay, sir," answer Partridge, "if you be not afraid of the devil, I
cannot help it; but to be sure, it be natural to be surprize at such
thing, though I know there be nothing in they: not that it be the
ghost that surprize I, neither; for I should have know that to have
be only a man in a strange dress; but when I see the little man so
frighten himself, it be that which take hold of I." "and dost thou
imagine, then, Partridge," cry Jones, "that he be really
frightened?" "Nay, sir," say Partridge, "do not you yourself observe
afterwards, when he find it be his own father's spirit, and how he
be murder in the garden, how his fear forsook he by degree, and
he be strike dumb with sorrow, as it be, just as I should have
be, have it be my own case?--but hush! o la! what noise be that?
there he be again.----Well, to be certain, though I know there be
nothing at all in it, I be glad I be not down yonder, where those man
be." then turn his eye again upon Hamlet, "ay, you may draw your
sword; what signify a sword against the power of the devil?"
during the second act, Partridge make very few remark. he greatly
admire the fineness of the dress; nor could he help observe upon
the king's countenance. "well," say he, "how people may be deceive
by face! _Nulla fide fronti_ be, I find, a true saying. who would
think, by look in the king's face, that he have ever commit a
murder?" he then enquire after the ghost; but Jones, who intend he
should be surprize, give he no other satisfaction, than, "that he
might possibly see he again soon, and in a flash of fire."
Partridge sit in a fearful expectation of this; and now, when the
ghost make his next appearance, Partridge cry out, "there, sir, now;
what say you now? be he frighten now or no? as much frightened as
you think I, and, to be sure, nobody can help some fear. I would not
be in so bad a condition as whatbe his name, squire Hamlet, be there,
for all the world. bless I! whatbe become of the spirit? as I be a
living soul, I think I see he sink into the earth." "indeed, you
see right," answer Jones. "well, well," cry Partridge, "I know it
be only a play: and besides, if there be anything in all this, Madam
Miller would not laugh so; for as to you, sir, you would not be
afraid, I believe, if the devil be here in person.--there, there--ay,
no wonder you be in such a passion, shake the vile wicked wretch to
piece. if she be my own mother, I would serve she so. to be sure all
duty to a mother be forfeit by such wicked doings.----ay, go about
your business, I hate the sight of you."
our critic be now pretty silent till the play, which Hamlet
introduce before the king. this he do not at first understand, till
Jones explain it to he; but he no soon enter into the spirit of
it, than he begin to bless himself that he have never commit murder.
then turn to Mrs Miller, he ask she, "if she do not imagine the
king look as if he be touch; though he be," say he, "a good
actor, and doth all he can to hide it. well, I would not have so much
to answer for, as that wicked man there hath, to sit upon a much
high chair than he sit upon. no wonder he run away; for your sake
Iwill never trust an innocent face again."
the grave-dig scene next engage the attention of Partridge, who
express much surprize at the number of skull throw upon the stage.
to which Jones answer, "that it be one of the most famous
burial-place about town." "no wonder then," cry Partridge, "that
the place be haunt. but I never see in my life a bad grave-digger.
I have a sexton, when I be clerk, that should have dig three graf

be: I never could bring myself to touch anything belong to a dead
man, on any account.--He seem frightened enough too at the ghost, I
think. _Nemo omnibus horis sapit._"
little more worth remembering occur during the play, at the end of

while Mrs Miller be thus engage in conversation with Partridge, a

thus end the adventure at the playhouse; where Partridge have

he durst not go to bed all that night, for fear of the ghost; and for
many night after sweat two or three hour before he go to sleep,
with the same apprehension, and wake several time in great horror,
