Chapter vi.
in which the history be oblige to look back.
it be almost impossible for the good parent to observe an exact
impartiality to his child, even though no superior merit should
bias his affection; but sure a parent can hardly be blame, when that
superiority determine his preference.
as I regard all the personage of this history in the light of my
child; so I must confess the same inclination of partiality to
Sophia; and for that I hope the reader will allow I the same excuse,
from the superiority of her character.
this extraordinary tenderness which I have for my heroine never
suffer I to quit she any long time without the utmost reluctance. I
could now, therefore, return impatiently to enquire what hath happen
to this lovely creature since her departure from her father's, but
that I be oblige first to pay a short visit to Mr Blifil.
Mr Western, in the first confusion into which his mind be cast upon
the sudden news he receive of his daughter, and in the first hurry to
go after she, have not once think of send any account of the
discovery to Blifil. he have not go far, however, before he
recollect himself, and accordingly stopt at the very first inn he
come to, and dispatch away a messenger to acquaint Blifil with his
have find Sophia, and with his firm resolution to marry she to he
immediately, if he would come up after he to town.
as the love which Blifil have for Sophia be of that violent kind,
which nothing but the loss of her fortune, or some such accident,
could lessen, his inclination to the match be not at all alter by
she have run away, though he be oblige to lay this to his own
account. he very readily, therefore, embrace this offer. indeed, he
now propose the gratification of a very strong passion besides
avarice, by marry this young lady, and this be hatre; for he
conclude that matrimony afford an equal opportunity of satisfy
either hatred or love; and this opinion be very probably verify by
much experience. to say the truth, if we be to judge by the ordinary
behaviour of married person to each other, we shall perhaps be apt to
conclude that the generality seek the indulgence of the former passion
only, in their union of everything but of heart.
there be one difficulty, however, in his way, and this arise from Mr
Allworthy. that good man, when he find by the departure of Sophia
(for neither that, nor the cause of it, could be conceal from he),
the great aversion which she have for his nephew, begin to be seriously
concerned that he have be deceive into carry matter so far. he
by no mean concur with the opinion of those parent, who think it
as immaterial to consult the inclination of their child in the
affair of marriage, as to solicit the good pleasure of their servant
when they intend to take a journey; and who be by law, or decency at
least, withhold often from use absolute force. on the contrary, as
he esteem the institution to be of the most sacred kind, he think
every preparatory caution necessary to preserve it holy and inviolate;
and very wisely conclude, that the sure way to effect this be by
lay the foundation in previous affection.
Blifil indeed soon cure his uncle of all anger on the score of
deceit, by many vow and protestation that he have be deceive
himself, with which the many declaration of Western very well
tally; but now to persuade Allworthy to consent to the renew his
address be a matter of such apparent difficulty, that the very
appearance be sufficient to have deter a less enterprizing genius;
but this young gentleman so well know his own talent, that nothing
within the province of cunning seem to he hard to be achieve.
here then he represent the violence of his own affection, and the
hope of subdue aversion in the lady by perseverance. he beg
that, in an affair on which depend all his future repose, he might
at least be at liberty to try all fair mean for success. Heaven
forbid, he say, that he should ever think of prevail by any other
than the most gentle method! "besides, sir," say he, "if they fail,
you may then (which will be surely time enough) deny your consent." he
urge the great and eager desire which Mr Western have for the match;
and lastly, he make great use of the name of Jones, to whom he impute
all that have happen; and from whom, he say, to preserve so valuable
a young lady be even an act of charity.
all these argument be well second by Thwackum, who dwelt a little
strong on the authority of parent than Mr Blifil himself have do.
he ascribe the measure which Mr Blifil be desirous to take to
christian motif; "and though," say he, "the good young gentleman
hath mention charity last, I be almost convinced it be his first and
principal consideration."
Square, possibly, have he be present, would have sing to the same
tune, though in a different key, and would have discover much moral
fitness in the proceeding: but he be now go to Bath for the
recovery of his health.
Allworthy, though not without reluctance, at last yield to the
desire of his nephew. he say he would accompany he to London, where
he might be at liberty to use every honest endeavour to gain the lady:
"but I declare," say he, "I will never give my consent to any
absolute force be put on her inclination, nor shall you ever have
she, unless she can be bring freely to compliance."
thus do the affection of Allworthy for his nephew betray the superior
understanding to be triumph over by the inferior; and thus be the
prudence of the good of head often defeat by the tenderness of the
good of heart.
Blifil, have obtain this unhoped-for acquiescence in his uncle,
rest not till he carry his purpose into execution. and as no
immediate business require Mr Allworthy's presence in the country,
and little preparation be necessary to man for a journey, they set out
the very next day, and arrive in town that evening, when Mr Jones, as
we have see, be divert himself with Partridge at the play.
the morning after his arrival Mr Blifil wait on Mr Western, by whom
he be most kindly and graciously receive, and from whom he have every
possible assurance (perhaps more than be possible) that he should
very shortly be as happy as Sophia could make he; nor would the
squire suffer the young gentleman to return to his uncle till he have,
almost against his will, carry he to his sister.
Chapter vii.
in which Mr Western pay a visit to his sister, in company with Mr
Blifil.
Mrs Western be read a lecture on prudence, and matrimonial
politic, to her niece, when her brother and Blifil break in with less
ceremony than the law of visit require. Sophia no soon see
Blifil than she turn pale, and almost lose the use of all her
faculty; but her aunt, on the contrary, wax red, and, have all
her faculty at command, begin to exert her tongue on the squire.
"Brother," say she, "I be astonish at your behaviour; will you
never learn any regard to decorum? will you still look upon every
apartment as your own, or as belong to one of your country tenant?
do you think yourself at liberty to invade the privacy of woman of
condition, without the least decency or notice?"----"why, what a pox
be the matter now?" quoth the squire; "one would think I have catch
you at--"--"none of your brutality, sir, I beseech you," answer
she.----"you have surprize my poor niece so, that she can hardly, I
see, support herself.----go, my dear, retire, and endeavour to recruit
your spirit; for I see you have occasion." at which word Sophia, who
never receive a more welcome command, hastily withdraw.
"to be sure, sister," cry the squire, "you be mad, when I have
bring Mr Blifil here to court she, to force she away."
"sure, brother," say she, "you be bad than mad, when you know in
what situation affair be, to----I be sure I ask Mr Blifil's pardon,
but he know very well to whom to impute so disagreeable a reception.
for my own part, I be sure I shall always be very glad to see Mr
Blifil; but his own good sense would not have suffer he to proceed
so abruptly, have you not compel he to it."
Blifil bow and stammer, and look like a fool; but western,
without give he time to form a speech for the purpose, answer,
"well, well, I be to blame, if you will, I always be, certainly; but
come, let the girl be fetch back again, or let Mr Blifil go to
her.----He's come up on purpose, and there be no time to be lose."
"Brother," cry Mrs Western, "Mr Blifil, I be confident, understand
himself well than to think of see my niece any more this morning,
after what hath happen. woman be of a nice contexture; and our
spirit, when disorder, be not to be recompose in a moment. have
you suffer Mr Blifil to have send his compliment to my niece, and
to have desire the favour of wait on she in the afternoon, I
should possibly have prevail on she to have see he; but now I
despair of bring about any such matter."
"I be very sorry, madam," cry Blifil, "that Mr Western's
extraordinary kindness to I, which I can never enough acknowledge,
should have occasioned--" "indeed, sir," say she, interrupt he,
"you need make no apology, we all know my brother so well."
"I donot care what anybody know of I," answer the squire;----"but
when must he come to see she? for, consider, I tell you, he be come
up on purpose, and so be allworthy."--"brother," say she, "whatever
message Mr Blifil think proper to send to my niece shall be
deliver to she; and I suppose she will want no instruction to make
a proper answer. I be convinced she will not refuse to see Mr Blifil
at a proper time."--"The devil she willnot!" answer the
squire.--"Odsbud!--Don't we know--I say nothing, but some volk be
wise than all the world.----If I might have have my will, she have not
run away before: and now I expect to hear every moment she be guone
again. for as great a fool as some volk think I, I know very well
she hates----" "no matter, brother," reply Mrs Western, "I will not
hear my niece abuse. it be a reflection on my family. she be an
honour to it; and she will be an honour to it, I promise you. I will
pawn my whole reputation in the world on her conduct.----i shall be
glad to see you, brother, in the afternoon; for I have somewhat of
importance to mention to you.--At present, Mr Blifil, as well as you,
must excuse I; for I be in haste to dress." "well, but," say the
squire, "do appoint a time." "indeed," say she, "I can appoint no
time. I tell you I will see you in the afternoon."--"what the devil
would you have I do?" cry the squire, turn to Blifil; "I can no
more turn she, than a beagle can turn an old hare. perhaps she will
be in a well humour in the afternoon."--"I be condemn, I see,
sir, to misfortune," answer Blifil; "but I shall always own my
obligation to you." he then take a ceremonious leave of Mrs Western,
who be altogether as ceremonious on her part; and then they
depart, the squire mutter to himself with an oath, that Blifil
should see his daughter in the afternoon.
if Mr Western be little pleased with this interview, Blifil be less.
as to the former, he impute the whole behaviour of his sister to her
humour only, and to her dissatisfaction at the omission of ceremony in
the visit; but Blifil see a little deep into thing. he suspect
somewhat of more consequence, from two or three word which dropt from
the lady; and, to say the truth, he suspect right, as will appear
when I have unfold the several matter which will be contain in
the follow chapter.
Chapter viii.
scheme of Lady Bellaston for the ruin of Jones.
Love have take too deep a root in the mind of Lord Fellamar to be
pluck up by the rude hand of Mr Western. in the heat of resentment
he have, indeed, give a commission to Captain Egglane, which the
captain have far exceed in the execution; nor have it be execute at
all, have his lordship be able to find the captain after he have see
Lady Bellaston, which be in the afternoon of the day after he have
receive the affront; but so industrious be the captain in the
discharge of his duty, that, have after long enquiry find out the
squire's lodging very late in the evening, he sit up all night at a
tavern, that he might not miss the squire in the morning, and by that
mean miss the revocation which my lord have send to his lodging.
in the afternoon then next after the intend rape of Sophia, his
lordship, as we have say, make a visit to Lady Bellaston, who lay
open so much of the character of the squire, that his lordship plainly
see the absurdity he have be guilty of in take any offence at his
word, especially as he have those honourable design on his daughter.
he then unbosom the violence of his passion to Lady Bellaston, who
readily undertake the cause, and encourage he with certain assurance
of a most favourable reception from all the elder of the family, and
from the father himself when he should be sober, and should be make
acquaint with the nature of the offer make to his daughter. the only
danger, she say, lie in the fellow she have formerly mention, who,
though a beggar and a vagabond, have, by some mean or other, she know
not what, procure himself tolerable cloath, and past for a
gentleman. "now," say she, "as I have, for the sake of my cousin,
make it my business to enquire after this fellow, I have luckily find
out his lodging;" with which she then acquaint his lordship. "I be
think, my lord," add she "(for this fellow be too mean for your
personal resentment), whether it would not be possible for your
lordship to contrive some method of have he press and send on
board a ship. neither law nor conscience forbid this project: for the
fellow, I promise you, however well drest, be but a vagabond, and as
proper as any fellow in the street to be press into the service;
and as for the conscientious part, surely the preservation of a young
lady from such ruin be a most meritorious act; nay, with regard to the
fellow himself, unless he could succeed (which Heaven forbid) with my
cousin, it may probably be the mean of preserve he from the
gallow, and perhaps may make his fortune in an honest way."
Lord Fellamar very heartily thank her ladyship for the part which
she be pleased to take in the affair, upon the success of which his
whole future happiness entirely depend. he say, he see at present
no objection to the press scheme, and would consider of put it
in execution. he then most earnestly recommend to her ladyship to do
he the honour of immediately mention his proposal to the family;
to whom he say he offer a _carte blanche_, and would settle his
fortune in almost any manner they should require. and after utter
many ecstasy and rapture concern Sophia, he take his leave and
depart, but not before he have receive the strong charge to
beware of Jones, and to lose no time in secure his person, where he
should no long be in a capacity of make any attempt to the ruin
of the young lady.
the moment Mrs Western be arrive at her lodging, a card be
despatch with her compliment to Lady Bellaston; who no soon
receive it than, with the impatience of a lover, she fly to her
cousin, rejoice at this fair opportunity, which beyond her hope
offer itself, for she be much well pleased with the prospect of
make the proposal to a woman of sense, and who know the world, than
to a gentleman whom she honour with the appellation of Hottentot;
though, indeed, from he she apprehend no danger of a refusal.
the two lady be meet, after very short previous ceremonial, fall
to business, which be indeed almost as soon conclude as begin; for
Mrs Western no soon hear the name of Lord Fellamar than her cheek
glow with pleasure; but when she be acquaint with the eagerness
of his passion, the earnestness of his proposal, and the generosity
of his offer, she declare her full satisfaction in the most explicit
term.
in the progress of their conversation their discourse turn to Jones,
and both cousin very pathetically lament the unfortunate attachment
which both agree Sophia have to that young fellow; and Mrs Western
entirely attribute it to the folly of her brother's management. she
conclude, however, at last, with declare her confidence in the good
understanding of her niece, who, though she would not give up her
affection in favour of Blifil, will, I doubt not, say she, soon be
prevail upon to sacrifice a simple inclination to the address of a
fine gentleman, who bring she both a title and a large estate: "for,
indeed," add she, "I must do Sophy the justice to confess this
Blifil be but a hideous kind of fellow, as you know, Bellaston, all
country gentleman be, and hath nothing but his fortune to recommend
he."
"Nay," say Lady Bellaston, "I donot then so much wonder at my cousin;
for I promise you this Jones be a very agreeable fellow, and hath one
virtue, which the man say be a great recommendation to we. what do you
think, Mrs Western--I shall certainly make you laugh; nay, I can
hardly tell you myself for laugh--will you believe that the fellow
hath have the assurance to make love to I? but if you should be
inclined to disbelieve it, here be evidence enough, his own
handwriting, I assure you." she then deliver her cousin the letter
with the proposal of marriage, which, if the reader hath a desire to
see, he will find already on record in the XVth book of this history.
"upon my word I be astonish," say Mrs Western; "this be, indeed, a
masterpiece of assurance. with your leave I may possibly make some use
of this letter." "you have my full liberty," cry Lady Bellaston, "to
apply it to what purpose you please. however, I would not have it
shewn to any but Miss Western, nor to she unless you find occasion."
"well, and how do you use the fellow?" return Mrs Western. "not as
a husband," say the lady; "I be not married, I promise you, my dear.
you know, Bell, I have try the comfort once already; and once, I
think, be enough for any reasonable woman."
this letter Lady Bellaston think would certainly turn the balance
against Jones in the mind of Sophia, and she be embolden to give it
up, partly by her hope of have he instantly dispatch out of the
way, and partly by have secure the evidence of Honour, who, upon
sound she, she see sufficient reason to imagine be prepare to
testify whatever she please.
but perhaps the reader may wonder why Lady Bellaston, who in her heart
hat Sophia, should be so desirous of promote a match which be so
much to the interest of the young lady. now, I would desire such
reader to look carefully into human nature, page almost the last, and
there he will find, in scarce legible character, that woman,
notwithstanding the preposterous behaviour of mother, aunt, &c., in
matrimonial matter, do in reality think it so great a misfortune to
have their inclination in love thwart, that they imagine they ought
never to carry enmity high than upon these disappointment; again,
he will find it write much about the same place, that a woman who
hath once be pleased with the possession of a man, will go above
halfway to the devil, to prevent any other woman from enjoy the
same.
if he will not be content with these reason, I freely confess I see
no other motive to the action of that lady, unless we will conceive
she be bribe by Lord Fellamar, which for my own part I see no cause
to suspect.
now this be the affair which Mrs Western be prepare to introduce
to Sophia, by some prefatory discourse on the folly of love, and on
the wisdom of legal prostitution for hire, when her brother and Blifil
break abruptly in upon she; and hence arise all that coldness in her
behaviour to Blifil, which, though the squire, as be usual with he,
impute to a wrong cause, infuse into Blifil himself (he be a much
more cunning man) a suspicion of the real truth.
chapter ix.
in which Jones pay a visit to Mrs Fitzpatrick.
the reader may now, perhaps, be pleased to return with we to Mr Jones,
who, at the appoint hour, attend on Mrs Fitzpatrick; but before we
relate the conversation which now past it may be proper, accord to
our method, to return a little back, and to account for so great an
alteration of behaviour in this lady, that from change her lodge
principally to avoid Mr Jones, she have now industriously, as hath be
see, seek this interview.
and here we shall need only to resort to what happen the precede
day, when, hear from Lady Bellaston that Mr Western be arrive in
town, she go to pay her duty to he, at his lodging at Piccadilly,
where she be receive with many scurvy compellation too coarse to be
repeat, and be even threaten to be kick out of door. from
hence, an old servant of her aunt Western, with whom she be well
acquaint, conduct she to the lodging of that lady, who treat
she not more kindly, but more politely; or, to say the truth, with
rudeness in another way. in short, she return from both, plainly
convince, not only that her scheme of reconciliation have prove
abortive, but that she must for ever give over all thought of
bring it about by any mean whatever. from this moment desire of
revenge only fill her mind; and in this temper meeting Jones at the
play, an opportunity seem to she to occur of effecting this purpose.
the reader must remember that he be acquaint by Mrs Fitzpatrick, in
the account she give of her own story, with the fondness Mrs Western
have formerly shew for Mr Fitzpatrick at Bath, from the disappointment
of which Mrs Fitzpatrick derive the great bitterness her aunt have
express toward she. she have, therefore, no doubt but that the good
lady would as easily listen to the address of Mr Jones as she have
before do to the other; for the superiority of charm be clearly on
the side of Mr Jones; and the advance which her aunt have since make in
age, she conclude (how justly I will not say), be an argument rather
in favour of her project than against it.
therefore, when Jones attend, after a previous declaration of her
desire of serve he, arise, as she say, from a firm assurance how
much she should by so do oblige Sophia; and after some excuse for
her former disappointment, and after acquaint Mr Jones in whose
custody his mistress be, of which she think he ignorant; she very
explicitly mention her scheme to he, and advise he to make sham
address to the old lady, in order to procure an easy access to the
young, inform he at the same time of the success which Mr
Fitzpatrick have formerly owe to the very same stratagem.
Mr Jones express great gratitude to the lady for the kind intention
towards he which she have express, and indeed testify, by this
proposal; but, besides intimate some diffidence of success from the
lady's knowledge of his love to her niece, which have not be her case
in regard to Mr Fitzpatrick, he say, he be afraid Miss Western would
never agree to an imposition of this kind, as well from her utter
detestation of all fallacy as from her avow duty to her aunt.
Mrs Fitzpatrick be a little nettled at this; and indeed, if it may
not be call a lapse of the tongue, it be a small deviation from
politeness in Jones, and into which he scarce would have fall, have
not the delight he feel in praise Sophia hurry he out of all
reflection; for this commendation of one cousin be more than a tacit
rebuke on the other.
"indeed, sir," answer the lady, with some warmth, "I cannot think
there be anything easy than to cheat an old woman with a profession
of love, when her complexion be amorous; and, though she be my aunt, I
must say there never be a more liquorish one than her ladyship. cannot
you pretend that the despair of possess her niece, from her be
promise to Blifil, have make you turn your thought towards she? as to
my cousin Sophia, I cannot imagine she to be such a simpleton as to
have the least scruple on such an account, or to conceive any harm in
punish one of these haggs for the many mischief they bring upon
family by their tragi-comic passion; for which I think it be a pity
they be not punishable by law. I have no such scruple myself; and yet
I hope my cousin Sophia will not think it an affront when I say she
cannot det every real specie of falsehood more than her cousin
Fitzpatrick. to my aunt, indeed, I pretend no duty, nor doth she
deserve any. however, sir, I have give you my advice; and if you
decline pursue it, I shall have the less opinion of your
understanding--thatbe all."
Jones now clearly see the error he have commit, and exert his
utmost power to rectify it; but he only faultere and stutter into
nonsense and contradiction. to say the truth, it be often safe to
abide by the consequence of the first blunder than to endeavour to
rectify it; for by such endeavour we generally plunge deeply instead
of extricate ourselves; and few person will on such occasion have
the good-nature which Mrs Fitzpatrick display to Jones, by say,
with a smile, "you need attempt no more excuse; for I can easily
forgive a real lover, whatever be the effect of fondness for his
mistress."
she then renew her proposal, and very fervently recommend it,
omit no argument which her invention could suggest on the subject;
for she be so violently incense against her aunt, that scarce
anything be capable of afford her equal pleasure with expose
she; and, like a true woman, she would see no difficulty in the
execution of a favourite scheme.
Jones, however, persist in decline the undertaking, which have not,
indeed, the least probability of success. he easily perceive the
motif which induce Mrs Fitzpatrick to be so eager in press she
advice. he say he would not deny the tender and passionate regard he
have for Sophia; but be so conscious of the inequality of their
situation, that he could never flatter himself so far as to hope that
so divine a young lady would condescend to think on so unworthy a man;
nay, he protest, he could scarce bring himself to wish she should.
he conclude with a profession of generous sentiment, which we have
not at present leisure to insert.
there be some fine woman (for I dare not here speak in too general
term) with whom self be so predominant, that they never detach it
from any subject; and, as vanity be with they a rule principle, they
be apt to lay hold of whatever praise they meet with; and, though the
property of other, convey it to their own use. in the company of
these lady it be impossible to say anything handsome of another
woman which they will not apply to themselves; nay, they often improve
the praise they seize; as, for instance, if her beauty, her wit, her
gentility, her good humour deserve so much commendation, what do I
deserve, who possess those quality in so much more eminent a degree?
to these lady a man often recommend himself while he be commend
another woman; and, while he be express ardour and generous
sentiment for his mistress, they be consider what a charming
lover this man would make to they, who can feel all this tenderness
for an inferior degree of merit. of this, strange as it may seem, I
have see many instance besides Mrs Fitzpatrick, to whom all this
really happen, and who now begin to feel a somewhat for Mr Jones,
the symptom of which she much soon understand than poor Sophia have
formerly do.
to say the truth, perfect beauty in both sex be a more irresistible
object than it be generally think; for, notwithstanding some of we
be content with more homely lot, and learn by rote (as child to
repeat what give they no idea) to despise outside, and to value more
solid charm; yet I have always observe, at the approach of
consummate beauty, that these more solid charm only shine with that
kind of lustre which the star have after the rise of the sun.
when Jones have finish his exclamation, many of which would have
become the mouth of Oroöndates himself, Mrs Fitzpatrick heave a
deep sigh, and, take her eye off from Jones, on whom they have be
some time fix, and drop they on the ground, she cry, "indeed,
Mr Jones, I pity you; but it be the curse of such tenderness to be
throw away on those who be insensible of it. I know my cousin well
than you, Mr Jones, and I must say, any woman who make no return to
such a passion, and such a person, be unworthy of both."
"sure, madam," say Jones, "you cannot mean----" "mean!" cry Mrs
Fitzpatrick, "I know not what I mean; there be something, I think, in
true tenderness bewitch; few woman ever meet with it in man, and
few still know how to value it when they do. I never hear such
truly noble sentiment, and I cannot tell how it be, but you force one
to believe you. sure she must be the most contemptible of woman who
can overlook such merit."
the manner and look with which all this be speak infuse a suspicion
into Jones which we donot care to convey in direct word to the
reader. instead of make any answer, he say, "I be afraid, madam, I
have make too tiresome a visit;" and offer to take his leave.
"not at all, sir," answer Mrs Fitzpatrick.--"Indeed I pity you, Mr
Jones; indeed I do: but if you be go, consider of the scheme I
have mention--I be convinced you will approve it--and let I see you
again as soon as you can.--to-morrow morning if you will, or at least
some time to-morrow. I shall be at home all day."
Jones, then, after many expression of thank, very respectfully
retire; nor could Mrs Fitzpatrick forbear make he a present of a
look at parting, by which if he have understand nothing, he must have
have no understanding in the language of the eye. in reality, it
confirm his resolution of return to she no more; for, faulty as
he hath hitherto appear in this history, his whole thought be now
so confine to his Sophia, that I believe no woman upon earth could
have now draw he into an act of inconstancy.
Fortune, however, who be not his friend, resolve, as he intend to
give she no second opportunity, to make the good of this; and
accordingly produce the tragical incident which we be now in
sorrowful note to record.
Chapter x.
the consequence of the precede visit.
Mr Fitzpatrick have receive the letter before mention from Mrs
Western, and be by that mean acquaint with the place to which
his wife be retire, return directly to Bath, and thence the day
after set forward to London.
the reader hath be already often inform of the jealous temper of
this gentleman. he may likewise be pleased to remember the suspicion
which he have conceive of Jones at Upton, upon his find he in the
room with Mrs Waters; and, though sufficient reason have afterwards
appear entirely to clear up that suspicion, yet now the reading so
handsome a character of Mr Jones from his wife, cause he to reflect
that she likewise be in the inn at the same time, and jumble
together such a confusion of circumstance in a head which be
naturally none of the clear, that the whole produce that
green-eyed monster mention by Shakespear in his tragedy of Othello.
and now, as he be enquire in the street after his wife, and have
just receive direction to the door, unfortunately Mr Jones be
issue from it.
Fitzpatrick do not yet recollect the face of Jones; however, see a
young well-dress fellow come from his wife, he make directly up to
he, and ask he what he have be do in that house? "for I be
sure," say he, "you must have be in it, as I see you come out of
it."
Jones answer very modestly, "that he have be visit a lady
there." to which Fitzpatrick reply, "what business have you with the
lady?" upon which Jones, who now perfectly remember the voice,
feature, and indeed coat, of the gentleman, cry out----"ha, my good
friend! give I your hand; I hope there be no ill blood remain
between we, upon a small mistake which happen so long ago."
"upon my soul, sir," say Fitzpatrick, "I donot know your name nor
your face." "indeed, sir," say Jones, "neither have I the pleasure of
know your name, but your face I very well remember to have see
before at Upton, where a foolish quarrel happen between we, which,
if it be not make up yet, we will now make up over a bottle."
"at Upton!" cry the other;----"Ha! upon my soul, I believe your name
be Jones?" "indeed," answer he, "it be."--"O! upon my soul," cry
Fitzpatrick, "you be the very man I want to meet.--upon my soul I
will drink a bottle with you presently; but first I will give you a
great knock over the pate. there be for you, you rascal. upon my soul,
if you do not give I satisfaction for that blow, I will give you
another." and then, draw his sword, put himself in a posture of
defence, which be the only science he understand.
Jones be a little stagger by the blow, which come somewhat
unexpectedly; but presently recover himself he also draw, and
though he understand nothing of fencing, pr on so boldly upon
Fitzpatrick, that he beat down his guard, and sheathe one half of his
sword in the body of the say gentleman, who have no soon receive it
than he stept backwards, drop the point of his sword, and lean
upon it, cry, "I have satisfaction enough: I be a dead man."
"I hope not," cry Jones, "but whatever be the consequence, you must
be sensible you have draw it upon yourself." at this instant a number
of fellow rush in and seize Jones, who tell they he should make no
resistance, and beg some of they at least would take care of the
wound gentleman.
"ay," cry one of the fellow, "the wound gentleman will be take
care enough of; for I suppose he hath not many hour to live. as for
you, sir, you have a month at least good yet." "D--n I, Jack," say
another, "he hath prevent his voyage; hebe bind to another port
now;" and many other such jest be our poor Jones make the subject of
by these fellow, who be indeed the gang employ by Lord Fellamar,
and have dog he into the house of Mrs Fitzpatrick, wait for he
at the corner of the street when this unfortunate accident happen.
the officer who command this gang very wisely conclude that his
business be now to deliver his prisoner into the hand of the civil
magistrate. he order he, therefore, to be carry to a
public-house, where, have send for a constable, he deliver he to
his custody.
the constable, see Mr Jones very well dr, and hear that the
accident have happen in a duel, treat his prisoner with great
civility, and at his request dispatch a messenger to enquire after
the wound gentleman, who be now at a tavern under the surgeon's
hand. the report bring back be, that the wound be certainly
mortal, and there be no hope of life. upon which the constable
inform Jones that he must go before a justice. he answer,
"wherever you please; I be indifferent as to what happen to I; for
though I be convinced I be not guilty of murder in the eye of the law,
yet the weight of blood I find intolerable upon my mind."
Jones be now conduct before the justice, where the surgeon who
dress Mr Fitzpatrick appear, and depose that he believe the
wound to be mortal; upon which the prisoner be commit to the
Gatehouse. it be very late at night, so that Jones would not send for
Partridge till the next morning; and, as he never shut his eye till
seven, so it be near twelve before the poor fellow, who be greatly
frightened at not hear from his master so long, receive a message
which almost deprive he of his being when he hear it.
he go to the Gatehouse with tremble knee and a beat heart, and
be no soon arrive in the presence of Jones than he lament the
misfortune that have befall he with many tear, look all the
while frequently about he in great terror; for as the news now
arrive that Mr Fitzpatrick be dead, the poor fellow apprehend
every minute that his ghost would enter the room. at last he deliver
he a letter, which he have like to have forget, and which come from
Sophia by the hand of Black George.
Jones presently dispatch every one out of the room, and, have
eagerly break open the letter, read as follows:--
"you owe the hearing from I again to an accident which I own
surprize I. my aunt hath just now show I a letter from you to
Lady Bellaston, which contain a proposal of marriage. I be
convince it be your own hand; and what more surprize I be, that
it be date at the very time when you would have I imagine you be
under such concern on my account.--i leave you to comment on this
fact. all I desire be, that your name may never more be mention
to
"S. W."
of the present situation of Mr Jones's mind, and of the pang with
which he be now torment, we cannot give the reader a well idea
than by say, his misery be such that even Thwackum would almost
have pity he. but, bad as it be, we shall at present leave he in
it, as his good genius (if he really have any) seem to have do. and
here we put an end to the sixteenth book of our history.
BOOK XVII.
contain three day.
Chapter i.
contain a portion of introductory writing.
when a comic writer hath make his principal character as happy as he
can, or when a tragic writer hath bring they to the high pitch of
human misery, they both conclude their business to be do, and that
their work be come to a period.
have we be of the tragic complexion, the reader must now allow we
be very nearly arrive at this period, since it would be difficult
for the devil, or any of his representative on earth, to have
contrive much great torment for poor Jones than those in which we
leave he in the last chapter; and as for Sophia, a good-natured woman
would hardly wish more uneasiness to a rival than what she must at
present be suppose to feel. what then remain to complete the tragedy
but a murder or two and a few moral sentence!
but to bring our favourite out of their present anguish and distress,
and to land they at last on the shore of happiness, seem a much
hard task; a task indeed so hard that we do not undertake to execute
it. in regard to Sophia, it be more than probable that we shall
somewhere or other provide a good husband for she in the end--either
Blifil, or my lord, or somebody else; but as to poor Jones, such be
the calamity in which he be at present involve, owe to his
imprudence, by which if a man doth not become felon to the world, he
be at least a _felo de se_; so destitute be he now of friend, and so
persecute by enemy, that we almost despair of bring he to any
good; and if our reader delight in see execution, I think he
ought not to lose any time in take a first row at Tyburn.
this I faithfully promise, that, notwithstanding any affection which
we may be suppose to have for this rogue, whom we have unfortunately
make our heroe, we will lend he none of that supernatural assistance
with which we be entrust, upon condition that we use it only on
very important occasion. if he doth not therefore find some natural
mean of fairly extricate himself from all his distress, we will
do no violence to the truth and dignity of history for his sake; for
we have rather relate that he be hang at Tyburn (which may very
probably be the case) than forfeit our integrity, or shock the faith
of our reader.
in this the antient have a great advantage over the modern. their
mythology, which be at that time more firmly believe by the vulgar
than any religion be at present, give they always an opportunity of
deliver a favourite heroe. their deity be always ready at the
writer's elbow, to execute any of his purpose; and the more
extraordinary the invention be, the great be the surprize and
delight of the credulous reader. those writer could with great ease
have convey a heroe from one country to another, nay from one world
to another, and have bring he back again, than a poor circumscribe
modern can deliver he from a jail.
the Arabians and Persians have an equal advantage in write their
tale from the genius and fairy, which they believe in as an article
of their faith, upon the authority of the Koran itself. but we have
none of these help. to natural mean alone we be confine; let we
try therefore what, by these mean, may be do for poor Jones; though
to confess the truth, something whisper I in the ear that he doth
not yet know the bad of his fortune; and that a more shocking piece
of news than any he hath yet hear remain for he in the unopened
leaf of fate.
Chapter ii.
the generous and grateful behaviour of Mrs Miller.
Mr Allworthy and Mrs Miller be just sit down to breakfast, when
Blifil, who have go out very early that morning, return to make one
of the company.
he have not be long seat before he begin as follow: "Good Lord! my
dear uncle, what do you think hath happen? I vow I be afraid of
tell it you, for fear of shock you with the remembrance of ever
have shew any kindness to such a villain." "what be the matter,
child?" say the uncle. "I fear I have shewn kindness in my life to
the unworthy more than once. but charity doth not adopt the vice of
its object." "o, sir!" return Blifil, "it be not without the secret
direction of Providence that you mention the word adoption. your
adopt son, sir, that Jones, that wretch whom you nourish in your
bosom, hath prove one of the great villain upon earth." "by all
thatbe sacred 'tis false," cry Mrs Miller. "Mr Jones be no villain.
he be one of the worthy creature breathing; and if any other
person have call he villain, I would have throw all this boiling
water in his face." Mr Allworthy look very much amazed at this
behaviour. but she do not give he leave to speak, before, turn to
he, she cry, "I hope you will not be angry with I; I would not
offend you, sir, for the world; but, indeed, I could not bear to hear
he call so." "I must own, madam," say Allworthy, very gravely, "I
be a little surprized to hear you so warmly defend a fellow you do not
know." "o! I do know he, Mr Allworthy," say she, "indeed I do; I
should be the most ungrateful of all wretch if I deny it. o! he
hath preserve I and my little family; we have all reason to bless
he while we live.--and I pray Heaven to bless he, and turn the
heart of his malicious enemy. I know, I find, I see, he hath such."
"you surprize I, madam, still more," say allworthy; "sure you must
mean some other. it be impossible you should have any such obligation
to the man my nephew mention." "too surely," answer she, "I have
obligation to he of the great and tender kind. he hath be
the preserver of I and mine. believe I, sir, he hath be abuse,
grossly abuse to you; I know he hath, or you, whom I know to be all
goodness and honour, would not, after the many kind and tender thing
I have hear you say of this poor helpless child, have so disdainfully
call he fellow.--indeed, my good of friend, he deserve a kind
appellation from you, have you hear the good, the kind, the grateful
thing which I have hear he utter of you. he never mention your
name but with a sort of adoration. in this very room I have see he
on his knee, implore all the blessing of heaven upon your head. I
do not love that child there well than he love you."
"I see, sir, now," say Blifil, with one of those grin sneer with
which the devil mark his good belove, "Mrs Miller really doth know
he. I suppose you will find she be not the only one of your
acquaintance to whom he hath expose you. as for my character, I
perceive, by some hint she hath throw out, he hath be very free
with it, but I forgive he." "and the Lord forgive you, sir!" say Mrs
Miller; "we have all sin enough to stand in need of his forgiveness."
"upon my word, Mrs Miller," say Allworthy, "I do not take this
behaviour of yours to my nephew kindly; and I do assure you, as any
reflection which you cast upon he must come only from that wicked
of man, they would only serve, if that be possible, to heighten my
resentment against he: for I must tell you, Mrs Miller, the young man
who now stand before you hath ever be the warm advocate for the
ungrateful wretch whose cause you espouse. this, I think, when you
hear it from my own mouth, will make you wonder at so much baseness
and ingratitude."
"you be deceive, sir," answer Mrs Miller; "if they be the last
word which be to issue from my lip, I would say you be deceive;
and I once more repeat it, the Lord forgive those who have deceive
you! I do not pretend to say the young man be without fault; but they
be all the fault of wildness and of youth; fault which he may, nay,
which I be certain he will, relinquish, and, if he should not, they
be vastly overbalance by one of the most humane, tender, honest
heart that ever man be bl with."
"indeed, Mrs Miller," say Allworthy, "have this be relate of you, I
should not have believe it." "indeed, sir," answer she, "you will
believe everything I have say, I be sure you will: and when you have
hear the story which I shall tell you (for I will tell you all), you
will be so far from be offend, that you will own (I know your
justice so well), that I must have be the most despicable and most
ungrateful of wretch if I have act any other part than I have."
"well, madam," say Allworthy, "I shall be very glad to hear any good
excuse for a behaviour which, I must confess, I think want an excuse.
and now, madam, will you be pleased to let my nephew proceed in his
story without interruption. he would not have introduce a matter of
slight consequence with such a preface. perhaps even this story will
cure you of your mistake."
Mrs Miller give token of submission, and then Mr Blifil begin thus:
"I be sure, sir, if you donot think proper to resent the ill-usage of
Mrs Miller, I shall easily forgive what affect I only. I think your
goodness hath not deserve this indignity at her hand." "well,
child," say Allworthy, "but what be this new instance? what hath he
do of late?" "what," cry Blifil, "notwithstanding all Mrs Miller
hath say, I be very sorry to relate, and what you should never have
hear from I, have it not be a matter impossible to conceal from the
whole world. in short he hath kill a man; I will not say
murder--for perhaps it may not be so construed in law, and I hope
the good for his sake."
Allworthy look shocked, and bless himself; and then, turn to
Mrs Miller, he cry, "well, madam, what say you now?"
"why, I say, sir," answer she, "that I never be more concerned at
anything in my life; but, if the fact be true, I be convince the man,
whoever he be, be in fault. Heaven know there be many villain in
this town who make it their business to provoke young gentleman.
nothing but the great provocation could have tempt he; for of
all the gentleman I ever have in my house, I never see one so gentle or
so sweet-temper. he be belove by every one in the house, and every
one who come near it."
while she be thus run on, a violent knock at the door
interrupt their conversation, and prevent she from proceed
far, or from receive any answer; for, as she conclude this be
a visitor to Mr Allworthy, she hastily retire, take with her her
little girl, whose eye be all over blubber at the melancholy news
she hear of Jones, who use to call she his little wife, and not only
give she many plaything, but spend whole hour in play with she
himself.
some reader may, perhaps, be pleased with these minute circumstance,
in relate of which we follow the example of Plutarch, one of the
good of our brother historian; and other, to whom they may appear
trivial, will, we hope, at least pardon they, as we be never prolix
on such occasion.
Chapter iii.
the arrival of Mr Western, with some matter concern the paternal
authority.
Mrs Miller have not long leave the room when Mr Western enter; but not
before a small wrangle bout have pass between he and his chairman;
for the fellow, who have take up their burden at the Hercules
Pillars, have conceive no hope of have any future good customer in
the squire; and they be moreover far encouraged by his
generosity (for he have give they of his own accord sixpence more than
their fare); they therefore very boldly demand another shilling,
which so provoke the squire, that he not only bestow many hearty
curse on they at the door, but retain his anger after he come into
the room; swear that all the Londoners be like the court, and
thought of nothing but plunder country gentleman. "D--n I," say
he, "if I willnot walk in the rain rather than get into one of their
hand-barrow again. they have jolt I more in a mile than Brown Bess
would in a long fox-chase."
when his wrath on this occasion be a little appeased, he resume the
same passionate tone on another. "there," say he, "there be fine
business forwards now. the hound have change at last; and when we
imagine we have a fox to deal with, od-rat it, it turn out to be a
badger at last!"
"pray, my good neighbour," say Allworthy, "drop your metaphor, and
speak a little plainer." "why, then," say the squire, "to tell you
plainly, we have be all this time afraid of a son of a whore of a
bastard of somebody's, I donot know whose, not i. and now herebe a
confounded son of a whore of a lord, who may be a bastard too for what
I know or care, for he shall never have a daughter of mine by my
consent. they have beggar the nation, but they shall never beggar
I. my land shall never be send over to Hanover."
"you surprize I much, my good friend," say allworthy. "why, zound!
I be surprize myself," answer the squire. "I go to zee sister
Western last night, accord to her own appointment, and there I be
have into a whole room full of woman. there be my lady cousin
Bellaston, and my Lady Betty, and my Lady Catherine, and my lady I
donot know who; d--n I, if ever you catch I among such a kennel of
hoop-petticoat b--s! D--n I, Iwould rather be run by my own dog, as one
Acton be, that the story-book say be turn into a hare, and his
own dog kill un and eat un. od-rabbit it, no mortal be ever run in
such a manner; if I dodge one way, one have I; if I offer to clap
back, another snap I. `o! certainly one of the great match in
England,' say one cousin (here he attempt to mimic they); `a very
advantageous offer indeed,' cry another cousin (for you must know
they be all my cousin, thof I never zeed half o' um before).
`surely,' say that fat a--se b--, my Lady Bellaston, `cousin, you
must be out of your wit to think of refuse such an offer.'"
"now I begin to understand," say Allworthy; "some person hath make
proposal to Miss Western, which the lady of the family approve, but
be not to your liking."
"my liking!" say Western, "how the devil should it? I tell you it be
a lord, and those be always volk whom you know I always resolve to
have nothing to do with. do unt I refuse a matter of vorty year'
purchase now for a bit of land, which one o' um have a mind to put into
a park, only because I would have no dealing with lord, and dost
think I would marry my daughter zu? besides, ben't I engage to you,
and do I ever go off any bargain when I have promise?"
"as to that point, neighbour," say Allworthy, "I entirely release you
from any engagement. no contract can be bind between party who
have not a full power to make it at the time, nor ever afterwards
acquire the power of fulfil it."
"Slud! then," answer Western, "I tell you I have power, and I will
fulfil it. come along with I directly to doctor' Commons, I will get
a licence; and I will go to sister and take away the wench by force,
and she shall ha un, or I will lock she up, and keep she upon bread
and water as long as she live."
"Mr Western," say Allworthy, "shall I beg you will hear my full
sentiment on this matter?"--"hear thee; ay, to be sure I will,"
answer he. "why, then, sir," cry allworthy, "I can truly say,
without a compliment either to you or the young lady, that when this
match be propose, I embrace it very readily and heartily, from my
regard to you both. an alliance between two family so nearly
neighbour, and between whom there have always exist so mutual an
intercourse and good harmony, I think a most desirable event; and
with regard to the young lady, not only the concurrent opinion of all
who know she, but my own observation assure I that she would be an
inestimable treasure to a good husband. I shall say nothing of her
personal qualification, which certainly be admirable; her good
nature, her charitable disposition, her modesty, be too well know to
need any panegyric: but she hath one quality which exist in a high
degree in that good of woman, who be now one of the first of angel,
which, as it be not of a glaring kind, more commonly escape
observation; so little indeed be it remark, that I want a word to
express it. I must use negative on this occasion. I never hear
anything of pertness, or what be call repartee, out of her mouth; no
pretence to wit, much less to that kind of wisdom which be the result
only of great learning and experience, the affectation of which, in a
young woman, be as absurd as any of the affectation of an ape. no
dictatorial sentiment, no judicial opinion, no profound criticism.
whenever I have see she in the company of man, she hath be all
attention, with the modesty of a learner, not the forwardness of a
teacher. youwill pardon I for it, but I once, to try she only, desire
her opinion on a point which be controvert between Mr Thwackum and
Mr Square. to which she answer, with much sweetness, `you will
pardon I, good Mr Allworthy; I be sure you cannot in earnest think I
capable of decide any point in which two such gentleman disagree.'
Thwackum and Square, who both alike think themselves sure of a
favourable decision, second my request. she answer with the same
good humour, `I must absolutely be excuse: for I will affront neither
so much as to give my judgment on his side.' indeed, she always shew
the high deference to the understanding of man; a quality
absolutely essential to the making a good wife. I shall only add, that
as she be most apparently void of all affectation, this deference must
be certainly real."
here Blifil sigh bitterly; upon which Western, whose eye be full
of tear at the praise of Sophia, blubber out, "donot be
chicken-hearte, for shat ha her, d--n I, shat ha she, if she be
twenty time as good."
"remember your promise, sir," cry allworthy, "I be not to be
interrupt." "well, shat unt," answer the squire; "I willnot speak
another word."
"now, my good friend," continue Allworthy, "I have dwell so long on
the merit of this young lady, partly as I really be in love with her
character, and partly that fortune (for the match in that light be
really advantageous on my nephew's side) might not be imagine to be
my principal view in have so eagerly embrace the proposal. indeed,
I heartily wish to receive so great a jewel into my family; but
though I may wish for many good thing, I would not, therefore, steal
they, or be guilty of any violence or injustice to possess myself of
they. now to force a woman into a marriage contrary to her consent or
approbation, be an act of such injustice and oppression, that I wish
the law of our country could restrain it; but a good conscience be
never lawless in the worst regulated state, and will provide those
law for itself, which the neglect of legislator hath forget to
supply. this be surely a case of that kind; for, be it not cruel, nay,
impious, to force a woman into that state against her will; for her
behaviour in which she be to be accountable to the high and most
dreadful court of judicature, and to answer at the peril of her soul?
to discharge the matrimonial duty in an adequate manner be no easy
task; and shall we lay this burthen upon a woman, while we at the same
time deprive she of all that assistance which may enable she to
undergo it? shall we tear her very heart from she, while we enjoin her
duty to which a whole heart be scarce equal? I must speak very
plainly here. I think parent who act in this manner be accessory
to all the guilt which their child afterwards incur, and of course
must, before a just judge, expect to partake of their punishment; but
if they could avoid this, good heaven! be there a soul who can bear
the thought of have contribute to the damnation of his child?
"for these reason, my good neighbour, as I see the inclination of
this young lady be most unhappily averse to my nephew, I must decline
any further thought of the honour you intend he, though I assure
you I shall always retain the most grateful sense of it."
"well, sir," say Western (the froth burst forth from his lip the
moment they be uncorked), "you cannot say but I have hear you out,
and now I expect youwill hear I; and if I donot answer every word
on't, why then Iwill consent to gee the matter up. first then, I
desire you to answer I one question--do not I beget she? do not I
beget she? answer I that. they say, indeed, it be a wise father that
know his own child; but I be sure I have the good title to she, for
I breed she up. but I believe you will allow I to be her father, and
if I be, be I not to govern my own child? I ask you that, be I not to
govern my own child? and if I be to govern she in other matter,
surely I be to govern she in this, which concern she most. and what
be I desire all this while? be I desire she to do anything for
I? to give I anything?--zu much on t'other side, that I be only
desire she to take away half my estate now, and t'other half when I
die. well, and what be it all vor? why, be unt it to make she happy?
itbe enough to make one mad to hear volk talk; if I be go to
marry myself, then she would ha reason to cry and to blubber; but, on
the contrary, han't I offer to bind down my land in such a manner,
that I could not marry if I would, see as narro' woman upon earth
would ha I. what the devil in hell can I do more? I contribute to
her damnation!--zounds! Iwould zee all the world d--n'd bevore her
little vinger should be hurt. indeed, Mr Allworthy, you must excuse
I, but I be surprize to hear you talk in zuch a manner, and I must
say, take it how you will, that I think you have more sense."
allworthy resent this reflection only with a smile; nor could he, if
he would have endeavour it, have convey into that smile any
mixture of malice or contempt. his smile at folly be indeed such as
we may suppose the angel bestow on the absurdity of mankind.
Blifil now desire to be permit to speak a few word. "as to use
any violence on the young lady, I be sure I shall never consent to it.
my conscience will not permit I to use violence on any one, much less
on a lady for whom, however cruel she be to I, I shall always
preserve the pure and sincere affection; but yet I have read that
woman be seldom proof against perseverance. why may I not hope then
by such perseverance at last to gain those inclination, in which for
the future I shall, perhaps, have no rival; for as for this lord, Mr
Western be so kind to prefer I to he; and sure, sir, you will not
deny but that a parent hath at least a negative voice in these
matter; nay, I have hear this very young lady herself say so more
than once, and declare that she think child inexcusable who
marry in direct opposition to the will of their parent. besides,
though the other lady of the family seem to favour the pretension
of my lord, I do not find the lady herself be incline to give he any
countenance; alas! I be too well assured she be not; I be too sensible
that wicked of man remain uppermost in her heart."
"ay, ay, so he do," cry western.
"but surely," say Blifil, "when she hear of this murder which he
hath commit, if the law should spare his life----"
"whatbe that?" cry western. "murder! hath he commit a murder, and
be there any hope of see he hanged?--Tol de rol, tol lol de rol."
here he fall a singing and caper about the room.
"child," say Allworthy, "this unhappy passion of yours distress I
beyond measure. I heartily pity you, and would do every fair thing to
promote your success."
"I desire no more," cry Blifil; "I be convince my dear uncle hath a
well opinion of I than to think that I myself would accept of
more."
"Lookee," say Allworthy, "you have my leave to write, to visit, if
she will permit it--but I insist on no thought of violence. I will
have no confinement, nothing of that kind attempt."
"well, well," cry the squire, "nothing of that kind shall be
attempt; we will try a little long what fair mean will effect;
and if this fellow be but hang out of the way--Tol lol de rol! I
never hear well news in my life--I warrant everything go to my
mind.--Do, prithee, dear Allworthy, come and dine with I at the
Hercules Pillars: I have bespeak a shoulder of mutton roasted, and a
spare-rib of pork, and a fowl and egg-sauce. there will be nobody but
ourselves, unless we have a mind to have the landlord; for I have send
Parson Supple down to Basingstoke after my tobacco-box, which I leave
at an inn there, and I would not lose it for the world; for it be an
old acquaintance of above twenty year' standing. I can tell you
landlord be a vast comical bitch, you will like un hugely."
Mr Allworthy at last agree to this invitation, and soon after the
squire go off, sing and caper at the hope of see the
speedy tragical end of poor Jones.
when he be go, Mr Allworthy resume the aforesaid subject with much
gravity. he tell his nephew, "he wish with all his heart he would
endeavour to conquer a passion, in which I cannot," say he, "flatter
you with any hope of succeed. it be certainly a vulgar error, that
aversion in a woman may be conquer by perseverance. Indifference
may, perhaps, sometimes yield to it; but the usual triumph gain by
perseverance in a lover be over caprice, prudence, affectation, and
often an exorbitant degree of levity, which excite woman not
over-warm in their constitution to indulge their vanity by prolong
the time of courtship, even when they be well enough pleased with the
object, and resolve (if they ever resolve at all) to make he a very
pitiful amend in the end. but a fixed dislike, as I be afraid this
be, will rather gather strength than be conquer by time. besides, my
dear, I have another apprehension which you must excuse. I be afraid
this passion which you have for this fine young creature hath her
beautiful person too much for its object, and be unworthy of the name
of that love which be the only foundation of matrimonial felicity. to
admire, to like, and to long for the possession of a beautiful woman,
without any regard to her sentiment towards we, be, I be afraid, too
natural; but love, I believe, be the child of love only; at least, I
be pretty confident that to love the creature who we be assure hat
we be not in human nature. examine your heart, therefore, thoroughly,
my good boy, and if, upon examination, you have but the least
suspicion of this kind, I be sure your own virtue and religion will
impel you to drive so vicious a passion from your heart, and your good
sense will soon enable you to do it without pain."
the reader may pretty well guess Blifil's answer; but, if he should be
at a loss, we be not at present at leisure to satisfy he, as our
history now hasten on to matter of high importance, and we can no
long bear to be absent from Sophia.
chapter iv.
an extraordinary scene between Sophia and her aunt.
the lowing heifer and the bleat ewe, in herd and flock, may
ramble safe and unregarde through the pasture. these be, indeed,
hereafter doom to be the prey of man; yet many year be they
suffer to enjoy their liberty undisturbed. but if a plump doe be
discover to have escape from the forest, and to repose herself in
some field or grove, the whole parish be presently alarm, every man
be ready to set his dog after she; and, if she be preserve from the
rest by the good squire, it be only that he may secure she for his own
eating.
I have often consider a very fine young woman of fortune and
fashion, when first find stray from the pale of her nursery, to be
in pretty much the same situation with this doe. the town be
immediately in an uproar; she be hunt from park to play, from court
to assembly, from assembly to her own chamber, and rarely escape a
single season from the jaw of some devourer or other; for, if her
friend protect she from some, it be only to deliver she over to one
of their own chusing, often more disagreeable to she than any of the
rest; while whole herd or flock of other woman securely, and scarce
regard, traverse the park, the play, the opera, and the assembly;
and though, for the most part at least, they be at last devour, yet
for a long time do they wanton in liberty, without disturbance or
controul.
of all these paragon none ever taste more of this persecution than
poor Sophia. her ill star be not content with all that she have
suffer on account of Blifil, they now raise she another pursuer,
who seem likely to torment she no less than the other have do. for
though her aunt be less violent, she be no less assiduous in teizing
she, than her father have be before.
the servant be no soon depart after dinner than Mrs Western,
who have open the matter to Sophia, inform she, "that she expect
his lordship that very afternoon, and intend to take the first
opportunity of leave she alone with he." "if you do, madam,"
answer Sophia, with some spirit, "I shall take the first opportunity
of leave he by himself." "how! madam!" cry the aunt; "be this the
return you make I for my kindness in relieve you from your
confinement at your father's?" "you know, madam," say Sophia, "the
cause of that confinement be a refusal to comply with my father in
accept a man I detest; and will my dear aunt, who hath relieve
I from that distress, involve I in another equally bad?" "and do you
think then, madam," answer Mrs Western, "that there be no difference
between my Lord Fellamar and Mr Blifil?" "very little, in my opinion,"
cry Sophia; "and, if I must be condemn to one, I would certainly
have the merit of sacrifice myself to my father's pleasure." "then
my pleasure, I find," say the aunt, "hath very little weight with
you; but that consideration shall not move I. I act from nobler
motif. the view of aggrandize my family, of ennoble yourself, be
what I proceed upon. have you no sense of ambition? be there no
charm in the thought of have a coronet on your coach?" "none, upon
my honour," say Sophia. "a pincushion upon my coach would please I
just as well." "never mention honour," cry the aunt. "it become not
the mouth of such a wretch. I be sorry, niece, you force I to use
these word, but I cannot bear your grovel temper; you have none of
the blood of the Westerns in you. but, however mean and base your own
idea be, you shall bring no imputation on mine. I will never suffer
the world to say of I that I encourage you in refuse one of the
good match in England; a match which, besides its advantage in
fortune, would do honour to almost any family, and hath, indeed, in
title, the advantage of our." "surely," say Sophia, "I be bear
deficient, and have not the sens with which other people be
bless; there must be certainly some sense which can relish the
delight of sound and show, which I have not; for surely mankind would
not labour so much, nor sacrifice so much for the obtaining, nor would
they be so elate and proud with possess, what appear to they, as
it doth to I, the most insignificant of all trifle."
"no, no, miss," cry the aunt; "you be bear with as many sens as
other people; but I assure you you be not bear with a sufficient
understanding to make a fool of I, or to expose my conduct to the
world; so I declare this to you, upon my word, and you know, I
believe, how fix my resolution be, unless you agree to see his
lordship this afternoon, I will, with my own hand, deliver you
to-morrow morning to my brother, and will never henceforth interfere
with you, nor see your face again." Sophia stand a few moment silent
after this speech, which be utter in a most angry and peremptory
tone; and then, burst into tear, she cry, "do with I, madam,
whatever you please; I be the most miserable undone wretch upon earth;
if my dear aunt forsake I where shall I look for a protector?" "my
dear niece," cry she, "you will have a very good protector in his
lordship; a protector whom nothing but a hankering after that vile
fellow Jones can make you decline." "indeed, madam," say Sophia, "you
wrong I. how can you imagine, after what you have shew I, if I have
ever any such thought, that I should not banish they for ever? if it
will satisfy you, I will receive the sacrament upon it never to see
his face again." "but, child, dear child," say the aunt, "be
reasonable; can you invent a single objection?" "I have already, I
think, tell you a sufficient objection," answer Sophia. "what?"
cry the aunt; "I remember none." "sure, madam," say Sophia, "I tell
you he have use I in the rude and vilest manner." "indeed, child,"
answer she, "I never hear you, or do not understand you:--but what
do you mean by this rude, vile manner?" "indeed, madam," say Sophia,
"I be almost ashamed to tell you. he catch I in his arm, pull I
down upon the settee, and thrust his hand into my bosom, and kiss it
with such violence that I have the mark upon my left breast at this
moment." "indeed!" say Mrs Western. "yes, indeed, madam," answer
Sophia; "my father luckily come in at that instant, or Heaven know
what rudeness he intend to have proceed to." "I be astonish and
confound," cry the aunt. "no woman of the name of western hath
be ever treat so since we be a family. I would have tear the
eye of a prince out, if he have attempt such freedom with I. it be
impossible! sure, Sophia, you must invent this to raise my indignation
against he." "I hope, madam," say Sophia, "you have too good an
opinion of I to imagine I capable of tell an untruth. upon my
soul it be true." "I should have stab he to the heart, have I be
present," return the aunt. "yet surely he could have no
dishonourable design; it be impossible! he durst not: besides, his
proposal shew he hath not; for they be not only honourable, but
generous. I donot know; the age allow too great freedom. a distant
salute be all I would have allow before the ceremony. I have have
lover formerly, not so long ago neither; several lover, though I
never would consent to marriage, and I never encourage the least
freedom. it be a foolish custom, and what I never would agree to. no
man kiss more of I than my cheek. it be as much as one can bring
oneself to give lip up to a husband; and, indeed, could I ever have
be persuade to marry, I believe I should not have soon be bring
to endure so much." "you will pardon I, dear madam," say Sophia, "if
I make one observation: you own you have have many lover, and the
world know it, even if you should deny it. you refuse they all, and,
I be convince, one coronet at least among they." "you say true, dear
Sophy," answer she; "I have once the offer of a title." "why, then,"
say Sophia, "will you not suffer I to refuse this once?" "it be
true, child," say she, "I have refuse the offer of a title; but it
be not so good an offer; that be, not so very, very good an
offer."--"Yes, madam," say Sophia; "but you have have very great
proposal from man of vast fortune. it be not the first, nor the
second, nor the third advantageous match that offer itself." "I own
it be not," say she. "well, madam," continue Sophia, "and why may
not I expect to have a second, perhaps, well than this? you be now
but a young woman, and I be convince would not promise to yield to
the first lover of fortune, nay, or of title too. I be a very young
woman, and sure I need not despair." "well, my dear, dear Sophy,"
cry the aunt, "what would you have I say?" "why, I only beg that I
may not be leave alone, at least this evening; grant I that, and I
will submit, if you think, after what be past, I ought to see he in
your company." "well, I will grant it," cry the aunt. "Sophy, you
know I love you, and can deny you nothing. you know the easiness of my
nature; I have not always be so easy. I have be formerly think
cruel; by the man, I mean. I be call the cruel Parthenissa. I have
break many a window that have have verse to the cruel Parthenissa in
it. Sophy, I be never so handsome as you, and yet I have something of
you formerly. I be a little altered. kingdom and state, as Tully
Cicero say in his epistle, undergo alteration, and so must the
human form." thus run she on for near half an hour upon herself, and
her conquest, and her cruelty, till the arrival of my lord, who,
after a most tedious visit, during which Mrs Western never once
offer to leave the room, retire, not much more satisfied with the
aunt than with the niece; for Sophia have bring her aunt into so
excellent a temper, that she consent to almost everything her niece
say; and agree that a little distant behaviour might not be improper
to so forward a lover.
thus Sophia, by a little well-direct flattery, for which surely none
will blame she, obtain a little ease for herself, and, at least, put
off the evil day. and now we have see our heroine in a well
situation than she hath be for a long time before, we will look a
little after Mr Jones, whom we leave in the most deplorable situation
that can be well imagine.
Chapter v.
Mrs Miller and Mr Nightingale visit Jones in the prison.
when Mr Allworthy and his nephew go to meet Mr Western, Mrs Miller
set forwards to her son-in-law's lodging, in order to acquaint he
with the accident which have befall his friend Jones; but he have
know it long before from Partridge (for Jones, when he leave Mrs
Miller, have be furnish with a room in the same house with Mr
Nightingale). the good woman find her daughter under great affliction
on account of Mr Jones, whom have comfort as well as she could,
she set forwards to the Gatehouse, where she hear he be, and where
Mr Nightingale be arrive before she.
the firmness and constancy of a true friend be a circumstance so
extremely delightful to person in any kind of distress, that the
distress itself, if it be only temporary, and admit of relief, be
more than compensate by bring this comfort with it. nor be
instance of this kind so rare as some superficial and inaccurate
observer have report. to say the truth, want of compassion be not
to be number among our general fault. the black ingredient which
foul our disposition be envy. hence our eye be seldom, I be afraid,
turn upward to those who be manifestly great, well, wiser, or
happy than ourselves, without some degree of malignity; while we
commonly look downwards on the mean and miserable with sufficient
benevolence and pity. in fact, I have remark, that most of the
defect which have discover themselves in the friendship within my
observation have arise from envy only: a hellish vice; and yet one
from which I have know very few absolutely exempt. but enough of a
subject which, if pursue, would lead I too far.
whether it be that Fortune be apprehensive lest Jones should sink
under the weight of his adversity, and that she might thus lose any
future opportunity of torment he, or whether she really abate
somewhat of her severity towards he, she seem a little to relax her
persecution, by send he the company of two such faithful friend,
and what be perhaps more rare, a faithful servant. for Partridge,
though he have many imperfection, want not fidelity; and though fear
would not suffer he to be hang for his master, yet the world, I
believe, could not have bribe he to desert his cause.
while Jones be express great satisfaction in the presence of his

which, Jones fetch a deep sigh, Nightingale say to he, "my dear
Tom, why should you afflict yourself so upon an accident, which,
whatever be the consequence, can be attend with no danger to you,
and in which your conscience cannot accuse you of have be the
least to blame? if the fellow should die, what have you do more than
take away the life of a ruffian in your own defence? so will the
coroner's inquest certainly find it; and then you will be easily
admit to bail; and, though you must undergo the form of a trial,
yet it be a trial which many man would stand for you for a shilling."
"come, come, Mr Jones," say Mrs Miller, "chear yourself up. I know
you could not be the aggressor, and so I tell Mr Allworthy, and so he
shall acknowledge too, before I have do with he."
Jones gravely answer, "that whatever might be his fate, he
should always lament the having shed the blood of one of his
fellow-creature, as one of the high misfortune which could
have befall he. but I have another misfortune of the tender
kind----O! Mrs Miller, I have lose what I hold most dear upon earth."
"that must be a mistress," say Mrs Miller; "but come, come; I know
more than you imagine" (for indeed Partridge have blab all); "and I
have hear more than you know. matter go well, I promise you, than
you think; and I would not give Blifil sixpence for all the chance
which he hath of the lady."
"indeed, my dear friend, indeed," answer Jones, "you be an entire
stranger to the cause of my grief. if you be acquaint with the
story, you would allow my case admit of no comfort. I apprehend no
danger from Blifil. I have undo myself." "donot despair," reply
Mrs Miller; "you know not what a woman can do; and if anything be in
my power, I promise you I will do it to serve you. it be my duty. my
son, my dear Mr Nightingale, who be so kind to tell I he hath
obligation to you on the same account, know it be my duty. shall I
go to the lady myself? I will say anything to she you would have I
say."
"thou good of woman," cry Jones, take she by the hand, "talk not
of obligation to me;--but as you have be so kind to mention it,
there be a favour which, perhaps, may be in your power. I see you be
acquaint with the lady (how you come by your information I know
not), who sit, indeed, very near my heart. if you could contrive to
deliver this (give she a paper from his pocket), I shall for ever
acknowledge your goodness."
"give it I," say Mrs Miller. "if I see it not in her own possession
before I sleep, may my next sleep be my last! comfort yourself, my
good young man! be wise enough to take warning from past folly, and
I warrant all shall be well, and I shall yet see you happy with the
most charming young lady in the world; for I so hear from every one
she be."
"believe I, madam," say he, "I do not speak the common canot of one
in my unhappy situation. before this dreadful accident happen, I have
resolve to quit a life of which I be become sensible of the
wickedness as well as folly. I do assure you, notwithstanding the
disturbance I have unfortunately occasion in your house, for which
I heartily ask your pardon, I be not an abandon profligate. though I
have be hurry into vice, I do not approve a vicious character,
nor will I ever, from this moment, deserve it."
Mrs Miller express great satisfaction in these declaration, in the
sincerity of which she aver she have an entire faith; and now the
remainder of the conversation past in the joint attempt of that good
woman and Mr Nightingale to cheer the dejected spirit of Mr Jones, in
which they so far succeed as to leave he much well comfort and
satisfied than they find he; to which happy alteration nothing so
much contribute as the kind undertaking of Mrs Miller to deliver his
letter to Sophia, which he despair of find any mean to
accomplish; for when Black George produce the last from Sophia, he
inform Partridge that she have strictly charge he, on pain of
have it communicate to her father, not to bring she any answer. he
be, moreover, not a little pleased to find he have so warm an advocate
to Mr Allworthy himself in this good woman, who be, in reality, one
of the worthy creature in the world.
after about an hour's visit from the lady (for Nightingale have be

good news from his mistress, and Mr Nightingale promise to enquire
into the state of Mr Fitzpatrick's wound, and likewise to find out
some of the person who be present at the rencounter.
the former of these go directly in quest of Sophia, whither we
likewise shall now attend she.
Chapter vi.
in which Mrs Miller pay a visit to Sophia.
access to the young lady be by no mean difficult; for, as she live
now on a perfect friendly footing with her aunt, she be at full

Sophia be dress when she be acquaint that there be a

admit.
curtsy and the usual ceremonial between woman who be stranger to

for intrude upon you. but when you know what have induce I to give
