say Sophia, with a little emotion. "Madam, we be not alone," reply
Mrs Miller, in a low voice. "go out, Betty," say Sophia.
when Betty be depart, Mrs Miller say, "I be desire, madam, by a
very unhappy young gentleman, to deliver you this letter." Sophia
change colour when she see the direction, well know the hand, and
after some hesitation, said--"i could not conceive, madam, from your
appearance, that your business have be of such a nature.--whomever
you bring this letter from, I shall not open it. I should be sorry
to entertain an unjust suspicion of any one; but you be an utter
stranger to I."
"if you will have patience, madam," answer Mrs Miller, "I will

on your deliver that letter back to the person who give it you."
Mrs Miller then fall upon her knee, and in the most passionate term

this person. I would not think, madam"--"No, madam," say Mrs Miller,

you will not wonder that I be interested. he be the well-nature

Mr Anderson.--After this she cry, "this, madam, this be his

preserve my child."--here, after shed some tear, she relate
everything concern that fact, suppress only those circumstance
which would have most reflect on her daughter, and conclude with
say, "now, madam, you shall judge whether I can ever do enough for
so kind, so good, so generous a young man; and sure he be the good and
worthy of all human being."
the alteration in the countenance of Sophia have hitherto be chiefly
to her disadvantage, and have incline her complexion to too great
paleness; but she now wax red, if possible, than vermilion, and
cry, "I know not what to say; certainly what arise from gratitude
cannot be blame--but what service can my read this letter do your
friend, since I be resolve never----" Mrs Miller fall again to her

carry it back. "well, madam," say Sophia, "I cannot help it, if you
will force it upon me.--certainly you may leave it whether I will or
no." what Sophia mean, or whether she mean anything, I will not
presume to determine; but Mrs Miller actually understand this as a
hint, and presently lay the letter down on the table, take she
leave, have first beg permission to wait again on Sophia; which
request have neither assent nor denial.
the letter lie upon the table no long than till Mrs Miller be out
of sight; for then Sophia open and read it.
this letter do very little service to his cause; for it consist of
little more than confession of his own unworthiness, and bitter
lamentation of despair, together with the most solemn protestation
of his unalterable fidelity to Sophia, of which, he say, he hop to
convince she, if he have ever more the honour of be admit to she
presence; and that he could account for the letter to Lady Bellaston
in such a manner, that, though it would not entitle he to her
forgiveness, he hop at least to obtain it from her mercy. and
conclude with vow that nothing be ever less in his thought than
to marry Lady Bellaston.
Though Sophia read the letter twice over with great attention, his
meaning still remain a riddle to she; nor could her invention
suggest to she any mean to excuse Jones. she certainly remain very
angry with he, though indeed Lady Bellaston take up so much of her
resentment, that her gentle mind have but little leave to bestow on any
other person.
that lady be most unluckily to dine this very day with her aunt
western, and in the afternoon they be all three, by appointment, to
go together to the opera, and thence to Lady Thomas Hatchet's drum.
Sophia would have gladly be excuse from all, but would not
disoblige her aunt; and as to the art of counterfeit illness, she
be so entirely a stranger to they, that it never once enter into
her head. when she be drest, therefore, down she go, resolve to
encounter all the horror of the day, and a most disagreeable one it
prove; for Lady Bellaston take every opportunity very civilly and
slily to insult she; to all which her dejection of spirit disable
she from make any return; and, indeed, to confess the truth, she be
at the very good but an indifferent mistress of repartee.
another misfortune which befel poor Sophia be the company of Lord
Fellamar, whom she meet at the opera, and who attend she to the drum.
and though both place be too publick to admit of any
particularity, and she be far relieved by the musick at the one
place, and by the card at the other, she could not, however, enjoy
herself in his company; for there be something of delicacy in woman,
which will not suffer they to be even easy in the presence of a man
whom they know to have pretension to they which they be disinclined
to favour.
have in this chapter twice mention a drum, a word which our

apply, we shall, notwithstanding our present haste, stop a moment to

a drum, then, be an assembly of well-dress person of both sex,
most of whom play at card, and the rest do nothing at all; while the

like the landlady of an inn pride herself in the number of her
guest, though she doth not always, like she, get anything by it.
no wonder then, as so much spirit must be require to support any
vivacity in these scene of dulness, that we hear person of fashion
eternally complain of the want of they; a complaint confine
entirely to upper life. how insupportable must we imagine this round
of impertinence to have be to Sophia at this time; how difficult
must she have find it to force the appearance of gaiety into her
look, when her mind dictate nothing but the tender sorrow, and
when every thought be charge with torment idea!
Night, however, at last restore she to her pillow, where we will

be now arrive at the eve of some great event.
Chapter vii.
a pathetic scene between Mr Allworthy and Mrs Miller.
Mrs Miller have a long discourse with Mr Allworthy, at his return from
dinner, in which she acquaint he with Jones's have unfortunately
lose all which he be pleased to bestow on he at their separation;
and with the distress to which that loss have subject he; of all
which she have receive a full account from the faithful retailer
Partridge. she then explain the obligation she have to Jones; not
that she be entirely explicit with regard to her daughter; for though
she have the utmost confidence in Mr Allworthy, and though there could
be no hope of keep an affair secret which be unhappily know to
more than half a dozen, yet she could not prevail with herself to
mention those circumstance which reflect most on the chastity of
poor Nancy, but smother that part of her evidence as cautiously as
if she have be before a judge, and the girl be now on her trial for
the murder of a bastard.
Allworthy say, there be few character so absolutely vicious as not
to have the least mixture of good in they. "however," say he, "I
cannot deny but that you have some obligation to the fellow, bad as
he be, and I shall therefore excuse what hath past already, but must
insist you never mention his name to I more; for, I promise you, it
be upon the full and plainest evidence that I resolve to take the
measure I have take." "well, sir," say she, "I make not the least
doubt but time will shew all matter in their true and natural
colour, and that you will be convince this poor young man deserve
well of you than some other folk that shall be nameless."
"Madam," cry allworthy, a little ruffle, "I will not hear any
reflection on my nephew; and if ever you say a word more of that
kind, I will depart from your house that instant. he be the worthy
and good of man; and I once more repeat it to you, he hath carry his
friendship to this man to a blameable length, by too long conceal
fact of the black die. the ingratitude of the wretch to this good
young man be what I most resent; for, madam, I have the great
reason to imagine he have lay a plot to supplant my nephew in my
favour, and to have disinherit he."
"I be sure, sir," answer Mrs Miller, a little frightened (for,
though Mr Allworthy have the utmost sweetness and benevolence in his
smile, he have great terror in his frown), "I shall never speak
against any gentleman you be pleased to think well of. I be sure,
sir, such behaviour would very little become I, especially when the
gentleman be your near relation; but, sir, you must not be angry
with I, you must not indeed, for my good wish to this poor wretch.
sure I may call he so now, though once you would have be angry with
I if I have speak of he with the least disrespect. how often have I
hear you call he your son? how often have you prattle to I of he
with all the fondness of a parent? Nay, sir, I cannot forget the many
tender expression, the many good thing you have tell I of his
beauty, and his part, and his virtue; of his good-nature and
generosity. I be sure, sir, I cannot forget they, for I find they all
true. I have experience they in my own cause. they have preserve my
family. you must pardon my tear, sir, indeed you must. when I
consider the cruel reverse of fortune which this poor youth, to whom I
be so much oblige, hath suffer; when I consider the loss of your
favour, which I know he value more than his life, I must, I must
lament he. if you have a dagger in your hand, ready to plunge into my
heart, I must lament the misery of one whom you have love, and I
shall ever love."
Allworthy be pretty much move with this speech, but it seem not to
be with anger; for, after a short silence, take Mrs Miller by the
hand, he say very affectionately to she, "Come, madam, let we
consider a little about your daughter. I cannot blame you for
rejoice in a match which promise to be advantageous to she, but you
know this advantage, in a great measure, depend on the father's
reconciliation. I know Mr Nightingale very well, and have formerly have
concern with he; I will make he a visit, and endeavour to serve you
in this matter. I believe he be a worldly man; but as this be an only
son, and the thing be now irretrievable, perhaps he may in time be
bring to reason. I promise you I will do all I can for you."
many be the acknowledgment which the poor woman make to Allworthy
for this kind and generous offer, nor could she refrain from take
this occasion again to express her gratitude towards Jones, "to whom,"
say she, "I owe the opportunity of give you, sir, this present
trouble." Allworthy gently stop she; but he be too good a man to
be really offend with the effect of so noble a principle as now
actuate Mrs Miller; and indeed, have not this new affair inflame his
former anger against Jones, it be possible he might have be a little
softened towards he, by the report of an action which malice itself
could not have derive from an evil motive.
Mr Allworthy and Mrs Miller have be above an hour together, when
their conversation be put an end to by the arrival of Blifil and
another person, which other person be no less than Mr Dowling, the
attorney, who be now become a great favourite with Mr Blifil, and
whom Mr Allworthy, at the desire of his nephew, have make his steward;
and have likewise recommend he to Mr Western, from whom the attorney
receive a promise of be promote to the same office upon the first
vacancy; and, in the meantime, be employ in transact some
affair which the squire then have in London in relation to a mortgage.
this be the principal affair which then bring Mr Dowling to town;
therefore he take the same opportunity to charge himself with some
money for Mr Allworthy, and to make a report to he of some other
business; in all which, as it be of much too dull a nature to find
any place in this history, we will leave the uncle, nephew, and their
lawyer concern, and resort to other matter.
Chapter viii.
contain various matter.
before we return to Mr Jones, we will take one more view of Sophia.
though that young lady have bring her aunt into great good humour by
those soothe method which we have before relate, she have not
bring she in the least to abate of her zeal for the match with Lord
Fellamar. this zeal be now inflame by Lady Bellaston, who have tell
she the precede evening, that she be well satisfied from the
conduct of Sophia, and from her carriage to his lordship, that all
delay would be dangerous, and that the only way to succeed be to
press the match forward with such rapidity that the young lady should
have no time to reflect, and be oblige to consent while she scarce
know what she do; in which manner, she say, one-half of the
marriage among people of condition be bring about. a fact very
probably true, and to which, I suppose, be owe the mutual tenderness
which afterwards exist among so many happy couple.
a hint of the same kind be give by the same lady to Lord Fellamar;
and both these so readily embrace the advice that the very next day
be, at his lordship's request, appoint by Mrs Western for a private
interview between the young party. this be communicate to Sophia
by her aunt, and insist upon in such high term, that, after have
urge everything she possibly could invent against it without the
least effect, she at last agree to give the high instance of
complacence which any young lady can give, and consent to see his
lordship.
as conversation of this kind afford no great entertainment, we shall
be excuse from recite the whole that past at this interview; in
which, after his lordship have make many declaration of the most pure
and ardent passion to the silent blushing Sophia, she at last
collect all the spirit she could raise, and with a trembling low
voice say, "my lord, you must be yourself conscious whether your
former behaviour to I hath be consistent with the profession you
now make." "be there," answer he, "no way by which I can atone for
madness? what I do I be afraid must have too plainly convinced you,
that the violence of love have deprive I of my sens." "indeed, my
lord," say she, "it be in your power to give I a proof of an
affection which I much rather wish to encourage, and to which I should
think myself more beholden." "name it, madam," say my lord, very
warmly. "my lord," say she, look down upon her fan, "I know you
must be sensible how uneasy this pretended passion of yours hath make
I." "can you be so cruel to call it pretend?" say he. "yes, my
lord," answer Sophia, "all profession of love to those whom we
persecute be most insulting pretence. this pursuit of yours be to I
a most cruel persecution: nay, it be take a most ungenerous
advantage of my unhappy situation." "most lovely, most adorable
charmer, do not accuse I," cry he, "of take an ungenerous
advantage, while I have no thought but what be direct to your
honour and interest, and while I have no view, no hope, no ambition,
but to throw myself, honour, fortune, everything at your foot." "my
lord," say she, "it be that fortune and those honour which give you
the advantage of which I complain. these be the charm which have
seduce my relation, but to I they be thing indifferent. if your
lordship will merit my gratitude, there be but one way." "pardon I,
divine creature," say he, "there can be none. all I can do for you be
so much your due, and will give I so much pleasure, that there be no
room for your gratitude." "indeed, my lord," answer she, "you may
obtain my gratitude, my good opinion, every kind think and wish
which it be in my power to bestow; nay, you may obtain they with ease,
for sure to a generous mind it must be easy to grant my request. let
I beseech you, then, to cease a pursuit in which you can never have
any success. for your own sake as well as mine I entreat this favour;
for sure you be too noble to have any pleasure in torment an
unhappy creature. what can your lordship propose but uneasiness to
yourself, by a perseverance, which, upon my honour, upon my soul,
cannot, shall not prevail with I, whatever distress you may drive
I to." here my lord fetch a deep sigh, and then said--"is it then,
madam, that I be so unhappy to be the object of your dislike and
scorn; or will you pardon I if I suspect there be some other?" here
he hesitate, and Sophia answer with some spirit, "my lord, I shall
not be accountable to you for the reason of my conduct. I be oblige
to your lordship for the generous offer you have make; I own it be
beyond either my desert or expectation; yet I hope, my lord, you
will not insist on my reason, when I declare I cannot accept it."
Lord Fellamar return much to this, which we do not perfectly
understand, and perhaps it could not all be strictly reconcile either
to sense or grammar; but he conclude his rant speech with say,
"that if she have pre-engage herself to any gentleman, however unhappy
it would make he, he should think himself bind in honour to desist."
perhaps my lord lay too much emphasis on the word gentleman; for we
cannot else well account for the indignation with which he inspire
Sophia, who, in her answer, seem greatly to resent some affront he
have give she.
while she be speak, with her voice more raise than usual, Mrs
Western come into the room, the fire glare in her cheek, and the
flame burst from her eye. "I be ashamed," say she, "my lord, of
the reception which you have meet with. I assure your lordship we be
all sensible of the honour do we; and I must tell you, Miss Western,
the family expect a different behaviour from you." here my lord
interfere on behalf of the young lady, but to no purpose; the aunt
proceed till Sophia pull out her handkerchief, throw herself into
a chair, and burst into a violent fit of tear.
the remainder of the conversation between Mrs Western and his
lordship, till the latter withdraw, consist of bitter lamentation
on his side, and on hers of the strong assurance that her niece
should and would consent to all he wish. "indeed, my lord," say
she, "the girl hath have a foolish education, neither adapt to her
fortune nor her family. her father, I be sorry to say it, be to blame
for everything. the girl hath silly country notion of bashfulness.
nothing else, my lord, upon my honour; I be convinced she hath a good
understanding at the bottom, and will be bring to reason."
this last speech be make in the absence of Sophia; for she have some
time before leave the room, with more appearance of passion than she
have ever show on any occasion; and now his lordship, after many
expression of thank to Mrs Western, many ardent profession of
passion which nothing could conquer, and many assurance of
perseverance, which Mrs Western highly encouraged, take his leave for
this time.
before we relate what now pass between Mrs Western and Sophia, it
may be proper to mention an unfortunate accident which have happen,
and which have occasion the return of Mrs Western with so much fury,
as we have see.
the reader then must know that the maid who at present attend on
Sophia be recommend by Lady Bellaston, with whom she have live for
some time in the capacity of a comb-brush: she be a very sensible
girl, and have receive the strict instruction to watch her young
lady very carefully. these instruction, we be sorry to say, be
communicate to she by Mrs Honour, into whose favour Lady Bellaston
have now so ingratiated herself, that the violent affection which the
good waiting-woman have formerly bear to Sophia be entirely
obliterate by that great attachment which she have to her new
mistress.
now, when Mrs Miller be depart, Betty (for that be the name of the
girl), return to her young lady, find she very attentively engage
in read a long letter, and the visible emotion which she betray
on that occasion might have well account for some suspicion which
the girl entertain; but indeed they have yet a strong foundation,
for she have overhear the whole scene which pass between Sophia and
Mrs Miller.
Mrs Western be acquaint with all this matter by Betty, who, after
receive many commendation and some reward for her fidelity, be
order, that, if the woman who bring the letter come again, she
should introduce she to Mrs Western herself.
Unluckily, Mrs Miller return at the very time when Sophia be
engage with his lordship. Betty, accord to order, send she
directly to the aunt; who, be mistress of so many circumstance
relate to what have past the day before, easily impose upon the poor
woman to believe that Sophia have communicate the whole affair; and so
pump everything out of she which she know relate to the letter and
relate to Jones.
this poor creature might, indeed, be call simplicity itself. she be
one of that order of mortal who be apt to believe everything which
be say to they; to whom nature hath neither indulge the offensive
nor defensive weapon of deceit, and who be consequently liable to be
impose upon by any one who will only be at the expense of a little
falshood for that purpose. Mrs Western, have drain Mrs Miller of
all she know, which, indeed, be but little, but which be sufficient
to make the aunt suspect a great deal, dismiss she with assurance
that Sophia would not see she, that she would send no answer to the
letter, nor ever receive another; nor do she suffer she to depart
without a handsome lecture on the merit of an office to which she
could afford no well name than that of procuress.--this discovery
have greatly discompose her temper, when, come into the apartment
next to that in which the lover be, she overhear Sophia very
warmly protest against his lordship's address. at which the rage
already kindle burst forth, and she rush in upon her niece in a
most furious manner, as we have already describe, together with what
past at that time till his lordship's departure.
no soon be Lord Fellamar go than Mrs Western return to Sophia,
whom she upbraid in the most bitter term for the ill use she have
make of the confidence repose in she; and for her treachery in
converse with a man with whom she have offer but the day before to
bind herself in the most solemn oath never more to have any
conversation. Sophia protest she have maintain no such
conversation. "how, how! Miss Western," say the aunt; "will you deny
your receive a letter from he yesterday?" "a letter, madam!"
answer Sophia, somewhat surprized. "it be not very well breed, miss,"
reply the aunt, "to repeat my word. I say a letter, and I insist
upon your show it I immediately." "I scorn a lie, madam," say
Sophia; "I do receive a letter, but it be without my desire, and,
indeed, I may say, against my consent." "indeed, indeed, miss," cry
the aunt, "you ought to be ashamed of own you have receive it at
all; but where be the letter? for I will see it."
to this peremptory demand, Sophia pause some time before she return
an answer; and at last only excuse herself by declare she have not
the letter in her pocket, which be, indeed, true; upon which her
aunt, lose all manner of patience, ask her niece this short
question, whether she would resolve to marry Lord Fellamar, or no? to
which she receive the strong negative. Mrs Western then reply
with an oath, or something very like one, that she would early the
next morning deliver she back into her father's hand.
Sophia then begin to reason with her aunt in the follow
manner:--"Why, madam, must I of necessity be force to marry at all?
consider how cruel you would have think it in your own case, and how
much kind your parent be in leave you to your liberty. what
have I do to forfeit this liberty? I will never marry contrary to my
father's consent, nor without ask yours----And when I ask the
consent of either improperly, it will be then time enough to force
some other marriage upon I." "can I bear to hear this," cry Mrs
Western, "from a girl who hath now a letter from a murderer in her
pocket?" "I have no such letter, I promise you," answer Sophia;
"and, if he be a murderer, he will soon be in no condition to give you
any further disturbance." "how, Miss Western!" say the aunt, "have
you the assurance to speak of he in this manner; to own your
affection for such a villain to my face?" "sure, madam," say Sophia,
"you put a very strange construction on my word." "indeed, Miss
Western," cry the lady, "I shall not bear this usage; you have
learn of your father this manner of treat I; he hath teach you
to give I the lie. he hath totally ruin you by this false system of
education; and, please heaven, he shall have the comfort of its
fruit; for once more I declare to you, that to-morrow morning I will
carry you back. I will withdraw all my force from the field, and
remain henceforth, like the wise king of Prussia, in a state of
perfect neutrality. you be both too wise to be regulate by my
measure; so prepare yourself, for to-morrow morning you shall
evacuate this house."
Sophia remonstrate all she could; but her aunt be deaf to all she
say. in this resolution therefore we must at present leave she, as
there seem to be no hope of bring she to change it.
chapter ix.
what happen to Mr Jones in the prison.
Mr Jones pass about twenty-four melancholy hour by himself, unless
when relieve by the company of Partridge, before Mr Nightingale
return; not that this worthy young man have desert or forgot his
friend; for, indeed, he have be much the great part of the time
employ in his service.
he have hear, upon enquiry, that the only person who have see the
beginning of the unfortunate rencounter be a crew belong to a
man-of-war which then lie at Deptford. to Deptford therefore he go
in search of this crew, where he be inform that the man he seek
after be all go ashore. he then trace they from place to place,
till at last he find two of they drink together, with a third
person, at a hedge-tavern near Aldersgate.
Nightingale desire to speak with Jones by himself (for Partridge be
in the room when he come in). as soon as they be alone, Nightingale,
take Jones by the hand, cry, "come, my brave friend, be not too
much deject at what I be go to tell you----I be sorry I be the
messenger of bad news; but I think it my duty to tell you." "I guess
already what that bad news be," cry Jones. "the poor gentleman then
be dead."--"I hope not," answer Nightingale. "he be alive this
morning; though I will not flatter you; I fear, from the account I
could get, that his wound be mortal. but if the affair be exactly as
you tell it, your own remorse would be all you would have reason to
apprehend, let what would happen; but forgive I, my dear Tom, if I
entreat you to make the bad of your story to your friend. if you
disguise anything to we, you will only be an enemy to yourself."
"what reason, my dear Jack, have I ever give you," say Jones, "to
stab I with so cruel a suspicion?" "have patience," cry
Nightingale, "and I will tell you all. after the most diligent enquiry
I could make, I at last meet with two of the fellow who be present
at this unhappy accident, and I be sorry to say, they do not relate
the story so much in your favour as you yourself have tell it." "why,
what do they say?" cry Jones. "indeed what I be sorry to repeat, as
I be afraid of the consequence of it to you. they say that they be
at too great a distance to overhear any word that pass between you:
but they both agree that the first blow be give by you." "then, upon
my soul," answer Jones, "they injure I. he not only strike I
first, but strike I without the least provocation. what should induce
those villain to accuse I falsely?" "Nay, that I cannot guess," say
Nightingale, "and if you yourself, and I, who be so heartily your
friend, cannot conceive a reason why they should belie you, what
reason will an indifferent court of justice be able to assign why they
should not believe they? I repeat the question to they several
time, and so do another gentleman who be present, who, I believe,
be a seafaring man, and who really act a very friendly part by you;
for he beg they often to consider that there be the life of a man
in the case; and ask they over and over, if they be certain; to
which they both answer, that they be, and would abide by their
evidence upon oath. for heaven's sake, my dear friend, recollect
yourself; for, if this should appear to be the fact, it will be your
business to think in time of make the good of your interest. I would
not shock you; but you know, I believe, the severity of the law,
whatever verbal provocation may have be give you." "alas! my
friend," cry Jones, "what interest hath such a wretch as I? besides,
do you think I would even wish to live with the reputation of a
murderer? if I have any friend (as, alas! I have none), could I have
the confidence to solicit they to speak in the behalf of a man
condemn for the black crime in human nature? believe I, I have
no such hope; but I have some reliance on a throne still greatly
superior; which will, I be certain, afford I all the protection I
merit."
he then conclude with many solemn and vehement protestation of the
truth of what he have at first assert.
the faith of Nightingale be now again stagger, and begin to incline
to credit his friend, when Mrs Miller appear, and make a sorrowful
report of the success of her embassy; which when Jones have hear, he
cry out most heroically, "well, my friend, I be now indifferent as
to what shall happen, at least with regard to my life; and if it be
the will of Heaven that I shall make an atonement with that for the
blood I have spill, I hope the Divine Goodness will one day suffer my
honour to be clear, and that the word of a die man, at least,
will be believe, so far as to justify his character."
a very mournful scene now past between the prisoner and his friend,
at which, as few reader would have be pleased to be present, so
few, I believe, will desire to hear it particularly related. we will,
therefore, pass on to the entrance of the turnkey, who acquaint
Jones that there be a lady without who desire to speak with he when
he be at leisure.
Jones declare his surprize at this message. he say, "he know no lady
in the world whom he could possibly expect to see there." however, as
he see no reason to decline see any person, Mrs Miller and Mr
Nightingale presently take their leave, and he give order to have the
lady admit.
if Jones be surprize at the news of a visit from a lady, how greatly
be he astonish when he discover this lady to be no other than Mrs
Waters! in this astonishment then we shall leave he awhile, in order
to cure the surprize of the reader, who will likewise, probably, not a
little wonder at the arrival of this lady.
who this Mrs Waters be, the reader pretty well know; what she be,
he must be perfectly satisfied. he will therefore be pleased to
remember that this lady depart from Upton in the same coach with Mr
Fitzpatrick and the other irish gentleman, and in their company
travel to Bath.
now there be a certain office in the gift of Mr Fitzpatrick at that
time vacant, namely that of a wife: for the lady who have lately fill
that office have resign, or at least desert her duty. Mr
Fitzpatrick therefore, have thoroughly examine Mrs Waters on the
road, find she extremely fit for the place, which, on their arrival
at Bath, he presently confer upon she, and she without any scruple
accept. as husband and wife this gentleman and lady continue
together all the time they stay at Bath, and as husband and wife
they arrive together in town.
whether Mr Fitzpatrick be so wise a man as not to part with one good
thing till he have secure another, which he have at present only a
prospect of regain; or whether Mrs Waters have so well discharge
her office, that he intend still to retain she as principal, and to
make his wife (as be often the case) only her deputy, I will not say;
but certain it be, he never mention his wife to she, never
communicate to she the letter give he by Mrs Western, nor ever once
hint his purpose of repossess his wife; much less do he ever
mention the name of Jones. for, though he intend to fight with he
wherever he meet he, he do not imitate those prudent person who
think a wife, a mother, a sister, or sometimes a whole family, the
safe second on these occasion. the first account therefore which
she have of all this be deliver to she from his lip, after he be
bring home from the tavern where his wound have be drest.
as Mr Fitzpatrick, however, have not the clear way of tell a
story at any time, and be now, perhaps, a little more confused than
usual, it be some time before she discover that the gentleman who
have give he this wound be the very same person from whom her heart
have receive a wound, which, though not of a mortal kind, be yet so
deep that it have leave a considerable scar behind it. but no soon be
she acquaint that Mr Jones himself be the man who have be
commit to the Gatehouse for this suppose murder, than she take the
first opportunity of commit Mr Fitzpatrick to the care of his
nurse, and hasten away to visit the conqueror.
she now enter the room with an air of gaiety, which receive an
immediate check from the melancholy aspect of poor Jones, who start
and bless himself when he see she. upon which she say, "Nay, I do
not wonder at your surprize; I believe you do not expect to see I;
for few gentleman be trouble here with visit from any lady, unless
a wife. you see the power you have over I, Mr Jones. indeed, I little
thought, when we part at Upton, that our next meeting would have
be in such a place." "indeed, madam," say Jones, "I must look upon
this visit as kind; few will follow the miserable, especially to such
dismal habitation." "I protest, Mr Jones," say she, "I can hardly
persuade myself you be the same agreeable fellow I see at Upton. why,
your face be more miserable than any dungeon in the universe. what can
be the matter with you?" "I think, madam," say Jones, "as you know
of my be here, you know the unhappy reason." "Pugh!" say she, "you
have pink a man in a duel, thatbe all." Jones expr some
indignation at this levity, and speak with the utmost contrition for
what have happen. to which she answer, "well, then, sir, if you
take it so much to heart, I will relieve you; the gentleman be not
dead, and, I be pretty confident, be in no danger of die. the
surgeon, indeed, who first dress he be a young fellow, and seem
desirous of represent his case to be as bad as possible, that he
might have the more honour from cure he: but the king's surgeon
hath see he since, and say, unless from a fever, of which there be
at present no symptom, he apprehend not the least danger of life."
Jones shew great satisfaction in his countenance at this report;
upon which she affirm the truth of it, add, "by the most
extraordinary accident in the world I lodge at the same house; and
have see the gentleman, and I promise you he doth you justice, and
say, whatever be the consequence, that he be entirely the aggressor,
and that you be not in the least to blame."
Jones express the utmost satisfaction at the account which Mrs
Waters bring he. he then inform she of many thing which she well
know before, as who Mr Fitzpatrick be, the occasion of his
resentment, &c. he likewise tell she several fact of which she be
ignorant, as the adventure of the muff, and other particular,
conceal only the name of Sophia. he then lament the folly and
vice of which he have be guilty; every one of which, he say, have
be attend with such ill consequence, that he should be
unpardonable if he do not take warning, and quit those vicious
course for the future. he lastly conclude with assure she of his
resolution to sin no more, lest a bad thing should happen to he.
Mrs Waters with great pleasantry ridicule all this, as the effect of
low spirit and confinement. she repeat some witticism about the
devil when he be sick, and tell he, "she doubt not but shortly to
see he at liberty, and as lively a fellow as ever; and then," say
she, "I donot question but your conscience will be safely deliver of
all these qualm that it be now so sick in breeding."
many more thing of this kind she utter, some of which it would do
she no great honour, in the opinion of some reader, to remember; nor
be we quite certain but that the answer make by Jones would be
treat with ridicule by other. we shall therefore suppress the rest
of this conversation, and only observe that it end at last with
perfect innocence, and much more to the satisfaction of Jones than of
the lady; for the former be greatly transport with the news she have
bring he; but the latter be not altogether so pleased with the
penitential behaviour of a man whom she have, at her first interview,
conceive a very different opinion of from what she now entertain of
he.
thus the melancholy occasion by the report of Mr Nightingale be
pretty well effaced; but the dejection into which Mrs Miller have
throw he still continue. the account she give so well tally with
the word of Sophia herself in her letter, that he make not the least
doubt but that she have disclose his letter to her aunt, and have take
a fixed resolution to abandon he. the torment this thought give he
be to be equal only by a piece of news which fortune have yet in
store for he, and which we shall communicate in the second chapter of
the ensue book.
BOOK XVIII.
contain about six day.
Chapter i.
a farewel to the reader.
we be now, reader, arrive at the last stage of our long journey. as
we have, therefore, travel together through so many page, let we
behave to one another like fellow-traveller in a stage coach, who
have pass several day in the company of each other; and who,
notwithstanding any bickering or little animosity which may have
occur on the road, generally make all up at last, and mount, for
the last time, into their vehicle with chearfulness and good humour;
since after this one stage, it may possibly happen to we, as it
commonly happen to they, never to meet more.
as I have here take up this simile, give I leave to carry it a
little far. I intend, then, in this last book, to imitate the good
company I have mention in their last journey. now, it be well know
that all joke and raillery be at this time lay aside; whatever
character any of the passenger have for the jest-sake personate on
the road be now throw off, and the conversation be usually plain and
serious.
in the same manner, if I have now and then, in the course of this
work, indulge any pleasantry for thy entertainment, I shall here lay
it down. the variety of matter, indeed, which I shall be oblige to
cram into this book, will afford no room for any of those ludicrous
observation which I have elsewhere make, and which may sometimes,
perhaps, have prevent thee from take a nap when it be begin
to steal upon thee. in this last book thou wilt find nothing (or at
most very little) of that nature. all will be plain narrative only;
and, indeed, when thou hast peruse the many great event which this
book will produce, thou wilt think the number of page contain in it
scarce sufficient to tell the story.
and now, my friend, I take this opportunity (as I shall have no other)
of heartily wish thee well. if I have be an entertaining
companion to thee, I promise thee it be what I have desire. if in
anything I have offend, it be really without any intention. some
thing, perhaps, here say, may have hit thee or thy friend; but I do
most solemnly declare they be not point at thee or they. I
question not but thou hast be tell, among other story of I, that
thou wast to travel with a very scurrilous fellow; but whoever tell
thee so do I an injury. no man detest and despise scurrility more
than myself; nor hath any man more reason; for none hath ever be
treat with more; and what be a very severe fate, I have have some of
the abusive writing of those very man father upon I, who, in other
of their work, have abuse I themselves with the utmost virulence.
all these work, however, I be well convinced, will be dead long
before this page shall offer itself to thy perusal; for however short
the period may be of my own performance, they will most probably
outlive their own infirm author, and the weakly production of his
abusive contemporary.
Chapter ii.
contain a very tragical incident.
while Jones be employ in those unpleasant meditation, with which
we leave he torment himself, Partridge come stumble into the room
with his face paler than ash, his eye fix in his head, his hair
stand an end, and every limb tremble. in short, he look as
he would have do have he see a spectre, or have he, indeed, be a
spectre himself.
Jones, who be little subject to fear, could not avoid be somewhat
shock at this sudden appearance. he do, indeed, himself change
colour, and his voice a little faultered while he ask he, what be
the matter?
"I hope, sir," say Partridge, "you will not be angry with I. indeed
I do not listen, but I be oblige to stay in the outward room. I be
sure I wish I have be a hundred mile off, rather than have hear
what I have hear." "why, what be the matter?" say Jones. "the
matter, sir? o good Heaven!" answer Partridge, "be that woman who
be just go out the woman who be with you at Upton?" "she be,
Partridge," cry Jones. "and do you really, sir, go to bed with that
woman?" say he, trembling.--"i be afraid what past between we be no
secret," say Jones.--"Nay, but pray, sir, for Heaven's sake, sir,
answer I," cry Partridge. "you know I do," cry Jones. "why then,
the Lord have mercy upon your soul, and forgive you," cry Partridge;
"but as sure as I stand here alive, you have be a-bed with your own
mother."
upon these word Jones become in a moment a great picture of horror
than Partridge himself. he be, indeed, for some time strike dumb with
amazement, and both stand star wildly at each other. at last his
word find way, and in an interrupted voice he say, "how! how!
whatbe this you tell I?" "Nay, sir," cry Partridge, "I have not
breath enough leave to tell you now, but what I have say be most
certainly true.--that woman who now go out be your own mother. how
unlucky be it for you, sir, that I do not happen to see she at that
time, to have prevent it! sure the devil himself must have contrive
to bring about this wickedness."
"sure," cry Jones, "Fortune will never have do with I till she
hath drive I to distraction. but why do I blame Fortune? I be myself
the cause of all my misery. all the dreadful mischief which have
befall I be the consequence only of my own folly and vice. what
thou hast tell I, Partridge, hath almost deprive I of my sens!
and be Mrs Waters, then--but why do I ask? for thou must certainly
know she--if thou hast any affection for I, nay, if thou hast any
pity, let I beseech thee to fetch this miserable woman back again to
I. o good Heavens! incest----with a mother! to what be I reserved!"
he then fall into the most violent and frantic agony of grief and
despair, in which Partridge declare he would not leave he; but at
last, have vent the first torrent of passion, he come a little to
himself; and then, have acquaint Partridge that he would find this
wretched woman in the same house where the wound gentleman be
lodge, he despatch he in quest of she.
if the reader will please to refresh his memory, by turn to the
scene at Upton, in the ninth book, he will be apt to admire the many
strange accident which unfortunately prevent any interview between
Partridge and Mrs Waters, when she spend a whole day there with Mr
Jones. instance of this kind we may frequently observe in life, where
the great event be produce by a nice train of little
circumstance; and more than one example of this may be discover by
the accurate eye, in this our history.
after a fruitless search of two or three hour, Partridge return
back to his master, without have see Mrs Waters. Jones, who be in
a state of desperation at his delay, be almost rave mad when he
bring he his account. he be not long, however, in this condition
before he receive the follow letter:
"SIR,
"since I leave you I have see a gentleman, from whom I have learn
something concern you which greatly surprize and affect I; but
as I have not at present leisure to communicate a matter of such
high importance, you must suspend your curiosity till our next
meeting, which shall be the first moment I be able to see you. o, Mr
Jones, little do I think, when I past that happy day at Upton, the
reflection upon which be like to embitter all my future life, who it
be to whom I owe such perfect happiness. believe I to be ever
sincerely your unfortunate
"J. water."
"P.S. I would have you comfort yourself as much as possible, for Mr
Fitzpatrick be in no manner of danger; so that whatever other
grievous crime you may have to repent of, the guilt of blood be not
among the number."
Jones have read the letter, let it drop (for he be unable to hold
it, and indeed have scarce the use of any one of his faculty).
Partridge take it up, and have receive consent by silence, read it
likewise; nor have it upon he a less sensible effect. the pencil, and
not the pen, should describe the horror which appear in both their
countenance. while they both remain speechless the turnkey enter
the room, and, without take any notice of what sufficiently
discover itself in the face of they both, acquaint Jones that a
man without desire to speak with he. this person be presently
introduce, and be no other than Black George.
as sight of horror be not so usual to George as they be to the
turnkey, he instantly see the great disorder which appear in the
face of Jones. this he impute to the accident that have happen,
which be report in the very bad light in Mr Western's family; he
conclude, therefore, that the gentleman be dead, and that Mr Jones
be in a fair way of come to a shameful end. a thought which give
he much uneasiness; for George be of a compassionate disposition,
and notwithstanding a small breach of friendship which he have be
over-tempt to commit, be, in the main, not insensible of the
obligation he have formerly receive from Mr Jones.
the poor fellow, therefore, scarce refrain from a tear at the
present sight. he tell Jones he be heartily sorry for his
misfortune, and beg he to consider if he could be of any manner
of service. "perhaps, sir," say he, "you may want a little matter of
money upon this occasion; if you do, sir, what little I have be
heartily at your service."
Jones shake he very heartily by the hand, and give he many thank
for the kind offer he have make; but answer, "he have not the least
want of that kind." upon which George begin to press his service more
eagerly than before. Jones again thank he, with assurance that he
want nothing which be in the power of any man live to give.
"come, come, my good master," answer George, "do not take the matter
so much to heart. thing may end well than you imagine; to be sure
you an't the first gentleman who hath kill a man, and yet come off."
"you be wide of the matter, George," say Partridge, "the gentleman
be not dead, nor like to die. donot disturb my master, at present, for
he be troubled about a matter in which it be not in your power to do
he any good." "you donot know what I may be able to do, Mr
Partridge," answer George; "if his concern be about my young lady, I
have some news to tell my master." "what do you say, Mr George?" cry
Jones. "hath anything lately happen in which my Sophia be concern?
my Sophia! how dare such a wretch as I mention she so profanely." "I
hope she will be yours yet," answer George. "why yes, sir, I have
something to tell you about she. Madam Western hath just bring Madam
Sophia home, and there hath be a terrible to do. I could not
possibly learn the very right of it; but my master he hath be in a
vast big passion, and so be Madam Western, and I hear she say, as
she go out of door into her chair, that she would never set she
foot in master's house again. I donot know whatbe the matter, not I,
but everything be very quiet when I come out; but Robin, who wait
at supper, say he have never see the squire for a long while in such
good humour with young madam; that he kiss she several time, and
swear she should be her own mistress, and he never would think of
confine she any more. I think this news would please you, and so I
slip out, though it be so late, to inform you of it." Mr Jones
assure George that it do greatly please he; for though he should
never more presume to lift his eye toward that incomparable creature,
nothing could so much relieve his misery as the satisfaction he should
always have in hearing of her welfare.
the rest of the conversation which pass at the visit be not
important enough to be here relate. the reader will, therefore,
forgive we this abrupt break off, and be pleased to hear how this
great good-will of the squire towards his daughter be bring about.
Mrs Western, on her first arrival at her brother's lodging, begin to
set forth the great honour and advantage which would accrue to the
family by the match with Lord Fellamar, which her niece have absolutely
refuse; in which refusal, when the squire take the part of his
daughter, she fall immediately into the most violent passion, and so
irritated and provoke the squire, that neither his patience nor his
prudence could bear it any long; upon which there ensue between
they both so warm a bout at altercation, that perhaps the region of
billingsgate never equal it. in the heat of this scold Mrs
Western depart, and have consequently no leisure to acquaint her
brother with the letter which Sophia receive, which might have
possibly produce ill effect; but, to say truth, I believe it never
once occur to her memory at this time.
when Mrs Western be go, Sophia, who have be hitherto silent, as
well indeed from necessity as inclination, begin to return the
compliment which her father have make she, in take her part against
her aunt, by take his likewise against the lady. this be the first
time of she so do, and it be in the high degree acceptable to
the squire. again, he remember that Mr Allworthy have insist on an
entire relinquishment of all violent mean; and, indeed, as he make no
doubt but that Jones would be hang, he do not in the least question
succeed with his daughter by fair mean; he now, therefore, once
more give a loose to his natural fondness for she, which have such an
effect on the dutiful, grateful, tender, and affectionate heart of
Sophia, that have her honour, give to Jones, and something else,
perhaps, in which he be concern, be remove, I much doubt whether
she would not have sacrifice herself to a man she do not like, to
have oblige her father. she promise he she would make it the whole
business of her life to oblige he, and would never marry any man
against his consent; which bring the old man so near to his high
happiness, that he be resolve to take the other step, and go to
bed completely drunk.
Chapter iii.
allworthy visit old Nightingale; with a strange discovery that he
make on that occasion.
the morning after these thing have happen, Mr Allworthy go,
accord to his promise, to visit old Nightingale, with whom his
authority be so great, that, after have sit with he three hour,
he at last prevail with he to consent to see his son.
here an accident happen of a very extraordinary kind; one indeed of
those strange chance whence very good and grave man have conclude
that Providence often interpose in the discovery of the most secret
villany, in order to caution man from quit the path of honesty,
however warily they tread in those of vice.
Mr Allworthy, at his entrance into Mr Nightingale's, see Black George;
he take no notice of he, nor do Black George imagine he have
perceive he.
however, when their conversation on the principal point be over,
Allworthy ask Nightingale, whether he know one George Seagrim, and
upon what business he come to his house? "yes," answer Nightingale,
"I know he very well, and a most extraordinary fellow he be, who, in
these day, hath be able to hoard up £500 from rent a very small
estate of £30 a year." "and be this the story which he hath tell you?"
cry allworthy. "Nay, it be true, I promise you," say Nightingale,
"for I have the money now in my own hand, in five bank-bill, which I
be to lay out either in a mortgage, or in some purchase in the north
of England." the bank-bill be no soon produce at Allworthy's
desire than he bless himself at the strangeness of the discovery. he
presently tell Nightingale that these bank-bill be formerly his,
and then acquaint he with the whole affair. as there be no man who
complain more of the fraud of business than highwayman, gamester,
and other thief of that kind, so there be none who so bitterly
exclaim against the fraud of gamester, &c., as usurer, broker, and
other thief of this kind; whether it be that the one way of cheat
be a discountenance or reflection upon the other, or that money, which
be the common mistress of all cheat, make they regard each other in
the light of rival; but Nightingale no soon hear the story than he
exclaim against the fellow in term much severe than the justice
and honesty of Allworthy have bestow on he.
Allworthy desire Nightingale to retain both the money and the secret
till he should hear far from he; and, if he should in the
meantime see the fellow, that he would not take the least notice to
he of the discovery which he have make. he then return to his
lodging, where he find Mrs Miller in a very dejected condition, on
account of the information she have receive from her son-in-law. Mr
Allworthy, with great chearfulness, tell she that he have much good
news to communicate; and, with little further preface, acquaint she
that he have bring Mr Nightingale to consent to see his son, and do
not in the least doubt to effect a perfect reconciliation between
they; though he find the father more sowere by another accident of
the same kind which have happen in his family. he then mention the
run away of the uncle's daughter, which he have be tell by the
old gentleman, and which Mrs Miller and her son-in-law do not yet
know.
the reader may suppose Mrs Miller receive this account with great
thankfulness, and no less pleasure; but so uncommon be her friendship
to Jones, that I be not certain whether the uneasiness she suffer
for his sake do not overbalance her satisfaction at hear a piece
of news tend so much to the happiness of her own family; nor
whether even this very news, as it remind she of the obligation she
have to Jones, do not hurt as well as please she; when her grateful
heart say to she, "while my own family be happy, how miserable be the
poor creature to whose generosity we owe the beginning of all this
happiness!"
Allworthy, have leave she a little while to chew the cud (if I may
use that expression) on these first tiding, tell she he have still
something more to impart, which he believe would give her pleasure.
"I think," say he, "I have discover a pretty considerable treasure
belong to the young gentleman, your friend; but perhaps, indeed,
his present situation may be such that it will be of no service to
he." the latter part of the speech give Mrs Miller to understand who
be mean, and she answer with a sigh, "I hope not, sir." "I hope so
too," cry allworthy, "with all my heart; but my nephew tell I this
morning he have hear a very bad account of the affair."----"good
Heaven! sir," say she--"Well, I must not speak, and yet it be
certainly very hard to be oblige to hold one's tongue when one
hear."--"Madam," say Allworthy, "you may say whatever you please,
you know I too well to think I have a prejudice against any one; and
as for that young man, I assure you I should be heartily pleased to
find he could acquit himself of everything, and particularly of this
sad affair. you can testify the affection I have formerly bear he.
the world, I know, censure I for love he so much. I do not
withdraw that affection from he without think I have the just
cause. believe I, Mrs Miller, I should be glad to find I have be
mistaken." Mrs Miller be go eagerly to reply, when a servant
acquaint she that a gentleman without desire to speak with she
immediately. Allworthy then enquire for his nephew, and be tell that
he have be for some time in his room with the gentleman who use to
come to he, and whom Mr Allworthy guess rightly to be Mr Dowling,
he desire presently to speak with he.
when Dowling attend, Allworthy put the case of the bank-note to
he, without mention any name, and ask in what manner such a
person might be punish. to which Dowling answer, "he think he
might be indict on the Black Act; but say, as it be a matter of
some nicety, it would be proper to go to counsel. he say he be to
attend counsel presently upon an affair of Mr Western's, and if Mr
Allworthy please he would lay the case before they." this be agree
to; and then Mrs Miller, open the door, cry, "I ask pardon, I do
not know you have company;" but Allworthy desire she to come in,
say he have finish his business. upon which Mr Dowling withdraw,
and Mrs Miller introduce Mr Nightingale the young, to return thank
for the great kindness do he by Allworthy: but she have scarce
patience to let the young gentleman finish his speech before she
interrupt he, say, "o sir! Mr Nightingale bring great news
about poor Mr Jones: he hath be to see the wound gentleman, who be
out of all danger of death, and, what be more, declare he fall upon
poor Mr Jones himself, and beat he. I be sure, sir, you would not
have Mr Jones be a coward. if I be a man myself, I be sure, if any
man be to strike I, I should draw my sword. do pray, my dear, tell
Mr Allworthy, tell he all yourself." Nightingale then confirm what
Mrs Miller have say; and conclude with many handsome thing of Jones,
who be, he say, one of the well-natured fellow in the world, and
not in the least inclined to be quarrelsome. here Nightingale be
go to cease, when Mrs Miller again beg he to relate all the
many dutiful expression he have hear he make use of towards Mr
Allworthy. "to say the utmost good of Mr Allworthy," cry
Nightingale, "be do no more than strict justice, and can have no
merit in it: but indeed, I must say, no man can be more sensible of
the obligation he hath to so good a man than be poor Jones. indeed,
sir, I be convince the weight of your displeasure be the heavy
burthen he lie under. he hath often lament it to I, and hath as
often protest in the most solemn manner he hath never be
intentionally guilty of any offence towards you; nay, he hath swear he
would rather die a thousand death than he would have his conscience
upbraid he with one disrespectful, ungrateful, or undutiful thought
towards you. but I ask pardon, sir, I be afraid I presume to
intermeddle too far in so tender a point." "you have speak no more
than what a christian ought," cry Mrs Miller. "indeed, Mr
Nightingale," answer Allworthy, "I applaud your generous friendship,
and I wish he may merit it of you. I confess I be glad to hear the
report you bring from this unfortunate gentleman; and, if that matter
should turn out to be as you represent it (and, indeed, I doubt
nothing of what you say), I may, perhaps, in time, be bring to think
well than lately I have of this young man; for this good gentlewoman
here, nay, all who know I, can witness that I love he as dearly as
if he have be my own son. indeed, I have consider he as a child
send by fortune to my care. I still remember the innocent, the
helpless situation in which I find he. I feel the tender pressure of
his little hand at this moment. he be my darling, indeed he be." at
which word he cease, and the tear stand in his eye.
as the answer which Mrs Miller make may lead we into fresh matter, we
will here stop to account for the visible alteration in Mr Allworthy's
mind, and the abatement of his anger to Jones. revolution of this
kind, it be true, do frequently occur in history and dramatic
writer, for no other reason than because the history or play draw to
a conclusion, and be justify by authority of author; yet, though
we insist upon as much authority as any author whatever, we shall use
this power very sparingly, and never but when we be drive to it by
necessity, which we do not at present foresee will happen in this
work.
this alteration then in the mind of Mr Allworthy be occasion by a
letter he have just receive from Mr Square, and which we shall give
the reader in the beginning of the next chapter.
chapter iv.
contain two letter in very different stile.
"MY WORTHY FRIEND,--I inform you in my last that I be forbid
the use of the water, as they be find by experience rather to
increase than lessen the symptom of my distemper. I must now
acquaint you with a piece of news, which, I believe, will afflict my
friend more than it hath afflict I. Dr Harrington and Dr
Brewster have inform I that there be no hope of my recovery.
"I have somewhere read, that the great use of philosophy be to learn
to die. I will not therefore so far disgrace mine as to shew any
surprize at receive a lesson which I must be think to have so
long study. yet, to say the truth, one page of the Gospel teach
this lesson well than all the volume of antient or modern
philosopher. the assurance it give we of another life be a much
strong support to a good mind than all the consolation that be
draw from the necessity of nature, the emptiness or satiety of our
enjoyment here, or any other topic of those declamation which be
sometimes capable of arm our mind with a stubborn patience in
bear the thought of death, but never of raise they to a real
contempt of it, and much less of make we think it be a real good.
I would not here be understand to throw the horrid censure of
atheism, or even the absolute denial of immortality, on all who be
call philosopher. many of that sect, as well antient as modern,
have, from the light of reason, discover some hope of a future
state; but in reality, that light be so faint and glimmering, and
the hope be so incertain and precarious, that it may be justly
doubt on which side their belief turn. Plato himself conclude
his Phaedon with declare that his good argument amount only to
raise a probability; and Cicero himself seem rather to profess an
inclination to believe, than any actual belief in the doctrine of
immortality. as to myself, to be very sincere with you, I never be
much in earnest in this faith till I be in earnest a Christian.
"you will perhaps wonder at the latter expression; but I assure you
it hath not be till very lately that I could, with truth, call
myself so. the pride of philosophy have intoxicate my reason, and
the sublimest of all wisdom appear to I, as it do to the Greeks
of old, to be foolishness. God hath, however, be so gracious to
shew I my error in time, and to bring I into the way of truth,
before I sink into utter darkness forever.
"I find myself begin to grow weak, I shall therefore hasten to
the main purpose of this letter.
"when I reflect on the action of my past life, I know of nothing
which sit heavy upon my conscience than the injustice I have be
guilty of to that poor wretch your adopt son. I have, indeed, not
only connive at the villany of other, but be myself active in
injustice towards he. believe I, my dear friend, when I tell you,
on the word of a die man, he hath be basely injure. as to the
principal fact, upon the misrepresentation of which you discard
he, I solemnly assure you he be innocent. when you lay upon your
suppose deathbe, he be the only person in the house who testify
any real concern; and what happen afterwards arise from the
wildness of his joy on your recovery; and, I be sorry to say it,
from the baseness of another person (but it be my desire to justify
the innocent, and to accuse none). believe I, my friend, this young
man hath the noble generosity of heart, the most perfect capacity
for friendship, the high integrity, and indeed every virtue which
can ennoble a man. he hath some fault, but among they be not to be
number the least want of duty or gratitude towards you. on the
contrary, I be satisfied, when you dismiss he from your house,
his heart bleed for you more than for himself.
"worldly motif be the wicked and base reason of my concealing
this from you so long; to reveal it now I can have no inducement but
the desire of serve the cause of truth, of do right to the
innocent, and of make all the amend in my power for a past
offence. I hope this declaration, therefore, will have the effect
desire, and will restore this deserve young man to your favour;
the hearing of which, while I be yet alive, will afford the utmost
consolation to,
Sir,
your most oblige,
obedient humble servant,
THOMAS SQUARE."
the reader will, after this, scarce wonder at the revolution so
visibly appear in Mr Allworthy, notwithstanding he receive from
Thwackum, by the same post, another letter of a very different kind,
which we shall here add, as it may possibly be the last time we shall
have occasion to mention the name of that gentleman.
"SIR,
"I be not at all surprize at hear from your worthy nephew a
fresh instance of the villany of Mr Square the atheist's young
pupil. I shall not wonder at any murder he may commit; and I
heartily pray that your own blood may not seal up his final
commitment to the place of wail and gnashing of tooth.
"though you cannot want sufficient call to repentance for the many
unwarrantable weakness exemplify in your behaviour to this
wretch, so much to the prejudice of your own lawful family, and of
your character; I say, though these may sufficiently be suppose to
prick and goad your conscience at this season, I should yet be
want to my duty, if I spar to give you some admonition in order
to bring you to a due sense of your error. I therefore pray you
seriously to consider the judgment which be likely to overtake this
wicked villain; and let it serve at least as a warning to you, that
you may not for the future despise the advice of one who be so
indefatigable in his prayer for your welfare.
"have not my hand be withhold from due correction, I have scourge
much of this diabolical spirit out of a boy, of whom from his
infancy I discover the devil have take such entire possession. but
reflection of this kind now come too late.
"I be sorry you have give away the living of Westerton so hastily.
I should have apply on that occasion early, have I think you
would not have acquaint I previous to the disposition.----your
objection to plurality be be righteous over-much. if there be
any crime in the practice, so many godly man would not agree to it.
if the vicar of Aldergrove should die (as we hear he be in a
decline way), I hope you will think of I, since I be certain you
must be convince of my most sincere attachment to your high
welfare--a welfare to which all worldly consideration be as
trifle as the small tithe mention in Scripture be, when
compare to the weighty matter of the law.
I be, sir,
your faithful humble servant,
ROGER THWACKUM."
this be the first time Thwackum ever write in this authoritative
stile to Allworthy, and of this he have afterwards sufficient reason to
repent, as in the case of those who mistake the high degree of
goodness for the low degree of weakness. Allworthy have indeed never
like this man. he know he to be proud and ill-natured; he also know
that his divinity itself be tincture with his temper, and such as in
many respect he himself do by no mean approve; but he be at the
same time an excellent scholar, and most indefatigable in teach the
two lad. add to this, the strict severity of his life and manner, an
unimpeached honesty, and a most devout attachment to religion. so
that, upon the whole, though Allworthy do not esteem nor love the
man, yet he could never bring himself to part with a tutor to the
boy, who be, both by learning and industry, extremely well qualified
for his office; and he hop, that as they be breed up in his own
house, and under his own eye, he should be able to correct whatever
be wrong in Thwackum's instruction.
Chapter v.
in which the history be continue.
Mr Allworthy, in his last speech, have recollect some tender idea
concern Jones, which have bring tear into the good man's eye.
this Mrs Miller observe, say, "yes, yes, sir, your goodness to this
poor young man be know, notwithstanding all your care to conceal it;
but there be not a single syllable of truth in what those villain
say. Mr Nightingale hath now discover the whole matter. it seem
these fellow be employ by a lord, who be a rival of poor Mr
Jones, to have press he on board a ship.--I assure they I donot
know who they will press next. Mr Nightingale here hath see the
officer himself, who be a very pretty gentleman, and hath tell he
all, and be very sorry for what he undertake, which he would never
have do, have he know Mr Jones to have be a gentleman; but he be
tell that he be a common stroll vagabond."
Allworthy star at all this, and declare he be a stranger to every
word she say. "yes, sir," answer she, "I believe you are.----it be
a very different story, I believe, from what those fellow tell this
lawyer."
"what lawyer, madam? what be it you mean?" say Allworthy. "Nay, nay,"
say she, "this be so like you to deny your own goodness: but Mr
Nightingale here see he." "see whom, madam?" answer he. "why, your
lawyer, sir," say she, "that you so kindly send to enquire into the
affair." "I be still in the dark, upon my honour," say Allworthy.
"why then do you tell he, my dear sir," cry she. "indeed, sir,"
say Nightingale, "I do see that very lawyer who go from you when I
come into the room, at an alehouse in Aldersgate, in company with two
of the fellow who be employ by Lord Fellamar to press Mr Jones,
and who be by that mean present at the unhappy rencounter between
he and Mr Fitzpatrick." "I own, sir," say Mrs Miller, "when I see
this gentleman come into the room to you, I tell Mr Nightingale that I
apprehend you have send he thither to inquire into the affair."
allworthy shew mark of astonishment in his countenance at this
news, and be indeed for two or three minute strike dumb by it. at
last, address himself to Mr Nightingale, he say, "I must confess
myself, sir, more surprized at what you tell I than I have ever be
before at anything in my whole life. be you certain this be the
gentleman?" "I be most certain," answer Nightingale. "at
aldersgate?" cry allworthy. "and be you in company with this lawyer
and the two fellows?"--"i be, sir," say the other, "very near half
an hour." "well, sir," say Allworthy, "and in what manner do the
lawyer behave? do you hear all that past between he and the
fellow?" "no, sir," answer Nightingale, "they have be together
before I came.--in my presence the lawyer say little; but, after I
have several time examine the fellow, who persist in a story
directly contrary to what I have hear from Mr Jones, and which I find
by Mr Fitzpatrick be a rank falshood, the lawyer then desire the
fellow to say nothing but what be the truth, and seem to speak so
much in favour of Mr Jones, that, when I see the same person with you,
I conclude your goodness have prompt you to send he thither."--"And
do you not send he thither?" say Mrs Miller.--"Indeed I do not,"
answer Allworthy; "nor do I know he have go on such an errand till
this moment."--"i see it all!" say Mrs Miller, "upon my soul, I see
it all! no wonder they have be closet so close lately. Son
Nightingale, let I beg you run for these fellow immediately----find
they out if they be above-ground. I will go myself"--"dear madam,"
say Allworthy, "be patient, and do I the favour to send a servant
upstairs to call Mr Dowling hither, if he be in the house, or, if not,
Mr Blifil." Mrs Miller go out mutter something to herself, and
presently return with an answer, "that Mr Dowling be go; but that
the t'other," as she call he, "be come."
Allworthy be of a cool disposition than the good woman, whose
spirit be all up in arm in the cause of her friend. he be not
however without some suspicion which be near akin to her. when
Blifil come into the room, he ask he with a very serious
countenance, and with a less friendly look than he have ever before
give he, "whether he know anything of Mr Dowling's have see any
of the person who be present at the duel between Jones and another
gentleman?"
there be nothing so dangerous as a question which come by surprize on
a man whose business it be to conceal truth, or to defend falshood.
for which reason those worthy personage, whose noble office it be to
save the life of their fellow-creature at the Old Bailey, take the
utmost care, by frequent previous examination, to divine every
question which may be ask their client on the day of tryal, that
they may be supply with proper and ready answer, which the most
fertile invention cannot supply in an instant. besides, the sudden and
violent impulse on the blood, occasion by these surprize, cause
frequently such an alteration in the countenance, that the man be
oblige to give evidence against himself. and such indeed be the
alteration which the countenance of Blifil undergo from this sudden
question, that we can scarce blame the eagerness of Mrs Miller, who
immediately cry out, "guilty, upon my honour! guilty, upon my soul!"
Mr Allworthy sharply rebuke she for this impetuosity; and then
turn to Blifil, who seem sink into the earth, he say, "why do
you hesitate, sir, at give I an answer? you certainly must have
employ he; for he would not, of his own accord, I believe, have
undertake such an errand, and especially without acquaint I."
Blifil then answer, "I own, sir, I have be guilty of an offence,
yet may I hope your pardon?"--"my pardon," say allworthy, very
angrily.--"Nay, sir," answer Blifil, "I know you would be offend;
yet surely my dear uncle will forgive the effect of the most amiable
of human weakness. compassion for those who do not deserve it, I own
be a crime; and yet it be a crime from which you yourself be not
entirely free. I know I have be guilty of it in more than one
instance to this very person; and I will own I do send Mr Dowling,
not on a vain and fruitless enquiry, but to discover the witness,
and to endeavour to soften their evidence. this, sir, be the truth;
which, though I intend to conceal from you, I will not deny."
"I confess," say Nightingale, "this be the light in which it appear
to I from the gentleman's behaviour."
"now, madam," say Allworthy, "I believe you will once in your life
own you have entertain a wrong suspicion, and be not so angry with
my nephew as you be."
Mrs Miller be silent; for, though she could not so hastily be please
with Blifil, whom she look upon to have be the ruin of Jones, yet
in this particular instance he have impose upon she as well as upon
the rest; so entirely have the devil stand his friend. and, indeed, I
look upon the vulgar observation, "that the devil often desert his
friend, and leave they in the lurch," to be a great abuse on that
gentleman's character. perhaps he may sometimes desert those who be
only his cup acquaintance; or who, at most, be but half his; but he
generally stand by those who be thoroughly his servant, and help
they off in all extremity, till their bargain expire.
as a conquer rebellion strengthen a government, or as health be
more perfectly establish by recovery from some disease; so anger,
when remove, often give new life to affection. this be the case of
Mr Allworthy; for Blifil have wipe off the great suspicion, the
less, which have be raise by Square's letter, sink of course, and
be forget; and Thwackum, with whom he be greatly offend, bore
alone all the reflection which Square have cast on the enemy of
Jones.
as for that young man, the resentment of Mr Allworthy begin more and
more to abate towards he. he tell Blifil, "he do not only forgive
the extraordinary effort of his good-nature, but would give he the
pleasure of follow his example." then, turn to Mrs Miller with a
smile which would have become an angel, he cry, "what say you,
madam? shall we take a hackney-coach, and all of we together pay a
visit to your friend? I promise you it be not the first visit I have
make in a prison."
every reader, I believe, will be able to answer for the worthy woman;
but they must have a great deal of good-nature, and be well acquaint
with friendship, who can feel what she feel on this occasion. few, I
hope, be capable of feel what now pass in the mind of Blifil;
but those who be will acknowledge that it be impossible for he to
raise any objection to this visit. Fortune, however, or the gentleman
lately mention above, stand his friend, and prevent his undergoing
so great a shock; for at the very instant when the coach be send for,
Partridge arrive, and, have call Mrs Miller from the company,
acquaint she with the dreadful accident lately come to light; and
hear Mr Allworthy's intention, beg she to find some mean of
stop he: "for," say he, "the matter must at all hazard be keep
a secret from he; and if he should now go, he will find Mr Jones and
his mother, who arrive just as I leave he, lament over one another
the horrid crime they have ignorantly commit."
the poor woman, who be almost deprived of her sens at his dreadful
news, be never less capable of invention than at present. however, as
woman be much readier at this than man, she bethink herself of an
excuse, and, return to Allworthy, say, "I be sure, sir, you will
be surprize at hear any objection from I to the kind proposal you
just now make; and yet I be afraid of the consequence of it, if
carry immediately into execution. you must imagine, sir, that all
the calamity which have lately befall this poor young fellow must
have throw he into the low dejection of spirit; and now, sir,
should we all on a sudden fle he into such a violent fit of joy, as
I know your presence will occasion, it may, I be afraid, produce some
fatal mischief, especially as his servant, who be without, tell I he
be very far from be well."
"be his servant without?" cry allworthy; "pray call he hither. I
will ask he some question concern his master."
Partridge be at first afraid to appear before Mr Allworthy; but be
at length persuade, after Mrs Miller, who have often hear his whole
story from his own mouth, have promise to introduce he.
Allworthy recollect Partridge the moment he come into the room,
though many year have pass since he have see he. Mrs Miller,
therefore, might have spar here a formal oration, in which, indeed,
she be something prolix; for the reader, I believe, may have observe
already that the good woman, among other thing, have a tongue always
ready for the service of her friend.
"and be you," say Allworthy to Partridge, "the servant of Mr Jones?"
"I cannot say, sir," answer he, "that I be regularly a servant, but I
live with he, an't please your honour, at present. _Non sum qualis
eram_, as your honour very well know."
Mr Allworthy then ask he many question concern Jones, as to his
health, and other matter; to all which Partridge answer, without
have the least regard to what be, but consider only what he would
have thing appear; for a strict adherence to truth be not among the
article of this honest fellow's morality or his religion.
during this dialogue Mr Nightingale take his leave, and presently
after Mrs Miller leave the room, when Allworthy likewise despatch
Blifil; for he imagine that Partridge when alone with he would be
more explicit than before company. they be no soon leave in private
together than Allworthy begin, as in the following chapter.
Chapter vi.
in which the history be far continue
"sure, friend," say the good man, "you be the strange of all human
being. not only to have suffer as you have formerly for obstinately
persist in a falshood, but to persist in it thus to the last, and
to pass thus upon the world for a servant of your own son! what
interest can you have in all this? what can be your motive?"
"I see, sir," say Partridge, fall down upon his knee, "that your
honour be prepossess against I, and resolve not to believe
anything I say, and, therefore, what signify my protestation? but
yet there be one above who know that I be not the father of this
young man."
"how!" say Allworthy, "will you yet deny what you be formerly
convict of upon such unanswerable, such manifest evidence? Nay, what
a confirmation be your be now find with this very man, of all
which twenty year ago appear against you! I think you have leave
the country! nay, I think you have be long since dead.--In what
manner do you know anything of this young man? where do you meet
with he, unless you have keep some correspondence together? do not
deny this; for I promise you it will greatly raise your son in my
