opinion, to find that he hath such a sense of filial duty as privately
to support his father for so many year."
"if your honour will have patience to hear I," say Partridge, "I
will tell you all."--being bid go on, he proceed thus: "when your
honour conceive that displeasure against I, it end in my ruin soon
after; for I lose my little school; and the minister, think I
suppose it would be agreeable to your honour, turn I out from the
office of clerk; so that I have nothing to trust to but the barber's
shop, which, in a country place like that, be a poor livelihood; and
when my wife die (for till that time I receive a pension of £12 a
year from an unknown hand, which indeed I believe be your honour's
own, for nobody that ever I hear of doth these thing besides)--but,
as I be say, when she die, this pension forsook I; so that now,
as I owe two or three small debt, which begin to be troublesome to
I, particularly one[*] which an attorney bring up by law-charge
from 15s. to near £30, and as I find all my usual mean of living have
forsook I, I pack up my little all as well as I could, and go
off.
[*] this be a fact which I know happen to a poor clergyman in
Dorsetshire, by the villany of an attorney who, not content with
the exorbitant cost to which the poor man be put by a single
action, bring afterwards another action on the judgment, as it be
call. a method frequently use to oppress the poor, and bring
money into the pocket of attorney, to the great scandal of the
law, of the nation, of Christianity, and even of human nature
itself.
"the first place I come to be Salisbury, where I get into the service
of a gentleman belong to the law, and one of the good gentleman
that ever I know, for he be not only good to I, but I know a
thousand good and charitable act which he do while I staid with he;
and I have know he often refuse business because it be paultry and
oppressive." "you need not be so particular," say Allworthy; "I know
this gentleman, and a very worthy man he be, and an honour to his
profession."--"Well, sir," continue Partridge, "from hence I remove
to Lymington, where I be above three year in the service of another
lawyer, who be likewise a very good sort of a man, and to be sure one
of the merry gentleman in England. well, sir, at the end of the
three year I set up a little school, and be likely to do well again,
have it not be for a most unlucky accident. here I keep a pig; and
one day, as ill fortune would have it, this pig break out, and do a
trespass, I think they call it, in a garden belong to one of my
neighbour, who be a proud, revengeful man, and employ a lawyer,
one--one--I cannot think of his name; but he send for a writ against
I, and have I to size. when I come there, Lord have mercy upon I--to
hear what the counsellor say! there be one that tell my lord a
parcel of the confoundedest lie about I; he say that I use to
drive my hog into other folk's garden, and a great deal more; and at
last he say, he hop I have at last bring my hog to a fair market.
to be sure, one would have think that, instead of be owner only
of one poor little pig, I have be the great hog-merchant in
England. Well--" "pray," say Allworthy, "do not be so particular, I
have hear nothing of your son yet." "o it be a great many year,"
answer Partridge, "before I see my son, as you be pleased to call
him.----I go over to Ireland after this, and teach school at Cork
(for that one suit ruin I again, and I lie seven year in
Winchester jail)."--"Well," say Allworthy, "pass that over till your
return to England."--"Then, sir," say he, "it be about half a year
ago that I land at Bristol, where I staid some time, and not find
it do there, and hearing of a place between that and Gloucester where
the barber be just dead, I go thither, and there I have be about
two month when Mr Jones come thither." he then give Allworthy a very
particular account of their first meeting, and of everything, as well
as he could remember, which have happen from that day to this;
frequently interlard his story with panegyric on Jones, and not
forget to insinuate the great love and respect which he have for
Allworthy. he conclude with say, "now, sir, I have tell your
honour the whole truth." and then repeat a most solemn protestation,
"that he be no more the father of Jones than of the Pope of Rome;"
and imprecate the most bitter curse on his head, if he do not speak
truth.
"what be I to think of this matter?" cry allworthy. "for what
purpose should you so strongly deny a fact which I think it would be
rather your interest to own?" "Nay, sir," answer Partridge (for he
could hold no long), "if your honour will not believe I, you be
like soon to have satisfaction enough. I wish you have mistake the
mother of this young man, as well as you have his father."--And now
be ask what he mean, with all the symptom of horror, both in
his voice and countenance, he tell Allworthy the whole story, which he
have a little before express such desire to Mrs Miller to conceal
from he.
Allworthy be almost as much shock at this discovery as Partridge
himself have be while he relate it. "good heaven!" say he, "in
what miserable distress do vice and imprudence involve man! how much
beyond our design be the effect of wickedness sometimes carry!"
he have scarce utter these word, when Mrs Waters come hastily and
abruptly into the room. Partridge no soon see she than he cry,
"here, sir, here be the very woman herself. this be the unfortunate
mother of Mr Jones. I be sure she will acquit I before your honour.
pray, madam----"
Mrs Waters, without pay any regard to what Partridge say, and
almost without take any notice of he, advance to Mr Allworthy. "I
believe, sir, it be so long since I have the honour of see you, that
you do not recollect I." "indeed," answer Allworthy, "you be so
very much alter, on many account, that have not this man already
acquaint I who you be, I should not have immediately call you to
my remembrance. have you, madam, any particular business which bring
you to I?" Allworthy speak this with great reserve; for the reader
may easily believe he be not well pleased with the conduct of this
lady; neither with what he have formerly hear, nor with what Partridge
have now deliver.
Mrs Waters answered--"indeed, sir, I have very particular business
with you; and it be such as I can impart only to yourself. I must
desire, therefore, the favour of a word with you alone: for I assure
you what I have to tell you be of the utmost importance."
Partridge be then order to withdraw, but before he go, he beg
the lady to satisfy Mr Allworthy that he be perfectly innocent. to
which she answer, "you need be under no apprehension, sir; I shall
satisfy Mr Allworthy very perfectly of that matter."
then Partridge withdraw, and that past between Mr Allworthy and Mrs
Waters which be write in the next chapter.
Chapter vii.
Continuation of the history.
Mrs Waters remain a few moment silent, Mr Allworthy could not
refrain from say, "I be sorry, madam, to perceive, by what I have
since hear, that you have make so very ill a use----" "Mr Allworthy,"
say she, interrupt he, "I know I have fault, but ingratitude to
you be not one of they. I never can nor shall forget your goodness,
which I own I have very little deserve; but be pleased to wave all
upbraid I at present, as I have so important an affair to
communicate to you concern this young man, to whom you have give
my maiden name of Jones."
"have I then," say Allworthy, "ignorantly punish an innocent man,
in the person of he who hath just leave we? be he not the father of
the child?" "indeed he be not," say Mrs Waters. "you may be please
to remember, sir, I formerly tell you, you should one day know; and I
acknowledge myself to have be guilty of a cruel neglect, in not
have discover it to you before. indeed, I little know how
necessary it be." "well, madam," say Allworthy, "be pleased to
proceed." "you must remember, sir," say she, "a young fellow, whose
name be Summer." "very well," cry allworthy, "he be the son of a
clergyman of great learning and virtue, for whom I have the high
friendship." "so it appear, sir," answer she; "for I believe you
breed the young man up, and maintain he at the university; where, I
think, he have finish his study, when he come to reside at your
house; a fine man, I must say, the sun never shine upon; for, besides
the handsome person I ever see, he be so genteel, and have so much
wit and good breeding." "poor gentleman," say Allworthy, "he be
indeed untimely snatch away; and little do I think he have any sin
of this kind to answer for; for I plainly perceive you be go to
tell I he be the father of your child."
"indeed, sir," answer she, "he be not." "how!" say Allworthy, "to
what then tend all this preface?" "to a story," say she, "which I be
concerned fall to my lot to unfold to you. o, sir! prepare to hear
something which will surprize you, will grieve you." "speak," say
Allworthy, "I be conscious of no crime, and cannot be afraid to hear."
"Sir," say she, "that Mr Summer, the son of your friend, educate at
your expense, who, after live a year in the house as if he have be
your own son, die there of the small-pox, be tenderly lament by
you, and bury as if he have be your own; that Summer, sir, be the
father of this child." "how!" say Allworthy; "you contradict
yourself." "that I do not," answer she; "he be indeed the father of
this child, but not by I." "take care, madam," say Allworthy, "do
not, to shun the imputation of any crime, be guilty of falshood.
remember there be one from whom you can conceal nothing, and before
whose tribunal falshood will only aggravate your guilt." "indeed,
sir," say she, "I be not his mother; nor would I now think myself so
for the world." "I know your reason," say Allworthy, "and shall
rejoice as much as you to find it otherwise; yet you must remember,
you yourself confest it before I." "so far what I confest," say she,
"be true, that these hand convey the infant to your bed; convey
it thither at the command of its mother; at her command I afterwards
own it, and think myself, by her generosity, nobly reward, both
for my secrecy and my shame." "who could this woman be?" say
Allworthy. "indeed, I tremble to name she," answer Mrs Waters. "by
all this preparation I be to guess that she be a relation of mine,"
cry he. "indeed she be a near one." at which word Allworthy
start, and she continued--"you have a sister, sir." "a sister!"
repeat he, look aghast.--"As there be truth in heaven," cry
she, "your sister be the mother of that child you find between your
sheet." "can it be possible?" cry he, "good heaven!" "have
patience, sir," say Mrs Waters, "and I will unfold to you the whole
story. just after your departure for London, Miss Bridget come one day
to the house of my mother. she be pleased to say she have hear an
extraordinary character of I, for my learning and superior
understanding to all the young woman there, so she be pleased to say.
she then bid I come to she to the great house; where, when I
attend, she employ I to read to she. she express great
satisfaction in my reading, shew great kindness to I, and make I
many present. at last she begin to catechise I on the subject of
secrecy, to which I give she such satisfactory answer, that, at last,
have lock the door of her room, she take I into her closet, and
then lock that door likewise, she say she should convince I of
the vast reliance she have on my integrity, by communicate a secret
in which her honour, and consequently her life, be concern. she
then stopt, and after a silence of a few minute, during which she
often wipe her eye, she enquire of I if I think my mother might
safely be confide in. I answer, I would stake my life on her
fidelity. she then impart to I the great secret which labour in
her breast, and which, I believe, be deliver with more pain than
she afterwards suffer in child-birth. it be then contrive that my
mother and myself only should attend at the time, and that Mrs Wilkins
should be send out of the way, as she accordingly be, to the very
furth part of dorsetshire, to enquire the character of a servant;
for the lady have turn away her own maid near three month before;
during all which time I officiate about her person upon trial, as she
say, though, as she afterwards declare, I be not sufficiently handy
for the place. this, and many other such thing which she use to say
of I, be all throw out to prevent any suspicion which Wilkins
might hereafter have, when I be to own the child; for she think it
could never be believe she would venture to hurt a young woman with
whom she have intrust such a secret. you may be assure, sir, I be
well pay for all these affront, which, together with be inform
with the occasion of they, very well content I. indeed, the lady
have a great suspicion of Mrs Wilkins than of any other person; not
that she have the least aversion to the gentlewoman, but she think
she incapable of keep a secret, especially from you, sir; for I
have often hear Miss Bridget say, that, if Mrs Wilkins have commit
a murder, she believe she would acquaint you with it. at last the
expect day come, and Mrs Wilkins, who have be keep a week in
readiness, and put off from time to time, upon some pretence or other,
that she might not return too soon, be dispatch. then the child be
bear, in the presence only of myself and my mother, and be by my
mother convey to her own house, where it be privately keep by she
till the evening of your return, when I, by the command of Miss
Bridget, convey it into the bed where you find it. and all
suspicion be afterwards lay asleep by the artful conduct of your
sister, in pretend ill-will to the boy, and that any regard she
shew he be out of meer complacence to you."
Mrs Waters then make many protestation of the truth of this story,
and conclude by say, "thus, sir, you have at last discover your
nephew; for so I be sure you will hereafter think he, and I question
not but he will be both an honour and a comfort to you under that
appellation."
"I need not, madam," say Allworthy, "express my astonishment at what
you have tell I; and yet surely you would not, and could not, have
put together so many circumstance to evidence an untruth. I confess I
recollect some passage relate to that Summer, which formerly give
I a conceit that my sister have some like to he. I mention it to
she; for I have such a regard to the young man, as well on his own
account as on his father's, that I should willingly have consent to
a match between they; but she expr the high disdain of my unkind
suspicion, as she call it; so that I never speak more on the
subject. good heaven! well! the Lord disposeth all things.--yet sure
it be a most unjustifiable conduct in my sister to carry this secret
with she out of the world." "I promise you, sir," say Mrs Waters,
"she always profest a contrary intention, and frequently tell I she
intend one day to communicate it to you. she say, indeed, she be
highly rejoice that her plot have succeed so well, and that you have
of your own accord take such a fancy to the child, that it be yet
unnecessary to make any express declaration. oh! sir, have that lady
live to have see this poor young man turn like a vagabond from
your house: nay, sir, could she have live to hear that you have
yourself employ a lawyer to prosecute he for a murder of which he
be not guilty----forgive I, Mr Allworthy, I must say it be
unkind.--indeed, you have be abuse, he never deserve it of you."
"indeed, madam," say Allworthy, "I have be abuse by the person,
whoever he be, that tell you so." "Nay, sir," say she, "I would not
be mistake, I do not presume to say you be guilty of any wrong.
the gentleman who come to I propose no such matter; he only say,
take I for Mr Fitzpatrick's wife, that, if Mr Jones have murder my
husband, I should be assist with any money I want to carry on the
prosecution, by a very worthy gentleman, who, he say, be well
apprize what a villain I have to deal with. it be by this man I find
out who Mr Jones be; and this man, whose name be dowle, Mr Jones
tell I be your steward. I discover his name by a very odd
accident; for he himself refuse to tell it I; but Partridge, who meet
he at my lodging the second time he come, know he formerly at
Salisbury."
"and do this Mr Dowling," say Allworthy, with great astonishment
in his countenance, "tell you that I would assist in the
prosecution?"--"No, sir," answer she, "I will not charge he
wrongfully. he say I should be assist, but he mention no name.
yet you must pardon I, sir, if from circumstance I think it could
be no other."--"Indeed, madam," say Allworthy, "from circumstance I
be too well convinced it be another. Good Heaven! by what wonderful
mean be the black and deep villany sometimes discovered!--shall
I beg you, madam, to stay till the person you have mention come,
for I expect he every minute? nay, he may be, perhaps, already in the
house."
Allworthy then stept to the door, in order to call a servant, when in
come, not Mr Dowling, but the gentleman who will be see in the next
chapter.
Chapter viii.
further continuation.
the gentleman who now arrive be no other than Mr Western. he no
soon see Allworthy, than, without consider in the least the
presence of Mrs Waters, he begin to vociferate in the follow
manner: "fine doing at my house! a rare kettle of fish I have
discover at last! who the devil would be plague with a daughter?"
"whatbe the matter, neighbour?" say Allworthy. "Matter enough,"
answer Western: "when I think she be just a come to; nay, when
she have in a manner promise I to do as I would ha she, and when I
be a hop to have have nothing more to do than to have send for the
lawyer, and finish all; what do you think I have find out? that the
little b-- hath bin play trick with I all the while, and carry
on a correspondence with that bastard of your. Sister Western, whom I
have quarrel with upon her account, send I word o't, and I order
her pocket to be search when she be asleep, and here I have get un
sign with the son of a whore's own name. I have not have patience to
read half o't, for 'tis long than one of parson Supple's sermon;
but I find plainly it be all about love; and indeed what should it be
else? I have pack she up in chamber again, and to-morrow morning
down she go into the country, unless she consent to be marry
directly, and there she shall live in a garret upon bread and water
all her day; and the soon such a b-- break her heart the well,
though, d--n she, that I believe be too tough. she will live long
enough to plague I." "Mr Western," answer Allworthy, "you know I
have always protest against force, and you yourself consent that
none should be use." "ay," cry he, "that be only upon condition
that she would consent without. what the devil and doctor Faustus!
shallnot I do what I will with my own daughter, especially when I desire
nothing but her own good?" "well, neighbour," answer Allworthy, "if
you will give I leave, I will undertake once to argue with the young
lady." "will you?" say western; "why that be kind now, and
neighbourly, and mayhap you will do more than I have be able to do
with she; for I promise you she hath a very good opinion of you."
"well, sir," say Allworthy, "if you will go home, and release the
young lady from her captivity, I will wait upon she within this
half-hour." "but suppose," say western, "she should run away with un
in the meantime? for lawyer Dowling tell I there be no hope of
hang the fellow at last; for that the man be alive, and like to do
well, and that he think Jones will be out of prison again presently."
"how!" say Allworthy; "what, do you employ he then to enquire or to
do anything in that matter?" "not I," answer Western, "he mention
it to I just now of his own accord." "just now!" cry allworthy,
"why, where do you see he then? I want much to see Mr Dowling."
"why, you may see un an you will presently at my lodging; for there
be to be a meeting of lawyer there this morning about a mortgage.
'Icod! I shall lose two or dree thousand pound, I believe, by that
honest gentleman, Mr Nightingale." "well, sir," say Allworthy, "I
will be with you within the half-hour." "and do for once," cry the
squire, "take a fool's advice; never think of deal with she by
gentle method, take my word for it those will never do. I have try
'um long enough. she must be frighten into it, there be no other
way. tell she Ibe her father; and of the horrid sin of disobedience,
and of the dreadful punishment of it in t'other world, and then tell
she about be lock up all her life in a garret in this, and be
keep only on bread and water." "I will do all I can," say Allworthy;
"for I promise you there be nothing I wish for more than an alliance
with this amiable creature." "Nay, the girl be well enough for matter
o' that," cry the squire; "a man may go far and meet with bad
meat; that I may declare o'her, thof she be my own daughter. and if
she will but be obedient to I, there be narrow a father within a
hundred mile o' the place, that love a daughter well than I do;
but I see you be busy with the lady here, so I will go huome and
expect you; and so your humble servant."
as soon as Mr Western be go Mrs Waters say, "I see, sir, the
squire hath not the least remembrance of my face. I believe, Mr
Allworthy, you would not have know I neither. I be very considerably
alter since that day when you so kindly give I that advice, which I
have be happy have I follow." "indeed, madam," cry allworthy, "it
give I great concern when I first hear the contrary." "indeed, sir,"
say she, "I be ruin by a very deep scheme of villany, which if you
know, though I pretend not to think it would justify I in your
opinion, it would at least mitigate my offence, and induce you to pity
I: you be not now at leisure to hear my whole story; but this I
assure you, I be betray by the most solemn promise of marriage;
nay, in the eye of heaven I be married to he; for, after much
reading on the subject, I be convinced that particular ceremony be
only requisite to give a legal sanction to marriage, and have only a
worldly use in give a woman the privilege of a wife; but that she
who live constant to one man, after a solemn private affiance,
whatever the world may call she, hath little to charge on her own
conscience." "I be sorry, madam," say Allworthy, "you make so ill a
use of your learning. indeed, it would have be well that you have
be possess of much more, or have remain in a state of ignorance.
and yet, madam, I be afraid you have more than this sin to answer
for." "during his life," answer she, "which be above a dozen year,
I most solemnly assure you I have not. and consider, sir, on my behalf,
what be in the power of a woman stript of her reputation and left
destitute; whether the good-natured world will suffer such a stray
sheep to return to the road of virtue, even if she be never so
desirous. I protest, then, I would have choose it have it be in my
power; but necessity drive I into the arm of Captain water, with
whom, though still unmarried, I live as a wife for many year, and
go by his name. I part with this gentleman at Worcester, on his
march against the rebel, and it be then I accidentally meet with Mr
Jones, who rescue I from the hand of a villain. indeed, he be the
worthiest of man. no young gentleman of his age be, I believe, free
from vice, and few have the twentieth part of his virtue; nay,
whatever vice he hath have, I be firmly persuade he hath now take a
resolution to abandon they." "I hope he hath," cry allworthy, "and I
hope he will preserve that resolution. I must say, I have still the
same hope with regard to yourself. the world, I do agree, be apt to
be too unmerciful on these occasion; yet time and perseverance will
get the well of this their disinclination, as I may call it, to
pity; for though they be not, like heaven, ready to receive a
penitent sinner; yet a continue repentance will at length obtain
mercy even with the world. this you may be assure of, Mrs Waters,
that whenever I find you be sincere in such good intention, you
shall want no assistance in my power to make they effectual."
Mrs Waters fall now upon her knee before he, and, in a flood of
tear, make he many most passionate acknowledgment of his goodness,
which, as she truly say, savour more of the divine than human
nature.
Allworthy raise she up, and speak in the most tender manner, make
use of every expression which his invention could suggest to comfort
she, when he be interrupt by the arrival of Mr Dowling, who, upon
his first entrance, see Mrs Waters, start, and appear in some
confusion; from which he soon recover himself as well as he could,
and then say he be in the utmost haste to attend counsel at Mr
Western's lodging; but, however, think it his duty to call and
acquaint he with the opinion of counsel upon the case which he have
before tell he, which be that the conversion of the money in that
case could not be question in a criminal cause, but that an action
of trover might be bring, and if it appear to the jury to be the
money of plaintiff, that plaintiff would recover a verdict for the
value.
Allworthy, without make any answer to this, bolt the door, and
then, advance with a stern look to dowle, he say, "whatever be
your haste, sir, I must first receive an answer to some question. do
you know this lady?"--"that lady, sir!" answer Dowling, with great
hesitation. allworthy then, with the most solemn voice, say, "look
you, Mr Dowling, as you value my favour, or your continuance a moment
long in my service, do not hesitate nor prevaricate; but answer
faithfully and truly to every question I ask.----do you know this
lady?"--"Yes, sir," say Dowling, "I have see the lady." "where,
sir?" "at her own lodging."--"upon what business do you go thither,
sir; and who send you?" "I go, sir, to enquire, sir, about Mr
Jones." "and who send you to enquire about he?" "who, sir? why, sir,
Mr Blifil send I." "and what do you say to the lady concern that
matter?" "Nay, sir, it be impossible to recollect every word." "will
you please, madam, to assist the gentleman's memory?" "he tell I,
sir," say Mrs Waters, "that if Mr Jones have murder my husband, I
should be assist by any money I want to carry on the prosecution,
by a very worthy gentleman, who be well apprize what a villain I have
to deal with. these, I can safely swear, be the very word he
speak."--"were these the word, sir?" say Allworthy. "I cannot charge
my memory exactly," cry dowle, "but I believe I do speak to that
purpose."--"And do Mr Blifil order you to say so?" "I be sure, sir, I
should not have go on my own accord, nor have willingly exceed my
authority in matter of this kind. if I say so, I must have so
understand Mr Blifil's instruction." "look you, Mr Dowling," say
Allworthy; "I promise you before this lady, that whatever you have
do in this affair by Mr Blifil's order I will forgive, provide you
now tell I strictly the truth; for I believe what you say, that you
would not have act of your own accord and without authority in this
matter.----Mr Blifil then likewise send you to examine the two fellow
at Aldersgate?"--"He do, sir." "well, and what instruction do he
then give you? recollect as well as you can, and tell I, as near as
possible, the very word he use."--"Why, sir, Mr Blifil send I to
find out the person who be eye-witness of this fight. he say, he
fear they might be tamper with by Mr Jones, or some of his
friend. he say, blood require blood; and that not only all who
conceal a murderer, but those who omit anything in their power to
bring he to justice, be sharer in his guilt. he say, he find you
be very desirous of have the villain bring to justice, though it
be not proper you should appear in it." "he do so?" say
Allworthy.--"Yes, sir," cry dowle; "I should not, I be sure, have
proceed such length for the sake of any other person live but
your worship."--"What length, sir?" say Allworthy.--"Nay, sir,"
cry dowle, "I would not have your worship think I would, on any
account, be guilty of subornation of perjury; but there be two way
of deliver evidence. I tell they, therefore, that if any offer
should be make they on the other side, they should refuse they, and
that they might be assure they should lose nothing by be honest
man, and tell the truth. I say, we be tell that Mr Jones have
assault the gentleman first, and that, if that be the truth, they
should declare it; and I do give they some hint that they should be
no loser."--"I think you go length indeed," cry
Allworthy.--"Nay, sir," answer Dowling, "I be sure I do not desire
they to tell an untruth;----nor should I have say what I do, unless
it have be to oblige you."--"you would not have think, I believe,"
say Allworthy, "to have oblige I, have you know that this Mr Jones
be my own nephew."--"I be sure, sir," answer he, "it do not become
I to take any notice of what I think you desire to
conceal."--"How!" cry allworthy, "and do you know it then?"--"nay,
sir," answer Dowling, "if your worship bid I speak the truth, I be
sure I shall do it.--indeed, sir, I do know it; for they be almost
the last word which Madam Blifil ever speak, which she mention to
I as I stand alone by her bedside, when she deliver I the letter I
bring your worship from she."--"What letter?" cry Allworthy.--"The
letter, sir," answer Dowling, "which I bring from Salisbury, and
which I deliver into the hand of Mr Blifil."--"O heavens!" cry
Allworthy: "well, and what be the word? what do my sister say to
you?"--"she take I by the hand," answer he, "and, as she deliver
I the letter, say, `I scarce know what I have write. tell my
brother, Mr Jones be his nephew--he be my son.--bless he,' say she,
and then fall backward, as if die away. I presently call in the
people, and she never speak more to I, and die within a few minute
afterwards."--Allworthy stand a minute silent, lift up his eye;
and then, turn to Dowling, say, "how come you, sir, not to deliver
I this message?" "your worship," answer he, "must remember that you
be at that time ill in bed; and, be in a violent hurry, as indeed
I always be, I deliver the letter and message to Mr Blifil, who tell
I he would carry they both to you, which he hath since tell I he
do, and that your worship, partly out of friendship to Mr Jones, and
partly out of regard to your sister, would never have it mention,
and do intend to conceal it from the world; and therefore, sir, if
you have not mention it to I first, I be certain I should never have
think it belong to I to say anything of the matter, either to
your worship or any other person."
we have remark somewhere already, that it be possible for a man to
convey a lie in the word of truth; this be the case at present; for
Blifil have, in fact, tell dowle what he now relate, but have not
impose upon he, nor indeed have imagine he be able so to do. in
reality, the promise which Blifil have make to Dowling be the
motif which have induce he to secrecy; and, as he now very plainly
see Blifil would not be able to keep they, he think proper now to
make this confession, which the promise of forgiveness, join to the
threat, the voice, the look of Allworthy, and the discovery he have
make before, extort from he, who be besides take unawares, and
have no time to consider of evasion.
Allworthy appear well satisfied with this relation, and, have
enjoin on dowle strict silence as to what have past, conduct that
gentleman himself to the door, lest he should see Blifil, who be
return to his chamber, where he exult in the thought of his last
deceit on his uncle, and little suspect what have since pass
below-stair.
as Allworthy be return to his room he meet Mrs Miller in the entry,
who, with a face all pale and full of terror, say to he, "o! sir, I
find this wicked woman hath be with you, and you know all; yet do
not on this account abandon the poor young man. consider, sir, he be
ignorant it be his own mother; and the discovery itself will most
probably break his heart, without your unkindness."
"Madam," say Allworthy, "I be under such an astonishment at what I
have hear, that I be really unable to satisfy you; but come with I
into my room. indeed, Mrs Miller, I have make surprize discovery,
and you shall soon know they."
the poor woman follow he tremble; and now Allworthy, go up to
Mrs Waters, take she by the hand, and then, turn to Mrs Miller,
say, "what reward shall I bestow upon this gentlewoman, for the
service she hath do me?--O! Mrs Miller, you have a thousand time
hear I call the young man to whom you be so faithful a friend, my
son. little do I then think he be indeed relate to I at all.--your
friend, madam, be my nephew; he be the brother of that wicked viper
which I have so long nourish in my bosom.--She will herself tell you
the whole story, and how the youth come to pass for her son. indeed,
Mrs Miller, I be convinced that he hath be wrong, and that I have
be abuse; abuse by one whom you too justly suspect of be a
villain. he be, in truth, the bad of villain."
the joy which Mrs Miller now feel bereft she of the power of speech,
and might perhaps have deprive she of her sens, if not of life, have
not a friendly shower of tear come seasonably to her relief. at
length, recover so far from her transport as to be able to speak,
she cry, "and be my dear Mr Jones then your nephew, sir, and not the
son of this lady? and be your eye open to he at last? and shall I
live to see he as happy as he deserve?" "he certainly be my nephew,"
say Allworthy, "and I hope all the rest."--"And be this the dear good
woman, the person," cry she, "to whom all this discovery be
owing?"--"she be indeed," say Allworthy.--"Why, then," cry Mrs
Miller, upon her knee, "may Heaven shower down its choice blessing
upon her head, and for this one good action forgive she all her sin,
be they never so many!"
Mrs water then inform they that she believe Jones would very
shortly be release; for that the surgeon be go, in company with a
nobleman, to the justice who commit he, in order to certify that
Mr Fitzpatrick be out of all manner of danger, and to procure his
prisoner his liberty.
Allworthy say he should be glad to find his nephew there at his
return home; but that he be then oblige to go on some business of
consequence. he then call to a servant to fetch he a chair, and
presently leave the two lady together.
Mr Blifil, hear the chair order, come downstairs to attend upon
his uncle; for he never be deficient in such act of duty. he ask
his uncle if he be go out, which be a civil way of ask a man
whither he be go: to which the other make no answer, he again
desire to know when he would be pleased to return?--allworthy make no
answer to this neither, till he be just go into his chair, and
then, turn about, he said--"Harkee, sir, do you find out, before my
return, the letter which your mother send I on her death-bed."
Allworthy then depart, and leave Blifil in a situation to be envy
only by a man who be just go to be hang.
chapter ix.
a further continuation.
Allworthy take an opportunity, whilst he be in the chair, of read
the letter from Jones to Sophia, which Western deliver he; and
there be some expression in it concern himself which draw tear
from his eye. at length he arrive at Mr Western's, and be
introduce to Sophia.
when the first ceremony be past, and the gentleman and lady have
take their chair, a silence of some minute ensue; during which the
latter, who have be prepare for the visit by her father, sit play
with her fan, and have every mark of confusion both in her countenance
and behaviour. at length Allworthy, who be himself a little
disconcert, begin thus: "I be afraid, Miss Western, my family hath
be the occasion of give you some uneasiness; to which, I fear, I
have innocently become more instrumental than I intend. be assure,
madam, have I at first know how disagreeable the proposal have be, I
should not have suffer you to have be so long persecute. I hope,
therefore, you will not think the design of this visit be to trouble
you with any further solicitation of that kind, but entirely to
relieve you from they."
"Sir," say Sophia, with a little modest hesitation, "this behaviour
be most kind and generous, and such as I could expect only from Mr
Allworthy; but as you have be so kind to mention this matter, you
will pardon I for say it hath, indeed, give I great uneasiness,
and hath be the occasion of my suffer much cruel treatment from
a father who be, till that unhappy affair, the tender and fond
of all parent. I be convince, sir, you be too good and generous to
resent my refusal of your nephew. our inclination be not in our own
power; and whatever may be his merit, I cannot force they in his
favour." "I assure you, most amiable young lady," say Allworthy, "I
be capable of no such resentment, have the person be my own son, and
have I entertain the high esteem for he. for you say truly,
madam, we cannot force our inclination, much less can they be
direct by another." "oh! sir," answer Sophia, "every word you
speak prove you deserve that good, that great, that benevolent
character the whole world allow you. I assure you, sir, nothing less
than the certain prospect of future misery could have make I resist
the command of my father." "I sincerely believe you, madam," reply
Allworthy, "and I heartily congratulate you on your prudent
foresight, since by so justifiable a resistance you have avoid
misery indeed!" "you speak now, Mr Allworthy," cry she, "with a
delicacy which few man be capable of feeling! but surely, in my
opinion, to lead our life with one to whom we be indifferent must
be a state of wretchedness.----Perhaps that wretchedness would be
even increase by a sense of the merit of an object to whom we
cannot give our affection. if I have marry Mr blifil--" "Pardon my
interrupt you, madam," answer Allworthy, "but I cannot bear the
supposition.--believe I, Miss Western, I rejoice from my heart, I
rejoice in your escape.--i have discover the wretch for whom you
have suffer all this cruel violence from your father to be a
villain." "how, sir!" cry Sophia--"you must believe this surprize
I."--"It hath surprize I, madam," answer Allworthy, "and so it
will the world.----but I have acquaint you with the real truth."
"nothing but truth," say Sophia, "can, I be convince, come from the
lip of Mr Allworthy.----Yet, sir, such sudden, such unexpected
news.----discovere, you say----may villany be ever so!"--"you will
soon enough hear the story," cry Allworthy;--"at present let we not
mention so detest a name.--I have another matter of a very serious
nature to propose.--o! Miss Western, I know your vast worth, nor can
I so easily part with the ambition of be ally to it.--I have a
near relation, madam, a young man whose character be, I be convince,
the very opposite to that of this wretch, and whose fortune I will
make equal to what his be to have be. could I, madam, hope you
would admit a visit from he?" Sophia, after a minute's silence,
answer, "I will deal with the utmost sincerity with Mr Allworthy.
his character, and the obligation I have just receive from he,
demand it. I have determine at present to listen to no such
proposal from any person. my only desire be to be restore to the
affection of my father, and to be again the mistress of his family.
this, sir, I hope to owe to your good office. let I beseech you,
let I conjure you, by all the goodness which I, and all who know
you, have experience, do not, the very moment when you have release
I from one persecution, do not engage I in another as miserable and
as fruitless." "indeed, Miss Western," reply Allworthy, "I be
capable of no such conduct; and if this be your resolution, he must
submit to the disappointment, whatever torment he may suffer under
it." "I must smile now, Mr Allworthy," answer Sophia, "when you
mention the torment of a man whom I do not know, and who can
consequently have so little acquaintance with I." "pardon I, dear
young lady," cry allworthy, "I begin now to be afraid he hath have
too much acquaintance for the repose of his future day; since, if
ever man be capable of a sincere, violent, and noble passion, such,
I be convince, be my unhappy nephew's for Miss Western." "a nephew
of your's, Mr Allworthy!" answer Sophia. "it be surely strange. I
never hear of he before." "indeed, madam," cry allworthy, "it be
only the circumstance of his be my nephew to which you be a
stranger, and which, till this day, be a secret to me.--mr Jones,
who have long love you, he! he be my nephew!" "Mr Jones your nephew,
sir!" cry Sophia, "can it be possible?"--"he be, indeed, madam,"
answer Allworthy; "he be my own sister's son--as such I shall
always own he; nor be I ashamed of own he. I be much more
ashamed of my past behaviour to he; but I be as ignorant of his
merit as of his birth. indeed, Miss Western, I have use he
cruelly----indeed I have."--Here the good man wipe his eye, and
after a short pause proceeded--"i never shall be able to reward he
for his suffering without your assistance.----believe I, most
amiable young lady, I must have a great esteem of that offering which
I make to your worth. I know he hath be guilty of fault; but there
be great goodness of heart at the bottom. believe I, madam, there
be." here he stop, seem to expect an answer, which he presently
receive from Sophia, after she have a little recover herself from
the hurry of spirit into which so strange and sudden information have
throw she: "I sincerely wish you joy, sir, of a discovery in which
you seem to have such satisfaction. I doubt not but you will have all
the comfort you can promise yourself from it. the young gentleman
hath certainly a thousand good quality, which make it impossible
he should not behave well to such an uncle."--"I hope, madam," say
Allworthy, "he hath those good quality which must make he a good
husband.--He must, I be sure, be of all man the most abandon, if a
lady of your merit should condescend--" "you must pardon I, Mr
Allworthy," answer Sophia; "I cannot listen to a proposal of this
kind. Mr Jones, I be convince, hath much merit; but I shall never
receive Mr Jones as one who be to be my husband--upon my honour I
never will."--"Pardon me, madam," cry allworthy, "if I be a little
surprized, after what I have hear from Mr Western--I hope the
unhappy young man hath do nothing to forfeit your good opinion, if
he have ever the honour to enjoy it.--perhap, he may have be
misrepresented to you, as he be to I. the same villany may have
injure he everywhere.--he be no murderer, I assure you; as he hath
be call."--"Mr Allworthy," answer Sophia, "I have tell you my
resolution. I wonder not at what my father hath tell you; but,
whatever his apprehension or fear have be, if I know my heart, I
have give no occasion for they; since it hath always be a fix
principle with I, never to have marry without his consent. this
be, I think, the duty of a child to a parent; and this, I hope,
nothing could ever have prevail with I to swerve from. I do not
indeed conceive that the authority of any parent can oblige we to
marry in direct opposition to our inclination. to avoid a force of
this kind, which I have reason to suspect, I leave my father's house,
and seek protection elsewhere. this be the truth of my story; and
if the world, or my father, carry my intention any far, my own
conscience will acquit I." "I hear you, Miss Western," cry
Allworthy, "with admiration. I admire the justness of your
sentiment; but surely there be more in this. I be cautious of
offend you, young lady; but be I to look on all which I have
hitherto hear or see as a dream only? and have you suffer so much
cruelty from your father on the account of a man to whom you have
be always absolutely indifferent?" "I beg, Mr Allworthy," answer
Sophia, "you will not insist on my reasons;--ye, I have suffer
indeed; I will not, Mr Allworthy, conceal----i will be very sincere
with you--I own I have a great opinion of Mr Jones--I believe--I know
I have suffer for my opinion--I have be treat cruelly by my
aunt, as well as by my father; but that be now past--I beg I may not
be far press; for, whatever hath be, my resolution be now
fix. your nephew, sir, hath many virtue--he hath great virtue, Mr
Allworthy. I question not but he will do you honour in the world, and
make you happy."--"I wish I could make he so, madam," reply
Allworthy; "but that I be convince be only in your power. it be that
conviction which hath make I so earnest a solicitor in his favour."
"you be deceive indeed, sir; you be deceive," say Sophia. "I
hope not by he. it be sufficient to have deceive I. Mr Allworthy,
I must insist on be press no far on this subject. I should
be sorry--nay, I will not injure he in your favour. I wish Mr Jones
very well. I sincerely wish he well; and I repeat it again to you,
whatever demerit he may have to I, I be certain he hath many good
quality. I do not disown my former thought; but nothing can ever
recal they. at present there be not a man upon earth whom I would
more resolutely reject than Mr Jones; nor would the address of Mr
Blifil himself be less agreeable to I."
Western have be long impatient for the event of this conference, and
be just now arrive at the door to listen; when, have hear the
last sentiment of his daughter's heart, he lose all temper, and,
burst open the door in a rage, cry out--"it be a lie! it be a
d--n'd lie! it be all owe to that d--n'd rascal Jones; and if she
could get at un, shewould ha un any hour of the day." here Allworthy
interpose, and address himself to the squire with some anger in
his look, he say, "Mr Western, you have not keep your word with I.
you promise to abstain from all violence."--"why, so I do," cry
western, "as long as it be possible; but to hear a wench tell such
confound lies----zound! doth she think, if she can make vool of
other volk, she can make one of me?--No, no, I know she well than
thee dost." "I be sorry to tell you, sir," answer Allworthy, "it
doth not appear, by your behaviour to this young lady, that you know
she at all. I ask pardon for what I say: but I think our intimacy,
your own desire, and the occasion justify I. she be your daughter,
Mr Western, and I think she doth honour to your name. if I be capable
of envy, I should soon envy you on this account than any other man
whatever."--"odrabbit it!" cry the squire, "I wish she be thine,
with all my heart--wouldst soon be glad to be rid of the trouble o'
she." "indeed, my good friend," answer Allworthy, "you yourself be
the cause of all the trouble you complain of. place that confidence in
the young lady which she so well deserve, and I be certain you will
be the happy father on earth."--"I confidence in she?" cry the
squire. "'Sblood! what confidence can I place in she, when she willnot
do as I would ha' she? let she gi' but her consent to marry as I would
ha' she, and Iwill place as much confidence in she as wouldst ha'
I."--"you have no right, neighbour," answer Allworthy, "to insist
on any such consent. a negative voice your daughter allow you, and
God and nature have think proper to allow you no more."--"a negative
voice!" cry the squire, "ay! ay! Iwill show you what a negative voice
I ha.--go along, go into your chamber, go, you stubborn----." "indeed,
Mr Western," say Allworthy, "indeed you use she cruelly--I cannot
bear to see this--you shall, you must behave to she in a kind
manner. she deserve the good of treatment." "yes, yes," say the
squire, "I know what she deserve: now shebe go, Iwill shew you what
she deserve. see here, sir, here be a letter from my cousin, my Lady
Bellaston, in which she be so kind to gi' I to understand that the
fellow be get out of prison again; and here she advise I to take all
the care I can o' the wench. odzooker! neighbour Allworthy, you donot
know what it be to govern a daughter."
the squire end his speech with some compliment to his own sagacity;
and then Allworthy, after a formal preface, acquaint he with the
whole discovery which he have make concern Jones, with his anger to
Blifil, and with every particular which hath be disclose to the
reader in the precede chapter.
man over-violent in their disposition be, for the most part, as
changeable in they. no soon then be Western inform of Mr
Allworthy's intention to make Jones his heir, than he join heartily
with the uncle in every commendation of the nephew, and become as
eager for her marriage with Jones as he have before be to couple she
to Blifil.
here Mr Allworthy be again force to interpose, and to relate what
have pass between he and Sophia, at which he testify great
surprize.
the squire be silent a moment, and look wild with astonishment at
this account.--At last he cry out, "why, what can be the meaning of
this, neighbour Allworthy? Vond o'un she be, that Iwill be swear
to.----odzooker! I have hit o't. as sure as a gun I have hit o' the
very right o't. itbe all along o' zister. the girl hath get a
hankering after this son of a whore of a lord. I vound them together at
my cousin my Lady Bellaston's. he hath turn the head o' she, thatbe
certain--but d--n I if he shall ha she--i'll ha no lord nor
courtier in my vamily."
Allworthy now make a long speech, in which he repeat his resolution
to avoid all violent measure, and very earnestly recommend gentle
method to Mr Western, as those by which he might be assure of
succeed well with his daughter. he then take his leave, and
return back to Mrs Miller, but be force to comply with the earnest
entreaty of the squire, in promise to bring Mr Jones to visit he
that afternoon, that he might, as he say, "make all matter up with
the young gentleman." at Mr Allworthy's departure, Western promise to
follow his advice in his behaviour to Sophia, say, "I donot know
how 'tis, but d--n I, Allworthy, if you donot make I always do just
as you please; and yet I have as good an estate as you, and be in the
commission of the peace as well as yourself."
Chapter x.
wherein the history begin to draw towards a conclusion.
when Allworthy return to his lodging, he hear Mr Jones be just
arrive before he. he hurry therefore instantly into an empty
chamber, whither he order Mr Jones to be bring to he alone.
it be impossible to conceive a more tender or move scene than the
meeting between the uncle and nephew (for Mrs Waters, as the reader
may well suppose, have at her last visit discover to he the secret
of his birth). the first agony of joy which be feel on both side
be indeed beyond my power to describe: I shall not therefore attempt
it. after Allworthy have raise Jones from his foot, where he have
prostrate himself, and receive he into his arm, "o my child!" he
cry, "how have I be to blame! how have I injure you! what amend
can I ever make you for those unkind, those unjust suspicion which I
have entertain, and for all the suffering they have occasion to
you?" "be I not now make amend?" cry Jones. "would not my
suffering, if they have be ten time great, have be now richly
repay? o my dear uncle, this goodness, this tenderness overpower,
unman, destroy I. I cannot bear the transport which flow so fast
upon I. to be again restore to your presence, to your favour; to be
once more thus kindly receive by my great, my noble, my generous
benefactor."--"Indeed, child," cry allworthy, "I have use you
cruelly."----he then explain to he all the treachery of Blifil, and
again repeat expression of the utmost concern, for have be
induce by that treachery to use he so ill. "o, talk not so!"
answer Jones; "indeed, sir, you have use I nobly. the wise man
might be deceive as you be; and, under such a deception, the good
must have act just as you do. your goodness display itself in the
midst of your anger, just as it then seem. I owe everything to that
goodness, of which I have be most unworthy. do not put I on
self-accusation, by carry your generous sentiment too far. alas!
sir, I have not be punish more than I have deserve; and it shall
be the whole business of my future life to deserve that happiness you
now bestow on I; for, believe I, my dear uncle, my punishment hath
not be throw away upon I: though I have be a great, I be not a
harden sinner; I thank Heaven, I have have time to reflect on my past
life, where, though I cannot charge myself with any gross villany, yet
I can discern folly and vice more than enough to repent and to be
ashamed of; folly which have be attend with dreadful
consequence to myself, and have bring I to the brink of
destruction." "I be rejoice, my dear child," answer Allworthy, "to
hear you talk thus sensibly; for as I be convince hypocrisy (good
Heaven! how have I be impose on by it in other!) be never among
your fault, so I can readily believe all you say. you now see, Tom,
to what danger imprudence alone may subject virtue (for virtue, I be
now convince, you love in a great degree). Prudence be indeed the
duty which we owe to ourselves; and if we will be so much our own
enemy as to neglect it, we be not to wonder if the world be
deficient in discharge their duty to we; for when a man lay the
foundation of his own ruin, other will, I be afraid, be too apt to
build upon it. you say, however, you have see your error, and will
reform they. I firmly believe you, my dear child; and therefore, from
this moment, you shall never be remind of they by I. remember they
only yourself so far as for the future to teach you the well to
avoid they; but still remember, for your comfort, that there be this
great difference between those fault which candor may construe into
imprudence, and those which can be deduce from villany only. the
former, perhaps, be even more apt to subject a man to ruin; but if he
reform, his character will, at length, be totally retrieve; the
world, though not immediately, will in time be reconcile to he; and
he may reflect, not without some mixture of pleasure, on the danger
he hath escape; but villany, my boy, when once discover be
irretrievable; the stain which this leave behind, no time will wash
away. the censure of mankind will pursue the wretch, their scorn will
abash he in publick; and if shame drive he into retirement, he will
go to it with all those terror with which a weary child, who be
afraid of hobgoblin, retreat from company to go to bed alone. here
his murder conscience will haunt him.--Repose, like a false friend,
will fly from he. wherever he turn his eye, horror present itself;
if he look backward, unavailable repentance tread on his heel; if
forward, incurable despair stare he in the face, till, like a
condemn prisoner confine in a dungeon, he detest his present
condition, and yet dread the consequence of that hour which be to
relieve he from it. Comfort yourself, I say, my child, that this be
not your case; and rejoice with thankfulness to he who hath suffer
you to see your error, before they have bring on you that
destruction to which a persistance in even those error must have lead
you. you have desert they; and the prospect now before you be such,
that happiness seem in your own power." at these word Jones fetch
a deep sigh; upon which, when Allworthy remonstrate, he say, "Sir, I
will conceal nothing from you: I fear there be one consequence of my
vice I shall never be able to retrieve. o, my dear uncle! I have lose
a treasure." "you need say no more," answer Allworthy; "I will be
explicit with you; I know what you lament; I have see the young lady,
and have discourse with she concern you. this I must insist on, as
an earnest of your sincerity in all you have say, and of the
stedfastness of your resolution, that you obey I in one instance. to
abide intirely by the determination of the young lady, whether it
shall be in your favour or no. she hath already suffer enough from
solicitation which I hate to think of; she shall owe no far
constraint to my family: I know her father will be as ready to torment
she now on your account as he hath formerly be on another's; but I
be determined she shall suffer no more confinement, no more violence,
no more uneasy hour." "o, my dear uncle!" answer Jones, "lay, I
beseech you, some command on I, in which I shall have some merit in
obedience. believe I, sir, the only instance in which I could disobey
you would be to give an uneasy moment to my Sophia. no, sir, if I be
so miserable to have incur her displeasure beyond all hope of
forgiveness, that alone, with the dreadful reflection of cause she
misery, will be sufficient to overpower I. to call Sophia mine be the
great, and now the only additional blessing which heaven can
bestow; but it be a blessing which I must owe to she alone." "I will
not flat you, child," cry allworthy; "I fear your case be
desperate: I never see strong mark of an unalterable resolution in
any person than appear in her vehement declaration against
receive your address; for which, perhaps, you can account well
than myself." "oh, sir! I can account too well," answer Jones; "I
have sin against she beyond all hope of pardon; and guilty as I be,
my guilt unfortunately appear to she in ten time blacker than the
real colour. o, my dear uncle! I find my folly be irretrievable;
and all your goodness cannot save I from perdition."
a servant now acquaint they that Mr Western be below-stair; for
his eagerness to see Jones could not wait till the afternoon. upon
which Jones, whose eye be full of tear, beg his uncle to
entertain Western a few minute, till he a little recover himself;
to which the good man consent, and, have order Mr Western to be
shewn into a parlour, go down to he.
Mrs Miller no soon hear that Jones be alone (for she have not yet
see he since his release from prison) than she come eagerly into the
room, and, advance towards Jones, wish he heartily joy of his
new-find uncle and his happy reconciliation; add, "I wish I could
give you joy on another account, my dear child; but anything so
inexorable I never see."
Jones, with some appearance of surprize, ask she what she mean.
"why then," say she, "I have be with your young lady, and have
explain all matter to she, as they be tell to I by my son
Nightingale. she can have no long any doubt about the letter; of
that I be certain; for I tell she my son Nightingale be ready to take
his oath, if she please, that it be all his own invention, and the
letter of his inditing. I tell she the very reason of send the
letter ought to recommend you to she the more, as it be all upon she
account, and a plain proof that you be resolve to quit all your
profligacy for the future; that you have never be guilty of a single
instance of infidelity to she since your see she in town: I be
afraid I go too far there; but Heaven forgive I! I hope your future
behaviour will be my justification. I be sure I have say all I can;
but all to no purpose. she remain inflexible. she say, she have
forgive many fault on account of youth; but express such
detestation of the character of a libertine, that she absolutely
silence I. I often attempt to excuse you; but the justness of her
accusation fly in my face. upon my honour, she be a lovely woman, and
one of the sweetest and most sensible creature I ever see. I could
have almost kiss she for one expression she make use of. it be a
sentiment worthy of Seneca, or of a bishop. `I once fancy madam.'
and she, `I have discover great goodness of heart in Mr Jones; and
for that I own I have a sincere esteem; but an entire profligacy of
manner will corrupt the good heart in the world; and all which a
good-natured libertine can expect be, that we should mix some grain
of pity with our contempt and abhorrence.' she be an angelic creature,
that be the truth on't." "o, Mrs Miller!" answer Jones, "can I bear
to think that I have lose such an angel?" "Lost! no," cry Mrs
Miller; "I hope you have not lose she yet. resolve to leave such
vicious course, and you may yet have hope, nay, if she would remain
inexorable, there be another young lady, a sweet pretty young lady,
and a swing fortune, who be absolutely die for love of you. I
hear of it this very morning, and I tell it to Miss Western; nay, I
go a little beyond the truth again; for I tell she you have refuse
she; but indeed I know you would refuse she. and here I must give you
a little comfort; when I mention the young lady's name, who be no
other than the pretty widow Hunt, I think she turn pale; but when
I say you have refuse she, I will be swear her face be all over
scarlet in an instant; and these be her very word: `I will not deny
but that I believe he have some affection for I.'"
here the conversation be interrupt by the arrival of Western, who
could no long be keep out of the room even by the authority of
allworthy himself; though this, as we have often see, have a wonderful
power over he.
Western immediately go up to Jones, cry out, "my old friend Tom,
I be glad to see thee with all my heart! all past must be forget; I
could not intend any affront to thee, because, as Allworthy here
know, nay, dost know it thyself, I take thee for another person; and
where a body mean no harm, what signify a hasty word or two? one
Christian must forget and forgive another." "I hope, sir," say Jones,
"I shall never forget the many obligation I have have to you; but as
for any offence towards I, I declare I be an utter stranger." "A't,"
say Western, "then give I thy fist; a't as hearty an honest cock as
any in the kingdom. come along with I; Iwill carry thee to thy
mistress this moment." here Allworthy interpose; and the squire be
unable to prevail either with the uncle or nephew, be, after some
litigation, oblige to consent to delay introduce Jones to Sophia
till the afternoon; at which time Allworthy, as well in compassion to
Jones as in compliance with the eager desire of Western, be
prevail upon to promise to attend at the tea-table.
the conversation which now ensue be pleasant enough; and with which,
have it happen early in our history, we would have entertain our
reader; but as we have now leisure only to attend to what be very
material, it shall suffice to say that matter be entirely adjust
as to the afternoon visit Mr Western again return home.
Chapter xi.
the history draw near to a conclusion.
when Mr Western be depart, Jones begin to inform Mr Allworthy and
Mrs Miller that his liberty have be procure by two noble lord, who,
together with two surgeon and a friend of Mr Nightingale's, have
attend the magistrate by whom he have be commit, and by whom, on
the surgeon' oath, that the wounded person be out of all manner of
danger from his wound, he be discharge.
one only of these lord, he say, he have ever see before, and that no
more than once; but the other have greatly surprize he by ask his
pardon for an offence he have be guilty of towards he, occasion,
he say, entirely by his ignorance who he be.
now the reality of the case, with which Jones be not acquaint till
afterwards, be this:--the lieutenant whom Lord Fellamar have employ,
accord to the advice of Lady Bellaston, to press Jones as a
vagabond into the sea-service, when he come to report to his lordship
the event which we have before see, speak very favourably of the
behaviour of Mr Jones on all account, and strongly assure that lord
that he must have mistake the person, for that Jones be certainly a
gentleman; insomuch that his lordship, who be strictly a man of
honour, and would by no mean have be guilty of an action which the
world in general would have condemn, begin to be much concerned for
the advice which he have take.
within a day or two after this, Lord Fellamar happen to dine with
the irish peer, who, in a conversation upon the duel, acquaint his
company with the character of Fitzpatrick; to which, indeed, he do
not do strict justice, especially in what relate to his lady. he say
she be the most innocent, the most injured woman alive, and that from
compassion alone he have undertake her cause. he then declare an
intention of go the next morning to Fitzpatrick's lodging, in
order to prevail with he, if possible, to consent to a separation
from his wife, who, the peer say, be in apprehension for her life,
if she should ever return to be under the power of her husband. Lord
Fellamar agree to go with he, that he might satisfy himself more
concern Jones and the circumstance of the duel; for he be by no
mean easy concern the part he have act. the moment his lordship
give a hint of his readiness to assist in the delivery of the lady, it
be eagerly embrace by the other nobleman, who depend much on the
authority of Lord Fellamar, as he think it would greatly contribute
to awe Fitzpatrick into a compliance; and perhaps he be in the right;
for the poor Irishman no soon see these noble peer have undertake
the cause of his wife than he submit, and article of separation
be soon draw up and sign between the party.
Fitzpatrick, who have be so well satisfied by Mrs Waters concern
the innocence of his wife with Jones at Upton, or perhaps, from some
other reason, be now become so indifferent to that matter, that he
speak highly in favour of Jones to Lord Fellamar, take all the blame
upon himself, and say the other have behave very much like a
gentleman and a man of honour; and upon that lord's further enquiry
concern Mr Jones, Fitzpatrick tell he he be nephew to a gentleman
of very great fashion and fortune, which be the account he have just
receive from Mrs Waters after her interview with Dowling.
Lord Fellamar now think it behove he to do everything in his power
to make satisfaction to a gentleman whom he have so grossly injure,
and without any consideration of rivalship (for he have now give over
all thought of Sophia), determine to procure Mr Jones's liberty,
be satisfy, as well from Fitzpatrick as his surgeon, that the
wound be not mortal. he therefore prevail with the irish peer to
accompany he to the place where Jones be confine, to whom he
behave as we have already relate.
when Allworthy return to his lodging, he immediately carry Jones
into his room, and then acquaint he with the whole matter, as well
what he have hear from Mrs Waters as what he have discover from Mr
Dowling.
Jones express great astonishment and no less concern at this
account, but without make any comment or observation upon it. and
now a message be bring from Mr Blifil, desire to know if his
uncle be at leisure that he might wait upon he. Allworthy start
and turn pale, and then in a more passionate tone than I believe he
have ever use before, bid the servant tell Blifil he know he not.
"consider, dear sir," cry Jones, in a trembling voice. "I have
consider," answer Allworthy, "and you yourself shall carry my
message to the villain. no one can carry he the sentence of his own
ruin so properly as the man whose ruin he hath so villanously
contrive." "pardon I, dear sir," say Jones; "a moment's reflection
will, I be sure, convince you of the contrary. what might perhaps be
but justice from another tongue, would from mine be insult; and to
whom?--my own brother and your nephew. nor do he use I so
barbarously--indeed, that would have be more inexcusable than
anything he hath do. Fortune may tempt man of no very bad
disposition to injustice; but insult proceed only from black and
rancorous mind, and have no temptation to excuse they. let I
beseech you, sir, to do nothing by he in the present height of your
anger. consider, my dear uncle, I be not myself condemn unheard."
Allworthy stand silent a moment, and then, embrace Jones, he say,
with tear gush from his eye, "o my child! to what goodness have I
be so long blind!"
Mrs Miller enter the room at that moment, after a gentle rap which
be not perceive, and see Jones in the arm of his uncle, the poor
woman in an agony of joy fall upon her knee, and burst forth into the
most ecstatic thanksgiving to heaven for what have happen; then,
run to Jones, she embrace he eagerly, cry, "my dearest
friend, I wish you joy a thousand and a thousand time of this blest
day." and next Mr Allworthy himself receive the same congratulation.
to which he answer, "indeed, indeed, Mrs Miller, I be beyond
expression happy." some few more rapture have pass on all side,
Mrs Miller desire they both to walk down to dinner in the parlour,
where she say there be a very happy set of people assemble--be
indeed no other than Mr Nightingale and his bride, and his cousin
Harriet with her bridegroom.
Allworthy excuse himself from din with the company, say he have
order some little thing for he and his nephew in his own apartment,
for that they have much private business to discourse of; but would not
resist promise the good woman that both he and Jones would make part
of her society at supper.
Mrs Miller then ask what be to be do with Blifil? "for indeed,"
say she, "I cannot be easy while such a villain be in my
house."--Allworthy answer, "he be as uneasy as herself on the same
account." "oh!" cry she, "if that be the case, leave the matter to
I, Iwill soon show he the outside out of my door, I warrant you.
here be two or three lusty fellow below-stair." "there will be no
need of any violence," cry allworthy; "if you will carry he a
message from I, he will, I be convince, depart of his own accord."
"will I?" say Mrs Miller; "I never do anything in my life with a
well will." here Jones interfere, and say, "he have consider the
matter well, and would, if Mr Allworthy please, be himself the
messenger. I know," say he, "already enough of your pleasure, sir,
and I beg leave to acquaint he with it by my own word. let I
beseech you, sir," add he, "to reflect on the dreadful consequence
of drive he to violent and sudden despair. how unfit, alas! be this
poor man to die in his present situation." this suggestion have not the
least effect on Mrs Miller. she leave the room, cry, "you be too
good, Mr Jones, infinitely too good to live in this world." but it
make a deep impression on Allworthy. "my good child," say he, "I be
equally astonish at the goodness of your heart, and the quickness of
your understanding. Heaven indeed forbid that this wretch should be
deprive of any mean or time for repentance! that would be a shocking
consideration indeed. go to he, therefore, and use your own
discretion; yet do not flatter he with any hope of my forgiveness;
for I shall never forgive villany far than my religion oblige I,
and that extend not either to our bounty or our conversation."
Jones go up to Blifil's room, whom he find in a situation which
move his pity, though it would have raise a less amiable passion in
many beholder. he cast himself on his bed, where he lie abandon
himself to despair, and drown in tear; not in such tear as flow
from contrition, and wash away guilt from mind which have be
seduce or surprize into it unawares, against the bent of their
natural disposition, as will sometimes happen from human frailty,
even to the good; no, these tear be such as the fright thief
shed in his cart, and be indeed the effect of that concern which
the most savage nature be seldom deficient in feeling for
themselves.
it would be unpleasant and tedious to paint this scene in full length.
let it suffice to say, that the behaviour of Jones be kind to excess.
he omit nothing which his invention could supply, to raise and
comfort the droop spirit of Blifil, before he communicate to he
the resolution of his uncle that he must quit the house that evening.
he offer to furnish he with any money he want, assure he of his
hearty forgiveness of all he have do against he, that he would
endeavour to live with he hereafter as a brother, and would leave
nothing unattempte to effectuate a reconciliation with his uncle.
Blifil be at first sullen and silent, balance in his mind whether
he should yet deny all; but, find at last the evidence too strong
against he, he betake himself at last to confession. he then ask
pardon of his brother in the most vehement manner, prostrate himself
on the ground, and kiss his foot; in short he be now as remarkably
mean as he have be before remarkably wicked.
Jones could not so far check his disdain, but that it a little
discover itself in his countenance at this extreme servility. he
raise his brother the moment he could from the ground, and advise
he to bear his affliction more like a man; repeat, at the same
time, his promise, that he would do all in his power to lessen they;
for which Blifil, make many profession of his unworthiness, pour
forth a profusion of thank; and then, he have declare he would
immediately depart to another lodging, Jones return to his uncle.
among other matter, Allworthy now acquaint Jones with the discovery
which he have make concern the £500 bank-note. "I have," say he,
"already consult a lawyer, who tell I, to my great astonishment,
that there be no punishment for a fraud of this kind. indeed, when I
consider the black ingratitude of this fellow toward you, I think a
highwayman, compare to he, be an innocent person."
"Good Heaven!" say Jones, "be it possible?--i be shock beyond
measure at this news. I think there be not an honester fellow in
the world.----the temptation of such a sum be too great for he to
withstand; for small matter have come safe to I through his hand.
indeed, my dear uncle, you must suffer I to call it weakness rather
than ingratitude; for I be convince the poor fellow love I, and
hath do I some kindness, which I can never forget; nay, I believe
he hath repent of this very act; for it be not above a day or two
ago, when my affair seem in the most desperate situation, that he
visit I in my confinement, and offer I any money I want.
consider, sir, what a temptation to a man who hath taste such bitter
distress, it must be, to have a sum in his possession which must put
he and his family beyond any future possibility of suffer the
like."
"child," cry allworthy, "you carry this forgiving temper too far.
such mistaken mercy be not only weakness, but border on injustice,
and be very pernicious to society, as it encourage vice. the
dishonesty of this fellow I might, perhaps, have pardon, but never
his ingratitude. and give I leave to say, when we suffer any
temptation to atone for dishonesty itself, we be as candid and
merciful as we ought to be; and so far I confess I have go; for I
have often pity the fate of a highwayman, when I have be on the
grand jury; and have more than once apply to the judge on the behalf
of such as have have any mitigating circumstance in their case; but
when dishonesty be attend with any blacker crime, such as cruelty,
murder, ingratitude, or the like, compassion and forgiveness then
become fault. I be convince the fellow be a villain, and he shall be
punish; at least as far as I can punish he."
this be speak with so stern a voice, that Jones do not think proper
to make any reply; besides, the hour appoint by Mr Western now draw
so near, that he have barely time leave to dress himself. here therefore
end the present dialogue, and Jones retire to another room, where
Partridge attend, accord to order, with his cloath.
Partridge have scarce see his master since the happy discovery. the
poor fellow be unable either to contain or express his transport. he
behave like one frantic, and make almost as many mistake while he
be dress Jones as I have see make by Harlequin in dress
himself on the stage.
his memory, however, be not in the least deficient. he recollect
now many omen and presage of this happy event, some of which he have
remark at the time, but many more he now remember; nor do he omit
the dream he have dream the evening before his meeting with Jones;
and conclude with say, "I always tell your honour something bode
in my mind that you would one time or other have it in your power to
make my fortune." Jones assure he that this boding should as
certainly be verify with regard to he as all the other omen have
be to himself; which do not a little add to all the rapture which
the poor fellow have already conceive on account of his master.
Chapter xii.
approach still near to the end.
Jones, be now completely dress, attend his uncle to Mr
Western's. he be, indeed, one of the fine figure ever beheld, and
his person alone would have charm the great part of womankind; but
we hope it hath already appear in this history that nature, when she
form he, do not totally rely, as she sometimes doth, on this merit
only, to recommend her work.
Sophia, who, angry as she be, be likewise set forth to the good
advantage, for which I leave my female reader to account, appear so
extremely beautiful, that even Allworthy, when he see she, could not
forbear whisper Western, that he believe she be the fine
creature in the world. to which Western answer, in a whisper,
overhear by all present, "so much the well for Tom;--for d--n I if
he shallnot ha the tousle she." Sophia be all over scarlet at these
word, while Tom's countenance be altogether as pale, and he be
almost ready to sink from his chair.
the tea-table be scarce remove before Western lug Allworthy out
of the room, tell he he have business of consequence to impart, and
must speak to he that instant in private, before he forget it.
the lover be now alone, and it will, I question not, appear strange
to many reader, that those who have so much to say to one another when
danger and difficulty attend their conversation, and who seem so
eager to rush into each other's arm when so many bar lie in their
way, now that with safety they be at liberty to say or do whatever
they please, should both remain for some time silent and motionless;
insomuch that a stranger of moderate sagacity might have well
conclude they be mutually indifferent; but so it be, however
strange it may seem; both sit with their eye cast downward on the
ground, and for some minute continue in perfect silence.
Mr Jones during this interval attempt once or twice to speak, but
be absolutely incapable, mutter only, or rather sigh out, some
break word; when Sophia at length, partly out of pity to he, and
partly to turn the discourse from the subject which she know well
enough he be endeavour to open, said--
"sure, sir, you be the most fortunate man in the world in this
discovery." "and can you really, madam, think I so fortunate," say
Jones, sigh, "while I have incur your displeasure?"--"nay, sir,"
say she, "as to that you well know whether you have deserve it."
"indeed, madam," answer he, "you yourself be as well apprized of
all my demerit. Mrs Miller hath acquaint you with the whole truth.
o! my Sophia, be I never to hope for forgiveness?"--"i think, Mr
Jones," say she, "I may almost depend on your own justice, and leave
it to yourself to pass sentence on your own conduct."--"Alas! madam,"
answer he, "it be mercy, and not justice, which I implore at your
hand. justice I know must condemn me.--Yet not for the letter I send
to Lady Bellaston. of that I most solemnly declare you have have a true
account." he then insist much on the security give he by
Nightingale of a fair pretence for break off, if, contrary to their
expectation, her ladyship should have accept his offer; but conf
that he have be guilty of a great indiscretion to put such a letter
as that into her power, "which," say he, "I have dearly pay for, in
the effect it have upon you." "I do not, I cannot," say she, "believe
otherwise of that letter than you would have I. my conduct, I think,
shews you clearly I do not believe there be much in that. and yet, Mr
Jones, have I not enough to resent? after what past at Upton, so soon
to engage in a new amour with another woman, while I fancy, and you
pretend, your heart be bleed for I? indeed, you have act
strangely. can I believe the passion you have prof to I to be
sincere? or, if I can, what happiness can I assure myself of with a
man capable of so much inconstancy?" "o! my Sophia," cry he, "do not
doubt the sincerity of the pure passion that ever inflame a human
breast. think, most adorable creature, of my unhappy situation, of my
despair. could I, my Sophia, have flatter myself with the most
distant hope of be ever permit to throw myself at your foot in
the manner I do now, it would not have be in the power of any other
