woman to have inspire a thought which the severe chastity could
have condemn. Inconstancy to you! o Sophia! if you can have goodness
enough to pardon what be past, do not let any cruel future
apprehension shut your mercy against I. no repentance be ever more
sincere. o! let it reconcile I to my heaven in this dear bosom."
"sincere repentance, Mr Jones," answer she, "will obtain the pardon
of a sinner, but it be from one who be a perfect judge of that
sincerity. a human mind may be impose on; nor be there any infallible
method to prevent it. you must expect, however, that if I can be
prevail on by your repentance to pardon you, I will at least insist
on the strong proof of its sincerity." "name any proof in my
power," answer Jones eagerly. "Time," reply she; "time alone, Mr
Jones, can convince I that you be a true penitent, and have resolve
to abandon these vicious course, which I should detest you for, if I
imagine you capable of persevere in they." "do not imagine it,"
cry Jones. "on my knee I intreat, I implore your confidence, a
confidence which it shall be the business of my life to deserve." "let
it then," say she, "be the business of some part of your life to shew
I you deserve it. I think I have be explicit enough in assure
you, that, when I see you merit my confidence, you will obtain it.
after what be past, sir, can you expect I should take you upon your
word?"
he reply, "donot believe I upon my word; I have a well security,
a pledge for my constancy, which it be impossible to see and to
doubt." "what be that?" say Sophia, a little surprized. "I will show
you, my charming angel," cry Jones, seize her hand and carry
her to the glass. "there, behold it there in that lovely figure, in
that face, that shape, those eye, that mind which shin through
these eye; can the man who shall be in possession of these be
inconstant? impossible! my Sophia; they would fix a Dorimant, a Lord
Rochester. you could not doubt it, if you could see yourself with any
eye but your own." Sophia blush and half smile; but, force
again her brow into a frown--"if I be to judge," say she, "of the
future by the past, my image will no more remain in your heart when I
be out of your sight, than it will in this glass when I be out of the
room." "by heaven, by all that be sacred!" say Jones, "it never be
out of my heart. the delicacy of your sex cannot conceive the
grossness of our, nor how little one sort of amour have to do with
the heart." "I will never marry a man," reply Sophia, very gravely,
"who shall not learn refinement enough to be as incapable as I be
myself of make such a distinction." "I will learn it," say Jones.
"I have learn it already. the first moment of hope that my Sophia
might be my wife teach it I at once; and all the rest of her sex
from that moment become as little the object of desire to my sense
as of passion to my heart." "well," say Sophia, "the proof of this
must be from time. your situation, Mr Jones, be now alter, and I
assure you I have great satisfaction in the alteration. you will now
want no opportunity of be near I, and convince I that your
mind be alter too." "o! my angel," cry Jones, "how shall I thank
thy goodness! and be you so good to own that you have a satisfaction
in my prosperity?----believe I, believe I, madam, it be you alone
have give a relish to that prosperity, since I owe to it the dear
hope----o! my Sophia, let it not be a distant one.--i will be all
obedience to your command. I will not dare to press anything far
than you permit I. yet let I intreat you to appoint a short trial.
o! tell I when I may expect you will be convince of what be most
solemnly true." "when I have go voluntarily thus far, Mr Jones,"
say she, "I expect not to be press. Nay, I will not."--"O! donot
look unkindly thus, my Sophia," cry he. "I do not, I dare not press
you.--Yet permit I at least once more to beg you would fix the
period. o! consider the impatience of love."--"a twelvemonth,
perhaps," say she. "o! my Sophia," cry he, "you have name an
eternity."--"perhap it may be something soon," say she; "I will
not be teaze. if your passion for I be what I would have it, I
think you may now be easy."--"Easy! Sophia, call not such an exulting
happiness as mine by so cold a name.----o! transporting thought! be I
not assure that the blessed day will come, when I shall call you
mine; when fear shall be no more; when I shall have that dear, that
vast, that exquisite, ecstatic delight of make my Sophia
happy?"--"indeed, sir," say she, "that day be in your own
power."--"o! my dear, my divine angel," cry he, "these word have
make I mad with joy.----but I must, I will thank those dear lip
which have so sweetly pronounce my bliss." he then catch she in his
arm, and kiss she with an ardour he have never venture before.
at this instant Western, who have stand some time listen, burst into
the room, and, with his hunt voice and phrase, cry out, "to she,
boy, to she, go to her.----That's it, little honey, o thatbe it!
well! what, be it all over? Hath she appoint the day, boy? what,
shall it be to-morrow or next day? it shallnot be put off a minute
long than next day, I be resolve." "let I beseech you, sir," say
Jones, "donot let I be the occasion"----"Beseech mine a----," cry
western. "I think thou hadst be a lad of high mettle than to
give way to a parcel of maidenish tricks.----I tell thee 'tis all
flimflam. zoodiker! shewould have the wedding to-night with all her
heart. Would'st not, Sophy? come, confess, and be an honest girl for
once. what, art dumb? why dost not speak?" "why should I confess,
sir," say Sophia, "since it seem you be so well acquainted with my
thoughts?"----"thatbe a good girl," cry he, "and dost consent then?"
"no, indeed, sir," say Sophia, "I have give no such consent."---"And
wunt not ha un then to-morrow, nor next day?" say Western.--"Indeed,
sir," say she, "I have no such intention." "but I can tell thee,"
reply he, "why hast nut; only because thou dost love to be
disobedient, and to plague and vex thy father." "pray, sir," say
Jones, interfering----"i tell thee thou art a puppy," cry he. "when
I vorbid she, then it be all nothing but sigh and whining, and
languishing and writing; now I be vor thee, she be against thee. all
the spirit of contrary, thatbe all. she be above be guide and
govern by her father, that be the whole truth on't. it be only to
disoblige and contradict I." "what would my papa have I do?" cry
Sophia. "what would I ha thee do?" say he, "why, gi' un thy hand this
moment."--"Well, sir," say Sophia, "I will obey you.--there be my
hand, Mr Jones." "well, and will you consent to ha un to-morrow
morning?" say western.--"i will be obedient to you, sir," cry
she.--"Why then to-morrow morning be the day," cry he. "why then
to-morrow morning shall be the day, papa, since you will have it so,"
say Sophia. Jones then fall upon his knee, and kiss her hand in an
agony of joy, while Western begin to caper and dance about the room,
presently cry out--"where the devil be allworthy? he be without
now, a talk with that d--d lawyer dowling, when he should be
mind other matter." he then sally out in quest of he, and very
opportunely leave the lover to enjoy a few tender minute alone.
but he soon return with Allworthy, say, "if you willnot believe I,
you may ask she yourself. Hast nut gin thy consent, Sophy, to be
married to-morrow?" "such be your command, sir," cry Sophia, "and
I dare not be guilty of disobedience." "I hope, madam," cry
Allworthy, "my nephew will merit so much goodness, and will be always
as sensible as myself of the great honour you have do my family. an
alliance with so charming and so excellent a young lady would indeed
be an honour to the great in England." "yes," cry western, "but
if I have suffer she to stand shill I shall I, dilly dally, you might
not have have that honour yet a while; I be force to use a little
fatherly authority to bring she to." "I hope not, sir," cry
allworthy, "I hope there be not the least constraint." "why, there,"
cry western, "you may bid she unsay all again if you will. dost
repent heartily of thy promise, dost not, Sophia?" "indeed, papa,"
cry she, "I do not repent, nor do I believe I ever shall, of any
promise in favour of Mr Jones." "then, nephew," cry allworthy, "I
felicitate you most heartily; for I think you be the happy of man.
and, madam, you will give I leave to congratulate you on this joyful
occasion: indeed, I be convinced you have bestow yourself on one who
will be sensible of your great merit, and who will at least use his
good endeavour to deserve it." "his good endeavour!" cry western,
"that he will, I warrant un.----Harkee, Allworthy, Iwill bet thee five
pound to a crown we have a boy to-morrow nine month; but prithee
tell I what wut ha! Wut ha Burgundy, Champaigne, or what? for, please
Jupiter, wewill make a night on't." "indeed, sir," say Allworthy, "you
must excuse I; both my nephew and I be engage before I suspect
this near approach of his happiness."--"engage!" quoth the squire,
"never tell me.--I willnot part with thee to-night upon any occasion.
Shalt sup here, please the lord Harry." "you must pardon I, my dear
neighbour!" answer Allworthy; "I have give a solemn promise, and
that you know I never break." "why, prithee, who art engage to?"
cry the squire.----allworthy then inform he, as likewise of the
company.----"odzooker!" answer the squire, "I will go with thee,
and so shall Sophy! for I willnot part with thee to-night; and it would
be barbarous to part Tom and the girl." this offer be presently
embrace by Allworthy, and Sophia consent, have first obtain a
private promise from her father that he would not mention a syllable
concern her marriage.
chapter the last.
in which the history be conclude.
Young Nightingale have be that afternoon, by appointment, to wait on
his father, who receive he much more kindly than he expect. there
likewise he meet his uncle, who be return to town in quest of his
new-married daughter.
this marriage be the lucky incident which could have happen to
the young gentleman; for these brother live in a constant state of
contention about the government of their child, both heartily
despise the method which each other take. each of they therefore now
endeavour, as much as he could, to palliate the offence which his
own child have commit, and to aggravate the match of the other. this
desire of triumph over his brother, add to the many argument
which Allworthy have use, so strongly operate on the old gentleman
that he meet his son with a smile countenance, and actually agree to
sup with he that evening at Mrs Miller's.
as for the other, who really love his daughter with the most
immoderate affection, there be little difficulty in incline he to
a reconciliation. he be no soon inform by his nephew where his
daughter and her husband be, than he declare he would instantly go
to she. and when he arrive there he scarce suffer she to fall upon
her knee before he take she up, and embrace she with a tenderness
which affect all who see he; and in less than a quarter of an hour
be as well reconcile to both she and her husband as if he have
himself join their hand.
in this situation be affair when Mr Allworthy and his company
arrive to complete the happiness of Mrs Miller, who no soon see
Sophia than she guess everything that have happen; and so great be
her friendship to Jones, that it add not a few transport to those
she feel on the happiness of her own daughter.
there have not, I believe, be many instance of a number of people
meet together, where every one be so perfectly happy as in this
company. amongst whom the father of young Nightingale enjoy the
least perfect content; for, notwithstanding his affection for his son,
notwithstanding the authority and the argument of Allworthy, together
with the other motive mention before, he could not so entirely be
satisfied with his son's choice; and, perhaps, the presence of Sophia
herself tend a little to aggravate and heighten his concern, as a
thought now and then suggest itself that his son might have have that
lady, or some other such. not that any of the charm which adorn
either the person or mind of Sophia create the uneasiness; it be the
content of her father's coffer which set his heart a longing. these
be the charm which he could not bear to think his son have
sacrifice to the daughter of Mrs Miller.
the bride be both very pretty woman; but so totally be they
eclipse by the beauty of Sophia, that, have they not be two of the
well-temper girl in the world, it would have raise some envy in
their breast; for neither of their husband could long keep his eye
from Sophia, who sit at the table like a queen receive homage, or,
rather, like a superior be receive adoration from all around she.
but it be an adoration which they give, not which she exact; for
she be as much distinguish by her modesty and affability as by all
her other perfection.
the evening be spend in much true mirth. all be happy, but those
the most who have be most unhappy before. their former suffering and
fear give such a relish to their felicity as even love and fortune,
in their full flow, could not have give without the advantage of
such a comparison. yet, as great joy, especially after a sudden change
and revolution of circumstance, be apt to be silent, and dwell
rather in the heart than on the tongue, Jones and Sophia appear the
least merry of the whole company; which Western observe with great
impatience, often cry out to they, "why dost not talk, boy? why
dost look so grave? Hast lose thy tongue, girl? drink another glass of
wine; sha't drink another glass." and, the more to enliven she, he
would sometimes sing a merry song, which bear some relation to
matrimony and the loss of a maidenhead. Nay, he would have proceed
so far on that topic as to have drive she out of the room, if Mr
Allworthy have not checkt he, sometimes by look, and once or twice by
a "Fie! Mr Western!" he begin, indeed, once to debate the matter, and
assert his right to talk to his own daughter as he think fit; but,
as nobody second he, he be soon reduce to order.
Notwithstanding this little restraint, he be so pleased with the
chearfulness and good-humour of the company, that he insist on their
meeting the next day at his lodging. they all do so; and the lovely
Sophia, who be now in private become a bride too, officiate as the
mistress of the ceremony, or, in the polite phrase, do the honour
of the table. she have that morning give her hand to Jones, in the
chapel at doctors'-common, where Mr Allworthy, Mr Western, and Mrs
Miller, be the only person present.
Sophia have earnestly desire her father that no other of the company,
who be that day to dine with he, should be acquaint with her
marriage. the same secrecy be enjoin to Mrs Miller, and Jones
undertake for Allworthy. this somewhat reconcile the delicacy of
Sophia to the public entertainment which, in compliance with her
father's will, she be oblige to go to, greatly against her own
inclination. in confidence of this secrecy she go through the day
pretty well, till the squire, who be now advanced into the second
bottle, could contain his joy no long, but, fill out a bumper,
drink a health to the bride. the health be immediately pledge by all
present, to the great confusion of our poor blushing Sophia, and the
great concern of Jones upon her account. to say truth, there be not a
person present make wise by this discovery; for Mrs Miller have
whisper it to her daughter, her daughter to her husband, her husband
to his sister, and she to all the rest.
Sophia now take the first opportunity of withdraw with the lady,
and the squire sit in to his cup, in which he be, by degree,
desert by all the company except the uncle of young Nightingale, who
love his bottle as well as Western himself. these two, therefore, sit
stoutly to it during the whole evening, and long after that happy hour
which have surrender the charming Sophia to the eager arm of she
enrapture Jones.
thus, reader, we have at length bring our history to a conclusion,
in which, to our great pleasure, though contrary, perhaps, to thy
expectation, Mr Jones appear to be the happy of all humankind; for
what happiness this world afford equal to the possession of such a
woman as Sophia, I sincerely own I have never yet discover.
as to the other person who have make any considerable figure in this
history, as some may desire to know a little more concern they, we
will proceed, in as few word as possible, to satisfy their curiosity.
allworthy hath never yet be prevail upon to see Blifil, but he
hath yield to the importunity of Jones, back by Sophia, to settle
£200 a-year upon he; to which Jones hath privately add a third.
upon this income he live in one of the northern county, about 200
mile distant from London, and lay up £200 a-year out of it, in order
to purchase a seat in the next parliament from a neighbouring borough,
which he have bargain for with an attourney there. he be also lately
turn Methodist, in hope of marry a very rich widow of that sect,
whose estate lie in that part of the kingdom.
Square die soon after he writ the before-mention letter; and as to
Thwackum, he continue at his vicarage. he hath make many fruitless
attempt to regain the confidence of Allworthy, or to ingratiate
himself with Jones, both of whom he flatter to their face, and
abuse behind their back. but in his stead, Mr Allworthy hath lately
take Mr Abraham Adams into his house, of whom Sophia be grow
immoderately fond, and declare he shall have the tuition of her
child.
Mrs Fitzpatrick be separate from her husband, and retain the little
remain of her fortune. she live in reputation at the polite end of
the town, and be so good an economist, that she spend three time
the income of her fortune, without run into debt. she maintain a
perfect intimacy with the lady of the irish peer; and in act of
friendship to her repay all obligation she owe her husband.
Mrs Western be soon reconcile to her niece Sophia, and hath spend
two month together with she in the country. Lady Bellaston make the
latter a formal visit at her return to town, where she behave to
Jones as a perfect stranger, and, with great civility, wish he joy
on his marriage.
Mr Nightingale hath purchase an estate for his son in the
neighbourhood of Jones, where the young gentleman, his lady, Mrs
Miller, and her little daughter reside, and the most agreeable
intercourse subsist between the two family.
as to those of low account, Mrs Waters return into the country,
have a pension of £60 a-year settle upon she by Mr Allworthy, and be
married to Parson Supple, on whom, at the instance of Sophia, Western
hath bestow a considerable living.
Black George, hear the discovery that have be make, run away, and
be never since hear of; and Jones bestow the money on his family,
but not in equal proportion, for Molly have much the great share.
as for Partridge, Jones hath settle £50 a-year on he; and he hath
again set up a school, in which he meet with much well
encouragement than formerly, and there be now a treaty of marriage on
foot between he and Miss Molly Seagrim, which, through the mediation
of Sophia, be likely to take effect.
we now return to take leave of Mr Jones and Sophia, who, within two
day after their marriage, attend Mr Western and Mr Allworthy into
the country. western hath resign his family seat, and the great
part of his estate, to his son-in-law, and hath retire to a less
house of his in another part of the country, which be well for
hunting. indeed, he be often as a visitant with Mr Jones, who, as well
as his daughter, hath an infinite delight in do everything in their
power to please he. and this desire of theirs be attend with such
success, that the old gentleman declare he be never happy in his
life till now. he hath here a parlour and ante-chamber to himself,
where he get drunk with whom he please: and his daughter be still as
ready as formerly to play to he whenever he desire it; for Jones
hath assure she that, as, next to please she, one of his high
satisfaction be to contribute to the happiness of the old man; so,
the great duty which she express and perform to her father, render
she almost equally dear to he with the love which she bestow on
himself.
Sophia hath already produce he two fine child, a boy and a girl,
of whom the old gentleman be so fond, that he spend much of his time
in the nursery, where he declare the tattling of his little
grand-daughter, who be above a year and a half old, be sweet music
than the fine cry of dog in England.
Allworthy be likewise greatly liberal to Jones on the marriage, and
hath omit no instance of shew his affection to he and his lady,
who love he as a father. whatever in the nature of Jones have a
tendency to vice, have be correct by continual conversation with
this good man, and by his union with the lovely and virtuous Sophia.
he hath also, by reflection on his past folly, acquire a discretion
and prudence very uncommon in one of his lively part.
to conclude, as there be not to be find a worthy man and woman,
than this fond couple, so neither can any be imagine more happy. they
preserve the pure and tender affection for each other, an
affection daily encrease and confirm by mutual endearment and
mutual esteem. nor be their conduct towards their relation and
friend less amiable than towards one another. and such be their
condescension, their indulgence, and their beneficence to those below
they, that there be not a neighbour, a tenant, or a servant, who doth
not most gratefully bless the day when Mr Jones be married to his
Sophia.
_FINIS_.
